[C10] PETROLEUM, GAS OR COKE INDUSTRIES -- C10 includes inventions dealing with the extraction, refining, treatment and conversion of petroleum, natural gas and coke, covering equipment, processes and methods for fractionation, cracking, desulfurisation, gas-oil separation and related chemical operations in the oil-and-gas and coke industries.
[C10B] DESTRUCTIVE DISTILLATION OF CARBONACEOUS MATERIALS FOR PRODUCTION OF GAS, COKE, TAR, OR SIMILAR MATERIALS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Retorts or coke ovens and details thereof, such as heating of coke ovens, doors or closures therefor, devices for charging or discharging coke ovens and mechanical treatments of coal charges, cooling or quenching coke, safety devices and other details.  Carbonising or coking processes, including pyrolysis and other methods of destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous materials, using direct heating (including the partial combustion of the material to be treated) and/or indirect heating (e.g. external combustion).  Destructive distillation specially adapted for particular types of solid raw material or for materials in special form (such as cellulose-containing materials, powdered coal, oil shale or bituminous rocks, synthetic polymeric materials e.g. tyres).  Coking mineral oils, bitumen, tar, etc. with solid carbonaceous materials.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cracking oils -> C10G - Hydrothermal carbonisation -> C10L9/086 - Underground gasification of minerals -> E21B43/295  **Glossary:** - Retort: An airtight vessel in which substances are heated for a chemical reaction producing gaseous products to be collected in a collection vessel or for further processing - Coke oven: An airless oven for driving of volatile constituents from coal during carbonisation to produce coke. In "by-product" coke ovens, the gaseous and liquid by-products obtained during the carbonisation process are recovered, the principal by-products are coal tar and coke-oven gas. The coke oven gas is used to heat the ovens by combustion is external heating flues.In "non-recovery" or "heat recovery" coke ovens, the raw gas produced in the ovens is completely combusted and used to generate power. - Beehive oven: Earliest type of coke oven, developed in the 1850s; its main characteristic is that the heat necessary for coking is produced by burning the volatile constituents within the oven, whereby all the gaseous and liquid by-products are lost, together with large amounts of heat. - Destructive distillation: The process of pyrolysis conducted in a distillation apparatus to allow the volatile products to be collected. An example is tar making from pinewood slices (which are rich in terpenes), which are heated in an airless container causing the material to decompose, leaving charcoal and turpentine as by-products. - Coke: A solid, high in carbon content, and structurally in the non-graphitic state, derived from the pyrolysis of organic material (especially low-ash, low-sulphur bituminous coal) which has passed, at least in part, through a liquid or liquid-crystalline state during the carbonization process. The volatile constituents of the coal (including water, coal gas and coal-tar) are driven off by baking in an airless oven at temperatures as high as 2000 degrees C. - Coking: The transformation of coal or heavy oil into coke. - Carbonisation: The conversion of an organic substance into carbon or a carbon-containing residue through pyrolysis or destructive distillation. - Pyrolysis: The chemical decomposition of organic materials by heating in the absence of oxygen or any other reagents, except possibly steam. - Ablative pyrolysis: Fast pyrolysis by contacting feed with hot surface, e.g. hot plate or heated wall. - Torrefaction: Pyrolysis at temperatures below 320 degrees C.
[C10B 1/00] Retort and coke ovens -- Retort and coke ovens are high-temperature vessels in which carbonaceous materials are destructively distilled to generate coke, combustible gases and tar. They may operate in batch or continuous modes and are essential equipment for the petroleum, gas and coke-production industries.
[C10B 1/02] Stationary retorts -- Stationary retorts are fixed-position vessels used for the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks such as coal or petroleum coke, converting them into gas, coke, tar and related products. They operate by heating the material in a sealed chamber where volatile compounds are driven off and collected while the solid residue remains as coke.
[C10B 1/04] Vertical retorts -- C10B 1/04 covers vertical retorts, a type of destructive distillation vessel in which the carbonaceous charge is loaded from the top and moves downward while being heated, allowing continuous production of gases, coke, tar or similar products. The vertical orientation facilitates efficient heat transfer and product discharge in processes such as coal or oil-shale coking. This subclass falls under retort and coke-oven technologies within the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10B 1/06] Horizontal retorts -- Horizontal retorts refer to cylindrical vessels positioned horizontally for the destructive distillation of coal or other carbonaceous feedstocks to produce gas, coke, tar and similar products. In this arrangement the material moves through the heated chamber while the retort lies on its side, facilitating even heating and efficient collection of the by-products.
[C10B 1/08] Inclined retorts -- C10B 1/08 refers to inclined retorts employed in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials, such as coal, where the retort is positioned at an angle to aid the flow of solids and gases during the production of coke, tar, gas or similar products.
[C10B 1/10] Rotary retorts -- Rotary retorts are vessels used in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous material within coke ovens, where the furnace rotates to provide uniform heating. This rotation improves heat transfer and promotes efficient conversion of the feedstock into combustible gas, solid coke and liquid tar.
[C10B 11/00] Coke ovens with inclined chambers -- Coke ovens with inclined chambers are equipment used in the destructive distillation of coal or other carbonaceous materials to produce coke, gas, tar and similar by-products. The inclined design facilitates the movement of material through the heating zone, improving heat transfer and coke quality while allowing continuous or semi-continuous operation. It falls under the CPC subclass for petroleum, gas and coke industries, specifically destructive distillation processes.
[C10B 13/00] Coke ovens with means for bringing and keeping the charge under mechanical pressure -- The subclass C10B 13/00 pertains to coke oven technologies that incorporate devices for applying and retaining mechanical pressure on the charge during carbonisation, thereby improving contact, reaction uniformity and productivity. It is situated within the broader group dealing with destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials for the petroleum, gas and coke sectors.
[C10B 15/00] Other coke ovens -- The CPC class C10B 15/00 covers coke ovens that do not fall under more specific types, including various designs and processes for the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock to produce coke, gas, or tar. It groups miscellaneous oven constructions and operating conditions not otherwise specified in the subclass.
[C10B 15/02] with floor heating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non recovery/heat recovery coke ovens, i.e. distillation gases are partially combusted above the charge and further combustion takes place in flue channels below the floor, such as illustrated by US4287024, DE10201985 and WO2006103043.
[C10B 17/00] Heating of coke ovens -- The CPC entry C10B 17/00 covers technologies and methods used to heat coke ovens during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks. It includes the design, operation and control of heating systems, burners, furnaces and auxiliary equipment that raise the oven temperature to drive the conversion of coal or similar materials into coke, gas, tar and other by-products.
[C10B 19/00] Heating of coke ovens by electrical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Heating of coke by electrical means, e.g. microwave heating.
[C10B 21/00] Heating of coke ovens with combustible gases -- This subclass covers techniques for providing heat to coke ovens by burning gaseous fuels, including composition, distribution, and control of the gas flow, aimed at maintaining required temperatures for coke production.
[C10B 21/02] with lean gas -- This CPC subclass concerns the destructive distillation of carbonaceous material where coke ovens are heated with lean gas, a low-calorific combustible gas. It specifies using such gas as the energy source for producing coke, tar and related by-products.
[C10B 21/04] with rich gas -- C10B 21/04 covers the heating of coke ovens using combustible gases that contain a high proportion of hydrocarbons, known as rich gas. It addresses processes where such gas is introduced to supply heat for the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock, aiding the production of coke, tar and by-product gases. This subclass falls under the broader area of petroleum and coke industry technologies.
[C10B 21/06] in coke ovens suitable for the use of lean gas or rich gas -- This subclass covers coke oven designs that can be heated with either lean (low-calorific) or rich (high-calorific) gases, allowing flexibility in fuel choice during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock to produce coke, tar and gas.
[C10B 21/08] by applying special heating gases -- This subclass covers the use of specially formulated combustible gases to heat coke ovens during destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock. The gases may be enriched with hydrogen, methane or other fuel components to improve energy efficiency, control temperature profiles, and influence coke quality.
[C10B 21/10] Regulating and controlling the combustion -- The CPC subclass C10B 21/10 addresses methods, apparatuses and control strategies used to regulate the combustion of fuel gases during the heating of coke ovens in destructive distillation processes. It includes devices that monitor temperature, pressure, gas flow and flame characteristics to maintain optimal oven operation and product quality.
[C10B 21/12] Burners -- C10B 21/12 - Burners covers devices that generate and deliver combustible gases for heating coke ovens in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials, a process that yields gas, coke, tar or similar products in the petroleum, gas and coke industries.
[C10B 21/14] Devices for reversing the draught -- Devices for reversing the draught are mechanisms installed in coke-oven heating systems that change the direction of airflow generated by the combustion gases. By altering the pressure profile, they allow controlled back-flow or venting to improve temperature uniformity, reduce emissions, and facilitate safe shutdown of the oven.
[C10B 21/16] by controlling or varying the openings between the heating flues and the regenerator flues -- This CPC subclass concerns processes for heating coke ovens in which the flow of gases between the heating flues and the regenerator flues is regulated by adjusting or varying the openings that connect them. By controlling these passages, the temperature and gas composition in the oven can be optimized for efficient coke production.
[C10B 21/18] Recirculating the flue gases -- C10B 21/18 concerns methods for heating coke ovens that reuse the oven's own combustion gases. It covers the capture and recirculation of flue gases back into the furnace to improve fuel efficiency, control temperature and lower emissions during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous material for gas, coke or tar production.
[C10B 21/20] Methods of heating ovens of the chamber oven type -- This subclass covers techniques for supplying heat to chamber-type coke ovens, including the use of combustible gases, preheated air, or indirect heating systems to reach the temperatures required for destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock. It focuses on the practical methods by which the ovens are heated rather than the chemical reactions occurring inside.
[C10B 21/22] by introducing the heating gas and air at various levels
[C10B 21/24] at the top and the bottom of the vertical heating flues -- C10B 21/24 covers the arrangement of heating flues in vertical coke ovens where the hot gases are introduced at both the top and the bottom of the furnace. This configuration is used in destructive distillation processes to improve temperature uniformity and efficiency during coke production.
[C10B 21/26] by introducing the heating gas and air at the top of the vertical flues only -- This subclass covers a method for heating coke ovens in which the combustible gas and air are fed only at the upper end of the vertical flues, creating a top-down combustion zone. The approach aims to improve temperature control and reduce gas uptake by the coke.
[C10B 23/00] Other methods of heating coke ovens -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any methods for heating coke ovens that is not covered in the subgroups C10B17/00 , C10B19/00 and C10B21/00 , e.g Solar heating
[C10B 25/00] Doors or closures for coke ovens -- Doors or closures for coke ovens are sealing components that allow entry, removal of coke and maintenance while preserving the high-temperature, low-oxygen environment needed for destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock. They are designed to withstand thermal stress, prevent gas leakage, and facilitate safe operation of the oven.
[C10B 25/02] Doors -- Doors and other closure devices designed for coke ovens, used in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products. They provide access for loading, unloading and maintenance while containing high temperatures and pressures inside the oven.
[C10B 25/04] for ovens with vertical chambers -- C10B 25/04 pertains to doors or closures designed for coke ovens that feature vertical chambers, used in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock to produce gas, coke, or tar. The classification addresses structural and sealing aspects specific to the vertical oven layout.
[C10B 25/06] for ovens with horizontal chambers
[C10B 25/08] Closing and opening the doors -- C10B 25/08 covers devices and methods for opening and closing the doors or closures of coke ovens used in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials. It includes mechanisms, actuators, seals and safety features that control access to the oven chamber during loading, operation and maintenance.
[C10B 25/10] for ovens with vertical chambers -- The C10B 25/10 subgroup covers doors or closures used on coke ovens that have vertical chambers, employed in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials for producing gas, coke, tar or similar products within the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10B 25/12] for ovens with horizontal chambers -- The C10B 25/12 subclass covers doors or closures specifically designed for coke ovens that have horizontal chambers, used in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous material to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products. It falls under the chemistry section for petroleum, gas or coke industries, focusing on equipment that seals horizontal-chamber coke ovens.
[C10B 25/14] Devices for lifting doors -- Devices for lifting doors in coke ovens are mechanisms that raise, lower, or swing the oven openings to facilitate loading, unloading, and maintenance during destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock. They may employ hydraulic, pneumatic, mechanical or combined systems designed to operate in high-temperature, corrosive environments while ensuring reliable sealing and safe access for workers. This class covers all such lifting arrangements used in coke- oven door or closure assemblies.
[C10B 25/16] Sealing -- C10B 25/16 covers sealing devices for doors or closures of coke ovens, including gaskets, packing or other arrangements that ensure gas-tight or airtight closure during destructive distillation of carbonaceous material.
[C10B 25/18] Cooling -- C10B 25/18 covers the cooling of doors or other closures used in coke ovens during destructive distillation of carbonaceous material. It includes systems and methods that reduce the temperature of these components to protect equipment and improve safety. This classification falls under chemistry, specifically petroleum, gas or coke industries, and the production of gas, coke, tar, or similar materials.
[C10B 25/20] Lids or closures for charging holes -- The CPC class C10B 25/20 covers lids or closures for the charging holes of coke ovens, which are used in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products. These components seal or protect the oven openings during operation.
[C10B 25/22] for ovens with vertical chambers -- C10B 25/22 covers doors or closures specifically designed for coke ovens that incorporate vertical chambers, used in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products. The classification relates to equipment that seals or accesses these vertical-chamber ovens.
[C10B 25/24] for ovens with horizontal chambers -- C10B 25/24 covers doors or closures specifically adapted for coke ovens that feature horizontal chambers, used in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous material to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products. It falls within the chemistry section for petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10B 27/00] Arrangements for withdrawal of the distillation gases -- This subclass covers equipment and methods used to draw off gases generated during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks such as coal, coke or petroleum residues. It includes piping, valves, collection systems and control arrangements designed to safely remove and direct the produced gases for further processing or disposal.
[C10B 27/02] with outlets arranged at different levels in the chamber -- C10B 27/02 covers designs for extracting gases produced during destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials where the chamber includes multiple outlets positioned at different heights. By arranging the outlets at varying levels the system can separate gases according to temperature or composition, improving collection efficiency and product quality.
[C10B 27/04] during the charging operation of the oven -- C10B 27/04 pertains to the control of gas withdrawal from a destructive distillation oven while the raw material is being loaded. It deals with equipment or methods that manage the escape of gases such as coke oven gas during the charging phase, ensuring safe and efficient operation.
[C10B 27/06] Conduit details, e.g. valves -- C10B 27/06 covers the design and arrangement of conduits used to withdraw gases generated during destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials, such as those employed in petroleum, gas or coke processing. It includes details of components like valves, pipelines and other flow-control devices that manage the removal of distillation gases, tar, coke and related products.
[C10B 29/00] Other details of coke ovens -- C10B 29/00 relates to supplementary aspects of coke oven construction and operation that are not covered by more specific subclasses, including auxiliary equipment, control mechanisms, modifications for improved efficiency, safety or product quality, and other detailed features of the coke-production process.
[C10B 29/02] Brickwork, e.g. casings, linings, walls -- The C10B 29/02 subclass relates to the brick structures employed in coke ovens, covering casings, linings and walls that contain the destructive distillation of carbonaceous material. It addresses the design, arrangement and material choices of refractory bricks used to withstand the high temperatures and corrosive environment of coke, gas and tar production.
[C10B 29/04] Controlling or preventing expansion or contraction -- C10B 29/04 covers techniques and equipment used to control or prevent the expansion and contraction of coke ovens and related structures during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials, aiming to manage thermal stresses and maintain oven integrity and product quality.
[C10B 29/06] Preventing or repairing leakages of the brickwork -- The subclass covers methods and devices for stopping or fixing leaks in the brickwork of coke ovens used in destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks. It includes sealing, patching, reinforcement or replacement techniques that maintain oven integrity and prevent loss of gas, tar or coke during operation.
[C10B 29/08] Bracing or foundation of the ovens -- The C10B 29/08 class covers the structural support and anchoring of coke ovens used in destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials. It includes bracing systems, foundations and related methods that stabilize oven walls and frames to resist thermal and mechanical stresses during gas, coke and tar production.
[C10B 3/00] Coke ovens with vertical chambers -- This subclass covers designs of coke ovens that use vertical chambers for the carbonization of coal, describing the arrangement, heating, and gas flow in vertical oven structures. It includes details on construction, loading, and discharge methods specific to vertical chamber coke ovens.
[C10B 3/02] with heat-exchange devices -- This subclass of the CPC covers coke ovens that incorporate heat-exchange devices to recover or transfer heat during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous material. The devices improve energy efficiency by capturing heat from the furnace gases for use elsewhere in the process or plant.
[C10B 31/00] Devices for charging and discharging coke ovens -- The C10B 31/00 subclass covers equipment and mechanisms used to load raw material into coke ovens and to remove the resulting coke after the carbonisation process. It includes conveyors, buckets, hoists, rollers and related automation systems that facilitate the charging of coal or other carbonaceous feedstock and the discharging of solid coke for further handling.
[C10B 31/02] for charging vertically -- This subclass covers apparatus designed to load coke ovens by introducing the charge material in a vertical orientation, typically using gravity or vertical feed mechanisms, facilitating efficient filling of the oven during the destructive distillation process. It falls under devices for charging and discharging coke ovens in the CPC.
[C10B 31/04] coke ovens with horizontal chambers -- C10B 31/04 designates coke ovens that use horizontal chambers for the destructive distillation of carbonaceous material, allowing continuous charging and discharging while producing coke, gas and tar.
[C10B 31/06] for charging horizontally -- The C10B 31/06 subclass covers devices used to charge coke ovens from a horizontal direction. It pertains to equipment that introduces the charge material into the oven without vertical insertion, facilitating the destructive distillation process in petroleum and gas-related coke production. This subclass falls under the broader group of devices for charging and discharging coke ovens in the CPC.
[C10B 31/08] coke ovens with horizontal chambers -- C10B 31/08 refers to coke ovens that use horizontal chambers for the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock, where the charging and discharging mechanisms operate along a horizontal axis. This layout aids material handling, promotes uniform heating and enables efficient recovery of gas, tar and coke.
[C10B 31/10] with one compact charge -- The code C10B 31/10 covers devices that charge and discharge coke ovens using a single compact charge of carbonaceous material. It pertains to equipment that introduces a unified charge mass into the oven for destructive distillation, and then removes the resulting coke, gas and tar in one operation.
[C10B 31/12] for liquid materials -- The C10B 31/12 subclass covers equipment for introducing or removing liquid substances in coke ovens, such as pumps, injectors, pipelines and delivery systems used to charge liquids into the oven or discharge liquid by-products during destructive distillation. It is applied to devices that handle liquids within the charging-discharging apparatus of coke-oven installations.
[C10B 33/00] Discharging devices -- C10B 33/00 covers devices used to discharge products from a furnace or reactor during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials such as coal, oil-shale or oil-sands, where the process yields gas, coke, tar or similar outputs. The classification applies to mechanisms that remove, collect or transport these streams, including valves, pistons, conveyors or other apparatus that control the flow of gases, liquids or solid residues from the reaction zone.
[C10B 33/003] Arrangements for pollution-free discharge -- The subclass deals with devices and methods that collect, treat, or otherwise prevent release of gases, tar, coke and related by-products generated during destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials, ensuring emissions are captured or neutralised. It includes seals, traps, condensers, scrubbers and other arrangements that enable pollution-free discharge.
[C10B 33/006] Decoking tools, e.g. hydraulic coke removing tools with boring or cutting nozzles -- Decoking tools are designed to remove coke build-up from equipment used in destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials. They often employ hydraulic pressure combined with boring or cutting nozzles to break, dislodge and extract the solid coke. Such devices are used in petroleum and coke production plants to restore inlet and outlet passages for continued operation.
[C10B 33/02] Extracting coke with built-in devices, e.g. gears, screws -- The code C10B 33/02 covers processes for removing coke from reactors or furnaces by employing integrated mechanical devices such as gears, screws or similar conveyance mechanisms, enabling continuous extraction during destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks.
[C10B 33/04] Pulling-out devices -- Pulling-out devices are mechanisms used to extract solid products such as coke, tar or other residues from the vessel during destructive distillation of carbonaceous material. They form a subclass of discharging devices that operate in processes producing gas, coke or tar from petroleum-related feedstocks. The devices typically engage the material and remove it from the reactor for further handling or processing.
[C10B 33/06] for horizontal chambers -- The CPC subclass C10B 33/06 covers devices used to discharge products from horizontal chambers in destructive-distillation operations such as coke ovens, where carbonaceous material is heated to produce gas, coke, or tar. It focuses on the design and arrangement of discharge mechanisms that operate within horizontally oriented reaction vessels. This subclass falls under the broader group concerning discharging devices for petroleum- and coke-industry processes.
[C10B 33/08] Pushers, e.g. rams -- Pushers, such as rams, are devices employed to forcibly discharge or move carbonaceous material during destructive distillation processes that produce gas, coke, tar or similar products. They are used in systems that handle the transfer of solid feedstock into furnaces, reactors or chambers where thermal decomposition occurs. This classification covers the mechanical means that apply pressure or impact to feed or eject the material.
[C10B 33/10] for horizontal chambers -- C10B 33/10 refers to discharging devices for horizontal chambers in the context of destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials. This classification is used in the petroleum and gas industries. It is a specific category within the broader chemistry classification.
[C10B 33/12] Discharge valves -- C10B 33/12 refers to discharge valves used in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials such as coal, coke or tar to regulate the flow of gas, vapour or liquid from the reactor. The subclass includes any valve designed to open, close or throttle the outlet of a furnace, retort or coke oven in processes that produce gas, coke or tar.
[C10B 33/14] Coke guides -- C10B 33/14 covers devices that guide, direct or support the flow of coke within furnaces, ovens or reactors during destructive distillation of carbonaceous material. The guides may be fixed or movable elements designed to maintain uniform coke movement, prevent channeling or blockages, and improve heat transfer and product quality. This subclass is part of the broader group on coke, tar and gas production from petroleum-related feedstocks.
[C10B 35/00] Combined charging and discharging devices -- Equipment that integrates the introduction of carbonaceous feedstock into a destructive distillation or coke-forming reactor with the simultaneous removal of gases, tar, coke or other products, allowing continuous charging and discharging in a single unit and improving process efficiency.
[C10B 37/00] Mechanical treatments of coal charges in the oven -- This subclass deals with mechanical actions applied to coal placed in a coke oven during its destructive distillation, such as stirring, pushing, rotating or compressing the charge to improve heat transfer, uniform coking or gas-tar yield. It pertains to the petroleum-gas-coke industry.
[C10B 37/02] Levelling charges, e.g. with bars -- This subclass covers methods of evening out the distribution of coal or other carbonaceous material in a coke oven, typically by using bars or similar implements to level the charges before destructive distillation. It is a mechanical treatment of the charge within the oven.
[C10B 37/04] Compressing charges -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Coal charge in the coke oven chambre normally from the top, then pressed or compacted in the oven, e.g compressing (not maintaining charge under mechanical pressure)  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Compressing charges during coking -> C10B47/12
[C10B 37/06] Forming holes in charges -- The subclass C10B 37/06 covers techniques for making perforations or holes in coal or other carbonaceous charges that are processed in an oven for destructive distillation. Such hole-forming methods are used to improve the flow of gases, enhance heat transfer, and promote uniform coke, tar or gas production during the treatment.
[C10B 39/00] Cooling or quenching coke -- C10B 39/00 covers methods for cooling or quenching coke after its formation in destructive distillation processes. This includes techniques to lower coke temperature to prevent further reactions, improve handling, or prepare it for subsequent processing.
[C10B 39/02] Dry cooling outside the oven -- This subclass deals with cooling coke after it leaves the furnace by blowing dry air or other non-moist gases over it, rather than immersing it in water. The dry-cooling step reduces the coke temperature to a level suitable for handling and further processing. It applies to coke produced by destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials.
[C10B 39/04] Wet quenching -- C10B 39/04 pertains to the wet quenching of coke generated by destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials, a step in the petroleum, gas or coke industries. It describes cooling the hot coke with water or aqueous solutions to rapidly lower temperature, control reaction progress and influence the physical properties of the final product.
[C10B 39/06] in the oven -- C10B 39/06 covers the step of cooling or quenching coke while still in the oven during destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks, a process used to produce gas, coke, tar or similar petroleum-related materials. It addresses methods and apparatus for rapidly lowering the temperature of coke before it is removed from the heating chamber.
[C10B 39/08] Coke-quenching towers -- Coke-quenching towers are equipment used in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous material to rapidly cool or quench freshly produced coke, reducing its temperature and preventing combustion or deformation. They typically employ water, steam or inert gas sprays within a vertical column to achieve uniform temperature reduction and improve coke quality.
[C10B 39/10] combined with agitating means, e.g. rotating tables or drums -- This subclass relates to cooling or quenching coke produced by destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials, where the coke is subjected to agitation by devices such as rotating tables or drums to enhance heat removal and material handling. The use of such agitating means is combined with the cooling step to improve efficiency and uniformity of the quenching process.
[C10B 39/12] combined with conveying means -- C10B 39/12 covers processes for cooling or quenching coke that are performed together with conveying equipment, such as rollers, conveyors or pneumatic transport, so that the coke is moved while its temperature is reduced after destructive distillation of carbonaceous material. This combines the handling and cooling steps into a single integrated operation.
[C10B 39/14] Cars
[C10B 39/16] combined with sorting -- C10B 39/16 covers methods where coke that has been cooled or quenched is simultaneously sorted, typically by size or quality, during the cooling step. The combined operation streamlines handling by integrating the cooling-quenching stage with a classification or separation process, improving efficiency in coke production from destructive distillation.
[C10B 39/18] Coke ramps -- C10B 39/18 covers coke ramps, which are devices or structures used to receive hot coke from destructive distillation and provide controlled cooling or quenching. The ramps guide the coke along a inclined path, often incorporating water or other cooling media to reduce temperature and prevent damage, facilitating safe handling and subsequent processing.
[C10B 41/00] Safety devices, e.g. signalling or controlling devices for use in the discharge of coke -- The classification C10B 41/00 pertains to safety devices such as signalling or controlling mechanisms used during the discharge of coke produced by destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials in the petroleum, gas or coke industries. It covers apparatus designed to monitor, warn or regulate coke flow to prevent accidents.
[C10B 41/005] for charging coal -- C10B 41/005 covers safety devices such as signalling or control mechanisms used during the discharge of coke in petroleum-related processes, specifically those employed when coal is charged into the system. The class addresses equipment that monitors or regulates coke release to protect personnel and plant equipment.
[C10B 41/02] for discharging coke -- C10B 41/02 covers safety-related apparatus used when coke is removed from furnaces or reactors in destructive distillation processes. It includes signalling, control or other protective devices that monitor and regulate the discharge of coke to prevent accidents, spills or uncontrolled releases during handling and transfer. This sub-class falls under the broader field of petroleum, gas or coke industry chemistry.
[C10B 41/04] by electrical means -- The subclass C10B 41/04 covers safety devices that employ electrical means to signal or control the discharge of coke produced in destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials. It applies to equipment such as electrically-operated alarms, cut-off switches or monitoring systems used in coke-handling processes within the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10B 41/06] by pneumatic or hydraulic means -- C10B 41/06 designates safety devices-such as signalling or control mechanisms-employed in the discharge of coke that are actuated by pneumatic or hydraulic means. It lies within the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials for gas, coke or tar production, a subgroup of petroleum, gas or coke industry processes.
[C10B 41/08] for the withdrawal of the distillation gases -- The CPC subclass C10B 41/08 covers safety and control apparatus designed for the withdrawal of gases generated during destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials such as coal. It includes signalling, regulating or shut-off devices that manage the flow of coke-oven gas, tar vapour or other distillation products to protect personnel and equipment.
[C10B 43/00] Preventing or removing incrustations -- C10B 43/00 covers techniques for preventing or eliminating incrustations-such as scale, deposits or fouling-formed during destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks in petroleum, gas or coke processes. It includes preventive measures, cleaning procedures and equipment designed to keep reactors, retorts or ovens free of such deposits. This subclass lies within the broader chemistry field under petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10B 43/02] Removing incrustations -- The CPC subclass C10B 43/02 covers methods and apparatus for preventing or eliminating incrustations-such as deposits of coke, tar, or ash-during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks used to produce gases, coke, or tar. It includes techniques for cleaning, treating, or protecting the distillation vessel and related equipment to maintain efficient operation and product quality.
[C10B 43/04] by mechanical means -- The code C10B 43/04 covers methods that prevent or eliminate incrustations on equipment used in destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock for gas, coke or tar production by applying mechanical actions such as scraping, brushing or other physical removal techniques.
[C10B 43/06] from conduits, valves or the like -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for preventing or removing incrustations on conduits, valves and similar equipment used in destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials that produce gas, coke, tar or related products. It falls under the chemistry field, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries, and deals with cleaning internal surfaces to maintain efficient operation.
[C10B 43/08] with liquids -- C10B 43/08 covers the use of liquids to prevent or remove incrustations that form during destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks for producing gas, coke, tar or similar products. It pertains to methods applied in petroleum, gas or coke processing where scale-forming deposits impair operation, focusing on liquid-based cleaning or inhibition techniques.
[C10B 43/10] by burning out -- Sub-class C10B 43/10 covers techniques for removing or preventing scale or other deposits that form during destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks such as coal, by burning out the incrustations. The method involves applying heat or flame to oxidise the deposits, keeping the reactor or furnace surfaces clean and maintaining product quality.
[C10B 43/12] Burners -- C10B 43/12 covers burners employed in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock such as coal, coke or petroleum residues, where the aim is to generate gas, coke, tar or similar products while preventing or removing incrustations on the heating surface. The classification falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and specifically addresses equipment that maintains clean operation during high-temperature thermal processing.
[C10B 43/14] Preventing incrustations -- The C10B 43/14 class covers techniques for preventing or removing incrustations that form on equipment during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials such as coal, coke, or tar. It includes preventive measures and cleaning methods applied in processes that produce gas, coke, tar, or similar products.
[C10B 45/00] Other details -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any devices not covered in the groups of the subclass, e.g. measuring devices  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Controlling -> C10B41/00
[C10B 45/005] Devices for recovering spilled coke, e.g. recovering the coke falling out the oven when opening doors or withdrawing the leveler bar -- C10B 45/005 covers equipment designed to collect and remove coke that falls from a coke oven when doors are opened or the leveler bar is withdrawn. Such devices may include conveyors, pits, brushes or suction systems that transport the spilled material back into the oven or to a handling area, preventing loss and improving safety.
[C10B 45/02] Devices for producing compact unified coal charges outside the oven -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[C10B 47/00] Carbonising or coking processes -- This CPC class covers processes that convert carbonaceous feedstocks into solid coke or liquid tar products through thermal treatment in the absence of oxygen. It includes carbonisation, coking, and related heat-driven transformations used in petroleum refining and coke production. The class falls under destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials.
[C10B 47/02] with stationary charge -- C10B 47/02 covers carbonising or coking operations in which the material to be carbonised remains stationary while heat is applied, typically in fixed-bed furnaces or reactors. The stationary charge is heated to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products without moving the feedstock during the process. This subgroup falls under destructive distillation of carbonaceous material for gas, coke, or tar production.
[C10B 47/04] in shaft furnaces -- This subclass concerns carbonising or coking operations performed in shaft furnaces, where solid carbonaceous feedstock is heated vertically to produce gas, coke, tar, or similar materials. It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, and destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials.
[C10B 47/06] in retorts -- C10B 47/06 designates carbonising or coking processes that are performed in retorts, which are sealed vessels used for the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products. The code applies to batch or continuous retort operations within the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10B 47/08] in beehive ovens -- This subclass (C10B 47/08) relates to the use of beehive ovens for the destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials such as coal or oil shale, producing gas, coke, tar or similar products.
[C10B 47/10] in coke ovens of the chamber type -- The CPC subclass C10B 47/10 covers coke-producing operations performed in chamber-type ovens, where the carbonaceous feed is heated in a closed cavity to effect destructive distillation, yielding coke, gas, tar and related products. It relates specifically to the design, use or improvement of these sealed, compartmentalised coke ovens within the broader field of petroleum and coke industry processes.
[C10B 47/12] in which the charge is subjected to mechanical pressures during coking -- C10B 47/12 covers coking or carbonising processes in which the material to be carbonised is simultaneously subjected to mechanical pressure, such as in pressurised coke ovens or reactors that compress the charge during heating. This subclass falls within the CPC hierarchy for destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials for gas, coke or tar production.
[C10B 47/14] with the aid of hot liquids, e.g. molten salts -- C10B 47/14 covers carbonising or coking operations in which hot liquids-such as molten salts-are used to facilitate the conversion of carbonaceous material into gas, coke, tar, or similar products. The liquid medium provides heat transfer or chemical assistance, improving efficiency or product quality during destructive distillation. This subclass falls under destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials in the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10B 47/16] with indirect heating means both inside and outside the retorts -- The subclass C10B 47/16 covers carbonising or coking operations where heat is applied indirectly from both inside and outside the retort vessels. Such heating arrangements use external furnaces or combustion gases together with internal heat exchangers to raise the temperature of the carbonaceous charge. This dual-heating approach improves temperature uniformity and process control in coke and tar production.
[C10B 47/18] with moving charge -- This subclass deals with carbonising or coking operations in which the material being processed moves through the furnace or reactor, such as on a conveyor, rotating drum, moving hearth or other advancing charge. It covers methods where the feed is continuously displaced while heat is applied to produce coke, gas, tar or similar products.
[C10B 47/20] according to the moving bed type -- The CPC class C10B 47/20 refers to carbonising or coking processes performed in a moving-bed reactor, where the solid carbonaceous material travels through the heating zone while undergoing destructive distillation to yield gas, coke, tar or similar products. It is applied to patents describing moving-bed designs for these carbonisation or coking operations.
[C10B 47/22] in dispersed form -- This subclass covers carbonising or coking operations in which the carbonaceous material is processed in a dispersed state, such as powders, slurries or aerosols, rather than as a compact mass. It falls under destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials for producing gas, coke, tar or similar products.
[C10B 47/24] according to the "fluidised bed" technique -- C10B 47/24 covers carbonising or coking processes in which a fluidised-bed reactor is employed to destructively distil carbonaceous feedstock into gases, coke, tar or similar products. The entry is situated in the chemistry branch for petroleum, gas or coke industries and specifies the fluidised-bed technique for producing these materials.
[C10B 47/26] with the aid of hot liquids, e.g. molten salts -- This subclass covers carbonising or coking methods that employ hot liquids, such as molten salts, to supply heat during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock. The liquid medium transfers thermal energy to the material, aiding its conversion into gases, coke, tar or related products.
[C10B 47/28] Other processes -- C10B 47/28 covers carbonising or coking methods that are not specifically covered by other subclasses, such as miscellaneous destructive distillation techniques used to convert carbonaceous feedstock into gases, coke, tar or similar products. It is applied to processes that fall outside the more detailed categories within the broader petroleum, gas or coke industry field.
[C10B 47/30] in rotary ovens or retorts -- C10B 47/30 covers carbonising or coking operations that are carried out in rotary ovens or retorts, where the material is heated in a rotating vessel to produce coke, gas, tar or similar products. This subclass falls under destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials within the petroleum, gas or coke industry.
[C10B 47/32] in ovens with mechanical conveying means -- C10B 47/32 covers carbonising or coking processes carried out in ovens that employ mechanical conveying means such as screws, belts or rollers to transport the feedstock through the reaction zone. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, and destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials for gas, coke or tar production.
[C10B 47/34] with rotary scraping devices -- C10B 47/34 covers carbonising or coking processes in which rotary scraping devices are used to remove coke, tar or similar deposits from the interior of the reactor or furnace during destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials. It falls within the chemistry sector, specifically petroleum, gas or coke industries, and the sub-category for destructive distillation for gas, coke or tar production.
[C10B 47/36] in multi-stage ovens -- C10B 47/36 covers carbonising or coking processes that are carried out in multi-stage ovens, where the material passes through successive heating zones to improve heat distribution and product quality. This subclass falls under destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks in the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10B 47/38] with shaking or vibrating devices -- C10B 47/38 relates to carbonising or coking processes in which the material is subjected to mechanical agitation, such as shaking or vibrating devices, to enhance heat transfer, promote uniform reaction, or improve the quality of the produced gas, coke, tar, or similar products.
[C10B 47/40] with endless conveying devices -- This subclass covers carbonisation or coking operations that use continuously moving conveying equipment such as belts or screws to transport material through a furnace or reactor, enabling the production of gas, coke, tar or similar products.
[C10B 47/42] in vertical direction -- C10B 47/42 relates to carbonising or coking operations in which the solid charge moves or is processed in a vertical orientation, such as in upright retorts, shaft ovens or vertical coke drums, emphasizing the direction of material flow during the reaction.
[C10B 47/44] with conveyor-screws -- C10B 47/44 covers carbonising or coking processes in which the solid carbonaceous feed is transported through the reaction zone by conveyor-screws. The screw system moves material continuously through a heated furnace or oven, enabling steady-state production of gas, coke, tar or similar outputs. This arrangement is used in industrial settings for efficient, large-scale destructive distillation of coal, oil shale or similar feedstocks.
[C10B 47/46] with trucks, containers, or trays -- C10B 47/46 pertains to carbonising or coking processes that are carried out using mobile equipment such as trucks, containers or trays, allowing the feedstock to be processed on-site or during transport.
[C10B 47/48] with tilting or rocking means -- C10B 47/48 relates to carbonising or coking processes in which the charge of carbonaceous material is subjected to tilting or rocking movements during destructive distillation, such as in rotary or rocking ovens, to enhance heat transfer, mixing and product uniformity. This sub-group falls under the broader categories of petroleum, gas or coke industries and the general field of destructive distillation for producing gas, coke, tar or similar substances.
[C10B 49/00] Destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous materials by direct heating with heat-carrying agents including the partial combustion of the solid material to be treated -- C10B 49/00 relates to the destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous materials performed by directly heating the solid with a heat-carrying agent, such as hot gases, liquids or solids, often allowing partial combustion of the material itself. The process is used to produce gases, coke, tar or similar products from the heated carbon feedstock.
[C10B 49/02] with hot gases or vapours, e.g. hot gases obtained by partial combustion of the charge -- C10B 49/02 covers the destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous materials where hot gases or vapours-often produced by partially combusting the charge itself-are used as heat-carrying agents to drive the process, yielding gas, coke, tar or similar products. It is a subcategory of methods for producing these materials within petroleum, gas and coke industries.
[C10B 49/04] while moving the solid material to be treated -- C10B 49/04 concerns destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous feeds where the material is conveyed or otherwise moved while being heated by a heat-carrying medium, including partial combustion, to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products.
[C10B 49/06] according to the moving bed type -- The code C10B 49/06 refers to destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous materials carried out in a moving-bed reactor, where the solid feed travels through the heated zone while heat-carrying agents or partial combustion provide the necessary energy, producing gas, coke, tar or similar products. This subclass falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and carbonaceous material distillation.
[C10B 49/08] in dispersed form -- C10B 49/08 covers destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous materials in dispersed form, where the material is heated directly with heat-carrying agents and may undergo partial combustion. It falls under petroleum and gas processing for producing gas, coke, tar or similar products.
[C10B 49/10] according to the "fluidised bed" technique -- C10B 49/10 covers the destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous materials in which the material is heated directly by a fluidised-bed of hot particles or gases, often involving partial combustion of the feedstock. The fluidised-bed technique improves heat transfer and product recovery during the production of gas, coke, tar or similar outputs.
[C10B 49/12] by mixing tangentially, e.g. in vortex chambers -- The CPC code C10B 49/12 covers destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous feedstocks in which the material is introduced and mixed tangentially, for example in vortex chambers, while being heated directly by heat-carrying agents or partial combustion. This process is used to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products in the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10B 49/14] with hot liquids, e.g. molten metals -- The subclass C10B 49/14 covers destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous materials in which the heat is supplied by hot liquids, such as molten metals, that contact the material during heating. The liquid acts as a heat-carrying medium and may partially react with the feedstock, producing gases, coke, tar or similar products.
[C10B 49/16] with moving solid heat-carriers in divided form -- C10B 49/16 covers destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous materials using moving solid heat-carriers in a divided granular form, such as sand or coke, which are circulated to supply heat through partial combustion.
[C10B 49/18] according to the "moving bed" type -- This subclass covers destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous feeds in a moving-bed reactor where the material is heated directly by a heat-carrying medium, optionally with partial combustion of the feed, to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products.
[C10B 49/20] in dispersed form -- C10B 49/20 refers to processes for destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous materials that are handled in a dispersed state, such as fine particles or slurry, where the material is directly heated by a heat-carrying medium and may undergo partial combustion to produce gases, coke, tar or similar products.
[C10B 49/22] according to the "fluidised bed" technique -- The C10B 49/22 subclass covers destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous feedstocks performed in a fluidised-bed reactor, where the material is directly heated by a heat-carrying medium and may undergo partial combustion to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products.
[C10B 5/00] Coke ovens with horizontal chambers -- C10B 5/00 refers to coke ovens that employ horizontal chambers for the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock, producing coke along with by-products such as gas and tar. The classification falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas or coke industries, and specifically addresses equipment designed for horizontal-chamber coke production.
[C10B 5/02] with vertical heating flues -- The C10B 5/02 subgroup covers coke ovens that have horizontal chambers but use vertically arranged heating flues to pass the hot gases. These vertical flues provide more uniform heat distribution during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous material, improving coke quality and process efficiency.
[C10B 5/04] with cross-over inter-connections -- C10B 5/04 covers coke ovens that have horizontal chambers linked by cross-over inter-connections, allowing the transfer of material or heat between adjacent chambers. This sub-class falls under destructive distillation processes for producing gas, coke, tar, or similar products.
[C10B 5/06] with horizontal heating flues -- This subclass covers coke ovens that have horizontal heating flues used in the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock to produce coke, gas and tar. The flues run horizontally within the oven chamber to provide uniform heat distribution.
[C10B 5/08] with horizontal and vertical heating flues -- The CPC code C10B 5/08 designates coke ovens that employ both horizontal and vertical heating flues to transfer heat through the carbonaceous charge during destructive distillation, enhancing the production of gas, coke, tar, or similar products. It is situated under chemistry, petroleum-gas-coke industries, and coke ovens with horizontal chambers.
[C10B 5/10] with heat-exchange devices -- C10B 5/10 covers coke ovens that have integrated heat-exchange devices for recovering heat from the oven gases. The classification falls under Destructive Distillation of Carbonaceous Materials for Production of Gas, Coke, Tar, or Similar Materials, specifically horizontal-chamber coke ovens. These devices improve energy efficiency by transferring heat to process streams or external systems.
[C10B 51/00] Destructive distillation of solid carbonaceous materials by combined direct and indirect heating -- The CPC subclass C10B 51/00 covers processes that thermally break down solid carbonaceous feedstocks, such as coal or coke, using a combination of direct flame heating and indirect heat transfer to produce gases, coke, tar or related products. It is applied in petroleum, gas or coke industry operations that employ destructive distillation.
[C10B 5/12] with regenerators -- This subclass covers coke ovens that have horizontal chambers and incorporate regenerators to recover heat from the furnace gases. The regenerators are used to improve energy efficiency during the destructive distillation of carbonaceous material and the production of coke, gas or tar. This falls under C10B 5/12.
[C10B 5/14] situated in the longitudinal direction of the chambers -- C10B 5/14 covers features of horizontal coke ovens that are oriented along the length of the chambers, describing elements situated in the longitudinal direction of the oven. This sub-group refines the broader class for destructive distillation of carbonaceous materials in the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10B 5/16] with separated flues -- C10B 5/16 covers coke ovens that have horizontal chambers equipped with separate flues for gas flow, allowing the gases produced during destructive distillation to be directed away from the solid coke and tar streams. This design facilitates independent extraction and handling of the gaseous products.
[C10B 5/18] situated in the longitudinal direction of the oven battery -- The classification refers to coke ovens that have horizontal chambers arranged in a battery and are positioned along the longitudinal axis of the oven line. It covers the layout and orientation of the ovens in the direction of the oven series rather than across or vertical arrangements.
[C10B 5/20] with recuperators -- C10B 5/20 covers coke ovens that have horizontal chambers equipped with recuperators, which are heat-exchange units that recover waste heat from the process gases. The classification is used for patents relating to the design and operation of such ovens in petroleum, gas or coke production.
[C10B 53/00] Destructive distillation, specially adapted for particular solid raw materials or solid raw materials in special form -- It covers methods of destructive distillation where the feedstock is a particular solid raw material or a solid prepared in a special form such as briquettes, pellets or pre-treated lumps, and the operating conditions are specifically adapted to that form to yield gas, coke, tar or similar products.
[C10B 53/02] of cellulose-containing material -- The C10B 53/02 entry covers destructive distillation processes that are specially adapted for solid raw materials containing cellulose, such as wood, paper or other cellulose-rich substances, and produced in special forms. [C10B 53/04] of powdered coal -- C10B 53/04 covers destructive distillation processes that use powdered coal as the feedstock. [C10B 53/06] of oil shale and/or or bituminous rocks -- C10B 53/06 covers the destructive distillation of oil shale or bituminous rocks, where these solid raw materials are heated to decompose them and produce gases, oils, tar and solid carbon residues such as coke. The classification addresses methods for converting these shaped solids into gases, coke, tar or related products, typically in the petroleum, gas or coke industry.
[C10B 55/00] Coking mineral oils, bitumen, tar, and the like or mixtures thereof with solid carbonaceous material -- C10B 55/00 covers processes in the petroleum and coke industries that coke mineral oils, bitumen, tar or similar liquids together with a solid carbonaceous material. The method involves destructive distillation at high temperature to produce coke, tar, gas or related products, often improving feedstock handling or product quality.
[C10B 55/02] with solid materials -- This subgroup covers destructive distillation processes in which liquid hydrocarbons such as mineral oils, bitumen, tar or similar mixtures are combined with solid carbonaceous material and heated to produce gas, coke, tar or related products. It is a subcategory of coking operations that specifically involve the presence of solid materials, designated by the CPC code C10B 55/02.
[C10B 55/04] with moving solid materials -- C10B 55/04 covers the coking of mineral oils, bitumen, tar or similar mixtures together with solid carbonaceous material that is moved during the process. The moving solid materials may be introduced, withdrawn or circulated to improve heat transfer, reaction contact or coke removal. This subclass falls under destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks within the petroleum, gas or coke industry.
[C10B 55/06] according to the "moving bed" type -- C10B 55/06 covers coking operations in which mineral oils, bitumen, tar or similar mixtures are processed together with solid carbonaceous material in a moving-bed reactor. The moving-bed arrangement provides continuous contact between the liquid feed and the solid charge for destructive distillation that yields gas, coke, tar or related products.
[C10B 55/08] in dispersed form -- C10B 55/08 covers coking processes in which the solid carbonaceous material is introduced as a dispersed phase within the liquid feed, such as slurry or finely divided particles mixed with mineral oils, bitumen or tar. [C10B 55/10] according to the "fluidised bed" technique -- C10B 55/10 covers the use of a fluidised-bed reactor for the destructive distillation or coking of mineral oils, bitumen, tar or similar mixtures together with solid carbonaceous material, producing gases, coke, tar or related products. The classification applies to processes that employ a fluidised-bed technique to improve heat transfer and reaction control during the coking operation.
[C10B 57/00] Other carbonising or coking processes -- This subclass covers carbonising or coking operations that do not fall under the more specific methods listed elsewhere, including alternative heating techniques, pressure regimes, or catalyst uses for converting carbonaceous material into gas, coke, tar or similar products. It is positioned within chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries and relates to destructive distillation processes.
[C10B 57/005] After-treatment of coke, e.g. calcination desulfurization -- C10B 57/005 pertains to post-production treatments of coke, such as calcination, desulfurization and related processes that alter its physical or chemical properties to improve its suitability for industrial applications.
[C10B 57/02] Multi-step carbonising or coking processes -- The class C10B 57/02 covers multi-step carbonising or coking processes in which a carbonaceous feedstock undergoes two or more distinct carbonisation stages, often with intermediate heating, cooling, or gas-removal steps to improve yield, quality or product distribution. It applies to petroleum, gas or coke-industry operations that combine successive carbonising steps or recycle intermediates within a single production sequence.
[C10B 57/04] using charges of special composition -- The subgroup C10B 57/04 covers carbonising or coking operations in which the charge introduced into the furnace contains additives or specially formulated mixtures-such as catalysts, binders, promoters, or other substances-designed to modify the reaction, improve yields or alter the properties of the produced gas, coke, tar or similar materials. It applies to other carbonising or coking processes within the destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstocks.

[C10B 57/06] containing additives -- C10B 57/06 covers destructive distillation or coking processes in which additives are included in the carbonaceous feedstock or reaction mixture to modify the properties of the resulting gas, coke, tar or similar products. [C10B 57/08] Non-mechanical pretreatment of the charge , -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pre-treatment of coal by non-mechanical process, e.g. desulfurization.

[C10B 57/14] Features of low-temperature carbonising processes -- C10B 57/14 classifies inventions that relate to the specific characteristics of low-temperature carbonising methods used in destructive distillation of carbonaceous feedstock to produce gas, coke, tar or similar products. [C10B 57/16] Features of high-temperature carbonising processes -- This subclass covers technical aspects of high-temperature carbonising processes used to convert carbonaceous materials into gas, coke, tar or similar products, including reactor design, temperature control, heating methods and other operational parameters.
[C10B 57/18] Modifying the properties of the distillation gases in the oven -- C10B 57/18 covers techniques for adjusting the composition or physical properties of gases generated during destructive distillation in ovens, including the use of temperature control, pressure variation, catalysts, additives or gas-treatment steps to improve gas quality, reduce impurities or tailor the gas for specific downstream uses.
[C10B 7/00] Coke ovens with mechanical conveying means for the raw material inside the oven -- C10B 7/00 covers coke ovens that employ mechanical conveying equipment to transport the raw carbonaceous material within the oven during the destructive distillation process. This includes systems such as screw, belt, or pneumatic conveyors used to feed, spread, or remove coal and coke inside the furnace.
[C10B 7/02] with rotary scraping devices -- The CPC subclass C10B 7/02 covers coke ovens that incorporate mechanical means, such as rotary scraping devices, to convey the raw carbonaceous material inside the oven during destructive distillation for gas, coke, tar or related products. This design improves material handling and uniform heating within the oven.
[C10B 7/04] with shaking or vibrating devices -- C10B 7/04 covers coke ovens equipped with shaking or vibrating devices that mechanically convey the raw material within the oven during destructive distillation for gas, coke or tar production. The vibration assists uniform heating and improves material throughput.
[C10B 7/06] with endless conveying devices -- C10B 7/06 covers coke ovens that use endless conveying devices, such as continuous belts, chains, or screws, to move the raw material through the oven during destructive distillation.
