[C10B 7/08] in vertical direction -- The C10B 7/08 subclass covers coke ovens that use mechanical means to move the raw carbonaceous feedstock vertically inside the oven. It falls under destructive distillation processes for producing gas, coke, or tar, specifically addressing vertical conveyance systems in petroleum-related coke production.
[C10B 7/10] with conveyor-screws -- C10B 7/10 covers coke ovens that use mechanical-conveying, specifically conveyor screws, to transport the coal or other carbonaceous feedstock within the oven during destructive distillation for gas, coke, tar or similar products.
[C10B 7/12] with tilting or rocking means -- C10B 7/12 covers coke ovens equipped with tilting or rocking means that shift the raw material inside the oven during destructive distillation. It falls under the petroleum, gas or coke industries for the production of gas, coke, tar or similar materials.
[C10B 7/14] with trucks, containers, or trays -- C10B 7/14 covers coke ovens that employ mechanical devices such as trucks, containers, or trays to convey the raw material inside the oven during destructive distillation, enabling continuous feeding and removal of coke, gas, tar and related products.
[C10B 9/00] Beehive ovens -- C10B 9/00 covers beehive ovens, a type of fixed-roof furnace used for the destructive distillation of coal, petroleum residues or other carbonaceous feedstocks to produce coke, gas, tar and related products. These ovens are typically dome-shaped, batch-operated units in which heating is applied from the outside.
[C10C] WORKING-UP PITCH, ASPHALT, BITUMEN, TAR -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Working-up, e.g.processing, of tar, pitch, asphalt or bitumen, including use of techniques such as distillation, heat-treatment, water removal or extraction with selective solvents.  Working up implies improvement of the material.  Production of pyroligneous acid.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Working up pyroligneous acid for production of acetic acid -> ( C07C51/42 , C07C53/08 ) - Carbonisation of wood -> C10B53/02 - Obtaining hydrocarbon oils -> C10G - Deasphalting hydrocarbon oils -> C10G21/00 - Dewatering of hydrocarbon oils -> C10G33/00  **Glossary:** - Tar: Tar is a black thermoplastic material produced by the destructive distillation of coal – sometimes referred to as coal tar. Is also a residue in the manufacture of coal gas and coke. May also be derived from plants such as pine trees. - Pitch: Pitch is the name of a large number of highly viscous liquids which appear solid. Pitch may be made from petroleum or plant sources. Petroleum derived pitch is also known as bitumen whereas plant derived pitch is known as resin. Pitch derived from plant resin however is known as rosin.Tar and pitch are often used interchangeably. However, pitch is considered to be more solid whilst tar is considered to be more liquid. - Asphalt: Asphalt is a sticky, black and highly viscous liquid or semi solid that is present in crude petroleum and in some natural deposits sometimes termed asphaltum. In US terminology asphalt (or asphalt cement) is the carefully refined residue from the distillation process of selected crude oils. Outside of the US, the product is called bitumen. - Bitumen: Bitumen is a mixture of organic liquids that are highly viscous, black, sticky, entirely soluble in carbon disulfide and composed primarily of highly condensed polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. - Pyroligneous acid: Pyroligneous acid, also called wood vinegar, is a dark liquid produced by the destructive distillation of wood. Its principal components are acetic acid and methanol. It was once used as a commercial source for acetic acid.
[C10C 1/00] Working-up tar -- C10C 1/00 concerns methods for processing tar obtained from petroleum, gas or coke streams. It includes steps such as heating, distillation, solvent extraction and other treatments aimed at separating useful fractions, removing impurities and upgrading the tar for further use.
[C10C 1/005] by mixing two or more coaltar fractions -- C10C 1/005 covers processes for preparing tar in which two or more coaltar fractions are combined. It is a subcategory of working-up tar within the petroleum, gas or coke industries, focusing on the mixing step to modify properties of the final product.
[C10C 1/02] Removal of water -- The C10C 1/02 class covers methods for removing water from tar during its working-up, including drying, dewatering, decanting or other separation techniques applied to petroleum-derived tar, asphalt, bitumen or similar residues. It is part of the CPC hierarchy under chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, and the working-up of pitch, asphalt, bitumen and tar.
[C10C 1/04] by distillation -- The C10C 1/04 subgroup covers the processing of tar obtained from petroleum, gas or coke by distillation to separate its fractions. This method is classified under working-up tar within the broader chemistry field of petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10C 1/06] Removal of water -- C10C 1/06 addresses processes for eliminating water from tar during its working-up stage. The subclass includes dewatering, drying and separation methods applied in the treatment of pitch, asphalt, bitumen or other tar-like substances in the petroleum and coke industries.
[C10C 1/08] Winning of aromatic fractions -- C10C 1/08 relates to methods for separating and recovering aromatic compounds from tar or similar hydrocarbon streams, typically using distillation, solvent extraction or other fractionation techniques to obtain valuable aromatic fractions.
[C10C 1/10] benzene fraction -- C10C 1/10 covers the benzene fraction obtained when tar, pitch, asphalt or bitumen is worked up in the petroleum, gas or coke industries; it includes methods for separating, treating or using that benzene-rich portion of the distilled tar.
[C10C 1/12] naphthalene fraction -- The CPC subclass C10C 1/12 concerns the naphthalene fraction obtained during the working-up of tar. It includes processes for separating, extracting or recovering naphthalene from petroleum, coal or coke tar and related products, as well as the resulting naphthalene-rich material used in chemical or fuel applications.
[C10C 1/14] Winning of tar oils from tar -- C10C 1/14 covers processes for extracting tar oils from tar during the working-up stage of pitch, asphalt, bitumen or tar production. It includes methods that separate and recover the oil fraction from raw tar, typically by solvent extraction, distillation or similar techniques.
[C10C 1/16] Winning of pitch -- C10C 1/16 covers the step in tar processing where pitch-solid, high-molecular-weight residues-is separated from the liquid tar. It includes methods such as skimming, decanting, filtration or centrifugation that isolate the pitch for further use or disposal, forming part of the working-up of pitch, asphalt, bitumen or tar.
[C10C 1/18] by extraction with selective solvents -- This subclass covers processes that treat tar by contacting it with solvents that preferentially dissolve specific constituents, allowing separation of valuable oils or removal of impurities. It is part of the CPC group for working-up tar within petroleum and coke industry chemistry.
[C10C 1/19] by thermal treatment not involving distillation -- C10C 1/19 covers processes for working-up tar by thermal treatment that does not involve distillation. It is part of the chemistry subclass for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the handling of pitch, asphalt, bitumen and tar.
[C10C 1/20] Refining by chemical means -- C10C 1/20 classifies the chemical refining of tar, a product of pitch and asphalt processing, by means such as oxidation, hydrogenation, or other reactions that alter its composition. It falls within the hierarchy of chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically under working-up pitch, asphalt, bitumen, and tar. This code is used by the EPO and USPTO to catalogue patents that describe chemical methods for improving tar's properties or converting it into more valuable chemicals.
[C10C 1/205] refining in the presence of hydrogen -- C10C 1/205 relates to processes that refine tar, pitch, asphalt or bitumen by treating them with hydrogen, typically through hydrogenation, hydrocracking or other hydrogen-based reactions that improve the material's quality, remove undesired components and increase its usefulness in the petroleum, gas or coke sectors. This subclass falls under working-up tar within the broader chemistry and petroleum industry hierarchy.
[C10C 3/00] Working-up pitch, asphalt, bitumen -- C10C 3/00 covers processes for treating and refining pitch, asphalt and bitumen after they have been produced, including heating, mixing, blending, and purification steps that improve their physical properties and suitability for applications such as paving, roofing or insulation. These working-up methods fall under the chemistry sub-class for petroleum, gas or coke industries and address the preparation of these heavy hydrocarbon materials for commercial use.
[C10C 3/002] by thermal means -- The C10C 3/002 subclass covers methods that employ heat-such as heating, distillation or thermal cracking-to work up or refine pitch, asphalt or bitumen, separating volatiles or modifying viscosity. It specifies thermal techniques used in the processing of these petroleum-derived materials.
[C10C 3/005] by mixing several fractions (also coaltar fractions with petroleum fractions) -- The subclass C10C 3/005 covers methods of preparing pitch, asphalt or bitumen by blending multiple fractions, including the mixing of co-tar or co-coke fractions with petroleum-derived fractions, to obtain a product with specified physical or chemical characteristics.
[C10C 3/007] winning and separation of asphalt from mixtures with aggregates, fillers and other products, e.g. winning from natural asphalt and regeneration of waste asphalt -- C10C 3/007 covers methods for winning and separating asphalt from mixtures that contain aggregates, fillers or other products, including the extraction of asphalt from natural sources and the regeneration of reclaimed asphalt. This entry falls under the working-up of pitch, asphalt and bitumen within petroleum and gas industry classifications.
[C10C 3/02] by chemical means -- C10C 3/02 classifies chemical methods for treating or modifying pitch, asphalt, bitumen or tar during the working-up stage in the petroleum and coke sectors. It includes processes such as sulfonation, oxidation, polymerisation, or other chemical reactions that change the physical or chemical properties of these heavy hydrocarbon products.
[C10C 3/023] with inorganic compounds -- C10C 3/023 covers processes for working up pitch, asphalt or bitumen that involve the addition or use of inorganic compounds, such as catalysts, fillers, or additives, to modify physical or chemical properties during refining or preparation.
[C10C 3/026] with organic compounds -- C10C 3/026 refers to methods of working-up pitch, asphalt, bitumen or tar that incorporate organic compounds. It falls under the chemistry field for petroleum, gas or coke industries and addresses how such additives are used to modify the physical or chemical properties of these materials.
[C10C 3/04] by blowing or oxidising -- C10C 3/04 covers processes for treating pitch, asphalt or bitumen in which the material is subjected to a blowing or oxidising step, typically using air, oxygen or other oxidising gases to increase hardness, reduce volatility, or improve durability. This sub-group is part of the working-up stage of petroleum-derived binders.
[C10C 3/06] by distillation -- This CPC subclass C10C 3/06 covers the working-up of pitch, asphalt or bitumen by distillation, i.e., separating the material into fractions through heating. It falls within the chemistry field, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10C 3/08] by selective extraction -- C10C 3/08 relates to processes for separating specific fractions from pitch, asphalt, bitumen or tar by selective extraction. The methods employ solvents, temperature or chemical agents to isolate desired components such as oils, resins or asphaltenes, improving product purity or enabling reuse. This subclass falls under working-up of petroleum-derived heavy residues.
[C10C 3/10] Melting -- The C10C 3/10 subclass covers methods and apparatuses used to melt pitch, asphalt, bitumen or tar during their working-up processes. It includes heating techniques, temperature control and related steps that prepare these materials for further refining, mixing or application, without addressing the chemical modification of the substances themselves.
[C10C 3/12] Devices therefor -- C10C 3/12 covers equipment and apparatus employed to process or treat pitch, asphalt, bitumen and tar, such as mixers, agitators, pumps, heating units and filtration systems used in the working-up stage of petroleum-derived products.
[C10C 3/14] Solidifying, Disintegrating, e.g. granulating -- C10C 3/14 covers processes that solidify or break down pitch, asphalt, bitumen or tar, such as granulating, pelletizing, crushing or other disintegration techniques that facilitate handling, storage, transport or further processing of these materials.
[C10C 3/16] by direct contact with liquids -- C10C 3/16 covers processes for working-up pitch, asphalt or bitumen in which the material is treated by direct contact with liquids, such as solvent extraction, washing or liquid-phase refining, to remove impurities or modify its properties. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the working-up of pitch, asphalt and bitumen.
[C10C 3/18] Removing in solid form from reaction vessels, containers and the like, e.g. by cutting out, by pressing -- C10C 3/18 covers methods for removing solid pitch, asphalt, bitumen or similar materials from reaction vessels, containers or apparatus, such as cutting the solid out or pressing it to extract it. It falls under the chemistry section for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the working-up of pitch, asphalt, bitumen and tar. This subclass addresses the mechanical disengagement of solid residues after processing.

[C10F] DRYING OR WORKING-UP OF PEAT -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drying, dewatering or working up of peat, including drying using presses, band-presses, rolls, centrifuges etc., including drying or dewatering in the field.  Drying or dewatering of peat combined with a carbonisation step for producing turfcoal.  Working-up of peat concerns peat processing processers and apparatus therefor, e.g. involving disintegration, moulding, briquetting, solubilizing and oxidising peat, etc.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Baling or bundling peat -> B65B27/12 - Extracting wax from peat -> C10G - Obtaining fibres from peat -> D01B1/50
[C10F 5/00] Drying or de-watering peat -- C10F 5/00 covers methods and apparatus for removing moisture from peat, including drying, de-watering, and related processing steps to prepare peat for use as fuel or raw material. It is part of the chemistry subclass dealing with petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the drying or working-up of peat.
[C10F 5/02] in the field
[C10F 5/04] by using presses, handpresses, rolls, or centrifuges -- The C10F 5/04 entry covers methods for drying or de-watering peat by mechanical means such as presses, handpresses, rolls or centrifuges. It falls under the chemistry section, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries branch dealing with drying or working-up of peat.
[C10F 5/06] combined with a carbonisation step for producing turfcoal -- This subclass covers processes that dry peat while simultaneously carbonising it to produce turfcoal, a solid fuel derived from peat. It includes integrated drying-carbonisation operations and the associated equipment for converting peat into turfcoal.
[C10F 7/00] Working-up peat -- C10F 7/00 covers methods for working-up peat, including drying, size reduction and other treatment steps to prepare the material for use in petroleum, gas or coke processes. The entry addresses techniques for removing moisture and improving peat's physical properties before further processing.
[C10F 7/02] Disintegrating peat -- C10F 7/02 covers methods and apparatus for breaking down peat into smaller particles during its working-up, such as mechanical, chemical, or thermal disintegration steps that prepare the material for use as fuel, soil amendment, or other applications. It falls under Chemistry → Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries → Drying or Working-up of Peat → Working-up peat.
[C10F 7/04] by moulding -- C10F 7/04 belongs to the Cooperative Patent Classification for chemistry, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries and the drying or working-up of peat. It covers processes that shape peat into a desired form by moulding, such as forming pellets or briquettes for later use. This subclass focuses on the moulding step within peat processing.
[C10F 7/06] Briquetting -- C10F 7/06 classifies processes for converting peat into compact fuel units, specifically the briquetting step in peat working-up. It covers methods that shape, compress or bind dried peat into briquettes for easier handling, transport and combustion in energy applications.
[C10F 7/08] by extrusion combined with cutting -- A method for working-up peat in which the dried material is forced through an extruder and simultaneously cut into smaller pieces, improving size uniformity and facilitating further handling or processing. This technique combines mechanical extrusion with a cutting action to refine peat after drying.
[C10G] CRACKING HYDROCARBON OILS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oil-shale, oil-sand or non-melting solid carbonaceous materials (e.g. wood, coal), from oxides of carbon (e.g. Fischer-Tropsch processes), from oxygen-containing organic materials (e.g. fatty acids or fatty oils) or from gases (e.g. natural gas).  Distillation, dewatering or demulsification of hydrocarbon oils.  Catalytic or non-catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen.  Refining of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen.  Reforming naphtha.  Hydrotreatment processes involving refining, cracking or other treatment of hydrocarbon oils in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen generating compounds.  Production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from lower carbon number hydrocarbons, e.g. oligomerisation or polymerisation to make longer carbon chains. Multi-step processes for treating hydrocarbon oils in the presence or absence of hydrogen.  Working up of normally gaseous mixtures of undefined composition obtained from cracking processes.  Treatment of hydrocarbon oils or fatty oils for lubricating purposes, including thickening by voltolisation. Recovery of refining of mineral waxes, e.g. montan wax.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cracking mainly to hydrogen or synthesis gas -> C01B - Preparation of individual hydrocarbons or mixtures thereof of definite or specified constitution, including by cracking or pyrolysis of hydrocarbon gases -> C07C  **Glossary:** - Cracking: Cracking is the process whereby complex organic molecules such as heavy hydrocarbons are broken down into simpler molecules (e.g. light hydrocarbons) by the breaking of carbon-carbon bonds in the precursors. the rate of cracking and the end products are strongly dependent on the temperature and the presence of any catalysts. - Refining: Refining is the process of purification of a substance. The term is usually used of a natural resource that is almost in a usable form, but which is more useful in a purer form. For instance, most types of natural petroleum will burn straight from the ground, but they will burn poorly and quickly clog an engine with residues and byproducts. the Term "refining" is broad and may include more drastic transformations. the refining of liquids is often accomplished by distillation or fractionation. - Reforming (catalytic): Thermal or catalytic reforming is a chemical process used to convert naphtha boiling range feedstocks, typically having low octane ratings, into high-octane liquid products called reformates which are components of high-octane gasoline (also known as petrol). The process represents the total effect of numerous simultaneous reactions, such as dehydrogenation, isomerisation, cracking and polymerisation. - Destructive hydrogenation: Splitting of molecules of the raw material with addition of hydrogen to them, also called direct liquefaction, liquefaction of coal by reacting it with hydrogen at high temperature and pressure. - Voltolising: Subjecting oils to treatment with an electric discharge.
[C10G 1/00] Production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oil-shale, oil-sand, or non-melting solid carbonaceous or similar materials, e.g. wood, coal -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mechanical separation of oil from oil-shales, oil-sand or the like -> B03B
[C10G 1/002] in combination with oil conversion- or refining processes -- C10G 1/002 covers methods for producing liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oil-shale, oil-sand, or other solid carbonaceous feedstocks such as wood or coal, when the process is integrated with oil conversion or refining operations. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for cracking hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 1/004] Inhibiting of corrosion -- C10G 1/004 relates to techniques for preventing or reducing corrosion during the production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oil-shale, oil-sand, coal, wood or other solid carbonaceous materials. It falls under the chemistry section for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils, and covers corrosion-inhibiting additives, treatments and processes applied to the reactors and equipment used in such conversions.
[C10G 1/006] Combinations of processes provided in groups  C10G1/02  -  C10G1/08 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Liquefaction processes of feedstocks, being already partially liquefied in a pretreatment step.  Combinations of processes provided in groups C10G1/02 - C10G1/08 .
[C10G 1/008] Controlling or regulating of liquefaction processes -- This subclass covers methods and systems for monitoring, adjusting, or otherwise controlling the liquefaction of solid carbonaceous materials such as oil-shale, oil-sand, wood or coal to produce liquid hydrocarbon mixtures. It includes process-parameter regulation, feedback control loops, temperature and pressure control, and automation techniques.
[C10G 1/02] by distillation -- This CPC subclass covers the production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oil-shale, oil-sand, wood, coal or similar solid carbonaceous feedstocks by distillation. It falls under the cracking hydrocarbon oils group of the petroleum, gas or coke industries within the chemistry section.
[C10G 1/04] by extraction -- This class covers processes that produce liquid hydrocarbon mixtures by extracting them from solid carbonaceous feedstocks such as oil-shale, oil-sand, wood, coal or similar non-melting materials. It is situated in the cracking hydrocarbon oils subgroup of petroleum and gas industry technology.
[C10G 1/042] by the use of hydrogen-donor solvents -- A process for producing liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from solid carbonaceous feedstocks such as oil-shale, oil-sand, wood or coal that employs hydrogen-donor solvents to supply hydrogen during cracking. The solvent transfers hydrogen to reactive fragments, stabilising radicals and enhancing conversion efficiency while reducing coke formation. This method falls under the C10G 1/042 subclass of petroleum and gas industry cracking technologies.
[C10G 1/045] Separation of insoluble materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of liquid hydrocarbons mixtures from oil-shale, oil-sand or non-melting solid carbonaceous by separation of insoluble materials.  Separation process of bitumen from froth, which can be prepared by a process is classified in C10G1/047 .
[C10G 1/047] Hot water or cold water extraction processes -- C10G 1/047 covers processes that use hot or cold water to extract liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oil-shale, oil-sand, wood, coal or other solid carbonaceous feedstocks. The methods belong to the cracking of hydrocarbon oils within the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10G 1/06] by destructive hydrogenation -- **Glossary:** - destructive hydrogenation: reaction of splitting of molecules of the raw material with addition of hydrogen to them, e.g. direct liquefaction of coal by reacting coal with hydrogen at high temperature and pressure
[C10G 1/065] in the presence of a solvent -- This subclass covers processes for producing liquid hydrocarbons from solid carbonaceous feedstocks such as oil-shale, oil-sand, coal or wood where a solvent is employed to aid cracking. The solvent assists in improving conversion efficiency and product selectivity.
[C10G 1/08] with moving catalysts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oil-shale, oil-sand or similar material in the presence of a catalyst, the catalyst being moving or not.
[C10G 1/083] in the presence of a solvent -- This subclass covers cracking of hydrocarbon oils performed in the presence of a solvent, aiding the reaction when processing oil-shale, oil-sand or other solid carbonaceous feedstocks to produce liquid hydrocarbon mixtures.
[C10G 1/086] Characterised by the catalyst used -- C10G 1/086 covers processes for producing liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oil-shale, oil-sand, wood, coal or similar solid carbonaceous feedstocks where the defining feature is the specific catalyst employed. It falls under cracking hydrocarbon oils within petroleum and gas industries.
[C10G 1/10] from rubber or rubber waste -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of liquid hydrocarbons from rubber or polymers as such.
[C10G 11/00] Catalytic cracking, in the absence of hydrogen, of hydrocarbon oils -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cracking in direct contact with molten metals or salts -> C10G9/34
[C10G 11/02] characterised by the catalyst used -- The subclass C10G 11/02 covers catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils performed without hydrogen where the process is distinguished by the specific catalyst employed, such as zeolites, acidic oxides or metal-containing agents. It relates to petroleum refining methods that use these catalysts to break larger molecules into lighter fractions.
[C10G 11/04] Oxides -- The C10G 11/04 subclass groups patents involving the use of metal oxides as catalysts in catalytic cracking processes that operate without hydrogen to break down hydrocarbon oils. It covers oxides employed to promote conversion of heavy petroleum fractions into lighter products within the petroleum, gas or coke industry.
[C10G 11/05] Crystalline alumino-silicates, e.g. molecular sieves -- C10G 11/05 covers crystalline alumino-silicates, commonly known as molecular sieves such as zeolites, employed as solid acid catalysts in the catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen. These materials provide shape-selective pore structures that enhance conversion of heavy fractions into lighter fuels.
[C10G 11/06] Sulfides -- C10G 11/06 relates to the use of sulfide compounds as catalysts or catalyst promoters in the catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils carried out without hydrogen, covering their composition, preparation and function in the cracking process.
[C10G 11/08] Halides -- Halides are chemical compounds containing fluorine, chlorine, bromine or iodine used as catalysts or catalyst promoters in the catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen. This subclass covers the formulation, addition, or effect of such halide agents on cracking performance, product distribution and catalyst stability.
[C10G 11/10] with stationary catalyst bed -- C10G 11/10 covers catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils where the catalyst remains fixed in a stationary bed, as opposed to moving-bed or fluidised systems. The process is used in petroleum refining to break large oil molecules into lighter products without adding hydrogen, relying on the solid catalyst's activity and shape to promote the cracking reactions.
[C10G 11/12] with discontinuously preheated non-moving solid catalysts, e.g. blast and run -- C10G 11/12 refers to catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen using solid catalysts that stay stationary and are heated in batches rather than continuously, as in blast or run procedures. The preheated, non-moving catalyst is introduced into the reactor to promote cracking of the oil feed.
[C10G 11/14] with preheated moving solid catalysts -- C10G 11/14 covers catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen when the solid catalyst is preheated and continuously moved through the reactor. The process is used in petroleum refining to break large hydrocarbons into lighter fractions, improving yield and efficiency. It falls under the broader category of cracking technologies in the petroleum, gas or coke industry.
[C10G 11/16] according to the "moving bed" method -- C10G 11/16 covers catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen when the process is carried out in a moving-bed reactor. It pertains to petroleum-related technologies for breaking down long-chain hydrocarbons into shorter, more useful fractions using a catalyst that moves relative to the oil feed. This classification falls under chemistry, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries and the cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 11/18] according to the "fluidised-bed" technique -- C10G 11/18 is a CPC subclass that deals with catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils using the fluidised-bed method, performed without hydrogen. It falls under chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the cracking of hydrocarbon oils. The classification covers processes and apparatus for fluidised-bed catalytic cracking.
[C10G 11/182] Regeneration -- C10G 11/182 covers methods for regenerating the catalyst used in catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen. It includes steps to remove coke and restore activity, such as burning off deposits or oxidative treatments, enabling the catalyst to be reused in the cracking process.
[C10G 11/185] Energy recovery from regenerator effluent gases -- C10G 11/185 covers technologies for capturing and re-using the heat contained in gases emitted from the regenerator of a catalytic cracking unit that operates without hydrogen. It includes systems that convert this waste heat into useful energy, such as steam generation or power production, improving overall plant efficiency.
[C10G 11/187] Controlling or regulating -- C10G 11/187 covers technologies for controlling or regulating the catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils when no hydrogen is present. It includes process adjustments, monitoring systems, catalyst management, temperature, pressure or feed-rate controls, and any measures that influence the efficiency and selectivity of the cracking reaction.
[C10G 11/20] by direct contact with inert heated gases or vapours -- C10G 11/20 pertains to catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils performed without hydrogen, where the feed oil is exposed directly to heated inert gases or vapors. This subcategory falls under petroleum-related chemistry, specifically cracking processes that rely on heat-transfer media rather than a hydrogen atmosphere.
[C10G 11/22] produced by partial combustion of the material to be cracked -- C10G 11/22 covers catalytic cracking processes where the feedstock is partially burnt to generate the heat needed for cracking hydrocarbon oils. The method relies on in-situ combustion of a portion of the material rather than external hydrogen or heat sources, producing cracked products while using the combustion gases as part of the reaction environment.
[C10G 15/00] Cracking of hydrocarbon oils by electric means, electromagnetic or mechanical vibrations, by particle radiation or with gases superheated in electric arcs -- C10G 15/00 covers cracking of hydrocarbon oils using non-thermal energy sources such as electric discharge, electromagnetic or mechanical vibrations, particle radiation or gases that have been superheated in electric arcs, rather than conventional thermal or catalytic methods. It includes processes that break down oil molecules by applying these electric-based or radiative techniques to achieve desired product specifications.
[C10G 15/08] by electric means or by electromagnetic or mechanical vibrations -- C10G 15/08 pertains to the cracking of hydrocarbon oils by electric means, including the use of electromagnetic or mechanical vibrations, rather than conventional thermal or catalytic methods. It falls under the CPC group for petroleum and gas industry processes that employ electrical or vibratory energy to break down oil molecules.
[C10G 15/10] by particle radiation -- The subclass C10G 15/10 covers methods for cracking hydrocarbon oils by exposing them to energetic particle radiation, such as electrons, protons, neutrons or ion beams, to break molecular bonds and produce smaller hydrocarbon fragments. It falls under chemical processes for petroleum refining that use non-thermal, radiation-induced reactions to achieve oil cracking.
[C10G 15/12] with gases superheated in an electric arc, e.g. plasma -- This CPC subclass C10G 15/12 pertains to cracking hydrocarbon oils by passing them through gases that have been heated to plasma temperatures in an electric arc. The high-energy plasma generated by the arc breaks molecular bonds, offering an alternative to conventional thermal, catalytic or mechanical cracking methods.
[C10G 17/00] Refining in the absence of hydrogen -- C10G 17/00 covers refining processes that break down hydrocarbon oils without using hydrogen, such as thermal or steam cracking methods. It includes techniques for converting heavy petroleum fractions into lighter products in the absence of hydrogen addition, addressing both the reaction conditions and equipment used for these non-hydrogenated operations.
[C10G 17/02] with acids or acid-containing liquids, e.g. acid sludge -- C10G 17/02 covers petroleum refining methods that crack hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by using acids or acid-containing liquids such as acid sludge as the reacting medium or catalyst. The classification includes processes where the acidic medium promotes bond breakage and rearrangement of the feedstock.
[C10G 17/04] Liquid-liquid treatment forming two immiscible phases -- This CPC subclass covers liquid-liquid treatments used in petroleum refining when cracking hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen, creating two immiscible liquid phases to separate or purify components. It includes processes that add solvents, reagents or adjust conditions so that the feed forms distinct phases for removal of undesired compounds or recovery of valuable fractions.
[C10G 17/06] using acids derived from sulfur or acid sludge thereof -- C10G 17/06 relates to non-hydrogenic cracking of hydrocarbon oils in which sulfur-derived acids or acid sludge are used as the reacting medium or catalyst, often to promote desulphurisation, dehydration or other conversions during refining.
[C10G 17/07] using halogen acids or oxyacids of halogen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen by liquid-liquid treatment forming two immiscible phases using halogen acids or oxyacids of halogen, e.g. acid halide, HF (hydrogen flouride)  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Refining with acids generating halogen -> C10G27/02
[C10G 17/08] with acid-forming oxides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen by liquid-liquid treatment forming two immiscible phases using acid-forming oxides, e.g. oleum  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Refining with CO2 or SO2 as a selective solvent -> C10G21/06
[C10G 17/085] with oleum -- C10G 17/085 covers processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen in which oleum (fuming sulfuric acid) is used as a catalyst, reactant or medium. The subclass details how oleum aids in breaking down heavy petroleum fractions during non-hydrogenative refining operations.
[C10G 17/09] with acid salts -- The C10G 17/09 subgroup relates to petroleum-refining processes that crack hydrocarbon oils without the presence of hydrogen while employing acid salts as catalysts or additives. It covers methods in which acid-salt compounds facilitate the cracking reaction, falling under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, specifically cracking hydrocarbon oils and refining in the absence of hydrogen.
[C10G 17/095] with "solid acids", e.g. phosphoric acid deposited on a carrier -- The CPC subclass C10G 17/095 covers processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen that employ solid acid catalysts, such as phosphoric acid deposited on a carrier material. This approach is used in petroleum refining to break down heavy oils into lighter fractions by catalytic action of the solid acid.
[C10G 17/10] Recovery of used refining agents -- C10G 17/10 covers methods and apparatus for recovering spent or used refining agents after petroleum processing that occurs without hydrogen, such as solvent extraction or physical separation techniques, allowing the regenerated agents to be reused in subsequent refining cycles. This subclass falls under cracking hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 19/00] Refining hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen, by alkaline treatment -- C10G 19/00 covers processes for refining hydrocarbon oils without using hydrogen, specifically those that employ alkaline treatment to remove impurities or modify the oil's composition. It falls under the broader category of cracking hydrocarbon oils within the petroleum, gas or coke industries. This subclass addresses non-hydrogenative, chemical refining methods that use bases such as alkalis to improve product quality or yield.
[C10G 19/02] with aqueous alkaline solutions -- C10G 19/02 covers the refining of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by treating them with aqueous alkaline solutions. The method uses water-based alkalis to neutralise acids, remove contaminants and improve product quality during cracking or other downstream processes.
[C10G 19/04] containing solubilisers, e.g. solutisers -- C10G 19/04 covers processes for refining hydrocarbon oils without the use of hydrogen, where an alkaline treatment is employed and solubilizers such as solutisers are added to improve the dispersion of additives or contaminants. The classification relates to cracking of petroleum fractions under basic conditions and specifies the inclusion of substances that increase solubility during the treatment.
[C10G 19/06] with plumbites or plumbates -- The subclass concerns non-hydrogen cracking of hydrocarbon oils by alkaline treatment using lead-based compounds, specifically plumbites or plumbates, as the catalytic or reacting agents. This method is applied in petroleum refining to promote oil cracking without hydrogen.
[C10G 19/067] with molten alkaline material -- C10G 19/067 covers processes for refining hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by treating them with a molten alkaline material. The method uses a liquid alkali at high temperature to crack the oils, improving yields of lighter fractions.
[C10G 19/073] with solid alkaline material -- C10G 19/073 covers processes for refining hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by treating the oil with a solid alkaline material. The method belongs to the cracking of hydrocarbon oils category and is used in petroleum and petrochemical refining to remove acids and impurities.
[C10G 19/08] Recovery of used refining agents -- C10G 19/08 covers methods for reclaiming or recycling alkaline agents that have been used in non-hydrogen cracking of petroleum oils, focusing on the recovery, regeneration and reuse of spent refining chemicals within the petroleum, gas or coke industry.
[C10G 2/00] Production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures of undefined composition from oxides of carbon -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from CO or CO 2 , Fisher-Tropsch reaction.
[C10G 21/00] Refining of hydrocarbon oils, in the absence of hydrogen, by extraction with selective solvents -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Refining of hydrocarbon oils, in the absence of hydrogen, with acids, acid-forming compounds or acid-containing liquids -> C10G17/00 - Refining hydrocarbon oils, in the absence of hydrogen, by alkaline treatment -> C10G19/00
[C10G 21/003] Solvent de-asphalting -- Solvent de-asphalting, classified under CPC C10G 21/003, is a refining method for hydrocarbon oils that operates without hydrogen. The process uses selective solvents to extract and separate the heavy asphaltene fraction from the oil, improving feed quality for further cracking or other downstream operations. This technique falls within the broader field of petroleum refining, specifically under cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 21/006] of waste oils, e.g. PCB's containing oils -- C10G 21/006 covers solvent-based refining methods for waste hydrocarbon oils, including those contaminated with polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs). The process operates without hydrogen, extracting undesirable components by contacting the oil with selective solvents to separate and recover usable fractions.
[C10G 21/02] with two or more solvents, which are introduced or withdrawn separately -- C10G 21/02 covers refining of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by solvent extraction using two or more selective solvents that are introduced or withdrawn separately, a subcategory of cracking hydrocarbon oils in petroleum and gas refining.
[C10G 21/04] by introducing simultaneously at least two immiscible solvents counter-current to each other -- C10G 21/04 covers a refining method for hydrocarbon oils that excludes hydrogen and uses two immiscible solvents introduced at the same time, flowing counter-current to each other. The simultaneous solvent streams selectively extract desired fractions, improving separation efficiency in petroleum cracking processes.
[C10G 21/06] characterised by the solvent used -- C10G 21/06 concerns the refining of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen, performed by extracting the oil with a selective solvent. The subclass is defined by the particular solvent used in the extraction process.
[C10G 21/08] Inorganic compounds only -- The CPC subclass C10G 21/08 covers refining methods that separate hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by using selective solvent extraction where the solvent system contains only inorganic compounds. It pertains to cracking-related processes in the petroleum, gas or coke industries and specifies an entirely inorganic extraction medium.
[C10G 21/10] Sulfur dioxide -- In the CPC, code C10G 21/10 belongs to the subclass dealing with refining hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by selective-solvent extraction, and it specifically covers the use of sulfur dioxide as the solvent. The entry addresses processes in which sulfur dioxide is employed to extract or separate desired components from petroleum fractions during cracking or other refining operations.
[C10G 21/12] Organic compounds only -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining processes using a mixture of compounds from groups C10G21/14 - C10G21/26 .
[C10G 21/14] Hydrocarbons -- C10G 21/14 is a CPC subclass for the refining of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen, using selective solvent extraction to crack petroleum fractions. It covers solvent-based processes that separate and upgrade hydrocarbons in the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 21/16] Oxygen-containing compounds -- The CPC subclass C10G 21/16 covers solvent-based refining steps that remove oxygen-containing compounds from cracked hydrocarbon oils without the use of hydrogen. It includes methods and selective solvents for extracting acids, phenols, aldehydes, ketones and other oxygenated species from the oil feed. Patents describing such extraction processes are classified under this entry.
[C10G 21/18] Halogen-containing compounds -- C10G 21/18 covers the use of halogen-containing compounds as solvents, co-solvents or additives in non-hydrogenated solvent-extraction processes for cracking petroleum oils. It pertains to the selection and application of such halogenated chemicals to separate or modify hydrocarbon fractions during refining.
[C10G 21/20] Nitrogen-containing compounds -- C10G 21/20 covers processes for refining hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen that use selective solvent extraction to remove nitrogen-containing compounds. It falls under the petroleum and gas industry branch of chemistry, specifically the cracking of hydrocarbon oils and solvent-based purification methods.
[C10G 21/22] Compounds containing sulfur, selenium, or tellurium -- C10G 21/22 covers the use of compounds that contain sulfur, selenium or tellurium in the solvent-based, non-hydrogenic cracking of hydrocarbon oils. It falls under the chemistry branch for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the sub-category of cracking hydrocarbon oils by selective solvent extraction.
[C10G 21/24] Phosphorus-containing compounds -- C10G 21/24 covers the use of phosphorus-containing compounds in the solvent-based refining of hydrocarbon oils where no hydrogen is added. The classification applies to processes that extract or upgrade oil fractions by selective solvents, improving properties such as stability or viscosity through phosphorus additives. This falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 21/26] Silicon-containing compounds -- C10G 21/26 covers silicon-containing compounds employed in the solvent-based, hydrogen-free refining of hydrocarbon oils, specifically in processes that crack and extract oils using selective solvents. These agents facilitate the separation of desired fractions in petroleum and coke industry operations.
[C10G 21/27] Organic compounds not provided for in a single one of groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining processes using as a solvent a compound containing two or more elements from the group (oxygen, halogen, nitrogen, sulfur, selenium, tellurium, phosphor and silicon);  organic compounds not provided for in a single one of groups C10G21/14 - C10G21/26 .
[C10G 21/28] Recovery of used solvent -- C10G 21/28 covers processes for recovering and re-using the selective solvents employed in solvent-extraction refining of hydrocarbon oils, where cracking is performed without hydrogen. It addresses methods such as distillation, stripping, or adsorption that separate spent solvent from oil fractions so the solvent can be regenerated and recycled in the petroleum, gas or coke industry.
[C10G 21/30] Controlling or regulating -- The CPC subclass C10G 21/30 falls under chemistry → petroleum, gas or coke industries → cracking hydrocarbon oils → refining of hydrocarbon oils, in the absence of hydrogen, by extraction with selective solvents. It relates to methods, devices or parameters used to control or regulate the solvent-extraction refining process, such as temperature, solvent composition, flow rates or separation stages.
[C10G 2/30] from carbon monoxide with hydrogen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures of undefined composition from gas not from a liquid like in reforming reaction
[C10G 2300/00] Aspects relating to hydrocarbon processing covered by groups -- C10G 2300/00 covers the various aspects of hydrocarbon processing that are addressed by the groups defined in the preceding sections of the Cooperative Patent Classification, relating to the cracking of petroleum, gas or coke-derived oils. It groups together methods, apparatus and conditions that fall under broader cracking operations but are not assigned to a more specific subgroup. This entry helps streamline the classification of patents dealing with general hydrocarbon-cracking technologies.
[C10G 2300/10] Feedstock materials -- C10G 2300/10 covers the raw materials used as feedstock in hydrocarbon cracking processes. It includes various petroleum fractions, natural gas liquids and other feedstocks that are introduced into cracking units to produce lighter olefins and aromatics.
[C10G 2300/1003] Waste materials -- The CPC entry C10G 2300/1003 covers waste materials generated during the cracking of hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum and gas industry. It includes methods for handling, treating, recycling or disposing of residues, by-products and sludge that arise from catalytic or thermal cracking processes. This grouping addresses environmental and operational aspects of waste management within hydrocarbon processing.
[C10G 2300/1007] Used oils -- C10G 2300/1007 pertains to used oils, i.e., oils that have previously been employed in industrial or petroleum-processing equipment and are subsequently reclaimed, reprocessed, or disposed of. The classification covers methods for treating, regenerating, or managing these spent oils within the context of hydrocarbon cracking and related petroleum operations.
[C10G 2300/1011] Biomass -- C10G 2300/1011 - Biomass refers to the use of renewable organic material as a feedstock in hydrocarbon cracking processes. It includes methods for converting biomass-derived oils, solids or gases into fuels, chemicals or intermediates within petroleum-related refining operations.
[C10G 2300/1014] of vegetal origin -- C10G 2300/1014 covers cracking processes that use hydrocarbon feedstocks derived from plant material. It relates to converting vegetal oils or bio-derived liquids into lighter hydrocarbons for fuels or chemicals. The classification applies to methods, catalysts, and conditions specific to plant-origin sources.
[C10G 2300/1018] of animal origin -- C10G 2300/1018 designates cracking processes that use feedstocks of animal origin, such as fats, oils or tallow, to produce hydrocarbon products. It covers any method, catalytic or thermal, where the primary raw material is derived from animals, within the broader context of petroleum and hydrocarbon processing.
[C10G 2300/1022] Fischer-Tropsch products -- C10G 2300/1022 covers Fischer-Tropsch products, which are synthetic hydrocarbons obtained by catalytically converting synthesis gas (a mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen) into liquid fuels, waxes and other chemical feedstocks. The classification applies to inventions dealing with the composition, preparation, and use of these products in the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10G 2300/1025] Natural gas -- C10G 2300/1025 relates to the use, handling, or processing of natural gas within petroleum and gas refining, particularly in hydrocarbon cracking operations. It covers methods, apparatuses and conditions where natural gas serves as feedstock, energy source or by-product in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 2300/1029] Gas hydrates -- C10G 2300/1029 covers gas hydrates, solid crystalline structures formed when water encases light hydrocarbon gases under high pressure and low temperature. In the context of petroleum processing, it addresses formation, prevention, and handling of these hydrates during hydrocarbon cracking and transport, which can cause blockages in pipelines and separators.
[C10G 2300/1033] Oil well production fluids -- It covers fluids that are produced from an oil-well during extraction, including crude oil, formation water, gas condensates and associated gases, and the methods for their handling, treatment and disposal. The classification addresses the chemical composition, physical properties and processing operations relevant to petroleum production.
[C10G 2300/1037] Hydrocarbon fractions -- The CPC entry C10G 2300/1037 "Hydrocarbon fractions" lies within chemistry → petroleum, gas or coke industries → cracking hydrocarbon oils and addresses the identification, separation, and further processing of the various hydrocarbon streams produced during cracking operations, including their composition, physical properties, and methods for refining or upgrading these fractions.
[C10G 2300/104] Light gasoline having a boiling range of about 20 - 100 °C -- This CPC subclass C10G 2300/104 covers light gasoline that boils roughly between 20 and 100 °C, produced by cracking hydrocarbon oils. It is used to classify inventions concerning the production, composition or use of such low-boiling gasoline fractions within the chemistry, petroleum and gas industries hierarchy.
[C10G 2300/1044] Heavy gasoline or naphtha having a boiling range of about 100 - 180 °C -- C10G 2300/1044 covers petroleum fractions classified as heavy gasoline or naphtha with a boiling range roughly between 100 and 180 °C. These liquids are intermediate products of cracking processes and are typically blended or further processed into fuels and petrochemical feedstocks. The class is used to identify patents dealing with production, treatment, or utilization of this specific boiling range.
[C10G 2300/1048] Middle distillates -- The C10G 2300/1048 entry covers middle-distillate fractions produced in petroleum cracking, such as diesel, kerosene and jet fuel, and the related processing steps, catalysts and conditions used to obtain and treat these fuels in refinery operations.
[C10G 2300/1051] Kerosene having a boiling range of about 180 - 230 °C -- This CPC entry (C10G 2300/1051) covers kerosene that boils between roughly 180 and 230 °C, a mid-range petroleum fraction produced in cracking processes. It pertains to the characterization, processing or use of this specific boiling-range fuel within the petroleum, gas or coke industry.
[C10G 2300/1055] Diesel having a boiling range of about 230 - 330 °C -- The CPC subgroup C10G 2300/1055 covers diesel fuel fractions whose boiling range is roughly 230 to 330 °C, typically produced in cracking operations and used as a middle-distillate product in petroleum refining.
[C10G 2300/1059] Gasoil having a boiling range of about 330 - 427 °C -- The code C10G 2300/1059 classifies gasoil whose boiling range lies between roughly 330 °C and 427 °C, a product of petroleum refining and hydrocarbon cracking. It is used to identify fuel oils in that temperature window for patents concerning processing, composition, or applications within the petrochemical industry.
[C10G 2300/1062] Lubricating oils -- Lubricating oils are fluid products, typically derived from refined petroleum or synthetic feedstocks, formulated to reduce friction, wear and heat in moving parts of machinery and engines. They comprise base oils blended with performance-enhancing additives and are classified under the cracking of hydrocarbon oils for petroleum-based processing.
[C10G 2300/1066] Special oils -- This CPC subclass covers special oils used in petroleum refining, such as synthetic or highly refined lubricants, additives, and other specialty products employed in cracking processes. It addresses the properties, production, and applications of these oils in hydrocarbon-processing.
[C10G 2300/107] Atmospheric residues having a boiling point of at least about 538 °C -- The code C10G 2300/107 refers to atmospheric residues with a boiling point of at least about 538 °C, i.e. the heavy, high-temperature fractions left after atmospheric distillation of crude oil. It covers processing methods for these residues in petroleum cracking and refining.
[C10G 2300/1074] Vacuum distillates -- The CPC class C10G 2300/1074 concerns vacuum distillates obtained from the cracking of hydrocarbon oils in petroleum processing. It covers the separation, composition, handling and related processing aspects of the low-pressure, high-temperature distillation fractions that follow cracking operations.
[C10G 2300/1077] Vacuum residues -- C10G 2300/1077 designates vacuum residues, the heavy fractions obtained after vacuum distillation of crude oil and related streams. It pertains to the processing, treatment or upgrading of these residues within petroleum refining, especially in cracking operations. The code is used to classify patents concerning technologies for handling, converting or improving vacuum residue quality.
[C10G 2300/1081] Alkanes -- C10G 2300/1081 - Alkanes is a CPC entry covering aliphatic hydrocarbons in the petroleum, gas or coke industries, focusing on their role and treatment in cracking hydrocarbon oils. It includes methods, catalysts and operating conditions for processing alkanes during petroleum refining.
[C10G 2300/1085] Solid paraffins -- C10G 2300/1085 - Solid paraffins covers waxy, high-molecular-weight paraffinic hydrocarbons that are solid at room temperature, used as lubricants, coatings, and additives, and obtained from the cracking or refining of petroleum streams.
[C10G 2300/1088] Olefins -- The CPC entry C10G 2300/1088 classifies technologies and processes that produce olefins-unsaturated hydrocarbons such as ethylene and propylene-through the cracking of hydrocarbon oils. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, and cracking hydrocarbon oils, and covers methods, reactors, and related apparatus used to generate these valuable building-block molecules.
[C10G 2300/1092] C2-C4 olefins -- C10G 2300/1092 relates to the processing of C2-C4 olefins-ethylene, propylene and butenes-generated in the catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils. The entry covers methods, conditions, catalysts and downstream handling for producing or separating these light olefins within petroleum, gas or coke industry operations.
[C10G 2300/1096] Aromatics or polyaromatics -- C10G 2300/1096 classifies aromatic and polyaromatic compounds that arise in or are used for hydrocarbon cracking operations. It includes their formation, separation, conversion and handling within petroleum, gas or coke processing. The code is part of the chemistry group covering cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 2300/20] Characteristics of the feedstock or the products -- C10G 2300/20 relates to the properties and composition of the feedstock or the resulting products in hydrocarbon cracking processes, such as molecular weight, sulfur content, or boiling range. It is used to classify patents that discuss how these characteristics affect the cracking operation.
[C10G 2300/201] Impurities -- The C10G 2300/201 class covers impurities that arise during the catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils in petroleum, gas and coke processing. It includes methods for identifying, removing, or mitigating contaminants such as metals, sulfur compounds, nitrogen species or coke deposits that affect catalyst performance and product quality. This subclass falls under the broader chemistry category for cracking processes.
[C10G 2300/202] Heteroatoms content, i.e. S, N, O, P -- C10G 2300/202 relates to the analysis, control or reduction of heteroatom content such as sulfur, nitrogen, oxygen and phosphorus in petroleum fractions during the cracking of hydrocarbon oils. It includes methods for measuring these elements in feedstock or products, as well as techniques to limit their presence to meet environmental or product quality standards.
[C10G 2300/203] Naphthenic acids, TAN -- C10G 2300/203 covers the characterization and measurement of naphthenic acids and the total acid number in petroleum streams, especially during cracking processes. It includes methods for determining acid content, its impact on corrosion and catalyst performance, and techniques for reducing or managing acidity in refinery feedstocks.
[C10G 2300/205] Metal content -- The C10G 2300/205 entry concerns the metal content of hydrocarbon oils that have undergone cracking. It includes methods for determining, controlling or reducing metallic impurities such as iron, nickel or vanadium in the cracked products, and any related procedures that affect catalyst performance or product quality.
[C10G 2300/206] Asphaltenes -- C10G 2300/206 covers asphaltenes, the high-molecular-weight polar fractions that precipitate from heavy crude oils during processing. These solid or colloidal substances, rich in carbon, heteroatoms and metals, affect viscosity, fouling and catalyst performance in petroleum cracking operations. The entry addresses their characteristics and handling in hydrocarbon refining.
[C10G 2300/207] Acid gases, e.g. H -- C10G 2300/207 covers methods and apparatus for dealing with acid gases such as hydrogen sulfide or carbon dioxide that accompany or are generated during the cracking of hydrocarbon oils. It includes removal, absorption, conversion or neutralisation steps integrated into petroleum refining processes. The entry falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, specifically the cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 2300/208] Sediments, e.g. bottom sediment and water or BSW -- C10G 2300/208 addresses the handling, treatment and disposal of sediments, including bottom sediment and water (BSW), generated during hydrocarbon cracking operations. It covers methods for separating, cleaning or managing these waste streams to improve process efficiency and meet environmental regulations.
[C10G 2300/30] Physical properties of feedstocks or products -- C10G 2300/30 addresses the physical characteristics of feedstocks and resulting products in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils, such as viscosity, density, boiling range, and thermal stability. It is used to classify inventions that measure or modify these properties for improved petroleum processing.
[C10G 2300/301] Boiling range -- C10G 2300/301 deals with the boiling range of hydrocarbon streams produced in cracking processes, covering methods for determining, specifying or controlling the temperature interval over which fractions evaporate. It classifies patents that address analytical techniques, specifications or process adjustments based on boiling-point distributions in petroleum refining.
[C10G 2300/302] Viscosity -- C10G 2300/302 covers the measurement, control and modification of viscosity in hydrocarbon oils that have been cracked. It pertains to processes and equipment used to adjust fluid flow properties during petroleum refining, such as blending, heating, or additive introduction, to achieve desired performance in downstream applications.
[C10G 2300/304] Pour point, cloud point, cold flow properties -- The CPC group C10G 2300/304 relates to the low-temperature flow behaviour of hydrocarbon oils, covering the determination and definition of pour point, cloud point and other cold-flow properties. These characteristics are important for assessing fuel performance and handling in petroleum, gas or coke processing, especially after cracking operations.
[C10G 2300/305] Octane number, e.g. motor octane number [MON], research octane number [RON] -- The C10G 2300/305 class groups inventions and methods dealing with the determination, specification or improvement of octane numbers for hydrocarbon fuels, including motor octane number (MON) and research octane number (RON). It applies to processes that assess or enhance fuel anti-knocking performance in the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 2300/307] Cetane number, cetane index -- C10G 2300/307 covers the determination, measurement and specification of cetane number and cetane index, the key ignition quality parameters for diesel and other compression-ignition fuels. The entry includes test methods, calculation procedures and related apparatus used to assess fuel performance in hydrocarbon processing and refining.
[C10G 2300/308] Gravity, density, e.g. API -- C10G 2300/308 pertains to the determination and control of gravity and density characteristics of hydrocarbon streams during cracking processes, including measurements such as API gravity. It covers apparatus, methods and analytical techniques for assessing these physical properties in petroleum refining.
[C10G 2300/40] Characteristics of the process deviating from typical ways of processing -- C10G 2300/40 covers hydrocarbon-oil cracking processes that depart from standard operating methods, such as atypical temperature, pressure, catalyst use, reactor design or feed composition. It includes any unconventional step or condition that alters the usual cracking pathway, and captures the distinctive technical features of these non-typical processes.
[C10G 2300/4006] Temperature -- C10G 2300/4006 classifies the temperature aspects of hydrocarbon oil cracking, covering the selection, control and effects of temperature parameters during petroleum, gas or coke processing. It applies to any specification of temperature ranges, gradients or thermal conditions that influence the cracking reaction.
[C10G 2300/4012] Pressure -- C10G 2300/4012 designates the pressure conditions applied during the cracking of hydrocarbon oils, covering the range of pressures used in thermal or catalytic cracking steps. It addresses how pressure influences conversion efficiency, product distribution and equipment design in petroleum refining processes.
[C10G 2300/4018] Spatial velocity, e.g. LHSV, WHSV -- This classification belongs to the CPC under chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, cracking hydrocarbon oils, covering aspects relating to hydrocarbon processing. Code C10G 2300/4018 refers to spatial velocity parameters such as liquid hourly space velocity (LHSV) and weight hourly space velocity (WHSV) used to characterize flow rates in catalytic cracking reactors.
[C10G 2300/4025] Yield -- C10G 2300/4025 concerns the yield of products obtained from cracking hydrocarbon oils, covering the determination, reporting and optimisation of conversion efficiencies in petroleum refining processes.
[C10G 2300/4031] Start up or shut down operations -- This classification covers procedures and considerations for initiating or terminating hydrocarbon-oil cracking processes in petroleum refining, including equipment start-up, shutdown, safety measures and operational control.
[C10G 2300/4037] In-situ processes -- The entry covers methods that crack hydrocarbon oils directly within the reservoir or formation, using heat, steam, catalysts or other agents introduced in-situ rather than extracting the oil first. It includes underground retorting, in-well catalytic cracking and similar processes that transform crude hydrocarbons where they are found.
[C10G 2300/4043] Limiting CO -- This CPC subclass deals with methods and apparatus for limiting carbon-monoxide formation or concentration in hydrocarbon-cracking processes. It includes process conditions, catalysts and equipment designed to control CO levels, improving product quality and meeting environmental standards in petroleum refining.
[C10G 2300/405] Limiting CO, NOx or SOx emissions -- This classification covers processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils that incorporate measures to limit emissions of carbon monoxide, nitrogen oxides, or sulfur oxides. It pertains to petroleum refining technologies that integrate emission-control strategies during the cracking stage.
[C10G 2300/4056] Retrofitting operations -- This code covers modifications or upgrades to existing hydrocarbon cracking units, such as installing new equipment or adapting processes to improve performance. It pertains to retrofit activities in petroleum, gas or coke industry cracking operations.
[C10G 2300/4062] Geographical aspects, e.g. different process units form a combination process at different geographical locations -- The code C10G 2300/4062 refers to aspects of hydrocarbon processing where separate unit operations are situated at different geographical sites and combined into a single process. It addresses design, integration and control of distributed cracking or other conversion units across locations.
[C10G 2300/4068] Moveable devices or units, e.g. on trucks, barges -- The code C10G 2300/4068 designates mobile cracking devices or units that can be mounted on trucks, barges or similar transport platforms for processing hydrocarbon oils. These movable installations enable petroleum cracking operations to be performed at various locations, providing flexibility in feedstock handling and on-site production.
[C10G 2300/4075] Limiting deterioration of equipment -- The CPC entry C10G 2300/4075 concerns methods and measures for preventing or reducing the degradation of plant and apparatus used in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils. It includes technical solutions aimed at limiting corrosion, fouling, wear or other forms of equipment deterioration during petroleum, gas or coke processing.
[C10G 2300/4081] Recycling aspects -- C10G 2300/4081 pertains to recycling aspects within the processing of cracked hydrocarbon oils. It covers methods and systems for recovering, re-using, or treating waste streams, residues, or spent catalysts that arise from cracking operations, including feedstock regeneration, material reclamation and environmentally-focused disposal techniques.
[C10G 2300/4087] Catalytic distillation -- Catalytic distillation merges catalytic cracking and distillation in a single column, using a catalyst-filled packing to simultaneously react and separate hydrocarbon oils. This integrated approach improves conversion efficiency, lowers energy use and reduces equipment count in petroleum refining processes focused on cracking feedstocks.
[C10G 2300/4093] Catalyst stripping -- Catalyst stripping refers to the process of removing spent or deactivated catalyst particles from hydrocarbon cracking reactors, typically by circulating a stripping gas or liquid to dislodge and recover the catalyst for regeneration or disposal. It is employed in fluid catalytic cracking units to maintain catalyst activity, prevent fouling, and improve oil yield.
[C10G 2300/42] Hydrogen of special source or of special composition -- The code C10G 2300/42 refers to the use of hydrogen that originates from a specific source or has a distinct composition in hydrocarbon cracking processes. It covers methods where such specially sourced or formulated hydrogen is employed to influence reaction conditions, improve yields, or control product distribution in petroleum refining.
[C10G 2300/44] Solvents -- C10G 2300/44 designates the use of solvents in hydrocarbon processing, particularly in cracking operations. It includes substances that serve as diluents, extraction agents or reaction media to improve conversion, separation or product quality in petroleum, gas or coke refining. The classification groups all related solvent applications under this code.
[C10G 2300/70] Catalyst aspects -- The CPC code C10G 2300/70 relates to catalysts used in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils within the petroleum and gas industry. It covers catalyst composition, preparation and performance aspects for hydrocarbon cracking processes.
[C10G 2300/701] Use of spent catalysts -- C10G 2300/701 relates to the handling of spent catalysts after they have been employed in hydrocarbon cracking operations. It covers methods for recycling, regenerating, or otherwise managing used catalysts in petroleum refining and petrochemical processes.
[C10G 2300/703] Activation -- The CPC entry C10G 2300/703 covers methods and agents that activate catalysts or reaction zones during the cracking of hydrocarbon oils, enhancing the conversion of heavy feedstocks into lighter products such as gasoline or olefins. It includes chemical, thermal, or mechanical techniques that increase catalyst activity or regeneration efficiency in petroleum refining processes.
[C10G 2300/705] Passivation -- C10G 2300/705 Passivation refers to processes that treat cracked hydrocarbon oils to reduce their chemical reactivity, improve stability and inhibit corrosion, polymerisation or other undesirable reactions during handling and storage. The methods typically involve adding chemicals or applying physical treatments that form a protective layer on oil surfaces, thereby extending product life and safety in petroleum refining operations.
[C10G 2300/706] Catalytic metal recovery -- C10G 2300/706 addresses methods and apparatus for recovering catalytic metals from hydrocarbon cracking catalysts, such as those used in fluid-catalytic cracking, including leaching, solvent extraction, and chemical reconditioning to restore activity and minimise waste.
[C10G 2300/708] Coking aspect, coke content and composition of deposits -- The entry covers aspects of coking that affect the amount and type of coke formed during hydrocarbon cracking processes, including the measurement and characterization of coke deposits on equipment and catalysts. It addresses how coke content and composition influence operational performance, product quality, and maintenance requirements in petroleum, gas and coke refining operations.
[C10G 2300/80] Additives -- The C10G 2300/80 entry covers additives employed in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils, including catalysts, inhibitors, promoters and other substances that improve or modify the efficiency, selectivity or stability of the cracking process. It falls under chemistry, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries, and pertains to aspects of hydrocarbon processing.
[C10G 2300/802] Diluents -- The CPC class C10G 2300/802 covers diluents used in hydrocarbon cracking processes. Diluents are added to feedstocks to lower viscosity, improve heat transfer and control temperature, thereby enhancing conversion efficiency and reducing coke formation during petroleum, gas or coke industry operations. This subclass falls under Chemistry → Petroleum, gas or coke industries → Cracking hydrocarbon oils → Aspects relating to hydrocarbon processing covered by groups.
[C10G 2300/805] Water -- The code covers the use, handling, and management of water in processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils, such as its supply to reactors, removal from product streams, recycling, and interaction with catalysts during petroleum refining.
[C10G 2300/807] Steam -- The C10G 2300/807 entry classifies the use of steam in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils, a process employed in petroleum, gas and coke refining. It pertains to hydrocarbon processing methods that involve steam as a reaction medium or heating source.
[C10G 2/31] thermal, non catalytic conversion -- C10G 2/31 covers processes that thermally convert oxides of carbon into liquid hydrocarbon mixtures without using a catalyst. It relates to non catalytic cracking methods for producing oils of undefined composition in the petroleum, gas or coke industries. The class sits under chemistry, then petroleum, gas or coke industries, and cracking hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 2/32] with the use of catalysts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes using Fischer-Tropsch reactions as such (no details), e.g. in combination with other process steps.
[C10G 2/33] characterised by the catalyst used -- C10G 2/33 covers processes for producing liquid hydrocarbon blends of undefined composition from carbon oxides where the distinguishing feature is the type of catalyst employed. It falls under cracking of hydrocarbon oils within the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10G 2/331] containing group VIII-metals -- This subclass covers methods for producing liquid hydrocarbon mixtures of indeterminate composition from carbon oxides, where the reaction medium includes a catalyst that contains at least one group VIII metal such as iron, nickel or cobalt. The presence of these metals improves activity or selectivity in the cracking or synthesis step.
[C10G 2/332] of the iron-group -- **Glossary:** - Iron group: Fe, Co and Ni
[C10G 2/333] of the platinum-group -- **Glossary:** - Platinum-group: Ru, Rh, Pd, Os, Ir, Pt
[C10G 2/334] containing molecular sieve catalysts -- The C10G 2/334 subclass deals with the production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures of undefined composition from carbon oxides in cracking processes that employ molecular-sieve catalysts. It falls under the chemistry branch for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically within cracking hydrocarbon oils. This classification covers methods where such zeolitic or other porous crystalline catalysts facilitate the synthesis of liquid fuels or intermediates from CO or CO₂ feedstocks.
[C10G 2/34] Apparatus, reactors -- C10G 2/34 pertains to the apparatus and reactors employed to produce liquid hydrocarbon mixtures of undefined composition from carbon oxides, a process classified under cracking of hydrocarbon oils within the petroleum, gas and coke industries.
[C10G 2/341] with stationary catalyst bed -- **Glossary:** - Stationary catalyst bed: Fixed bed reactor, trickle bed reactor
[C10G 2/342] with moving solid catalysts -- **Glossary:** - Moving solid catalysts: an ebullated bed reactor, a slurry bed reactor, or a loop reactor
[C10G 2/343] according to the "moving-bed" method -- **Glossary:** - Moving bed method: process where there is a netto movement of the catalyst, i.e. a laminar flow, the whole catalyst moves up or down in one direction. (all the catalyst moves up or down, moves in one direction
[C10G 2/344] according to the "fluidised-bed" technique
[C10G 2/35] with the use of another activation, e.g. radiation, vibration, electrical or electromagnetic means -- C10G 2/35 relates to processes that produce liquid hydrocarbon mixtures of undefined composition from carbon oxides by cracking hydrocarbon oils, where the cracking reaction is assisted with an additional activation such as radiation, vibration, electrical or electromagnetic energy.
[C10G 2/40] from carbon monoxide with water vapor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures of undefined composition from carbon monoxide with water vapour
[C10G 2400/00] Products obtained by processes covered by groups -- C10G 2400/00 covers the substances and mixtures that result from any of the cracking processes defined in the preceding groups of the C10G subclass, which deals with cracking hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum, gas or coke industries. It is used to classify the end-products-such as gasoline-range hydrocarbons, light olefins, aromatics and other refined fractions-produced by those specific cracking methods.
[C10G 2400/02] Gasoline -- C10G 2400/02 designates gasoline produced as a product of hydrocarbon oil cracking processes. It falls under the chemistry sector, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries and the sub-category of cracking hydrocarbon oils. The code is used to classify patents relating to gasoline composition, production methods, and related applications.
[C10G 2400/04] Diesel oil -- C10G 2400/04 designates diesel oil obtained from the catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils. It falls under the chemistry section for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the subgroup for cracking hydrocarbon oils and the products derived from those processes. This code is used in the Cooperative Patent Classification system shared by the EPO and USPTO.
[C10G 2400/06] Gasoil -- C10G 2400/06 (Gasoil) covers gasoil-a middle-distillate diesel fuel-obtained from cracking processes applied to hydrocarbon oils. The classification includes products generated by catalytic or thermal cracking methods within the petroleum, gas or coke industry, focusing on the specific composition and properties of the resulting gasoil fraction.
[C10G 2400/08] Jet fuel -- Jet fuel is a petroleum-derived aviation turbine fuel produced by cracking hydrocarbon oils, meeting specifications for combustion performance, freezing point, and energy content in aircraft engines.
