It encompasses products such as Jet A, Jet A-1, and JP-8 obtained through the processes classified under CPC group C10G 2400/08.
[C10G 2400/10] Lubricating oil -- C10G 2400/10 covers lubricating oil that is produced through cracking of hydrocarbon oils. It includes oils intended for reducing friction in engines and machinery, obtained by processes such as catalytic or thermal cracking of petroleum fractions. The class is nested under chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, and cracking hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 2400/12] Electrical isolation oil -- C10G 2400/12 classifies electrical isolation oils, insulating liquids derived from cracked hydrocarbon oils, used in transformers, capacitors and other electrical equipment to provide dielectric strength and cooling. These oils are obtained by the processes defined in the parent groups of the cracking hydrocarbon oils subclass.
[C10G 2400/14] White oil, eating oil -- The CPC entry C10G 2400/14 classifies white oil and edible oil obtained from cracking hydrocarbon oils, i.e., highly refined, colourless, odourless mineral oils used as food-grade lubricants, carriers or processing aids in the food, cosmetics and pharmaceutical sectors. It falls under Chemistry → Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries → Cracking Hydrocarbon Oils.
[C10G 2400/16] Residues -- Residues are the solid, liquid or semi-solid by-products that remain after cracking hydrocarbon oils in petroleum, gas or coke refining processes, such as coke, heavy oil fractions, tar and other unconverted material.
[C10G 2400/18] Solvents -- C10G 2400/18 designates solvents that are produced as downstream products from the cracking of hydrocarbon oils in petroleum refining. It includes aromatic, aliphatic and mixed solvents obtained by catalytic, thermal or hydrocracking processes and used for extraction, cleaning or diluting purposes.
[C10G 2400/20] C2-C4 olefins -- The C10G 2400/20 subclass covers the production and processing of C2-C4 olefins such as ethylene, propylene and butenes obtained by catalytic or thermal cracking of petroleum-derived oils. It includes methods for separating, purifying and utilizing these low-molecular-weight olefins.
[C10G 2400/22] Higher olefins -- This CPC subgroup covers processes and products related to higher olefins, unsaturated hydrocarbons with six or more carbon atoms, that are obtained by cracking hydrocarbon oils in petroleum, gas or coke industries, including their separation, purification and downstream uses.
[C10G 2400/24] Acetylene and homologues -- C10G 2400/24 designates acetylene and its homologues produced in cracking processes of hydrocarbon oils. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the cracking of hydrocarbon oils, and groups together products derived from those processes.
[C10G 2400/26] Fuel gas -- C10G 2400/26 pertains to fuel gases generated in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils, such as synthesis gas, refinery gas, or other combustible gaseous mixtures used as energy sources. The subclass includes methods for producing, separating, or purifying these gases and their application as fuel in industrial or automotive settings.
[C10G 2400/28] Propane and butane -- C10G 2400/28 refers to patents dealing with the production or processing of propane and butane obtained from hydrocarbon cracking operations. It is situated under chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the cracking of hydrocarbon oils, and covers methods, apparatuses and products related to these light alkanes.
[C10G 2400/30] Aromatics -- C10G 2400/30 is a CPC subgroup covering aromatic compounds produced in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils. It falls under the hierarchy of Chemistry → Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries → Cracking Hydrocarbon Oils → Products obtained by processes covered by groups. This entry classifies aromatic products such as benzene, toluene and xylenes derived from petroleum cracking.
[C10G 2/50] from carbon dioxide with hydrogen -- C10G 2/50 covers processes that produce liquid hydrocarbon mixtures of undefined composition by reacting carbon dioxide with hydrogen. It falls under the cracking of hydrocarbon oils within petroleum and gas refining, specifically describing synthesis routes that convert CO₂ and H₂ into liquid fuel-like products.
[C10G 25/00] Refining of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen, with solid sorbents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining processes using physical adsorbents.
[C10G 25/003] Specific sorbent material, not covered by  C10G25/02  or  C10G25/03 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Specific sorbent materials, like silica, alumina, etc. not covered by C10G25/02 or C10G25/03 .
[C10G 25/006] of waste oils, e.g. PCB's containing oils -- C10G 25/006 covers refining of waste hydrocarbon oils, such as oils that contain polychlorinated biphenyls, by means of solid sorbents in cracking operations performed without hydrogen. The process falls within the chemistry class, under petroleum, gas or coke industries and the subgroup for cracking hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 25/02] with ion-exchange material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes using ion-exchange material as adsorbent, thus no ion-exchange takes place, i.e. the material adsorbs the impurity without leaving an ion (being part of the material) in the hydrocarbon liquid.  **Glossary:** - Ion-exchange material: material that can exchange ions, e.g. clay
[C10G 25/03] with crystalline alumino-silicates, e.g. molecular sieves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen, with crystalline alumino silicates, e.g. molecular sieves, zeolites.  **Glossary:** - Molecular sieves: Crystalline alumino-silicates, Aluminophosphate [ALPO], silicoaluminophosphate [SAPO]
[C10G 25/05] Removal of non-hydrocarbon compounds, e.g. sulfur compounds -- C10G 25/05 covers processes for refining hydrocarbon oils by cracking without hydrogen, where solid sorbents are used to eliminate non-hydrocarbon impurities such as sulfur-containing compounds. The method improves product quality and reduces catalyst poisoning in petroleum cracking operations.
[C10G 25/06] with moving sorbents or sorbents dispersed in the oil -- This CPC subclass covers processes for refining hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen where solid sorbents are used in a dynamic manner, either moving through the oil stream or being dispersed within it, to facilitate cracking or other modifications. It falls under C10G 25 concerning non-hydrogen refining with solid sorbents.
[C10G 25/08] according to the "moving bed" method -- The CPC subclass C10G 25/08 covers processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen that employ solid sorbents arranged in a moving-bed configuration, wherein the sorbent material moves relative to the feed to enhance contact and catalytic activity. This method is used in petroleum refining to break down heavy oils into lighter fractions while avoiding hydrogen addition.
[C10G 25/09] according to the "fluidised bed" technique
[C10G 25/11] Distillation in the presence of moving sorbents -- C10G 25/11 covers processes where hydrocarbon oils are distilled while solid sorbents are moved through the material, enabling separation or cracking without hydrogen. It falls under cracking of hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum industry, specifically refining without hydrogen using moving sorbents.
[C10G 25/12] Recovery of used adsorbent -- This CPC subclass C10G 25/12 addresses methods for recovering and regenerating solid adsorbents that have been used in non-hydrogen cracking of hydrocarbon oils. It relates to petroleum refining processes where spent sorbents are reclaimed for reuse or disposal.
[C10G 27/00] Refining of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen, by oxidation -- C10G 27/00 covers methods for refining petroleum or other hydrocarbon oils without the use of hydrogen, relying instead on oxidative reactions. It includes oxidative cracking, dehydrogenation, desulfurisation and related treatments that convert heavy fractions into lighter products through controlled exposure to oxygen or oxidising agents.
[C10G 27/02] with halogen or compounds generating halogen -- C10G 27/02 covers a refining step in which hydrocarbon oils are cracked without hydrogen by oxidative processes that involve a halogen or a halogen-generating compound, such as chlorine, bromine or their precursors. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, and cracking hydrocarbon oils, and details the use of halogen-based oxidation to break down oil molecules during refining.
[C10G 27/04] with oxygen or compounds generating oxygen -- The C10G 27/04 subclass defines catalytic or thermal processes that crack hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by employing oxygen or compounds that generate oxygen, effecting oxidative cracking during petroleum refining. It is situated under petroleum-related chemistry, specifically oxidation-based oil cracking.
[C10G 27/06] in the presence of alkaline solutions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Oxidation processes using as the catalyst an alkaline solution, e.g. sodium hydroxide solution
[C10G 27/08] in the presence of copper chloride -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Oxidation processes using as the catalyst copper chloride.
[C10G 27/10] in the presence of metal-containing organic complexes, e.g. chelates, or cationic ion-exchange resins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen by oxidation processes using as the catalyst metal-containing organic complexes or cationic ion-exchange resins
[C10G 27/12] with oxygen-generating compounds, e.g. per-compounds, chromic acid, chromates -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Use of plumbites or plumbates -> C10G19/06
[C10G 27/14] with ozone-containing gases -- C10G 27/14 covers petroleum-refining processes that crack hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by using oxidative reactions that involve ozone-containing gases, enabling the breakdown of heavy oils into lighter products through ozone-mediated oxidation.
[C10G 29/00] Refining of hydrocarbon oils, in the absence of hydrogen, with other chemicals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining of hydrocarbons oils by adding a catalyst; chemisorption, other chemicals being any compound that will react with the hydrocarbon oils in order to refine them, to remove impurities.
[C10G 29/02] Non-metals -- C10G 29/02 covers the use of non-metallic substances such as oxides, halides, silicates or other inorganic compounds as catalysts or reagents in the non-hydrogenating cracking of petroleum oils. It relates to refining operations that employ these non-metals to promote oil cracking or upgrading without the addition of hydrogen.
[C10G 29/04] Metals, or metals deposited on a carrier -- C10G 29/04 covers the use of metals, or metals that have been deposited on a carrier, in processes that refine hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen. The metals act as catalysts or promoters in chemical cracking reactions, improving conversion efficiency and product selectivity.
[C10G 29/06] Metal salts, or metal salts deposited on a carrier -- C10G 29/06 relates to the use of metal salts, or metal salts supported on a carrier, in the chemical cracking of hydrocarbon oils when no hydrogen is present. It falls under the petroleum and gas refining hierarchy, specifically the non-hydrogenative cracking of oil streams.
[C10G 29/08] containing the metal in the lower valency -- C10G 29/08 relates to refining hydrocarbon oils by non-hydrogen cracking with chemical agents where a metal present in a lower oxidation state is used, typically as a catalyst or additive, to influence the cracking reaction.
[C10G 29/10] Sulfides -- C10G 29/10 deals with the use of sulfide compounds in the non-hydrogenic cracking of hydrocarbon oils during refining. It includes processes where sulfides act as catalysts, promoters or additives to facilitate the breakdown of oil fractions in the presence of other chemicals but without hydrogen.
[C10G 29/12] Halides -- C10G 29/12 covers the use of halide compounds in the non-hydrogenating cracking of hydrocarbon oils during petroleum refining. It includes processes where halides act as catalysts or reagents to promote the chemical breakdown of oils in the absence of hydrogen.
[C10G 29/16] Metal oxides -- The class deals with the application of metal oxides in the non-hydrogen cracking of hydrocarbon oils during petroleum refining. It includes processes where metal-oxide catalysts or additives facilitate thermal cracking, promote reaction control, or aid in desulfurisation and other upgrades of the oil.
[C10G 29/20] Organic compounds not containing metal atoms -- C10G 29/20 covers organic compounds that contain no metal atoms and are used in the refining of hydrocarbon oils through cracking processes that do not involve hydrogen. The entry pertains to chemical additives that facilitate thermal or catalytic cracking in petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 29/205] by reaction with hydrocarbons added to the hydrocarbon oil -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen by reaction with hydrocarbons added to the hydrocarbon oil, e.g.  Alkylation processes.
[C10G 29/22] containing oxygen as the only hetero atom -- C10G 29/22 covers refining processes that crack hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen, using chemical agents that contain only oxygen as the hetero atom. It includes methods where oxygen-containing compounds such as alcohols, acids or esters are employed to facilitate cracking, while no other hetero atoms like nitrogen or sulfur are present.
[C10G 29/24] Aldehydes or ketones -- C10G 29/24 covers the use or production of aldehydes or ketones during the chemical cracking of petroleum-derived hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen. It includes processes in which these carbonyl compounds are formed, added, or removed as part of refining operations that employ other chemicals.
[C10G 29/26] Halogenated hydrocarbons -- C10G 29/26 concerns the use or production of halogenated hydrocarbons in non-hydrogenic cracking of petroleum oils, where these compounds serve as solvents, catalysts or reaction modifiers during the chemical refining of hydrocarbon streams.
[C10G 29/28] containing sulfur as the only hetero atom, e.g. mercaptans, or sulfur and oxygen as the only hetero atoms -- The subclass C10G 29/28 covers processes for refining hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen in which the only hetero atoms present are sulfur alone, as in mercaptans, or sulfur together with oxygen. It pertains to chemical cracking methods used in petroleum, gas or coke refining.
[C10G 3/00] Production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organic materials, e.g. fatty oils, fatty acids -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Production from non-melting solid oxygen-containing carbonaceous materials -> C10G1/00
[C10G 31/00] Refining of hydrocarbon oils, in the absence of hydrogen, by methods not otherwise provided for -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Refining by distillation -> C10G7/00
[C10G 31/06] by heating, cooling, or pressure treatment -- C10G 31/06 covers petroleum-refining processes that crack hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by applying heat, cooling or pressure. It includes thermal or pressure-induced cracking methods such as pyrolysis, steam cracking, and other non-hydrogenic treatments used to break down oil fractions.
[C10G 31/08] by treating with water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Desalting processes, refining of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen, by treating with liquid water or steam
[C10G 31/09] by filtration -- C10G 31/09 designates the use of filtration to treat hydrocarbon oils during petroleum refining operations that crack the feedstock without hydrogen, covering methods that separate solids, catalysts or contaminants by passing the oil through filter media. This subclass falls under non-hydrogen cracking processes in the CPC hierarchy.
[C10G 31/10] with the aid of centrifugal force -- C10G 31/10 covers cracking of hydrocarbon oils without added hydrogen where the process is aided by centrifugal force, such as using rotating vessels or centrifuges that generate high shear and separation to promote molecular breakdown.
[C10G 31/11] by dialysis -- C10G 31/11 covers refining of cracked hydrocarbon oils performed without added hydrogen, where the separation of fractions is achieved by dialysis-a process using a semipermeable membrane to separate components according to molecular size or solubility, and is not otherwise provided for in the CPC scheme.
[C10G 32/00] Refining of hydrocarbon oils by electric or magnetic means, by irradiation, or by using microorganisms -- C10G 32/00 covers technologies for improving hydrocarbon oils that employ non-thermal physical forces or biological agents, such as electric or magnetic fields, ionising radiation, or microorganisms, to alter composition, remove impurities or enhance fuel properties. These methods are alternatives to conventional chemical cracking in the petroleum refining sector.
[C10G 32/02] by electric or magnetic means -- C10G 32/02 covers processes for refining hydrocarbon oils that employ electric or magnetic energy, such as electro-chemical cracking, induction heating, microwave or radio-frequency treatment, and other electrically driven methods that alter the molecular structure of the oil to improve its quality or produce specific products.
[C10G 32/04] by particle radiation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining of hydrocarbon oils by particle radiation  Nuclear radiation processes.
[C10G 33/00] Dewatering or demulsification of hydrocarbon oils -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Dewatering by distillation -> C10G7/04
[C10G 33/02] with electrical or magnetic means -- C10G 33/02 covers the dewatering or demulsification of hydrocarbon oils during cracking operations by applying electrical or magnetic techniques, such as electro-static separators, magnetic fields, or induced currents, to break emulsions and remove water from the oil stream. This subclass falls under the broader categories of Chemistry, Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries, and Cracking Hydrocarbon Oils.
[C10G 33/04] with chemical means -- C10G 33/04 covers methods for removing water from hydrocarbon oils or breaking emulsions by adding chemical agents, typically employed in petroleum-refinery cracking processes to improve oil quality.
[C10G 33/06] with mechanical means, e.g. by filtration -- The code C10G 33/06 relates to the removal of water from hydrocarbon oils during cracking operations by using purely mechanical techniques, most commonly filtration, rather than chemical or thermal treatments. It covers apparatuses and methods that separate water droplets or emulsions from the oil phase through physical means.
[C10G 33/08] Controlling or regulating -- C10G 33/08 covers methods and apparatus for controlling or regulating the dewatering or demulsification of hydrocarbon oils, particularly in cracking processes. It relates to adjusting parameters such as temperature, pressure, chemical additives, or mechanical means to separate water from oil streams in petroleum refining.
[C10G 3/40] Thermal non-catalytic treatment -- C10G 3/40 covers thermal non-catalytic processes that convert oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or fatty acids into liquid hydrocarbon mixtures. The methods rely on heat alone, without catalysts, to crack or decompose the feedstock into fuels and other hydrocarbons.
[C10G 3/42] Catalytic treatment -- The CPC entry C10G 3/42 covers catalytic treatment processes used in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils to produce liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or fatty acids, typical of petroleum and gas industry refining.
[C10G 3/44] characterised by the catalyst used -- This subclass covers methods for producing liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or fatty acids, where the defining feature is the particular catalyst employed in the cracking process. It falls under the broader groups dealing with petroleum, gas or coke industries and the cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 3/45] containing iron group metals or compounds thereof -- C10G 3/45 pertains to methods for producing liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or fatty acids, where the conversion is carried out in the presence of iron-group metals or their compounds. The metals act as catalysts or promoters in the cracking or hydrogenation steps. This subclass is part of the broader cracking hydrocarbons group within petroleum and gas processing.
[C10G 3/46] in combination with chromium, molybdenum, tungsten metals or compounds thereof -- C10G 3/46 relates to processes for producing liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or fatty acids, where the reaction is carried out together with chromium, molybdenum, tungsten metals or their compounds. The classification applies to catalytic systems that incorporate these metals to improve cracking or hydrogenation steps.
[C10G 3/47] containing platinum group metals or compounds thereof -- This CPC subclass deals with processes for producing liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or acids, where the catalyst system includes platinum-group metals or their compounds. It falls under the cracking of hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum, gas or coke industry sector of chemistry.
[C10G 3/48] further characterised by the catalyst support -- This subclass covers processes for producing liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or fatty acids, where the catalyst is specifically defined by the nature of its support material. It is situated in the cracking hydrocarbon oils group of the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 3/49] containing crystalline aluminosilicates, e.g. molecular sieves
[C10G 3/50] in the presence of hydrogen, hydrogen donors or hydrogen generating compounds -- The C10G 3/50 subclass covers processes that convert oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or fatty acids into liquid hydrocarbon mixtures by cracking in the presence of hydrogen, hydrogen-donor substances, or compounds that generate hydrogen in situ. These hydrogen-assisted methods improve the yield and quality of the resulting hydrocarbon fuels.
[C10G 35/00] Reforming naphtha -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Treatment of naphtha, in order to improve the octane number or its aromatic content.
[C10G 35/02] Thermal reforming -- C10G 35/02 covers thermal reforming processes applied to naphtha within the cracking of hydrocarbon oils, a sub-field of petroleum, gas and coke industry chemistry. It pertains to heating naphtha to induce molecular rearrangements that improve its octane quality without using catalytic reactors.
[C10G 35/04] Catalytic reforming -- C10G 35/04 covers catalytic reforming processes in which naphtha feedstocks are converted into higher-octane gasoline components by contacting the oil with a metal-based catalyst under elevated temperature and hydrogen pressure, typically using platinum or other noble metals to promote dehydrogenation, isomerization, and cyclisation reactions.
[C10G 35/06] characterised by the catalyst used -- C10G 35/06 covers processes for reforming naphtha in which the defining feature is the specific catalyst employed. It includes methods that use particular metal, acid or mixed catalysts to promote the conversion of hydrocarbon feedstock during cracking. The classification distinguishes these processes from others by the nature and composition of the catalyst used.
[C10G 35/065] containing crystalline zeolitic molecular sieves, other than aluminosilicates -- This subclass relates to reforming naphtha in petroleum cracking processes that employ crystalline zeolitic molecular sieves other than aluminosilicates as catalysts. It covers the use of alternative zeolite frameworks, such as pure-silica or heteroatom-substituted zeolites, to facilitate hydrocarbon conversion.
[C10G 35/085] containing platinum group metals or compounds thereof -- The subclass C10G 35/085 covers catalytic reforming of naphtha in which platinum-group metals or their compounds are employed as the active catalyst or are present in the reforming system. It pertains to processes that improve octane rating and hydrogen production by using PGMs during hydrocarbon cracking.
[C10G 35/09] Bimetallic catalysts in which at least one of the metals is a platinum group metal -- C10G 35/09 covers bimetallic catalysts that contain at least one platinum-group metal and are used for reforming naphtha in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils. It falls under the Chemistry section, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries branch for cracking hydrocarbon oils. This subclass deals with catalyst formulations that combine a platinum-group metal with another metal to enhance reforming performance.
[C10G 35/095] containing crystalline alumino-silicates, e.g. molecular sieves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Catalytic reforming naphta characterised by the catalyst containing crystalline alumino-silicates, e.g. molecular sieves, zeolites including only silica or alumina.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Crystalline zeolitic molecular sieves, other than aluminosilicates, e.g. zeolie including other elements with or without silica or alumina like SAPO, ALPO -> C10G35/065
[C10G 35/10] with moving catalysts -- C10G 35/10 covers processes for reforming naphtha in which the catalyst is not fixed but moves relative to the feed, such as fluid-catalyst, slurry or suspended-catalyst cracking of hydrocarbon oils. This subclass falls under the broader category of petroleum refining methods for cracking hydrocarbons.
[C10G 35/12] according to the "moving-bed" method -- C10G 35/12 covers reforming of naphtha using a moving-bed process, where the catalyst bed is continuously shifted relative to the hydrocarbon feed. This arrangement improves heat distribution and catalyst utilization during the conversion of naphtha into higher-octane aromatics and is classified under cracking hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10G 35/14] according to the "fluidised-bed" technique -- The C10G 35/14 entry covers the reforming of naphtha using a fluidised-bed process, a method for upgrading hydrocarbon feedstocks in petroleum refining. It falls under the cracking hydrocarbons category within the petroleum, gas or coke industries. This technique improves octane rating by promoting catalytic reactions in a fluidised-bed reactor.
[C10G 35/16] with electric, electromagnetic, or mechanical vibrations -- C10G 35/16 covers methods for reforming naphtha in which electric, electromagnetic or mechanical vibrations are applied to the hydrocarbon feed, typically to enhance cracking or isomerisation during petroleum refining processes.
[C10G 3/52] Hydrogen in a special composition or from a special source -- C10G 3/52 covers methods of providing hydrogen for the cracking of oxygen-containing oils and acids using unusual hydrogen carriers or sources, such as metal hydrides, ammonia-derived compounds, or other special compositions, to produce liquid hydrocarbon blends.
[C10G 35/22] Starting-up reforming operations -- C10G 35/22 covers the start-up phase of catalytic reforming of naphtha, describing the procedures, conditions and controls needed to bring a reformer from idle to normal operating mode, including catalyst activation, temperature and pressure ramp-up, and related monitoring.
[C10G 35/24] Controlling or regulating of reforming operations -- C10G 35/24 covers techniques and equipment used to monitor, control or adjust the conditions of naphtha reforming during hydrocarbon cracking, such as temperature, pressure, catalyst activity or feed composition, to optimise product yield and quality. It falls under the chemistry branch for petroleum and gas industries, specifically the cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 3/54] characterised by the catalytic bed -- C10G 3/54 relates to processes that produce liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or acids using a catalytic bed. The subclass specifies that the conversion is carried out over a fixed or fluid catalytic medium that promotes cracking or deoxygenation to generate fuels and other hydrocarbons.
[C10G 3/55] with moving solid particles, e.g. moving beds -- The C10G 3/55 subclass covers processes for producing liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or fatty acids, where the reaction is conducted with moving solid particles, for example in a moving-bed reactor. It falls under the broader category of cracking hydrocarbon oils within petroleum and chemical processing.
[C10G 3/56] suspended in the oil, e.g. slurries, ebullated beds -- C10G 3/56 relates to hydrocarbon production processes in which the reacting phase is dispersed as a suspension within the oil, for example in slurry or ebullated-bed reactors. The classification covers the use of solid catalysts or other materials kept suspended to crack oxygen-containing feedstocks such as fatty oils or acids into liquid hydrocarbon mixtures.
[C10G 3/57] according to the fluidised bed technique -- The C10G 3/57 entry covers processes that produce liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or fatty acids using a fluidised-bed technique. It falls within the petroleum, gas and coke industry's cracking of hydrocarbon oils, specifying the fluidised-bed method for converting these materials into liquid hydrocarbons.
[C10G 3/60] Controlling or regulating the processes -- C10G 3/60 covers the control and regulation of processes that produce liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organic feedstocks such as fatty oils or fatty acids. It includes methods, sensors, feedback systems and operating strategies used to monitor and adjust reaction conditions during cracking of these materials.
[C10G 3/62] Catalyst regeneration -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus for regenerating catalysts used in producing liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from oxygen-containing organics such as fatty oils and fatty acids, typically in cracking processes. It includes techniques for removing coke or other deactivating deposits to restore catalyst activity.
[C10G 45/00] Hydrotreatment processes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydrogenation of olefins.
[C10G 45/02] to eliminate hetero atoms without changing the skeleton of the hydrocarbon involved and without cracking into lower boiling hydrocarbons -- C10G 45/02 covers hydrotreatment methods that remove hetero-atoms such as sulfur, nitrogen or oxygen from cracked hydrocarbon oils while preserving the original carbon skeleton and avoiding further cracking into lower-boiling fractions. It is applied in petroleum refining to improve fuel quality after cracking steps, falling under the hierarchy of Chemistry → Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries → Cracking Hydrocarbon Oils → Hydrotreatment processes.
[C10G 45/04] characterised by the catalyst used -- In the CPC, class C10G deals with hydroprocessing of cracked oils; subgroup 45/00 addresses hydrotreatment of hydrocarbon oils obtained by cracking; 45/04 further narrows to processes defined by the type of catalyst employed, such as sulfided metal, zeolite or other supported catalysts, influencing reaction conditions and product distribution.
[C10G 45/06] containing nickel or cobalt metal, or compounds thereof -- C10G 45/06 covers hydrotreatment steps used in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils when nickel, cobalt or their compounds are present, typically as catalysts or catalyst promoters. The classification pertains to petroleum refining processes that employ these metals to enhance hydrogenation and removal of impurities.
[C10G 45/08] in combination with chromium, molybdenum, or tungsten metals, or compounds thereof -- C10G 45/08 relates to hydrotreatment of cracked hydrocarbon oils in which the catalyst system contains chromium, molybdenum, tungsten or compounds of these metals, typically employed to remove impurities or improve the quality of the refined product.
[C10G 45/10] containing platinum group metals or compounds thereof -- C10G 45/10 relates to hydrotreatment processes applied to cracked hydrocarbon oils in which platinum-group metals or their compounds are used as catalysts or additives to remove impurities, improve product quality, or modify oil properties during refinery upgrading.
[C10G 45/12] containing crystalline alumino-silicates, e.g. molecular sieves -- The CPC subclass C10G 45/12 covers hydrotreatment steps in petroleum cracking where crystalline alumino-silicates, for example molecular sieves, are employed as catalysts or support materials. It applies to processes that use these sorbent materials to improve hydrocarbon conversion and product quality.
[C10G 45/14] with moving solid particles -- C10G 45/14 classifies hydrotreatment processes for cracked hydrocarbon oils that employ moving solid particles, such as fluidised-bed catalysts or packed beds, to achieve reactions like hydrodesulphurisation, denitrogenation, or hydrogenation. The moving solids provide contact with the oil stream to improve conversion and product quality.
[C10G 45/16] suspended in the oil, e.g. slurries -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining processes in the presence of an ebullated bed.
[C10G 45/18] according to the "moving-bed" technique -- C10G 45/18 covers hydrotreatment processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils that use a moving-bed technique. In this method solid catalyst particles flow through the reactor while continuously contacting feed and being regenerated, which improves efficiency and catalyst life.
[C10G 45/20] according to the "fluidised-bed" technique -- C10G 45/20 covers hydrotreatment of cracked hydrocarbon oils carried out in a fluidised-bed reactor, where solid catalyst particles are suspended in an upward flow of gas or liquid. The technique provides efficient heat and mass transfer, allowing continuous catalyst renewal and effective removal of sulfur, nitrogen or metals during petroleum refining.
[C10G 45/22] with hydrogen dissolved or suspended in the oil -- C10G 45/22 covers hydrotreatment processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils in which hydrogen is present in the oil phase, either dissolved or suspended, rather than being supplied solely as a separate gaseous stream. The subclass specifies the use of hydrogen-rich oil to facilitate catalytic cracking or upgrading.
[C10G 45/24] with hydrogen-generating compounds -- C10G 45/24 covers hydrotreatment of cracked hydrocarbon oils where hydrogen is supplied by compounds that generate hydrogen in situ, such as water, alcohols or other hydrogen-rich substances. The process provides the needed hydrogen through decomposition or reaction of the hydrogen-generating compound, reducing reliance on external hydrogen gas.
[C10G 45/26] Steam or water -- C10G 45/26 covers the use of steam or water as a treating medium in hydrotreatment processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils, typically employed to remove sulfur, nitrogen or other contaminants from petroleum streams.
[C10G 45/28] Organic compounds -- C10G 45/28 covers organic compounds that are employed in or result from hydrotreatment processes applied to cracked hydrocarbon oils, a sub-field of petroleum refining. It includes catalysts, additives, solvents and reaction intermediates specifically designed to improve the quality, stability or purity of the treated fuels.
[C10G 45/30] characterised by the catalyst used -- C10G 45/30 covers hydrotreatment processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils where the specific feature is the catalyst employed. It pertains to petroleum refining operations that use particular catalyst types to improve desulfurization, denitrogenation, or hydrogenation during hydrocracking.
[C10G 45/32] Selective hydrogenation of the diolefin or acetylene compounds -- The CPC subclass C10G 45/32 covers processes for selectively hydrogenating diolefin or acetylene compounds during hydrotreatment of cracked hydrocarbon oils, aimed at saturating these unsaturated molecules to improve fuel stability and quality in petroleum refining.
[C10G 45/34] characterised by the catalyst used -- C10G 45/34 covers hydrotreatment steps applied to cracked hydrocarbon oils where the defining element is the catalyst employed. It relates to processes that specify particular catalyst types, compositions, supports or promoters used to remove impurities, saturate olefins or improve product quality during petroleum refining.
[C10G 45/36] containing nickel or cobalt metal, or compounds thereof -- This CPC subclass (C10G 45/36) covers hydrotreatment processes applied to cracked hydrocarbon oils that employ nickel, cobalt or their compounds as catalysts or additives. It is situated under Chemistry → Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries → Cracking Hydrocarbon Oils → Hydrotreatment processes, defining methods that specifically contain these metals.
[C10G 45/38] in combination with chromium, molybdenum or tungsten metals, or compounds thereof
[C10G 45/40] containing platinum group metals or compounds thereof -- C10G 45/40 covers hydrotreatment processes applied to cracked hydrocarbon oils that employ platinum-group metals or their compounds as catalysts or catalyst components. It addresses the use of these metals to enhance reactions such as desulfurization, denitrogenation or hydrogenation during upgrading of petroleum fractions.
[C10G 45/42] with moving solid particles -- C10G 45/42 covers hydro-treatment of cracked hydrocarbon oils in which solid particles are moved through the liquid, for example in a slurry, fluidised-bed or moving-bed reactor. The classification applies to processes that use a solid catalyst or adsorbent to remove sulfur, nitrogen, metals or to saturate unsaturated compounds while the solids are continuously circulated.
[C10G 45/44] Hydrogenation of the aromatic hydrocarbons -- C10G 45/44 covers processes that hydrogenate aromatic hydrocarbons, typically converting compounds such as benzene, toluene, or xylene into their saturated counterparts by adding hydrogen in the presence of a catalyst. The entry falls under cracking hydrocarbon oils and hydrotreatment, focusing on selective hydrogenation steps employed in petroleum refining to improve product stability and meet fuel specifications.
[C10G 45/46] characterised by the catalyst used -- The CPC subclass C10G 45/46 covers hydrotreatment processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils that are defined by the specific catalyst employed. It includes methods where the nature, composition or formulation of the catalyst is the distinguishing feature of the treatment. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and cracking hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 45/48] containing nickel or cobalt metal, or compounds thereof
[C10G 45/50] in combination with chromium, molybdenum or tungsten metal, or compounds thereof -- This CPC subclass concerns hydrotreatment methods for cracked hydrocarbon oils that employ a catalyst system containing chromium, molybdenum or tungsten metals or their compounds, used to remove impurities such as sulfur or nitrogen and to improve product quality in petroleum refining.
[C10G 45/52] containing platinum group metals or compounds thereof -- This subclass covers hydrotreatment steps used in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils when the catalyst contains platinum-group metals or their compounds. The presence of these noble metal catalysts enhances dehydrogenation, hydrogenation and desulphurisation efficiency in petroleum refining processes.
[C10G 45/54] containing crystalline alumino-silicates, e.g. molecular sieves -- C10G 45/54 covers hydrotreatment processes applied to cracked hydrocarbon oils in which the catalyst contains crystalline alumino-silicates such as molecular sieves. The classification groups methods that use these porous materials to remove impurities, improve stability or modify the composition of the hydrotreated product.
[C10G 45/56] with moving solid particles -- This subclass covers hydrotreatment of cracked hydrocarbon oils that employs moving solid particles, such as fluidized or moving-bed reactors, where the catalyst or other solids circulate to contact the oil for desulfurisation, denitrogenation or hydrogenation.
[C10G 45/58] to change the structural skeleton of some of the hydrocarbon content without cracking the other hydrocarbons present, e.g. lowering pour point -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dewaxing processes by selective hydroisomerisation and/or selective hydrocracking, without changing the boiling point.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Refining of hydrocarbons oils by electric or magnetic means, by irradiation or by using microorganism -> C10G32/00 - Improving or increasing the octane number or aromatic content of naphtha -> C10G35/00
[C10G 45/60] characterised by the catalyst used -- C10G 45/60 covers hydrotreatment processes applied to cracked hydrocarbon oils where the specific catalyst employed defines the method. The subclass groups any treatment step, such as hydrodesulfurisation or hydrodenitrification, that is distinguished by the type, composition or preparation of the catalyst used.
[C10G 45/62] containing platinum group metals or compounds thereof -- The subclass C10G 45/62 covers hydro-treatment steps applied in the cracking of petroleum fractions in which the catalyst or additive includes platinum-group metals or their compounds. It pertains to processes that use these noble metals to improve hydrogenation, denitrogenation or desulfurization of hydrocarbon streams during refining.
[C10G 45/64] containing crystalline alumino-silicates, e.g. molecular sieves -- C10G 45/64 covers hydrotreatment steps in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils where the process employs crystalline alumino-silicates, such as molecular-sieve zeolites, as catalysts, adsorbents or supports. The classification pertains to refining operations that use these porous inorganic materials to remove impurities, improve catalyst stability, or modify reaction pathways during petroleum, gas or coke industry processing.
[C10G 45/66] with moving solid particles -- C10G 45/66 covers hydrotreatment of cracked hydrocarbon oils in which solid particles move relative to the liquid feed, such as slurry reactors, fluidized-bed or moving-bed systems. The classification is used for processes that employ solid catalysts or adsorbents in motion to remove sulfur, nitrogen, metals or saturate olefins during treatment of petroleum fractions.
[C10G 45/68] Aromatisation of hydrocarbon oil fractions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dehydrogenation processes of naphthenes.
[C10G 45/70] with catalysts containing platinum group metals or compounds thereof -- This subclass covers hydrotreatment processes for cracked hydrocarbon oils that employ catalysts containing platinum-group metals or their compounds, focusing on refining methods that remove impurities such as sulfur, nitrogen or metals using these specialized catalytic systems.
[C10G 45/72] Controlling or regulating -- C10G 45/72 covers methods, devices and control strategies for regulating the conditions of hydrotreatment processes applied to cracked hydrocarbon oils, such as temperature, pressure, hydrogen feed rate and catalyst activity. It includes monitoring and feedback systems that maintain optimal reaction parameters to achieve desired desulphurisation, denitrogenation or saturating of the feedstock.
[C10G 47/00] Cracking of hydrocarbon oils, in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen- generating compounds, to obtain lower boiling fractions -- The CPC subclass C10G 47/00 relates to processes that crack hydrocarbon oils while hydrogen or hydrogen-producing agents are present, producing lighter, lower-boiling fractions. It describes refining methods that combine hydrogenation and cracking to improve product yields and quality.
[C10G 47/02] characterised by the catalyst used -- C10G 47/02 covers processes for hydrogen-assisted cracking of hydrocarbon oils where the distinguishing feature is the type of catalyst employed. It includes catalytic hydrogenolysis or hydrocracking methods using metals, zeolites, or other catalyst systems to produce lower-boiling fractions. The classification falls under petroleum refining and cracking operations.
[C10G 47/04] Oxides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cracking processes of hydrocabon oils in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen generating compounds characterised by the catalyst being oxides, the oxides does not form part of the reaction. It's a bulk catalyst  Cracking processes using a bulk catalyst, i.e. in the absence of a support.
[C10G 47/06] Sulfides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cracking processes of hydrocabon oils in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen generating compounds characterised by the catalyst being sulfides  Cracking processes using a bulk catalyst, i.e. in the absence of a support.
[C10G 47/08] Halides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cracking processes of hydrocabon oils in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen generating compounds characterised by the catalyst being halides  Cracking processes using a bulk catalyst, i.e. in the absence of a support.
[C10G 47/10] with catalysts deposited on a carrier -- A process for hydrocarbon-oil cracking that uses hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds together with a catalyst that is deposited on a solid carrier, allowing the catalyst to be recovered and reused. This subclass falls under cracking of petroleum fractions to produce lower-boiling products.
[C10G 47/12] Inorganic carriers -- The CPC subclass C10G 47/12 refers to inorganic carriers employed in the catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds, a process used to produce lower-boiling fractions in petroleum refining.

[C10G 47/16] Crystalline alumino-silicate carriers -- The CPC subclass C10G 47/16 covers the use of crystalline alumino-silicate carriers, such as zeolites, as catalyst supports in hydrocracking processes that break down petroleum fractions in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen-generating agents to produce lighter, lower-boiling products. This subclass falls under the broader field of petroleum, gas or coke industry cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 47/18] the catalyst containing platinum group metals or compounds thereof -- C10G 47/18 covers the use of catalysts that contain platinum group metals or their compounds in hydrocarbon oil cracking processes carried out with hydrogen or hydrogen-generating agents to produce lower-boiling fractions.
[C10G 47/20] the catalyst containing other metals or compounds thereof -- This subclass covers the use of catalysts that incorporate additional metals or their compounds in hydrocracking processes where hydrocarbon oils are cracked with hydrogen to produce lighter fractions. The classification falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 47/22] Non-catalytic cracking in the presence of hydrogen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Thermal hydrovisbreaking processes.
[C10G 47/24] with moving solid particles -- C10G 47/24 covers processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen-generating agents to produce lighter fractions, where the cracking is carried out with moving solid particles such as fluidised-bed catalysts or suspended catalyst beads. This method enhances contact between the oil and catalyst, improving conversion efficiency.
[C10G 47/26] suspended in the oil, e.g. slurries -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining processes in the presence of an ebullated bed.
[C10G 47/28] according to the "moving-bed" technique -- C10G 47/28 covers the moving-bed technique for catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds, a process used to produce lower-boiling fractions in petroleum, gas and coke industries. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy, specifically cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 47/30] according to the "fluidised-bed" technique -- C10G 47/30 covers hydrocracking processes in which the cracking of petroleum oils with hydrogen is carried out in a fluidised-bed reactor. The technique uses a gas-fluidised catalyst bed to provide intimate contact between oil, hydrogen and catalyst, allowing continuous operation and improved heat transfer. It is applied to produce lower-boiling fractions from heavy hydrocarbons.
[C10G 47/32] in the presence of hydrogen-generating compounds -- C10G 47/32 is a CPC subclass covering the cracking of hydrocarbon oils when hydrogen-generating compounds are present, enabling the production of lower-boiling fractions. It falls under Chemistry → Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries → Cracking Hydrocarbon Oils, specifying processes that combine cracking with in-situ hydrogen generation.
[C10G 47/34] Organic compounds, e.g. hydrogenated hydrocarbons -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydrocarbons that can give hydrogen, e.g. parafine transformed to olefine, the hydrogen being released with the reaction is used for the cracking process.  Hydrogen donor diluent cracking, e.g. tetraline.
[C10G 47/36] Controlling or regulating -- C10G 47/36 covers the control and regulation of hydrocarbon oil cracking processes that use hydrogen or hydrogen-producing agents to obtain lower-boiling fractions. It includes devices, parameters and feedback systems that maintain optimal reaction conditions such as temperature, pressure and catalyst activity during the cracking operation.
[C10G 49/00] Treatment of hydrocarbon oils, in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds, not provided for in a single one of groups -- This subgroup covers processes for upgrading or refining hydrocarbon oils using hydrogen or in-situ hydrogen donors, where the specific method does not fall within any other defined sub-groups of C10G 49. It includes catalytic hydrogenation, hydrocracking variations, and other hydrogen-based treatments that are not otherwise classified.
[C10G 49/002] Apparatus for fixed bed hydrotreatment processes -- Code C10G 49/002 covers equipment used in fixed-bed hydrotreatment of cracked hydrocarbon oils, where hydrogen or hydrogen-producing agents are employed to modify the oil. The classification pertains to apparatus designed for such treatment processes.
[C10G 49/005] Inhibiting corrosion in hydrotreatment processes -- C10G 49/005 pertains to methods and additives that prevent corrosion in hydrotreated oil processes, where hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds are used to upgrade petroleum fractions.
