[C10G 49/007] in the presence of hydrogen from a special source or of a special composition or having been purified by a special treatment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Treatment in the presence of hydrogen from a special source (refining, cracking, general) , e.g. Fischer-Tropsch tail gas.  Any specific source other than hydrogen, specific cleaning treatment
[C10G 49/02] characterised by the catalyst used -- C10G 49/02 relates to methods for treating hydrocarbon oils, especially in cracking operations conducted in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen-generating agents, where the defining criterion is the particular catalyst employed. The subclass captures processes that are not covered by other groups and are distinguished solely by the nature of the catalyst used.
[C10G 49/04] containing nickel, cobalt, chromium, molybdenum, or tungsten metals, or compounds thereof -- The code C10G 49/04 covers hydrocracking processes for petroleum oils in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds where the catalyst contains nickel, cobalt, chromium, molybdenum or tungsten metals or their compounds, providing combined hydrogenation and cracking activity.
[C10G 49/06] containing platinum group metals or compounds thereof -- The C10G 49/06 subclass covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils with hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds where the catalyst contains platinum-group metals or their compounds. It pertains to catalytic hydrogenation or hydrocracking operations that are not covered by other, more specific groups within the cracking of hydrocarbon oils. This classification is used for petroleum-industry methods that rely on the activity of platinum-group metal catalysts.
[C10G 49/08] containing crystalline alumino-silicates, e.g. molecular sieves -- C10G 49/08 covers hydrocarbon-oil treatment processes that employ crystalline alumino-silicates, such as molecular sieves, as catalysts or adsorbents in hydrogen-rich environments. It falls under cracking of hydrocarbon oils where hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds are used, but the method is not specified in any other subgroup. This classification is used for petroleum-related refining steps.
[C10G 49/10] with moving solid particles -- C10G 49/10 covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils with hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds in the presence of moving solid particles, such as in fluidised-bed reactors. It is a sub-group of cracking hydrocarbon oils within the petroleum, gas or coke industry classification.
[C10G 49/12] suspended in the oil, e.g. slurries -- C10G 49/12 covers processes for hydro-treating or hydro-cracking hydrocarbon oils in which the active ingredient, such as a catalyst or additive, is kept suspended in the oil as a slurry rather than being fixed on a support. The classification applies when hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds are used and the suspension is not otherwise defined by a single group. It relates to petroleum refining operations employing slurry-based treatment.
[C10G 49/14] according to the "moving-bed" technique -- The C10G 49/14 subclass covers the "moving-bed" technique for treating hydrocarbon oils, wherein the oil contacts a moving solid catalyst or adsorbent while hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds are present. This method is used in cracking or upgrading operations that are not otherwise defined in the preceding groups.
[C10G 49/16] according to the "fluidised-bed" technique -- C10G 49/16 relates to the treatment of hydrocarbon oils with hydrogen or hydrogen-generating agents using a fluidised-bed process, where the specific conditions are not covered by any other subgroup. It falls under the petroleum, gas or coke industries branch of the chemistry classification, specifically in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 49/18] in the presence of hydrogen-generating compounds, e.g. ammonia, water, hydrogen sulfide -- This subclass covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils in which hydrogen is supplied in situ by compounds that generate hydrogen under reaction conditions, such as ammonia, water or hydrogen sulfide, rather than by adding external hydrogen gas. It falls under the broader group dealing with hydrocarbon-oil treatment with hydrogen-producing agents.
[C10G 49/20] Organic compounds -- The C10G 49/20 subclass concerns organic compounds employed in the hydrogen-assisted cracking of hydrocarbon oils, such as in petroleum refining. It covers additives, catalysts or reagents used to treat oil streams in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen-generating agents where the specific compound is not captured by any other more specific subclass.
[C10G 49/22] Separation of effluents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Treatment in the presence of hydrogen (refining, cracking, general) comprising separation of the effluents.
[C10G 49/24] Starting-up hydrotreatment operations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Start-up treatment in the presence of hydrogen (refining, cracking, general).
[C10G 49/26] Controlling or regulating -- The C10G 49/26 subclass covers methods and devices for controlling or regulating the catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils when hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds are present, addressing process parameters such as temperature, pressure, feed rate or catalyst activity that are not covered by other specific groups. It pertains to petroleum refining operations that improve product quality or conversion efficiency through such regulation.
[C10G 5/00] Recovery of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from gases, e.g. natural gas -- C10G 5/00 covers processes for extracting liquid hydrocarbon mixtures, such as condensates or condensable gases, from gaseous streams like natural gas. It includes methods that separate and recover these liquids during or after gas production, typically using cooling, compression, or absorption techniques.
[C10G 50/00] Production of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from lower carbon number hydrocarbons, e.g. by oligomerisation -- The CPC subclass C10G 50/00 covers processes for making liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from lower-carbon-number feedstocks, primarily through oligomerisation or related reactions. It falls under the chemistry of petroleum and gas industries, specifically the cracking of hydrocarbon oils to generate higher-molecular-weight liquids.
[C10G 50/02] of hydrocarbon oils for lubricating purposes -- C10G 50/02 covers the production of hydrocarbon oils intended for lubricating purposes, typically obtained by oligomerising lower-carbon-number hydrocarbons into liquid mixtures suitable as lubricants.
[C10G 5/02] with solid adsorbents -- The C10G 5/02 subclass concerns processes for recovering liquid hydrocarbon mixtures, such as natural-gas liquids, from gaseous streams. It specifically addresses methods that employ solid adsorbent materials to separate and collect the liquid hydrocarbons. This falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and hydrocarbon cracking.
[C10G 5/04] with liquid absorbents -- C10G 5/04 covers processes for recovering liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from gas streams, such as natural gas, by using liquid absorbents. It falls within the chemistry field, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries, under cracking of hydrocarbon oils and the recovery of liquids from gases. The classification addresses techniques where a liquid phase selectively absorbs the desired hydrocarbons from the gas phase.
[C10G 5/06] by cooling or compressing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery of liquid hydrocarbon mixtures from gases by cooling or compressing, e.g. straight condensation cooling
[C10G 51/00] Multi-step processes -- C10G 51/00 covers multi-step processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils, where the feedstock undergoes two or more distinct reaction stages to achieve desired product distributions. The classification includes sequences such as primary thermal cracking followed by catalytic treatment, or successive catalytic cracking steps, aimed at improving conversion, selectivity or product quality in petroleum refining.
[C10G 51/02] plural serial stages only -- C10G 51/02 relates to hydrocarbon cracking carried out in a series of separate reactors each performing the same operation, with the product of one stage fed directly to the next without interstage mixing or blending, i.e., plural serial stages only.
[C10G 51/023] only thermal cracking steps -- This subclass covers processes in petroleum refining where hydrocarbon oils are cracked solely by thermal means, without catalytic assistance. It includes multi-step cracking sequences in which each step relies on high temperature to break the carbon-carbon bonds. The focus is on purely thermal cracking operations within the broader cracking of hydrocarbon oils field.
[C10G 51/026] only catalytic cracking steps -- This subclass covers multi-step processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils where the only unit operations are catalytic cracking steps, excluding any preceding or following non-catalytic operations. It falls under C10G 51, which addresses catalytic cracking of heavy oils, and specifically limits the process to sequences composed solely of catalytic reactors.
[C10G 51/04] including only thermal and catalytic cracking steps -- C10G 51/04 classifies multi-step processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils that consist solely of thermal and catalytic cracking stages, without any other types of conversion steps. It is used to index methods where the sequence of operations involves only these two cracking mechanisms.
[C10G 51/06] plural parallel stages only -- C10G 51/06 denotes multi-step cracking processes in which several identical reactors operate in parallel rather than in series. It covers the use of plural, simultaneous stages for breaking down hydrocarbon oils in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10G 53/00] Treatment of hydrocarbon oils, in the absence of hydrogen, by two or more refining processes -- C10G 53/00 covers methods for treating hydrocarbon oils without using hydrogen, where the treatment combines two or more refining processes such as thermal, catalytic, or mechanical steps, to improve oil quality or recover valuable fractions. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 53/02] plural serial stages only -- C10G 53/02 covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen that use two or more refining steps arranged in a series, where the same unit operates repeatedly in a plural serial configuration. It applies to multi-stage cracking operations that are not simultaneous.
[C10G 53/04] including at least one extraction step -- This subclass covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils in petroleum refining where the oil undergoes two or more refining operations, such as cracking, without the addition of hydrogen, and includes at least one extraction step to separate desired fractions or contaminants.
[C10G 53/06] including only extraction steps, e.g. deasphalting by solvent treatment followed by extraction of aromatics -- C10G 53/06 concerns the treatment of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by employing two or more refining stages, limited to extraction operations. It includes methods such as solvent deasphalting followed by the extraction of aromatics.
[C10G 53/08] including at least one sorption step -- A non-hydrogenative refining method for hydrocarbon oils that combines two or more processing stages, one of which uses a sorption operation such as adsorption or absorption to modify the oil or remove undesired components.
[C10G 53/10] including at least one acid-treatment step -- C10G 53/10 covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen that employ two or more refining operations, where at least one of the steps involves an acid treatment. This subcategory falls under cracking of hydrocarbon oils within the petroleum and gas industry and details combined refining methods that include acid-mediated reactions.
[C10G 53/12] including at least one alkaline treatment step -- C10G 53/12 covers the treatment of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by employing two or more refining operations, where at least one of the steps involves an alkaline process such as caustic washing, neutralisation or saponification. It is used in petroleum, gas or coke refining to modify oil properties after cracking.
[C10G 53/14] including at least one oxidation step -- This CPC subclass covers the treatment of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by applying two or more refining operations, where at least one of the steps is an oxidation stage. It is situated under the broader category of cracking hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 53/16] plural parallel stages only -- C10G 53/16 refers to the use of multiple refining steps arranged in parallel, rather than in series, for treating hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen. It covers processes where two or more cracking operations are conducted simultaneously on the same feedstock, each operating in a separate parallel stage. This classification is applied to petroleum refining methods that employ parallel cracking stages.
[C10G 55/00] Treatment of hydrocarbon oils, in the absence of hydrogen, by at least one refining process and at least one cracking process -- The C10G 55/00 entry covers methods for treating hydrocarbon oils without using hydrogen, combining at least one refining step with at least one cracking step. It pertains to petroleum and gas processing, specifically to cracking of hydrocarbon oils to improve product quality or yield. This classification falls under the broader chemistry category for petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 55/02] plural serial stages only -- C10G 55/02 covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen that employ at least one refining step followed by one cracking step arranged in multiple serial stages. The classification is used for technologies where the oil passes through a series of cracking reactors or units, each operating after the previous one, to progressively break down the feedstock. It falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, specifically cracking hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 55/04] including at least one thermal cracking step -- The CPC subclass C10G 55/04 covers methods for treating hydrocarbon oils without using hydrogen that involve at least one refining operation together with at least one thermal cracking step, combining processes such as distillation, desulfurisation or deasphalting with thermal cracking of the oil.
[C10G 55/06] including at least one catalytic cracking step -- C10G 55/06 covers the treatment of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen that incorporates at least one catalytic cracking step, usually as part of a refining operation that combines cracking with other processes.
[C10G 55/08] plural parallel stages only -- C10G 55/08 covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen that employ at least one refining step together with a cracking step arranged in multiple parallel stages. The classification is used when the cracking operations are carried out simultaneously in separate lines rather than sequentially, allowing concurrent processing of the feedstock. This entry falls under the broader category of petroleum refining and cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 57/00] Treatment of hydrocarbon oils, in the absence of hydrogen, by at least one cracking process or refining process and at least one other conversion process -- C10G 57/00 covers processes that treat hydrocarbon oils without adding hydrogen, combining at least one cracking or refining operation with another conversion step such as isomerisation, alkylation or dehydrogenation. These methods modify oil composition for fuels or petrochemical feedstocks while avoiding hydrogen consumption.
[C10G 57/005] with alkylation -- This subclass covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen that combine a cracking or refining step with an alkylation step. It is used for converting cracked oil fractions by adding alkyl groups to improve fuel properties. The code C10G 57/005 falls under the CPC group for petroleum cracking and other conversion processes.
[C10G 57/02] with polymerisation -- C10G 57/02 covers processes that treat hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by using a cracking or refining step together with an additional conversion, specifically where polymerisation is employed as the secondary operation. This subclass falls under the chemistry area for petroleum, gas or coke industries and relates to cracking hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 59/00] Treatment of naphtha by two or more reforming processes only or by at least one reforming process and at least one process which does not substantially change the boiling range of the naphtha -- C10G 59/00 covers processes that treat naphtha using multiple reforming steps, either solely by two or more reforming operations or by combining at least one reforming step with another operation that leaves the naphtha's boiling range essentially unchanged. The classification targets methods that improve octane or product quality without substantially altering the feed's distillation profile.
[C10G 59/02] plural serial stages only -- C10G 59/02 covers methods for treating naphtha that use more than one reforming step arranged in series, without combining them with processes that substantially alter the naphtha's boiling range. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy for petroleum and gas industries, specifically cracking hydrocarbon oils and naphtha reforming. This subclass is applied when only plural serial reforming stages are employed.
[C10G 59/04] including at least one catalytic and at least one non-catalytic reforming step -- The CPC subclass C10G 59/04 covers treatment of naphtha in which at least one catalytic reforming step is combined with at least one non-catalytic reforming step, or with another operation that does not substantially alter the naphtha's boiling range. It is situated under cracking hydrocarbons within the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 59/06] plural parallel stages only -- C10G 59/06 covers processes that treat naphtha using two or more reforming units arranged in parallel, where the streams are processed simultaneously rather than sequentially, and may include additional operations that do not significantly alter the boiling range.
[C10G 61/00] Treatment of naphtha by at least one reforming process and at least one process of refining in the absence of hydrogen -- The C10G 61/00 class covers methods for treating naphtha that combine at least one reforming operation with at least one refining step performed without added hydrogen. It encompasses integrated processes where catalytic reforming or other hydrogen-dependent reactions are omitted, and the naphtha is upgraded through thermal, oxidative, or other hydrogen-free refining techniques. This classification falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas, and cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 61/02] plural serial stages only -- The subclass C10G 61/02 covers processes that treat naphtha by carrying out at least one reforming operation and at least one refining operation in a sequence of serial stages, all performed without the presence of hydrogen. It applies to multi-stage configurations used in petroleum cracking and upgrading.
[C10G 61/04] the refining step being an extraction -- This subclass covers processes where, after reforming naphtha without hydrogen, the subsequent refining stage consists of an extraction step to separate valuable components. It belongs to the C10G group dealing with cracking hydrocarbon oils, specifically the treatment of naphtha by at least one reforming process followed by refining without hydrogen.
[C10G 61/06] the refining step being a sorption process -- C10G 61/06 covers petroleum-refining methods where, after at least one reforming step, the naphtha is processed without hydrogen by using a sorption technique-such as adsorption, absorption or ion-exchange-to separate, purify or upgrade the hydrocarbon fractions. This subclass falls under cracking hydrocarbon oils in the chemistry hierarchy.
[C10G 61/08] plural parallel stages only -- C10G 61/08 covers processes that treat naphtha using at least one reforming step and an additional refining step without hydrogen, where the operational units are arranged in parallel rather than in series. The classification is applied when multiple identical or similar stages run simultaneously to achieve the desired conversion, reflecting a parallel-stage configuration in petroleum refining.
[C10G 61/10] processes also including other conversion steps -- C10G 61/10 covers methods for treating naphtha that combine at least one reforming step with at least one refining operation carried out without hydrogen, and that may incorporate further conversion stages such as isomerisation, alkylation or hydrocracking. It is a subclass of cracking hydrocarbon oils within the chemistry of petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 63/00] Treatment of naphtha by at least one reforming process and at least one other conversion process -- C10G 63/00 concerns the processing of naphtha where at least one catalytic reforming operation is performed together with one or more other conversion steps such as cracking, isomerisation, alkylation or hydro-treating, in order to modify the hydrocarbon composition and improve the quality of the resulting fuels or petrochemical products.
[C10G 63/02] plural serial stages only -- The C10G 63/02 entry covers processes that treat naphtha by employing at least one reforming step together with another conversion step, arranged in successive (serial) stages rather than parallel. It applies to multistage configurations where the reforming and the additional conversion operations are performed one after another within the same plant.
[C10G 63/04] including at least one cracking step -- The CPC subclass C10G 63/04 covers processes for treating naphtha that combine at least one reforming operation with another conversion step, such as a cracking stage. It includes methods where a cracking step is integrated into the overall treatment sequence for hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 63/06] plural parallel stages only -- C10G 63/06 designates processes for treating naphtha in which several parallel reactors are employed, each carrying out at least one reforming operation together with another conversion step such as cracking or isomerisation. The parallel arrangement allows simultaneous processing to improve efficiency or product distribution. This subclass falls under the broader category of petroleum refining and hydrocarbon cracking.
[C10G 63/08] including at least one cracking step -- The CPC subclass C10G 63/08 covers processes that treat naphtha by combining at least one reforming step with at least one additional conversion step, typically a cracking operation, to improve product yields or qualities. It falls within the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas refining, and cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 65/00] Treatment of hydrocarbon oils by two or more hydrotreatment processes only -- C10G 65/00 covers methods for processing hydrocarbon oils by two-or-more hydrotreatment steps in sequence, such as combining hydrodesulfurization, hydrodenitrogenation, hydrodeoxygenation or hydrogenation to improve product quality. The approach is applied after or instead of cracking to remove impurities and adjust properties.
[C10G 65/02] plural serial stages only -- This CPC subclass C10G 65/02 covers the treatment of hydrocarbon oils by two or more hydrotreatment steps arranged in series, where the stages operate consecutively without intermediate blending or separation. It applies to refinery processes that use plural serial stages only.
[C10G 65/04] including only refining steps -- It refers to processes that treat hydrocarbon oils using at least two hydrotreatment operations in a refinery, such as successive hydrodesulfurization and hydrocracking, and does not include any non-hydrotreatment steps.
[C10G 65/043] at least one step being a change in the structural skeleton -- This subclass covers processes where hydrocarbon oil undergoes two or more hydrotreatment steps and includes at least one operation that changes the molecular skeleton of the feedstock, such as cracking, isomerisation or skeletal rearrangement.
[C10G 65/046] at least one step being an aromatisation step -- The code C10G 65/046 classifies processes for treating cracked hydrocarbon oils that incorporate at least one aromatisation step together with other hydrotreatment operations. It covers refining methods where aromatisation is combined with additional treatment stages to modify the composition and properties of the oil.
[C10G 65/06] at least one step being a selective hydrogenation of the diolefins -- This CPC subcategory covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils that involve two or more hydrotreatment steps, where at least one step is a selective hydrogenation of diolefins, the compounds containing two carbon-carbon double bonds. The selective hydrogenation reduces unsaturation, improving stability and preventing gum formation in downstream products.
[C10G 65/08] at least one step being a hydrogenation of the aromatic hydrocarbons -- This CPC subclass covers the treatment of cracked hydrocarbon oils using two or more hydrotreatment stages, where at least one stage is a hydrogenation of aromatic hydrocarbons to saturate the ring structures and improve fuel quality.
[C10G 65/10] including only cracking steps -- C10G 65/10 covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils in which the operation consists solely of one or more cracking steps, without any additional hydrotreatment such as hydrodesulfurisation or hydrodenitrogenation. The classification is used for catalytic cracking, hydrocracking or similar methods where the feed is only subjected to cracking to break large molecules into smaller ones.
[C10G 65/12] including cracking steps and other hydrotreatment steps -- C10G 65/12 covers processes that treat hydrocarbon oils through a combination of at least two hydrotreatment operations, such as hydrodesulfurisation, hydrodenitrogenation, or hydrogenation, together with one or more cracking steps. The classification is used for methods that integrate these treatments to improve oil quality in petroleum refining.
[C10G 65/14] plural parallel stages only -- The code C10G 65/14 covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils that use two or more hydrotreatment operations arranged in parallel rather than in series. It specifies that only plural parallel stages are employed, without any sequential or single-stage configurations. This subclass falls under cracking hydrocarbon oils within the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10G 65/16] including only refining steps -- C10G 65/16 covers refinery methods in which hydrocarbon oils undergo two or more consecutive hydrotreatment steps, such as repeated hydrodesulfurization, hydrodenitrogenation or hydrocracking, without any additional processing stages. It falls under the cracking hydrocarbons oils section of the chemistry field, specifically within petroleum, gas or coke industry classifications.
[C10G 65/18] including only cracking steps -- C10G 65/18 covers processes in which hydrocarbon oils that have already undergone two or more hydrotreatment steps are further treated exclusively by cracking operations. It belongs to the chemistry section of the CPC, under petroleum refining, specifically the cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 67/00] Treatment of hydrocarbon oils by at least one hydrotreatment process and at least one process for refining in the absence of hydrogen only -- The subclass C10G 67/00 covers processes that treat hydrocarbon oils using at least one hydrotreatment step-such as hydrogenation, desulphurisation or denitrification-together with an additional refining operation that is carried out without the use of hydrogen, for example catalytic cracking or thermal reforming.
[C10G 67/02] plural serial stages only -- C10G 67/02 relates to methods for treating hydrocarbon oils where the operation consists of two or more identical or different treatment stages arranged in series, without intervening separate processes. The classification is used for patents describing sequential hydrotreatment or refining steps performed one after another, rather than a single-stage operation.
[C10G 67/04] including solvent extraction as the refining step in the absence of hydrogen -- Code C10G 67/04 covers methods for refining hydrocarbon oils where solvent extraction is used as a refining step performed without hydrogen. It falls under treatments that combine at least one hydrotreatment operation with a non-hydrogen refining process in the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10G 67/0409] Extraction of unsaturated hydrocarbons -- The C10G 67/0409 subclass covers processes that extract unsaturated hydrocarbons from hydrocarbon oils during treatment operations that combine at least one hydrotreatment step with refining steps carried out without hydrogen. It applies to methods used in petroleum, gas or coke industries for upgrading cracked oils where the removal of olefins or other unsaturated compounds is a required sub-operation.
[C10G 67/0418] The hydrotreatment being a hydrorefining -- This CPC subclass addresses hydrorefining, where a hydrocarbon oil undergoes hydrotreatment together with a refining step performed without hydrogen. It falls under cracking hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum and gas industry and specifies combined processes that treat the oil in a single operation.
[C10G 67/0427] The hydrotreatment being a selective hydrogenation of diolefins or acetylenes -- C10G 67/0427 relates to the hydrotreatment step in refining hydrocarbon oils where the process selectively hydrogenates diolefins or acetylenes. The aim is to reduce unsaturation and improve oil stability by converting these reactive compounds into saturated hydrocarbons, often as part of a broader cracking or treatment sequence that may also include non-hydrogen refining steps.
[C10G 67/0436] The hydrotreatment being an aromatic saturation -- The subclass C10G 67/0436 covers processes in which hydrocarbon oils undergo hydrotreatment specifically to saturate aromatic compounds, often using catalytic hydrogenation while the overall refining sequence is conducted without the addition of hydrogen. It is situated under the broader category of cracking hydrocarbon oils within petroleum and gas industry classifications.
[C10G 67/0445] The hydrotreatment being a hydrocracking -- The code C10G 67/0445 designates processes in which hydrocracking is employed as the hydrotreatment step for refining hydrocarbon oils. It covers catalytic cracking of heavy oil fractions in the presence of hydrogen, converting them to lighter, more valuable products while simultaneously removing impurities.
[C10G 67/0454] Solvent desasphalting -- Solvent desasphalting separates the heavy, asphalt-like fraction from crude or residuum by mixing it with a light hydrocarbon solvent, allowing the asphaltic material to precipitate while the remaining deasphalted oil stays dissolved. The deasphalted oil can then undergo further hydrotreatment or refining without additional hydrogen, improving feed quality for downstream processes.
[C10G 67/0463] The hydrotreatment being a hydrorefining -- C10G 67/0463 covers processes in which a hydrorefining hydrotreatment is applied to hydrocarbon oils as part of a combined sequence that also includes at least one refining operation performed without hydrogen. The classification is used for technologies that integrate hydrorefining with non-hydrogen catalytic cracking, distillation or other hydrogen-free treatments. It falls under the broader categories of petroleum processing and cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 67/0472] The hydrotreatment being a selective hydrogenation of diolefines or acetylenes -- A process for treating hydrocarbon oils in which the hydrotreatment step selectively hydrogenates diolefins or acetylenes, without employing bulk hydrogenation. This falls under the broader category of cracking hydrocarbon oils and is used in petroleum, gas or coke industry refining operations.
[C10G 67/0481] The hydrotreatment being an aromatics saturation -- The entry covers processes that saturate aromatic compounds in hydrocarbon oils through hydrotreatment, typically using hydrogen to convert aromatics into saturated hydrocarbons, and may be combined with refining steps that operate without hydrogen. It is classified under treatments of cracked oils for petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 67/049] The hydrotreatment being a hydrocracking -- C10G 67/049 covers processes in which the hydrotreatment of hydrocarbon oils is performed by hydrocracking, i.e., catalytic cracking in the presence of hydrogen to break large molecules while simultaneously saturating them. The classification applies to combined refining routes where at least one hydrotreatment step uses hydrocracking together with other refining operations that may proceed without added hydrogen.
[C10G 67/06] including a sorption process as the refining step in the absence of hydrogen -- C10G 67/06 relates to methods for treating hydrocarbon oils where the refining step is carried out without hydrogen and incorporates a sorption process as part of the treatment. It is classified under cracking hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum, gas or coke industries, covering combined hydrotreatment and non-hydrogen refining operations.
[C10G 67/08] including acid treatment as the refining step in the absence of hydrogen -- This subclass covers processes that refine hydrocarbon oils without using hydrogen, specifically incorporating an acid treatment as the refining step. It falls within non-hydrogen hydrotreatment and refining methods for cracked oils in the chemistry and petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 67/10] including alkaline treatment as the refining step in the absence of hydrogen
[C10G 67/12] including oxidation as the refining step in the absence of hydrogen -- C10G 67/12 relates to refining hydrocarbon oils without the use of hydrogen, where the refining stage incorporates oxidation as the key step and is accompanied by at least one hydrotreatment operation. It is situated in the chemistry field for petroleum, gas or coke industries under the cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 67/14] including at least two different refining steps in the absence of hydrogen -- C10G 67/14 covers methods for treating hydrocarbon oils where at least two different refining operations are carried out without using hydrogen. The sequence may combine processes such as thermal cracking, catalytic cracking, visbreaking or other hydrotreatment-free steps to improve oil quality while avoiding hydrogen addition.
[C10G 67/16] plural parallel stages only -- The subclass C10G 67/16 covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils that combine at least one hydrotreatment step with at least one refining step performed without hydrogen, and that are carried out in several parallel stages rather than in a single or series sequence. It focuses on the configuration of plural, simultaneous processing units.
[C10G 69/00] Treatment of hydrocarbon oils by at least one hydrotreatment process and at least one other conversion process -- C10G 69/00 covers methods that apply a hydrotreatment step such as hydrogenation, desulfurization or denitrogenation to hydrocarbon oils together with another conversion operation like cracking, reforming or isomerisation. The combined processes aim to improve fuel quality, remove contaminants and alter molecular structure within a single treatment scheme.
[C10G 69/02] plural serial stages only -- The CPC code C10G 69/02 concerns hydrocarbon-oil treatment processes that combine at least one hydrotreatment step with at least one additional conversion step, and where these steps are carried out in multiple serial stages. It applies specifically to configurations that use plural serial stages only, rather than parallel or single-stage arrangements. This sub-class falls under cracking hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10G 69/04] including at least one step of catalytic cracking in the absence of hydrogen -- C10G 69/04 covers processes that treat hydrocarbon oils using at least one hydrotreatment step together with another conversion step, specifically when a catalytic cracking operation is performed without the presence of hydrogen. This sub-class falls under chemistry → petroleum, gas or coke industries → cracking hydrocarbon oils. It defines the combination of hydrotreatment and hydrogen-free catalytic cracking in a single treatment scheme.
[C10G 69/06] including at least one step of thermal cracking in the absence of hydrogen -- The subclass C10G 69/06 includes refining methods that combine a hydrotreatment step with an additional conversion step, specifically a thermal cracking operation carried out without hydrogen. It addresses processes for treating hydrocarbon oils where at least one hydrogen-free thermal cracking stage is integrated with other treatment actions.
[C10G 69/08] including at least one step of reforming naphtha -- C10G 69/08 covers methods for treating hydrocarbon oils in which the feed includes naphtha that undergoes at least one reforming step, together with hydrotreatment and another conversion operation such as cracking or isomerisation. The classification falls under the chemistry hierarchy for petroleum refining processes.
[C10G 69/10] hydrocracking of higher boiling fractions into naphtha and reforming the naphtha obtained -- C10G 69/10 covers the combined process of hydrocracking heavier boiling hydrocarbon fractions to produce naphtha, followed by catalytic reforming of that naphtha to increase its octane rating. It involves at least one hydrotreatment step and an additional conversion step to upgrade the oil.
[C10G 69/12] including at least one polymerisation or alkylation step -- This subgroup covers processes that combine a hydrotreatment operation such as hydrodesulfurisation with an additional conversion step like polymerisation or alkylation to modify hydrocarbon oils. It is used for producing higher-value products from petroleum streams, integrating at least one polymerisation or alkylation stage after the hydrotreatment.
[C10G 69/123] alkylation -- C10G 69/123 classifies methods that treat hydrocarbon oils with a hydrotreatment operation together with another conversion step, namely alkylation, to modify the oil's molecular structure, improve its octane rating and enhance fuel quality. It falls under chemistry, petroleum cracking and oil-treatment categories.
[C10G 69/126] polymerisation, e.g. oligomerisation -- C10G 69/126 covers processes that convert cracked hydrocarbon oils into higher-molecular-weight products by polymerisation or oligomerisation, typically as part of a hydrotreatment or other conversion sequence. The subclass addresses the chemical linking of short-chain hydrocarbons to form longer chains in petroleum refining operations.
[C10G 69/14] plural parallel stages only -- The C10G 69/14 subclass covers processes for treating hydrocarbon oils that employ two or more parallel processing stages, where each stage operates simultaneously rather than in sequence. It applies to combined hydrotreatment and other conversion steps performed in a parallel configuration, distinguishing it from serial-stage arrangements. This classification falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 7/00] Distillation of hydrocarbon oils -- The C10G 7/00 class concerns the distillation of hydrocarbon oils, describing methods that separate crude or refined petroleum fractions by heating the oil to vaporise its components and then condensing the vapours to obtain distinct products such as gasoline, kerosene or lubricating oils. This includes continuous and batch column operations, reflux control, and related equipment used in petroleum, gas or coke processing.
[C10G 70/00] Working-up undefined normally gaseous mixtures obtained by processes covered by groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  working-up undefined normally gaseous mixture obtained by processes covered by C10G9/00 , C10G11/00 , C10G15/00 , C10G47/00 and C10G51/00 .  working up implies improvement of the material, it's a kind of processing
[C10G 70/002] by forming adducts or complexes -- C10G 70/002 covers the treatment of undefined gaseous streams produced in hydrocarbon cracking by forming adducts or complexes with the mixture components, enabling their separation, recovery or further processing. This method is a specific working-up step within petroleum cracking operations.
[C10G 70/004] with solutions of copper salts -- C10G 70/004 covers processes for treating undefined gaseous products obtained from petroleum cracking by contacting them with aqueous or alcoholic solutions of copper salts, typically to remove sulfur or nitrogen compounds and improve gas quality. The method is used in the working-up stage of refinery operations to help meet specifications for downstream processing.
[C10G 70/006] with the use of acids or sulfur oxides -- C10G 70/006 covers processes for treating undefined gaseous mixtures produced in hydrocarbon oil cracking by contacting them with acids or sulfur oxides to effect separation, purification or conversion.

[C10G 70/02] by hydrogenation -- C10G 70/02 covers the treatment of undefined gaseous mixtures obtained from hydrocarbon oil cracking by hydrogenation, i.e., reacting the gases with hydrogen to convert unsaturated components into saturated ones. This step is used to remove impurities or to adjust the composition of the gas stream.
[C10G 7/003] distillation of lubricating oils -- The C10G 7/003 entry covers processes and equipment for the distillation of lubricating oils, a sub-field of hydrocarbon oil cracking. This classification lies under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 70/04] by physical processes -- C10G 70/04 covers the physical treatment of undefined gaseous reaction mixtures that result from hydrocarbon cracking processes, such as separation, compression, cooling, or adsorption, to recover useful products. The subclass falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and the cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 70/041] by distillation -- The CPC subclass C10G 70/041 covers the working-up of normally gaseous mixtures produced in petroleum-cracking processes, specifically when the mixture is treated by distillation to separate, recover or purify its hydrocarbon components. This includes any distillation step applied after cracking to obtain liquid fractions or refined products.
[C10G 70/042] with the use of auxiliary compounds -- C10G 70/042 relates to the working-up of undefined gaseous mixtures produced in hydrocarbon-oil cracking processes, specifically when auxiliary compounds are employed. The auxiliary agents are added to modify the composition, aid separation or improve the quality of the resulting gases.
[C10G 70/043] by fractional condensation -- A process for treating the undefined, typically gaseous products generated in hydrocarbon cracking operations by cooling the mixture so that components condense at different temperatures, allowing their separation through fractional condensation. This method is used to recover valuable liquids from the gas stream produced in petroleum, gas or coke industry cracking processes.
[C10G 70/044] by crystallisation -- C10G 70/044 pertains to the processing of normally gaseous mixtures produced in hydrocarbon cracking, where the mixtures are worked up by crystallisation to separate or recover components.
[C10G 70/045] using membranes, e.g. selective permeation -- Using membranes, such as selective permeation, to treat or purify gaseous mixtures obtained from hydrocarbon-oil cracking processes; the method separates desired components from the undefined gas stream.
[C10G 70/046] by adsorption, i.e. with the use of solids -- The subgroup C10G 70/046 covers working-up of undefined normally gaseous mixtures produced in petroleum cracking processes by means of adsorption, i.e., using solid adsorbents to separate or treat the gases. It is part of the hierarchy for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically under cracking of hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 70/047] by molecular sieve technique -- C10G 70/047 covers the working-up of undefined gaseous mixtures, typically obtained from cracking or related hydrocarbon processes, using a molecular-sieve technique to separate, adsorb or purify the components. The method employs porous inorganic materials such as zeolites to selectively trap certain gases while allowing others to pass, improving product recovery and stream cleanliness. This subclass falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and cracking hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 70/048] by liquid-liquid extraction -- C10G 70/048 covers the working-up of undefined gaseous mixtures produced by hydrocarbon cracking by means of liquid-liquid extraction. It falls under the Chemistry > Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries > Cracking Hydrocarbon Oils hierarchy and specifies the use of a liquid phase to selectively dissolve and separate target compounds from the gas stream.

This falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, specifically cracking hydrocarbon oils and their distillation.
[C10G 7/06] Vacuum distillation -- C10G 7/06 covers vacuum distillation, a method used after cracking hydrocarbon oils to separate heavy fractions by heating the oil under reduced pressure. The lower pressure lowers boiling points, allowing the removal of high-boiling components without thermal degradation and increasing the yield of lighter, marketable products.
[C10G 7/08] Azeotropic or extractive distillation -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Refining of hydrocarbon oils, in the absence of hydrogen, by extraction with selective solvents -> C10G21/00

[C10G 71/00] Treatment by methods not otherwise provided for of hydrocarbon oils or fatty oils for lubricating purposes -- C10G 71/00 addresses processes that improve the lubricating performance of hydrocarbon or fatty oils by applying treatment methods that are not otherwise listed in the classification. [C10G 73/02] Recovery of petroleum waxes from hydrocarbon oils -- A process for extracting and purifying mineral waxes, such as montan wax, from hydrocarbon oil streams during petroleum refining; it details methods of recovering waxes from cracked oils within the petroleum, gas and coke industry.

[C10G 73/06] with the use of solvents -- C10G 73/06 covers processes for recovering or refining mineral waxes, such as montan wax, from cracked hydrocarbon oils that use solvents to extract, purify or separate the waxes during the refining stage. [C10G 73/20] containing phosphorus -- The CPC subclass C10G 73/20 covers methods and products for recovering or refining mineral waxes, such as montan wax, in which phosphorus is present, either as a constituent of the wax or as a phosphorus-containing additive used during the cracking or purification steps.
[C10G 73/22] Mixtures or organic compounds -- The C10G 73/22 class concerns mixtures or organic compounds employed in the recovery or refining of mineral waxes, such as montan wax, from cracked hydrocarbon oils. [C10G 73/24] by formation of adducts -- This subclass covers methods of recovering or refining mineral waxes such as montan wax in which the wax is converted into an adduct- typically a chemically bound complex with another compound to facilitate separation or purification. The adduct formation may involve reacting the wax with reagents that modify its physical or chemical properties, allowing easier extraction or further processing.
[C10G 73/26] by flotation -- The C10G 73/26 subclass covers processes for recovering or refining mineral waxes, such as montan wax, from cracked hydrocarbon oils by using flotation methods. It includes techniques where wax particles are separated from oil streams through the addition of a flotation aid in a liquid medium, allowing the wax to rise and be collected.
