[C10G 73/28] by centrifugal force -- C10G 73/28 covers processes that use centrifugal force to separate and recover mineral waxes, such as montan wax, from hydrocarbon streams during cracking or refining operations. The classification includes centrifugation steps that isolate wax fractions from oils or other liquids.
[C10G 73/30] with electric means -- C10G 73/30 classifies processes for recovering or refining mineral waxes such as montan wax from cracked hydrocarbon oils that employ electric means, including electrical heating, plasma treatment or electro-chemical techniques. It falls under Chemistry → Petroleum, gas or coke industries → Cracking hydrocarbon oils.
[C10G 73/32] Methods of cooling during dewaxing -- C10G 73/32 relates to procedures for lowering the temperature of a hydrocarbon oil during the dewaxing stage used to recover mineral waxes such as montan wax. The methods control cooling of the oil or wax stream to promote wax crystallisation and separation while avoiding overheating or wax degradation.
[C10G 73/34] Controlling or regulating -- The CPC subclass C10G 73/34 lies within chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, cracking hydrocarbon oils and the recovery or refining of mineral waxes such as montan wax. It addresses methods, devices or control systems used to regulate the refining or recovery process of these mineral waxes.
[C10G 73/36] Recovery of petroleum waxes from other compositions containing oil in minor proportions, from concentrates or from residues -- The CPC subclass C10G 73/36 covers processes for extracting petroleum waxes, such as montan wax, from mixtures where oil is present only in minor amounts, including from concentrated streams or waste residues generated during oil refining. It pertains to the recovery of waxes as a by-product of petroleum processing.
[C10G 73/38] Chemical modification of petroleum -- C10G 73/38 covers processes that chemically modify petroleum, especially methods for recovering or refining mineral waxes such as montan wax within the cracking of hydrocarbon oils in the petroleum, gas or coke industries. It falls under the chemistry branch of petroleum refining.
[C10G 73/40] Physical treatment of waxes or modified waxes, e.g. granulation, dispersion, emulsion, irradiation -- C10G 73/40 relates to physical methods for treating waxes or modified waxes, such as granulation, dispersion, emulsification or irradiation, aimed at altering particle size, morphology or functional properties during the recovery and refining of mineral waxes from cracked hydrocarbon streams.
[C10G 73/42] Refining of petroleum waxes -- C10G 73/42 defines processes for refining petroleum-derived waxes, such as montan wax, after they have been recovered from crude oil or other petroleum streams. It covers steps that purify, decolorize, deoxygenate or otherwise improve the physical and chemical properties of these mineral waxes for commercial applications, and is situated under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, specifically cracking hydrocarbon oils and wax recovery.
[C10G 73/44] in the presence of hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds -- C10G 73/44 covers processes for recovering or refining mineral waxes, such as montan wax, from cracked hydrocarbon oils where hydrogen or hydrogen-generating compounds are employed. The presence of hydrogen aids the cracking reaction, improving wax yield and quality.
[C10G 75/00] Inhibiting corrosion or fouling in apparatus for treatment or conversion of hydrocarbon oils, in general -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Treatment of apparatus for treatment or conversion of hydrocarbons oils in general, against corrosion or fouling.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Inhibiting corrosion during distillation -> C10G7/10 - Preventing or removing incrustation -> C10G9/16
[C10G 75/02] by addition of corrosion inhibitors -- This subclass covers methods for preventing corrosion or fouling in equipment used to treat or convert cracked hydrocarbon oils by adding chemical corrosion inhibitors. It applies to general approaches within petroleum-refining processes where inhibitors are introduced to protect apparatus from damage.
[C10G 75/04] by addition of antifouling agents -- C10G 75/04 covers techniques for preventing corrosion or fouling in equipment used to treat or convert hydrocarbon oils, specifically during cracking processes, by introducing antifouling additives. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and hydrocarbon oil cracking.
[C10G 9/00] Cracking in the absence of hydrogen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dehydrogenation of parafins to olefins; dehydrogenation of naphthenes to aromatics.
[C10G 9/002] Cooling of cracked gases -- C10G 9/002 concerns the cooling of gases generated in hydrocarbon cracking performed without hydrogen. It includes techniques and equipment for lowering the temperature of cracked vapors to condense heavy components, recover liquids, or prepare the stream for further separation and treatment.
[C10G 9/005] Coking (in order to produce liquid products mainly) -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Coking, in the absence of hydrogen, of hydrocarbon oils to produce liquid products.
[C10G 9/007] Visbreaking -- Visbreaking is a mild thermal cracking process applied to heavy hydrocarbon oils that reduces their viscosity and improves flow properties without the addition of hydrogen. It operates at lower temperatures and shorter residence times than severe cracking, producing lighter fractions while preserving a higher proportion of the original molecular structure.
[C10G 9/02] in retorts -- C10G 9/02 covers cracking of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen when the process is carried out in retorts, which are sealed, heated vessels used for thermal decomposition. The classification applies to petroleum-related operations that rely on high-temperature, non-catalytic cracking in batch or continuous retort systems.
[C10G 9/04] Retorts -- C10G 9/04 Retorts refers to the equipment - specifically retort vessels - used for thermal cracking of hydrocarbon oils without added hydrogen. These units operate at high temperatures to break large molecules into smaller fractions in petroleum, gas or coke processing.
[C10G 9/06] by pressure distillation -- C10G 9/06 covers cracking of hydrocarbon oils without added hydrogen that is achieved through pressure distillation. The process uses high pressure to induce thermal cracking, separating heavier fractions into lighter products in petroleum refining. It falls under the broader class of petroleum and coke industry methods for cracking hydrocarbons.
[C10G 9/08] Apparatus therefor -- C10G 9/08 designates equipment used to crack hydrocarbon oils without the addition of hydrogen, a process employed in petroleum, gas or coke refining. The class falls under Chemistry → Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries → Cracking Hydrocarbon Oils → Cracking in the Absence of Hydrogen, and covers reactors, furnaces, and related installations that perform thermal or catalytic cracking under hydrogen-free conditions.
[C10G 9/12] Removing incrustation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Thermal non-catalytic cracking, in the absence of hydrogen, of hydrocarbon oils by pressure distillation characterised by the apparatus for removing incrustation, e.g corrosion
[C10G 9/14] in pipes or coils with or without auxiliary means, e.g. digesters, soaking drums, expansion means -- C10G 9/14 covers cracking of hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen carried out inside pipes, coils or similar vessels, optionally employing auxiliary equipment such as digesters, soaking drums or expansion means to control temperature or residence time.
[C10G 9/16] Preventing or removing incrustation -- C10G 9/16 covers methods and apparatus for preventing or removing incrustation-scale or deposit buildup-during catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon oils performed without hydrogen. It includes techniques such as cleaning, chemical treatment, or mechanical removal aimed at maintaining reactor efficiency and product quality. This subclass falls under cracking processes in the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10G 9/18] Apparatus -- This subclass covers equipment used to perform thermal or catalytic cracking of hydrocarbon feedstocks where no hydrogen is added. It includes reactors, furnaces, and supporting devices for non-hydrogenated cracking processes. Used in petroleum refining to break large molecules into lighter fractions.
[C10G 9/20] Tube furnaces -- C10G 9/20 Tube furnaces refers to the use of tubular heating devices for thermal cracking of hydrocarbon oils without added hydrogen. It includes designs where the feed passes through heated tubes to induce cracking, covering furnace construction, temperature control and related process equipment.
[C10G 9/203] chemical composition of the tubes -- C10G 9/203 concerns the chemical composition of tubes used in hydrocarbon cracking units that operate without hydrogen, such as visbreaking or steam-cracking reactors. It includes the materials, alloys and coatings formulated to resist the high temperatures, pressures and corrosive environments encountered in petroleum-refining cracking processes.
[C10G 9/206] controlling or regulating the tube furnaces -- The subclass concerns systems and methods for controlling tube-furnace operation in non-hydrogen cracking of hydrocarbon oils. It includes sensors, controllers and automation that regulate temperature, pressure and atmosphere to maintain consistent cracking performance and product quality.
[C10G 9/24] by heating with electrical means -- C10G 9/24 relates to the cracking of hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen in which the feedstock is heated by electrical means, such as resistance heating, induction or electric arcs, to achieve thermal decomposition. It is a subcategory of petroleum refining processes that use electrical energy to break down large hydrocarbon molecules into smaller, more useful fractions.
[C10G 9/26] with discontinuously preheated non-moving solid material, e.g. blast and run -- C10G 9/26 covers hydrocarbon cracking processes carried out without hydrogen where a solid medium is intermittently pre-heated and stays stationary during the reaction. The solid material, such as a blast or run charge, is heated in separate steps and then used to contact the hydrocarbon feed, promoting thermal cracking.
[C10G 9/28] with preheated moving solid material -- The subclass C10G 9/28 covers petroleum-oil cracking processes that operate without added hydrogen and employ a preheated solid material that moves through the reaction zone, such as heated catalyst particles or other solid media used to transfer heat to the hydrocarbon feed.
[C10G 9/30] according to the "moving bed" method -- C10G 9/30 pertains to petroleum‐industry processes that crack hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen using a moving-bed arrangement, where the solid catalyst is displaced relative to the feedstock. The subclass covers methods in which the catalyst moves through the reactor, such as fluidised-bed or mechanically stirred beds, to achieve thermal or catalytic cracking.
[C10G 9/32] according to the "fluidised-bed" technique -- The C10G 9/32 subclass covers petroleum refining processes that crack hydrocarbon oils without adding hydrogen, using a fluidised-bed reactor. In this method the feed contacts hot solid catalyst particles suspended in a gas stream, enabling thermal or catalytic cracking to produce lighter fractions such as gasoline and olefins.
[C10G 9/34] by direct contact with inert preheated fluids, e.g. with molten metals or salts -- A method of cracking hydrocarbon oils without hydrogen by directly contacting the feedstock with an inert, pre-heated fluid such as molten metal or molten salt, which supplies the required thermal energy for the cracking reaction.
[C10G 9/36] with heated gases or vapours -- **Glossary:** - Steam cracking: cracking in the presence of steam
[C10G 9/38] produced by partial combustion of the material to be cracked or by combustion of another hydrocarbon -- C10G 9/38 covers processes for cracking hydrocarbon oils in the absence of hydrogen where the feedstock is heated by partial combustion of the material itself or by burning a separate hydrocarbon. The method supplies the required energy through internal or external combustion rather than hydrogen-based reforming.
[C10G 9/40] by indirect contact with preheated fluid other than hot combustion gases -- C10G 9/40 covers a petroleum-refining process in which hydrocarbon oils are cracked without hydrogen by passing them indirectly past a preheated fluid that is not hot combustion gas, such as steam or heated oil, to transfer heat and promote thermal cracking. This method is used to produce lighter fractions while avoiding direct flame contact.
[C10G 9/42] by passing the material to be cracked in thin streams or as spray on or near continuously heated surfaces -- This subclass covers thermal cracking of hydrocarbons without hydrogen by feeding the material as a thin stream or spray onto or near continuously heated surfaces, allowing rapid heating and conversion of heavy oils into lighter fractions. It is applied in refining processes where direct contact with a hot surface promotes efficient cracking of the feedstock.
[C10G 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- C10G 99/00 is a residual group in the cracking of hydrocarbon oils subclass that captures any processes, apparatus, catalysts or conditions for petroleum or oil cracking that are not specifically covered by the other defined groups of this subclass. It serves as a catch-all for novel or uncommon cracking techniques that fall outside the existing detailed classifications.
[C10H] PRODUCTION OF ACETYLENE BY WET METHODS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and apparatus for production of acetylene by wet methods, including arrangements for water feed and carbide feed; high-pressure acetylene generators; details of acetylene generators, e.g. carbide cartridges, carbide compositions, safety devices, sludge removal.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Purification of acetylene -> C07C7/00 - Gaseous fuel compositions containing acetylene -> C10L3/02 - Absorbing compositions for acetylene -> C10L3/04 - Use of gas-solvents or gas-sorbents for acetylene in vessels -> F17C11/002  **Glossary:** - Wet methods for acetylene production: Traditionally acetylene is manufactured from calcium carbonate (limestone) and coal. The calcium carbonate is first converted into calcium oxide and the coal into coke, then the two are reacted together to form calcium carbide and carbon monoxide:CaO + 3C → CaC2 + COCalcium carbide (calcium acetylide) and water are then reacted by any of several methods to produce acetylene and calcium hydroxide, by a reaction discovered by Friedrich Wöhler in 1862.CaC2 + 2H2O → Ca(OH)2 + C2H2
[C10H 1/00] Acetylene gas generators with dropwise, gravity, non-automatic water feed -- A C10H 1/00 acetylene gas generator uses a dropwise, gravity-driven water feed that is not automatically regulated, supplying water to a wet reaction where calcium carbide reacts to produce acetylene. The design emphasizes simple, manual feeding to control gas generation rates in petroleum-related applications.
[C10H 1/02] Valves -- The C10H 1/02 subclass groups valves employed in acetylene gas generators that use a dropwise, gravity-fed, non-automatic water supply. These valves regulate the water-to-calcium carbide reaction, control gas output, and provide safety shut-off in the wet production of acetylene.
[C10H 1/04] Screw valves -- The subclass C10H 1/04 pertains to screw-type valves that regulate the gravity-driven, dropwise water feed in non-automatic acetylene gas generators, controlling water introduction to calcium carbide to produce acetylene in petroleum, gas or coke industry processes.
[C10H 1/06] Cocks
[C10H 1/08] Other means for controlling the water feed -- The code C10H 1/08 covers alternative techniques for regulating the water feed in wet-method acetylene generators that use dropwise, gravity or non-automatic systems. It includes mechanical, pressure-based or valve-controlled approaches that adjust water flow without relying on the standard automatic feed mechanisms.
[C10H 1/10] Water feed from above through a central or lateral pipe -- C10H 1/10 covers acetylene generators that introduce water from above through a central or lateral pipe, allowing drop-wise or gravity feed without automatic control. It pertains to wet-method acetylene production used in petroleum, gas or coke industry processes.
[C10H 11/00] Acetylene gas generators with submersion of the carbide in water -- This subclass covers apparatuses that generate acetylene by immersing solid carbide, typically calcium carbide, directly in water, allowing the chemical reaction to produce the gas for lighting, welding or other industrial uses, with emphasis on the submersion arrangement and gas-release control.
[C10H 11/02] inside the gas-holder -- The CPC subclass C10H 11/02 concerns acetylene generators in which the calcium carbide reacts with water while being submerged inside the gas-holder itself. It covers designs that integrate the reaction chamber within the storage vessel, allowing continuous production of acetylene directly into the holder. This subcategory falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, specifically wet-method acetylene production.
[C10H 11/04] with sealing and reaction water separated from each other -- C10H 11/04 covers wet-method acetylene generators in which the calcium carbide is submerged in water but isolated by a seal so that the water used for the reaction is kept separate from any water that may collect as a product. This arrangement is used in the petroleum, gas and coke sectors for producing acetylene while preventing mixing of reaction and condensate water.
[C10H 1/12] Water feed from above through porous materials -- C10H 1/12 pertains to acetylene gas generators in which water is supplied from above through a porous material, enabling a dropwise, gravity-driven, non-automatic feed into the reaction zone. This subclass addresses the specific configuration of water introduction for wet-method acetylene production.
[C10H 13/00] Acetylene gas generation with combined dipping and drop-by-drop system -- The CPC subclass C10H 13/00 covers wet-method production of acetylene that uses a combined dipping and drop-by-drop addition system, wherein a solid reactant is immersed while a liquid reagent is introduced dropwise to generate acetylene gas. It includes apparatus and processes for controlling the simultaneous immersion and incremental liquid dosing to improve gas yield and safety.
[C10H 15/00] Acetylene gas generators with carbide feed, with or without regulation by the gas pressure -- This subclass deals with acetylene gas generators that introduce calcium carbide into water to produce the gas, and may incorporate systems to regulate the output by controlling gas pressure. It covers both continuous and batch carbide feed arrangements and any pressure-regulating mechanisms used to maintain a steady acetylene flow.
[C10H 15/02] with non-automatic carbide feed -- The subclass C10H 15/02 covers acetylene generators that use a manually-controlled calcium carbide feed, where the feed is not automatically regulated and gas pressure may or may not be used to influence it. It pertains to wet-method acetylene production within the petroleum, gas or coke industry.
[C10H 15/04] Closure means at the filling-hopper -- A closure means at the filling-hopper of a wet acetylene generator is a device that seals the hopper where calcium carbide is introduced to water, preventing feed loss and uncontrolled gas release. The means may be hinged, threaded, spring-loaded or otherwise secured, and can incorporate pressure-regulating features to maintain safe generator operation.
[C10H 15/06] with automatic carbide feed by valves -- This subclass pertains to acetylene generators that automatically supply calcium carbide through valve mechanisms, allowing continuous carbide delivery that may be linked to gas-pressure regulation for steady wet-process acetylene production.
[C10H 15/08] by flap or slide valves -- C10H 15/08 covers acetylene gas generators that feed carbide and may regulate pressure, specifically those that use flap or slide valves to control the flow of gas. It falls under the chemistry sector for petroleum, gas or coke industries, within wet-method acetylene production.
[C10H 15/10] by float valves -- This subclass covers acetylene gas generators that use float-type valves to control the feed of calcium carbide or the pressure of the generated gas. It falls under chemistry processes for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically wet methods of acetylene production.
[C10H 15/12] by measuring valves, including pocket-wheels -- This CPC subclass covers acetylene generators that use a carbide feed and regulate gas output through measuring valves such as pocket-wheels, which monitor pressure to control production. It is located in the chemistry section for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically under production of acetylene by wet methods.
[C10H 15/14] with feed worm or feed conveyors -- This subclass covers acetylene gas generators that use a carbide feed system equipped with a feed worm or feed conveyors to move the material into the reaction zone. It falls within the wet-method production of acetylene in the petroleum, gas and coke industry portion of the CPC.
[C10H 15/16] with feed drums -- Acetylene generators that employ a drum or similar container to hold calcium carbide feed, supplying the material to the reaction zone where water or steam generates acetylene. The drum may be designed for continuous or batch feeding and may include simple pressure-based regulation of the gas output. This arrangement is used in on-site production for welding, lighting or chemical synthesis.
[C10H 15/18] with movable feed disc and fixed carbide-receptacle -- This subclass covers acetylene gas generators in which the carbide is supplied from a fixed receptacle while the feed disc rotates or moves to regulate the contact with water. The movable feed disc controls the rate of carbide feed, allowing adjustment of gas pressure without moving the carbide container. It is used in wet-type acetylene production systems.
[C10H 15/20] with carbide feed by cartridges or other packets -- The C10H 15/20 subclass covers wet-process acetylene generators that feed calcium carbide using removable cartridges or other packet-type containers. It includes systems where the carbide supply may be regulated by gas pressure, describing equipment used in petroleum-related acetylene production.
[C10H 15/22] with carbide feed of pulverous carbide from receptacles or through the gas-holder -- The entry covers acetylene gas generators that feed pulverous carbide either from dedicated receptacles or directly through the gas-holder, including designs that may regulate gas pressure. It applies to wet-method acetylene production within the broader chemistry and petroleum, gas or coke industry categories.
[C10H 15/24] with carbide feed by pistons -- The code C10H 15/24 classifies acetylene generators that introduce solid carbide into the reaction chamber by means of pistons, either with or without automatic regulation by gas pressure. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum/gas or coke industry processes, and wet-method production of acetylene. This subclass covers piston-driven carbide feeding mechanisms in such generators.
[C10H 17/00] High-pressure acetylene gas generators -- C10H 17/00 covers high-pressure acetylene gas generators used in the petroleum, gas or coke sectors to produce acetylene through wet methods such as water-gas or calcium carbide-water reactions. The classification addresses equipment design, pressure control, and safety features for continuous or batch generation.
[C10H 19/00] Other acetylene gas generators -- C10H 19/00 pertains to acetylene gas generators that employ wet production methods but are not classified in more specific sub-categories. It includes miscellaneous or auxiliary generators used in petroleum, gas, or coke industry processes where acetylene is produced on-site for applications such as welding, cutting, or chemical synthesis.
[C10H 19/02] Rotary carbide receptacles -- Rotary carbide receptacles are rotating containers used in wet acetylene production, where calcium carbide is fed into water while the vessel turns to enhance mixing, heat removal and continuous gas generation. This type of apparatus falls under "other acetylene gas generators" within the petroleum, gas or coke industries segment of the CPC.
[C10H 21/00] Details of acetylene generators -- The CPC subclass C10H 21/00 concerns devices and processes for producing acetylene by wet methods, detailing the construction, operation and control of generators that react calcium carbide or related feedstocks with water. It includes information on reactor design, heat management, catalyst use, safety features and handling of the generated acetylene.
[C10H 21/02] Packages of carbide for use in generators, e.g. cartridges -- This subclass covers packaged forms of calcium carbide, such as cartridges or pre-formed bundles, intended for insertion into wet acetylene generators used in petroleum, gas or coke production. The packages are designed to simplify handling, loading and controlled reaction of carbide with water to generate acetylene on demand.
[C10H 21/04] Placing packages in the generator -- The code covers methods and apparatus for loading solid feedstock containers into wet-type acetylene generators, describing how packages are positioned, secured and inserted to ensure safe, efficient production of acetylene. It includes arrangements for alignment, sealing and automation of the loading process.
[C10H 21/06] Opening devices for packages in the generator -- This subclass relates to mechanisms that open the feedstock packages inside wet-acetylene generators, such as valves or rupturing devices that permit the material to be released for the acetylene-forming reaction.
[C10H 21/08] Safety devices for acetylene generators -- The subclass C10H 21/08 covers safety mechanisms incorporated into wet-process acetylene generators, such as pressure-relief valves, flashback arresters and automatic shut-off devices that prevent over-pressure, explosion or uncontrolled reaction in the production of acetylene from calcium carbide or other wet methods.
[C10H 21/10] Carbide compositions -- C10H 21/10 Carbide compositions covers the chemical formulations used to generate acetylene in wet-process generators, typically mixtures of calcium or other metal carbides with water-reactive agents. The entry addresses the preparation, composition, and handling of these carbides, which release acetylene upon hydrolysis in petroleum-related applications.
[C10H 21/12] Gas-tight sealing means, e.g. liquid seals in generators -- The C10H 21/12 subclass covers sealing solutions that keep wet acetylene generators gas-tight, focusing on devices such as liquid seals that prevent the escape of generated acetylene. It includes designs and arrangements that maintain a sealed environment during production, ensuring safety and efficiency.
[C10H 21/14] Ventilation means -- The classification C10H 21/14 covers ventilation arrangements used in wet-process acetylene generators, such as fans, ducts and exhaust openings that provide airflow, remove excess heat and hazardous gases, and maintain safe operating conditions within the generator.
[C10H 21/16] Removing sludge from generators
[C10H 3/00] Acetylene gas generators with automatic water feed regulation by means independent of the gas-holder -- This subclass covers acetylene generators that incorporate an automatic water-feed system that operates independently of the gas-holder, controlling water addition to the calcium carbide reaction without relying on holder pressure. The arrangement stabilizes acetylene output and reduces manual adjustment.
[C10H 3/02] with membranes -- The CPC subclass C10H 3/02 covers wet-method acetylene generators that incorporate a membrane element, typically to regulate water feed or separate the gas, providing automatic control that does not depend on the gas-holder.
[C10H 3/04] with floats -- The subclass C10H 3/04 pertains to acetylene gas generators that employ floats to regulate the automatic feed of water, operating independently of the gas-holder's pressure. It specifically addresses designs where the float mechanism controls water supply to maintain consistent acetylene production in wet-method processes.
[C10H 3/06] with pistons -- This CPC subgroup (C10H 3/06) covers wet-process acetylene generators that use pistons to control the automatic water feed, operating independently of the gas holder. The pistons regulate the water-acetylene reaction, ensuring consistent gas production without relying on pressure changes in the storage vessel.
[C10H 5/00] Acetylene gas generators with automatic water feed regulation by the gas-holder -- The code C10H 5/00 covers acetylene generators that automatically regulate the water feed to the gas-holder during wet-method production. It pertains to equipment designed to maintain optimal water-acetylene reaction rates, improving safety and efficiency in petroleum-related gas-generation systems.
[C10H 5/02] with overflow for the water -- Sub-class C10H 5/02 covers wet-method acetylene generators that incorporate an overflow device for the water, enabling the gas-holder to automatically regulate water feed and maintain the proper level during operation.
[C10H 5/04] by drop-by-drop water valves connected with the gas-holder -- The C10H 5/04 subclass covers acetylene generators that control water supply through drop-by-drop valves directly linked to the gas-holder. It specifies automatic, precise water feeding mechanisms for wet-method acetylene production, a niche within petroleum, gas and coke industry processes for generating acetylene gas.
[C10H 5/06] by drop-by-drop water cocks connected with the gas-holder -- The CPC subclass C10H 5/06 covers acetylene gas generators that automatically control water input by using drop-by-drop water cocks attached to the gas holder, enabling precise, continuous water feed during wet-method acetylene production. This falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas, and the production of acetylene by wet methods.
[C10H 5/08] with gas-holder-connected water valves or cocks according to the submersion system -- C10H 5/08 covers acetylene generators that regulate water feed automatically through valves or cocks mounted on the gas-holder, using a submersion system where the water supply is controlled directly by the holder's position or pressure. This subcategory pertains to wet-method acetylene production in petroleum-related processes.
[C10H 7/00] Acetylene gas generators with water feed by Kipp's principle -- This CPC subclass deals with acetylene generators that use a water-feed system based on Kipp's principle, where water is introduced into a sealed chamber containing calcium carbide to produce acetylene on demand. The entry covers the design and operation of such wet-method generators for laboratory or small-scale industrial use.
[C10H 7/02] with water feed from below -- A CPC subclass covering acetylene generators that employ the Kipp-type wet-method and introduce water from beneath the reaction mixture. It falls under chemistry, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke sector, and addresses the production of acetylene by wet processes where water is supplied from below.
[C10H 7/04] with water feed from above -- This CPC subclass (C10H 7/04) covers acetylene generators that use Kipp's principle and receive water feed from above the reaction vessel. It falls under wet methods of acetylene production, a sub-category of petroleum, gas or coke industry processes in the chemistry section.
[C10H 9/00] Acetylene gas generators according to Dobereiner's principle with fixed carbide bell -- These generators produce acetylene by wetly reacting calcium carbide with water, employing Dobereiner's principle that confines the carbide in a fixed bell to control gas release and improve safety. The design is used in petroleum, gas and coke processing where on-site acetylene is required.
[C10H 9/02] with water feed from below through porous materials (by capillary feed) -- This subclass covers acetylene gas generators that operate on Dobereiner's principle and feed water from below through a porous medium by capillary action. It falls under production of acetylene by wet methods in the petroleum, gas or coke industry.
[C10H 9/04] with gas cock actuated by the gas holder -- A wet-method acetylene generator employing Dobereiner's fixed carbide bell includes a gas cock that is automatically opened by the rising gas holder, allowing acetylene to be released only when sufficient pressure has built up. This arrangement provides a simple, self-regulating control for safe gas delivery in petroleum-related acetylene production, classified under C10H 9/04.
[C10H 9/06] with the depth of the gas outlet pipe regulated by the gas-holder -- This CPC subclass covers acetylene generators that operate on Dobereiner's principle with a fixed carbide bell, in which the depth of the gas-outlet pipe is regulated by the gas holder.
[C10H 9/08] with movable gas-holder -- C10H 9/08 classifies acetylene gas generators that employ Dobereiner's principle with a fixed carbide bell and a movable gas-holder, used in wet-process production of acetylene for petroleum and related industries. The movable holder allows continuous collection of the gas while the reaction chamber remains stationary.
[C10H 9/10] by wetting the carbide only at the bottom -- The subclass C10H 9/10 covers acetylene generators that apply Dobereiner's principle with a fixed carbide bell and introduce water only at the bottom of the carbide mass, so that wetting occurs solely at the lower region of the reactor. This method is used to control gas production in wet-carbide acetylene processes.
[C10J] PRODUCTION OF PRODUCER GAS, WATER-GAS, SYNTHESIS GAS FROM SOLID CARBONACEOUS MATERIAL, OR MIXTURES CONTAINING THESE GASES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes or apparatus for production of fuel gases by carburetting air or other gases without pyrolysis, including controlling supply of air or liquid, temperature, humidity and other parameters.  Processes or apparatus for production of combustible gases containing carbon monoxide (including producer gas, wood gas, town gas, synthesis gas (syngas), manufactured gas and water gas) from solid carbonaceous materials. This includes fixed-bed gasification of lump fuel, gasification of granular or pulverulent fuels in suspension, gasification using molten salts or metals, carburetting by pyrolysis of carbonaceous material in the fuel bed and carburetting by pyrolysis of carbonaceous material in a carburettor.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Destructive distillation processes -> C10B - Underground gasification of minerals -> E21B43/295  **Glossary:** - Carburetting: Carburetting air or gas generally comprises passing it in contact with liquid fuel and thereby mixing the air/gas and fuel. This often involves lowering the air pressure e.g. in a venturi. - Destructive distillation: The process of pyrolysis conducted in a distillation apparatus to allow the volatile products to be collected. An example is tar making from pinewood slices (which are rich in terpenes), which are heated in an airless container causing the material to decompose, leaving charcoal and turpentine as by-products. - Gasification: Gasification is somewhat similar to pyrolysis and confusion between these terms is common. Gasification is a partial oxidation process that converts materials such as coal, biomass or plastic waste into a gaseous mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen (also known as synthesis gas) by reacting the raw material at high temperatures with controlled amounts of oxygen and/or steam. See also the entry for pyrolysis. - Producer gas: A gas mixture containing carbon monoxide (CO), hydrogen (H 2 ), carbon dioxide (CO 2 ) and nitrogen (N 2 ). In the USA, producer gas is a generic term referring to wood gas, town gas, synthesis gas, syngas or raw gas. In the UK, producer gas, also known as suction gas, means a fuel gas made from coke or other carbonaceous material. Air is passed over the red-hot fuel and carbon monoxide is produced in an exothermic reaction which reads 2C + O 2 → 2CO. The nitrogen in the air remains unchanged and dilutes the gas, so it has a low calorific value. The gas may be used to power gas turbines which are suited to fuels of low calorific value. - Pyrolysis: The chemical decomposition of organic materials by heating in the absence of oxygen or any other reagents, except possibly steam. Pyrolysis is somewhat endothermic and the products can be gases, liquids (e.g. light crude oil from depolymerisation of organic waste) and/or solids (e.g. coke and volatiles produced by coking coal). See also the entry for gasification. - Synthesis gas (syngas): A gas mixture that contains varying amounts of carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H 2 ) generated by the gasification of a carbon-containing material to a gaseous product with a heating value (but less than half the energy density of natural gas). When used as a fuel, it is produced by gasification of coal or municipal waste by the following reactions: C + O 2 → CO 2 ; CO 2 + C → 2CO; C + H 2 O → CO + H 2 . The name comes from the gas's use as an intermediate in creating synthetic natural gas (SNG) and in producing ammonia or methanol. - Town gas: Also known as coal gas, and contains hydrogen (H 2 ), carbon monoxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO 2 ), methane (CH 4 ), nitrogen (N 2 ) and volatile hydrocarbons. It is made by blowing air and steam over an incandescent fuel bed, usually of coke or coal. The words "coal gas" could also be used to mean gas made by the destructive distillation of coal. The gas was used inter alia for lighting before the advent of electric lighting, and for heating and cooking before natural gas became widely available. - Water-gas: A mixture of carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H 2 ) produced by passing steam over red-hot coke using the endothermic reaction C + H 2 O → CO + H 2 . This product had a lower calorific value than coal gas so the gas was often passed through a heated retort into which oil was sprayed; the resulting mixed gas was called carburetted water gas. - Wood gas: The product of thermal gasification of biomass (e.g. coal, wood chips, sawdust, charcoal) in a gasifier or wood gas generator. It is the result of a high temperature reaction (> 700 degrees C) where carbon reacts with steam or a limited amount of air producing carbon monoxide (CO), carbon dioxide (CO 2 ), hydrogen (H 2 ) and methane (CH 4 ). It can be filtered, purified or scrubbed and used to power internal combustion engines, gas turbines, Stirling engines or fuel cells.
[C10J 1/00] Production of fuel gases by carburetting air or other gases without pyrolysis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel gases produced by carburetting air or other gases without pyrolysis
[C10J 1/02] Carburetting air -- C10J 1/02 relates to processes that generate fuel gases by carburetting air or other gases with solid carbonaceous material, producing producer, water-gas or synthesis gas without undergoing pyrolysis. The subclass falls under chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the production of such gases from solid feedstock.
[C10J 1/04] Controlling supply of air -- C10J 1/04 covers techniques for regulating the amount of air introduced into a gas-producing reactor that carburets air or other gases with solid carbonaceous feedstock. It includes devices such as valves, dampers, controllers and feedback systems that adjust air flow to maintain desired gas composition, temperature or pressure during fuel-gas production.
[C10J 1/06] with materials which are liquid at ordinary temperatures -- The CPC subclass C10J 1/06 covers processes that produce fuel gases by carburetting air or other gases without using pyrolysis, where the feedstock or additives are liquids at normal temperatures. It falls under the chemistry category for producing synthesis or water-gas from solid carbonaceous materials.
[C10J 1/08] by passage of air through or over the surface of the liquid -- C10J 1/08 covers methods of generating fuel gases by carburetting air or other gases without pyrolysis, specifically through the passage of air across or through the surface of a liquid. This technique is used to produce producer, water-gas, or synthesis gases from liquid feedstocks in petroleum and gas processing.
[C10J 1/10] with the liquid absorbed on carriers -- The C10J 1/10 subclass covers methods in which a liquid is first absorbed onto a carrier material and then used to carburet air or other gases for producing fuel gases such as producer, water- or synthesis gas, without employing pyrolysis. It falls under the broader field of petroleum, gas or coke industry processes for generating these gases from solid carbonaceous feedstocks.
[C10J 1/12] by atomisation of the liquid -- This subclass deals with producing fuel gases such as producer-gas, water-gas or synthesis-gas by atomising a liquid fuel into fine droplets and mixing it with air or other gases, without any pyrolysis step. It falls under the CPC group C10J 1/12, which covers gas production by carburetting air or other gases in petroleum, gas or coke processing.
[C10J 1/14] Controlling the supply of liquid in accordance with the air supply -- C10J 1/14 covers methods and devices that regulate the feed of a liquid-such as water, fuel oil or other condensable fluid-in proportion to the amount of air or other gas supplied in a carburetion system used to produce fuel gases from solid carbonaceous material, ensuring optimal gas composition without pyrolysis.
[C10J 1/16] with solid hydrocarbons -- This subclass covers methods for producing fuel gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas by carburetting air or other gases with solid hydrocarbon materials, without subjecting the solid to pyrolysis. The process mixes the solid fuel with the oxidising gas and reacts it at comparatively low temperatures to release combustible gas.
[C10J 1/18] in rotary carburettors -- Section C10J 1/18 deals with the production of fuel gases by using rotary carburettors, where air or other gases are mixed with solid carbonaceous material or other fuel in a rotating device to generate producer, water-gas or synthesis gas without undergoing pyrolysis. The classification applies to processes that carburett the gas stream in a rotary mechanism for efficient gas formation.
[C10J 1/20] Carburetting gases other than air -- The CPC subclass C10J 1/20 covers processes that carburett gases other than air to produce fuel gases from solid carbonaceous material. It falls within the production of producer, water-gas or synthesis gas and involves adding gases such as oxygen, steam or other oxidants rather than air during the carburetting stage.
[C10J 1/207] Carburetting by pyrolysis of solid carbonaceous material in a fuel bed -- C10J 1/207 covers the carburetting of air or other gases by pyrolising solid carbonaceous material within a fuel bed, producing combustible fuel gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas without a separate pyrolysis stage. The process mixes the solid fuel with the oxidising gas so that partial combustion and thermal decomposition occur simultaneously, generating a combustible gas stream.
[C10J 1/213] Carburetting by pyrolysis of solid carbonaceous material in a carburettor -- C10J 1/213 covers processes that generate fuel gases by feeding solid carbonaceous material into a carburettor, where it is partially pyrolysed and the resulting gases are mixed with air or another carrier gas. The subclass includes designs that integrate the pyrolysis zone and the mixing chamber to produce a combustible gas directly for combustion devices.
[C10J 1/22] Adding materials to prevent vapour deposition -- The subclass covers the use of additives introduced into the gas stream or reactor to inhibit condensation of vapour-phase components that could deposit as solids or liquids on equipment during the carburetting of air or other gases to produce fuel gases. Such materials include anti-fouling agents, inhibitors or surface-active compounds that keep surfaces clean and maintain stable operation.
[C10J 1/24] Controlling humidity of the air or gas to be carburetted -- This subclass deals with methods and apparatus for adjusting the moisture content of air or other gases before they are mixed with fuel in a carburettor, ensuring optimal combustion and preventing condensation. It is applied in processes that produce producer gas, water-gas, or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstock without using pyrolysis.
[C10J 1/26] using raised temperatures or pressures -- The subclass C10J 1/26 covers processes that generate fuel gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas by carburetting air or other gases with solid carbonaceous material under raised temperatures or pressures, without employing pyrolysis. It pertains to methods that rely on combustion or oxidation at elevated conditions to produce the desired gas mixture.
[C10J 1/28] Odorising air gas -- This subclass covers the addition of odorising agents to air gas produced from solid carbonaceous feedstocks or related mixtures, enabling the gas to be readily detected for safety. It includes methods that odorise the gas by carburetting air or other gases, rather than by pyrolytic processes.
[C10J 2200/00] Details of gasification apparatus -- The C10J 2200/00 entry pertains to the design and functional elements of gasification equipment used to convert solid carbonaceous materials into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It includes reactors, feed mechanisms, heat-transfer devices, and related control or auxiliary components.
[C10J 2200/06] Catalysts as integral part of gasifiers -- The subclass C10J 2200/06 covers gasification technologies in which a catalyst is built into the gasifier itself, used to convert solid carbonaceous materials into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It applies to petroleum, gas and coke industry processes that integrate catalytic action directly within the gasification apparatus to enhance reaction rates, selectivity or product composition.
[C10J 2200/09] Mechanical details of gasifiers not otherwise provided for, e.g. sealing means -- C10J 2200/09 pertains to the mechanical aspects of gasification equipment that are not specified elsewhere, such as sealing arrangements and other device‐specific features. It falls under the chemistry field for petroleum, gas or coke industries, focusing on the production of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous material.
[C10J 2200/12] Electrodes present in the gasifier -- The CPC entry C10J 2200/12 covers the use of electrodes within gasifiers that convert solid carbonaceous feedstock into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It deals with the design, material choice and configuration of electrodes incorporated in the gasification apparatus.
[C10J 2200/15] Details of feeding means -- C10J 2200/15 relates to the specific feeding mechanisms employed in gasification units that convert solid carbonaceous material into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It includes designs of feeders, conveyors and other devices that introduce the solid feedstock into the reactor.
[C10J 2200/152] Nozzles or lances for introducing gas, liquids or suspensions -- This subclass concerns the design and use of nozzles or lances that inject gases, liquids or particle suspensions into a gasifier or similar reactor where solid carbonaceous feedstock is gasified. It includes features such as spray patterns, mixing arrangements and materials for withstanding high temperature and pressure. It falls under C10J 2200/152 in the CPC.
[C10J 2200/154] Pushing devices, e.g. pistons -- C10J 2200/154 covers pushing mechanisms such as pistons employed in gasification equipment for producing producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstocks. The classification concerns devices that move or compress material within the reactor or related conveyance systems. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and the production of synthesis gases from solid fuels.
[C10J 2200/156] Sluices, e.g. mechanical sluices for preventing escape of gas through the feed inlet -- A CPC subclass C10J 2200/156 refers to mechanical sluices or similar sealing devices installed at the feed inlet of a gasification unit to prevent the escape of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas during processing of solid carbonaceous materials. It falls under the chemistry section for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically detailing components of gasification apparatus that control gas leakage.
[C10J 2200/158] Screws -- The CPC subclass C10J 2200/158 covers screws used in gasification equipment, such as auger or helical screw conveyors that transport solid carbonaceous feedstock or residues within a producer-gas, water-gas or synthesis-gas reactor. It pertains to the design, arrangement and operation of these screw mechanisms as part of the overall gasification apparatus.
[C10J 2200/31] Mobile gasifiers, e.g. for use in cars, ships or containers -- C10J 2200/31 covers mobile gasifiers designed to convert solid carbonaceous materials into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas for use on vehicles, ships or portable containers. The classification includes the design, arrangement and operation of compact gasification units that can be moved or installed on transport platforms.
[C10J 2200/33] Laboratory scale gasifiers -- C10J 2200/33 covers laboratory-scale gasifiers used to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstocks. The entry details the design, components and operation of small-capacity gasification apparatus for research or pilot testing. It falls under chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the production of gas from solid carbon sources.
[C10J 2200/36] Moving parts inside the gasification reactor not otherwise provided for -- C10J 2200/36 covers moving components located inside a gasification reactor that are not otherwise specified, such as stirrers, agitators or feed mechanisms, used in the production of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous material.
[C10J 2200/39] Gasifiers designed as centrifuge -- C10J 2200/39 classifies gasifiers that function as centrifuges, used to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous materials. It covers the design features of centrifuge-type gasification equipment within petroleum, gas and coke processing.
[C10J 2300/00] Details of gasification processes -- The CPC subclass C10J 2300/00 covers details of gasification processes used to produce producer gas, water-gas, or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous materials. It includes the design, operation, control and optimisation of reactors, feed preparation and gas cleanup within the petroleum, gas and coke industries.
[C10J 2300/06] Modeling or simulation of processes -- The CPC subclass C10J 2300/06 relates to the modeling or simulation of gasification processes used to convert solid carbonaceous feedstocks into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It falls under the chemistry sector, specifically petroleum, gas or coke industries, and covers computational analysis of the details of gasification operations.
[C10J 2300/09] Details of the feed, e.g. feeding of spent catalyst, inert gas or halogens -- C10J 2300/09 covers aspects of the feed material used in gasification of solid carbonaceous substances, such as the introduction of spent catalyst, inert gases or halogens, within processes that produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It falls under the chemistry category for petroleum, gas or coke industry production methods.
[C10J 2300/0903] Feed preparation -- The C10J 2300/0903 entry defines the technical steps used to ready solid carbonaceous feedstock for gasification, such as crushing, grinding, drying, blending and impurity removal, to ensure efficient production of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It falls under chemistry and the petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically detailing feed preparation in the gasification process.
[C10J 2300/0906] Physical processes, e.g. shredding, comminuting, chopping, sorting -- C10J 2300/0906 classifies the physical preparation steps used in producing producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstocks, including shredding, comminuting, chopping and sorting of the material before gasification. It falls under the broader chemistry category for petroleum, gas or coke industry processes.
[C10J 2300/0909] Drying -- C10J 2300/0909 covers drying operations associated with gasifying solid carbonaceous feedstocks such as coal, coke or biomass. The classification applies to methods that remove moisture from the material before or during its conversion into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas as part of the overall gasification process.
[C10J 2300/0913] Carbonaceous raw material -- C10J 2300/0913 covers carbonaceous raw material, such as coal, coke, peat or biomass, used as the solid feedstock in gasification processes that produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas.
[C10J 2300/0916] Biomass -- The code designates the portion of the CPC dealing with gasification of biomass, i.e., the conversion of solid organic material into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas, and includes the associated processes, reactors, feeding systems and gas-cleaning steps. It is used for patents on technologies that transform wood, agricultural residues or other biomass into combustible gases for energy or chemical synthesis.
[C10J 2300/092] Wood, cellulose -- C10J 2300/092 pertains to gasification processes in which wood or cellulose is used as the solid carbonaceous feedstock to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas, detailing the specific steps and conditions for converting these biomaterials into gaseous fuels.
[C10J 2300/0923] Sludge, e.g. from water treatment plant -- The C10J 2300/0923 entry addresses the use or treatment of sludge, such as waste from water-treatment plants, in gasification processes that convert solid carbonaceous material into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It falls under the chemistry section for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the production of these gases from solid feeds.
[C10J 2300/0926] Slurries comprising bio-oil or bio-coke, i.e. charcoal, obtained, e.g. by fast pyrolysis of biomass -- C10J 2300/0926 covers slurry mixtures containing bio-oil or bio-coke, such as charcoal, produced by fast pyrolysis of biomass and employed in gasification or related processes for making synthesis gases.
[C10J 2300/093] Coal -- C10J 2300/093 Coal - covers the use of coal as a solid carbonaceous feedstock in gasification processes that produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas, detailing methods and conditions for converting coal into these gaseous products.
[C10J 2300/0933] Coal fines for producing water gas -- This CPC class covers the use of fine coal particles as feedstock in water-gas production, a gasification process that yields a carbon-monoxide and hydrogen mixture. It includes methods for handling, pre-treatment and feeding of coal fines into reactors to optimise gas yield and quality. The entry sits under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, specifically the gasification of solid carbonaceous material.
[C10J 2300/0936] Coal fines for producing producer gas -- This CPC subclass concerns the use of coal fines as feedstock in gasification to produce producer gas, detailing methods for handling, preparing and feeding fine coal particles into gasifiers. It falls under chemistry, petroleum-gas-coke industries, and the production of producer, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous material.
[C10J 2300/094] Char -- C10J 2300/094 - Char refers to the solid carbon-rich residue that remains after gasification of coal or other carbonaceous feedstocks, used as a raw material or fuel in further processes. It covers the properties, handling, and utilization of char in industrial applications.
[C10J 2300/0943] Coke -- C10J 2300/0943 classifies technologies and methods related to the production and handling of coke, a high-carbon solid derived from coal or other carbonaceous feedstocks. It covers processes that generate coke for use in metallurgy, energy recovery, or as a feedstock for gasification and synthesis-gas production.
[C10J 2300/0946] Waste, e.g. MSW, tires, glass, tar sand, peat, paper, lignite, oil shale -- This classification pertains to gasification processes that use various waste streams such as municipal solid waste, tires, glass, tar sand, peat, paper, lignite or oil shale as feedstock to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It covers the methods, apparatus and operating conditions for converting these waste materials into gaseous fuels.
[C10J 2300/095] Exhaust gas from an external process for purification -- The subclass deals with using exhaust gases that originate from an outside process to purify producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas in carbonaceous-material gasification. It includes methods such as contacting the fuel-derived gas with the external exhaust stream for impurity removal, temperature control or scrubbing, thereby improving the quality of the generated synthesis gases.
[C10J 2300/0953] Gasifying agents -- The CPC entry C10J 2300/0953 "Gasifying agents" covers substances employed to promote the gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks, such as coal, coke or biomass, by reacting with the material to produce synthesis gas, water-gas or producer gas. It includes oxidants, steam, carbon dioxide and other additives that facilitate the conversion process.
[C10J 2300/0956] Air or oxygen enriched air -- C10J 2300/0956 relates to gasification processes in which air or air enriched with oxygen is used as the oxidising agent. It covers the provision, mixing, and control of such oxidant streams for producing producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstocks.
[C10J 2300/0959] Oxygen -- The subclass C10J 2300/0959 refers to the use of oxygen as the oxidising agent in gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It covers methods of supplying, controlling and reacting oxygen in the reactor and related equipment within petroleum, gas or coke industry processes.
[C10J 2300/0963] Ozone -- C10J 2300/0963 covers the use of ozone in gasification processes that convert solid carbonaceous materials into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. The subclass includes methods, reactors or conditions where ozone is employed to enhance oxidation, improve gas composition or facilitate catalytic steps in the production of these gases.
[C10J 2300/0966] Hydrogen -- Hydrogen is covered as a specific component or product in gasification processes that convert solid carbonaceous materials, such as coal or coke, into producer gas, water-gas, or synthesis gas. The entry applies to methods, conditions and equipment that generate, handle or utilize hydrogen within these petrochemical and energy production operations.
[C10J 2300/0969] Carbon dioxide -- Covers aspects of carbon dioxide in the gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks, including its formation, use, removal or recycling within water-gas, synthesis-gas or producer-gas production.
[C10J 2300/0973] Water -- The subclass C10J 2300/0973 addresses the use of water in gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks, such as coal, for producing producer gas, water-gas, or synthesis gas. It covers methods that introduce water or steam, steam-reforming steps, and related techniques that affect temperature, reaction rate, or gas-stream composition.
[C10J 2300/0976] as steam -- C10J 2300/0976 relates to gasification methods that employ steam in the conversion of solid carbonaceous materials into producer gas, water-gas, or synthesis gas, detailing how steam is introduced as a reactant, carrier or heat source during the process.
[C10J 2300/0979] as supercritical steam -- The classification covers the use of supercritical steam as the reacting medium in gasification of coal or other solid carbonaceous feedstocks to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It applies to methods where steam at pressures and temperatures above its critical point facilitates the conversion.
[C10J 2300/0983] Additives -- The C10J 2300/0983 entry covers additives used in the gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It includes substances introduced to modify reaction chemistry, enhance conversion efficiency, control product gas composition, reduce slag formation, or protect equipment from corrosion and fouling during the gasification process.
[C10J 2300/0986] Catalysts -- C10J 2300/0986 covers the use of catalysts in gasification methods that convert solid carbonaceous feedstocks, such as coal or coke, into producer gas, water-gas, or synthesis gas. It includes catalytic formulations, supports and operating conditions that improve gas quality, yield or reaction efficiency.
[C10J 2300/0989] Hydrocarbons as additives to gasifying agents to improve caloric properties -- The CPC subclass C10J 2300/0989 covers the use of hydrocarbons added to gasifying agents to improve the caloric value of the gas produced in gasification processes. It falls under chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, and the production of producer gas, water-gas, and synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous material.
[C10J 2300/0993] Inert particles, e.g. as heat exchange medium in a fluidized or moving bed, heat carriers, sand -- C10J 2300/0993 classifies the use of inert particles, such as sand or other solid heat-exchange media, in fluidized-bed or moving-bed gasifiers for producing producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from carbonaceous feedstocks. It covers the role of these particles as heat carriers within the gasification process.
[C10J 2300/0996] Calcium-containing inorganic materials, e.g. lime -- The C10J 2300/0996 subclass covers calcium-containing inorganic materials such as lime used in gasification processes for producing producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstock. These materials are considered in the context of petroleum, gas or coke industry operations involving the preparation or treatment of the gasifying medium.
[C10J 2300/12] Heating the gasifier -- The C10J 2300/12 subclass relates to methods, devices and arrangements for heating a gasifier used in gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstock, such as coal or biomass, to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It covers temperature-control strategies, heat-transfer systems and the use of external or internal energy sources to raise the reaction temperature and improve gas quality and conversion efficiency.
[C10J 2300/1207] using pyrolysis gas as fuel -- C10J 2300/1207 concerns the use of pyrolysis gas as a fuel in gasification operations that produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstocks. It falls within the chemistry sector, specifically the petroleum, gas and coke industries, and addresses details of the gasification process.
[C10J 2300/1215] using synthesis gas as fuel -- The CPC entry C10J 2300/1215 concerns the use of synthesis gas as a fuel in industrial processes. It falls under chemistry, specifically petroleum, gas or coke industries, and deals with gasification methods that produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous materials or their mixtures. This subclass details how the generated synthesis gas is employed as an energy source.
[C10J 2300/1223] by burners -- The CPC subclass C10J 2300/1223 pertains to gasification methods that employ burners to convert solid carbonaceous feedstock or gas-containing mixtures into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It is situated within chemistry, the petroleum-gas-coke sector, and the production of these synthesis gases.
[C10J 2300/123] by electromagnetic waves, e.g. microwaves -- This CPC subclass deals with gasifying solid carbonaceous feedstocks by applying electromagnetic energy, typically microwaves, to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. The method uses microwave heating to initiate and sustain the gasification reaction, offering rapid, localized heating and improved control compared with conventional thermal sources. It falls under chemistry processes for petroleum, gas and coke industries.

[C10J 2300/1246] by external or indirect heating -- C10J 2300/1246 covers gasification processes in which solid carbonaceous material is heated by external or indirect sources, such as heat-exchange surfaces, radiant heaters or combustion gases that do not directly contact the feed. The classification applies to the production of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas where the energy is supplied from outside the reaction zone.
[C10J 2300/1253] by injecting hot gas -- C10J 2300/1253 covers methods of producing producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstock in which a hot gas is injected into the gasifier to promote the gasification reaction. The injection may be performed before, during or after contact with the solid fuel and is used to control temperature, improve conversion or modify gas composition.
[C10J 2300/1261] by pulse burners -- C10J 2300/1261 classifies gasification methods that employ pulse burners, which deliver intermittent bursts of fuel and oxidant to improve mixing and temperature control during the production of producer gas, water-gas, or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstock.
[C10J 2300/1269] by radiating device, e.g. radiant tubes -- Within the chemistry section for petroleum and gas industries, this CPC subgroup deals with gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstock where the reaction heat is supplied by a radiating device such as radiant tubes. The radiant heating element provides the energy to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas.
[C10J 2300/1276] by electricity, e.g. resistor heating -- This subclass covers gasification processes that generate producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous materials using electrical heating, for example by resistive heaters. [C10J 2300/1284] by renewable energy, e.g. solar energy, photovoltaic cells, wind -- C10J 2300/1284 refers to gasification methods that use renewable energy-such as solar power, photovoltaic cells or wind-to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous materials or gas mixtures containing these gases. It is a sub-category of chemical processes for petroleum, gas and coke industries.
