[C10J 2300/16] Integration of gasification processes with another plant or parts within the plant -- This CPC subclass covers the integration of gasification units with other plant sections, describing how a gasifier is coupled to downstream or upstream processes within the same facility. It includes linking gasification with power generation, chemical synthesis, or material handling to improve efficiency or resource use.
[C10J 2300/1603] with gas treatment -- This CPC subclass covers methods for treating the gas produced during gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks such as coal, coke or biomass, including cleaning, cooling, scrubbing or removal of contaminants to improve the quality of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It falls under chemistry, petroleum/gas or coke industries, production of these gases.
[C10J 2300/1606] Combustion processes -- C10J 2300/1606 covers combustion processes used in gasifying solid carbonaceous feedstocks to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It includes methods for burning the material or the resulting gases to control temperature, improve efficiency, or remove impurities. The classification falls under the broader categories of chemistry and petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10J 2300/1609] Post-reduction, e.g. on a red-white-hot coke or coal bed -- It covers the post-reduction stage applied to a red-white-hot coke or coal bed during gasification of solid carbonaceous material. In this step the hot solid reacts with produced gases such as CO₂ or H₂O, reducing them to CO and H₂ and improving overall gas yield.
[C10J 2300/1612] CO -- C10J 2300/1612 covers methods and apparatus for producing, separating, purifying, or utilizing carbon monoxide in gasification processes that convert solid carbonaceous materials such as coal, coke, or biomass into synthesis gas, focusing on control of CO content in the resulting gas stream.
[C10J 2300/1615] Stripping -- C10J 2300/1615 - Stripping deals with the removal of condensable components, acid gases, tar, or other contaminants from producer gas, water-gas, or synthesis gas produced by gasifying solid carbonaceous feedstocks. The methods involve contacting the hot gas with a liquid or solid absorbent to separate unwanted substances before downstream use.
[C10J 2300/1618] Modification of synthesis gas composition, e.g. to meet some criteria -- C10J 2300/1618 covers methods for adjusting the composition of synthesis gas produced in gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks, such as altering the ratios of CO, H₂, CO₂ or adding/removing components to meet specified performance, purity or emission criteria. It falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, specifically the production and detailed processing of producer gas, water-gas and synthesis gas.
[C10J 2300/1621] Compression of synthesis gas -- C10J 2300/1621 concerns the compression of synthesis gas produced in gasification of solid carbonaceous materials. It falls under details of gasification processes within petroleum, gas or coke industries, focusing on handling and pressurizing the resulting mixture of CO, H2 and other gases for downstream use.
[C10J 2300/1625] with solids treatment -- This subclass covers gasification methods that include handling, removal, recycling or conditioning of solid materials generated during the production of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstocks. It details steps such as solid-residue treatment, separation, and disposal within petroleum, gas or coke-industry processes.
[C10J 2300/1628] Ash post-treatment -- Ash post-treatment comprises the operations applied to ash residues after gasifying solid carbonaceous materials, aiming to remove contaminants, recover valuable components, or stabilize the material for safe disposal or reuse. Methods may include washing, drying, solidification, or chemical neutralisation.
[C10J 2300/1631] Ash recycling -- C10J 2300/1631 - Ash recycling pertains to methods and systems for recovering, processing, or reusing ash generated during the gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks such as coal, coke, or biomass in the production of producer gas, water-gas, or synthesis gas. It includes techniques for separating ash, treating it for disposal or reuse, and integrating ash handling into the overall gasification plant.
[C10J 2300/1634] Ash vitrification -- The C10J 2300/1634 entry concerns methods for converting ash generated in gasifying solid carbonaceous feedstocks into a glass-like, vitrified material. It includes processes that melt or sinter the ash to immobilise contaminants and facilitate safer disposal or reuse, and falls under detailed gasification techniques within the petroleum, gas and coke chemistry sector.
[C10J 2300/1637] Char combustion -- Char combustion covers the burning of solid carbonaceous residues that remain after partial gasification, converting the char to CO2, CO and heat that affect the composition and efficiency of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis-gas streams. The sub-entry records operating conditions, reaction mechanisms and control methods specific to the combustion stage within the overall gasification of coal, coke or similar feedstocks.
[C10J 2300/164] with conversion of synthesis gas -- C10J 2300/164 covers methods that take synthesis gas produced by gasifying solid carbonaceous material and convert it into other chemicals or fuels, such as hydrocarbons, alcohols or synthetic gases, usually by catalytic or thermal treatment. It is a subcategory of the production of producer gas, water-gas and synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstocks.
[C10J 2300/1643] Conversion of synthesis gas to energy -- C10J 2300/1643 covers methods that convert synthesis gas, a mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen produced by gasifying solid carbonaceous materials, into usable energy such as electricity, heat, or fuels. It falls under the chemistry subclass dealing with petroleum and gas industry processes for producing producer gas, water-gas, and synthesis gas.
[C10J 2300/1646] integrated with a fuel cell -- This subclass covers gasification methods that combine the production of synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstock with a fuel-cell system, where the generated gases are directly supplied to the fuel cell for electricity generation or where the fuel-cell's heat or electricity is used to improve the gasification process.
[C10J 2300/165] integrated with a gas turbine or gas motor -- A process in which solid carbonaceous material is gasified and the resulting producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas is fed directly to a gas turbine or gas-driven motor for power generation, often in an integrated or combined-cycle arrangement. This sub-group covers details of linking the gasification stage with the turbine or motor to improve efficiency and utilization of the produced gas.
[C10J 2300/1653] integrated in a gasification combined cycle [IGCC] -- The classification C10J 2300/1653 covers the integration of a gasification unit with a combined-cycle power plant, where solid carbonaceous feedstock is gasified to produce synthesis gas that directly fuels a gas-turbine and steam-turbine system for electricity generation. It relates to process designs that combine gasification, cleanup, and combined-cycle generation in a single plant.
[C10J 2300/1656] Conversion of synthesis gas to chemicals -- C10J 2300/1656 covers methods for converting synthesis gas - a mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen - into value-added chemicals such as alcohols, hydrocarbons, acids or fertilizers. The classification includes catalytic, thermal or biochemical routes that transform syngas derived from gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks. It is used for patents describing processes that upgrade syngas to downstream chemical products.
[C10J 2300/1659] to liquid hydrocarbons -- C10J 2300/1659 classifies processes that convert synthesis-gas or other gases derived from solid carbonaceous feedstock into liquid hydrocarbon fuels or chemicals, typically by catalytic synthesis, Fischer-Tropsch conversion or related liquid-phase methods. It is a detailed sub-category of gasification-derived gas utilization within petroleum-related chemistry.
[C10J 2300/1662] to methane -- The C10J 2300/1662 entry covers steps in gasification processes that convert synthesis-gas, water-gas or producer-gas mixtures into methane, typically as part of producing fuel from solid carbonaceous materials. It details techniques for methanation within the broader context of petroleum, gas and coke industry chemical manufacturing.
[C10J 2300/1665] to alcohols, e.g. methanol or ethanol -- C10J 2300/1665 concerns processes where producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas produced from solid carbonaceous material is converted into alcohols such as methanol or ethanol. It falls under the chemistry branch dealing with petroleum, gas and coke industries and specifies details of the gasification steps leading to alcoholic fuel production.
[C10J 2300/1668] to urea -- This subclass concerns methods that convert gases obtained from gasifying solid carbonaceous materials into urea, typically by first synthesising ammonia from the gas mixture and then reacting the ammonia with carbon dioxide to produce urea. It details the specific steps and conditions required for the urea-formation stage within gasification-derived feedstocks.
[C10J 2300/1671] with the production of electricity -- The subclass C10J 2300/1671 covers gasification methods that generate producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstocks and incorporate the simultaneous production of electricity. It details processes where the resulting gases are used to drive power-generation equipment.
[C10J 2300/1675] making use of a steam turbine -- The C10J 2300/1675 subclass covers gasification operations where the generated producer gas or synthesis gas is expanded in a steam turbine to produce mechanical power or electricity. It includes configurations that integrate the turbine directly with the gasifier, recover heat from the turbine exhaust, and optimize overall energy efficiency of the process.
[C10J 2300/1678] with air separation -- C10J 2300/1678 relates to gasification techniques that incorporate an air-separation step before reacting solid carbonaceous material, enabling the production of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It falls under the chemistry subclass for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the production of these gases from solid feedstock.
[C10J 2300/1681] with biological plants, e.g. involving bacteria, algae, fungi -- This subclass covers gasification processes where biological organisms are employed to convert solid carbonaceous feedstock into producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It includes methods that use bacteria, algae or fungi to facilitate the reaction.
[C10J 2300/1684] with electrolysis of water -- The subclass C10J 2300/1684 covers gasification techniques in which water is electrolysed to supply hydrogen that is combined with gases generated from solid carbonaceous feedstock, such as coal or coke, to produce synthesis gas, water-gas or producer gas. It details the integration of electrolysis into the overall gasification process.
[C10J 2300/1687] with steam generation -- This subclass deals with gasification techniques that incorporate steam generation while producing producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous materials, detailing how steam is generated and used within the reaction process.
[C10J 2300/169] with water treatments -- C10J 2300/169 covers water-treatment steps used in gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks for producing producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It includes methods such as quenching, scrubbing, cooling, condensation and moisture removal that control steam content, remove impurities and improve gas quality during the gasification process.
[C10J 2300/1693] with storage facilities for intermediate, feed and/or product -- This CPC subclass refers to gasification methods for producing producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous material where the plant incorporates storage facilities for the feedstock, intermediate streams or the final gas product. It specifies the inclusion of such storage in the overall gasification process.
[C10J 2300/1696] with phase separation, e.g. after condensation -- This subcategory refers to gasification methods in which, after producing producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous material, the gas stream undergoes phase separation-typically by condensing moisture or other condensable components-to separate liquid phases from the remaining gas. It applies when a condensation step is used to remove or recover condensates as part of the overall gasification process.
[C10J 2300/18] Details of the gasification process, e.g. loops, autothermal operation -- C10J 2300/18 covers the specific aspects of gasification processes used in petroleum, gas or coke industries, focusing on operational details such as loop configurations and autothermal operation. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas processing, and production of synthesis gases from solid carbonaceous feedstock.
[C10J 2300/1807] Recycle loops, e.g. gas, solids, heating medium, water -- The classification C10J 2300/1807 concerns gasification systems in which gases, solid residues, heating media and water are continuously recirculated to improve efficiency and control process conditions. It includes the design and operation of recycle loops that return producer gas, char, hot oil, steam or cooling water back into the reactor or related units.
[C10J 2300/1815] for carbon dioxide -- This CPC subclass deals with methods and apparatus where carbon dioxide is involved in the gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It includes steps such as feeding CO₂, reacting, or using CO₂ as a reactant or carrier in the process.
[C10J 2300/1823] for synthesis gas -- The C10J 2300/1823 subclass covers gasification techniques specifically aimed at producing synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstocks. It includes detailed methods, conditions and apparatus for converting coal, coke, biomass or similar solids into a mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen used as synthesis gas in downstream chemical processes.
[C10J 2300/183] Non-continuous or semi-continuous processes -- The subclass C10J 2300/183 covers gasification methods that are performed in batch, batch-wise or semi-continuous mode rather than continuously, including start-up, shut-down and intermittent feeding of solid carbonaceous feedstock to produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas.
[C10J 2300/1838] Autothermal gasification by injection of oxygen or steam -- Autothermal gasification by injection of oxygen or steam refers to a gasification process in which solid carbonaceous material is converted into synthesis gas while the heat required for the reaction is supplied by the exothermic oxidation of a portion of the feedstock using injected oxygen or steam. The method maintains a self-sustaining temperature without external fuel, producing producer gas, water-gas or syngas as desired.
[C10J 2300/1846] Partial oxidation, i.e. injection of air or oxygen only -- The subclass C10J 2300/1846 covers gasification techniques in which solid carbonaceous material is partially oxidised by injecting air or oxygen, creating synthesis or producer gas without full combustion. It deals specifically with the controlled introduction of oxidant to achieve partial oxidation as part of the producer-gas, water-gas or synthesis-gas production process.
[C10J 2300/1853] Steam reforming, i.e. injection of steam only -- Steam reforming is the process in which only steam is injected into a solid carbonaceous feedstock to generate synthesis gas, typically a mixture of hydrogen and carbon monoxide. This method is employed in petroleum and gas industries to produce producer gas, water-gas, or synthesis gas without additional oxidants.
[C10J 2300/1861] Heat exchange between at least two process streams -- The code C10J 2300/1861 classifies technologies that provide heat exchange between two or more process streams within gasification operations that produce producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstocks. It applies to equipment and methods that transfer thermal energy among the reacting, cooling or heating streams to improve efficiency or control temperature.
[C10J 2300/1869] with one stream being air, oxygen or ozone -- The CPC subclass C10J 2300/1869 covers gasification processes in which the single gas stream introduced to the solid carbonaceous feed is air, oxygen or ozone. This subclass falls under the production of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous material within petroleum, gas or coke industry operations.
[C10J 2300/1876] with one stream being combustion gas -- This CPC subclass deals with gasification of solid carbonaceous feedstocks where one of the product streams is a combustion gas, used for heating, power generation or as a reactant. It covers methods and apparatus that generate synthesis, water-gas or producer gas while separating out a combustion-gas stream.
[C10J 2300/1884] with one stream being synthesis gas -- C10J 2300/1884 covers gasification methods in which the output includes a synthesis-gas stream as one of multiple product streams. It pertains to the production of producer, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous feedstocks, typically in petroleum, gas or coke operations. The classification is used for patents describing such single-stream synthesis-gas configurations.
[C10J 2300/1892] with one stream being water/steam -- This CPC subclass covers gasification methods in which a single stream of water or steam is introduced to react with solid carbonaceous material, generating producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It details the use of water/steam as the sole fluidizing and reacting medium within gasification processes.
[C10J 3/00] Production of combustible gases containing carbon monoxide from solid carbonaceous fuels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of combustible gases containing carbon monoxide from solid carbonaceous fuels as well as a slurry of solid carbonaceous, such as a coal water slurry.
[C10J 3/002] Horizontal gasifiers, e.g. belt-type gasifiers -- Horizontal gasifiers, such as belt-type gasifiers, are reactor designs where solid fuel travels horizontally on a moving belt while reacting with oxygen or steam to generate combustible gases rich in carbon monoxide, hydrogen and nitrogen. This layout enables continuous feeding and ash removal, enhancing heat transfer and scalability for producing producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous materials.
[C10J 3/005] Rotary drum or kiln gasifiers -- This CPC subclass covers rotary drum or kiln gasifiers used to produce combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels. The technology involves a rotating drum or stationary kiln where the fuel is heated and partially oxidized to generate a gas mixture rich in carbon monoxide and hydrogen. It is classified under C10J 3/005 within the chemistry section for petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10J 3/007] Screw type gasifiers -- The CPC entry C10J 3/007 covers screw-type gasifiers, which are rotating-shaft devices that continuously feed solid carbonaceous material and air or steam to generate combustible producer gases such as water-gas or synthesis gas rich in carbon monoxide. These units are used in petroleum, gas and coke processing to convert solid fuels into synthesis-gas streams.
[C10J 3/02] Fixed-bed gasification of lump fuel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All beds undergoing reaction of gasification of solid fuel, e.g. fixed-bed, moving bed gasification, such as a Lurgi gasifier, rotary drum gasification.
[C10J 3/04] Cyclic processes, e.g. alternate blast and run -- C10J 3/04 covers cyclic methods for producing combustible gases such as carbon-monoxide-rich producer, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels, where the process alternates between a blasting phase that introduces air or oxygen and a run phase that allows the gas to be generated and collected.
[C10J 3/06] Continuous processes -- C10J 3/06 covers continuous methods for generating combustible gases rich in carbon monoxide from solid carbonaceous fuels such as coal, coke or carbon residues. It focuses on uninterrupted, industrial-scale production of water-gas, producer gas or synthesis gas, rather than batch or intermittent techniques.
[C10J 3/08] with ash-removal in liquid state -- C10J 3/08 is a CPC subclass within the chemistry section that deals with producing combustible gases, such as carbon monoxide-rich producer gas, from solid carbonaceous fuels. It specifically covers processes that remove ash in a liquid phase during gas generation.
[C10J 3/10] using external heating -- C10J 3/10 covers processes for producing combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels or mixtures containing them, where the reaction is driven by external heat sources rather than by the fuel's own combustion.
[C10J 3/12] using solid heat-carriers -- C10J 3/12 covers processes that use solid heat-carriers such as sand, metal particles or ceramics to transfer heat to solid carbonaceous fuels during gasification, producing combustible gases like producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. The subclass focuses on the role of the solid carrier in facilitating the reaction rather than the fuel or gas composition.
[C10J 3/14] using gaseous heat-carriers -- C10J 3/14 covers the use of gaseous heat-carriers in the production of combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels. It includes methods where hot gases like steam, carbon dioxide or inert gases transfer heat to the solid material to generate carbon-monoxide-rich gas.
[C10J 3/16] simultaneously reacting oxygen and water with the carbonaceous material -- The C10J 3/16 subclass covers processes in which solid carbonaceous material is contacted simultaneously with oxygen and water (steam), allowing both oxidation and steam-carbon reactions to occur in the same reactor. This combined approach produces combustible gases such as synthesis gas, water-gas or producer gas, enhancing efficiency and control of the gas composition.
[C10J 3/18] using electricity -- This subclass covers methods for producing combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous materials where electricity is used as the energy source, for example by electric-arc, plasma or resistive heating techniques.
[C10J 3/20] Apparatus -- C10J 3/20 covers apparatus used to generate combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels, typically by partial combustion or gasification of coal, coke, charcoal or similar materials. The devices are designed to control reaction conditions and handle the resulting carbon-monoxide-rich gas streams for industrial or energy applications.
[C10J 3/22] Arrangements or dispositions of valves or flues -- C10J 3/22 pertains to the placement and design of valves, flues and related piping in facilities that generate producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels. It covers how these components are arranged to control the flow and composition of combustible gases.
[C10J 3/24] to permit flow of gases or vapours other than upwardly through the fuel bed -- This classification covers processes for generating synthesis or producer gas from solid carbon fuels where the design intentionally allows gases or vapours to move through the fuel bed in a direction other than upward, such as horizontally or downward, often using specific bed structures, channels or pressure differentials. It falls under the broader class of production of combustible gases from solid carbonaceous material.
[C10J 3/26] downwardly -- This CPC subclass C10J 3/26 covers gasification processes in which solid carbonaceous fuels are converted to combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas using a downward flow configuration. The term "downwardly" refers to reactors or gas-moving zones that operate with the gas or solid moving from top to bottom.
[C10J 3/28] fully automatic -- C10J 3/28 covers fully automatic methods for producing combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels, including coal or coke, using continuous, self-regulating equipment.
[C10J 3/30] Fuel charging devices -- C10J 3/30 pertains to devices that introduce solid carbonaceous fuel into gas-producing reactors for the manufacture of combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It includes hoppers, feeders, conveyors and other mechanisms that control the rate, distribution and feeding of solid fuel during gasification. The classification is used for patents concerning fuel charging equipment in the petroleum and coke industry.
[C10J 3/32] Devices for distributing fuel evenly over the bed or for stirring up the fuel bed -- The C10J 3/32 subclass covers apparatus designed to spread solid carbonaceous fuel uniformly across a reaction bed or to agitate the bed during gasification. Such devices ensure consistent contact with the oxidizing medium, improving efficiency and product quality in the production of synthesis or water-gas from solid fuels.
[C10J 3/34] Grates -- C10J 3/34 "Grates" classifies the design, arrangement and components of grates used to support solid carbonaceous fuels in producer-gas, water-gas or synthesis-gas generation processes, covering grate structures, feeding mechanisms and related features that enable efficient gasification of solid fuels.
[C10J 3/36] Fixed grates -- Fixed grates are stationary grate structures used in reactors that carbonize solid carbonaceous fuels to generate combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. They support the fuel while allowing controlled air flow and ash removal, enabling continuous gas production in petroleum-related gasification processes.
[C10J 3/38] with stirring beams -- The C10J 3/38 entry covers methods for producing combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels in which stirring beams are employed to agitate the material and improve the gas-forming reaction. It falls under the chemistry domain for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the production of carbon-monoxide-rich gases from solid feedstocks.
[C10J 3/40] Movable grates -- Movable grates are mechanical components used in reactors that gasify solid carbonaceous materials such as coal, coke or biomass to produce combustible gases like producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. By shifting the grate surface, the feedstock is continuously exposed to the high-temperature, oxidizing zone, ensuring uniform combustion and efficient gas generation.
[C10J 3/42] Rotary grates -- C10J 3/42 covers rotary grates employed in the production of combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels. The classification pertains to the design and operation of rotating grate systems that feed, support and move the solid material through the gasifier, facilitating efficient carbon monoxide generation. It falls under the chemistry branch for petroleum, gas or coke industry processes.
[C10J 3/44] adapted for use on vehicles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for fixed bed gasification of lump fuel adapted for use on vehicles, e.g. portable gasification apparatus, gasification occurring on a vehicle burning the produced gas in the engine
[C10J 3/46] Gasification of granular or pulverulent flues in suspension -- The CPC entry C10J 3/46 covers the gasification of solid carbonaceous fuels that are in granular or pulverulent form and are processed in a suspension. It refers to methods where these particles are reacted with oxidising agents in a fluidised or slurry environment to produce combustible gases rich in carbon monoxide, hydrogen and other synthesis-gas components.

[C10J 3/48] Apparatus -- C10J 3/48 covers apparatus for producing combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels like coal, coke or charcoal. The devices are designed for gasification processes that generate carbon monoxide-rich gases used in heating, power generation or chemical synthesis.
[C10J 3/482] Gasifiers with stationary fluidised bed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gasifiers with stationary fluidised bed  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gasifiers with circulating fluidised bed -> C10J3/56
[C10J 3/485] Entrained flow gasifiers -- C10J 3/485 covers entrained flow gasifiers, which are devices that gasify solid carbonaceous fuels by injecting them with oxidants at high speed, producing a combustible synthesis gas containing carbon monoxide, hydrogen and other gases. These units operate at high-temperature and high-pressure conditions, commonly used in petrochemical or power generation settings.
[C10J 3/487] Swirling or cyclonic gasifiers -- This subclass covers gasifiers that generate combustible gases from solid carbonaceous feedstocks by creating a swirling or cyclonic flow within the reaction zone. The rotational motion promotes intense mixing of fuel, oxidant and steam, improving conversion efficiency and uniformity in the production of producer-gas, water-gas or synthesis-gas.
[C10J 3/50] Fuel charging devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gasifiers with nozzles, gasifiers with screw feeders, feed pressurisation using lock hoppers, or preparation, such as milling and drying, of fuel for gasification process
[C10J 3/503] for gasifiers with stationary fluidised bed -- The CPC subclass C10J 3/503 covers gasifiers that employ a stationary fluidised-bed reactor to convert solid carbonaceous fuels into combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas, typically rich in carbon monoxide and hydrogen. It falls under the broader category of producing these gases from solid fuels.
[C10J 3/506] for entrained flow gasifiers -- C10J 3/506 covers entrained flow gasifiers used to produce combustible gases such as synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels. These devices inject solid feedstock into a high-temperature reaction zone where it reacts with oxygen or steam, yielding a mixture rich in carbon monoxide and hydrogen.
[C10J 3/52] Ash-removing devices -- C10J 3/52 covers devices used to remove ash from reactors or furnaces during the production of combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous materials. The devices may be mechanical, pneumatic or magnetic systems that separate solid residues from the gas stream to improve efficiency and prevent fouling of downstream equipment. This subclass is part of the chemistry field for petroleum, gas or coke industry processes.
[C10J 3/523] for gasifiers with stationary fluidised bed -- C10J 3/523 covers gasifiers that use a stationary fluidised-bed to convert solid carbonaceous fuels into combustible synthesis gases rich in carbon monoxide. It falls under the chemistry section for petroleum and gas industries, specifically the production of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid fuels.
[C10J 3/526] for entrained flow gasifiers -- The subclass C10J 3/526 covers gasification processes that employ entrained-flow reactors, where solid carbonaceous fuels such as coal or coke are injected into a high-temperature oxygen-rich stream to produce combustible gases rich in carbon monoxide and hydrogen. It applies to the production of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from these solid feedstocks.

[C10J 3/56] Apparatus -- The C10J 3/56 section covers the apparatus employed to generate combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels. It includes equipment for gasification, combustion chambers, reactors and related supporting devices that facilitate the production of carbon-monoxide-rich gases.
[C10J 3/57] Gasification using molten salts or metals -- The CPC subclass C10J 3/57 covers processes that gasify solid carbonaceous materials-such as coal, coke or biomass-by employing molten salts or molten metals as the reaction medium, thereby producing combustible synthesis gases rich in carbon monoxide and hydrogen. This method is a variant of producer-gas production within the broader chemistry field for petroleum, gas and coke industries.
[C10J 3/58] combined with pre-distillation of the fuel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gasification including a pre-distillation before gasification, such as a pyrolysis step prior to gasification step.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Pre-distillation, such as a pyrolysis, followed by gasification of pyrolysis gas and/or pyrolysis liquids, such as pyrolysis tar cracking or reforming, without gasification step of solid charge -> C10K , C10G9/00 , C01B3/24 , C01B3/34
[C10J 3/60] Processes -- C10J 3/60 covers processes for producing combustible gases rich in carbon monoxide from solid carbonaceous fuels such as coal, coke or carbonaceous mixtures, including the generation of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas by reacting the solid material with steam, oxygen, air or other oxidising agents. It focuses on the methods of converting these solid fuels into gaseous mixtures suitable for energy or chemical applications.
[C10J 3/62] with separate withdrawal of the distillation products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gasification including a pre-distillation before gasification whereby the distillation products such as pyrolysis gas (pyrogas) are withdrawn separately from the syngas. Examples are shown in the following figures:  **Glossary:** - Distillation products: Volatile products released from solid charge during pre-distillation or pyrolysis pre-step.

The decomposition may occur in a reforming zone separate from the gasification zone, such as partial combustion of pyrolysis gas or cracking of pyrolysis tar. Examples are shown in the following figures:  **Glossary:** - Distillation products: Volatile products released from solid charge during pre-distillation or pyrolysis pre-step.
[C10J 3/66] by introducing them into the gasification zone -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gasification including the decomposition of the distillation products, such as pyrolysis gas (pyrogas) and/or pyrolysis liquids (pyroliquids).  Examples are shown in the following figures:  **Glossary:** - Gasification zone: Zone where solids are converted by gasification, such as by partial combustion or steam reforming
[C10J 3/72] Other features -- C10J 3/72 covers miscellaneous or additional aspects of producing combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels that are not specifically covered by other subclasses. It includes auxiliary steps, equipment configurations, or process conditions that fall outside the main feature categories within this group.
[C10J 3/721] Multistage gasification, e.g. plural parallel or serial gasification stages -- C10J 3/721 relates to multistage gasification processes, where solid carbonaceous fuels are converted to combustible gases such as carbon monoxide and hydrogen through two or more parallel or serial reaction stages. The classification addresses the design and operation of these sequential gas-ification steps within the broader production of producer, water-gas, or synthesis gas.
[C10J 3/723] Controlling or regulating the gasification process -- The C10J 3/723 subclass covers technologies for controlling or regulating the gasification of solid carbonaceous fuels, including methods and devices that monitor and adjust parameters such as temperature, pressure, feed rate, air-or-steam supply, and gas composition to optimise the production of combustible gases like producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas. It focuses on process-control strategies that ensure stable operation, improve efficiency and maintain desired gas quality.
[C10J 3/725] Redox processes -- C10J 3/725 redox processes cover methods that employ oxidation-reduction reactions to convert solid carbonaceous fuels into combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas, typically through partial combustion or gasification.
[C10J 3/726] Start-up -- C10J 3/726 concerns the start-up phase of producer-gas or synthesis-gas plants that convert solid carbonaceous fuels into combustible gases. It covers the procedures, control methods and equipment used to ignite the fuel, introduce the feed, and bring the gasifier from a cold condition to stable operation.
[C10J 3/728] Shut down -- The subclass addresses techniques for terminating or pausing the generation of combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas produced from solid carbonaceous fuels. It includes methods, control systems and operational steps used to shut down the gas-producing reaction safely and efficiently.
[C10J 3/74] Construction of shells or jackets -- The C10J 3/74 entry covers the design and fabrication of outer protective structures such as shells, jackets or casings that surround reactors, furnaces or other equipment used to produce combustible gases from solid carbonaceous fuels. These constructions provide mechanical strength, thermal insulation and containment for high-temperature processes involved in making producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas.
[C10J 3/76] Water jackets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gasifiers having cooling means inside or on the outside of the gasification chamber itself, i.e. same vessel, such as shown in the following figures:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gasification with separate waste heat boilers -> C10J3/86
[C10J 3/78] High-pressure apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Supercritical gasification
[C10J 3/80] with arrangements for preheating the blast or the water vapour -- This subclass deals with processes for producing combustible gases such as producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas from solid carbonaceous fuels where the incoming blast air or steam is preheated before entering the gasification zone. It includes arrangements such as heat exchangers or other means to raise the temperature of the blast or water vapour to improve efficiency.
[C10J 3/82] Gas withdrawal means -- C10J 3/82 concerns the devices and methods for extracting the gaseous products, such as carbon-monoxide rich synthesis gas, from reactors where solid carbonaceous fuels are gasified. The classification covers pipes, ducts, valves and other withdrawal equipment used in the production of producer gas, water-gas or synthesis gas.
[C10J 3/84] with means for removing dust or tar from the gas -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gasifiers having a gas filter, cyclone, water spray or a quench, e.g. such as shown in the following figures:  Tar cracking, e.g. reforming, partial oxidation
[C10J 3/845] Quench rings -- Quench rings are tubular or perforated devices installed in the discharge zone of solid-fuel gasifiers to rapidly cool the hot synthesis or producer gas. By lowering temperature quickly they prevent tar condensation, control reaction rates and protect downstream equipment, facilitating the production of combustible carbon-monoxide-rich gases from coal, coke or other carbonaceous feedstocks.
[C10J 3/86] combined with waste-heat boilers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gasifiers connected with a separate heat exchanger, such as shown in following figures:
[C10K] PURIFYING OR MODIFYING THE CHEMICAL COMPOSITION OF COMBUSTIBLE GASES CONTAINING CARBON MONOXIDE -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Purifying combustible gases containing carbon monoxide, e.g. synthesis gas, e.g. by dust removal, condensing non-gaseous materials, washing, adsorption using solids such as active carbon; including regeneration of purifying material.  Modifying the chemical composition of combustible gases containing carbon monoxide to produce an improved fuel, e.g. by altering the calorific value, by reducing the carbon monoxide content (e.g. to zero), by catalytic treatment or by mixing with gases.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Isolation of hydrogen from mixtures containing hydrogen and carbon monoxide -> C01B3/50  **Glossary:** - Combustible gas containing carbon monoxide: syngas, synthesis gas, coke oven gas, pyrolysis gas, carbonisation gas
[C10K 1/00] Purifying combustible gases containing carbon monoxide -- The CPC subclass C10K 1/00 covers processes and equipment for removing carbon monoxide from combustible gas streams, a step often required in petroleum, gas or coke operations to improve fuel quality or meet emissions limits. Methods include catalytic oxidation, adsorption, membrane separation and other treatments that lower CO concentrations.
[C10K 1/001] working-up the condensates  recovering of NH 3  and NH 4  salts   C01C1/00 ; working-up or purifying tars and tar-oils  C10C1/00 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Working-up or purifying tars and tar-oils -> C10C1/00
[C10K 1/002] Removal of contaminants -- C10K 1/002 covers techniques for purifying combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by removing contaminants such as sulfur compounds, nitrogen oxides, moisture and other impurity species. It includes physical, chemical or catalytic methods and apparatus designed to improve the gas quality for downstream use.
[C10K 1/003] of acid contaminants, e.g. acid gas removal -- C10K 1/003 pertains to processes for eliminating acid contaminants from combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide, such as acid-gas removal in petroleum, gas or coke operations. It falls under the broader category of purifying or modifying the chemical composition of such gases.
[C10K 1/004] Sulfur containing contaminants, e.g. hydrogen sulfide -- C10K 1/004 covers methods and apparatus for removing sulfur-containing contaminants, such as hydrogen sulfide, from combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide. It is placed under purifying or modifying the chemical composition of combustible gases containing carbon monoxide within the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10K 1/005] Carbon dioxide -- C10K 1/005 relates to the removal or control of carbon dioxide during the purification of combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide, such as syngas or blast-furnace gas. It covers processes and equipment used in petroleum, gas and coke industries to adjust the gas composition by extracting CO₂, thereby improving fuel quality or meeting emission standards.
[C10K 1/006] Hydrogen cyanide -- This subclass covers techniques for the removal or conversion of hydrogen cyanide from combustible gas mixtures that contain carbon monoxide. It includes processes, reactors, and catalysts used to reduce HCN concentrations in synthesis gas, blast-furnace gas or other CO-rich streams.
[C10K 1/007] of metal compounds -- This subclass covers methods that employ metal compounds-such as oxides, sulfides, or other metallic agents-to remove, convert, or otherwise modify carbon monoxide in combustible gas streams. It includes catalytic, adsorption-based, or absorption processes that purify the gas for industrial use.
[C10K 1/008] Alkali metal compounds -- C10K 1/008 concerns the use of alkali metal compounds, such as sodium or potassium salts, in processes that purify combustible gases containing carbon monoxide. It falls under chemistry for petroleum and gas industries and addresses methods for modifying the chemical composition of CO-rich gases to reduce carbon monoxide levels or improve gas quality.
[C10K 1/02] Dust removal -- C10K 1/02 addresses techniques for removing dust from combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide, such as those produced in petroleum, gas or coke processing. It covers filtration, cyclonic separation, electrostatic precipitation and related methods that clean the gas stream before further use or emission.
[C10K 1/022] by baffle plates -- The classification C10K 1/022 falls under chemistry for the petroleum, gas or coke industries and concerns methods of purifying or modifying the chemical composition of combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide. This subclass specifically covers purification processes that use baffle plates to remove or reduce CO in the gas stream.
[C10K 1/024] by filtration -- This subclass covers methods for cleaning combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide using filtration techniques. It falls under the broader field of chemistry for petroleum and coke industry processes, specifically the purification of carbon monoxide-containing gases. The code C10K 1/024 designates filtration as a means of removing impurities from such gases.
[C10K 1/026] by centrifugal forces  cyclones  B04C -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus using free vortex flow, e.g. cyclones, per se -> B04C
[C10K 1/028] by electrostatic precipitation  separating dispersed particles from gases or vapour by electrostatic effect in general  B03C3/00 -- The C10K 1/028 subclass covers purification of combustible gases containing carbon monoxide by electrostatic precipitation, a process that removes dispersed particles from the gas stream using an electrostatic field. This method falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and gas-cleaning techniques.
[C10K 1/04] by cooling to condense non-gaseous materials -- C10K 1/04 covers the purification of combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by cooling the gas to condense non-gaseous constituents. The method removes soot, tar or other liquids by lowering temperature, allowing them to separate from the gas stream.
[C10K 1/043] adding solvents as vapour to prevent naphthalene- or resin deposits -- This CPC subclass (C10K 1/043) covers the step of introducing solvent vapours into the purification process for combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide in order to prevent the buildup of naphthalene- or resin-type deposits on equipment. It is nested under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, specifically the purification or modification of the chemical composition of such gases.
[C10K 1/046] Reducing the tar content -- The C10K 1/046 subclass covers processes and apparatuses for lowering tar levels in combustible gas streams that contain carbon monoxide, typically through treatment steps such as catalytic cracking, adsorption, scrubbing, or filtration. It is applied in petroleum and gas refining where purified fuel gases are required, focusing on extracting or destroying tar-rich hydrocarbons before the gas is used or further processed.
[C10K 1/06] combined with spraying with water -- A process for cleaning combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by atomising or spraying water into the gas stream, where the water spray is used alone or in combination with other treatment steps to absorb or oxidise the carbon monoxide.
[C10K 1/08] by washing with liquids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Acid gas removal, e.g. H2S, HCN, CO2, by absorption process, e.g. amine scrubbing
[C10K 1/085] two direct washing treatments, one with an aqueous liquid and one with a non-aqueous liquid -- C10K 1/085 covers a purification process for combustible gases containing carbon monoxide that uses two successive direct washing steps, first employing an aqueous liquid and then a non-aqueous liquid, to remove impurities or alter the gas composition.
[C10K 1/10] with aqueous liquids -- C10K 1/10 covers processes for cleaning combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by contacting them with water-based liquids. The aqueous phase removes impurities such as sulfur compounds, nitrogen oxides, or other pollutants, improving gas quality for downstream use in petroleum, gas or coke operations.
[C10K 1/101] with water only -- C10K 1/101 covers processes for cleaning combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide using water as the only treating medium. It falls under methods for purifying or modifying the chemical composition of such gases within the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10K 1/102] containing free acid -- The CPC subclass C10K 1/102 deals with processes or equipment for purifying combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide when the gas also includes free acid, falling under the broader category of refining or modifying the chemical composition of such gases in the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10K 1/103] alkali- or earth-alkali- or NH 4  salts or inorganic acids derived from sulfur -- The C10K 1/103 subclass covers processes that use alkaline, earth-alkaline or ammonium salts, or inorganic acids derived from sulfur, to purify or chemically modify combustible gases containing carbon monoxide, typically in petroleum-related gas treatment operations.
[C10K 1/105] containing metal compounds other than alkali- or earth-alkali carbonates, -hydroxides, oxides, or salts of inorganic acids derived from sulfur -- The CPC subclass C10K 1/105 covers purification processes for combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide in which the removal agent is a metal compound other than alkali- or earth-alkali carbonates, hydroxides, oxides or salts of inorganic acids derived from sulfur. It specifies the use of alternative metal-based sorbents, catalysts or reagents for CO removal within the broader context of petroleum- and gas-industry gas-cleaning technologies.
[C10K 1/106] containing Fe compounds -- C10K 1/106 covers methods for purifying combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by using iron-based compounds. It falls under the chemistry subgroup for petroleum, gas or coke industries and specifically addresses the treatment of gas streams with iron compounds to remove or modify carbon monoxide content.
[C10K 1/107] containing As-, Sb-, Sn compounds -- C10K 1/107 covers methods for purifying combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by using compounds of arsenic, antimony or tin. These agents are employed to remove or convert carbon monoxide during gas cleaning in the petroleum, gas or coke industries. The classification applies to processes that modify the chemical composition of such gases using the specified metalloid compounds.
[C10K 1/108] containing Cu compounds -- C10K 1/108 covers methods for purifying combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by using copper compounds. The classification includes processes in which copper salts, oxides or other copper-based substances act as catalysts or absorbents to remove CO or other impurities.
[C10K 1/12] alkaline-reacting -- The C10K 1/12 subclass deals with methods for purifying combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by reacting the gas with alkaline substances. It covers processes in which an alkaline reagent-such as sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide or other bases-is used to absorb or chemically convert the carbon monoxide into less hazardous compounds, thereby reducing its concentration in the gas stream.
[C10K 1/121] containing NH 3  only (possibly in combination with NH 4  salts) -- C10K 1/121 covers processes for purifying combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by treating them with ammonia alone or in combination with ammonium salts to remove impurities or modify the gas composition. The method is used in petroleum, gas or coke industry settings to improve gas quality.
[C10K 1/122] containing only carbonates, bicarbonates, hydroxides or oxides of alkali-metals (including Mg) -- This subclass defines methods for purifying combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by using only carbonates, bicarbonates, hydroxides or oxides of alkali-metals, including magnesium, as the reacting agents. It falls under C10K 1 for purifying CO-containing gases and specifies that the absorbent or reagent must be limited to those alkaline mineral compounds.
[C10K 1/123] containing alkali-, earth-alkali- or NH 4  salts of inorganic acids derived from sulfur -- This CPC entry covers purification of combustible gases containing carbon monoxide by using alkali, alkaline-earth or ammonium salts of inorganic acids derived from sulfur. It falls within the chemistry subclass for petroleum and gas processing, specifically methods for modifying the chemical composition of CO-rich fuels.
[C10K 1/124] containing metal compounds other than alkali- or earth-alkali carbonates, hydroxides- or oxides- or salts of inorganic acids derived from sulfur -- C10K 1/124 classifies methods for removing carbon monoxide from combustible gases that employ metal compounds other than alkali- or alkaline-earth carbonates, hydroxides, oxides, or salts of inorganic sulfur-derived acids; it includes the use of transition-metal sulfides, phosphates or other non-alkaline metal reagents in the purification step.
[C10K 1/125] containing Fe compounds -- C10K 1/125 pertains to processes or devices for purifying combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by employing iron compounds. It includes the use of Fe-based sorbents, catalysts or additives to remove, convert or reduce CO in gas streams, typically in petroleum, gas or coke operations.
[C10K 1/126] containing As-, Sb-, Sn compounds -- This CPC subclass C10K 1/126 covers methods for purifying combustible gases containing carbon monoxide using compounds of arsenic, antimony or tin. It is placed under chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the purification or modification of the chemical composition of such gases.
[C10K 1/127] containing Cu compounds -- The subclass C10K 1/127 covers processes or devices that purify combustible gases containing carbon monoxide by using copper-based compounds. It pertains to the treatment of gas streams in petroleum, gas or coke operations where copper compounds act as catalysts or absorbents to modify or remove the CO component.
[C10K 1/128] containing organic oxygen transferring compounds, e.g. sulfoxides -- C10K 1/128 pertains to methods for cleaning combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by employing organic oxygen-transferring agents, such as sulfoxides, to alter or remove the CO. This sub-class falls under the broader category of petroleum-related chemistry focused on purifying or modifying the chemical composition of such gases.
[C10K 1/14] organic -- C10K 1/14 concerns organic techniques for cleaning combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide, as used in petroleum, natural-gas and coke processing. It includes chemical processes, reagents or solvents of organic nature that remove or modify CO and related contaminants to improve gas quality.
[C10K 1/143] containing amino groups -- C10K 1/143 covers methods for cleaning combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by using substances that have amino groups as the active component. The classification is situated under chemistry, petroleum and gas processing, focusing on purification or modification of the gas's chemical composition.
[C10K 1/146] alkali-, earth-alkali- or NH 4  salts -- The subclass C10K 1/146 covers purification of combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by using alkali, alkaline-earth or ammonium salts. It includes processes in which these salts remove or modify contaminants during petroleum or gas treatment.
[C10K 1/16] with non-aqueous liquids -- This subclass covers methods for cleaning or modifying combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by contacting them with liquids that are not water-based, such as organic solvents or other non-aqueous media. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry and petroleum/gas industry processes.
[C10K 1/165] at temperatures below zero degrees Celsius -- C10K 1/165 covers processes for removing carbon monoxide from combustible gas streams at temperatures below 0 °C, typically by absorption, adsorption or catalytic conversion performed under sub-freezing conditions. The classification is used for technologies in the petroleum and gas sector that purify fuel gases while operating in cold environments.
[C10K 1/18] hydrocarbon oils -- C10K 1/18 covers the use of hydrocarbon oils in the purification or modification of combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide, typically as a scrubbing or absorption medium to remove or reduce CO levels during gas processing. This subclass falls under the broader category of methods for treating petroleum-derived gases.
[C10K 1/20] by treating with solids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Adsorption of impurities  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Separation of gases by adsorption, e.g. PSA -> B01D53/02 - Separation of gases by chemical reaction -> B01D53/34
[C10K 1/205] Methods and apparatus for treating the purifying masses without their regeneration  recovering of sulfur  C01B17/00 ; recovering of cyanide compounds  C01C3/00 -- This CPC subclass covers processes and equipment that treat purification media used for cleaning carbon-monoxide-containing gases without regenerating the media, and includes the recovery of sulfur and cyanide compounds from the spent material.
[C10K 1/22] Apparatus, e.g. dry box purifiers -- C10K 1/22 covers devices for removing carbon monoxide from combustible gas streams, such as dry-box type purifiers that adsorb or chemically convert CO. It falls under the chemistry group for treating gases in the petroleum, gas or coke sector.
[C10K 1/24] Supporting means for the purifying material -- C10K 1/24 covers the fixtures, frames, or other support structures that hold the purification media used to clean combustible gases containing carbon monoxide. It includes any device that positions, secures, or channels the material-such as filters, cartridges, or beds-within gas-purifying installations in petroleum, gas or coke processing.
[C10K 1/26] Regeneration of the purifying material -- The subclass C10K 1/26 covers techniques for restoring or regenerating the materials-such as sorbents, catalysts or absorbents-used to purify combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide. It includes thermal, chemical or pressure-based processes that refresh the purifying medium for repeated use.
[C10K 1/28] Controlling the gas flow through the purifiers -- This CPC subclass deals with methods and apparatus for regulating the flow of gas through purification units used to treat combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide, such as in petroleum or coke processing. The focus is on controlling gas distribution to improve removal of CO during purification.
[C10K 1/30] with moving purifying masses -- This subclass covers methods that clean carbon-monoxide-containing fuel gases by passing them through a bed of solid purifying material that moves relative to the gas flow, such as fluidized, rotating or conveyor-type beds of sorbents or catalysts. The moving masses provide continuous contact and regeneration while the gas is purified.
[C10K 1/32] with selectively adsorptive solids, e.g. active carbon -- The CPC subclass C10K 1/32 pertains to processes for cleaning combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide by employing selectively adsorptive solid materials, such as activated carbon, to remove the carbon monoxide or other contaminants. It falls under the broader hierarchy of chemistry, specifically petroleum, gas or coke industries, and focuses on purifying or modifying the chemical composition of such gases.
[C10K 1/34] by catalytic conversion of impurities to more readily removable materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. carbonyl sulfide (COS) hydrolysis
[C10K 3/00] Modifying the chemical composition of combustible gases containing carbon monoxide to produce an improved fuel, e.g. one of different calorific value, which may be free from carbon monoxide -- **Glossary:** - Calorific value: Thermal energy content
[C10K 3/001] by thermal treatment -- This subclass relates to processes that thermally treat combustible gases containing carbon monoxide in order to alter their chemical composition, such as reducing CO levels or changing the calorific value, thereby producing an improved fuel.
[C10K 3/003] Reducing the tar content -- The CPC entry C10K 3/003 covers methods for lowering the tar content in combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide, typically as part of processes that modify gas composition to yield a cleaner or higher-calorific-value fuel.
[C10K 3/005] by partial oxidation -- Partial oxidation processes that modify the chemical composition of combustible gases containing carbon monoxide, converting them into improved fuels with altered calorific values or reduced carbon-monoxide content. This subclass covers methods that use controlled oxidation to refine the gas mixture rather than complete combustion, aiming to enhance fuel quality and safety.
[C10K 3/006] by steam reforming -- C10K 3/006 covers the modification of combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide through steam-reforming processes. The aim is to alter the gas's chemical composition to create a fuel with a different calorific value, often removing carbon monoxide for a cleaner, more efficient product.
[C10K 3/008] by cracking -- This subclass addresses the production of improved fuels by cracking combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide, breaking larger molecules into smaller hydrocarbons to alter calorific value and reduce CO content. The process typically involves high-temperature treatment or catalytic reactors to achieve the desired composition change.
[C10K 3/02] by catalytic treatment -- C10K 3/02 relates to the catalytic treatment of combustible gases that contain carbon monoxide in order to alter their chemical composition. The process aims to produce an improved fuel, such as one with a different calorific value or reduced carbon-monoxide content, by using catalysts to convert or remove the CO during purification or modification.
[C10K 3/023] Reducing the tar content -- This CPC subclass covers processes that lower the tar content of combustible gases containing carbon monoxide, typically during purification or modification steps to produce a cleaner fuel with altered calorific value. Methods may involve catalytic treatment, adsorption, or other techniques to remove or convert tar, improving fuel performance and reducing emissions.
[C10K 3/026] Increasing the carbon monoxide content, e.g. reverse water-gas shift [RWGS] -- The entry covers processes that increase the carbon-monoxide concentration in combustible gas streams, such as the reverse water-gas shift reaction, to produce fuels with altered calorific values or other improved properties.
[C10K 3/04] reducing the carbon monoxide content -- C10K 3/04 pertains to processes that lower the carbon-monoxide level in combustible gas streams, typically by chemical treatment or purification steps, to produce a fuel with altered calorific value or reduced toxicity. It is used in the petroleum and gas sector to improve fuel quality.
[C10K 3/06] by mixing with gases -- The CPC subclass C10K 3/06 covers processes that modify the chemical composition of combustible gases containing carbon monoxide by mixing them with other gases. The purpose is to produce an improved fuel, such as one with a different calorific value or reduced carbon-monoxide content.
[C10L] FUELS NOT OTHERWISE PROVIDED FOR -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - fire-lighter: easily-combustible composition or shaped product which is designed to initiate the combustion of a larger body of fuel, e.g. briquettes mainly consisting of charcoal
[C10L 1/00] Liquid carbonaceous fuels -- In groups C10L1/12 - C10L1/14 , the last place priority rule is applied, i.e. at each hierarchical level, in the absence of an indication to the contrary, a compound is classified in the last appropriate place. This Note corresponds to IPC Note (1) relating to C10L1/12 - C10L1/14 . If an additive is a mixture of compounds, classification is made for each compound of interest. This Note corresponds to IPC Note (2) relating to C10L1/12 - C10L1/14 . A metal salt or an ammonium salt of a compound is classified as that compound, e.g. a chromium sulfonate is classified as a sulfonate in group C10L1/24 and not in group C10L1/30 . This Note corresponds to IPC Note (3) relating to C10L1/12 - C10L1/14 . When classifying in this group, it is desirable to classify the individual additional components using Combination Sets with symbols chosen from groups C10L1/12 - C10L1/308 . Mixtures of additives are classified in the corresponding main group. Individual additives can be classified using Combination Sets according to the Note above. When several alternatives for the same individual additive are mentioned, e.g. as a Markush-formula, classification may be done in the corresponding main group only, the alternatives being classified using Combination Sets, according to the Note above. Documents classified until April 2003, have been classified with Combination Sets as explained in the Notes above, however using symbols chosen from groups C10L1/10 - C10L1/308 .
[C10L 10/00] Use of additives to fuels or fires for particular purposes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Use of the additive for a specific purpose which is not one mentioned in one of the groups C10L10/02 - C10L10/18 .  Use of the additive which is not a detergent or dispersant ( C10L10/18 ) to prevent, decrease or clean deposits (keep-clean, clean-up), antifouling additive.  **Glossary:** - Deposits: Sediments or sludges coming from deterioration of liquid fuels in a storage vessel, pipes or apparatuses; Also solid carbonaceous residues from fuel combustion, on the walls of the combustion enclosure or apparatuses.
[C10L 10/02] for reducing smoke development -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A combustion improver which decreases emissions from the combustion of a fuel containing it relatively to the emissions from the combustion of the same fuel but without the combustion improver, is classified in this group.
[C10L 1/003] Marking, e.g. coloration by addition of pigments -- C10L 1/003 concerns the incorporation of pigments or other colorants into liquid hydrocarbon fuels to provide visual marking for identification, safety or anti-theft purposes. It covers the selection, preparation and application of such coloring agents in fuels such as gasoline, diesel, or jet fuel.
[C10L 10/04] for minimising corrosion or incrustation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Use of the additive to prevent or clean deposits -> C10L10/00 - Use of the additive to facilitate soot removal -> C10L10/06
[C10L 1/006] Making uninflammable or hardly inflammable -- The code covers processes for decreasing the ignition or combustion propensity of liquid hydrocarbon fuels, including the addition of fire-retardant compounds, chemical modification, or blending to produce fuels that are non-flammable or have very low flammability. It applies to petroleum-derived liquids such as gasoline, diesel or jet fuel that require enhanced safety in storage or transport.
[C10L 10/06] for facilitating soot removal -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Use of the additive to prevent or clean deposits -> C10L10/00 - Use of detergents or dispersants for decreasing, removing or preventing deposits -> C10L10/18
[C10L 10/08] for improving lubricity -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lubricants used in combination with gasoline, for two cycle engines -> C10M

[C10L 10/12] for improving the cetane number -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - CN: Cetane number

[C10L 10/16] Pour-point depressants -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[C10L 10/18] use of detergents or dispersants for purposes not provided for in groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Use of the additive to prevent or clean deposits -> C10L10/00 - Use of additives for facilitating soot removal -> C10L10/06
[C10L 1/02] essentially based on components consisting of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen only -- C10L 1/02 classifies liquid carbonaceous fuels whose constituent molecules contain only carbon, hydrogen and oxygen atoms. It covers fuels derived from such C-H-O components, including various bio-derived or synthetic oils, when no other element types (e.g., nitrogen, sulfur) are present.
[C10L 1/023] for spark ignition -- This CPC subclass deals with liquid hydrocarbon fuels intended for spark-ignition internal combustion engines, covering formulation, additives and treatment methods to improve the performance of gasoline-like fuels.
[C10L 1/026] for compression ignition -- C10L 1/026 pertains to liquid carbonaceous fuels specifically formulated for use in compression-ignition engines, such as diesel or kerosene-based fuels, including additives or blends that enhance self-ignition characteristics.
[C10L 1/04] essentially based on blends of hydrocarbons -- C10L 1/04 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels whose composition is essentially based on blends of hydrocarbons. The class includes mixtures of two or more hydrocarbon fractions, such as gasoline-ethanol or diesel-biofuel blends, designed to achieve specific performance, handling or environmental characteristics. It is used for patents describing fuel formulations that rely on the combined properties of the constituent hydrocarbons.
[C10L 1/06] for spark ignition -- The C10L 1/06 subclass covers inventions relating to the spark-ignition of liquid carbonaceous fuels, such as gasoline, in internal-combustion engines. It includes spark-plug designs, ignition timing systems and related methods that initiate combustion by an electrical spark. This subclass falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, fuels not otherwise provided for, and liquid carbonaceous fuels.
[C10L 1/08] for compression ignition -- C10L 1/08 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels intended for compression ignition, such as diesel and biodiesel used in engines where the fuel self-ignites under high pressure and temperature without a spark. This subclass falls under fuels not otherwise provided for in the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10L 1/10] containing additives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Most of the liquid carbonaceous fuels comprising an inorganic additive and an organic additive have been classified in C10L1/10 , the organic additive being macromolecular or not.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - The components of the liquid carbonaceous fuels; Marking or making uninflammable per se -> C10L1/00 - C10L1/08 - The fuels consisting of coal-oil suspensions or aqueous emulsions -> C10L1/32 - Gaseous fuels and solid fuels -> C10L3/00 - C10L9/12 - Fuels for generating pressure gas, e.g. for rockets -> C06D5/00 - Candles -> C11C - Nuclear fuel -> G21C3/00  **Glossary:** - Polyether polymer: Macromolecular compounds obtained by reactions forming an ether link in themain chain of the macromolecule and comprising 4 or more monomers. - Hydrocarbon polymer or macromolecular compound: Hydrocarbyl group containing 30 or more carbon atoms - Additive: Any compound in the composition with a concentration of at most 2%, in volume or weight
[C10L 11/00] Fire-lighters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Firelighter compositions, usually solid or gel/liquid.
[C10L 11/02] based on refractory porous bodies -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Firelighter which is fire resistant itself and does not or hardly burn along.
[C10L 1/103] stabilisation of anti-knock agents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Liquid carbonaceous fuels comprising an anti-knock additive stabilised by an other additive or compound; the deterioration with time of the anti-knock agent is prevented; the anti-knock agent may be maintained uniformly dispersed in the fuel.  The anti-knock additive and the other additive or compound can be both organic compounds. The fuel may contain no inorganic additive.  Use of a stabilised anti-knock agent as additive in a liquid carbonaceous fuel.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Liquid carbo naceous fuels containing additives, at least one being inorganic -> C10L1/10 , C10L1/106 - Liquid carbonaceous fuels containing only organic compounds as additives -> C10L1/14 , C10L1/143  **Glossary:** - Anti-knock agent: Octane improver, ignition improver in a gasoline fuel . The meaning of "anti-knock" can be broadened to ignition-improving agent for diesel fuels - Ignition-improving agent for diesel fuels: Cetane improver, ignition improver in diesel fuels - Stabilised: Showing oxidative resistance, maintained dispersed
[C10L 11/04] consisting of combustible material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compositions meant to start a fire and to burn along with it, e.g. barbecue lighters.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Matches -> C06F

[C10L 11/06] of a special shape -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Firelighters when the shape of the firelighter is special, i.e. not common and one of the main objects of the invention.
[C10L 11/08] Apparatus therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for the production of firelighters and details thereof.
[C10L 1/12] Inorganic compounds -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[C10L 1/1208] elements -- C10L 1/1208 classifies patents concerning the use or effect of individual chemical elements in liquid carbonaceous fuels such as gasoline, diesel or jet fuel. It covers inventions where elements act as additives, catalysts, corrosion inhibitors or performance enhancers in petroleum-derived liquid fuels.
[C10L 1/1216] metal compounds, e.g. hydrides, carbides -- The CPC class C10L 1/1216 pertains to metal compounds, for example hydrides and carbides, that are incorporated in liquid carbonaceous fuels. It is situated under chemistry, the petroleum, gas or coke industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for, specifically addressing additives for liquid fuel formulations.
[C10L 1/1225] halogen containing compounds -- C10L 1/1225 covers halogen-containing compounds relevant to liquid carbonaceous fuels, such as petroleum-derived gasoline or diesel. The subclass includes additives, intermediates or contaminants that incorporate fluorine, chlorine, bromine or iodine and are used to modify fuel properties, improve performance or meet regulatory standards. It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 1/1233] oxygen containing compounds, e.g. oxides, hydroxides, acids and salts thereof -- This subclass concerns oxygen-containing compounds such as oxides, hydroxides, acids and their salts that are used in or affect liquid carbonaceous fuels. It falls under the C10L group for petroleum-derived fuel additives within the broader chemistry hierarchy of petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10L 1/1241] metal carbonyls -- C10L 1/1241 covers metal carbonyls, inorganic complexes in which one or more metal atoms are bound to carbon monoxide ligands. These compounds are relevant to the processing or synthesis of liquid carbonaceous fuels, serving as catalysts, fuel additives, or intermediates in refining and chemical conversion of petroleum products.
[C10L 1/125] water -- C10L 1/125 covers the presence, removal, or control of water in liquid carbonaceous fuels such as gasoline, diesel or heavy fuel oil. It relates to processes for separating, reducing, or managing water to improve fuel quality, storage stability, or combustion performance. The subclass is used for inventions dealing with water content, dewatering, or water-related additives in petroleum fuels.
[C10L 1/1258] hydrogen peroxide, oxygenated water -- C10L 1/1258 covers liquid fuels in which hydrogen peroxide is dissolved or mixed with water, creating an oxygen-enriched aqueous solution used as an oxidizer or additive in petroleum-derived fuels. Such oxygenated water can improve combustion, reduce emissions, or serve as a carrier for peroxide in fuel formulations.
[C10L 1/1266] nitrogen containing compounds, (e.g. NH 3 )
[C10L 1/1275] sulfur, tellurium, selenium containing compounds -- The class C10L 1/1275 concerns chemical compounds that contain sulfur, tellurium or selenium and are used as additives or components in liquid carbonaceous fuels such as gasoline, diesel or jet fuel. These substances are addressed for their roles in improving fuel performance, stability or emissions.
[C10L 1/1283] phosphorus, arsenicum, antimonium containing compounds -- C10L 1/1283 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels that include compounds of phosphorus, arsenic or antimony. It is nested in the CPC hierarchy for petroleum, gas or coke industries under fuels not otherwise provided for. These additives are used to modify combustion characteristics, reduce deposits or improve lubricity in fuels such as gasoline or diesel.
[C10L 1/1291] Silicon and boron containing compounds -- C10L 1/1291 classifies silicon and boron containing compounds that are employed in liquid carbonaceous fuels. These additives are used to modify fuel properties such as combustion temperature, soot formation, or lubricity in petroleum-derived fuels.
