[C10L 1/16] Hydrocarbons -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[C10L 1/1608] Well defined compounds, e.g. hexane, benzene -- The CPC subclass C10L 1/1608 defines well-known liquid hydrocarbon compounds that are used as fuels, such as hexane, benzene and similar defined molecules. It falls under chemistry → petroleum, gas or coke industries → fuels not otherwise provided for → liquid carbonaceous fuels, covering individual, characterised fuel components rather than mixtures.
[C10L 1/1616] fractions, e.g. lubricants, solvents, naphta, bitumen, tars, terpentine -- C10L 1/1616 covers the handling, separation or processing of specific liquid carbonaceous fuel fractions such as lubricants, solvents, naphtha, bitumen, tars and turpentine, focusing on their preparation or use as distinct product streams within the petroleum and fuel industry.
[C10L 1/1625] macromolecular compounds -- C10L 1/1625 covers macromolecular compounds employed as liquid carbonaceous fuels, such as heavy oils, residues, or polymer-based fuels, within the petroleum, gas or coke sectors. The classification is used for inventions that modify, produce, or improve the performance of these high-molecular-weight fuel substances.
[C10L 1/1633] homo- or copolymers obtained by reactions only involving carbon-to carbon unsaturated bonds -- A subclass for liquid carbonaceous fuels that covers polymers-either homopolymers or copolymers-produced exclusively through reactions between carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds, such as olefin polymerisation. These materials are intended for use as fuel additives or as components of specialized fuel blends.
[C10L 1/1641] from compounds containing aliphatic monomers -- C10L 1/1641 covers methods for preparing liquid carbonaceous fuels from feedstocks that consist of aliphatic monomer compounds, such as olefins or saturated chain molecules, including their conversion, polymerisation or cracking to produce gasoline, diesel or jet fuel.
[C10L 1/165] from compounds containing aromatic monomers -- C10L 1/165 covers methods for producing liquid carbonaceous fuels from feedstocks that contain aromatic monomers. The subclass includes catalytic or thermal processes that convert aromatic compounds into gasoline, diesel or other liquid fuels. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 1/1658] from compounds containing conjugated dienes -- This CPC sub-group deals with liquid carbonaceous fuels such as gasoline or diesel that are obtained from feedstocks containing conjugated diene compounds, typically by hydrogenating or polymerising those dienes to form suitable fuel molecules. It falls under the petroleum-based fuels hierarchy.
[C10L 1/1666] from compounds containing non-conjugated dienes -- This CPC subgroup covers liquid hydrocarbon fuels obtained from feedstocks that contain non-conjugated diene compounds. It includes processes for converting such dienes into gasoline-type or other liquid fuels, often via polymerisation, cracking or hydrogenation steps, and relates to fuels not otherwise specified in the classification.
[C10L 1/1675] natural rubbers -- C10L 1/1675 covers natural rubbers, elastomeric polymers harvested from the latex of plants such as Hevea brasiliensis. The entry relates to their extraction, purification and basic physical-chemical properties for use in products such as tires, adhesives and other elastomeric goods.
[C10L 1/1683] obtained otherwise than by reactions only involving carbon to carbon unsaturated bonds -- This subclass deals with the production of liquid carbonaceous fuels by routes that do not rely solely on reactions involving carbon-carbon unsaturated bonds, encompassing processes such as hydrogenation, cracking, or other chemical conversion methods. It is placed under fuels not otherwise provided for in the petroleum, gas or coke industry hierarchy.
[C10L 1/1691] petroleum waxes, mineral waxes; paraffines; alkylation products; Friedel-Crafts condensation products; petroleum resins; modified waxes (oxidised) -- C10L 1/1691 defines the subgroup of liquid carbonaceous fuels that comprise petroleum-derived and mineral waxes, including paraffins, alkylation products, Friedel-Crafts condensation products, petroleum resins and waxes that have been chemically modified, such as oxidised waxes. These materials are used primarily as fuel components or additives in the petroleum, gas and coke industries.
[C10L 1/18] containing oxygen -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[C10L 1/1802] natural products, e.g. waxes, extracts, fatty oils -- C10L 1/1802 covers natural products such as waxes, extracts and fatty oils used as liquid carbonaceous fuels. The subclass groups inventions involving these biomaterials in the context of petroleum, gas or coke industry fuel technologies. It falls under the broader category of fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 1/1805] oxidised hydrocarbon fractions -- C10L 1/1805 covers oxidised hydrocarbon fractions obtained from liquid carbonaceous fuels such as crude oil or refined products. The classification pertains to fractions that have undergone oxidation, yielding oxygen-containing compounds used as intermediates, fuel additives or specialty chemicals, and is situated under petroleum-based fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 1/1808] oxidised mineral waxes -- C10L 1/1808 concerns oxidised mineral waxes, which are mineral-derived waxes that have been chemically oxidised to modify their physical or chemical characteristics. The class is used for patents dealing with the production, processing, or use of these modified waxes as liquid carbonaceous fuels or related applications in the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10L 1/1811] peroxides; ozonides -- C10L 1/1811 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain peroxides or ozonides, such as certain peroxide-stabilized fuels or ozonide additives used to improve combustion or storage. The subclass deals with the composition, preparation and use of these oxidative compounds in petroleum-derived fuels.
[C10L 1/1814] Chelates -- C10L 1/1814 covers the use of chelating agents in liquid carbonaceous fuels such as oil or coal-derived liquids. The classification pertains to additives that form stable complexes with metal ions to improve fuel stability, combustion or storage characteristics. It falls under the CPC section on fuels not otherwise provided for within petroleum and gas industries.
[C10L 1/1817] Compounds of uncertain formula; reaction products where mixtures of compounds are obtained -- C10L 1/1817 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain compounds whose exact molecular formula cannot be definitively determined, as well as the reaction products that arise as mixtures of such undefined compounds. The subclass is used for patents describing fuel compositions where the constituent chemicals are uncertain or variable.
[C10L 1/182] containing hydroxy groups -- C10L 1/182 classifies liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain hydroxy functional groups, such as alcohols or phenols, within the broader category of petroleum, gas or coke industry fuels. The subgroup covers compositions, production processes and uses of these hydroxyl-containing fuels.
[C10L 1/1822] hydroxy group directly attached to (cyclo)aliphatic carbon atoms -- This subclassification concerns liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain a hydroxyl group attached directly to an aliphatic or cycloaliphatic carbon atom in the fuel molecules. It groups such fuels with hydroxyl-functionalized hydrocarbons, falling under the broader category of functional groups in liquid fuels.
[C10L 1/1824] mono-hydroxy -- The subclass C10L 1/1824 concerns liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain a single hydroxyl group in their molecular structure, such as mono-hydroxy alcohols used as fuel components or additives. It covers the preparation, formulation, and use of these mono-hydroxy fuels in internal-combustion engines and related applications.
[C10L 1/1826] poly-hydroxy -- It covers polymeric substances that contain multiple hydroxyl groups and are used as liquid carbonaceous fuels, such as polyols derived from biomass or synthetic routes, serving as fuels or fuel additives. The subclass falls under C10L, which addresses liquid fuels with poly-hydroxy components.
[C10L 1/1828] Salts thereof -- The C10L 1/1828 subgroup covers salts derived from liquid carbonaceous fuels, such as petroleum or other hydrocarbon fuel oils, where an acidic component of the fuel is neutralised by a metal, ammonium or organic cation. It includes these fuel-salt additives that improve storage stability, corrosion inhibition or combustion characteristics of the fuel.
[C10L 1/183] at least one hydroxy group bound to an aromatic carbon atom -- C10L 1/183 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels such as diesel or gasoline that contain at least one hydroxy group attached directly to an aromatic carbon atom. The classification is used for patents describing such hydroxy-substituted aromatic compounds in fuel formulations.
[C10L 1/1832] mono-hydroxy  C10L1/1802 ,  C10L1/1805 ,  C10L1/1808 ,  C10L1/1811 ,  C10L1/1814 ,  C10L1/1817 ,  C10L1/1828  take precedence -- The subclass C10L 1/1832 deals with liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain mono-hydroxy compounds-hydrocarbon molecules bearing a single hydroxyl group-used as fuel components or additives. It supersedes the earlier mono-hydroxy entries (C10L 1/1802, 1/1805, 1/1808, 1/1811, 1/1814, 1/1817, 1/1828) and specifies the relevant fuel-related applications.

[C10L 1/185] Ethers -- C10L 1/185 covers ethers used as liquid carbonaceous fuels, including gasoline-like or alternative fuel mixtures where ether compounds provide combustion properties. It addresses the preparation, composition and application of such ether-based fuels within the broader category of petroleum-derived fuel classifications.
[C10L 1/1852] Ethers; Acetals; Ketals; Orthoesters -- This CPC subclass C10L 1/1852 covers ethers, acetals, ketals and orthoesters used in liquid carbonaceous fuels. It resides in the chemistry hierarchy under petroleum, gas or coke industries and fuels not otherwise provided for, and relates to compounds employed in fuel formulations.
[C10L 1/1855] Cyclic ethers, e.g. epoxides, lactides, lactones -- This CPC subclass C10L 1/1855 covers cyclic ethers such as epoxides, lactides and lactones, which are organic compounds containing an oxygen atom in a ring structure. They are relevant as components or additives in liquid carbonaceous fuels used in the petroleum, gas and coke industries. The classification groups these chemicals for their role in fuel performance, stability and synthesis processes.
[C10L 1/1857] Aldehydes; Ketones -- The CPC entry C10L 1/1857 covers aldehydes and ketones that are present in or added to liquid carbonaceous fuels such as gasoline, diesel or jet fuel. It includes these carbonyl compounds when they are used as fuel components, additives for performance improvement, or contaminants that affect fuel properties.
[C10L 1/188] Carboxylic acids; salts thereof -- C10L 1/188 covers the incorporation of carboxylic acids and their salts as components of liquid carbonaceous fuels. It includes formulations where these acids or salts are used to modify fuel properties such as corrosion inhibition, combustion efficiency, or emission reduction in gasoline, diesel or related fuels.
[C10L 1/1881] carboxylic group attached to an aliphatic carbon atom -- C10L 1/1881 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels in which a carboxylic group is bonded directly to an aliphatic carbon atom, such as certain modified diesel or kerosene blends. It falls under the Chemistry section, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries, and deals with fuels not otherwise provided for. The class is used to classify patents that modify fuel molecules by adding a carboxyl functionality to improve performance or emissions.
[C10L 1/1883] polycarboxylic acid -- The subclass C10L 1/1883 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers the incorporation of polycarboxylic acids into liquid carbonaceous fuels. It relates to the use of these acids as additives for improving fuel properties such as stability, viscosity control, or corrosion inhibition within petroleum-derived fuels. This entry falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, petroleum-related industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 1/1885] resin acid -- C10L 1/1885 designates resin acids, natural organic acids obtained mainly from coniferous wood processing and employed in adhesives, coatings, surfactants and similar applications. In the CPC hierarchy they fall under liquid carbonaceous fuels within the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10L 1/1886] naphthenic acid -- C10L 1/1886 covers naphthenic acid, a mixture of cycloaliphatic carboxylic acids derived from crude oil and other refined petroleum fractions. These acids are employed as corrosion inhibitors, surfactants and metal-cleaning agents, and are relevant to the production and treatment of liquid carbonaceous fuels.
[C10L 1/1888] tall oil -- Tall oil is a dark, viscous liquid obtained as a by-product of the kraft pulping process in the paper industry. It consists mainly of fatty acids, resin acids and neutral oils and is used as a source of chemicals for soaps, adhesives, varnishes and as a low-grade fuel. This entry classifies tall oil within the liquid carbonaceous fuels subgroup of petroleum-related chemicals.
[C10L 1/189] having at least one carboxyl group bound to an aromatic carbon atom -- It refers to liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain aromatic molecules in which at least one carboxyl group is directly bonded to an aromatic carbon atom. The COOH substituent can modify the fuel's reactivity, acidity and combustion behavior.
[C10L 1/1895] polycarboxylic acid  C10L1/1802 ,  C10L1/1805 ,  C10L1/1808 ,  C10L1/1811 ,  C10L1/1814 ,  C10L1/1817 ,  C10L1/1885 ,  C10L1/1886 ,  C10L1/1888  take precedence
[C10L 1/19] Esters -- C10L 1/19 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels that are esters, including mono- and poly-esters derived from fatty acids, alcohols or other organic acids, used as diesel-type fuels or fuel additives. The subclass addresses their preparation, composition and utilization in engines or heating systems, distinguishing them from other fuel categories.
[C10L 1/1905] of di- or polycarboxylic acids -- C10L 1/1905 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels-such as gasoline, diesel or jet fuel-that incorporate di- or polycarboxylic acids, either as components of the fuel itself or as additives to modify properties, improve stability, or aid combustion. This subclass falls under chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically fuels not otherwise provided for.

[C10L 1/192] Macromolecular compounds -- C10L 1/192 refers to macromolecular compounds employed in liquid carbonaceous fuels. It addresses their preparation, modification, and application as fuel components, additives, or intermediates within petroleum, gas, or coke-derived fuel technologies.
[C10L 1/1966] poly-carboxylic -- C10L 1/1966 relates to the use or preparation of polymeric carboxylic acids in liquid carbonaceous fuels. The subclass covers poly-carboxylic compounds employed as fuel additives, emulsifiers, or as components that modify combustion or stability of gasoline, diesel or other petroleum-derived liquids. It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, fuels not otherwise provided for, and liquid carbonaceous fuels.
[C10L 1/197] derived from monomers containing a carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bond and an acyloxy group of a saturated carboxylic or carbonic acid -- This subclass covers liquid carbonaceous fuels produced by polymerizing or synthesizing from monomers that have an unsaturated C-C bond and an acyloxy substituent derived from a saturated carboxylic or carbonic acid. It includes processes and products where such monomers are used to make fuels.
[C10L 1/1973] mono-carboxylic -- The C10L 1/1973 subclass covers liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain a single carboxyl functional group, i.e. mono-carboxylic compounds used as fuel or fuel additive. It relates to petroleum-derived or synthetic fuel streams where a mono-carboxylic component is present, such as fatty acids or related esters.
[C10L 1/1976] poly-carboxylic -- The C10L 1/1976 subclass concerns the use of poly-carboxylic compounds in liquid carbonaceous fuels, covering their preparation, blending and function as fuel additives. It includes inventions that improve fuel stability, lubricity, emissions or other performance properties.
[C10L 1/198] obtained otherwise than by reactions involving only carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds -- C10L 1/198 covers processes for producing liquid carbonaceous fuels that are obtained by methods other than reactions limited to carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds, such as hydrogenation, hydrocracking or catalytic reforming where hetero-atoms, hydrogen addition or other bond types are involved. This subclass separates those broader synthetic routes from purely unsaturated-bond transformations.
[C10L 1/1981] Condensation polymers of aldehydes or ketones -- C10L 1/1981 covers condensation polymers formed from aldehyde or ketone monomers, including polyaldehydes, polyketones and related copolymers produced by step-growth reactions. The entry pertains to such polymeric materials when they are relevant to liquid carbonaceous fuels, for example as fuel additives, processing aids or specialty fuel components. It captures inventions concerning their synthesis, characterization and use within the broader context of petroleum-derived liquid fuels.
[C10L 1/1983] polyesters -- The subclass covers polyester compounds employed in or produced from liquid carbonaceous fuels, including their synthesis, modification and role as fuel additives or blending components. It relates to the chemistry of fuel formulations derived from petroleum, gas or coke streams.
[C10L 1/1985] polyethers, e.g. di- polygylcols and derivatives; ethers - esters -- C10L 1/1985 classifies liquid carbonaceous fuel compositions that contain polyethers, for example dipolyglycols, their derivatives and ether-ester compounds. These materials are used as additives or fuel components to improve performance or stability.
[C10L 1/1986] complex polyesters -- C10L 1/1986 designates complex polyesters that are employed as components or additives in liquid carbonaceous fuels, such as certain synthetic lubricants or fuel blends derived from petroleum, gas or coke processing. The subclass addresses their synthesis, composition and use within the broader field of fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 1/1988] epoxy resins and derivatives; natural resins, e.g. colophony -- This subclass covers epoxy resins and their chemical derivatives as well as natural resins such as colophony, when used as components, additives or raw materials in liquid carbonaceous fuels. It falls under chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically fuels not otherwise provided for.

[C10L 1/221] compounds of uncertain formula; reaction products where mixtures of compounds are obtained -- C10L 1/221 covers liquid carbonaceous fuel compositions in which the precise chemical formula of the constituent compounds is not definitively known, including reaction products that consist of mixtures of such undefined compounds. It pertains to fuels derived from petroleum, gas or coke processes where the resulting fuel blend is characterized only by its overall properties, not by exact molecular structures.
[C10L 1/222] containing at least one carbon-to-nitrogen single bond -- This subclass covers liquid carbonaceous fuels that incorporate molecules with at least one carbon-nitrogen single bond. It includes fuels blended with nitrogen-containing compounds such as amines, nitriles or heterocyclic additives used to modify combustion properties or reduce emissions. The classification is applied to petroleum-derived or synthetic gasoline, diesel, kerosene and similar fuels where the C-N bond is a defining feature of the additive.

The subclass is applied when such functional groups are present in the fuel composition and are not covered by the higher-priority subclasses C10L1/221 and C10L1/226.

It falls under the hierarchy Chemistry - Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries - Fuels not otherwise provided for - Liquid carbonaceous fuels. The more specific subclasses C10L 1/221 and C10L 1/226 have priority over this entry.
[C10L 1/23] containing at least one nitrogen-to-oxygen bond, e.g. nitro-compounds, nitrates, nitrites -- C10L 1/23 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels that incorporate at least one nitrogen-to-oxygen bond, including nitro-compounds, nitrates and nitrites. The subclass is used for patents on fuel formulations where such nitrogen-oxygen groups are added to improve performance, stability or emissions characteristics.
[C10L 1/231] nitro compounds; nitrates; nitrites  C10L1/221  takes precedence -- C10L 1/231 covers nitro compounds, nitrates and nitrites used as additives or components in liquid carbonaceous fuels such as gasoline, diesel or jet fuel. The class addresses their role in improving combustion, stability or emissions. For more specific sub-features see C10L 1/221, which has priority over this entry.
[C10L 1/232] containing nitrogen in a heterocyclic ring -- The C10L 1/232 subclass concerns liquid carbonaceous fuels that incorporate nitrogen atoms within heterocyclic ring structures. It addresses the formulation, processing, or use of such nitrogen-containing fuel components to influence performance, combustion, or other fuel characteristics. This subclass falls under Chemistry → Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries → Fuels Not Otherwise Provided For → Liquid carbonaceous fuels.
[C10L 1/233] containing nitrogen and oxygen in the ring, e.g. oxazoles -- C10L 1/233 classifies liquid carbonaceous fuels that incorporate heterocyclic compounds whose rings contain both nitrogen and oxygen atoms, such as oxazoles. These substances are used as fuel additives or components to modify combustion properties and fall under the broader category of fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 1/2335] morpholino, and derivatives thereof  C10L1/221  takes precedence -- C10L 1/2335 designates morpholino compounds and their derivatives as additives or components in liquid carbonaceous fuels such as gasoline, diesel or kerosene. The subclass falls under Chemistry → Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries → Fuels not otherwise provided for → Liquid carbonaceous fuels, and is superseded by the broader C10L 1/221 grouping.
[C10L 1/234] Macromolecular compounds -- C10L 1/234 refers to macromolecular compounds, such as polymers or high-molecular-weight substances, that are employed as liquid carbonaceous fuels. The subclass falls under the CPC hierarchy for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically within fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 1/236] obtained by reactions involving only carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds -- C10L 1/236 classifies processes for making liquid carbonaceous fuels that rely solely on reactions of carbon-carbon unsaturated bonds, such as olefin polymerisation or oligomerisation, without involvement of heteroatoms or saturated linkages. These methods fall under the broader category of petroleum-related fuel production.
[C10L 1/2362] homo- or copolymers derived from unsaturated compounds containing nitrile groups  C10L1/221  takes precedence -- The C10L 1/2362 subclass covers liquid carbonaceous fuels that are homo- or copolymers formed from unsaturated compounds containing nitrile groups. It is a deeper division of the broader category for fuels not otherwise provided for, and is subordinated to C10L 1/221, which must be consulted first for related polymeric fuel technologies.
[C10L 1/2364] homo- or copolymers derived from unsaturated compounds containing amide and/or imide groups  C10L1/221  takes precedence -- C10L 1/2364 covers homo- or copolymers obtained from unsaturated compounds that contain amide or imide groups, intended for use in liquid carbonaceous fuels. The subclass falls under the chemistry of fuels not otherwise provided for and is subordinate to C10L 1/221, which has priority over this entry.
[C10L 1/2366] homo- or copolymers derived from unsaturated compounds containing amine groups  C10L1/221  takes precedence
[C10L 1/2368] homo- or copolymers derived from unsaturated compounds containing heterocyclic compounds containing nitrogen in the ring  C10L1/221  takes precedence -- This subclass covers homo- or copolymers made from unsaturated compounds that contain nitrogen-bearing heterocyclic rings, intended for use as liquid carbonaceous fuels. It is situated in the C10L group for liquid hydrocarbon fuels and takes precedence over the broader C10L 1/221 category.
[C10L 1/238] obtained otherwise than by reactions involving only carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds -- C10L 1/238 covers methods for producing liquid carbonaceous fuels-such as gasoline, kerosene or diesel-by processes that do not rely solely on carbon-to-carbon unsaturated-bond reactions. It includes alternative conversion routes like hydrogenation, cracking, reforming or other catalytic transformations that involve saturated-bond or hetero-atom chemistry.
[C10L 1/2381] polyamides; polyamide-esters; polyurethane, polyureas  C10L1/221  takes precedence
[C10L 1/2383] Polyamines or polyimines, or derivatives thereof -- This CPC subclass falls under C10L (fuels, liquid carbonaceous), specifically 1/2383 which deals with polyamines, polyimines and their derivatives used as fuel additives or components. It includes compounds containing multiple amine or imine groups employed to improve fuel properties such as stability, lubricity, or emissions in petroleum-derived liquids.
[C10L 1/2387] Polyoxyalkyleneamines -- A polyoxyalkyleneamine is a polymeric compound containing ether linkages and amine groups that is used as an additive or processing aid for liquid carbonaceous fuels such as gasoline, diesel or jet fuel. These substances improve fuel stability, emulsification and detergency, helping to meet performance and emissions standards.
[C10L 1/24] containing sulfur, selenium and/or tellurium -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[C10L 1/2406] mercaptans; hydrocarbon sulfides -- The subclass C10L 1/2406 covers liquid hydrocarbon fuels that contain sulfur-bearing organic compounds such as mercaptans (thiols) and other hydrocarbon sulfides, addressing their presence, handling, processing or use in petroleum-derived fuel formulations.
[C10L 1/2412] sulfur bond to an aromatic radical -- C10L 1/2412 addresses liquid carbonaceous fuels containing molecules in which a sulfur atom is directly bonded to an aromatic radical. The classification includes sulfur-linked aromatic additives, stabilizers or fuel components designed to modify combustion, corrosion resistance or lubricity of petroleum-derived fuels.
[C10L 1/2418] containing a carboxylic substituted; derivatives thereof, e.g. esters -- This CPC subclass covers liquid carbonaceous fuels that incorporate a carboxylic substituted compound or its derivatives such as esters. It applies to fuels used in petroleum, gas or coke processes where such functional groups are present to modify fuel properties.
[C10L 1/2425] Thiocarbonic acids and derivatives thereof, e.g. xanthates; Thiocarbamic acids or derivatives thereof, e.g. dithio-carbamates; Thiurams -- C10L 1/2425 covers thiocarbonic acids and their derivatives, including xanthates, thiocarbamic acids, dithiocarbates and thiurams, which are chemical compounds containing sulfur-linked carbonyl groups. These substances are typically employed as additives, stabilizers or processing agents in liquid carbonaceous fuels such as petroleum-derived oils and gases.
[C10L 1/2431] sulfur bond to oxygen, e.g. sulfones, sulfoxides -- C10L 1/2431 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain sulfur atoms bonded to oxygen, such as sulfones and sulfoxides. The subclass falls under the chemistry group for petroleum, gas or coke industries and relates to fuel formulations where these sulfur-oxygen compounds are present or processed.
[C10L 1/2437] Sulfonic acids; Derivatives thereof, e.g. sulfonamides, sulfosuccinic acid esters -- The CPC subclass C10L 1/2437 covers sulfonic acids and their derivatives, including sulfonamides and sulfosuccinic-acid esters, which are used as additives or components in liquid carbonaceous fuels. These compounds modify fuel properties such as stability, combustion or emissions.
[C10L 1/2443] heterocyclic compounds -- This subclass deals with heterocyclic compounds that are components or additives of liquid carbonaceous fuels such as gasoline, diesel or jet fuel. It includes nitrogen-, oxygen-, sulfur-or phosphorus-containing ring structures used to improve combustion, stability, or emissions.
[C10L 1/245] only sulfur as hetero atom -- C10L 1/245 covers liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain sulfur as the sole hetero atom, meaning no nitrogen, oxygen or other hetero elements are present. The classification is used for petroleum or coke industry products such as diesel or fuel oil where sulfur is the only non-carbon element in the molecular structure.
[C10L 1/2456] sulfur with oxygen and/or nitrogen in the ring, e.g. thiazoles -- A C10L 1/2456 class covers heterocyclic compounds in which sulfur is present together with oxygen and/or nitrogen within the ring structure, exemplified by thiazoles. These substances are relevant as additives, intermediates or components of liquid carbonaceous fuels such as gasoline or diesel.
[C10L 1/2462] macromolecular compounds -- C10L 1/2462 classifies macromolecular compounds used as liquid carbonaceous fuels, covering polymeric fuel additives, synthetic oil basestock and other high-molecular-weight substances that serve as or modify liquid fuels derived from petroleum, gas or coke.
[C10L 1/2468] obtained by reactions involving only carbon to carbon unsaturated bonds; derivatives thereof -- C10L 1/2468 covers processes for making liquid carbonaceous fuels through reactions that involve only carbon-carbon unsaturated bonds, such as polymerization, oligomerization, or cyclisation of alkenes, and the resulting fuel derivatives. The classification addresses synthetic routes that do not introduce heteroatoms, focusing on hydrocarbon fuels like gasoline, diesel or jet fuel derived from unsaturated precursors.
[C10L 1/2475] obtained otherwise than by reactions only involving unsaturated carbon to carbon bonds -- C10L 1/2475 covers processes for making liquid carbonaceous fuels that do not rely exclusively on reactions between unsaturated carbon-carbon bonds. It includes methods such as hydrogenation, hydrocracking or other conversion steps where saturated or partially saturated feedstocks are transformed into gasoline, diesel or related fuels, expanding the range of feedstock types beyond simple olefinic compounds.
[C10L 1/2481] polysulfides (3 carbon to sulfur bonds) -- C10L 1/2481 concerns polysulfides that contain three carbon-sulfur bonds and are used in liquid carbonaceous fuels. These polymeric sulfur compounds are linked to hydrocarbon groups, affecting fuel stability, handling and combustion characteristics.
[C10L 1/2487] polyoxyalkylene thioethers (O + S 3=) -- C10L 1/2487 designates polyoxyalkylene thioethers, liquid organic compounds in which a polyoxyalkylene chain is bonded to a thioether group (-S-) and may contain additional oxygen or sulfur double-bonded atoms. These molecules are disclosed as additives or components in liquid carbonaceous fuels and related fuel-related formulations.
[C10L 1/2493] compounds of uncertain formula; reactions of organic compounds (hydrocarbons, acids, esters) with sulfur or sulfur containing compounds -- C10L 1/2493 relates to liquid carbonaceous fuels and covers compounds of uncertain molecular formula, as well as processes in which organic substances such as hydrocarbons, acids or esters react with elemental sulfur or other sulfur-containing reagents.
[C10L 1/26] containing phosphorus -- **Definition:** This place covers:

It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for, detailing the chemical makeup of such fuels.
[C10L 1/2616] sulfur containing -- The C10L 1/2616 subclass relates to liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain sulfur, covering compositions, preparation methods, and treatment processes for sulfur-bearing fuels such as heavy oils, coal-derived liquids, or other petroleum fractions. It includes the handling, refining, and upgrading of these sulfur-containing fuels to control emissions, improve performance, or meet regulatory standards.
[C10L 1/2625] amine salts -- C10L 1/2625 covers amine salts used in liquid carbonaceous fuels, including additives or catalysts that improve fuel stability, combustion or handling. The entry falls under chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 1/2633] phosphorus bond to oxygen (no P. C. bond) -- C10L 1/2633 classifies liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain a phosphorus atom bonded to oxygen without a direct phosphorus-carbon bond. The subclass includes additives, treatment methods, and formulations where P-O linkages are used to modify fuel properties such as lubricity, ash formation, or combustion behavior. It falls under the broader CPC hierarchy for petroleum, gas or coke industry fuels.
[C10L 1/2641] oxygen bonds only -- C10L 1/2641 classifies liquid carbonaceous fuels whose molecular structure contains only carbon-oxygen bonds, meaning the fuel molecules have no carbon-carbon linkages. It is used for categorising oxygenated fuels such as certain biodiesels or synthetic oils derived from petroleum, gas or coke processes, fitting within the broader chemistry hierarchy for petroleum and gas industry fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 1/265] oxygen and/or sulfur bonds -- This subclass deals with liquid carbonaceous fuels that contain oxygen or sulfur atoms bonded within the fuel molecules, including oxygenated fuel additives, sulfur-containing fuel components and related preparation or treatment processes. It also covers methods for improving combustion or reducing emissions by manipulating these bonds.
[C10L 1/2658] amine salts -- C10L 1/2658 concerns the use of amine salts in liquid carbonaceous fuels such as gasoline, diesel or fuel oil. It covers formulations, production methods and applications where amine salts act as stabilizers, detergents, corrosion inhibitors or other fuel additives to improve performance and storage.
[C10L 1/2666] macromolecular compounds -- C10L 1/2666 identifies macromolecular compounds that are part of liquid carbonaceous fuels, covering polymers, heavy oil fractions and other high-molecular-weight substances used as fuel components or additives in the petroleum, gas or coke industry. This subclass falls under the hierarchy CHEMISTRY → PETROLEUM, GAS OR COKE INDUSTRIES → FUELS NOT OTHERWISE PROVIDED FOR → Liquid carbonaceous fuels.
[C10L 1/2675] obtained by reactions involving only carbon to carbon unsaturated bonds; derivatives thereof -- The subclass C10L 1/2675 covers processes for producing liquid carbonaceous fuels that rely solely on reactions involving carbon-carbon unsaturated bonds, such as alkenes or alkynes, without the use of heteroatoms or other functional groups. It also includes the downstream derivatives obtained from those carbon-only unsaturated-bond reactions.
[C10L 1/2683] obtained otherwise than by reactions only involving unsaturated carbon to carbon bonds -- The subclass C10L 1/2683 covers processes for obtaining liquid carbonaceous fuels by methods that do not rely solely on reactions involving unsaturated carbon-carbon bonds. It includes conversion techniques such as hydrogenation, catalytic cracking or other treatments where saturated, aromatic or otherwise processed feedstocks are transformed into fuels, and falls within the broader CPC group for petroleum and fuel manufacturing.
[C10L 1/2691] Compounds of uncertain formula; reaction of organic compounds (hydrocarbons acids, esters) with Px Sy, Px Sy Halz or sulfur and phosphorus containing compounds -- This CPC subclass covers compounds of indeterminate formula and the reactions in which organic substances such as hydrocarbons, acids or esters are treated with phosphorus-sulfur reagents, phosphorus-sulfur halides or other sulfur- and phosphorus-containing compounds. It is situated under liquid carbonaceous fuels within the petroleum, gas and coke industry.

[C10L 1/30] compounds not mentioned before (complexes) -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The additive is an organic compound which contains at least one element different from carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, halogens, nitrogen, sulphur, selenium, tellurium, phosphorus or silicon; the additive is for example an organic metal complex.
[C10L 1/301] derived from metals -- This subclass covers liquid carbonaceous fuels produced from metallic compounds such as metal carbonyls, metal hydrides or other metal-derived intermediates, where the metal serves as a reactant or catalyst to form a combustible liquid. It includes methods that convert metals into fuel-grade liquids for use in internal combustion engines or other energy applications.
[C10L 1/303] boron compounds -- A subclass of liquid carbonaceous fuels that addresses the use of boron-containing compounds, typically as additives, catalysts or performance-enhancing agents in gasoline, diesel or other petroleum-derived liquids. The classification covers formulations, methods of incorporation and the effects of boron chemicals on fuel properties such as octane rating, emissions reduction or lubricity.
[C10L 1/305] organo-metallic compounds (containing a metal to carbon bond) -- C10L 1/305 covers organo-metallic compounds that feature a direct metal-to-carbon bond, used as additives, catalysts or fuels in liquid carbonaceous fuels. [C10L 1/306] organo Pb compounds -- C10L 1/306 covers organolead compounds, such as tetraethyllead, that are added to liquid carbonaceous fuels to enhance antiknock performance. The subclass is situated within fuels not otherwise provided for in the petroleum, gas or coke industries. These organolead additives are subject to regulatory control because of their environmental and health impacts.
[C10L 1/308] organo tin compounds -- Organo-tin compounds are organometallic chemicals in which tin atoms are bonded to carbon groups, used as catalysts or additives in the production and performance of liquid carbonaceous fuels. They help improve fuel stability, combustion efficiency and resistance to corrosion in petroleum processing.
[C10L 1/32] consisting of coal-oil suspensions or aqueous emulsions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel compositions which are a mixture of solid coal or biomass particles with a hydrophobic and/or hydrophilic phase.  Coal particles including biomass particles  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Emulsifying process -> B01F23/00 - Emulsifying agents -> C09K23/00
[C10L 1/322] Coal-oil suspensions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solid coal particles in an oil phase to form an oily slurry.
[C10L 1/324] Dispersions containing coal, oil and water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Slurries comprising solid coal and water (or another hydrophilic phase) and oil.
[C10L 1/326] Coal-water suspensions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solid coal particles in a water phase to form a slurry.
[C10L 1/328] Oil emulsions containing water or any other hydrophilic phase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel emulsion compositions and/or preparations therefore (clearly indicating fuel composition though) comprising a hydrophobic phase and a hydrophilic phase, usually comprising water but also including and/or a (short-chained) alcohol.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Emulsifying process in general -> B01F23/00 - Emulsifying agents -> C09K23/00
[C10L 2200/00] Components of fuel compositions -- Additives in liquid fuels present in concentrations lower than 5% get a class taken from C10L1/10 - C10L1/308 and corresponding C10L1/10 - C10L1/308 . In groups C10L1/32 - C10L11/08 is such distinction between the terms additive and component not made.
[C10L 2200/02] Inorganic or organic compounds containing atoms other than C, H or O, e.g. organic compounds containing heteroatoms or metal organic complexes -- C10L 2200/02 covers compounds-either inorganic or organic-that include atoms other than carbon, hydrogen or oxygen, such as heteroatom-bearing organics and metal-organic complexes, when they are used as components of fuel compositions. This subclass falls under the fuels not otherwise provided for section of the petroleum, gas or coke industries.

It falls within the hierarchy of chemistry, petroleum-gas or coke industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for.

[C10L 2200/0254] Oxygen containing compounds -- The code refers to patents concerning oxygen-containing compounds used as components in fuel formulations, especially within the petroleum, gas or coke industries. It covers additives such as oxygenates, peroxides and related substances that introduce oxygen into fuels to improve combustion, reduce emissions or modify fuel properties.
[C10L 2200/0259] Nitrogen containing compounds -- C10L 2200/0259 covers nitrogen-containing compounds that are added to fuel mixtures. [C10L 2200/0263] Sulphur containing compounds -- Sulphur containing compounds are chemical additives or constituents incorporated into fuel mixtures to modify combustion characteristics, improve lubricity, or serve as detergents and anti-wear agents. In petroleum-derived fuels they help control emissions, stabilize the fuel, and can enhance performance in engines and industrial burners.
[C10L 2200/0268] Phosphor containing compounds -- C10L 2200/0268 covers phosphor-containing compounds that are added to fuel mixtures, such as additives or catalysts, to improve performance, stability, or emissions in petroleum-derived fuels. It falls under chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically fuels not otherwise provided for and their components.
[C10L 2200/0272] Silicon containing compounds -- The CPC subclass C10L 2200/0272 covers silicon-containing compounds used as additives or components in fuel formulations. These substances are employed to improve fuel properties such as stability, combustion efficiency or emission reduction in petroleum-derived fuels.
[C10L 2200/0277] Hydrogen -- C10L 2200/0277 designates hydrogen as a component in fuel compositions, covering its inclusion, preparation or functional role in fuels derived from petroleum, gas or coke processes.
[C10L 2200/0281] Carbon monoxide -- Carbon monoxide is a gaseous component used in certain fuel formulations, particularly for its high flame temperature and ability to promote rapid combustion. In the context of petroleum-derived fuels it is classified as a specific fuel-composition additive that influences performance, emissions and ignition characteristics.
[C10L 2200/0286] Carbon dioxide -- The CPC code C10L 2200/0286 designates carbon dioxide when it is used as a component in fuel compositions. It is placed within the "fuels not otherwise provided for" subgroup of the petroleum, gas or coke industries, reflecting its role in modifying or enhancing fuel properties.
[C10L 2200/029] Salts, such as carbonates, oxides, hydroxides, percompounds, e.g. peroxides, perborates, nitrates, nitrites, sulfates, and silicates -- C10L 2200/029 pertains to salts-including carbonates, oxides, hydroxides, per-compounds such as peroxides and perborates, as well as nitrates, nitrites, sulfates and silicates-that are employed as components of fuel compositions.
[C10L 2200/0295] Water -- The subclass C10L 2200/0295 designates water as a component of fuel compositions within the broader fields of chemistry and petroleum, gas or coke industries. [C10L 2200/0407] Specifically defined hydrocarbon fractions as obtained from, e.g. a distillation column -- The CPC subclass C10L 2200/0407 covers distinct hydrocarbon fractions that are isolated from processes such as a distillation column and intended for use as components of fuel blends. It applies to specifically defined cuts of petroleum streams used in the formulation of fuels, particularly within the petroleum, gas or coke industry.
[C10L 2200/0415] Light distillates, e.g. LPG, naphtha -- This subclass covers compositions comprising light petroleum distillates such as liquefied petroleum gas and naphtha used as fuel components. It relates to fuel formulations within the petroleum industry where these low-boiling fractions are employed. The classification helps identify patents concerning the preparation, blending or use of LPG, naphtha and similar light distillates.
[C10L 2200/0423] Gasoline -- C10L 2200/0423 classifies gasoline, the main fuel for spark-ignition internal-combustion engines. It addresses the composition, additives and performance properties of gasoline as a component of petroleum-derived fuel formulations within the broader chemistry and fuel categories.
[C10L 2200/043] Kerosene, jet fuel -- The CPC subclass C10L 2200/043 covers kerosene and jet fuel, which are liquid hydrocarbon mixtures derived from petroleum refining and used primarily as aviation turbine engine fuels. The entry pertains to the composition of these fuels, including additives and specifications that affect performance, stability and safety in aircraft propulsion systems.
[C10L 2200/0438] Middle or heavy distillates, heating oil, gasoil, marine fuels, residua -- This CPC subclass covers middle and heavy petroleum distillates employed as fuels, such as heating oil, gasoil, marine fuels and various residue fractions. It pertains to the components of fuel compositions derived from refined crude oil.
[C10L 2200/0446] Diesel -- C10L 2200/0446 classifies diesel as a component of fuel compositions within the petroleum, gas or coke sector. This code is part of the hierarchy for fuels not otherwise provided for under chemistry.
[C10L 2200/0453] Petroleum or natural waxes, e.g. paraffin waxes, asphaltenes -- This CPC subclass relates to petroleum or natural waxes, such as paraffin waxes and asphaltenes, when used as components of fuel compositions. It is situated under chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for, and applies to patents describing wax additives in fuels.
[C10L 2200/0461] Fractions defined by their origin -- C10L 2200/0461 covers fuel fractions that are classified according to the source from which they are obtained, such as crude oil, natural gas, or coal-derived streams. It includes specifications of these fractions based on their origin rather than their chemical composition, and is used to group component claims in patents for fuels derived from distinct feedstocks.
[C10L 2200/0469] Renewables or materials of biological origin -- C10L 2200/0469 classifies components of fuel compositions that are derived from renewable or biologically sourced materials, covering substances such as bio-based oils, algae-derived fuels, and other organic feedstocks used to replace or supplement conventional petroleum-based fuels.
[C10L 2200/0476] Biodiesel, i.e. defined lower alkyl esters of fatty acids first generation biodiesel -- C10L 2200/0476 classifies biodiesel, defined as lower alkyl esters of fatty acids derived from first-generation feedstocks, as components of fuel compositions used in petroleum, gas or coke industry fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 2200/0484] Vegetable or animal oils -- The CPC subclass C10L 2200/0484 covers the use of vegetable or animal oils as components in fuel formulations. It falls under the chemistry area dealing with fuels not otherwise provided for, specifically within petroleum, gas or coke industry technologies. These oils are examined for their suitability to replace or supplement conventional petroleum-derived fuel constituents.
[C10L 2200/0492] Fischer-Tropsch products -- The CPC subclass C10L 2200/0492 covers Fischer-Tropsch products, which are synthetic hydrocarbons such as liquids, gases, waxes and lubricants obtained by catalytically converting synthesis gas (a mixture of carbon monoxide and hydrogen) in the Fischer-Tropsch process. These materials are used as fuel components or specialty chemicals within the petroleum, gas and coke sector.
[C10L 2230/00] Function and purpose of a components of a fuel or the composition as a whole -- The CPC subclass C10L 2230/00 covers the functional role and intended purpose of individual components within a fuel, as well as the overall objectives of the fuel composition itself. It is applied to inventions relating to how additives, blend constituents or the complete fuel formulation achieve desired performance, emissions or handling characteristics.
[C10L 2230/02] Absorbents, e.g. in the absence of an actual absorbent column or scavenger
[C10L 2230/04] Catalyst added to fuel stream to improve a reaction -- This classification covers the addition of a catalyst to a fuel stream to enhance a chemical reaction during fuel use. It relates to fuel compositions where the catalyst improves performance, efficiency or emissions, typically in petroleum-derived fuels.
[C10L 2230/06] Firelighters or wicks, as additive to a solid fuel -- This subclass covers additives for solid fuels that act as firelighters or wicks, facilitating ignition, flame initiation or sustained burning of the fuel. [C10L 2230/08] Inhibitors -- C10L 2230/08 covers inhibitors used in fuels, specifically substances that prevent undesirable reactions such as oxidation, corrosion, or polymerization within the fuel or its components. It pertains to additives that enhance fuel stability and performance in petroleum, gas, or coke industry applications. The classification falls under chemistry, fuels not otherwise provided for, focusing on function of fuel components.
[C10L 2230/081] Anti-oxidants -- C10L 2230/081 refers to antioxidants added to fuels to prevent oxidative degradation of fuel components, thereby improving stability, extending storage life and maintaining performance. The subclass covers any agent or additive whose function is to inhibit the chemical reactions that cause fuel oxidation, applicable to petroleum-derived fuels and related compositions.
[C10L 2230/082] for anti-foaming -- The code C10L 2230/082 designates anti-foaming agents added to fuels, covering the function and purpose of such components in petroleum, gas or coke-derived fuels to prevent foam formation during storage, transport or use, thereby improving fuel stability and performance.
[C10L 2230/083] Disinfectants, biocides, anti-microbials
[C10L 2230/085] Metal deactivators -- Metal deactivators are fuel additives that bind trace metal ions such as copper, iron or nickel, thereby inhibiting metal-catalysed oxidation and degradation of the fuel. By deactivating these catalytic species, they help maintain fuel stability, extend storage life and improve performance of engines or combustion systems.
[C10L 2230/086] Demulsifiers -- C10L 2230/86 covers demulsifiers, chemicals added to petroleum-derived fuels to break oil-water emulsions and facilitate water removal. These agents improve fuel stability and performance in applications such as engine combustion and storage.
[C10L 2230/087] for inhibiting misting -- C10L 2230/087 covers additives, processes or compositions that are used to prevent the formation of mist in liquid fuels, such as during storage, handling or combustion. The subclass includes substances that stabilize the fuel and reduce aerosolization, improving safety and efficiency of fuel systems.
[C10L 2230/088] for inhibiting or avoiding odor -- C10L 2230/088 covers fuel additives whose function is to suppress or eliminate unpleasant odors in fuels or fuel blends. It pertains to compositions used in petroleum-derived or other fuels where the primary purpose of the additive is odor inhibition. The class falls under the chemistry hierarchy for fuels not otherwise provided for, specifically the functional role of fuel components.
[C10L 2230/10] for adding an odor to the fuel or combustion products -- The code C10L 2230/10 covers inventions that impart a detectable smell to a fuel or to the gases produced when the fuel is burned, typically by adding odorants or scented additives. It is used in the context of petroleum-derived fuels where the purpose is to provide a warning odor or improve handling safety. This falls under the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and fuel formulations.
[C10L 2230/12] for producing sound, e.g. during burning an artificial fire log to mimic sound of real wood -- C10L 2230/12 covers fuel compositions or components designed to generate audible sounds when burned, such as artificial fire logs that mimic the crackling of real wood. The classification applies to additives or formulations that create sound as a functional feature of the fuel.
[C10L 2230/14] for improving storage or transport of the fuel -- C10L 2230/14 covers inventions that improve the storage or transport of fuels by modifying the function or composition of fuel components. It includes additives, stabilizers, or formulations that enhance stability, safety, or handling during storage or moving the fuel. The class falls under chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 2230/16] Tracers which serve to track or identify the fuel component or fuel composition -- C10L 2230/16 relates to tracers incorporated into fuel components or whole fuel compositions to enable monitoring, tracking or identification of the material during manufacture, distribution or use. Such tracers may be chemical markers, isotopes or other detectable agents used for composition verification, performance testing or fraud detection.
[C10L 2230/18] for rendering the fuel or flame visible or for adding or altering its color -- The CPC subclass C10L 2230/18 covers additives or processes applied to fuels or flames to make them visible or to change their color. It includes compounds that produce a visible flame, color-changing agents, and methods for rendering the combustion traceable, primarily for safety, detection or aesthetic purposes.
[C10L 2230/20] for improving conductivity -- C10L 2230/20 covers additives or processes aimed at improving the electrical conductivity of fuels, especially petroleum-derived fuels, by modifying component properties or the overall composition. It falls within the chemistry of petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the function and purpose of fuel components.
[C10L 2230/22] for improving fuel economy or fuel efficiency -- C10L 2230/22 is a Cooperative Patent Classification sub-group that covers inventions aimed at improving fuel economy or fuel-efficiency of fuels, including the function or purpose of fuel components or the overall composition, within the broader context of petroleum, gas or coke industry fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 2250/00] Structural features of fuel components or fuel compositions, either in solid, liquid or gaseous state -- This subclass covers inventions that disclose or analyze the molecular architecture, functional groups, or physical arrangement of individual fuel components or whole fuel mixtures, regardless of whether they are solids, liquids or gases. It includes methods for tailoring or assessing these structural features to improve performance, emissions or handling, and relates to fuels not covered by more specific categories.
[C10L 2250/02] Microbial additives -- C10L 2250/02 covers the use of microorganisms or biologically derived substances as additives in fuels, whether solid, liquid or gaseous. It includes microbes or their metabolites that enhance combustion, stability, emissions or storage properties of petroleum-based fuels, and is situated within the chemistry hierarchy for petroleum, gas or coke industries and fuels not otherwise specified.
[C10L 2250/04] Additive or component is a polymer -- The CPC subclass C10L 2250/04 covers fuel additives or components that are polymers, describing their structural features within solid, liquid or gaseous fuel compositions. It pertains to the use of polymeric substances to modify properties such as stability, combustion or emissions in fuels not otherwise classified.
[C10L 2250/06] Particle, bubble or droplet size -- C10L 2250/06 covers the size characteristics of particles, bubbles or droplets present in fuel components or fuel mixtures, whether solid, liquid or gaseous. It is used to classify inventions that define or control these dimensions to affect fuel performance, handling or combustion, falling under the broader category of structural features of fuels.
[C10L 2250/08] Emulsion details -- C10L 2250/08 addresses the structural characteristics of fuel emulsions, describing the continuous and dispersed phases, emulsifier type and amount, droplet-size distribution, and preparation or stabilisation methods for liquid or solid fuel mixtures used in petroleum-based applications.
[C10L 2250/082] Oil in water (o/w) emulsion -- C10L 2250/082 covers structural features of oil-in-water (o/w) emulsions, describing the composition and arrangement of the dispersed oil phase within a continuous water phase, applicable to solid, liquid or gaseous fuel forms.
[C10L 2250/084] Water in oil (w/o) emulsion -- C10L 2250/084 covers water-in-oil (w/o) emulsions employed as fuel components, focusing on the structural features of the dispersed water phase within a hydrocarbon continuous phase. The subclass belongs to fuels not otherwise provided for in the petroleum, gas or coke sectors and applies to solid, liquid or gaseous fuel formulations.
[C10L 2250/086] Microemulsion or nanoemulsion -- This subclass covers fuels where the dispersed phase is a micro- or nano-scale emulsion, i.e., droplets below about 100 nm, formed by surfactants to improve mixing of immiscible liquids, enhancing combustion or stability. It includes methods and compositions in solid, liquid or gaseous fuels that employ such emulsions.
[C10L 2250/088] Complex emulsions, e.g. water in oil in water (w/o/w) or oil in water in oil (o/w/o), bicontinuous emulsion, e.g. wherein both phases are continuous or multiple emulsions -- This subclass covers fuel compositions that are complex emulsions such as water-in-oil-in-water or oil-in-water-in-oil systems, including bicontinuous structures where both phases are continuous, as well as multiple emulsions. It is used to classify patents relating to the structural features of such fuel emulsions in solid, liquid or gaseous state.
[C10L 2270/00] Specifically adapted fuels -- C10L 2270/00 covers fuels that have been specially adapted for particular uses, including modifications to improve performance, reduce emissions, or meet the requirements of specific engines or processes. It falls under chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for, and is used in the CPC system shared by the EPO and USPTO.
[C10L 2270/02] for internal combustion engines -- C10L 2270/02 covers fuels that are specially formulated for use in internal combustion engines, including blended or additive-enhanced petroleum products designed to meet specific performance, efficiency or emissions requirements.
[C10L 2270/023] for gasoline engines -- C10L 2270/023 classifies fuels that are specially formulated for use in gasoline engines, including tailored fuel blends, additives and performance-enhancing compositions designed to meet the operating requirements of spark-ignition internal-combustion engines.
[C10L 2270/026] for diesel engines, e.g. automobiles, stationary, marine -- This subgroup covers fuels specially formulated for diesel engines used in automotive, stationary and marine applications, including additives or blending methods that optimize performance and emissions. It pertains to petroleum-derived fuels adapted to diesel engine requirements.
[C10L 2270/04] for turbines, planes, power generation -- C10L 2270/04 covers fuels specially adapted for use in turbines, aircraft and power-generation systems. The classification includes formulations such as high-energy jet fuels, turbine lubricants and other petroleum-derived or synthetic blends designed to meet the performance and safety requirements of these applications.
[C10L 2270/06] for fuel cells -- The CPC subclass C10L 2270/06 covers fuels specifically adapted for use in fuel-cell systems, including hydrogen, reformate or other energy carriers formulated to be supplied directly to a fuel cell. It addresses the preparation, composition and treatment of such fuels to meet the performance and safety requirements of fuel-cell technologies.
[C10L 2270/08] for small applications, such as tools, lamp oil, welding -- C10L 2270/08 classifies specially adapted fuels intended for small-scale uses, covering fuels formulated for devices such as hand tools, lamp oil, and welding applications within the petroleum and coke industry sector.
[C10L 2270/10] for transport, e.g. in pipelines as a gas hydrate slurry -- A C10L 2270/10 entry covers specially adapted fuels intended for transport, such as in pipelines, where the fuel is conveyed as a gas-hydrate slurry. This subclass falls under chemistry-related fuel categories for petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10L 2290/00] Fuel preparation or upgrading, processes or apparatus therefore, comprising specific process steps or apparatus units -- C10L 2290/00 covers methods and equipment for preparing or upgrading fuels that are not otherwise classified, focusing on specific process steps or distinct apparatus units used in the treatment of petroleum-derived fuels. It falls under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 2290/02] Combustion or pyrolysis -- C10L 2290/02 covers combustion or pyrolysis steps used in the preparation or upgrading of fuels within the petroleum, gas or coke sectors. It classifies processes or apparatus that intentionally burn or thermally decompose fuel feedstocks to modify their chemical composition or energy content.
[C10L 2290/04] Gasification -- C10L 2290/04 covers gasification processes and equipment that convert solid, liquid or gaseous carbonaceous feedstocks into synthesis gas by partial oxidation or other chemical reactions. The entry includes specific steps such as feed preparation, reactor operation, heat integration and gas cleaning, and applies to fuel preparation or upgrading in the petroleum, gas and coke industries.
[C10L 2290/06] Heat exchange, direct or indirect -- The C10L 2290/06 entry covers heat-exchange operations-both direct and indirect-used in the preparation or upgrading of fuels within petroleum, gas or coke processes. It includes the apparatus, configurations and specific process steps that transfer thermal energy between streams to improve fuel quality or efficiency.
[C10L 2290/08] Drying or removing water -- C10L 2290/08 pertains to methods and apparatus for drying or removing water from fuels during their preparation or upgrading. It includes processes such as dehydration, moisture extraction and related equipment used in petroleum, gas or coke-industry fuel processing, covering specific steps or units designed to reduce water content.
[C10L 2290/10] Recycling of a stream within the process or apparatus to reuse elsewhere therein -- C10L 2290/10 relates to fuel-preparation or upgrading processes and apparatus in which a material stream is diverted and recycled for reuse elsewhere within the same system. It covers methods and equipment that create internal loops to improve efficiency in petroleum, gas or coke-industry fuel production.
[C10L 2290/12] Regeneration of a solvent, catalyst, adsorbent or any other component used to treat or prepare a fuel -- The CPC subclass C10L 2290/12 concerns processes for restoring a solvent, catalyst, adsorbent or other component that has been employed in treating or preparing a fuel. It includes regeneration steps for these materials after use in fuel-preparation operations, such as desulfurisation, deoxygenation or upgrading, and may describe apparatus or conditions used to accomplish the regeneration.
[C10L 2290/14] Injection, e.g. in a reactor or a fuel stream during fuel production -- C10L 2290/14 covers the injection of substances into a reactor or fuel stream during fuel production, including methods and apparatus for introducing additives, reagents or catalysts. It is used to classify processes that modify fuel characteristics by adding components during preparation or upgrading.
[C10L 2290/141] of additive or catalyst -- C10L 2290/141 relates to fuel preparation or upgrading steps or equipment that incorporate an additive or catalyst, defining processes where such substances are introduced to promote chemical conversion, purification or performance improvement of petroleum-derived fuels.
[C10L 2290/143] of fuel -- C10L 2290/143 covers processes or apparatus for preparing or upgrading a fuel, including specific operational steps or unit operations within petroleum and gas fuel production. It falls under the subclass dealing with fuel preparation methods, encompassing technologies that convert, treat or blend feedstocks to produce a usable fuel.
[C10L 2290/145] of air -- C10L 2290/145 covers processes or apparatus for preparing or upgrading fuels in which air is employed as a reactant, such as oxidative, partial-oxidation or combustion steps that modify petroleum-derived or other hydrocarbon streams. It falls within the fuels-not-otherwise-provided-for subgroup of petroleum, gas or coke industry technologies.
[C10L 2290/146] of water -- C10L 2290/146 concerns fuel preparation or upgrading steps that involve water, covering methods where water is added, used as a reaction medium, or removed during processes such as steam treatment, aqueous-phase reactions, and water-based cleaning or conditioning of fuels.
[C10L 2290/148] of steam -- This subclass covers fuel preparation or upgrading methods that specifically involve steam, such as steam-driven reforming, cracking or other processing steps where steam is a reactant, carrier or heating medium, and the corresponding apparatus designed for those steam-based operations.
[C10L 2290/18] Spraying or sprinkling -- The CPC subclass C10L 2290/18 covers methods and apparatus that apply spraying or sprinkling techniques in the preparation or upgrading of fuels. It includes atomising, dispersing or misting liquids or gases onto or into fuel streams or reaction zones to improve mixing, heat transfer or chemical conversion during refining processes.
[C10L 2290/20] Coating of a fuel as a whole or of a fuel component -- This CPC sub-group covers methods for applying a coating to an entire fuel or to a specific fuel component, usually to enhance stability, handling, emissions or other performance characteristics. The coating can be applied during fuel preparation or upgrading and involves defined process steps or apparatus.
[C10L 2290/22] Impregnation or immersion of a fuel component or a fuel as a whole -- C10L 2290/22 covers methods in which a fuel component or the entire fuel is impregnated or immersed in a substance such as a catalyst, solvent or additive to modify its characteristics during preparation or upgrading. The process may involve soaking, percolation or other contact techniques applied to petroleum, gas or coke-derived fuels.
[C10L 2290/24] Mixing, stirring of fuel components -- C10L 2290/24 pertains to the mixing or stirring of fuel components in the preparation or upgrading of fuels. It covers processes and apparatus that combine different fuel streams or additives to produce a homogeneous mixture, commonly applied in petroleum refining and synthetic fuel production.
[C10L 2290/26] Composting, fermenting or anaerobic digestion fuel components or materials from which fuels are prepared -- C10L 2290/26 covers methods and apparatus for preparing fuel components or raw materials obtained through composting, fermenting or anaerobic digestion. It includes the treatment of organic waste, biomass or other biodegradable substrates to produce substances that can be further processed into fuels such as biogas, biodiesel or synthetic hydrocarbons.
[C10L 2290/28] Cutting, disintegrating, shredding or grinding -- C10L 2290/28 covers methods and equipment for cutting, disintegrating, shredding or grinding fuel materials during preparation or upgrading. It pertains to processes used in the petroleum, gas or coke industries for producing fuels not otherwise provided for. The classification includes specific steps or apparatus units that break down solid fuel feedstocks.
[C10L 2290/30] Pressing, compressing or compacting -- C10L 2290/30 covers the pressing, compressing or compacting steps used in preparing or upgrading fuels, including methods and apparatus that apply mechanical force to solid or semi-solid fuel feedstocks in order to improve handling, density or reactivity. The classification applies to processes where the primary purpose is to reduce volume or shape material rather than to chemically transform it. It is used for technologies such as pelletizing, briquetting or densification of coal, bio-fuels or other hydrocarbon-based fuels.
[C10L 2290/32] Molding or moulds -- C10L 2290/32 covers the design and use of moulds or moulding techniques in the preparation or upgrading of fuels, including equipment and steps for shaping solid or semi-solid fuel products. The subclass is applied to processes that form fuel material into defined shapes such as pellets, briquettes or blocks as part of petroleum, gas or coke-derived fuel manufacturing.
[C10L 2290/34] Applying ultrasonic energy -- The C10L 2290/34 subclass covers the use of ultrasonic energy within fuel preparation or upgrading operations, such as cracking, reforming, or cleaning processes. It includes methods or apparatus that apply high-frequency sound waves to accelerate reactions, improve mixing, or enhance product quality.
[C10L 2290/36] Applying radiation such as microwave, IR, UV -- This subclass covers methods and equipment for preparing or upgrading fuels that employ electromagnetic radiation such as microwaves, infrared or ultraviolet. The radiation is used to induce chemical or physical changes in the feedstock, for example cracking, desulfurisation or dehydrogenation, as part of a fuel-processing step.
[C10L 2290/38] Applying an electric field or inclusion of electrodes in the apparatus -- C10L 2290/38 covers fuel-preparation or upgrading processes in which an electric field is applied or electrodes are incorporated into the apparatus. It pertains to chemical methods for handling petroleum-derived fuels that are not covered by other specific fuel categories, focusing on electrical assistance in reaction or separation steps.
[C10L 2290/40] Applying a magnetic field or inclusion of magnets in the apparatus -- The C10L 2290/40 subclass covers fuel preparation or upgrading equipment that incorporates magnetic fields or magnets as part of the process or apparatus. Such magnetic assistance may be used to enhance reaction rates, improve product quality, or aid separation in petroleum, gas, or coke-derived fuel production.
[C10L 2290/42] Fischer-Tropsch steps -- C10L 2290/42 relates to fuel preparation or upgrading processes that use Fischer-Tropsch synthesis, focusing on the individual reaction steps or corresponding unit operations. It is part of the chemistry section for petroleum, gas or coke industries, under fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 2290/44] Deacidification step, e.g. in coal enhancing -- C10L 2290/44 covers a deacidification step incorporated into fuel-preparation or upgrading processes, especially for coal, where acidic components are removed to improve fuel quality. The classification applies to methods or apparatus that include this specific treatment as part of the overall fuel-making operation.
[C10L 2290/46] Compressors or pumps -- The C10L 2290/46 entry covers compressors and pumps employed in fuel-preparation or upgrading operations within the petroleum, gas or coke sector. It pertains to apparatus that moves, compresses or pressurises liquids or gases as part of specific process steps for producing fuels not otherwise classified. This falls under the broader hierarchy of Chemistry → Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries → Fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 2290/48] Expanders, e.g. throttles or flash tanks -- C10L 2290/48 covers expanders such as throttles or flash tanks used in fuel preparation or upgrading processes. These devices provide pressure reduction or rapid expansion of hydrocarbon streams to facilitate cracking, vaporization or heat recovery within petroleum refining or fuel production.
[C10L 2290/50] Screws or pistons for moving along solids -- C10L 2290/50 is a subclass of the CPC that deals with screws or pistons designed to move along solid materials in fuel preparation or upgrading processes. It covers mechanical elements used to transport, mix or convey solid feedstock within petroleum, gas or coke industry operations.
[C10L 2290/52] Hoppers -- C10L 2290/52 - Hoppers refers to bulk storage or feeding devices employed in fuel preparation or upgrading operations, including vessels, bins or containers that receive, hold and discharge solid or liquid fuel intermediates as part of the process flow.
[C10L 2290/54] Specific separation steps for separating fractions, components or impurities during preparation or upgrading of a fuel -- C10L 2290/54 covers specific separation operations carried out while preparing or upgrading fuels, such as distillation, extraction, adsorption, membrane or filtration steps used to isolate particular fractions, components or impurities. The code is part of the CPC hierarchy under chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 2290/541] Absorption of impurities during preparation or upgrading of a fuel -- C10L 2290/541 covers processes and equipment that remove contaminant species from fuels by absorption during preparation or upgrading. It includes the use of liquid or solid absorbents, selective solvents or contacted phases to capture sulfur, nitrogen, metal compounds or other unwanted impurities before the fuel is finalized.
[C10L 2290/542] Adsorption of impurities during preparation or upgrading of a fuel -- The CPC class C10L 2290/542 covers fuel preparation or upgrading steps that employ adsorption to remove impurity species from the fuel. It includes processes, reactor configurations and apparatus units designed specifically for adsorbing contaminants during the conversion or cleansing of petroleum-derived fuels. This subclass falls under the broader "Fuels not otherwise provided for" group within the petroleum, gas or coke industry sector.
[C10L 2290/543] Distillation, fractionation or rectification for separating fractions, components or impurities during preparation or upgrading of a fuel -- A process that uses distillation, fractionation or rectification to separate desired fuel fractions, components or impurities while preparing or upgrading a fuel. It covers the specific steps, equipment and operating conditions employed to achieve the separation in petroleum and related fuel production.
[C10L 2290/544] Extraction for separating fractions, components or impurities during preparation or upgrading of a fuel -- The C10L 2290/544 entry covers extraction steps used to separate fractions, individual components or impurities while preparing or upgrading fuels. It applies to processes and apparatus that isolate desired fuel streams or remove unwanted substances during petroleum, gas, coke or other fuel production, ensuring cleaner or more refined products.
[C10L 2290/545] Washing, scrubbing, stripping, scavenging for separating fractions, components or impurities during preparation or upgrading of a fuel -- C10L 2290/545 covers washing, scrubbing, stripping and scavenging operations employed to separate fractions, components or impurities while preparing or upgrading a fuel. It relates to processes and equipment that clean or purge feedstocks in petroleum-derived fuel production.
[C10L 2290/546] Sieving for separating fractions, components or impurities during preparation or upgrading of a fuel -- The CPC entry C10L 2290/546 covers sieving methods applied during the preparation or upgrading of fuels to separate fractions, components or impurities. It pertains to specific process steps or apparatus units used for mechanical screening in petroleum, gas or coke industry fuel production.
[C10L 2290/547] Filtration for separating fractions, components or impurities during preparation or upgrading of a fuel -- This CPC subclass covers filtration methods applied during the preparation or upgrading of fuels to separate fractions, components or impurities. It includes both the specific filtration steps and the apparatus units that perform them within petroleum, gas or coke-derived fuel processes.
[C10L 2290/548] Membrane- or permeation-treatment for separating fractions, components or impurities during preparation or upgrading of a fuel -- The code covers methods and equipment that employ membranes or other permeation technologies to selectively remove or recover specific fractions, components or contaminants while refining or upgrading fuels such as gasoline, diesel or jet fuel. It includes integrated steps and unit operations designed for separation during fuel processing.
[C10L 2290/56] Specific details of the apparatus for preparation or upgrading of a fuel -- C10L 2290/56 covers the detailed design features of equipment used in the preparation or upgrading of fuels, such as reactors, separators, heat exchangers or mixers, where the apparatus includes particular structural elements, connections or control components that affect the fuel-processing operation. It refines broader categories on fuel preparation and upgrading.
[C10L 2290/562] Modular or modular elements containing apparatus -- This CPC subclass covers modular units that incorporate apparatus used in fuel preparation or upgrading processes. It includes prefabricated sections, interchangeable modules, or containerized systems that perform specific steps of the process. The classification is applied to inventions where equipment is integrated into a modular configuration to facilitate installation, transport, or scaling.
[C10L 2290/565] Apparatus size -- C10L 2290/565 classifies the size characteristics of equipment used in fuel preparation or upgrading, such as reactors, separators and other units, within petroleum, gas or coke industry processes. It addresses dimensions, capacity and scale of apparatus that affect the performance of fuel processing operations.
[C10L 2290/567] Mobile or displaceable apparatus -- This CPC subclass (C10L 2290/567) covers mobile or displaceable equipment used for fuel preparation or upgrading. It includes apparatus that can be moved or relocated during operation, such as portable reactors, reformers, desulfurization units, and other transportable devices employed in petroleum, gas or coke industry fuel processing.
[C10L 2290/58] Control or regulation of the fuel preparation of upgrading process -- C10L 2290/58 covers methods and devices for controlling or regulating the preparation and upgrading of fuels, such as processes that adjust temperature, pressure, flow or composition during refining steps. The entry includes sensors, control algorithms, feedback loops and instrumentation used to optimize fuel quality and efficiency.
