It falls under the chemical sector, petroleum and gas industries, specifically fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 2290/60] Measuring or analysing fractions, components or impurities or process conditions during preparation or upgrading of a fuel -- C10L 2290/60 covers methods and apparatus for monitoring the composition, impurities, or operational parameters of fuels during their preparation or upgrading, such as real-time analysis of fractions and process conditions. It falls under the chemistry category, specifically petroleum, gas or coke industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 2300/00] Mixture of two or more additives covered by the same group of -- C10L 2300/00 covers blends that contain two or more fuel additives belonging to the same functional group. The classification is used for inventions where the combined additives are administered together to improve properties of petroleum, gas or coke derived fuels. It falls under chemistry, petroleum and fuel categories.
[C10L 2300/20] Mixture of two components -- C10L 2300/20 classifies mixtures that contain exactly two components, each being an additive from the same defined group, intended for use in fuels not otherwise specified. It lies within the hierarchy of chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, and fuels not otherwise provided for.
[C10L 2300/30] Mixture of three components -- C10L 2300/30 covers fuel mixtures that combine exactly three additives belonging to the same additive group. The classification is used for formulations in the petroleum, gas or coke sectors where the components are chemically related and serve a common function, such as improving combustion or stability.
[C10L 2300/40] Mixture of four or more components -- C10L 2300/40 covers fuel mixtures that contain four or more additives belonging to the same subgroup of petroleum, gas or coke industry additives. The classification is used for formulations in which multiple components are combined to modify fuel properties such as stability, combustion or emissions.
[C10L 3/00] Gaseous fuels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any gaseous hydrocarbonaceous fuel, which is not covered by a subgroup or has too little detail or too much of a deviating detail from the titles of subgroups to be included therein.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Hydrogen -> C01B3/00 - Rocket fuels (inorganic compounds mostly) -> C06B - Production of rocket fuel -> F02K
[C10L 3/003] Additives for gaseous fuels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gaseous fuel compositions comprising additives such as a marker, non detectable by senses, which could be to increase heat value or storage stability.
[C10L 3/006] detectable by the senses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Adding colour or smell to a gas for recognition, or aesthetic reasons.
[C10L 3/02] Compositions containing acetylene -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Acetylene comprising gas fuel compositions which could be used e.g. for welding tools, mixed with other compounds to e.g. increase heat value or improve storage.
[C10L 3/04] Absorbing compositions, e.g. solvents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Additives used to improve storage of acetylene, e.g. as an absorbing mass in a container
[C10L 3/06] Natural gas -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of natural gas (NG), e.g. transport, storage, or alteration which is not considered working-up according to C10L3/10 or production according to C10L3/08 , e.g. pipeline transport or hydrate formation or prevention.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cryogenic processes to separate or otherwise treat a gas, i.e. the gas to be treated is in (cold) liquid state -> F25J
[C10L 3/08] Production of synthetic natural gas -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes of synthetic natural gas, e.g. production of biogas through e.g. anaerobic digestion or by methanation from syngas.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methanation when the goal is not methane production, but purification of hydrogen -> C01B3/586 - Gasification processes where methane may be a byproduct, or goes on to further use other than as a fuel -> C10J
[C10L 3/10] Working-up natural gas or synthetic natural gas -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Further treatment of natural gas (no matter what the origin of the gas stream), not exhaust gases.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cryogenic processes to separate or otherwise treat a gas, i.e. the gas to be treated is in (cold) liquid state -> F25J
[C10L 3/101] Removal of contaminants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  removal of contaminants, e.g. unwanted heavy hydrocarbons.
[C10L 3/102] of acid contaminants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Removal of acid compounds in general, other than S or CO 2 or when no detail is given at all.
[C10L 3/103] Sulfur containing contaminants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Removal of S-containing compounds in particular.
[C10L 3/104] Carbon dioxide -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Removal of CO 2 -containing compounds in particular.
[C10L 3/105] of nitrogen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Denitrification of natural gas.
[C10L 3/106] of water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dehydration of natural gas.
[C10L 3/107] Limiting or prohibiting hydrate formation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for removal or inhibition of gas hydrate formation.
[C10L 3/108] Production of gas hydrates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods to produce gas hydrates, e.g. for transport.
[C10L 3/12] Liquefied petroleum gas -- **Definition:** This place covers:  LPG compositions (mainly a composition comprising propane and butane), characteristics, production and/or upgrading.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cryogenic processes to separate or otherwise treat a gas, i.e. the gas to be treated is in (cold) liquid state -> F25J
[C10L 5/00] Solid fuels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A solid matter which is of mineral origin, e.g. coal or non-mineral origin, e.g. wood and which is clearly usable as a fuel.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Solid fuels produced by solidifying fluid fuels -> C10L7/00 - Peat briquettes -> C10F7/06
[C10L 5/02] briquettes consisting mainly of carbonaceous materials of mineral origin -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Briquettes or other solid shapes of carbonaceous material.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Peat briquettes -> C10F
[C10L 5/04] Raw material to be used -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The type of mineral feed material is an object of the invention.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Pretreatment of raw materials of non-mineral origin -> C10L5/40
[C10L 5/06] Methods of briquetting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The method of forming the solid fuel, e.g. briquettes, pellets or logs, independently of the origin of the fuel.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Moulding of materials in general, not for use as fuel -> B27N - Mechanical part of pressing briquettes per se -> B30B11/00
[C10L 5/08] without the aid of extraneous binders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Briquettes are formed and bound e.g. by the action of pressing itself or heat, but without addition of a binding agent.
[C10L 5/10] with the aid of binders, e.g. pretreated binders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods where the solid fuel composition includes a composition which functions to keep the briquette in shape. This is valid for compositions both of mineral origin or non-mineral origin, which feature is to be classified separately in C10L5/02 or C10L5/40 , or subgroups.
[C10L 5/105] with a mixture of organic and inorganic binders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Organic binders. mixed with inorganic binders  **Glossary:** - Organic binders: (Hydro)carbonaceous binders, i.e. comprising a hydrocarbon chain
[C10L 5/12] with inorganic binders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  An inorganic compound usually being an ionic compound added to the solid fuel to serve as a binder.
[C10L 5/14] with organic binders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  An organic compound could be any compound comprising at least partly a hydrocarbon structure, which is added to the solid fuel to serve as a binder. Either naturally occurring or a (side) product.
[C10L 5/143] with lignin-containing products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lignin binders mainly used in biomass fuels as it is itself derived from biomass (mainly wood).
[C10L 5/146] with wax, e.g. paraffin wax -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Binders comprising longer chain alkanes (paraffins) which are solid at room temperature, e.g. waxes.
[C10L 5/16] with bituminous binders, e.g. tar, pitch -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Binders with bituminous binders, e.g. tar, pitch or other heavy hydrocarbonaceous fractions used to bind the briquettes.
[C10L 5/18] with naphthalene -- **Definition:** This place covers:  binders comprising naphthalene, (molecular formula C 10 H 8 ).
[C10L 5/20] with sulfite lye -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Organic binders comprising hydrogen sulfite.
[C10L 5/22] Methods of applying the binder to the other compounding ingredients -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of the specific application of the binder to the fuel, either method or apparatus
[C10L 5/24] Combating dust during briquetting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Dust combating either by a process step or alteration of briquetting process or by addition of a compound which reduces dust formation
[C10L 5/26] After-treatment of the briquettes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any process steps applied after the briquetting to serve a particular purpose such as improving storage or transport stability.
[C10L 5/28] Heating the briquettes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  When heating is done after briquettes are formed in order to coke them and stabilize their structure.
[C10L 5/30] Cooling the briquettes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  When cooling is done in a particular way which is an object of the invention, e.g. after a briquetting process from which the briquettes emerge at too high a temperature.
[C10L 5/32] Coating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cating of briquettes either superficially or including also impregnation with a certain compound.
[C10L 5/34] Other details of the briquettes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any detail other than the shape or size of the briquette.
[C10L 5/36] Shape -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Shape or also including specific dimensions being part of the object of the invention.
[C10L 5/361] Briquettes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Briquettes, e.g. such as used for barbecues.
[C10L 5/363] Pellets or granulates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pellets as produced by pelletizer.
[C10L 5/365] Logs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Log-shaped fuel, e.g. to imitate actual wood logs.
[C10L 5/366] Powders -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Powdered fuel as the end product, which could be pulverised pellets or briquettes, to be used e.g. in powdered coal fuelled combustor.
[C10L 5/368] Shaped fuels bundled or contained in a bag or other container -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The bundling or collecting of individual pieces of fuel, e.g. logs or briquettes, by e.g. a rope around them or putting them in bags or any other type of container.
[C10L 5/38] Briquettes consisting of different layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The composition of the briquettes comprises several components and each of them is applied in a separate layer, e.g.details on layer thickness.
[C10L 5/40] essentially based on materials of non-mineral origin -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any solid fuel which includes material of non-mineral origin.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Candles -> C11C5/00
[C10L 5/403] on paper and paper waste -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuel produced from paper waste, e.g. pulp or black liquor.
[C10L 5/406] on plastic -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solid fuel based at least partly on plastic (waste) material.
[C10L 5/42] on animal substances or products obtained therefrom -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Meat or bone waste from a slaughterhouse.  Excrement or manure waste.
[C10L 5/44] on vegetable substances -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Biomass, as broadly defined as possible, but not including animal substances, usable as a fuel.
[C10L 5/442] Wood or forestry waste -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solid fuel based explicitly on wood or wood waste, i.e. a further limitation over biomass.
[C10L 5/445] Agricultural waste, e.g. corn crops, grass clippings, nut shells or oil pressing residues -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solid fuel based on biomass comprising different agricultural waste other than wood.
[C10L 5/447] Carbonized vegetable substances, e.g. charcoal, or produced by hydrothermal carbonization of biomass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Solid fuels based on e.g. charcoal or other biomass which has been carbonised before treatment to solid fuel.
[C10L 5/46] on sewage, house, or town refuse -- **Definition:** This place covers:  House or town refuse, i.e. residential mixed wastes. Also includes pretreated house or townrefuse, which fraction is known as RDF (refuse derived fuel) or SRF (solid recovered fuel).
[C10L 5/48] on industrial residues and waste materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Waste on industrial level.
[C10L 7/00] Fuels produced by solidifying fluid fuels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gel or gellified fuels. Compositions or process of manufacture thereof.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Candles -> C11C5/00
[C10L 7/02] liquid fuels -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuels when the fuel is solidified from a liquid fuel not based on an alcohol.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lubricating compositions -> C10M
[C10L 7/04] alcohol -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fuels when the fuel is solidified from a liquid fuel based on an alcohol (of any chain).
[C10L 8/00] Fuels not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Residual group for special applications which really cannot possibly be classified anywhere else.
[C10L 9/00] Treating solid fuels to improve their combustion -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Treatments of solid fuels after they have been formed, specifically to improve combustion in some way, e.g. heat value or contaminants in flue gas.
[C10L 9/02] by chemical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means when chemicals are added and/or a chemical reaction is required to treat the solid fuel in order to improve combustion characteristics.
[C10L 9/04] by hydrogenating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods of treating solid fuels by addition of hydrogenating compounds.
[C10L 9/06] by oxidation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods of treating solid fuels by addition oxidising compounds.
[C10L 9/08] by heat treatments, e.g. calcining -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Treatments which consist mainly of heating the solid in order to improve its combustion characteristics.
[C10L 9/083] Torrefaction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Torrefaction or bertinisation whereby the solid (waste) feed is treated around 150-350°C under low oxygen atmosphere to produce a more dense and valuable solid fuel product, e.g. low temperature- or pre-pyrolysis.
[C10L 9/086] Hydrothermal carbonization -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydrothermal carbonization, which can be defined as combined dehydration and decarboxylation of a fuel to raise its carbon content with the aim of achieving a higher calorific value. It is realized by applying elevated temperatures (180–220°C) to biomass in a suspension with water under saturated pressure for several hours.
[C10L 9/10] by using additives -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Treating solid fuels when any kind of additive is mixed with the solid to improve its combustion.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Addition of additive during combustion or at entry of combustion/gasification/or other chamber -> F02D19/00 , C10J
[C10L 9/12] Oxidation means, e.g. oxygen-generating compounds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compounds that facilitate oxidation or generate oxygen at combustion.
[C10M] LUBRICATING COMPOSITIONS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubricating compositions may have three types of essential ingredients: Base materials (main ingredient of the lubricating composition), thickeners (used for preparing grease compositions) and additives.  Additives are those chemicals used to enhance the performance characteristics of the base material where the additives are normally present in a minor amount of a fully formulated lubricating composition, but can be present in a larger amount of an additive concentrate.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Production of hydrocarbon oils from lower carbon number hydrocarbons for lubricating purposes, e.g. by oligomerisation -> C10G50/02
[C10M 1/00] Liquid compositions essentially based on mineral lubricating oils or fatty oils; Their use as lubricants -- Group C10M1/00 is no longer used for the classification of documents as of 2025-05-01 . The content of this group is being reclassified into groups C10M101/00 - C10M177/00 . Groups C10M1/00 and C10M101/00 - C10M177/00 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[C10M 101/00] Base-Materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Composition containing more than 10 % water -> C10M173/00  **Glossary:** - Group I (API): Base oil having a viscosity index of 80-120 and comprising greater than 0.03 % sulfur and/or less than 90 % saturates - Group II (API): Base oil having a viscosity index of 80-120 and comprising 0.03 % or less sulfur and 90 % or more saturates - Group III (API): Base oil having a viscosity index of 120 or more and comprising 0.03 % or less sulfur and 90 % or more saturates
[C10M 101/02] Petroleum fractions -- The CPC class C10M 101/02 falls under Chemistry, Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries, Lubricating Compositions, Base-Materials and covers petroleum fractions used as base stocks in lubricating oils. It includes mineral oil fractions derived from crude oil that serve as the primary component in lubricants.
[C10M 101/025] waxes -- C10M 101/025 covers waxes employed as base materials in lubricating compositions for the petroleum, gas or coke sectors. These solid hydrocarbons, derived from petroleum or mineral sources, are added to improve viscosity, film strength and wear protection in oils and greases. The classification groups formulations where waxes serve as the primary structural component of the lubricant.
[C10M 101/04] Fatty oil fractions -- The classification C10M 101/04 covers fatty oil fractions that serve as base-materials in lubricating compositions. It pertains to the selection, processing and use of these oil fractions, typically derived from petroleum-related sources, to provide desirable lubrication properties.
[C10M 103/00] Lubricating compositions characterised by the base-material being an inorganic material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubricating compositions comprising a base-material being an inorganic material as single ingredient or a composition comprising an inorganic material, e.g.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Composition containing more than 10 % water -> C10M173/00
[C10M 103/02] Carbon -- C10M 103/02 relates to lubricating compositions whose principal inorganic base material is carbon, including forms such as graphite, carbon black, carbon fibers or other solid carbon particles. These carbon-based lubricants provide solid-film lubrication, wear reduction and thermal stability in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10M 103/04] Metals -- C10M 103/04 classifies lubricating compositions whose primary base material is an inorganic metal or metal-based compound, including metallic powders, oxides, alloys or other metal derivatives used to provide lubrication. These metal-based bases are employed in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications where inorganic lubricants are required.
[C10M 103/06] Metal compounds -- Lubricating compositions whose primary base material is an inorganic substance, specifically metal compounds, are covered by this subclass. It includes formulations in which metallic salts, oxides, sulfides or other metal-based inorganic agents serve as the main lubricating medium, often for petroleum-derived or industrial applications.
[C10M 105/00] Lubricating compositions characterised by the base-material being a non-macromolecular organic compound -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubricating compositions comprising a base-material being a non-macromolecular organic compound, as single ingredient or a composition comprising a non-macromolecular organic compound e.g.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fischer-Tropsch base oil -> C10M107/02
[C10M 105/02] Well-defined hydrocarbons -- C10M 105/02 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound, specifically well-defined hydrocarbons such as individual alkanes, cycloalkanes, aromatics or their mixtures. These formulations are used in petroleum-related applications where precise chemical structure of the base oil is required for performance.
[C10M 105/04] aliphatic -- C10M 105/04 pertains to lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound, specifically aliphatic substances, used as lubricants in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10M 105/06] aromatic -- The subclass C10M 105/06 pertains to lubricating compositions whose principal base is a low-molecular-weight aromatic organic compound, such as benzene-derived oils. These non-macromolecular aromatics serve as the fluid carrier that provides viscosity, lubricity and thermal stability in petroleum-related applications.
[C10M 105/08] containing oxygen -- C10M 105/08 designates lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound that includes oxygen in its molecular structure, for example esters, alcohols, ethers or similar oxygen-bearing substances used as the primary lubricant component.
[C10M 105/10] having hydroxy groups bound to acyclic or cycloaliphatic carbon atoms -- This CPC subclass C10M 105/10 covers lubricating compositions whose base is a non-macromolecular organic compound that contains hydroxy groups bound to acyclic or cycloaliphatic carbon atoms. It sits within the hierarchy chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, lubricating compositions.
[C10M 105/12] monohydroxy -- C10M 105/12 classifies lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound and specifically includes monohydroxy substances. It falls under Chemistry → Petroleum, Gas or Coke Industries → Lubricating Compositions. This subclass covers formulations where a single hydroxyl-containing organic molecule serves as the primary lubricant base.
[C10M 105/14] polyhydroxy -- This subclass covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound, specifically polyhydroxy substances such as polyhydric alcohols. The formulations are designed to reduce friction and wear in machinery and may include additional additives for anti-wear or extreme-pressure performance.
[C10M 105/16] having hydroxy groups bound to a carbon atom of a six-membered aromatic ring -- The subclass C10M 105/16 defines lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound that bears hydroxy groups directly attached to a carbon atom of a six-membered aromatic ring. It is situated under C10M 105, which covers non-macromolecular base materials.
[C10M 105/18] Ethers, e.g. epoxides -- This CPC subclass covers lubricating compositions whose primary base material is a low-molecular-weight organic ether, including epoxide derivatives, used to provide specific friction-reducing or protective properties in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications. These ethers function as solvents, viscosity modifiers or anti-wear agents within the formulation.
[C10M 105/20] Aldehydes -- C10M 105/20 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular organic compound and specifically includes the use of aldehydes as additives or base components. Aldehydes may serve as viscosity modifiers, anti-wear agents or corrosion inhibitors in such formulations.
[C10M 105/22] Carboxylic acids or their salts -- Carboxylic acids or their salts are employed as additives in lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound, providing functions such as viscosity improvement, corrosion inhibition, or extreme-pressure protection. This subclass includes formulations where these acids or salts serve as the primary active ingredient within the lubricant.
[C10M 105/24] having only one carboxyl group bound to an acyclic carbon atom, cycloaliphatic carbon atom or hydrogen -- The subclass C10M 105/24 pertains to lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound containing exactly one carboxyl group that is linked to an acyclic carbon atom, a cycloaliphatic carbon atom, or directly to hydrogen. Such formulations are used where a single carboxylic functionality provides the desired lubricating properties.
[C10M 105/26] having more than one carboxyl group bound to an acyclic carbon atom or cycloaliphatic carbon atom -- C10M 105/26 relates to lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound that contains two or more carboxyl groups attached to a linear or cycloaliphatic carbon atom. These compositions are classified under lubricating compositions within the petroleum, gas or coke industries in the CPC system.
[C10M 105/28] having only one carboxyl group bound to a carbon atom of a six-membered aromatic ring -- This subgroup refers to lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound that contains a single carboxyl group directly attached to a carbon atom of a six-membered aromatic ring, used as oils or additives in petroleum-based lubricants.
[C10M 105/30] having more than one carboxyl group bound to a carbon atom of a six-membered aromatic ring -- This subgroup covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a small organic compound that contains two or more carboxyl groups attached to a carbon atom of a six-membered aromatic ring, such as phthalic-acid-derived molecules. The classification is used for formulations that employ these polycarboxylic aromatic bases to enhance wear protection, corrosion resistance, or viscosity-modifying properties.
[C10M 105/32] Esters -- C10M 105/32 covers lubricating compositions whose base component is a low-molecular-weight organic ester rather than a polymeric material. These ester-based oils and greases are used to provide lubrication in engines, gearboxes and other mechanical systems, offering good thermal stability and biodegradability.
[C10M 105/34] of monocarboxylic acids -- C10M 105/34 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound derived from monocarboxylic acids, typically used in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications to provide lubrication and reduce wear.
[C10M 105/36] of polycarboxylic acids -- Lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound, specifically those that incorporate polycarboxylic acids as the primary component. These formulations are used in petroleum-related applications to provide viscosity control, corrosion resistance and wear protection. The classification falls under chemistry for lubricating compositions in the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10M 105/38] of polyhydroxy compounds -- Lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic polyhydroxy compound, such as glycerol, sorbitol or their derivatives, are covered by this subclass. These formulations are intended to provide film-forming lubrication and corrosion protection in petroleum-related applications while maintaining low viscosity and high thermal stability.
[C10M 105/40] containing free hydroxy or carboxyl groups -- This CPC subclass covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound that includes at least one free hydroxyl or carboxyl functional group. The presence of these groups influences the oil's polarity, detergency, and anti-wear properties. Such formulations are used in industrial and automotive applications.
[C10M 105/42] Complex esters, i.e. compounds containing at least three esterified carboxyl groups and derived from the combination of at least three different types of the following five types of compound: monohydroxy compounds, polyhydroxy compounds, monocarboxylic acids, polycarboxylic acids and hydroxy carboxylic acids
[C10M 105/44] derived from the combination of monocarboxylic acids, dicarboxylic acids and dihydroxy compounds only and having no free hydroxy or carboxyl groups -- This CPC subclass covers lubricating compositions where the base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound formed solely from monocarboxylic acids, dicarboxylic acids and dihydroxy compounds that have reacted to eliminate all free hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. It falls under C10M 105/44 in the hierarchy of chemistry - petroleum, gas or coke industries - lubricating compositions.
[C10M 105/46] derived from the combination of monohydroxy compounds, dihydroxy compounds and dicarboxylic acids only and having no free hydroxy or carboxyl groups -- This subclass covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound formed by reacting monohydroxy and dihydroxy compounds with dicarboxylic acids, leaving no free hydroxy or carboxyl groups. It falls under CPC entry C10M 105/46.
[C10M 105/48] of carbonic acid -- C10M 105/48 relates to lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound and that contain carbonic acid as a component. These compositions are employed to reduce friction in machinery used in the petroleum, gas or coke sectors.
[C10M 105/50] containing halogen -- This subclass pertains to lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound that incorporates halogen atoms. The presence of halogen improves wear resistance, corrosion protection, or extreme-pressure performance in the formulation.
[C10M 105/52] containing carbon, hydrogen and halogen only -- This CPC subclass covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound made up only of carbon, hydrogen and halogen atoms, typically used in petroleum-related applications to provide friction reduction and wear protection.
[C10M 105/525] halogenated waxes -- C10M 105/525 covers lubricating compositions in which the base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound, specifically halogenated waxes. These are waxes whose hydrocarbon chains have been chemically modified by incorporation of halogen atoms to improve properties such as wear resistance, oxidation stability, and extreme-pressure performance in lubricants.
[C10M 105/54] containing carbon, hydrogen, halogen and oxygen -- C10M 105/54 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound that includes carbon, hydrogen, at least one halogen and oxygen. The classification is used for formulations where these elements are present in the base oil or fluid, distinguishing them from polymeric or purely hydrocarbon bases. It falls under the hierarchy for petroleum-based lubricants in the CPC system.
[C10M 105/56] containing nitrogen -- C10M 105/56 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound that contains nitrogen atoms. Such nitrogen-containing bases are used to modify viscosity, anti-wear or corrosion-inhibiting performance in petroleum-related lubricants.
[C10M 105/58] Amines, e.g. polyalkylene polyamines, quaternary amines -- It pertains to lubricating compositions whose base is a low-molecular-weight organic compound and that incorporate amine additives, such as polyalkylene polyamines or quaternary amines, to enhance performance characteristics.
[C10M 105/60] having amino groups bound to an acyclic or cycloaliphatic carbon atom -- C10M 105/60 defines lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound that includes amino groups directly bonded to an acyclic or cycloaliphatic carbon atom, distinguishing them from macromolecular bases. This subclass falls under lubricating compositions within the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10M 105/62] containing hydroxy groups -- This subgroup covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound that contains hydroxy groups. The presence of hydroxyl functionality adds polarity, enhancing film-forming ability and anti-wear performance in the lubricant.
[C10M 105/64] having amino groups bound to a carbon atom of a six-membered aromatic ring -- This subclass covers lubricating compositions whose base is a low-molecular-weight organic compound containing an aromatic ring with an amino substituent attached directly to a carbon of the six-membered ring. Such compounds are used as additives to improve anti-wear, friction reduction, or extreme-pressure performance.
[C10M 105/66] containing hydroxy groups -- C10M 105/66 classifies lubricating compositions whose base material is a non-macromolecular organic compound and that include hydroxy groups. Such compositions are used to reduce friction and wear in machinery.
[C10M 105/68] Amides -- C10M 105/68 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is an amide, a non-macromolecular organic compound used to provide film-forming and friction-reducing properties in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications. The classification includes formulations where the amide functions as the primary oil-soluble base component.
[C10M 105/70] as ring hetero atom -- C10M 105/70 covers lubricating compositions whose base oil is a non-macromolecular organic compound containing a hetero atom within a ring structure. These compositions are intended for lubrication in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10M 105/72] containing sulfur, selenium or tellurium -- The CPC subclass C10M 105/72 covers lubricating compositions whose base is a non-macromolecular organic compound and that include sulfur, selenium or tellurium as additives. These elements are used to enhance the anti-wear, extreme-pressure or antioxidant properties of the lubricant. The subclass falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, petroleum and gas industries, and lubricating compositions.
[C10M 105/74] containing phosphorus -- C10M 105/74 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound and that include phosphorus as a constituent, typically to provide anti-wear or extreme-pressure properties.
[C10M 105/76] containing silicon -- A lubricating composition in which the base fluid is a low-molecular-weight organic compound and which includes silicon-containing ingredients, such as silanes, silicones or silica derivatives, to impart enhanced thermal stability, reduced friction or wear-protective properties.
[C10M 105/78] containing boron -- This subclass covers lubricating compositions where the base material is a low-molecular-weight organic compound and includes boron compounds as additives or components, intended to improve wear resistance, extreme-pressure performance, or corrosion protection.
[C10M 105/80] containing atoms of elements not provided for in groups -- C10M 105/80 pertains to lubricating compositions whose base is a low-molecular-weight organic compound and that include atoms of elements not listed in the other specified groups. The classification captures additives or base materials incorporating unconventional or rare elements beyond the usual carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, silicon, etc. It falls under lubricating compositions for petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10M 107/00] Lubricating compositions characterised by the base-material being a macromolecular compound -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubricating compositions comprising a base-material being a macromolecular organic compound as single ingredient or a composition comprising a macromolecular organic compound, e.g.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mineral or fatty oil -> C10M101/00  **Glossary:** - Group IV: Polyalphaolefins
[C10M 107/02] Hydrocarbon polymers -- C10M 107/02 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular hydrocarbon polymer. These polymeric lubricants are used to reduce friction and wear in machinery, often providing improved thermal stability and load-bearing capacity compared with conventional oil-based lubricants.
[C10M 107/04] Polyethylene -- C10M 107/04 relates to lubricating compositions where the base material is polyethylene, a polymeric hydrocarbon used as the primary carrier or additive. Such compositions exploit the low friction, chemical resistance and film-forming properties of polyethylene to provide lubrication in metalworking, bearing and other industrial applications.
[C10M 107/06] containing propene -- C10M 107/06 classifies lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular compound that incorporates propene as a monomer or polymer component. These compositions are designed to reduce friction and wear in machinery, particularly in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10M 107/08] containing butene -- The subclass C10M 107/08 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular compound and that incorporate butene, usually as a solvent, co-polymer monomer or performance-enhancing additive. It falls under Chemistry → Petroleum, gas or coke industries → Lubricating compositions → Base-material macromolecular compounds.
[C10M 107/10] containing aliphatic monomer having more than 4 carbon atoms -- The subclass C10M 107/10 pertains to lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular compound that incorporates aliphatic monomers containing more than four carbon atoms. These compositions are employed in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications to provide lubrication and wear protection.
[C10M 107/12] containing aromatic monomer, e.g. styrene -- C10M 107/12 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a polymeric or macromolecular compound that includes an aromatic monomer such as styrene. The aromatic monomer is incorporated to modify the viscosity-index, oxidative stability or wear-protective properties of the lubricant, providing improved performance in petroleum-derived applications.
[C10M 107/14] containing conjugated diene -- C10M 107/14 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular compound and that comprise a conjugated diene component. The diene is used to impart specific oxidative stability, viscosity-modifying or friction-reducing properties to the lubricant. This subclass falls within the broader categories of chemistry, petroleum or gas industries, and specifically lubricating compositions.
[C10M 107/16] containing non-conjugated diene -- Lubricating compositions whose polymeric base material incorporates a non-conjugated diene unit, providing characteristics such as enhanced elasticity, resistance to oxidation or improved film-forming ability. The diene is part of the macromolecular structure rather than a separate additive.
[C10M 107/18] Hydrocarbon polymers modified by oxidation -- This classification covers lubricants whose primary base is a hydrocarbon polymer that has undergone oxidation to introduce oxygen-containing groups. Oxidative modification adjusts the polymer's polarity, viscosity index and anti-wear performance, making it suitable for high-temperature or extreme-pressure applications.
[C10M 107/20] containing oxygen -- C10M 107/20 refers to lubricating compositions whose macromolecular base material contains oxygen, such as polymeric esters, polyalkyleneglycols or other oxygen-bearing polymers. These oxygen-containing macromolecules serve as the primary lubricating component in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10M 107/22] Macromolecular compounds obtained by reactions only involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds -- The subclass includes lubricating base materials that are polymers created exclusively through reactions between carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds, such as olefin or diene polymerisation. These macromolecular compounds serve as the primary component of the lubricant composition.
[C10M 107/24] containing monomers having an unsaturated radical bound to an alcohol, aldehyde, ketonic, ether, ketal or acetal radical -- C10M 107/24 is a CPC subclass within chemistry → petroleum, gas or coke industries → lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular compound. It covers lubricants that contain monomers with an unsaturated radical bound to an alcohol, aldehyde, ketone, ether, ketal or acetal functional group.
[C10M 107/26] containing monomers having an unsaturated radical bound to an acyloxy radical of a saturated carboxylic or carbonic acid -- C10M 107/26 covers lubricating compositions whose base is a polymeric material that includes monomer units in which an unsaturated radical is attached to an acyloxy group derived from a saturated carboxylic or carbonic acid. These formulations are used to provide lubrication in petroleum-related applications.
[C10M 107/28] containing monomers having an unsaturated radical bound to a carboxyl radical, e.g. acrylate -- Lubricating compositions whose base is a macromolecular polymer that incorporates monomers in which an unsaturated radical is directly attached to a carboxyl group, such as acrylate monomers. The unsaturated-carboxyl linkage provides reactive sites that can improve film-forming, adhesion and anti-wear performance of the lubricant.
[C10M 107/30] Macromolecular compounds obtained otherwise than by reactions only involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds -- This subclass covers lubricating base stocks that are macromolecular compounds formed by synthetic routes that do not rely solely on carbon-carbon unsaturated bond reactions. It includes polymers such as polyesters, polyalkylene glycols, polyurethanes and similar high-molecular-weight materials used as the primary lubricant base.
[C10M 107/32] Condensation polymers of aldehydes or ketones -- It covers lubricating compositions that use condensation polymers derived from aldehydes or ketones as the base material. These macromolecular compounds, such as polyacetals and related polymers, serve as the primary lubricating medium in the formulation.
[C10M 107/34] Polyoxyalkylenes -- The CPC class C10M 107/34 refers to lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular compound, specifically polyoxyalkylenes such as polyoxyethylene or polyoxypropylene polymers. These polymeric additives are used to modify viscosity, improve film-forming ability and enhance temperature stability in petroleum-based lubricants.
[C10M 107/36] Polysaccharides, e.g. cellulose -- C10M 107/36 refers to lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular polysaccharide such as cellulose, used primarily as a thickening or carrier agent in oils for petroleum, gas or coke applications. The subclass groups formulations that rely on these natural polymer backbones to provide lubrication, stability and film-forming properties.
[C10M 107/38] containing halogen -- Lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular compound and that incorporate halogen atoms, typically to enhance thermal stability, oxidation resistance and anti-wear performance. These formulations are used in petroleum-related applications where high-temperature operation and durability are required.
[C10M 107/40] containing nitrogen -- C10M 107/40 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a nitrogen-containing macromolecular compound, such as nitrogen-functionalised polymers used to improve load-bearing or anti-wear properties. These compositions fall within the broader category of petroleum-related lubricants.
[C10M 107/42] Macromolecular compounds obtained by reactions only involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds -- The entry covers polymeric base materials for lubricating compositions that are produced exclusively through reactions of carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds, such as olefin metathesis or addition polymerisation, resulting in macromolecular compounds without incorporating hetero-atoms or functional groups from other reactants.
[C10M 107/44] Macromolecular compounds obtained otherwise than by reactions only involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds -- The CPC subclass C10M 107/44 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular compound that is produced by synthesis routes other than reactions limited to carbon-to-carbon unsaturated (C═C) bonds, such as condensation, polymerisation involving heteroatoms, or other non-C═C mechanisms. This allows inclusion of polymers formed by alternative chemical pathways in lubricant formulations.
[C10M 107/46] containing sulfur -- A lubricating composition whose base material is a macromolecular (polymeric) substance and that includes sulfur as a constituent falls under CPC class C10M 107/46. The presence of sulfur is typically intended to enhance extreme-pressure or anti-wear properties of the polymer-based lubricant.
[C10M 107/48] containing phosphorus -- This subclass covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular compound and that contain phosphorus, typically as phosphates, phosphonates, or other organophosphorus additives. The phosphorus component is used to enhance anti-wear, extreme-pressure, or antioxidant performance.
[C10M 107/50] containing silicon -- This group covers lubricating compositions whose primary base is a macromolecular material that includes silicon, such as silicone polymers or organosilicon compounds, used to provide lubrication and protective properties in petroleum, gas or coke industry contexts.
[C10M 107/52] containing boron -- C10M 107/52 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a macromolecular compound and that contain boron, typically as boron-based additives for anti-wear, extreme-pressure or antioxidant functions in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10M 107/54] containing atoms of elements not provided for in groups -- This subclass covers lubricating compositions whose macromolecular base includes atoms of elements that are not listed in any other groups of the classification. It applies to formulations where such atypical elements are present in the polymeric base, distinguishing them from standard hydrocarbon-based lubricants.
[C10M 1/08] with additives -- Group C10M1/08 is no longer used for the classification of documents as of 2025-05-01 . The content of this group is being reclassified into groups C10M101/00 - C10M177/00 . Groups C10M1/08 and C10M101/00 - C10M177/00 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[C10M 109/00] Lubricating compositions characterised by the base-material being a compound of unknown or incompletely defined constitution -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubricating compositions comprising a base-material being a compound of unknown or incompletely defined constitution as single ingredient or a composition comprising this compound, e.g.
[C10M 109/02] Reaction products -- This subclass comprises lubricating compositions whose base material is a reaction product formed from one or more components of unknown or only partially defined chemical structure, often generated in situ or by processing. It includes mixtures where the active base is not a single identified compound but a product mixture resulting from such reactions.
[C10M 111/00] Lubrication compositions characterised by the base-material being a mixture of two or more compounds covered by more than one of the main groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixtures of essential ingredients as base materials. The ingredients are not covered by the same above-mentioned group.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mixtures of base oil and thickener -> C10M169/02 - Mixtures of base oil and additive -> C10M169/04
[C10M 111/02] at least one of them being a non-macromolecular organic compound -- C10M 111/02 covers lubricating compositions whose base material consists of a blend of two or more compounds drawn from different main groups, with at least one component being a low-molecular-weight organic compound. Such mixtures are used to provide tailored friction and wear properties in petroleum-related applications.
[C10M 111/04] at least one of them being a macromolecular organic compound -- C10M 111/04 covers lubrication compositions whose base-material is a blend of two or more compounds drawn from different main groups, with at least one component being a macromolecular organic compound. This subclass falls under lubricating compositions within petroleum-related chemistry.
[C10M 111/06] at least one of them being a compound of the type covered by group -- C10M 111/06 covers lubricating compositions whose base material is a blend of two or more compounds drawn from different main groups, with at least one component belonging to a specific group of compounds defined in the subclass. This subclass falls under lubricating compositions within the petroleum and gas industry sector of the CPC.
[C10M 113/00] Thickeners -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubricating compositions comprising a thickening agent being an inorganic material as single ingredient or a composition comprising an inorganic material, e.g.
[C10M 113/02] Carbon -- C10M 113/02 relates to lubricating compositions in which carbon is employed as a thickening agent. It falls under the chemistry category for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically addressing thickeners used to modify the viscosity and stability of lubricants.
[C10M 113/04] Sulfur -- Sulfur is used as a thickening agent in lubricating compositions for petroleum, gas or coke applications, providing viscosity enhancement and extreme-pressure protection. It may be employed in elemental or polymeric form to create a network that improves shear stability and load-bearing capacity of the lubricant.
[C10M 113/06] Metals
[C10M 113/08] Metal compounds -- C10M 113/08 covers metal compounds employed as thickeners in lubricating compositions, typically used in the petroleum, gas or coke sectors to modify viscosity and improve load-bearing performance. The class includes inorganic salts, oxides, hydroxides and organometallic complexes that function as viscosity-raising agents in oil-based lubricants.
[C10M 113/10] Clays -- C10M 113/10 refers to the use of natural or synthetic clays as thickening agents in lubricating compositions, particularly those employed in the petroleum, gas or coke sectors. The class covers formulations where clays impart desirable viscosity and shear-stability to the lubricant, improving its performance under high-temperature and high-load conditions.
[C10M 113/12] Silica -- Silica, an inorganic mineral, is used as a thickening agent in lubricating compositions for petroleum, gas and coke processing. It provides rheological stability, shear resistance and temperature tolerance, enhancing the load-carrying capacity of the lubricant.
[C10M 113/14] Glass -- The C10M 113/14 subclass covers glass used as a thickening agent in lubricating compositions, including glass particles, fibers or powders that modify viscosity and stability. It addresses the selection, preparation and incorporation of glass materials into greases, oils or other lubricants, typically for petroleum-based applications.
[C10M 113/16] Inorganic material treated with organic compounds, e.g. coated -- This CPC subclass covers lubricating compositions where the thickening agent is an inorganic material that has been treated with organic compounds, such as coated particles, to improve performance. It includes examples where inorganic powders like silica, alumina or metal oxides are surface-modified with organic compounds to act as thickeners in lubricants. The focus is on the coating process and resulting properties.

This category falls under chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries and deals with formulations where hydrocarbons serve as the main lubricant base with such alternative thickeners.
[C10M 115/04] containing oxygen -- C10M 115/04 covers lubricating compositions whose thickening agent is a low-molecular-weight organic compound that contains oxygen but is not a carboxylic acid or its salt. Typical thickeners include oxygen-bearing molecules such as esters, ethers, ketones or alcohol-derived compounds used to impart viscosity to the lubricant.
[C10M 115/06] containing halogen -- The C10M 115/06 subclass covers lubricating compositions whose thickening agent is a non-macromolecular organic compound other than a carboxylic acid or its salt, and which incorporate halogen atoms. Such formulations are used in petroleum-related applications where halogen-based thickeners provide specific performance or stability benefits.
[C10M 115/08] containing nitrogen -- C10M 115/08 covers lubricating compositions in which the thickener is a low-molecular organic compound that is not a macromolecule and is not a carboxylic acid or its salt, and that composition includes nitrogen. [C10M 115/10] containing sulfur -- C10M 115/10 covers lubricating compositions whose thickening agent is a low-molecular-weight organic compound that is not a carboxylic acid or its salt, and that incorporate sulfur within the formulation. [C10M 115/12] containing phosphorus -- C10M 115/12 covers lubricating compositions whose thickener is a non-macromolecular organic compound other than a carboxylic acid or its salt, and which include a phosphorus component. [C10M 117/06] having more than one carboxyl group bound to an acyclic carbon atom or cycloaliphatic carbon atom -- C10M 117/06 covers lubricating compositions whose thickener is a low-molecular-weight carboxylic acid or its salt that contains at least two carboxyl groups bound to a non-macromolecular acyclic or cycloaliphatic carbon atom. [C10M 117/08] having only one carboxyl group bound to a carbon atom of a six-membered aromatic ring -- This CPC subgroup covers lubricating compositions whose thickener is a low-molecular-weight carboxylic acid or its salt that contains exactly one -COOH group attached directly to a carbon of a six-membered aromatic ring, such as benzoic-type acids. [C10M 119/06] Macromolecular compounds obtained by reactions only involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds

[C10M 119/10] containing monomers having an unsaturated radical bound to an acyloxy radical of a saturated carboxylic or carbonic acid -- The subclass covers lubricating compositions whose thickener is a macromolecular polymer made from monomers that feature an unsaturated radical bound via an acyloxy group to a saturated carboxylic or carbonic acid unit. It classifies these alternative-synthesis polymers used to modify viscosity and film-forming properties in oils and greases.
[C10M 119/16] Condensation polymers of aldehydes or ketones -- C10M 119/16 covers lubricating compositions that use a macromolecular condensation polymer made from aldehydes or ketones as the thickening agent, providing increased viscosity and film strength in petroleum-based lubricants.
[C10M 119/18] Polyoxyalkylenes -- C10M 119/18 covers lubricating compositions whose thickening agent is a macromolecular polyoxyalkylene. The class is used for formulations in engines, gearboxes and industrial equipment.
[C10M 119/20] Polysaccharides, e.g. cellulose -- C10M 119/20 designates lubricating compositions that use a macromolecular polysaccharide, such as cellulose, as the thickening agent. [C10M 119/22] containing halogen -- This subclass includes lubricating oils or greases whose thickener is a macromolecular compound and that contain a halogen element such as fluorine, chlorine, bromine or iodine. [C10M 119/28] containing phosphorus -- C10M 119/28 pertains to lubricating compositions that employ a macromolecular thickener and incorporate phosphorus, typically as a phosphorus-containing additive or compound. [C10M 129/36] containing hydroxy groups -- C10M 129/36 covers lubricating compositions where the additive is a low-molecular-weight organic compound containing oxygen and at least one hydroxy group. Such additives improve lubricity, corrosion resistance, or anti-wear performance in petroleum-based or synthetic lubricants.
[C10M 129/38] having 8 or more carbon atoms -- C10M 129/38 covers lubricating compositions that contain an additive which is an organic, non-macromolecular compound with oxygen atoms and a carbon chain of eight or more atoms. The classification falls under lubricating compositions in the petroleum and gas industry.
[C10M 129/40] monocarboxylic -- C10M 129/40 covers lubricating compositions that contain an organic, non-macromolecular additive with oxygen atoms, specifically monocarboxylic compounds. These monocarboxylic additives are used to enhance the performance of lubricants in petroleum-related applications, such as reducing wear, friction, or corrosion.
[C10M 129/42] polycarboxylic -- The C10M 129/42 class covers lubricating compositions that contain polycarboxylic compounds-organic, non-macromolecular additives with oxygen atoms-used to enhance lubrication performance, reduce wear and improve oxidation resistance. It is situated within the hierarchy of chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries, lubricating compositions, and the subgroup for oxygen-containing organic additives.
[C10M 129/44] containing hydroxy groups -- C10M 129/44 covers lubricating compositions that employ an organic non-macromolecular additive containing oxygen, specifically additives that include hydroxy groups. These formulations are designed to reduce friction and wear in mechanical systems.
[C10M 129/46] cycloaliphatic -- C10M 129/46 covers lubricating compositions where the additive is an organic non-macromolecular compound containing oxygen, specifically cycloaliphatic substances. These additives are used to improve properties such as viscosity index or anti-wear performance in petroleum-based lubricants.
[C10M 129/48] having carboxyl groups bound to a carbon atom of a six-membered aromatic ring -- This subclass covers lubricating compositions that use an organic, non-macromolecular additive containing oxygen, where the additive features a carboxyl group directly attached to a carbon atom of a six-membered aromatic ring. The definition is used to classify formulations that rely on such aromatic carboxylic compounds for improving lubrication performance.

[C10M 129/56] Acids of unknown or incompletely defined constitution -- This subclass C10M 129/56 covers lubricating compositions that contain acidic additives whose chemical structure is unknown or only partially defined, such as complex reaction mixtures or poorly characterized organic acids. The acids are used as oxygen-containing, non-macromolecular components to modify friction, wear or corrosion properties of the lubricant.

[C10M 141/00] Lubricating compositions characterised by the additive being a mixture of two or more compounds covered by more than one of the main groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compositions comprising at least 2 different essential additives, each of the additives being in a different main group.
[C10M 141/02] at least one of them being an organic oxygen-containing compound -- C10M 141/02 covers lubricating compositions in which the additive is a blend of two or more compounds from different main groups, with at least one component being an organic compound containing oxygen. These formulations are used in petroleum, gas or coke industry contexts.
[C10M 141/04] at least one of them being an organic halogen-containing compound -- A lubricating composition that contains an additive mixture of two or more compounds belonging to different main groups, where at least one of the compounds is an organic halogen-containing substance. This falls under the CPC class C10M 141/04 for petroleum-related lubricants with mixed-group additives.
[C10M 141/06] at least one of them being an organic nitrogen-containing compound -- C10M 141/06 covers lubricating compositions that contain a blend of two or more additives belonging to different main groups, where at least one additive is an organic nitrogen-containing compound. This subclass falls under the CPC hierarchy for petroleum-based lubricants and specifies mixtures that combine nitrogen-based agents with other types of performance enhancers.
[C10M 141/08] at least one of them being an organic sulfur-, selenium- or tellurium-containing compound -- A lubricating composition in which the additive consists of a blend of two or more compounds drawn from different main groups, with at least one component being an organic compound that contains sulfur, selenium or tellurium. This falls under the CPC class C10M 141/08, within the chemistry sector for petroleum, gas or coke industry lubricants.
[C10M 141/10] at least one of them being an organic phosphorus-containing compound -- C10M 141/10 relates to lubricating compositions that include a mixture of two or more additives drawn from different main groups, with at least one additive being an organic phosphorus-containing compound. These blends are designed to enhance properties such as wear protection, extreme-pressure performance, or corrosion resistance in petroleum-based lubricants.
[C10M 141/12] at least one of them being an organic compound containing atoms of elements not provided for in groups -- This CPC subgroup covers lubricating compositions that use a blend of two or more additive compounds drawn from different main groups, where at least one additive is an organic compound that contains elements not listed in the standard groups for lubricating additives. It defines the formulation scope for such mixed-additive lubricants.
[C10M 143/00] Lubricating compositions characterised by the additive being a macromolecular hydrocarbon or such hydrocarbon modified by oxidation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubricating compositions comprising an additive being a macromolecular hydrocarbon or such hydrocarbon modified by oxidation, e.g.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Natural resin -> C10M159/02 - Natural waxes -> C10M159/06 - Natural rubber -> C10M159/10
[C10M 143/02] Polyethylene -- C10M 143/02 covers lubricating compositions that incorporate polyethylene as the polymeric hydrocarbon additive, either in its native macromolecular form or after oxidative modification. The polyethylene serves to improve viscosity, film-forming ability, and wear protection in oils and greases used in petroleum-related machinery. This subclass falls under lubricating compositions defined by macromolecular hydrocarbon additives.
[C10M 143/04] containing propene -- The CPC subclass C10M 143/04 refers to lubricating compositions whose additive is a macromolecular hydrocarbon, or a hydrocarbon that has been oxidatively modified, and that contains propene as part of the additive structure. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of chemistry → petroleum, gas or coke industries → lubricating compositions.
[C10M 143/06] containing butene -- C10M 143/06 refers to lubricating compositions that include a macromolecular hydrocarbon or oxidatively modified hydrocarbon additive and contain butene as a component. These compositions fall under the petroleum, gas or coke industries branch of chemistry. The classification is used by EPO and USPTO for patents related to such formulations.
[C10M 143/08] containing aliphatic monomer having more than 4 carbon atoms -- This subclass covers lubricating compositions that include a macromolecular hydrocarbon or an oxidatively modified hydrocarbon as the additive, where the polymer is built from an aliphatic monomer containing more than four carbon atoms. Such formulations are used in petroleum, gas or coke industry lubricants.
[C10M 143/10] containing aromatic monomer, e.g. styrene -- C10M 143/10 covers lubricating compositions whose additive is a macromolecular hydrocarbon or an oxidatively modified hydrocarbon that includes an aromatic monomer such as styrene. The classification is used for formulations in the petroleum, gas or coke sectors where the aromatic component is intentionally incorporated to modify performance characteristics.
[C10M 143/12] containing conjugated diene -- C10M 143/12 covers lubricating compositions that employ a macromolecular hydrocarbon or an oxidatively modified hydrocarbon as an additive, specifically when the additive contains conjugated diene structures. These formulations are used in petroleum, gas or coke industry lubricants.
[C10M 143/14] containing non-conjugated diene -- Lubricating compositions that incorporate a macromolecular hydrocarbon additive, or that hydrocarbon after oxidation, are classified under C10M 143/14. The additive must contain non-conjugated diene structures within its molecular framework.
[C10M 143/16] containing cycloaliphatic monomer -- A lubricating composition that includes a cycloaliphatic monomer as part of its formulation, where the additive is a macromolecular hydrocarbon or a hydrocarbon that has been modified by oxidation. This falls under the CPC class C10M 143/16, relating to petroleum-derived lubricants with polymeric or oxidised hydrocarbon additives.
[C10M 143/18] Oxidised hydrocarbons, i.e. oxidised subsequent to macromolecular formation -- C10M 143/18 covers lubricating compositions that use macromolecular hydrocarbons, or those same polymers after they have been oxidised, as performance additives. The oxidation occurs after the polymer has formed, changing the oil's viscosity, extreme-pressure or anti-wear properties. This subclass falls under chemistry to petroleum, gas or coke industries to lubricating compositions.
[C10M 145/00] Lubricating compositions characterised by the additive being a macromolecular compound containing oxygen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lubricating compositions comprising an additive being a macromolecular compound containing oxygen, e.g.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - PIB-substituted succinic acid/anhydride -> C10M129/93 - PIB-substituted succinate -> C10M129/95 - Oxidised hydrocarbons -> C10M143/18
[C10M 145/02] Macromolecular compounds obtained by reactions only involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds -- C10M 145/02 refers to macromolecular compounds that are synthesized exclusively through reactions involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds, such as polymerizations of alkenes or dienes. These oxygen-containing polymers are used as additives in lubricating compositions for improving performance in petroleum-based applications.
[C10M 145/04] containing monomers having an unsaturated radical bound to an alcohol, aldehyde, ketonic, ether, ketal or acetal radical -- C10M 145/04 covers lubricating compositions whose additives are macromolecular oxygen-containing compounds formed from monomers that possess an unsaturated radical linked to groups such as alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, ethers, ketals or acetals. These additives improve film strength and wear protection in oil-based lubricants.
[C10M 145/06] containing monomers having an unsaturated radical bound to an acyloxy radical of a saturated carboxylic or carbonic acid -- C10M 145/06 covers lubricating compositions where the additive is a macromolecular compound that includes monomers with an unsaturated radical bound to an acyloxy group derived from a saturated carboxylic or carbonic acid.
[C10M 145/08] Vinyl esters of a saturated carboxylic or carbonic acid -- The subclass covers lubricating formulations that incorporate vinyl ester polymers formed from saturated carboxylic or carbonic acids, which act as oxygen-containing macromolecular additives. These additives are used to enhance the lubricity, load-bearing capacity and anti-wear performance of the composition.
[C10M 145/10] containing monomers having an unsaturated radical bound to a carboxyl radical, e.g. acrylate -- This subclass covers lubricating compositions that include as additive a polymeric oxygen-containing compound formed from monomers whose unsaturated radical is linked to a carboxyl group, for example acrylate-based polymers. Such additives provide specific performance characteristics in petroleum-based lubricants.
[C10M 145/12] monocarboxylic -- The CPC subclass C10M 145/12 covers lubricating compositions that contain a macromolecular oxygen-bearing additive, specifically those in which the additive is a monocarboxylic compound. These formulations are used in the petroleum and gas sectors to improve lubrication performance by incorporating polymeric acids that provide film-forming and anti-wear properties.
[C10M 145/14] Acrylate -- C10M 145/14 covers lubricating compositions that contain a macromolecular additive with oxygen, specifically acrylate-based polymers. These acrylate compounds are used as thickening or functional additives to improve viscosity, film strength and wear protection in oils for petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10M 145/16] polycarboxylic -- A lubricating composition in which the functional additive is an oxygen-containing macromolecular compound, specifically a polycarboxylic polymer, used to modify viscosity, reduce wear and improve film-forming properties in oil-based lubricants. This falls under chemistry → petroleum, gas or coke industries → lubricating compositions.
[C10M 145/18] Macromolecular compounds obtained otherwise than by reactions only involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds -- C10M 145/18 covers lubricating compositions that use macromolecular oxygen-containing additives produced by synthetic routes other than reactions limited to carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds. The subclass includes polymers, oligomers or other large molecules prepared through cross-linking, condensation or similar chemistries that add oxygen functions, used to improve viscosity, film formation or extreme-pressure performance in oil-based lubricants.
[C10M 145/20] Condensation polymers of aldehydes or ketones
[C10M 145/22] Polyesters -- C10M 145/22 - Polyesters covers lubricating compositions where the functional additive is a macromolecular polyester containing oxygen, used to improve viscosity, film formation or wear resistance. These polyesters are employed in petroleum, gas or coke related lubricants.
[C10M 145/24] Polyethers -- This subclass covers lubricating compositions that contain polyether macromolecular compounds as oxygen-bearing additives. Polyethers are polymeric ethers used to modify viscosity, reduce friction and improve wear protection in oils for petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10M 145/26] Polyoxyalkylenes -- Polyoxyalkylenes are macromolecular oxygen-containing compounds used as additives in lubricating compositions, belonging to the CPC subclass for petroleum and gas industry lubricants. They improve viscosity, film strength and oxidative stability of oils. The code C10M 145/26 covers such polyether structures.
[C10M 145/28] of alkylene oxides containing 2 carbon atoms only -- This CPC subclass covers lubricating compositions that use a macromolecular additive containing oxygen, specifically polymers derived from alkylene oxides limited to two-carbon units such as ethylene oxide. The classification applies to formulations where these oxygen-containing macromolecules are the primary performance-enhancing component.
[C10M 145/30] of alkylene oxides containing 3 carbon atoms only -- The subclass C10M 145/30 covers lubricating compositions that incorporate macromolecular oxygen-containing additives formed from alkylene oxides limited to three-carbon units, such as propylene-oxide-based polymers. These additives are used to modify viscosity, improve film strength and provide oxidative stability in petroleum-derived lubricants.
[C10M 145/32] of alkylene oxides containing 4 or more carbon atoms -- C10M 145/32 covers lubricating compositions that use a macromolecular oxygen-containing additive derived from alkylene oxides with four or more carbon atoms. These macromolecules are employed to improve viscosity, film strength and wear protection in petroleum-based lubricants.
[C10M 145/34] of two or more specified different types -- The subclass C10M 145/34 covers lubricating compositions in which the additive is an oxygen-containing macromolecular compound and the formulation contains two or more different specified types of such macromolecular additives, typically used in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10M 145/36] etherified -- C10M 145/36 relates to lubricating compositions that incorporate a macromolecular additive containing oxygen which has been etherified. Such etherified polymers are employed to enhance viscosity, film strength and oxidative stability of petroleum-based lubricants.
[C10M 145/38] esterified -- Lubricating compositions that contain a macromolecular additive with oxygen atoms which has been modified by esterification. The esterified additive improves viscosity, film strength and load-carrying capacity of the lubricant, especially for petroleum-based applications.
[C10M 145/40] Polysaccharides, e.g. cellulose -- C10M 145/40 covers lubricating compositions in which the additive is a macromolecular, oxygen-containing compound, specifically polysaccharides such as cellulose. These polysaccharide additives are used to enhance the lubrication performance of petroleum-based fluids.

[C10M 149/22] Polyamines -- This subclass covers lubricating oils and greases that contain polyamine polymers as nitrogen-bearing macromolecular additives. [C10M 157/04] at least one of them being a nitrogen-containing compound -- C10M 157/04 covers lubricating compositions that contain a mixture of two or more macromolecular additives drawn from different main groups, with the requirement that at least one of the macromolecules includes nitrogen in its structure. [C10M 157/10] at least one of them being a compound containing atoms of elements not provided for in groups -- A lubricating composition whose additive is a blend of two or more macromolecular compounds that belong to different main groups, with at least one component containing atoms of elements not listed in the standard groups. [C10M 159/005] Macromolecular compounds, e.g. macromolecular compounds composed of alternatively specified monomers not covered by the same main group -- The code C10M 159/005 covers lubricating compositions that contain macromolecular compounds formed from alternating monomers whose structures are not fully defined or do not belong to the same main chemical group. These macromolecules act as additives of unknown or incompletely specified constitution, enhancing lubrication performance.
[C10M 159/02] Natural products -- The C10M 159/02 subclass covers lubricating compositions that contain natural-product additives whose chemical constitution is unknown or only partially defined. [C10M 159/04] Petroleum fractions, e.g. tars, solvents -- A lubricating composition whose additive has an unknown or only partially defined chemical structure and contains petroleum-derived fractions such as tars or solvents. [C10M 159/06] Waxes, e.g. ozocerite, ceresine, petrolatum, slack-wax -- C10M 159/06 classifies lubricating compositions that contain waxes of unknown or partially defined structure, such as ozocerite, ceresine, petrolatum or slack-wax, used as additives in petroleum based lubricants. [C10M 159/10] Rubber -- C10M 159/10 classifies lubricating compositions that contain rubber as an additive of unknown or incompletely defined constitution. The rubber serves to modify viscosity, improve film strength, or provide protective properties in petroleum-based lubricants for engines, gearboxes and other machinery.
[C10M 159/12] Reaction products -- The class covers lubricating compositions where the additive is a reaction product whose exact chemical constitution is unknown or only partially defined. It includes mixtures formed by reacting two or more components to create a lubricating additive of indeterminate composition, primarily used in petroleum, gas or coke industry applications.
[C10M 159/123] obtained by phosphorus or phosphorus-containing compounds, e.g. P x S x with organic compounds -- C10M 159/123 covers lubricating compositions that include a phosphorus-based additive of undefined or partially known structure, such as phosphorus sulfide compounds combined with organic substances.
