[C10N 2040/42] Flashing oils or marking oils -- C10N 2040/42 covers lubricating compositions specifically formulated as flashing or marking oils, which are applied to metal surfaces to provide a visible indication of contact or movement, often used in the petroleum, gas or coke industries for pipeline inspection, valve positioning and equipment alignment. These oils contain additives that produce a colored or luminescent trace that remains on the substrate for easy detection.
[C10N 2040/44] Super vacuum or supercritical use -- C10N 2040/44 classifies lubricating compositions that are formulated for operation under super-vacuum or supercritical conditions, such as those encountered in advanced petroleum, gas or coke processing where extreme pressure and temperature exist. The entry falls under chemistry and the petroleum-related indexing scheme, indicating the intended application rather than a specific chemical structure.
[C10N 2040/46] Textile oils -- C10N 2040/46 - Textile oils covers lubricating compositions specifically formulated for textile processing applications such as spinning, weaving and finishing, where the oil provides lubrication, heat transfer and protection against wear.
[C10N 2040/48] Slushing oils -- C10N 2040/48 designates slushing oils, a type of lubricating composition formulated to protect, clean and seal equipment such as pumps, compressors and pipelines during start-up, shutdown or intermittent operation. The entry falls under chemistry, petroleum, gas or coke industries and specifies the intended use of the lubricating product.
[C10N 2040/50] Medical uses -- C10N 2040/50 classifies lubricating compositions that are specifically formulated for medical applications, such as for use in medical devices, implants or procedures. It is part of the chemistry section covering petroleum, gas or coke industry products and indicates the intended medical use of the lubricant.
[C10N 2050/00] Form in which the lubricant is applied to the material being lubricated -- C10N 2050/00 covers the physical form in which a lubricant is introduced onto a material, such as liquid, spray, paste, foam or solid coating, and related details of its application method. It is part of the CPC group for lubricants within the chemistry area for petroleum, gas or coke industries, indexing how the lubricant is presented to the surface being lubricated.
[C10N 2050/01] Emulsions, colloids, or micelles -- C10N 2050/01 pertains to lubricants that are applied in the form of emulsions, colloidal dispersions or micelles, especially for use in petroleum, gas or coke processing. The subclass includes compositions and methods where the lubricant is delivered as a dispersed phase to enhance film formation, stability and performance on the material being lubricated.
[C10N 2050/011] Oil-in-water -- C10N 2050/011 refers to oil-in-water lubricant application. It falls under petroleum and gas industries. This code is used for classification purposes.
[C10N 2050/013] Water-in-oil -- The C10N 2050/013 subclass covers lubricating compositions where water is dispersed in oil, forming water-in-oil emulsions applied to a surface to reduce friction and wear. It includes formulations that incorporate water as the dispersed phase within an oil carrier to achieve specific lubrication or cooling properties.
[C10N 2050/015] Dispersions of solid lubricants -- C10N 2050/015 refers to dispersions of solid lubricants. This classification is used for lubricants applied in a specific form. It falls under petroleum and gas industries.
[C10N 2050/02] dissolved or suspended in a carrier which subsequently evaporates to leave a lubricant coating -- C10N 2050/02 refers to lubricants dissolved or suspended in a carrier. The carrier evaporates, leaving a lubricant coating. This is a subclass of petroleum and gas industries.
[C10N 2050/023] Multi-layer lubricant coatings -- C10N 2050/023 covers multi-layer lubricant coatings, describing the form and method by which a lubricant is applied as successive layers onto a material to reduce friction, wear or corrosion, often used in petroleum-related equipment and processes. It falls under the chemistry field within the petroleum, gas or coke industries and specifies how the coating is built up rather than its chemical composition.
[C10N 2050/025] in the form of films or sheets -- C10N 2050/025 refers to lubricants applied in the form of films or sheets. This classification is used in the petroleum and gas industries. It is a specific category within the chemistry section.
[C10N 2050/04] Aerosols -- C10N 2050/04 covers lubricants that are provided as aerosols, meaning the lubricant is dispensed as a spray or mist onto the material being lubricated. It includes pressurised containers that atomise oil or grease for quick, even application, commonly used in petroleum-related equipment and processes.
[C10N 2050/06] Gaseous phase, at least during working conditions -- This CPC subclass C10N 2050/06 concerns lubricants that are applied in a gaseous form, such as vapors or aerosols, at least while the equipment is in operation. It falls under chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries and addresses the method of delivering the lubricant in the gas phase during working conditions.
[C10N 2050/08] Solids -- C10N 2050/08 refers to solids as a form of lubricant application. It is a subclass in the Cooperative Patent Classification system. This code falls under chemistry and petroleum industries.
[C10N 2050/10] semi-solid -- Group C10N2050/10 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups C10M5/00 and C10M7/00 . Groups C10M5/00 , C10M7/00 and C10N2050/10 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[C10N 2050/12] Micro capsules -- C10N 2050/12 covers the use of micro-capsules as a form for applying lubricants to a material. The classification relates to petroleum, gas or coke processes where the lubricant is encapsulated in microscopic shells that rupture or dissolve to release the oil at the contact surface, providing controlled lubrication and reduced contamination.
[C10N 2050/14] Composite materials or sliding materials in which lubricants are integrally molded -- C10N 2050/14 covers composite or sliding materials in which a lubricant is integrally molded into the structure, creating a self-lubricating component. It falls under the chemistry field, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries, and is part of an indexing scheme for lubricant-application forms.
[C10N 2060/00] Chemical after-treatment of the constituents of the lubricating composition -- C10N 2060/00 covers chemical after-treatment processes applied to the components of lubricating compositions, such as oils, greases or additives, to modify their properties, remove impurities or enhance performance. The methods include reactions, neutralisation, or other chemical modifications performed after the base lubricant has been formulated.
[C10N 2060/01] by organic hydroxy group containing compounds -- C10N 2060/01 covers the chemical after-treatment of lubricating-oil constituents using compounds that contain an organic hydroxy group. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically addressing post-production modifications of lubricants with hydroxy-functional additives.
[C10N 2060/02] Reduction, e.g. hydrogenation -- C10N 2060/02 refers to processes that reduce or hydrogenate constituents of a lubricating composition after its initial preparation. The reduction modifies the chemical structure of oil additives or base stocks to improve stability, viscosity or performance, and is classified under chemical after-treatment of lubricating compositions in the petroleum and gas industry sector.
[C10N 2060/04] Oxidation, e.g. ozonisation -- C10N 2060/04 covers methods for chemically treating lubricating compositions after formulation, specifically by oxidising their components such as through ozonisation to enhance performance or remove undesirable substances. This falls within the petroleum, gas or coke industries branch of chemistry.
[C10N 2060/06] by epoxydes -- C10N 2060/06 refers to chemical after-treatment of lubricating composition constituents by epoxydes. This code is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system. It falls under petroleum and gas industries.
[C10N 2060/08] Halogenation -- The CPC subclass C10N 2060/08 covers halogenation of the chemical constituents of a lubricating composition, a post-treatment step applied to base oils, additives or finished lubricants to incorporate fluorine, chlorine, bromine or iodine. This modification aims to enhance properties such as oxidation resistance, extreme-pressure performance and thermal stability in petroleum-based or synthetic lubricants.
[C10N 2060/09] Treatment with nitrogen containing compounds -- This subclass concerns processes that apply nitrogen-containing compounds to a lubricating composition after it has been formulated, altering its properties such as wear protection, oxidation resistance or viscosity stability. It falls under chemistry for petroleum, gas or coke industries, specifically the chemical after-treatment of lubricating-oil constituents, and is indexed as C10N 2060/09.
[C10N 2060/10] by sulfur or a compound containing sulfur -- This subclass covers methods that treat or modify components of lubricating oils using sulfur or sulfur-containing compounds, typically to improve properties such as oxidative stability or anti-wear performance.
[C10N 2060/12] by phosphorus or a compound containing phosphorus, e.g. P -- This classification code refers to chemical after-treatment of lubricating composition constituents using phosphorus or a phosphorus compound. It falls under petroleum and gas industries in the chemistry category. The code is C10N 2060/12.
[C10N 2060/14] by boron or a compound containing boron -- C10N 2060/14 refers to chemical after-treatment of lubricating composition constituents by boron or a compound containing boron. This code is used in the Cooperative Patent Classification system. It falls under petroleum and gas industries.
[C10N 2070/00] Specific manufacturing methods for lubricant compositions -- This CPC subclass C10N 2070/00 covers particular processes for producing lubricant compositions, including blending of base oils with additives, refining steps and post-treatment methods that improve performance characteristics. It falls under the chemistry area, specifically the petroleum, gas or coke industries.
[C10N 2070/02] Concentrating of additives -- C10N 2070/02 refers to methods of concentrating additives in lubricant compositions. This classification is used in the petroleum and gas industries. It involves specific manufacturing processes.
[C10N 2080/00] Special pretreatment of the material to be lubricated, e.g. phosphatising or chromatising of a metal -- This subclass covers pretreatment steps applied to a material before lubrication, such as phosphating or chromating metal surfaces to enhance lubricity and protect against wear. It is part of the chemistry branch related to petroleum, gas or coke industries and deals with methods that modify the surface chemistry prior to applying lubricants.
[C11] ANIMAL OR VEGETABLE OILS, FATS, FATTY SUBSTANCES OR WAXES -- C11 covers the production, processing, and use of oils, fats, fatty substances and waxes derived from animal or vegetable sources. It includes methods for extraction, refining, hydrogenation, fractionation and applications such as lubricants, cosmetics, food, and biopolymers. The class also addresses compositions and additives related to these materials.
[C11B] PRODUCING, e.g. BY PRESSING RAW MATERIALS OR BY EXTRACTION FROM WASTE MATERIALS, REFINING OR PRESERVING FATS, FATTY SUBSTANCES, e.g. LANOLIN, FATTY OILS OR WAXES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Drying oils -> C09F  **Glossary:** - Essential oil: A concentrated, hydrophobic liquid containing volatile aromatic compounds from plants. - Esterification: Reaction between acid and alcohol resulting in ester bond. - Fat: Esters of glycerol and fatty acids, generally in solid form. - Fatty acid: A carboxylic acid consisting of a hydrocarbon chain, which may have unsaturations and a terminal carboxyl group. - Hydrogenation: Chemical process that adds hydrogen atoms to a molecule. - Isomerizing: Process by which one molecule is transformed into another molecule which has exactly the same atoms. - Lipid: Organic compounds based on fatty acids, generally hydrophobic. - Oil: Fat which is in liquid form. - Wax: Any of a group of substances composed of hydrophobic hydrocarbons, alcohols, fatty acids, and esters that are solid at ambient temperatures.
[C11B 1/00] Production of fats or fatty oils from raw materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating fats/oils from source.
[C11B 1/02] Pretreatment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for facilitating separation of fats/oils from sources.
[C11B 1/025] by enzymes or microorganisms, living or dead -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pretreatment with objective of facilitating separation essentially when enzyme mediated, either via direct addition or indirectly via microbes.
[C11B 1/04] of vegetable raw material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating from fats/oils sources which have cell walls.
[C11B 1/06] by pressing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Facilitating separation using extrusion, rollers, expressers, etc.
[C11B 1/08] by hot pressing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Facilitating separation by raising temperature or not cooling.
[C11B 1/10] by extracting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating from fats/oil sources using extractants (typically fluids)
[C11B 11/00] Recovery or refining of other fatty substances, e.g. lanolin or waxes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Specific cases of recovery and refining of non-glyceride material containing fatty acids.
[C11B 11/005] Lanolin; Woolfat -- Lanolin and woolfat are classified under recovery or refining of other fatty substances. This category falls under producing, refining, or preserving fats and fatty substances. The code for this is C11B 11/005.
[C11B 1/102] in counter-current; utilisation of an equipment wherein the material is conveyed by a screw -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating from fats/oil sources when source and extractant move in opposite directions and use of extruders in combination with added extractant.
[C11B 1/104] using super critical gases or vapours -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating from fats/oil sources with the aid of super- of near -critical fluids
[C11B 1/106] using ultra-sounds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating from fats/oil sources with the aid of sound above 20kHz.
[C11B 1/108] after-treatment, e.g. of miscellae -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All post separation treatment of extraction media.
[C11B 1/12] by melting out -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating from fats/oil sources with direct application of heat.
[C11B 1/14] with hot water or aqueous solutions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating from fats/oil sources with application of heat via water or solutions.
[C11B 1/16] with steam -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating from fats/oil sources with application of heat via gaseous water.
[C11B 13/00] Recovery of fats, fatty oils or fatty acids from waste materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separating greasy materials, e.g. trap grease, yellow grease or used cooking oil from refuse, garbage or rubbish.
[C11B 13/005] of residues of the fabrication of wood-cellulose (in particular tall-oil) -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery of fats, fatty oils or fatty acidsobtained as by-products of wood-cellulose process, e.g. rosin.
[C11B 13/02] from soap stock -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery or refining of fats/oils from aqueous caustic refining by-product.  **Glossary:** - soap stock: a by-product of vegetable oil refinement, containing lipids, fatty oils and salts.
[C11B 13/04] from spent adsorption materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery of fats, fatty oils or fatty acids from used adhered substances, e.g from filter cake, bleaching earths.
[C11B 15/00] Solidifying fatty oils, fats, or waxes by physical processes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Coagulating fatty oils, fats or waxes by non-chemical processes, e.g. prilling, coating.
[C11B 3/00] Refining fats or fatty oils -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Undefined processes for removing undesired material from fats/oils.
[C11B 3/001] by a combination of two or more of the means hereafter -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where a combination of processes is required.
[C11B 3/003] by enzymes or microorganisms, living or dead -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process is essentially enzyme mediated, either via direct addition or indirectly via microbes.
[C11B 3/005] by wave energy or electric current, e.g. electrodialysis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process is based energy which has a frequency, e.g. sound or radiation.
[C11B 3/006] by extraction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process uses fluids.
[C11B 3/008] by filtration, e.g. including ultra filtration, dialysis  C11B3/005  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process depends on size retention.
[C11B 3/02] by chemical reaction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process depends chemical change.
[C11B 3/04] with acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process depends chemical change influenced by acidic material or pH<7.
[C11B 3/06] with bases -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process depends chemical change influenced by alkaline material or pH>7.
[C11B 3/08] with oxidising agents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process depends chemical change influenced by reactions involving removal of electrons, e.g. peroxy compounds or hypochlorites.
[C11B 3/10] by adsorption -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process depends physico-chemical attraction to solids, e.g. zeolites.
[C11B 3/12] by distillation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process depends on change to gaseous phase, e.g. short path or wiped film.
[C11B 3/14] with the use of indifferent gases or vapours, e.g. steam -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process depends change to gaseous phase using steam as boiling point depressant.
[C11B 3/16] by mechanical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fats/oil where process depends on purely physical forces, e.g. gravity, decantation or centrifugation.
[C11B 5/00] Preserving by using additives, e.g. anti-oxidants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Prevention of deterioration of oils/fats by addition of substances.
[C11B 5/0007] Organic substances -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Prevention of deterioration of oils/fats by addition of carbon containing substances generally.
[C11B 5/0014] Hydrocarbons -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Prevention of deterioration of oils/fats by addition of substances having only C and H constituents.
[C11B 5/0021] containing oxygen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Prevention of deterioration of oils/fats by addition of O containing substances, generally organic.
[C11B 5/0028] Carboxylic acids; Their derivates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Prevention of deterioration of oils/fats by addition of substances containing COOH groups or anions, esters or salts thereof, e.g. acetate or fatty acids.
[C11B 5/0035] Phenols; Their halogenated and aminated derivates, their salts, their esters with carboxylic acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Prevention of deterioration of oils/fats by addition of substances containing hydroxy group (including deprotonated) attached to aromatic ring structure , e.g. quinones, tocols or polyphenols.
[C11B 5/0042] containing nitrogen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Prevention of deterioration of oils/fats by addition of nitrogen containing substances, e.g. N 2 , peptides or amino acids.
[C11B 5/005] Amines or imines  C11B5/0035  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Prevention of deterioration of oils/fats by addition of substances with a free amine or imine groups.
[C11B 5/0057] Amides or imides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Prevention of deterioration of oils/fats by addition of substances with free amide or imide group.
[C11B 5/0064] Heterocyclic compounds containing nitrogen in the ring -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. quinolines or pyroles.
[C11B 5/0071] containing halogens, sulfur or phosphorus  halogenated phenols  C11B5/0035 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. chloro, thio or methio compounds.
[C11B 5/0078] Metal hydrides or organo-metallic compounds, i.e. organic compounds containing a metal-to-carbon link -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. butyltin, ferrocene or silica hydrides.
[C11B 5/0085] Substances of natural origin of unknown constitution, f.i. plant extracts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. rosemary, sage, garlic or tea.
[C11B 5/0092] Mixtures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Prevention of deterioration of oils/fats by addition of known mixtures of substances.
[C11B 7/00] Separation of mixtures of fats or fatty oils into their constituents, e.g. saturated oils from unsaturated oils -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separation where the process is undefined.
[C11B 7/0008] by differences of solubilities, e.g. by extraction, by separation from a solution by means of anti-solvents  addition of a minor amount of solvent intended to lower the melting point  C11B7/0083 ,  C11B7/0091 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separation of oils/fats where the process uses fluids and differential solubilities/miscibilities.
[C11B 7/0016] in hydrocarbons or halogenated hydrocarbons  in pressure liquefied gases  C11B7/005 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. hexanes.
[C11B 7/0025] in solvents containing oxygen in their molecule -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. acetone, carboyxlic acid derivatives or alcohols.
[C11B 7/0033] in solvents containing other heteroatoms in their molecule -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. nitropropane or sulfoxides.
[C11B 7/0041] in mixtures of individualized solvents (water is not taken into account) -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. blends or mixtures.
[C11B 7/005] in solvents used at superatmospheric pressures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. near or super-critical fluids.
[C11B 7/0058] in solvents or mixtures of solvents of different natures or compositions used in succession -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. solvent gradient systems or gas liquid mixtures.
[C11B 7/0066] between two or more non-miscible solvent phases -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g bi- or tri-phasic systems.
[C11B 7/0075] by differences of melting or solidifying points -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separation of oils/fats where the process uses heating or cooling and exploits, e.g. winterizing or crystallizing.
[C11B 7/0083] with addition of auxiliary substances, e.g. cristallisation promotors, filter aids, melting point depressors,  if a characterised solution is formed  C11B7/0008 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separation of oils/fats where the process uses separation additives, e.g. urea, chelation, salts or complexation.
[C11B 7/0091] non-solvents, e.g. water wherein the fat or oil is dispersed account a more washing out of fractions is not taken into -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. wetting agents or pressure.
[C11B 9/00] Essential oils -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Perfume compositions otherwise not falling under the subclasses C11B9/0003 - C11B9/0096 ; Essential oil compositions; testing methods for perfumes which are not characterized by the equipment used, e.g. sniffer tests.
[C11B 9/0003] Compounds of unspecified constitution defined by the chemical reaction for their preparation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Reaction products of fragrant compounds with further chemical compounds, adapted to release the fragrant compound when later activated by e.g. heat.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Essential oils -> C11B9/00 , - Protected perfumes in detergent compositions -> C11D3/502 - Perfume compounds in detergent compositions which generate a fragrance upon thermal or chemical activation -> C11D3/507  **Glossary:** - Pro-perfume: Fragrant compound obtained by reacting a fragrance-generating precursor compound, typically an aldehyde or ketone, with at least one additional compound
[C11B 9/0007] Aliphatic compounds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fragrant compounds with no rings.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Alicyclic ring compounds; Aromatic ring compounds; Heterocyclic ring compounds -> C11B9/0026 - C11B9/0069
[C11B 9/0011] containing S -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Aliphatic sulfur compounds, e.g. mercaptans or thiols.
[C11B 9/0015] containing oxygen as the only heteroatom -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Aliphatic compounds containing an oxygen atom, e.g. alcohols, ethers, aldehydes or ketones.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Carboxylic acids; Salts or esters thereof -> C11B9/0019 - Aliphatic aminoalcohols -> C11B9/0023
[C11B 9/0019] carbocylic acids; Salts or esters thereof -- C11B 9/0019 covers carbocyclic acids, which are organic acids whose carbon atoms form a ring structure, together with their salts and ester derivatives. These compounds are classified under the broader group dealing with the processing of animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes, particularly where essential oils are involved. The entry is used to identify inventions related to the preparation, modification or use of such cyclic acid compounds.
[C11B 9/0023] containing nitrogen as the only heteroatom -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Aliphatic compounds containing nitrogen atoms, e.g. amines or aminoalcohols.
[C11B 9/0026] compounds containing an alicyclic ring not condensed with another ring -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Aliphatic ring compounds, saturated or unsaturated, optionally with substituents bonded to the ring, these substituents may include heteroatoms, e.g. halogen atoms, hydroxy groups or ether linkages.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Condensed rings; Spiro compounds; Aromatic ring compounds; Heterocyclic ring compounds -> C11B9/0042 - C11B9/0069
[C11B 9/003] the ring containing less than six carbon atoms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compounds containing cyclopentane, cyclopentene, cyclobutane, cyclobutene or cyclopropane rings.
[C11B 9/0034] the ring containing six carbon atoms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cyclohexane or cyclohexene compounds.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Compounds with a benzene ring -> C11B9/0061
[C11B 9/0038] the ring containing more than six carbon atoms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cycloheptane; Cycloheptene or larger ring compounds.
[C11B 9/0042] compounds containing condensed hydrocarbon rings -- C11B 9/0042 covers compounds that contain fused or condensed hydrocarbon ring structures, generally derived from or employed in the processing of animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances and waxes. It includes polycyclic aromatic or similar ring systems used as carriers, solvents or functional additives in the production, refining or preservation of essential oils such as lanolin, fatty oils or waxes.
[C11B 9/0046] containing only two condensed rings -- A CPC subclass C11B 9/0046 designates essential-oil products whose constituents consist exclusively of molecules with two fused (condensed) rings, such as certain phenanthrene or naphthyl derivatives. It falls under the broader chemistry group for animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes and the sub-group concerned with producing, refining or preserving those oils. This code is used for patent filings that describe the preparation or use of such two-ring essential-oil compositions.
[C11B 9/0049] the condensed rings sharing two common C atoms -- C11B 9/0049 pertains to essential-oil constituents that contain condensed ring systems in which two rings share a pair of carbon atoms, forming fused bicyclic or polycyclic structures such as naphthalene-type cores. The subclass addresses the chemistry of these shared-carbon ring frameworks as they arise in the production, refinement or preservation of animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances and waxes.
[C11B 9/0053] both rings being six-membered -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Naphthalene compounds.
[C11B 9/0057] Spiro compounds -- The CPC subclass C11B 9/0057 includes spirocyclic compounds that occur in essential oils derived from animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes, and which are relevant to methods of producing, refining or preserving these materials.
[C11B 9/0061] compounds containing a six-membered aromatic ring not condensed with another ring -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Spiro compounds containing a heterocyclic ring system which has only O or S as ring heteroatoms -> C11B9/0088
[C11B 9/0065] Nitriles -- C11B 9/0065 classifies the use of nitrile compounds in methods for producing, refining or preserving essential oils obtained from animal or vegetable fats, fatty substances or waxes. It covers processes such as pressing, extraction from waste material, and related chemical treatments where nitriles serve as reagents or intermediates.
[C11B 9/0069] Heterocyclic compounds -- C11B 9/0069 covers heterocyclic compounds that are components of, or added to, essential oils derived from animal or vegetable fats, waxes or oils. The subclass includes such compounds when they are used in the preparation, refining, preservation or modification of these oil-based products.
[C11B 9/0073] containing only O or S as heteroatoms -- This subclass pertains to essential oils derived from animal or vegetable fats, waxes or oils in which the only heteroatoms present in the constituent molecules are oxygen or sulfur. It covers processes for producing, refining or preserving such oils and their compositions where no other heteroatoms such as nitrogen or halogens occur.
[C11B 9/0076] the hetero rings containing less than six atoms -- C11B 9/0076 pertains to essential oils that contain heterocyclic rings with fewer than six atoms, such as five-membered oxygen, nitrogen or sulfur rings. The subclass addresses methods for producing, extracting, refining or preserving these oils and related fatty substances, including lanolin, fatty oils and waxes derived from animal or vegetable sources. It falls under the broader category of chemical processes for fats, oils and waxes.
[C11B 9/008] the hetero rings containing six atoms -- C11B 9/008 refers to essential oils with hetero rings containing six atoms. This code is used in the Cooperative Patent Classification system. It falls under chemistry and animal or vegetable oils.
[C11B 9/0084] the hetero rings containing more than six atoms -- This subclass covers essential oil compositions that contain hetero-ring structures larger than six-membered rings, where the ring includes at least one hetero atom such as nitrogen, oxygen or sulfur. It includes processes and products where such large heterocyclic constituents are obtained, refined or used in the oil.
[C11B 9/0088] Spiro compounds -- Spiro compounds are a class of bicyclic organic molecules where two rings share a single atom, giving them a distinctive three-dimensional shape. They occur frequently in essential oils and are relevant to the processing, refining or preservation of animal or vegetable fats, fatty substances and waxes.
[C11B 9/0092] containing only N as heteroatom -- C11B 9/0092 covers essential oil compositions in which nitrogen is the sole heteroatom present, meaning the oil contains nitrogen-bearing constituents but no other heteroatoms such as oxygen or sulfur. The classification applies to methods of producing, extracting, refining or preserving such nitrogen-only essential oils.
[C11B 9/0096] containing at least two different heteroatoms, at least one being nitrogen -- This subgroup pertains to essential oils, fats or waxes whose molecules contain at least two different heteroatoms, with one of them being nitrogen. It is used to classify compositions or processes involving oils or waxes that incorporate nitrogen-bearing groups together with another heteroatom such as oxygen or sulfur.
[C11B 9/02] Recovery or refining of essential oils from raw materials -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Essential oil compositions for perfume applications -> C11B9/00
[C11B 9/022] Refining -- C11B 9/022 refers to methods for refining animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes, including essential oils, after extraction or pressing. The subclass covers processes such as degumming, neutralisation, bleaching, deodorisation and other treatments that improve purity, colour, stability or odor before final use.
[C11B 9/025] Recovery by solvent extraction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Extraction of essential oils from raw materials using an organic solvent or an inorganic solvent, e.g. water or supercritical CO 2 .
[C11B 9/027] Recovery of volatiles by distillation or stripping -- **Glossary:** - Stripping: Removal of volatile compounds from a liquid stream by a vapour stream, typically steam or a heated gas
[C11C] FATTY ACIDS FROM FATS, OILS OR WAXES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparation of carboxylic acid esters -> C07C67/00 - Separation, purification, stabilisation, or use of additives associated with the preparation of carboxylic acid esters -> C07C67/48 - Esters of carboxylic acids, e.g. fatty acid esters -> C07C69/00 - Sulfonated fats or oils -> C07C309/62 - Epoxidised fats -> C07D303/42 - Natural vulcanized oils, e.g. factice -> C08H3/00 - Compositions of oils, fats or waxes; Compositions of derivatives thereof -> C08L91/00 - Production, refining, preservation of fats -> C11B - Recovery of fatty acids from waste materials -> C11B13/00 - Preparation of fats, fatty oils, ester-type waxes, higher fatty acids or oxidized oils or fats using enzymes or microorganisms -> C12P7/64 - Lighting devices -> F21  **Glossary:** - Fat: Esters of glycerol and fatty acids, generally in solid form - Fatty acid: A carboxylic acid consisting of a hydrocarbon chain, which may have unsaturations and a terminal carboxyl group - Oil: Fat which is in liquid form - Esterification: Reaction between acid and alcohol resulting in ester bond - Hydrogenation: Chemical process that adds hydrogen atoms to a molecule - Isomerizing: Process by which one molecule is transformed into another molecule which has exactly the same atoms - Lipid: Organic compounds based on fatty acids, generally hydrophobic - Wax: Any of a group of substances composed of hydrophobic hydrocarbons, alcohols, fatty acids, and esters that are solid at ambient temperatures.
[C11C 1/00] Preparation of fatty acids from fats, fatty oils, or waxes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of fatty acids in general.
[C11C 1/002] Sources of fatty acids, e.g. natural glycerides, characterised by the nature, the quantities or the distribution of said acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sources selected for production of particular types of fatty acids (e.g. HUFA, PUFA, CLA, branched fatty acids, etc.)
[C11C 1/005] Splitting up mixtures of fatty acids into their constituents  processes intended for both fats and fatty acids  C11B7/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes specifically for separating fatty acids (e.g. stearic from oleic, CLA from C 18 in general)
[C11C 1/007] using organic solvents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery and refining of fatty acids specifically.
[C11C 1/02] from fats or fatty oils -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery and refining of fatty acids specifically from pre-separated oils/fats in general.
[C11C 1/025] by saponification and release of fatty acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery and refining of fatty acids specifically from pre-separated oils/fats using base hydrolysis.
[C11C 1/04] by hydrolysis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery and refining of fatty acids specifically from pre-separated oils/fats using non-base hydrolysis.
[C11C 1/045] using enzymes or microorganisms, living or dead -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of fatty acids from fatty acid containing material, e.g. hydrolysis of fats or fatty oils using esterase, lipase to prepare fatty acids
[C11C 1/06] using solid catalysts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  e.g. clays, resins, etc.
[C11C 1/08] Refining -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Undefined processes for removing undesired material from fatty acids.
[C11C 1/10] by distillation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for removing undesired material from fatty acids where process depends on change to gaseous phase., e.g. distillation, short path, wiped film
[C11C 1/103] after or with the addition of chemicals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. steam.
[C11C 1/106] inert gases or vapors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Refining of fatty acids by distillation after or with the addition of expandable fluids, either noble type, e.g. inert gases, inert co-solvents or molecules, e.g.CO2
[C11C 3/00] Fats, oils, or fatty acids by chemical modification of fats, oils, or fatty acids obtained therefrom -- This CPC class C11C 3/00 covers the chemical modification of fats, oils, or fatty acids obtained from them. It includes processes for converting, functionalising, or derivatising these lipids, such as esterification, hydrogenation, oxidation, or polymerisation to produce new compounds or materials.
[C11C 3/003] by esterification of fatty acids with alcohols  C11C3/02  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of fatty acid esters where fatty acid containing material is esterified with an alcohol per se.
[C11C 3/006] by oxidation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fats, oils, or fatty acids obtained by combination with oxidation, e.g. epoxidation, peroxidation, lactones,
[C11C 3/02] by esterification of fatty acids with glycerol -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of fatty acid esters where fatty acids which are not esterified are esterified with glycerol.
[C11C 3/025] with a stoechiometric excess of glycerol -- The subclass C11C 3/025 covers chemical modifications of fats, oils or fatty acids in which glycerol is present in a stoichiometric excess, typically for glycerolysis reactions that produce mono- and diglycerides or other glycerol-based derivatives. It is used to classify processes and products where the excess glycerol drives the conversion of triglycerides into value-added glyceride compounds.
[C11C 3/04] by esterification of fats or fatty oils -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of fatty acid esters where non-free fatty acid containing material is esterified.
[C11C 3/06] with glycerol -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of fatty acid esters where non-free fatty acid containing material is esterified with glycerol (e.g. triglyceride reaction with glycerol)
[C11C 3/08] with fatty acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of fatty acid esters where non-free carboxylic acid with a long unbranched aliphatic tail (fatty acid) containing material is esterified with a free carboxylic cid with a long unbranched aliphatic tail (fatty acid ), for example triglyceride reacted with oleic acid
[C11C 3/10] Ester interchange -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of fatty acid esters where non-free fatty acid containing material is esterified with esterifed material by swapping one type of acid-alcohol with another acid-alcohol type, for example triglyceride with ethyl stearate, triglyceride with diglyceride, interesterification.
[C11C 3/12] by hydrogenation -- C11C 3/12 covers methods that hydrogenate fats, oils or fatty acids, meaning the catalytic addition of hydrogen to unsaturated carbon-carbon bonds to convert them into more saturated compounds. These processes are used to produce solid or semi-solid fats, improve oxidative stability, or modify melting points and other physical properties.
[C11C 3/123] using catalysts based principally on nickel or derivates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of fats, oils or fatty acids by hydrogenation using catalysts based principally on nickel or derivates, i.e. any compounds derived from a nickel based compound , e.g. nickel based organometallic
[C11C 3/126] using catalysts based principally on other metals or derivates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of fats, oils or fatty acids by hydrogenation using catalysts based principally on other metals or derivates, i.e. any compounds derived from a other metal based compound , e.g. other metal based organometallic
[C11C 3/14] by isomerisation -- C11C 3/14 refers to the production of fats, oils, or fatty acids by chemical modification, specifically through isomerisation. This process involves rearranging molecular structures. It is a subclass of fatty acids from fats, oils, or waxes.
[C11C 5/00] Candles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wick containing products and processes to prepare.
[C11C 5/002] Ingredients -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. fuel components, blends, additives, etc.
[C11C 5/004] dyes, pigments; products giving a coloured flame -- The CPC subclass C11C 5/004 covers chemical compounds and formulations used as dyes or pigments that produce a coloured flame, such as metal salts, organic colourants and related mixtures employed in pyrotechnic displays, fireworks and flame-colouring applications. It includes products designed specifically to impart visual colour to combustion while not necessarily serving as conventional surface pigments.
[C11C 5/006] wicks, related accessories -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Material, usually a fiber bundle, that draws, usually by capillary action, the combustible substance to be burned, wick components, and related apparatus, wicks, wicks related accessories of a candle,eg. wick holders, wick clips, etc.
[C11C 5/008] Candles characterised by their form; Composite candles, e.g. candles containing zones of different composition, inclusions, or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. decorative, aesthetic, novelty, segmented, etc.

[C11C 5/021] by compressing solid materials in a mould without heating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes using pressure and no external heat
[C11C 5/023] by casting or melting in a mould -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes whereby candle material is poured
[C11C 5/025] by dipping a wick in a melt -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes involving repetitive dipping of wick into molten candle material
[C11C 5/026] by a continuous process, e.g. by extrusion by conveying a melt together with the wick through a solidification zone -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes whereby candle material is continuously injection molded or extruded
[C11C 5/028] by shaping a preform, e.g. forming the butts, trimming -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes whereby the candle is carved or compressed into shape from preformed candle.
[C11D] DETERGENT COMPOSITIONS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - amphoteric surfactant: amphoteric surfactants contain both acidic and basic groups in their molecule, and can act as cationic or anionic detergents, depending on the pH of the solution, or as both cation and anion. Betains are included. - anionic surfactant: anionic surfactants have a negatively-charged surface-active ion. An example is sodium alkylbenzene sulfonate. - cationic surfactant: cationic surfactants may have a positively-charged cation; typical are the quaternary ammonium salts; they may also be amides or amines - compounding ingredients: the non-surface-active ingredients in a detergent composition, e.g. builder, water-softening agents or solvent - detergents: detergents are basically any cleaning materials. Such materials may or may not contain surfactants. The term "synthetic detergent" is also used as a synonym for "surfactant" (excluding soaps, which are not considered synthetic), which is a compound or a mixture of compounds, whose molecules have two distinct regions – one that is hydrophilic and has an affinity for water and another that is hydrophobic, with little (if any) affinity for water. These compounds can aid in the solubilisation of hydrophobic compounds in water. See examples below of particular types of surfactant (detergent). - nonionic surfactant: nonionic surfactants are synthetic surface-active agents which are such that the molecules do not ionise in aqueous solution. Typical are the surfactants based on condensation products of ethylene oxide with a hydrophobe. - resin soap: a yellow soap containing resin, used for bleaching. It is made by adding a certain quantity of resin (rosin or colophony), as much as 50% and more of the fat employed, to the mass in the soap boiler towards the end of the process. - syndet bars: piece of hard soap made from synthetic surfactants - soaps: soaps have the same properties as those mentioned above for surfactants, but in general soaps are not considered as synthetic detergents. Soap is a particular type of surfactant derived from oils and fats created through the saponification process whereby the ester linkage in a vegetable oil or fat is hydrolytically cleaved using an alkali or (NaOH or KOH) or ammonia or amine yielding glycerol and crude soap. Soaps usually consist of the alkaline salts of fatty acids such as palmitic, stearic or oleic acids. Soft soaps contain the potassium salts, whereas the sodium salts are hard soaps. Soaps are examples of anionic surfactants. - surface-active compounds/agents (surfactants): substances which have the effect of reducing the surface tension of a solvent, an example being a detergent/surfactant or soap dissolved in water. These substances, also known as wetting agents, contain a combination of polar (hydrophilic) and non-polar (hydrophobic) parts which serve to bind oil and water together. They are located at the phase boundary between the water phase and the organic phase or if there is no room there, they will congregate together and form micelles. Both synthetic detergents and soaps are surfactants. - surfactants: see "surface-active compounds/agents"
[C11D 1/00] Detergent compositions based essentially on surface-active compounds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Detergents compositions based on surfactants, surface active agents  C11D1/00 - C11D1/94 subgroups
[C11D 10/00] Compositions of detergents, not provided for by one single preceding group -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mixtures of soap and non soap surfactants
[C11D 1/002] Surface-active compounds containing sulfur  SO 3  and SO 4 ,   C11D1/12 ; sulfonium compounds  C11D1/60 ; sulfoxides  C11D1/755 ; sulfobetaines  C11D1/92
[C11D 1/004] Surface-active compounds containing F -- C11D 1/004 covers detergent compositions whose surfactants are fatty-derived molecules that incorporate fluorine atoms. These fluorinated surface-active compounds provide low-temperature cleaning and oil-repellent properties, and are used in formulations based essentially on such surfactants.
[C11D 10/04] based on mixtures of surface-active non-soap compounds and soap -- C11D 10/04 refers to detergent compositions based on mixtures of surface-active non-soap compounds and soap. It is a subgroup of detergent compositions. This code is used in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[C11D 10/042] based on anionic surface-active compounds and soap -- C11D 10/042 covers detergent compositions that rely on anionic surface-active agents together with soap, falling under animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes in the chemistry hierarchy. These formulations are distinguished from other detergent groups by the specific combination of anionic surfactants and soap bases.
[C11D 10/045] based on non-ionic surface-active compounds and soap -- C11D 10/045 refers to detergent compositions based on non-ionic surface-active compounds and soap. This classification falls under chemistry and animal or vegetable oils. It is a specific subgroup of detergent compositions.
[C11D 10/047] based on cationic surface-active compounds and soap -- C11D 10/047 covers detergent compositions that combine cationic surface-active agents with traditional soap, forming blends used for cleaning, emulsifying or softening tasks. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, oils and fats, and detergent formulations, and includes mixtures not specified in a single preceding group.
[C11D 1/006] Surface-active compounds containing fluorine and phosphorus -- The CPC subclass C11D 1/006 defines detergent compositions that rely on surface-active agents incorporating both fluorine and phosphorus atoms. These fluorophosphorous surfactants are used to impart specific wetting, foaming or oil-removal properties in cleaning formulations.
[C11D 1/008] Polymeric surface-active agents -- C11D 1/008 refers to detergent compositions based on polymeric surface-active agents. These are compounds with repeating units that reduce surface tension. They are used in various cleaning products.
[C11D 1/02] Anionic compounds -- Subclass C11D 1/02 covers detergent compositions whose active surfactant is an anionic compound, such as soaps, alkyl sulfates, sulfonates or phosphates. These surface-active agents are water-soluble salts that lower surface tension and are employed for cleaning, emulsifying or wetting in a variety of household and industrial products.
[C11D 1/04] Carboxylic acids or salts thereof -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Soap -> C11D9/00
[C11D 1/06] Ether- or thioether carboxylic acids
[C11D 1/08] Polycarboxylic acids containing no nitrogen or sulfur -- C11D 1/08 covers polycarboxylic acids that contain only carbon, hydrogen and oxygen and lack nitrogen or sulfur atoms, used primarily as builders or chelating agents in detergent formulations. These compounds improve cleaning efficiency by softening water and dispersing soils.
[C11D 1/10] Amino carboxylic acids -- C11D 1/10 refers to detergent compositions based on amino carboxylic acids. These are surface-active compounds used in detergents. They are classified under chemistry.
[C11D 11/00] Special methods for preparing compositions containing mixtures of detergents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any specific features related to processes for preparing compositions containing mixtures of detergents.
[C11D 11/0082] one or more of the detergent ingredients being in a liquefied state, e.g. slurry, paste or melt, and the process resulting in solid detergent particles such as granules, powders or beads -- C11D 11/0082 refers to special methods for preparing detergent compositions. It involves one or more detergent ingredients in a liquefied state - resulting in solid particles. This includes granules, powders or beads.
[C11D 11/0088] the liquefied ingredients being sprayed or adsorbed onto solid particles -- C11D 11/0088 refers to special methods for preparing detergent compositions. It involves liquefied ingredients being sprayed or adsorbed onto solid particles. This is a subclass of detergent compositions.
[C11D 11/0094] Process for making liquid detergent compositions, e.g. slurries, pastes or gels -- C11D 11/0094 relates to processes for producing liquid detergent compositions such as slurries, pastes or gels, where surfactants, animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes are mixed with water and other additives. The methods aim to control viscosity, stability and homogeneity during manufacture.
[C11D 11/02] Preparation in the form of powder by spray drying -- This CPC subclass covers processes for converting detergent mixtures, often containing fatty substances, into a powdered product using spray drying. The method involves atomizing the liquid feed into a hot drying chamber where water evaporates, leaving a fine powder that can be used as a detergent.
[C11D 11/04] by chemical means, e.g. by sulfonating in the presence of other compounding ingredients followed by neutralising -- C11D 11/04 relates to the manufacture of detergent compositions by chemical processes, specifically methods that involve sulfonating the raw material in the presence of other compounding ingredients and then neutralising the product. The subclass falls within the chemistry hierarchy for animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes and their use in detergent formulations.
[C11D 1/12] Sulfonic acids or sulfuric acid esters -- C11D 1/12 designates detergent compositions in which the primary surfactants are sulfonic acids or sulfuric-acid esters, used as the active cleaning agents in formulations based on surface-active compounds within the chemistry subclass for animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes.
[C11D 1/123] derived from carboxylic acids, e.g. sulfosuccinates -- C11D 1/123 relates to detergent compositions whose primary surfactants are derived from carboxylic acids, notably sulfosuccinates, providing the essential cleaning action. These formulations fall under the broader class of detergent compositions based essentially on surface-active compounds.
[C11D 1/126] Acylisethionates -- Acylisethionates are surface-active compounds formed by esterifying isethionic acid with fatty acyl groups, used as mild, high-foaming agents in detergent formulations. The classification C11D 1/126 covers detergent compositions whose cleaning action relies essentially on these acylisethionate surfactants, typically for household and industrial washing applications.
[C11D 1/14] derived from aliphatic hydrocarbons or mono-alcohols -- C11D 1/14 covers detergent formulations whose surfactants are produced from aliphatic hydrocarbons or from mono-alcohols, such as alkyl or fatty-alcohol-derived sulfates, sulfonates and related compounds. These substances serve as the active cleaning agents in detergent compositions derived from oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes.
[C11D 1/143] Sulfonic acid esters -- C11D 1/143 covers detergent compositions in which the principal surfactants are sulfonic acid esters, compounds formed by esterifying fatty acids or related acids with sulfonic acids. These esters provide surface-active properties that facilitate the removal of soils and greases in cleaning applications.
[C11D 1/146] Sulfuric acid esters -- C11D 1/146 covers detergent compositions that rely mainly on surface-active agents formed as esters of sulfuric acid. These sulfuric-acid esters act as surfactants in cleaning formulations, providing emulsifying and wetting properties derived from their acidic ester structure.
[C11D 1/16] derived from divalent or polyvalent alcohols -- C11D 1/16 covers detergent compositions whose surface-active agents are produced from divalent or polyvalent alcohols, such as diols or polyols. These surfactants are used as the primary cleansing component in formulations derived from animal or vegetable oils, fats or waxes. The subclass falls under detergent compositions within the broader chemistry domain.
[C11D 1/18] derived from amino alcohols -- This subclass comprises detergent formulations whose surface-active agents are synthesized from amino alcohols, providing surfactants that effectively solubilise oils, fats and waxes in cleaning applications.
[C11D 1/20] Fatty acid condensates -- C11D 1/20 covers fatty acid condensates, which are products obtained by chemically linking fatty acids through condensation reactions. These condensates serve as key surface-active agents in detergent formulations, providing cleaning, emulsifying, and foaming properties derived from animal or vegetable oils, fats, or waxes.
[C11D 1/22] derived from aromatic compounds -- C11D 1/22 refers to detergent compositions whose surface-active agents are sourced from aromatic compounds, such as aromatic sulfonates or sulfates, used to enhance cleaning performance. These formulations fall under the broader categories of chemistry, animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes, and detergent compositions based essentially on surface-active substances. The subclass captures any detergent where the active component is chemically derived from an aromatic precursor.
[C11D 1/24] containing ester or ether groups directly attached to the nucleus -- C11D 1/24 refers to detergent compositions with ester or ether groups attached to the nucleus. This classification is under detergent compositions based on surface-active compounds. It falls under animal or vegetable oils and fats.
[C11D 1/26] derived from heterocyclic compounds -- C11D 1/26 designates detergent compositions whose essential surface-active agents are derived from heterocyclic compounds, covering formulations where the surfactant core contains a ring structure incorporating nitrogen, oxygen or sulfur atoms. This subclass falls under detergent compositions based essentially on surface-active compounds within the broader category of animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes.
[C11D 1/28] Sulfonation products derived from fatty acids or their derivatives, e.g. esters, amides
[C11D 1/29] Sulfates of polyoxyalkylene ethers -- C11D 1/29 is a CPC subclass for detergent compositions whose active agents are sulfates of polyoxyalkylene ethers, i.e., surfactants formed by attaching sulfate groups to polyether chains. These compounds provide surface-active properties for cleaning applications and fall under the broader category of detergent compositions based essentially on surface-active compounds.
[C11D 1/30] Sulfonation products derived from lignin -- C11D 1/30 covers detergent compositions that use sulfonated lignin as the primary surface-active agent. These sulfonation products, obtained by treating lignin with sulfonating agents, function as biodegradable anionic surfactants for cleaning applications.
[C11D 13/00] Soap or soap-making -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Soap or making soap or soap solutions  Apparatus therefor  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Resin soap -> C11D15/00
[C11D 13/02] Boiling soap -- C11D 13/02 covers processes for making soap in which the ingredients are heated or boiled together. It includes the preparation of soap by boiling fats or oils with alkaline substances and related steps such as cooling and shaping. The subclass is part of detergent compositions within the broader category of animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes.
[C11D 13/04] Continuous methods therefor -- C11D 13/04 covers continuous processes for producing soap or detergent compositions from animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes. It details methods where the reaction, mixing or shaping steps are carried out in a nonstop flow, rather than in batch. The entry is used to classify inventions that improve efficiency, control or scale-up of soap-making in industrial settings.
[C11D 13/06] Bleaching of soap or soap solutions -- This subcategory concerns methods for whitening or removing colour from soaps and soap solutions, usually by applying chemical or oxidative agents during manufacture or post-production processing. It falls under detergent compositions within the animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes subclass of chemistry.
[C11D 13/08] Colouring or perfuming -- The C11D 13/08 entry covers detergent compositions, particularly soaps, that are formulated with added colourants or fragrances. It pertains to the step of colouring or perfuming during the soap-making process.
[C11D 13/10] Mixing -- The CPC subclass C11D 13/10 concerns processes for mixing the ingredients used in soap-making, including the blending of fatty substances, alkalies, solvents and additives to form detergent compositions. It addresses the mechanical and procedural steps required to achieve a homogeneous soap product during manufacture.
[C11D 13/12] Cooling -- C11D 13/12 covers methods and apparatus for cooling soap or soap-making mixtures, typically after saponification, to control solidification, improve texture, or enhance product stability. The subclass includes cooling steps integrated into detergent composition processes that use animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances, or waxes.
[C11D 13/14] Shaping -- C11D 13/14 Shaping covers methods for forming soap or detergent bodies into specific shapes, such as molding, extrusion, pressing, cutting or stamping, as part of manufacturing detergent compositions derived from animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes.
[C11D 13/16] in moulds -- The C11D 13/16 subclass covers methods of producing soap or soap-making where the material is shaped or set within moulds. It includes processes that form solid soap bodies by casting, pressing or extruding the soap mixture into a moulded form.
[C11D 13/18] by extrusion or pressing -- C11D 13/18 covers processes for making soap or other detergent compositions by mechanically shaping the material, specifically through extrusion or pressing of fats, oils or waxes, rather than by traditional batch or melt-mixing methods. The classification applies to continuous or batch operations where the soap mixture is forced through dies or compressed into forms to achieve the final product. This subclass is used for patents describing equipment and methods that form soap bars, tablets or shaped blocks by these mechanical techniques.
[C11D 1/32] Protein hydrolysates -- C11D 1/32 refers to detergent compositions based on protein hydrolysates, which are surface-active compounds. This classification is used for detergents containing protein breakdown products. It falls under detergent compositions in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[C11D 13/20] in the form of small particles, e.g. powder or flakes -- The code C11D 13/20 covers soaps or soap-making processes that produce the final product as small particles such as powders or flakes. It applies to detergent compositions derived from animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes, focusing on the particulate form of the soap for use in cleaning applications.
[C11D 13/22] Cutting -- C11D 13/22 covers methods for cutting or shaping soap or other detergent compositions during manufacture, such as dividing raw soap into bars, flakes, pellets or other units by mechanical cutting, shearing or slicing operations.
[C11D 13/24] Slicing soap on the cooling drum -- C11D 13/24 refers to a process in soap-making. It involves slicing soap on a cooling drum, a step in the manufacturing process. This classification falls under detergent compositions.
[C11D 13/26] Drying -- The C11D 13/26 subclass covers drying methods applied to soap or soap-making detergent compositions, such as removing moisture from animal or vegetable oil-based soaps and related fatty substances. It includes techniques for evaporating water or other liquids to produce solid or semi-solid soap products. This subclass falls under the broader categories of chemistry, oils and fats, detergent compositions, and soap manufacturing.
[C11D 13/28] Embossing -- The C11D 13/28 subclass covers techniques and apparatus for embossing soap or other detergent bars, including the creation of surface patterns, textures, or decorative designs during or after molding. It addresses methods that impart raised or recessed features for aesthetic, grip, or branding purposes.
[C11D 13/30] Recovery of soap, e.g. from spent solutions -- C11D 13/30 refers to recovery of soap from spent solutions. This classification is under detergent compositions. It involves soap or soap-making processes.
[C11D 1/34] Derivatives of acids of phosphorus -- C11D 1/34 covers detergent compositions that contain derivatives of phosphorus acids, such as phosphates, phosphonates and related compounds, used as surfactants, builders or bleaching agents in cleaning formulations. These phosphorus-derived ingredients provide alkalinity, water-softening and enhanced soil-removal performance in laundry and household detergents.
[C11D 1/342] Phosphonates; Phosphinates or phosphonites -- C11D 1/342 covers detergent compositions that use phosphonate, phosphinate or phosphonite compounds as the surface-active agents. These phosphorus-containing surfactants provide cleaning and anticorrosive properties in formulations for washing fabrics, hard surfaces or industrial cleaning.
[C11D 1/345] Phosphates or phosphites -- C11D 1/345 designates detergent compositions that incorporate phosphates or phosphites as primary components. These inorganic salts serve as builders, water softeners, or buffering agents in surfactant systems to enhance cleaning efficiency in aqueous applications. The subclass falls under chemistry, animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes, and detergent compositions based essentially on surface-active compounds.

[C11D 1/36] of unknown constitution -- C11D 1/36 covers detergent compositions that rely principally on surface-active agents derived from animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes, where the precise chemical identity of the active components is not disclosed. The class is used for formulations whose constituent substances are of unknown constitution but function as surfactants in cleaning applications.
[C11D 1/37] Mixtures of compounds all of which are anionic -- C11D 1/37 covers detergent compositions that consist exclusively of anionic surfactants, i.e. mixtures where every component carries a negative charge. These formulations are used for cleaning applications and fall under the broader class of detergent compositions derived from animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes.
[C11D 1/38] Cationic compounds -- C11D 1/38 refers to detergent compositions based on cationic surface-active compounds. These compounds have a positive charge. They are used in various cleaning products.
[C11D 1/385] Cationic compounds containing P -- C11D 1/385 covers detergent compositions in which the primary surfactants are cationic phosphorus-containing compounds. These agents are typically derived from animal or vegetable oils, fats or waxes and function as surface-active agents in cleaning formulations.
[C11D 1/40] Monoamines or polyamines -- C11D 1/40 refers to detergent compositions based on monoamines or polyamines. These are surface-active compounds used in detergents. They are classified under detergent compositions.
[C11D 1/42] Amino alcohols or amino ethers -- C11D 1/42 is a classification for detergent compositions. It covers amino alcohols or amino ethers - surface-active compounds.
[C11D 1/44] Ethers of polyoxyalkylenes with amino alcohols -- C11D 1/44 refers to detergent compositions based on ethers of polyoxyalkylenes with amino alcohols. These are surface-active compounds used in detergents. They have specific properties.
[C11D 1/46] Esters of carboxylic acids with amino alcohols -- The C11D 1/46 subclass includes detergent formulations that contain esters formed by reacting carboxylic acids with amino alcohols. These compounds act as surface-active agents in cleaning compositions derived from animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes.
[C11D 1/48] N-containing polycondensation products -- Subgroup C11D 1/48 covers detergent formulations that contain polymeric surfactants obtained by polycondensation reactions in which nitrogen-bearing monomers are incorporated. These N-containing polycondensation products, such as polyamides, polyureas or related copolymers, provide surface-active properties for cleaning or emulsifying applications.
[C11D 1/50] Derivatives of urea, thiourea, cyanamide, guanidine or urethanes -- This subclass (C11D 1/50) covers detergent compositions that employ surface-active agents derived from urea, thiourea, cyanamide, guanidine or urethane structures. Such derivatives act as surfactants, providing cleaning, emulsifying or foaming functions in formulations and are classified under chemistry → animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes → detergent compositions.

[C11D 15/04] Compositions containing resin soap or soap derived from naphthenic acids -- This CPC subclass C11D 15/04 falls under chemistry, specifically animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes and detergent compositions, and it relates to compositions that contain a resin soap or soap derived from naphthenic acids, typically used in the manufacture of such detergent products.
[C11D 1/52] Carboxylic amides, alkylolamides or imides -- C11D 1/52 covers detergent compositions in which the active surfactant component is a carboxylic amide, an alkylolamide or an imide. These compounds provide surface-active properties derived from fatty acid or fatty alcohol structures and are used to formulate cleaning agents for a range of applications. The subclass is part of the broader group of detergent compositions based on surface-active compounds derived from animal or vegetable oils, fats, fatty substances or waxes.
[C11D 1/521] Carboxylic amides (R 1 -CO-NR 2 R 3 ), where R 1 , R 2  and R 3  are alkyl or alkenyl groups -- C11D 1/521 covers detergent compositions that use carboxylic amides as the primary surfactant, where the amide has an alkyl or alkenyl group attached to both the carbonyl carbon and the nitrogen. These compounds function as surface-active agents in cleaning formulations derived from animal or vegetable oils, fats, or waxes. [C11D 1/523] Carboxylic alkylolamides, or dialkylolamides, or hydroxycarboxylic amides (R 1 -CO-NR 2 R 3 ), where R 1 , R 2  or R 3  contain one hydroxy group per alkyl group -- C11D 1/523 covers detergent compositions that contain carboxylic alkylolamides, dialkylolamides or hydroxycarboxylic amides of the formula R1-CO-NR2 R3, where at least one of the R groups includes a hydroxy-substituted alkyl chain.
