[C12F] RECOVERY OF BY-PRODUCTS OF FERMENTED SOLUTIONS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery of by-products of fermented solutions;  Preparation of denatured alcohol;  Denatured alcohol, per se.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Removal of yeast from wine or sparkling wine -> C12G1/08  **Glossary:** - Denatured alcohol: Ethanol that has additives to make it inedible (poisonous) in order to prevent human consumption. Denatured alcohol permits legitimate use and manufacture of ethanol, since its manufacturing process creates an ethanol-containing solution that is not suitable for drinking, but it is similar to ethanol for most purposes.
[C12F 3/00] Recovery of by-products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery of by-products of only fermented solutions, e.g. from beer, wine, and distillery slops.  The recovered (secondary) by-product may be obtained from a (primary) by-product of the preparation of a fermented solution.
[C12F 3/02] of carbon dioxide -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery of carbon dioxide as by-product of fermented solutions.
[C12F 3/04] Recovery of volatile fermentation products from carbon dioxide -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery of volatile fermentation products from carbon dioxide obtained as by-product of fermented solutions.
[C12F 3/06] from beer and wine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery of by-products of fermented solutions obtained in beer brewing or wine making.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Recovery of carbon dioxide as by-product of fermented solutions -> C12F3/02 - Removal of yeast ("degorgeage") -> C12G1/08
[C12F 3/08] Recovery of alcohol from press residues or other waste material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery of alcohol from press residues or other waste material obtained in beer brewing or wine making.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Recovery of alcohol from carbon dioxide -> C12F3/04
[C12F 3/10] from distillery slops -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Recovery of by-products from distillery slops, i.e. from the residue remaining after distillation of fermented solutions.
[C12F 5/00] Preparation of denatured alcohol -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Denatured alcohol and preparation methods therefore.
[C12G] WINE -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Beer; Preparation thereof -> C12C  **Glossary:** - Dégorgeage: Process for removing yeast which has settled in the neck of a bottle or on a specially formed closure - Desulfiting: Process for the removal of excess sulfur dioxide - Fermentation: Process whereby yeast converts sugar to ethyl alcohol. - Immobilized yeast: The yeast is absorbed on a material instead of being dispersed throughout the liquid - Must: Juice extracted from grapes - Pompage: Pumping - Sulfiting: Process for adding sulfur dioxide (SO 2 ) - Wine: This term includes fortified wines such as Sherry, Port or Madeira, but does not include barley wine which is a synonym for beer used in older documents - Winestone: Crystals of potassium bitartrate precipitated from the wine
[C12G 1/00] Preparation of wine or sparkling wine -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[C12G 1/005] Methods or means to load or unload, to weigh or to sample the vintage; Replenishing; Separation of the liquids from the solids before or after fermentation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or means for loading, unloading, weighing and/or sampling vintage, i.e. grape material to be used in winemaking, for replenishing, or for separation of the liquids from the solids before or after fermentation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Extraction of juices -> A23L2/04
[C12G 1/02] Preparation of must from grapes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Separation of the liquids from the solids before or after fermentation -> C12G1/005
[C12G 1/0203] by microbiological or enzymatic treatment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Treatment or fermentation of the must (i.e. grape juice) using microorganisms and/or enzymes.
[C12G 1/0206] using a home wine making vessel -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation, treatment or fermentation of the must (i.e. grape juice) using a home (or comparable small scale) wine making vessel.
[C12G 1/0209] in a horizontal or rotatably mounted vessel  C12G1/0206  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation, treatment or fermentation of the must (i.e. grape juice) in a horizontal and/or rotatably mounted vessel.
[C12G 1/0213] with thermal treatment of the vintage  C12G1/0206  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation, treatment or fermentation of the must (i.e. grape juice) wherein the vintage is subjected to thermal treatment (i.e. heating and/or cooling).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Using a home wine making vessel -> C12G1/0206 - Post fermentation heating and/or cooling -> C12H  **Glossary:** - Cryomaceration: Maceration of vintage at decreased temperature - Thermovinification: Vinification at increased temperature
[C12G 1/0216] with recirculation of the must for pomage extraction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation, treatment or fermentation of the must (i.e. grape juice) wherein the must is recirculated for pomace extraction.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Immersion (or "plunging") of the pomace (or "cap") in/into the must -> C12G2200/25
[C12G 1/04] Sulfiting the must -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus for sulfiting and/or desulfiting of must (i.e. grape juice) as part of a winemaking process.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preservation of fruit juices -> A23B70/10  **Glossary:** - sulfiting: preservation by the addition of compounds such as sulfuric acid, sulfur dioxide or SO 2
[C12G 1/06] Preparation of sparkling wine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods for reducing the alcohol content after fermentation -> C12H3/00 - Methods for increasing the alcohol content after fermentation -> C12H6/00
[C12G 1/062] Agitation, centrifugation, or vibration of bottles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for agitation, centrifugation or vibration of bottles in sparkling wine production and apparatus specifically designed or adapted therefor.
[C12G 1/064] using enclosed yeast -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of sparkling wine (such as champagne) or impregnation of wine with carbon dioxide, wherein enclosed yeast is used.  **Glossary:** - Enclosed yeast: This term seems not to be completely clear. It may be useful to replace it e.g. by "immobilised yeast" (although this implies a larger scope)
[C12G 1/08] Removal of yeast ["degorgeage"] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices and methods for removing yeast in the preparation of sparkling wine or champagne
[C12G 1/14] Preparation of wine or sparkling wine with low alcohol content
[C12G 2200/00] Special features
[C12G 2200/05] Use of particular microorganisms in the preparation of wine -- The CPC entry C12G 2200/05 covers the use of specific microorganisms, such as selected yeast or bacterial strains, to influence the fermentation and organoleptic properties of wine. It includes methods for inoculating grape must, controlling microbial populations, and optimizing flavor, aroma, and stability during wine production.
[C12G 2200/11] Use of genetically modified microorganisms in the preparation of wine -- The CPC subclass C12G 2200/11 covers the use of genetically-modified microorganisms in the preparation of wine. It includes methods that employ engineered microbial strains for fermentation, flavor development, or other alterations of grape must, and encompasses related biotechnological processes and applications in winemaking.
[C12G 2200/15] Use of particular enzymes in the preparation of wine -- This CPC subclass covers inventions that involve adding specific enzymes to the winemaking process to modify fermentation, flavor, or stability. It includes methods that select particular enzymes, such as glycosidases or proteases, to enhance aroma, clarify, or accelerate the conversion of grape must into wine.
[C12G 2200/21] Wine additives, e.g. flavouring or colouring agents -- C12G 2200/21 refers to additives used in winemaking, including substances that impart flavour or colour to the final product. It classifies chemical compounds applied to modify wine characteristics.
[C12G 2200/25] Preparation of wine or sparkling wine in vessels with movable equipment for mixing the content -- The CPC subclass C12G 2200/25 covers processes for preparing wine or sparkling wine in vessels that contain movable equipment for mixing the contents, such as agitators, stirrers or rotating racks, used during fermentation, ageing or carbonation. It falls under the hierarchy Chemistry → Biochemistry → Wine.
[C12G 2200/31] Wine making devices having compact design or adapted for home use -- C12G 2200/31 covers wine making devices that are compact in size or specifically adapted for domestic use, such as small fermenters, portable chillers and home-scale filtration units. The entry is situated in the Chemistry → Biochemistry → Wine hierarchy of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the EPO and USPTO.
[C12G 3/00] Preparation of other alcoholic beverages -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing alcoholic beverages other than beer, wine (derived from grapes) or sparkling wine (derived from grapes) and apparatus adapted therefor.
[C12G 3/005] Solid or pasty alcoholic beverage-forming compositions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing solid or pasty compositions forming (typically upon reconstitution or dilution) alcoholic beverages other than beer, wine (derived from grapes) or sparkling wine (derived from grapes).  Apparatus adapted therefor.
[C12G 3/02] by fermentation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing alcoholic beverages other than beer, wine (derived from grapes) or sparkling wine (derived from grapes) by straight fermentation.  Apparatus adapted therefor.
[C12G 3/021] of botanical family Poaceae, e.g. wheat, millet, sorghum, barley, rye, or corn -- This CPC subclass covers processes for producing alcoholic beverages that use grains from the Poaceae family-such as wheat, millet, sorghum, barley, rye, or corn-as the fermentable material. It applies to the preparation of wine or other spirits employing these cereals.
[C12G 3/022] of botanical genus Oryza, e.g. rice -- C12G 3/022 refers to processes that use the botanical genus Oryza, such as rice, in the preparation of alcoholic beverages other than wine. It covers methods for fermenting or otherwise converting rice into drinks like sake or rice-based spirits, including any associated biotechnological steps. This subclass falls under chemistry, biochemistry and the broader category of preparing non-wine alcoholic beverages.
[C12G 3/023] of botanical family Solanaceae, e.g. potato
[C12G 3/024] of fruits other than botanical genus Vitis -- C12G 3/024 covers methods for producing alcoholic beverages from fruits that are not members of the Vitis genus. It addresses fermentation, extraction and related processing steps for fruit wines such as those made from apples, berries, tropical fruits and other non-grape sources, within the broader field of wine and other alcoholic beverage preparation.
[C12G 3/025] Low-alcohol beverages -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing low alcoholic beverages other than beer, wine (derived from grapes) or sparkling wine (derived from grapes) by straight fermentation.  Apparatus adapted therefor.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparation of low alcoholic beverages other than beer, wine (derived from grapes) or sparkling wine (derived from grapes) by after-treatment of fermented solutions -> C12H3/00
[C12G 3/026] with health-improving ingredients, e.g. flavonoids, flavones, polyphenols or polysaccharides, added before or during the fermentation stage -- C12G 3/026 covers processes for preparing wine or other fermented alcoholic drinks in which health-enhancing ingredients such as flavonoids, flavones, polyphenols or polysaccharides are added before or during fermentation, aiming to improve the nutritional or functional qualities of the final product.
[C12G 3/04] by mixing, e.g. for preparation of liqueurs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing alcoholic beverages other than beer, wine (derived from grapes) or sparkling wine (derived from grapes) by mixing (e.g. liqueurs)  Apparatus adapted therefor.
[C12G 3/05] with health-improving ingredients, e.g. flavonoids, flavones, polyphenols or polysaccharides
[C12G 3/055] extracted from plants -- C12G 3/055 concerns methods for obtaining plant extracts used in the preparation of alcoholic beverages, particularly wine and other drinks. It includes techniques for extracting, purifying and formulating bio-active or flavor compounds from plant material, covering both conventional solvent extraction and newer biotechnological processes.
[C12G 3/06] with flavouring ingredients -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[C12G 3/07] Flavouring with wood extracts, e.g. generated by contact with wood -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing alcoholic beverages other than beer, wine (derived from grapes) or sparkling wine (derived from grapes) by mixing and thereby flavouring with wood or wood extract;  Apparatus adapted therefor;  Pre-treatment of the wood used therefor.
[C12G 3/08] by methods for altering the composition of fermented solutions or alcoholic beverages not provided for in groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods for reducing the alcohol content after fermentation -> C12H3/00 - Methods for increasing the alcohol content after fermentation -> C12H6/00
[C12H] PASTEURISATION, STERILISATION, PRESERVATION, PURIFICATION, CLARIFICATION OR AGEING OF ALCOHOLIC BEVERAGES -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparation of wine or sparkling wine -> C12G1/00 - Simulation of ageing by flavouring -> C12G3/06 - Simulation of ageing by flavouring -> C12G3/06  **Glossary:** - adsorption: capability of all solid substances to attract to their surfaces molecules of gases or solutions with which they are in contact - clarification: removal of the suspended material during aging - lagering: ageing or ripening of the beer by storing. During lagering, fermentation and clarification take place - sulfiting: process for adding sulfur dioxide (SO 2 )
[C12H 1/00] Pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification, or ageing of alcoholic beverages -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods as well as apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification, ageing of alcoholic beverages (typically wine, beer, distilled alcoholic beverages).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparation of beer -> C12C - Simulation of ageing by flavouring -> C12C3/06 - Recovery of by-products from fermented solutions (in particular derived from the preparation of alcoholic beverages) -> C12F - Preparation of wine and alcoholic beverages other than beer and wine -> C12G - Pitching / depitching machines and cellar tools -> C12L
[C12H 1/003] by a biochemical process -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, by a biochemical process.
[C12H 1/006] using bacterial cultures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein bacterial cultures are used.
[C12H 1/02] combined with removal of precipitate or added materials, e.g. adsorption material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein the precipitate, e.g. protein haze or colloidal haze, and/or added materials, e.g. PVPP or silica gel is/are removed.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Removal of yeast from (sparkling) wine by "degorgeage" -> C12G1/08
[C12H 1/04] with the aid of ion-exchange material or inert clarification material, e.g. adsorption material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein ion-exchange and/or inert clarification material is removed.
[C12H 1/0408] with the aid of inorganic added material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods as well as apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein inorganic added clarification material is removed.
[C12H 1/0416] with the aid of organic added material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein added organic clarification material is removed.
[C12H 1/0424] with the aid of a polymer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein added polymeric organic clarification material is removed.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Use of ion-exchange material (in so far as the ion-exchange functionality is of essential importance) -> C12H1/0432
[C12H 1/0432] with the aid of ion-exchange material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein an ion-exchange material is removed.
[C12H 1/06] Precipitation by physical means, e.g. by irradiation, vibrations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein precipitate is generated by physical means (in particular by irradiation and/or vibration) and/or separated by filtration.
[C12H 1/061] Separation by centrifugation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein precipitate is generated by physical means, in particular by irradiation and/or vibration, and removed by centrifugation.
[C12H 1/063] Separation by filtration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein  (a) precipitation is generated by physical means, in particular by irradiation and/or vibration, and removed by filtration, or (more generally)  (b) filtration is performed, irrespective of precipitation, adsorption, or the nature of the material to be separated.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Post fermentation treatment of beer involving filtration -> C12C11/11 - Yeast removal in wine or champagne production by degorgage type methods -> C12G1/08
[C12H 1/08] by heating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein precipitate is generated by heating.
[C12H 1/10] Precipitation by chemical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages, wherein precipitate is generated by chemical means.
[C12H 1/12] without precipitation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages not relying on or involving precipitation and/or removal of added materials.
[C12H 1/14] with non-precipitating compounds, e.g. sulfiting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages not relying on or involving precipitation and/or removal of added materials, wherein non-precipitating compounds e.g. sulfite or chelating agents, are used.
[C12H 1/16] by physical means, e.g. irradiation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages not relying on or involving precipitation and/or removal of added materials, wherein physical means (in particular irradiation and/or vibration) are applied.
[C12H 1/165] by irradiation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages not relying on or involving precipitation and/or removal of added materials, wherein irradiation is applied.
[C12H 1/18] by heating -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages not relying on or involving precipitation and/or removal of added materials, wherein heating is applied.
[C12H 1/20] in containers allowing for expansion of the contents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus pertaining to pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages not relying on or involving precipitation and/or removal of added materials, wherein heating is applied and a flexible container allowing for expansion of the contents is used.
[C12H 1/22] Ageing or ripening by storing, e.g. lagering of beer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for ageing of alcoholic beverages, e.g. wine, beer or distilled alcoholic beverages.  Apparatus, e.g. tanks or barrels, specifically adapted for ageing of alcoholic beverages.
[C12H 3/00] Methods for reducing the alcohol content of fermented solutions or alcoholic beverage to obtain low alcohol or non-alcoholic beverages -- The CPC subclass C12H 3/00 covers processes that lower the ethanol concentration in fermented liquids or alcoholic drinks to produce low-alcohol or alcohol-free beverages. It includes techniques such as dilution, evaporation, membrane separation, biological or chemical conversion, and any other method aimed at reducing alcohol content.
[C12H 3/02] by evaporating -- This subclass covers processes that lower the alcohol level of fermented solutions or alcoholic drinks by removing ethanol through evaporation. The technique is applied to produce low-alcohol or non-alcoholic beverages as part of pasteurisation, sterilisation, preservation, purification, clarification or ageing operations.
[C12H 3/04] using semi-permeable membranes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for reducing the alcohol content of fermented solutions or alcoholic beverages to obtain low alcohol or non-alcoholic beverages other than beer, wine (derived from grapes) or sparkling wine (derived from grapes) wherein membranes are used (e.g. ultrafiltration, dialysis, electrodialysis, osmosis, reverse osmosis), and apparatus specifically adapted therefor.
[C12H 6/00] Methods for increasing the alcohol content of fermented solutions or alcoholic beverages -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing alcoholic beverages other than beer, wine (derived from grapes) or sparkling wine (derived from grapes), by increasing the alcohol content;  Apparatus specifically adapted therefor.
[C12H 6/02] by distillation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing alcoholic beverages other than beer, wine (derived from grapes) or sparkling wine (derived from grapes), by increasing the alcohol content by distillation.  Apparatus specifically adapted therefor.
[C12H 6/04] by freezing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing alcoholic beverages other than beer, wine (derived from grapes) or sparkling wine (derived from grapes), by increasing the alcohol content by refrigeration and separation of the crystals thus formed (i.e. freeze-concentration);  Apparatus specifically adapted therefor.
[C12J] VINEGAR -- C12J covers vinegar and related acidic fermentation products, detailing methods for producing, concentrating, stabilising and utilising acetic acid solutions derived from alcoholic fermentation. It also includes formulations, applications in food preservation, flavoring, cleaning and other industrial uses, as well as the handling of by-products and waste streams.
[C12J 1/00] Vinegar -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any vinegar , i.e. from fermentation processes, synthetic vinegar based on synthetic acetic acid, and methods for the preparation thereof, including purification.
[C12J 1/02] from wine -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing vinegar from wine and wine vinegar prepared thereby.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparation of wine and sparkling wine -> C12G1/00
[C12J 1/04] from alcohol -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing vinegar from alcohol and vinegar prepared thereby.
[C12J 1/06] from milk -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing vinegar from milk and vinegar prepared thereby.
[C12J 1/08] Addition of flavouring ingredients -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing vinegar by addition of flavouring ingredients and flavoured vinegar prepared thereby.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Flavouring agents for non-alcoholic beverages -> A23L2/56 - Beer flavouring preparations -> C12C5/026 - Flavouring ingredients for alcoholic beverages other than wine and beer -> C12G3/06
[C12J 1/10] Apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for preparing vinegar.
[C12L] PITCHING OR DEPITCHING MACHINES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pitching and depitching machines, venting devices (for casks, barrels, and the like), cellar tools.
[C12L 11/00] Cellar tools -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cellar tools (typically used in wine or champagne production).
[C12L 3/00] Pitching or depitching machines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pitching and depitching machines, typically devices for coating the inner surface of wooden barrels with pitch, or for removing pitch therefrom, respectively.
[C12L 9/00] Venting devices for casks, barrels, or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Venting devices for casks, barrels, or the like.
[C12M] APPARATUS FOR ENZYMOLOGY OR MICROBIOLOGY -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus or devices specially adapted to the fermentation or to the growth of microorganisms, animal or plant cells or tissues, i.e. bioreactors and fermenters. The apparatus or devices covered by this subclass are designed for producing biomass or artificial tissues in laboratory or industrial scale.  The most simple bioreactor or fermenter is an apparatus comprising at least a container where the microorganisms or cells are placed and which is put under conditions that make it suitable for the culturing of the microorganisms or cells.  This subclass covers also devices for the pre-treatment of the material to be treated in the apparatus and for the after-treatment of the biomass, cells or tissues grown in the apparatus.  This subclass does not cover the methods or processes taking place in bioreactors which are not based on the use of the apparatus or its parts.  In this subclass the term microorganism includes prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. Viruses, human, animal or plant cells, protozoa and unicellular algae are considered microorganisms.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus for culturing plant tissue -> A01H4/001 - Apparatus for preservation of excised living parts of the human or animal body -> A01N1/142 - Chemical or physical processes in general. Chemical or physical laboratory apparatus for general use. Investigating or analysing biological material -> B01J , B01L , G01N33/48 - Apparatus or devices for DNA or RNA technology -> B01L7/52 , B01J19/0046 , C12N15/1003 - Apparatus for the biological treatment of water, waste water, sewage or sludge -> C02F3/00 , C02F11/00 - Apparatus for preparing fertilisers characterised by the composting step -> C05F17/90 - Apparatus for beer brewing -> C12C - Apparatus for production of wine or vinegar -> C12G , C12J1/10 - Fermentation processes -> C12P - Apparatus for bioleaching of ores -> C22B3/18 - Removing non-cellulose substances from cellulose-containing materials -> D21C - Investigating individual particles -> G01N15/10 - Investigating or analysing the degree of sterilisation of non-bioligical materials using chemical indicators -> G01N31/226 - Testing or evaluating the effect of a chemical or biological compound involving human or animal cells -> G01N33/5005 - Investigating or analysing biological material by immunoassay -> G01N33/5302 - Automatic analysis -> G01N35/00  **Glossary:** - Bioreactor: Apparatus comprising at least a container where the microorganisms or cells are placed and which is put under conditions that make it suitable for the culturing of the microorganisms or cells. - Fermenter: Term used for bioreactors for culturing bacteria or yeast with the purpose of obtaining their metabolic or fermentation products
[C12M 1/00] Apparatus for enzymology or microbiology -- C12M 1/00 covers devices used in the study or manipulation of enzymes and microorganisms, such as reactors, fermenters, mixers, and analytical instruments. The classification applies to equipment that supports cultivation, reaction, separation, or measurement of biological activity in enzymology and microbiology.
[C12M 1/002] Photo bio reactors -- C12M 1/002 covers photo-bio reactors, which are apparatus that integrate a light source with a bioreactor to drive phototrophic microbial or enzymatic processes, enabling cultivation or synthesis under controlled illumination. These systems are used for photosynthetic microorganisms, photobiocatalysis, and related applications.
[C12M 1/005] Incubators -- Incubators are devices that maintain controlled temperature, humidity, and gas environments to support the growth, cultivation, or preservation of microorganisms, cells, or biochemical reactions. They provide stable conditions for enzymatic assays, microbial culture, or tissue incubation, often featuring adjustable settings and monitoring systems to ensure reproducibility in laboratory experiments.
[C12M 1/007] Flexible bags or containers -- C12M 1/007 pertains to flexible bags or containers employed in enzymology or microbiology, serving as reaction vessels, storage or transport pouches for enzymes, microorganisms, or culture media. These items are typically made of pliable materials such as plastic film and are designed to maintain sterility while allowing easy handling.
[C12M 1/02] with agitation means -- C12M 1/02 covers apparatus for enzymology or microbiology that includes means for agitation. It includes devices such as stirrers, shakers, rotors or impellers used to mix culture media, enzyme solutions or microbial suspensions during incubation or reaction. The classification groups any system where mechanical or hydraulic agitation is integral to the equipment.
[C12M 1/04] with gas introduction means -- C12M 1/04 covers apparatus used in enzymatic or microbial processes that incorporate means for introducing gas into the reaction zone. This includes spargers, aeration shafts, or diffusion plates that supply oxygen, carbon dioxide or other gases to cultures or enzyme reactors to enhance activity or growth.
[C12M 1/045] providing an anaerobic atmosphere -- C12M 1/045 relates to apparatus that creates or maintains an oxygen-free environment for use in enzymology or microbiology. Such devices may include sealed chambers, gas-mixing systems or other means to provide an anaerobic atmosphere required for studying obligate anaerobes or oxygen-sensitive reactions.
[C12M 1/06] with agitator, e.g. impeller -- The C12M 1/06 subclass covers laboratory or industrial devices used in enzymology or microbiology that include an agitator, such as an impeller, to mix or stir reaction mixtures, cultures, or substrates during processing, ensuring uniform conditions and improved reaction efficiency.
[C12M 1/065] on a horizontal axis
[C12M 1/08] with draft tube -- The C12M 1/08 class covers apparatus for enzymology or microbiology that includes a draft tube, a vertical conduit placed inside a bioreactor or fermenter to improve mixing and gas-liquid mass transfer by allowing liquid to circulate upward and downward, reducing vortex formation and enhancing microbial growth.
[C12M 1/09] Flotation apparatus -- The C12M 1/09 subclass covers flotation apparatus employed in enzymology or microbiology to separate cells, organelles, proteins or other biomolecules by attaching them to rising bubbles. It includes tanks, columns, chambers and related equipment designed to generate and control gas-liquid interfaces for phase separation in biochemical processes.
[C12M 1/10] rotatably mounted -- C12M 1/10 covers apparatus for enzymology or microbiology in which the working part is mounted on a rotating support. It includes devices such as rotating mixers, shakers, and rotors that provide agitation, mixing or orientation control for biological samples. The classification applies when rotation is a key functional feature.
[C12M 1/107] with means for collecting fermentation gases, e.g. methane -- C12M 1/107 covers apparatus used in enzymology or microbiology that incorporates a system for gathering gases released during fermentation, such as methane. The design typically integrates gas-collection chambers, conduits or seals within bioreactors or digesters to capture, measure or channel biogas for further use or analysis.
[C12M 1/113] with transport of the substrate during the fermentation
[C12M 1/12] with sterilisation, filtration or dialysis means -- The C12M 1/12 subclass covers apparatus for enzymology or microbiology that incorporates built-in sterilisation, filtration or dialysis means. Devices such as bioreactors, incubators or culture vessels in this group provide internal mechanisms to eliminate contaminants, separate particles or exchange fluids while the biological process proceeds. This integration helps maintain sterile conditions and control the reaction environment without external equipment.
[C12M 1/121] with sterilisation means -- C12M 1/121 covers apparatus used in enzymology or microbiology that incorporates built-in sterilisation means, such as heating, irradiation, filtration or chemical agents, to render the working chamber or components sterile before, during or after a biological process.
[C12M 1/123] with flat plate filter elements -- The C12M 1/123 subclass covers laboratory or industrial devices for enzymology or microbiology that incorporate flat plate filter elements to separate cells, particles or enzymes from liquids. These flat plate filters provide a large surface area and allow continuous or batch filtration in bioprocessing operations.
[C12M 1/125] Culture inserts
[C12M 1/126] with hollow fibres or tubular filter elements -- The subclass C12M 1/126 covers apparatus used in enzymology or microbiology that incorporate hollow-fibres or tubular filter elements. These devices employ cylindrical porous membranes to retain cells, enzymes or particulates while allowing liquids or gases to pass, facilitating processes such as fermentation, dialysis, cell culture or enzyme reactors.
[C12M 1/128] with moving or mobile filter elements -- C12M 1/128 covers devices for enzymology or microbiology that incorporate moving or mobile filter elements, such as filter plates, cartridges or membranes that can be shifted, rotated or otherwise repositioned during operation to facilitate separation, washing or reagent exchange in assays, fermentations or other bioprocesses. These apparatuses enable continuous or batch processing while maintaining precise control over filtration conditions.
[C12M 1/14] with means providing thin layers or with multi-level trays -- C12M 1/14 covers devices used in enzymology or microbiology that provide one or more thin reaction layers or incorporate multi-level trays, such as microtiter plates, thin-layer reactors and stacked culture trays, enabling simultaneous processing of many small-volume samples or reactions.
[C12M 1/16] containing, or adapted to contain, solid media -- C12M 1/16 covers apparatus that includes, or is adapted to include, solid growth or reaction media for enzymology or microbiology work. It comprises devices such as plates, trays, or containers that hold agar or other solid substrates to support microbial cultures or enzyme assays, facilitating observation, incubation, or testing.
[C12M 1/165] treated with gel punching devices -- C12M 1/165 relates to devices that treat biological gels by punching or cutting them, for example tools that remove defined sections from agar or polyacrylamide gels for sampling, analysis or further processing. The classification covers apparatuses designed to position, drive and control a punching element to create holes or excise portions of the gel without disrupting surrounding material.
[C12M 1/18] Multiple fields or compartments -- C12M 1/18 covers apparatuses used in enzymology or microbiology that provide two or more separate fields or compartments within a single device. The compartments can be isolated to permit different reactions, substrates, or conditions to be carried out simultaneously or sequentially, facilitating multiplexed analysis or parallel processing.
[C12M 1/20] Horizontal planar fields -- C12M 1/20 covers devices that provide a horizontal planar field for conducting enzymatic or microbiological reactions, such as flat surfaces, plates or slides where samples are spread in a uniform layer. These fields enable consistent exposure to reagents, temperature control or illumination across the plane.
[C12M 1/203] Disc dispensing devices therefor -- The CPC subclass C12M 1/203 covers devices that store, handle, and dispense culture or diagnostic discs used in enzymology or microbiology experiments. Such apparatus may include racks, trays, or automated dispensers that release individual discs for use in assays.
[C12M 1/206] Multiple discs supporting devices -- C12M 1/206 covers devices that hold several flat discs in a fixed arrangement for parallel biochemical or microbiological testing, such as multi-well plates, filter or culture discs, and the supporting frames or trays that keep the discs aligned and accessible during incubation, staining or enzymatic assays. These apparatuses enable high-throughput screening, comparative analysis and standardized handling of multiple samples in a single experiment.
[C12M 1/21] Froth suppressors -- Froth suppressors are devices or agents used in enzymology or microbiology apparatus, such as fermenters and bioreactors, to control and reduce foam formation during liquid-phase processes. They improve process stability and product recovery by preventing overflow, contamination and excessive agitation caused by excess foam.
[C12M 1/22] Petri dishes -- Petri dishes are shallow, sterile containers-typically glass or plastic-with a lid, used for culturing microorganisms or conducting enzymatic assays. Their flat bottom and transparent walls allow observation of colony growth, making them essential laboratory tools in microbiology and biochemistry. This apparatus falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification C12M 1/22 for devices employed in enzymology or microbiology.
[C12M 1/24] tube or bottle type -- C12M 1/24 designates tube or bottle type apparatus used in enzymology or microbiology, such as containers for culturing or reacting enzymes or microorganisms. These devices provide a sealed environment for biochemical processes and are typically made of glass or plastic.
[C12M 1/26] Inoculator or sampler -- An inoculator or sampler is a device used to introduce microorganisms, cells, or enzymes into a substrate or to collect samples from a culture, typically enabling precise transfer and minimal contamination. Such tools are employed in laboratories for microbiological or enzymatic experiments, often featuring mechanisms for controlled dispensing or extraction.
[C12M 1/261] Airborne microorganism samplers -- Airborne microorganism samplers are devices designed to collect bacteria, fungi, or viruses from the atmosphere for analysis. They include impactors, impingers, filters, cyclones and other fluid-or solid-based collectors that draw air and capture microorganisms on a substrate or within a liquid medium.
[C12M 1/262] Handle streaking devices -- C12M 1/262 covers handles designed for streaking devices used in microbiology and enzymology. These handles enable precise manipulation of inoculation tools to spread microorganisms evenly across culture media, improving isolation and colony selection in laboratory procedures.
[C12M 1/263] Replica plating devices -- Replica plating devices are apparatus that copy microbial colonies from a primary agar plate onto a series of secondary plates, allowing parallel screening of many strains for phenotypic traits such as antibiotic resistance or metabolic capabilities. The devices typically hold a replica matrix or stamping tool that contacts the donor plate and transfers cells in a consistent pattern.
[C12M 1/264] Devices involving centrifugal, centripetal or rotational forces -- C12M 1/264 covers devices that apply centrifugal, centripetal or rotational forces in enzymology or microbiology, such as centrifuges, rotors and similar apparatus used to separate or manipulate biological samples by spinning. These tools exploit differences in density or sedimentation rate to isolate cells, organelles, enzymes or other biomolecules.
[C12M 1/265] Pipettes; Syringes; Suction devices -- C12M 1/265 covers laboratory tools that move fluids for enzymological or microbiological work, such as pipettes, syringes and suction devices. These apparatuses are used to dispense, transfer, inject or withdraw liquids, reagents, samples or cultures in experimental or analytical procedures.
[C12M 1/266] Magnetic separators -- C12M 1/266 covers magnetic separators used in enzymology and microbiology, devices that employ magnetic fields to isolate cells, proteins, nucleic acids or other biomolecules bound to magnetic particles. These tools facilitate rapid, non-chemical separation in laboratory and industrial processes.
[C12M 1/267] Biofilm separators -- Biofilm separators are devices designed to detach, collect, or isolate microbial biofilms from liquids in biotechnological or analytical processes. They may use mechanical, hydraulic or acoustic methods to prevent fouling of reactors, filters or diagnostic equipment, facilitating downstream processing or measurement.
[C12M 1/268] Positioning tools for sampling or inoculating devices -- C12M 1/268 covers devices that hold, guide or otherwise position tools used for sampling or inoculating biological specimens, such as holders, manipulators or alignment fixtures for probes, pipettes, needles or similar instruments employed in enzymology or microbiology experiments. These tools ensure precise placement of the sampling or inoculating element during analysis or culture procedures.
[C12M 1/28] being part of container -- C12M 1/28 covers features of containers that are integral to devices used in enzymology or microbiology, such as the walls, caps, or internal surfaces that hold reaction mixtures, cultures, or enzyme preparations. It includes designs where the container itself contributes to the functioning of the apparatus, for example by providing controlled atmospheres, temperature regulation, or sterile sealing. This subclass addresses the structural and functional aspects of the container as part of the overall laboratory equipment.
[C12M 1/30] Sampler being a swab -- The C12M 1/30 subclass covers swab-type samplers designed to collect biological material for enzymology or microbiology analysis. These devices are typically handheld implements with a fibrous tip that contacts a surface to pick up cells, tissues, or fluids, enabling subsequent laboratory testing or diagnostic procedures.
[C12M 1/32] multiple field or continuous type -- C12M 1/32 covers apparatus for enzymology or microbiology that operate in a multiple-field or continuous mode, such as multi-well plates, continuous-flow reactors, or platforms with several compartments allowing simultaneous or uninterrupted enzymatic or microbial reactions. These devices are designed to increase throughput or maintain steady-state conditions during assays.
[C12M 1/33] Disintegrators -- C12M 1/33 covers disintegrators, devices that mechanically break up biological material such as cell aggregates, tissues, or biofilms to release individual cells or particles for further analysis or processing. These instruments are used in enzymology and microbiology labs to improve sample homogenisation, facilitate extraction, and enhance downstream assays.
[C12M 1/34] Measuring or testing with condition measuring or sensing means, e.g. colony counters -- C12M 1/34 defines apparatus that includes condition-measuring or sensing elements for analyzing biological samples, exemplified by colony counters used to quantify microbial growth. It belongs to the "apparatus for enzymology or microbiology" subclass within the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy.
[C12M 1/3407] Measure of electrical or magnetical factor -- The C12M 1/3407 subclass covers devices that monitor electrical or magnetic properties in enzymology or microbiology experiments, such as electrodes, conductance meters, magnetometers or related sensors used to detect changes in current, voltage, impedance, magnetic field strength or related parameters during biochemical or microbial processes. These instruments enable quantitative measurement of electrical or magnetic factors that reflect activity, growth or metabolic states of enzymes or microorganisms.
[C12M 1/3415] Pressure measure, e.g. with manometers, respirometers -- The CPC code C12M 1/3415 covers devices that measure pressure in enzymatic or microbiological processes, such as manometers and respirometers used to monitor gas evolution or consumption. These instruments enable quantitative control of reaction conditions in biochemical experiments.
[C12M 1/3423] Calorimetry -- Calorimetry refers to the use of heat-measuring devices within enzymology or microbiology equipment to monitor the thermal effects of biochemical reactions. It enables quantitative analysis of enzyme activity, microbial growth or metabolic processes by detecting temperature changes that correspond to reaction rates.
[C12M 1/343] Mass spectrometry -- C12M 1/343 designates mass-spectrometry instruments and related methods employed in enzymology or microbiology for analyzing enzymes, microbes or their metabolites. The classification covers devices that ionise, separate and detect biomolecular species to provide quantitative or structural information, supporting research and diagnostic applications in biochemical and microbiological contexts.
[C12M 1/3438] with use of isotopes, e.g. radiorespirometers, scintillometers -- The C12M 1/3438 subclass covers apparatus used in enzymology or microbiology that employ isotopic techniques, such as radiorespirometers and scintillometers, to detect or measure biological activity through radioactive tracers. These devices enable quantitative analysis of metabolic or enzymatic processes by monitoring emitted radiation.
[C12M 1/3446] Photometry, spectroscopy, laser technology -- The CPC code C12M 1/3446 is placed under Chemistry, Biochemistry and Apparatus for Enzymology or Microbiology and relates to instruments that measure optical properties of enzymatic or microbial samples. It covers photometric, spectroscopic and laser-based devices such as spectrophotometers, fluorometers and laser scanners used for biochemical analysis.
[C12M 1/3453] Opacity, turbidity or light transmission measure; Nephelometry -- The C12M 1/3453 subclass covers apparatus and methods that measure opacity, turbidity or light transmission, particularly through nephelometric techniques. It includes devices that assess particle concentration or scattering of light in samples for enzymology or microbiology applications. This classification groups instruments used to evaluate cloudiness or clarity of liquids in biochemical analysis.
[C12M 1/3461] Bio- or chemi-luminescence -- C12M 1/3461 covers devices that generate or detect light produced by biological or chemical reactions, such as bioluminescent enzymes or chemiluminescent reagents, for use in enzymology or microbiology experiments. The apparatus may include reaction chambers, light-emitting substrates, detectors, and associated control or read-out systems designed to measure luminescence signals.
[C12M 1/3469] Infra red spectroscopy -- Infrared spectroscopy in the context of enzymology or microbiology refers to the use of IR radiation to analyze molecular vibrations of enzymes, microorganisms or related biochemical samples, enabling identification of functional groups and structural features. The technique is employed in specialized apparatus for studying biochemical reactions, assessing purity, monitoring conformational changes and detecting metabolic products. It forms part of the analytical methods classified under CPC code C12M 1/3469.
[C12M 1/3476] Fluorescence spectroscopy -- C12M 1/3476 denotes apparatus for enzymology or microbiology that employs fluorescence spectroscopy to detect, quantify or monitor biological samples. The classification covers devices using excitation-induced emission, such as fluorometers, spectrofluorometers and related optical systems, for analysing enzymes, cells, microbes or biomolecules. It includes components for light sources, detectors, filters and sample handling designed specifically for fluorescence-based assays.
[C12M 1/3484] Pen or contact colony counters -- C12M 1/3484 designates pen or contact colony counters, handheld instruments that touch individual microbial colonies on agar plates to record their number without staining or imaging. These devices enable rapid, manual enumeration of colonies in microbiology and related enzymology experiments.
[C12M 1/3492] with use of lecture and interpretation devices, grids -- The CPC subclass C12M 1/3492 covers laboratory apparatus used in enzymology or microbiology that incorporates lecture and interpretation devices such as grids for visualising or analysing data, typically for instructional or analytical purposes. It resides under chemistry, biochemistry and apparatus for enzymology or microbiology.
[C12M 1/36] including condition or time responsive control, e.g. automatically controlled fermentors -- C12M 1/36 covers apparatus for enzymology or microbiology that incorporates condition- or time-responsive control systems, such as fermentors that automatically adjust temperature, pH, nutrient feed or other parameters based on sensor inputs or preset schedules.
[C12M 1/38] Temperature-responsive control -- C12M 1/38 pertains to devices that modulate enzymatic or microbial activity by automatically adjusting operating conditions in response to temperature changes, such as thermostatic controllers, heating or cooling elements, and temperature-sensitive materials incorporated into reactors or incubators.
[C12M 1/40] Apparatus specially designed for the use of free, immobilised, or carrier-bound enzymes, e.g. apparatus containing a fluidised bed of immobilised enzymes -- This class covers devices specifically designed to hold free, immobilised or carrier-bound enzymes, such as reactors that contain a fluidised-bed of immobilised enzymes. The apparatus provides controlled environments for enzymatic reactions, ensuring effective mass transfer, temperature regulation and substrate contact.
[C12M 1/42] Apparatus for the treatment of microorganisms or enzymes with electrical or wave energy, e.g. magnetism, sonic waves -- C12M 1/42 covers devices that use electrical, magnetic, acoustic or other wave energy to treat microorganisms or enzymes, such as for sterilisation, activation or inactivation. The apparatus may apply fields, currents, electromagnetic radiation or sonic vibrations to influence biological activity.

[C12M 21/14] for producing enzymes -- C12M 21/14 covers devices or processes designed to produce enzymes, typically using bioreactors or fermenters that are specially adapted for enzymatic synthesis. The classification includes systems employing genetically engineered microorganisms, nucleic acid amplification, or waste-to-enzyme conversion, and falls within the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry and apparatus for enzymology or microbiology.
[C12M 21/16] Solid state fermenters, e.g. for koji production -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bioreactors or fermenters for solid state fermentation. Solid state fermentation is characterized by a content of at least 90% of dry matter. In the solid state fermentation the liquid phase is not continuous.  **Glossary:** - SSF: Solid-state fermentation - SLF: Submerged liquid fermentation - Non submerged state fermenter: Solid-state fermenter
[C12M 21/18] Apparatus specially designed for the use of free, immobilized or carrier-bound enzymes -- C12M 21/18 covers equipment built specifically to exploit free, immobilized or carrier-bound enzymes in biochemical processes. This includes reactors, columns or other devices that maintain optimal conditions for enzyme activity, facilitate substrate contact and allow product recovery.
[C12M 23/00] Constructional details, e.g. recesses, hinges  flow directing inserts in  C12M27/18 - C12M27/24 ; apparatus for chemical or physical processes in general  B01J , chemical or physical laboratory apparatus in general  B01L -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus or devices for culturing microorganisms or cells or for culturing tissues characterized by constructional details of the vessel of the bioreactor or characterized by parts with a mechanical function not covered by groups C12M25/00 - C12M41/00 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Flow directing inserts -> C12M27/18

[C12M 23/08] Flask, bottle or test tube -- This subclass covers laboratory containers such as flasks, bottles and test tubes used for carrying out chemical or biochemical reactions, including their constructional features like openings, supports and flow-directing inserts. It is placed under apparatus for enzymology or microbiology within chemistry and relates to broader categories B01J and B01L.
[C12M 23/10] Petri dish  crystallising dishes  B01L3/06 -- C12M 23/10 covers petri dishes and similar crystallising dishes used in laboratory settings for culturing microorganisms or growing crystals, emphasizing their constructional features such as recesses, hinges, and flow-directing inserts. It classifies these shallow, usually transparent containers that provide a controlled surface for biological or chemical processes.

[C12M 23/14] Bags -- C12M 23/14 covers bags employed in biochemical or microbiological processes, such as containers for enzymes, cultures, or reagents. The classification includes design features like recessed sections, hinges, or flow-directing inserts that facilitate handling, sealing, or fluid movement within the bag.
[C12M 23/16] Microfluidic devices; Capillary tubes  integrated microfluidic structures  B01L3/5027 ; microreactors  B01J19/0093 -- C12M 23/16 covers microfluidic devices and capillary tubes that incorporate integrated fluidic channels, chambers or reactors for carrying out chemical or biochemical reactions on a miniature scale. It includes designs of micro-reactors and lab-on-a-chip structures that direct flow, mix reagents or perform analysis within very small volumes.
[C12M 23/18] Open ponds; Greenhouse type or underground installations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  i.a. combinations of closed and open bioreactors.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Closed bioreactors which are situated in or on an open water space itself not used for culture -> C12M23/56
[C12M 23/20] Material Coatings  immunocoatings  C12M25/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Materials and coatings for the materials other than immunocoatings.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Immunocoatings -> C12M25/00
[C12M 23/22] Transparent or translucent parts  glassware for laboratory use  B01L3/00 -- The CPC entry C12M 23/22 covers transparent or translucent components of laboratory glassware, such as vessels, tubes or plates made of glass or similar materials that allow visual observation of reactions. It falls within the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy for apparatus used in enzymology or microbiology, addressing constructional details like recesses, hinges or flow-directing inserts in equipment for chemical or physical laboratory processes.
[C12M 23/24] Gas permeable parts -- Gas permeable parts are components of biochemical or microbiological equipment that allow selective passage of gases while retaining liquids, such as membranes, seals or filter elements. They are used in reactors, fermenters and culture vessels to provide controlled gas exchange and maintain sterile conditions.
[C12M 23/26] flexible  flexible containers for laboratory use  B01L3/505 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bags -> C12M23/14
[C12M 23/28] disposable or single use -- C12M 23/28 covers disposable or single-use apparatus used in enzymology or microbiology, such as containers, pipettes, or reactors that incorporate constructional details like recesses, hinges, or flow-directing inserts. The classification applies to devices designed for one-time use in chemical or physical laboratory processes, often falling under broader classes B01J and B01L.
[C12M 23/30] biodegradable -- C12M 23/30 covers apparatus and structural features whose components are designed to degrade biologically, such as biodegradable reactors, containers, hinges, recesses or flow-directing inserts used in enzymology or microbiology processes. The classification applies to devices where the material itself or its construction purposefully breaks down by microbial or enzymatic action during or after use.
[C12M 23/32] Frangible parts -- C12M 23/32 covers frangible parts of apparatus used in enzymology or microbiology, such as breakable seals, disposable inserts, or consumable components designed to split or be removed during operation. These elements facilitate safe handling, prevent contamination, or enable single-use functions in laboratory equipment.
[C12M 23/34] Internal compartments or partitions -- C12M 23/34 covers devices that incorporate internal compartments or partitions, such as separate chambers, inserts, or barriers within reactors or laboratory apparatus used for enzymology or microbiology. These structural elements allow distinct zones for reagents, organisms, or reaction conditions, facilitating controlled flow, segregation, or staged processing. The code is applied to designs where the partitioning is integral to the equipment's function.
[C12M 23/36] Means for collection or storage of gas; Gas holders -- The C12M 23/36 subclass covers devices and structures that collect, contain, or store gases, such as gas holders, within biochemical or microbiological apparatus. It includes designs for gas reservoirs, storage chambers, and related mechanisms that manage gas accumulation in laboratory or industrial settings.
[C12M 23/38] Caps; Covers; Plugs; Pouring means -- The C12M 23/38 subclass covers caps, covers, plugs and pouring devices that are part of apparatus used in enzymology or microbiology. These are constructional elements such as recesses, hinges or flow-directing inserts that seal or guide fluids in laboratory or process equipment.
[C12M 23/40] Manifolds; Distribution pieces  fluid transfer means  B01L3/563 -- The C12M 23/40 subclass covers manifolds and distribution pieces used for transferring fluids in chemical or biochemical processes. It includes devices that direct, split, combine or regulate the flow of liquids or gases, such as pipe assemblies, connectors and flow-directing inserts within laboratory or industrial apparatus.
[C12M 23/42] Integrated assemblies, e.g. cassettes or cartridges -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bioreactors or fermenters built as integral units comprising all the different parts disposed in a compact unit. This compact unit comprising all parts of the bioreactor can be in the form of a cassette or a cartridge which could also be assembled with other devices.
[C12M 23/44] Multiple separable units; Modules -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bioreactors or fermenters comprising separated or separable units which can be mounted or dismounted independently from each other.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Reaction vessels connected in series or in parallel -> C12M23/58
[C12M 23/46] Means for fastening -- The C12M 23/46 subclass covers fastening elements used in laboratory equipment for enzymology or microbiology, such as clamps, brackets, screws, hinges and other devices that secure or attach components of the apparatus. These means enable assembly, adjustment or disassembly of instruments that perform chemical or physical processes in biochemical work, and are considered constructional details within the broader categories of chemical laboratory apparatus.
[C12M 23/48] Holding appliances; Racks; Supports  holding devices for laboratory apparatus  B01L9/00 -- C12M 23/48 covers holding appliances such as racks, supports and other devices that secure laboratory apparatus, especially in biochemical or microbiological settings. It pertains to constructional details like recesses, hinges or flow-directing inserts used to position equipment during chemical or physical processes, linking to broader categories B01J and B01L.
