[C12N 15/1041] Ribosome/Polysome display, e.g. SPERT, ARM -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Method for selecting high-affinity polypeptide ligands that specifically bind target molecules by ribosome/polysome display.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - mRNA display -> C12N15/1062 - DNA display -> C12N15/1068
[C12N 15/1044] Preparation or screening of libraries displayed on scaffold proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation or screening a library of polypeptides comprising a scaffold-based molecule comprising loop domains, e.g. constructing or screening a library of a scaffold-based proteins which are derived from a stability enhanced consensus sequence of a fibronectin type III (FN3) domain incorporating randomized codons in order to produce polypeptide variants.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Library of scaffold based polypeptides -> C40B40/00
[C12N 15/1048] SELEX -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Aptamers per se -> C12N15/115 - Peptide aptamers, i.e. build from aminoacids rather than nucleotidesamino acids rather than nucleotides -> C07K - Library of aptamers -> C40B40/00  **Glossary:** - SELEX: Systematic Evolution of Ligands by EXponential amplification - Aptamers: Nucleic acid ligands
[C12N 15/1051] Gene trapping, e.g. exon-, intron-, IRES-, signal sequence-trap cloning, trap vectors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Method of creating or screening a library of gene sequences by gene-trap cloning, isolation of gene sequences by gene-trap cloning, e.g. exons, introns, promoters, enhancer, signal sequences, IRES-, IME-sequences.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparation and screening expression libraries using reporter assays -> C12N15/1086 - Trap vectors per se -> C12N15/70 - C12N15/82 , C12N15/85 - C12N15/86  **Glossary:** - IRES: Internal Ribosome Entry Site - IME: sequence responsible for Intron Mediated Enhancement of gene expression
[C12N 15/1055] Protein x Protein interaction, e.g. two hybrid selection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparation or screening expression libraries, e.g. reporter assays -> C12N15/1086
[C12N 15/1058] Directional evolution of libraries, e.g. evolution of libraries is achieved by mutagenesis and screening or selection of mixed population of organisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Method for the preparation, screening and isolation of gene sequences by a methods of directed molecular evolution using the modified polynucleotide libraries, e.g. cellular transformation, directed evolution, and screening methods for creating novel transgenic organisms having desirable properties, method of screening gene libraries derived from a mixed population of organisms for a bioactivity of biomolecule of interest.
[C12N 15/1062] mRNA-Display, e.g. polypeptide and encoding template are connected covalently -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Linking phenotype (polypeptide) covalently to mRNA (genotype) and screening libraries of such polypeptide-mRNA-display molecules for activity to identify single library member (polynucleotide sequence) that bind with a target molecule.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Polypeptide non-covalently bound to mRNA -> C12N15/1075
[C12N 15/1065] Preparation or screening of tagged libraries, e.g. tagged microorganisms by STM-mutagenesis, tagged polynucleotides, gene tags -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - RBS-assay: Replica Barcode Selection assay - GIS: Gene Identification Signature - STM: Signature-Tagged-Mutagenesis
[C12N 15/1068] Template (nucleic acid) mediated chemical library synthesis, e.g. chemical and enzymatical DNA-templated organic molecule synthesis, libraries prepared by non ribosomal polypeptide synthesis [NRPS], DNA/RNA-polymerase mediated polypeptide synthesis -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[C12N 15/1072] Differential gene expression library synthesis, e.g. subtracted libraries, differential screening -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[C12N 15/1075] by coupling phenotype to genotype, not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In vitro method of preparation or screening of libraries in cell-free compartmentalization systems (IVC), e.g. screening libraries prepared in emulsions (water in oil), droplets in contact with oil; directed evolution of polypeptide/enzyme (peptide display library) by in vitro compartmentalization;  covalent or non-covalent DNA-display.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cellular display -> C12N15/1037 - Ribosome/Polysome display -> C12N15/1041 - mRNA display -> C12N15/1062 - Library of templated molecules covalently or non-covalently linked to the encoded nuclei acid template, library of templated molecules linked to identifier oligonucleotides which have participated in the synthesis of said templated molecules -> C12N15/1068 - Libraries per se -> C40B  **Glossary:** - DNA display: Polypeptide is covalently or non-covalently bound to DNA - IVC: In vitro cell-free compartmentalization systems
[C12N 15/1079] Screening libraries by altering the phenotype or phenotypic trait of the host  reporter assays  C12N15/1086 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Method of identifying nuclei acids that contributes to cell phenotype or phenotypic traits, e.g. method for the identification of genes that are essential for the maintenance of specific cell phenotypes.
[C12N 15/1082] Preparation or screening gene libraries by chromosomal integration of polynucleotide sequences, HR-, site-specific-recombination, transposons, viral vectors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Method of identifying nuclei acids by preparation or screening a genome library in which at least one nucleic acid was stable integrated e.g. using HR, site-specific recombination, transposons, viral vectors, into the genome of the host cell, e.g. using methods for site-specifically integrating at least one first nucleic acid into a genome of at least one cell.  **Glossary:** - HR: Homologous Recombination
[C12N 15/1086] Preparation or screening of expression libraries, e.g. reporter assays -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Method of identifying nuclei acids by preparation or screening a library of nucleic acids using reporter assays, wherein the reporter confers a selectable phenotype on cells, e.g. lacZ, GFP, YFP, Luc.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gene-trapping -> C12N15/1051 - Libraries per se -> C40B  **Glossary:** - lacZ: Beta-galactosidase - GFP: Green Fluorescent Protein - YFP: Yellow Fluorescent Protein - Luc: Luciferase
[C12N 15/1089] Design, preparation, screening or analysis of libraries using computer algorithms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Method of identifying nuclei acids by preparation or screening a library of nucleic acids in which at least one step comprises the use of a computer algorithm or an in silico step e.g. to align nucleotide sequences, in silico recombination techniques by designing oligonucleotides for regulated recombination.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Libraries per se -> C40B
[C12N 15/1093] General methods of preparing gene libraries, not provided for in other subgroups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Method of identifying nuclei acids by preparation or screening a library of nucleic acids/host cells not provided for in other subgroups.
[C12N 15/1096] cDNA Synthesis; Subtracted cDNA library construction, e.g. RT, RT-PCR -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Method for preparing cDNA by reverse translation of RNA, e.g. preparation and cloning DNA into a vector in which the reverse transcription is an essential feature of the process.
[C12N 15/11] DNA or RNA fragments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Natural or synthetic nucleic acids used in biotechnology and genetic engineering.  Subgroups C12N15/11 - C12N15/117 also cover the use of non-coding nucleic acids as active ingredients medicinal preparations  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - DNA or RNA not used in recombinant technology. -> C07H21/00 - Nucleotides and nucleosides per se and modified forms thereof. -> C07H21/00 - Encoding nucleic acids, i.e. genes, with the exception of operons. -> C07K14/00  **Glossary:** - DNA: DeoxyriboNucleic Acid - RNA: RiboNucleic Acid
[C12N 15/111] General methods applicable to biologically active non-coding nucleic acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and processes of general interest for one or more class(es) of non-coding nucleic acids.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - The products per se -> C12N15/11 , C12N15/113 - C12N15/117
[C12N 15/113] Non-coding nucleic acids modulating the expression of genes, e.g. antisense oligonucleotides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Nucleic acids having a direct effect on the expression of a gene or the transcription of its messenger such as Antisense nucleic acids, Catalytic nucleic acids, e.g. ribozymes, Triplex-forming oligonucleotides, Decoys, Nucleic acids used in gene silencing or RNA interference, or MicroRNAs.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Regulatory sequences being part of genes, e.g. promoters or terminators -> C12N15/63 - C12N15/90 - Their use in plants -> C12N15/8218 - Encoding nucleic acids, i.e. genes -> C07K14/00 , C12N9/00 - Probes and primers -> C12Q  **Glossary:** - Antisense nucleic acid: DNA or RNA composed of the complementary sequence to the target DNA/RNA - Ribozyme: Nucleic acid with a catalytic activity - MicroRNA: Small double-stranded RNA (21-25 nucleotides) generated from a precursor transcript by cleavage with Dicer - Decoy oligonucleotide: A (double-stranded) oligonucleotide comprising a binding-site for a protein - Gene silencing: Interruption or suppression of the expression of a gene at transcriptional or translational levels.
[C12N 15/1131] against viruses -- C12N 15/1131 relates to biotechnological inventions wherein microorganisms, enzymes or other biological materials are genetically modified to act against viruses, including the creation of antiviral agents, virus-resistant strains, and vaccine constructs. The code covers methods, compositions and cells engineered for prophylaxis or therapy of viral diseases.
[C12N 15/1132] against retroviridae, e.g. HIV -- This CPC subclass pertains to biotechnological methods, compositions or genetic modifications aimed at combating retroviridae, exemplified by HIV, and includes antiviral agents, vaccines and engineered microorganisms designed for such therapeutic or preventive purposes.
[C12N 15/1133] against herpetoviridae, e.g. HSV -- The CPC subclass C12N 15/1133 covers therapeutic agents, compositions or genetically engineered microorganisms specifically designed to act against viruses of the Herpetoviridae family, such as herpes simplex virus. It falls under the broader category of mutation or genetic engineering of microorganisms or enzymes for biochemical applications.
[C12N 15/1135] against oncogenes or tumor suppressor genes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-coding nucleic acids directed against targets directly involved in the oncogenic processes, e.g. when their expression or activity is de-regulated.  **Glossary:** - Oncogenes: Genes whose gain-of-function alterations lead to neoplastic cell transformation - Tumour suppressor genes: Genes that inhibit formation and/or development of the tumorigenic phenotype
[C12N 15/1136] against growth factors, growth regulators, cytokines, lymphokines or hormones -- C12N 15/1136 covers biotechnological inventions that provide agents or processes for inhibiting or neutralising growth factors, growth regulators, cytokines, lymphokines or hormones, such as antibodies, inhibitors, or genetically engineered microorganisms designed to block their activity.
[C12N 15/1137] against enzymes  viral enzymes  C12N15/1131 ; receptors  C12N15/1138 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-coding nucleic acids directed to nucleic acids encoding proteins with an enzymatic activity.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Viral enzymes -> C12N15/1131 - Receptors having also an enzymatic domain -> C12N15/1138
[C12N 15/1138] against receptors or cell surface proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Nucleic acids against binding proteins other than receptors -> C12N15/113
[C12N 15/115] Aptamers, i.e. nucleic acids binding a target molecule specifically and with high affinity without hybridising therewith -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Nucleic acids binding a target molecule specifically and with high affinity without hybridising therewith.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Peptide aptamers, i.e. built from aminoacids rather than nucleotides. -> C07K
[C12N 15/117] Nucleic acids having immunomodulatory properties, e.g. containing CpG-motifs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-coding nucleic acids having a direct impact on the immune system.
[C12N 15/52] Genes encoding for enzymes or proenzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Operons: DNA constructs containing a cluster of genes under the control of a single regulatory signal or promoter.  Groups of genes for enzymes providing an organism with the ability to synthesize a specific compound or compounds (e.g. synthetic (partial) pathways)  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Genes coding for single enzymes -> C12N9/00
[C12N 15/62] DNA sequences coding for fusion proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods to produce a fusion protein by recombinant DNA technology  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fusion proteins per se and the nucleic acids encoding them. -> C12N9/00 , C07K2319/00 , C07K14/00 , C07K16/00
[C12N 15/625] containing a sequence coding for a signal sequence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for producing, by recombinant DNA technology, a fusion protein in which one of the fused polypeptides consists of a signal sequence with or without (part of) its mature protein.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Fusion proteins per se -> C12N9/00 , C07K2319/00 , C07K14/00 , C07K16/00  **Glossary:** - Fusion protein: A polypeptide consisting of (parts of) two or more different proteins covalently linked to each other by a peptide bond - Signal sequence: A 3-60 amino acid long peptide that directs the transport of the protein that is attached to it
[C12N 15/63] Introduction of foreign genetic material using vectors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General method for regulating (enhancing, inhibiting) the gene expression by modifying the operator, enhancer or promoter dependent transcription of the messenger RNA.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Ribosome mediated translational regulation of gene expression -> C12N15/67
[C12N 15/635] Externally inducible repressor mediated regulation of gene expression, e.g. tetR inducible by tetracyline -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General method for regulating (enhancing, inhibiting) the gene expression by modifying/regulating the repressor or inducer mediated transcription of the messenger RNA.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Ribosome mediated translational regulation of gene expression -> C12N15/67
[C12N 15/64] General methods for preparing the vector, for introducing it into the cell or for selecting the vector-containing host -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General methods for generating vectors for use in recombinant technology i.e. cloning methods for preparing a general vector. A general vector is to be understood as being one which is independent of the origin of replication. Methods for specifically preparing e.g. a plant, viral or mammalian vector, wherein the method of preparation is restricted to and applicable only in e.g. a plant, virus or mammal are classified in their corresponding vector group.
[C12N 15/65] using markers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General methods for preparing the vector, for introducing it into the cell or for selecting the vector-containing host using polypeptides as marker molecules, wherein the polypeptides do not comprise enzymatic activity.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Enzymes used as marker molecules -> C12N15/52
[C12N 15/66] General methods for inserting a gene into a vector to form a recombinant vector using cleavage and ligation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General method for preparing a recombinant vector using cleavage and ligation.  **Glossary:** - Non-functional linkers: DNA sequences which are used to link DNA sequences and which have no known function of structural gene or regulating function.
[C12N 15/67] General methods for enhancing the expression -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General method for ribosome mediated regulating (i.e. enhancing, inhibiting) the gene expression by modifying the ribosomal mediated translation of the messenger RNA.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Transcriptional regulation of gene expression -> C12N15/63 , C12N15/635
[C12N 15/68] Stabilisation of the vector -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General method of enhancing the expression a nucleic acid sequence, by stabilizing the vector in the host cell, e.g. preserving DNA in a stable form over time, temperature, culture conditions.
[C12N 15/69] Increasing the copy number of the vector -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General method of enhancing the expression a nucleic acid sequence, by increasing the copy number of the vector in the host cell, e.g. conditions that results in an increase in plasmid copy number in comparison to a control plasmid, e.g. mutation in the copy number control region.
[C12N 15/70] Vectors or expression systems specially adapted for E. coli -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for E.coli, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in Escherichia coli.  **Glossary:** - Shuttle vector: Vector comprising at least two different origins of replication
[C12N 15/71] Expression systems using regulatory sequences derived from the trp-operon -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for E.coli, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in Escherichia coli, and comprising regulatory sequences from the trp-operon.  **Glossary:** - Trp-operon: Tryprophan-operon - Operon: Set of neighbouring prokaryotic genes whose transcription is simultaneously controlled
[C12N 15/72] Expression systems using regulatory sequences derived from the lac-operon -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for E.coli, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in Escherichia coli, and comprising regulatory sequences from the lac-operon.  **Glossary:** - Lac-operon: Lactose-operon
[C12N 15/73] Expression systems using phage (lambda) regulatory sequences -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for E.coli, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in Escherichia coli, and comprising regulatory sequences from the phage (lambda) regulatory sequences.
[C12N 15/74] Vectors or expression systems specially adapted for prokaryotic hosts other than E. coli, e.g. Lactobacillus, Micromonospora -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for prokaryotic hosts other than E.coli, i.e. comprising at last one origin of replication working in prokaryotic hosts other than E. coli, e.g. Lactobacillus, Micromonospora.
[C12N 15/743] for Agrobacterium; Rhizobium; Bradyrhizobium -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for prokaryotic hosts other than E.coli, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in prokaryotic hosts other than E. coli, e.g. for Agrobacterium, Rhizobium, Bradyrhizobium.
[C12N 15/746] for lactic acid bacteria (Streptococcus; Lactococcus; Lactobacillus; Pediococcus; Enterococcus; Leuconostoc; Propionibacterium; Bifidobacterium; Sporolactobacillus) -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for prokaryotic hosts other than E.coli, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in prokaryotic hosts other than E. coli, e.g.: for lactic acid bacteria (Streptococcus; Lactococcus; Lactobacillus; Pediococcus; Enterococcus; Leuconostoc; Propionibacterium; Bifidobacterium; Sporolactobacillus).
[C12N 15/75] for Bacillus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for prokaryotic hosts other than E.coli, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in prokaryotic hosts other than E. coli, e. g. for Bacillus.
[C12N 15/76] for Actinomyces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for prokaryotic hosts other than E.coli, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in prokaryotic hosts other than E. coli, e. g. for Actinomyces; for Streptomyces.
[C12N 15/77] for Corynebacterium -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for prokaryotic hosts other than E.coli, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in prokaryotic hosts other than E. coli, e. g. Corynebacterium, for Brevibacterium.
[C12N 15/78] for Pseudomonas -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for prokaryotic hosts other than E.coli, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in prokaryotic hosts other than E. coli, e. g. Pseudomonas.
[C12N 15/79] Vectors or expression systems specially adapted for eukaryotic hosts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for eukaryotic hosts, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in eukaryotic hosts, and which can not be classified in the subgroups below C12N15/80 – C12N15/86 .
[C12N 15/80] for fungi -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for eukaryotic hosts, i.e. comprising at least origin of replication working in eukaryotic hosts, e.g. fungi.
[C12N 15/81] for yeasts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for eukaryotic hosts, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in eukaryotic hosts, e.g. yeast.
[C12N 15/815] for yeasts other than Saccharomyces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All application dealing with vectors especially adapted for eukaryotic hosts, i.e. comprising at least one origin of replication working in eukaryotic hosts, e.g. other than Saccharomyces.
[C12N 15/82] for plant cells -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[C12N 15/8201] Methods for introducing genetic material into plant cells, e.g. DNA, RNA, stable or transient incorporation, tissue culture methods adapted for transformation -- This subclass covers techniques used to deliver nucleic acids into plant cells, including DNA and RNA, through stable or transient incorporation, often employing tissue-culture-based transformation methods. It includes approaches such as Agrobacterium-mediated transfer, biolistics, electroporation and other protocols designed for plant genetic modification.
[C12N 15/8202] by biological means, e.g. cell mediated or natural vector -- C12N 15/8202 covers processes that generate a product through biological means, such as using living cells or natural vectors, rather than purely chemical synthesis. It is applied to methods that employ cell-mediated transformation, infection or symbiosis to introduce genetic changes or produce desired compounds in microorganisms or enzymes. This subgroup falls under mutation or genetic engineering within the broader chemistry and biochemistry fields.
[C12N 15/8203] Virus mediated transformation -- It covers methods that use viruses as vectors to introduce genetic material into host cells for the purpose of genetic modification. The class includes design, production and application of viral vectors for transforming microorganisms, plant or animal cells and related techniques in mutation or genetic engineering.
[C12N 15/8205] Agrobacterium mediated transformation -- C12N 15/8205 covers the use of Agrobacterium tumefaciens to transfer foreign DNA into plant cells, a method that exploits the bacterium's natural T-DNA transfer system for genetic modification of plants. This technique is fundamental in plant biotechnology for research, trait improvement and crop development.
[C12N 15/8206] by physical or chemical, i.e. non-biological, means, e.g. electroporation, PEG mediated -- The subclass C12N 15/8206 covers methods for mutating or genetically engineering microorganisms or enzymes by non-biological techniques, such as electroporation, polyethylene-glycol treatment or other physical and chemical means, rather than using biological vectors. These approaches effect the introduction or alteration of genetic material through external physical or chemical forces.
[C12N 15/8207] by mechanical means, e.g. microinjection, particle bombardment, silicon whiskers -- Techniques that introduce nucleic acids or modify microorganisms by applying physical force, such as microinjection, biolistic particle bombardment, or silicon-whisker methods, to achieve mutation or genetic engineering. This covers mechanical means of delivering genetic material into cells.
[C12N 15/8209] Selection, visualisation of transformants, reporter constructs, e.g. antibiotic resistance markers -- C12N 15/8209 covers methods for selecting and visualising transformed microorganisms, typically by employing reporter constructs such as antibiotic-resistance markers or visual markers that indicate successful genetic modification. It includes techniques for identifying transformants after mutation or genetic-engineering procedures.
[C12N 15/821] Non-antibiotic resistance markers, e.g. morphogenetic, metabolic markers -- C12N 15/821 covers the use of non-antibiotic markers for selecting genetically modified microorganisms, including morphogenetic and metabolic traits that confer a detectable phenotype without relying on antibiotic resistance. These markers facilitate screening and maintenance of engineered strains in research and industrial processes.
[C12N 15/8212] Colour markers, e.g. beta-glucoronidase [GUS], green fluorescent protein [GFP], carotenoid -- C12N 15/8212 covers the use of colour-forming markers, such as β-glucuronidase (GUS), green fluorescent protein (GFP), carotenoids and similar reporters, for detecting or visualising genetic changes, expression or enzymatic activity in microorganisms or engineered cells. The markers serve as visual indicators of mutation, gene transfer or metabolic activity.
[C12N 15/8213] Targeted insertion of genes into the plant genome by homologous recombination -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for DNA/chromosomal rearrangements
[C12N 15/8214] Plastid transformation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mitochondrial transformation
[C12N 15/8216] Methods for controlling, regulating or enhancing expression of transgenes in plant cells -- C12N 15/8216 refers to techniques that manipulate the expression of introduced genes in plant cells, encompassing strategies such as promoter or enhancer use, RNA interference, gene editing and other regulatory methods to increase, decrease or otherwise control transgene activity for research, breeding or crop improvement.
[C12N 15/8217] Gene switch -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Multiple cis/trans cascaded systems
[C12N 15/8218] Antisense, co-suppression, viral induced gene silencing [VIGS], post-transcriptional induced gene silencing [PTGS] -- The CPC subclass C12N 15/8218 covers techniques that silence genes in microorganisms or enzymes using antisense molecules, co-suppression, viral-induced gene silencing (VIGS) and post-transcriptional gene silencing (PTGS). It addresses engineered genetic modifications that suppress target gene expression after transcription, enabling functional studies and trait control.
[C12N 15/822] Reducing position variability, e.g. by the use of scaffold attachment region/matrix attachment region (SAR/MAR); Use of SAR/MAR to regulate gene expression -- C12N 15/822 covers techniques for minimizing gene-position effects in genetically engineered microorganisms, particularly by employing scaffold-attachment or matrix-attachment regions (SAR/MAR) to stabilize where inserted DNA integrates. The use of SAR/MAR elements is described for regulating and making gene expression more consistent across different integration sites.
[C12N 15/8221] Transit peptides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Signal peptides
[C12N 15/8222] Developmentally regulated expression systems, tissue, organ specific, temporal or spatial regulation
[C12N 15/8223] Vegetative tissue-specific promoters -- This CPC subclass covers nucleic-acid sequences that act as vegetative-tissue-specific promoters, driving gene expression only in plant parts such as leaves, stems or roots. It includes both engineered and naturally occurring promoters and their variants used in mutation or genetic-engineering applications.
[C12N 15/8225] Leaf-specific, e.g. including petioles, stomata -- The code C12N 15/8225 covers genetically engineered microorganisms, enzymes or nucleic-acid constructs that are designed to act specifically on leaf tissues, including structures such as petioles and stomata, for purposes like expression, delivery, or modification of plant functions.
[C12N 15/8226] Stem-specific, e.g. including tubers, beets -- This CPC subclass covers genetic-engineering methods that create stem-specific traits in plants, including storage organs such as tubers and beets, by using modified microorganisms or enzymes to introduce or regulate genes in stem tissue.
[C12N 15/8227] Root-specific
[C12N 15/8229] Meristem-specific, e.g. nodal, apical -- C12N 15/8229 pertains to the genetic manipulation of microorganisms or enzymes that are applied specifically to plant meristems, such as nodal or apical regions, to induce desired traits. It falls under mutation or genetic engineering within biochemistry and covers methods and compositions targeting these meristematic sites.
[C12N 15/823] Reproductive tissue-specific promoters -- C12N 15/823 relates to promoters that are active primarily in reproductive tissues, enabling tissue-specific gene expression in microorganisms or engineered cells. These promoters are used in genetic engineering to control when and where a gene is expressed, facilitating precise manipulation of reproductive pathways.
[C12N 15/8231] Male-specific, e.g. anther, tapetum, pollen -- C12N 15/8231 refers to inventions dealing with male-specific plant tissues such as anthers, tapetum and pollen, including their modification, use or engineering in biotechnological applications. It is positioned under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and specifically the mutation or genetic engineering subclass.
[C12N 15/8233] Female-specific, e.g. pistil, ovule -- C12N 15/8233 refers to inventions that engineer genetic material or microorganisms to produce traits expressed only in female plant reproductive organs such as pistils or ovules. It covers methods, constructs, or modified organisms designed to alter or confer female-specific characteristics in plants.
[C12N 15/8234] Seed-specific, e.g. embryo, endosperm -- C12N 15/8234 covers biotechnological inventions in which a mutation or genetically engineered trait is designed to be active only in seed tissues such as the embryo or endosperm. It includes methods, constructs and organisms that confer seed-specific expression of proteins, enzymes or other functional products to modify seed characteristics, yield or composition.
[C12N 15/8235] Fruit-specific -- C12N 15/8235 designates patent subject matter involving the use of genetically modified microorganisms or enzymes that are engineered specifically for fruit-related processes, such as improving fruit quality, extending shelf life, or facilitating fruit processing. It covers methods of mutation, recombinant DNA techniques, and the resulting biological material tailored to fruit applications.
[C12N 15/8237] Externally regulated expression systems -- C12N 15/8237 covers systems that enable the controlled expression of genes or enzymes in microorganisms by using external signals such as chemicals, temperature, light or other stimuli. The classification includes inducible promoters, regulatory circuits and related methods that allow on-demand production of desired biomolecules.
[C12N 15/8238] chemically inducible, e.g. tetracycline -- The CPC subclass C12N 15/8238 covers genetically engineered microorganisms or enzymes whose activity or gene expression is regulated by a chemical inducer, most notably tetracycline-controlled systems. It includes compositions, vectors and methods that use such inducible elements to switch on or off target functions in response to the inducer.
[C12N 15/8239] pathogen inducible -- C12N 15/8239 covers genetic modifications that enable a microorganism or cell to activate or increase expression of a gene or pathway when a pathogen is detected. The claim typically involves promoter elements, regulatory sequences or engineered circuits that respond specifically to pathogen-derived signals, allowing inducible production of proteins, metabolites or defensive traits.
[C12N 15/8241] Phenotypically and genetically modified plants via recombinant DNA technology -- C12N 15/8241 covers the creation and use of plants that have been altered both phenotypically and at the DNA level through recombinant DNA techniques, encompassing methods for inserting, deleting or editing genes to confer new traits. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Mutation or genetic engineering, focusing on plant biotechnology applications.
[C12N 15/8242] with non-agronomic quality (output) traits, e.g. for industrial processing; Value added, non-agronomic traits -- **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: Output trait influences the output products of plants or their parts
[C12N 15/8243] involving biosynthetic or metabolic pathways, i.e. metabolic engineering, e.g. nicotine, caffeine -- C12N 15/8243 covers inventions that engineer biosynthetic or metabolic pathways in microorganisms or enzymes to produce target compounds, exemplified by metabolic engineering of routes for nicotine, caffeine or similar metabolites. It includes methods for modifying genes, enzymes or regulatory networks to optimise synthesis, degradation or conversion of such natural products.
[C12N 15/8245] involving modified carbohydrate or sugar alcohol metabolism, e.g. starch biosynthesis -- The subclass captures inventions that genetically modify the metabolism of carbohydrates or sugar-alcohols in microorganisms or engineered cells, such as altering enzymes, regulatory elements or pathways to affect starch biosynthesis or related polyol production. It includes methods for creating, expressing or optimizing mutant genes that change the synthesis, degradation or conversion of these sugars.
[C12N 15/8246] Non-starch polysaccharides, e.g. cellulose, fructans, levans -- C12N 15/8246 covers non-starch polysaccharides such as cellulose, fructans and levans, focusing on their use, modification or production through microbial or enzymatic processes. It falls under the CPC hierarchy under chemistry to biochemistry to microorganisms or enzymes to mutation or genetic engineering, and pertains to biotechnological applications involving these polysaccharides.
[C12N 15/8247] involving modified lipid metabolism, e.g. seed oil composition -- C12N 15/8247 covers methods and organisms engineered to modify lipid biosynthesis, such as altering the fatty-acid profile of plant seeds to produce desired oil compositions. It includes genetic alterations that affect enzymes or pathways involved in lipid metabolism, enabling production of novel or improved seed oils with specific nutritional or industrial properties.
[C12N 15/8249] involving ethylene biosynthesis, senescence or fruit development, e.g. modified tomato ripening, cut flower shelf-life -- The CPC subclass C12N 15/8249 pertains to biotechnological inventions that modify ethylene biosynthesis to influence plant senescence, fruit development or ripening, such as genetically engineered tomatoes with altered ripening or cut flowers with extended shelf-life. It covers methods and organisms engineered for these ethylene-related traits.
[C12N 15/825] involving pigment biosynthesis -- C12N 15/825 covers genetic modifications of microorganisms or enzymes that affect the biosynthesis of pigments. It includes recombinant strains engineered to over-produce natural colorants, pathways altered to create novel pigments, and related methods for cultivating such modified organisms.
[C12N 15/8251] Amino acid content, e.g. synthetic storage proteins, altering amino acid biosynthesis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Modulating protein content
[C12N 15/8253] Methionine or cysteine -- C12N 15/8253 covers genetic engineering techniques that introduce, replace, or modify the amino acids methionine or cysteine in microorganisms or enzymes. The classification is used for patents describing mutations that alter biosynthetic pathways, protein stability, or functional properties by targeting these sulfur-containing residues.
[C12N 15/8254] Tryptophan or lysine -- This CPC subclass covers biotechnological methods, engineered microorganisms or enzymes, and related processes for the production, modification, or use of the amino acids tryptophan or lysine, including genetic modifications that enhance their synthesis or incorporate them into novel compounds.
[C12N 15/8255] involving lignin biosynthesis -- The CPC entry C12N 15/8255 covers biotechnological methods that involve mutating or genetically engineering microorganisms or enzymes to produce or modify lignin, including recombinant-DNA techniques, expression systems and related processes for lignin biosynthesis.
[C12N 15/8257] for the production of primary gene products, e.g. pharmaceutical products, interferon -- This subclass covers processes for producing primary gene products, such as therapeutic proteins and interferons, by using recombinant DNA techniques or genetically engineered microorganisms, including fermentation, cell-culture expression, and downstream purification steps.
[C12N 15/8258] for the production of oral vaccines (antigens) or immunoglobulins -- C12N 15/8258 covers biotechnological processes that employ mutated or genetically engineered microorganisms or enzymes to produce oral vaccines-antigens intended for ingestion-or immunoglobulins. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and specifically addresses production methods for orally administered immunological agents.
[C12N 15/8259] Phytoremediation -- C12N 15/8259 covers methods that employ genetically modified or naturally occurring microorganisms and enzymes in plants to remove, stabilize or detoxify environmental pollutants. The classification includes techniques such as the expression of degradative enzymes in plants, accumulation of heavy metals, and the use of plant-microbe systems for soil or water clean-up. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and mutation or genetic engineering.
[C12N 15/8261] with agronomic (input) traits, e.g. crop yield -- **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: Input trait influences the input required for growth and development of the plant or its parts
[C12N 15/8262] involving plant development -- C12N 15/8262 covers inventions that use mutation or genetic engineering techniques to modify or control plant development, including the creation of transgenic plants, targeted gene edits, and methods for altering growth, morphology, or reproductive traits. It applies to biotechnological processes that enable improved crops, novel plant varieties, or enhanced agronomic characteristics through engineered genetic changes.
[C12N 15/8263] Ablation; Apoptosis -- C12N 15/8263 covers methods and compositions for inducing ablation or programmed cell death in microorganisms or enzymes through mutation or genetic engineering. The entry includes techniques that trigger apoptosis as a means to eliminate or modify target cells, and related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 15/8265] Transgene containment, e.g. gene dispersal -- The code refers to techniques for preventing the release or spread of transgenes from genetically engineered microorganisms, including physical, biological or molecular strategies that contain or limit gene dispersal. It addresses containment measures applied to engineered microbes in biotechnological processes.
[C12N 15/8266] Abscission; Dehiscence; Senescence -- C12N 15/8266 covers technologies that control or exploit the natural processes of abscission, dehiscence and senescence in organisms, especially plants. It includes genetic engineering, mutation or biochemical methods that induce or inhibit tissue separation, organ dropping, seed release or programmed cell death, as well as related compositions and treatment regimes. This subclass is used for inventions aimed at managing crop yield, harvest timing, seed dispersal or lifespan of biological products.
[C12N 15/8267] Seed dormancy, germination or sprouting -- C12N 15/8267 refers to inventions that address seed dormancy, germination or sprouting, including methods, agents or genetic modifications that break dormancy, stimulate or control germination, and improve seedling emergence, primarily for agricultural or biotechnological applications. It falls within the mutation or genetic engineering subclass of microorganisms or enzymes in biochemistry.
[C12N 15/8269] Photosynthesis -- C12N 15/8269 addresses the genetic modification of microorganisms or enzymes to carry out or improve photosynthetic functions, including engineered pathways for light-driven carbon fixation, pigment synthesis, and energy conversion.
[C12N 15/827] Flower development or morphology, e.g. flowering promoting factor [FPF] -- C12N 15/827 covers inventions that modify or control plant flowering by using genetically engineered microorganisms, enzymes or nucleic acids to produce or influence flowering-promoting factors. The classification applies to methods, compositions or vectors that affect flower development, morphology or timing through engineered biological agents. It is used for patents on biotechnological approaches to enhance or alter flowering traits in plants.
[C12N 15/8271] for stress resistance, e.g. heavy metal resistance -- The code C12N 15/8271 covers genetically modified microorganisms engineered to withstand environmental stresses, particularly resistance to toxic heavy metals. It pertains to the use of mutation or recombinant techniques to confer stress-tolerance traits, enabling applications such as bioremediation of contaminated sites or robust industrial bioprocesses.
[C12N 15/8273] for drought, cold, salt resistance -- C12N 15/8273 covers the use of genetic engineering or mutagenesis to give microorganisms or enzymes traits that improve survival or performance under water-deficit, low-temperature or high-salinity conditions, typically for agricultural, industrial or environmental applications. It falls within the chemistry-biochemistry branch dealing with microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 15/8274] for herbicide resistance -- C12N 15/8274 relates to the genetic modification of microorganisms or enzymes to confer resistance to herbicides. It includes engineered mutations or recombinant DNA constructs that enable the host to survive or degrade herbicidal agents, facilitating the development of herbicide-tolerant crops or biocontrol agents.
[C12N 15/8275] Glyphosate -- C12N 15/8275 covers glyphosate, a phosphonate herbicide that inhibits the enzyme EPSPS in plants. The classification applies to inventions dealing with the compound itself, its synthesis, formulation, or use in genetically engineered microorganisms or plants for weed control.
[C12N 15/8277] Phosphinotricin -- Phosphinotricin (also known as glufosinate) is a broad-spectrum herbicidal compound that inhibits glutamine synthetase and is used both as a weed control agent and as a selectable marker in genetically engineered microorganisms. The classification covers methods for producing phosphinotricin, engineering resistance genes, and employing the compound in mutation or genetic-engineering protocols involving microbes or enzymes.
[C12N 15/8278] Sulfonylurea -- C12N 15/8278 covers the use of sulfonylurea compounds in the genetic manipulation of microorganisms or enzymes, for example as selectable markers, gene-expression regulators or agents that confer resistance after mutation or engineered pathways. Sulfonylureas are organic molecules that can act as herbicides or antidiabetic drugs, but in this subclass their role is limited to biotechnology applications involving mutation or genetic engineering.
[C12N 15/8279] for biotic stress resistance, pathogen resistance, disease resistance -- C12N 15/8279 covers methods, compositions, or genetically engineered microorganisms aimed at increasing biotic stress resistance, including pathogen and disease resistance, in plants or other hosts. It includes the introduction or modification of genes that confer protective traits against pests and diseases.
[C12N 15/8281] for bacterial resistance -- C12N 15/8281 addresses methods and compositions for conferring resistance to bacteria, including genetic modifications that enable survival against antibiotics, biocides, or environmental stresses. It includes the engineering of resistance genes, regulatory elements, or pathways in bacterial strains for industrial or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 15/8282] for fungal resistance -- The C12N 15/8282 subclass covers genetic-engineering approaches that introduce or modify genes to give organisms, especially plants, resistance to fungal pathogens. It includes methods for constructing vectors, transformation techniques, and the resulting transgenic material designed to prevent fungal infection. This subclass falls under mutation or genetic engineering of microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 15/8283] for virus resistance -- C12N 15/8283 classifies inventions that provide virus resistance by genetically modifying microorganisms, enzymes or cells, typically through mutation, recombinant DNA or other engineering techniques. It covers methods, nucleic acid constructs and engineered strains designed to prevent viral infection in host organisms.
[C12N 15/8285] for nematode resistance -- The classification covers biotechnological inventions that use mutation or genetic engineering of microorganisms or enzymes to confer resistance against nematodes. It includes methods, engineered strains, or genetic constructs designed to protect plants, animals or other hosts from nematode damage.
[C12N 15/8286] for insect resistance -- C12N 15/8286 covers genetic engineering approaches that confer insect resistance, including the modification of microorganisms, enzymes or host organisms to produce traits that deter or kill insects. The entry addresses recombinant DNA constructs, transformed cells and related biotechnological methods aimed at protecting crops or other biological systems from insect damage.
[C12N 15/8287] for fertility modification, e.g. apomixis -- The CPC subclass C12N 15/8287 covers biotechnological inventions that modify an organism's fertility, for example by introducing or editing genes to induce apomixis or other reproductive changes. It includes methods, nucleic-acid constructs and engineered microorganisms designed to control or bypass sexual reproduction.
[C12N 15/8289] Male sterility -- C12N 15/8289 covers genetic alterations, mutations or engineered traits that render an organism unable to produce functional male gametes, thereby creating male-sterile lines for use in breeding, hybrid production or biotechnological applications. The classification includes methods for inducing, detecting or maintaining male sterility in microorganisms, plants or related biological systems.
[C12N 15/829] Female sterility -- C12N 15/829 covers biotechnological approaches that induce sterility in female organisms through genetic manipulation or other engineered means. It includes methods for creating or delivering genes, nucleic acids or other agents that disrupt female reproductive capability, often used for pest control, livestock breeding or research. The classification lies within mutation or genetic engineering of microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 15/8291] Hormone-influenced development -- C12N 15/8291 covers biotechnological methods that use hormones to direct the growth, differentiation or morphogenesis of microorganisms or enzymatic systems. It includes techniques that engineer or modify cells so that hormonal signals control developmental pathways, often for producing valuable metabolites or traits.
[C12N 15/8293] Abscisic acid [ABA] -- The CPC subclass C12N 15/8293 relates to abscisic acid (ABA), a plant hormone that regulates seed dormancy, germination and stress responses. It covers the chemical nature of ABA, its derivatives, methods of synthesis or genetic engineering to produce it, and its biotechnological applications in crops or other organisms.
[C12N 15/8294] Auxins -- C12N 15/8294 covers auxins, a class of plant hormones that regulate cell elongation, division and differentiation, and includes natural auxins such as indole-3-acetic acid as well as synthetic analogues used in agriculture and biotech. The subclass also addresses the genetic engineering of microorganisms or enzymes to produce or modify auxins for research, crop improvement or industrial applications.
[C12N 15/8295] Cytokinins -- C12N 15/8295 classifies cytokinin compounds, a group of plant hormones that promote cell division, growth and development. The entry covers chemical structures, synthesis routes, formulations and methods of using cytokinins in agriculture, horticulture or biotechnology, reflecting their role in regulating plant physiology.
[C12N 15/8297] Gibberellins; GA3 -- C12N 15/8297 covers biotechnological methods for the production and manipulation of gibberellins, particularly gibberellic acid (GA₃), using microorganisms or enzymes that have been mutated or genetically engineered. It includes processes that exploit recombinant DNA techniques or strain improvement to increase GA₃ yields for agricultural or industrial applications.
[C12N 15/8298] Brassinosteroids -- C12N 15/8298 covers brassinosteroids, a class of plant steroid hormones that regulate growth and development. The code applies to their chemical structures, biosynthesis, and methods of producing them by genetic engineering of microorganisms. It is situated in the CPC hierarchy under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and mutation or genetic engineering.
[C12N 15/85] for animal cells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Vectors are nucleic acid constructs capable of introducing genetic information into cells.This group covers vectors for animal cells ( C12N15/85 ), in particular for the production of transgenic animals ( C12N15/8509 ), and viral vectors ( C12N15/86 ).  Introduction of foreign genetic material using processes not otherwise provided for, e.g. co-transformation, cloning and microinjection are covered by C12N15/87 - C12N15/907 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Vectors specialised for bacterial cells -> C12N15/70 - C12N15/78 - Vectors specialised for yeast cells -> C12N15/80 - C12N15/81 - Vectors specialised for plant cells -> C12N15/82
[C12N 15/8509] for producing genetically modified animals, e.g. transgenic -- This subclass covers biotechnological processes for creating genetically modified or transgenic animals, including techniques such as gene insertion, embryo manipulation, and related methods used to introduce foreign genetic material into animal genomes.
[C12N 15/86] Viral vectors -- C12N 15/86 covers viral vectors, which are genetically modified viruses employed to transfer nucleic acids into host cells for purposes such as gene therapy, vaccine development, or functional genomics. The classification addresses the design, production, and use of these engineered viruses, focusing on their role in delivering therapeutic or experimental genetic material.
[C12N 15/861] Adenoviral vectors -- This CPC subclass deals with adenoviral vectors, which are genetically modified adenoviruses used to deliver DNA into host cells for research, vaccine development or gene therapy. It falls under chemistry - biochemistry - microorganisms or enzymes - mutation or genetic engineering and covers vector construction, payload insertion and functional modifications.
[C12N 15/8613] Chimaeric vector systems comprising heterologous sequences for production of another viral vector -- C12N 15/8613 covers chimaeric vector systems that incorporate heterologous DNA segments to enable the production of a second viral vector, often employed in gene-therapy or vaccine applications. It relates to engineered microorganisms whose genetic alterations create composite vectors for replication or packaging of another virus.
[C12N 15/8616] Special methods for targeting systems -- C12N 15/8616 covers specialised techniques for directing targeting systems used in mutational or genetic-engineering processes. It includes methods that guide vectors, nucleic-acid constructs, enzymes or engineered cells to specific biological sites such as particular genes, chromosomes or cellular compartments, often to improve precision of gene editing, therapy delivery or functional studies.
[C12N 15/863] Poxviral vectors, -- C12N 15/863 covers the use of poxviruses as vectors for delivering genetic material into target cells, including their engineering, replication-deficient designs, and applications in vaccine development or gene therapy. The classification pertains to genetic manipulation of poxviral particles for therapeutic or research purposes.
[C12N 15/8633] Avian poxviral vectors -- A C12N 15/8633 avian poxviral vector is a genetically engineered virus derived from avian pox that serves as a delivery system for foreign DNA in research, vaccine development, or therapeutic applications. It enables targeted expression of inserted genes in avian or other host cells while preserving the virus's replication characteristics.
[C12N 15/8636] Vaccina virus vectors -- C12N 15/8636 covers virus vectors employed as carriers for vaccine antigens, focusing on their design, construction and use in immunisation strategies. It pertains to the mutation or genetic engineering of microorganisms to produce recombinant viral vaccine platforms.
[C12N 15/864] Parvoviral vectors -- C12N 15/864 covers parvoviral vectors, which are genetically modified parvoviruses engineered to transport nucleic-acid sequences into target cells for research, gene therapy or vaccine purposes. The classification includes the design, production, and application of these virus-based delivery systems within the fields of biochemistry and molecular genetics.
[C12N 15/8645] Adeno-associated virus -- The CPC entry C12N 15/8645 covers adeno-associated viruses, which are small, non-pathogenic viruses used as vectors for delivering genetic material into cells. It pertains to the manipulation, mutation or engineering of these viruses for applications such as gene therapy and biomedical research.
[C12N 15/866] Baculoviral vectors -- C12N 15/866 refers to baculoviral vectors, which are genetically modified baculoviruses used as carriers to introduce foreign DNA into host cells. They are widely employed for recombinant protein production in insect cell cultures and for gene-delivery applications in biotechnology.
[C12N 15/867] Retroviral vectors -- The C12N 15/867 class covers retroviral vectors, which are modified retroviruses engineered to carry foreign nucleic-acid sequences into target cells. These vectors exploit the natural integration capability of retroviruses for gene delivery in research, pharmaceutical production, and therapeutic gene-transfer applications.
[C12N 15/8673] Special methods for packaging systems -- C12N 15/8673 concerns specialised techniques for preparing or handling packaging systems that incorporate genetically modified microorganisms or enzymes, such as encapsulation, immobilisation, or delivery devices designed to protect or release biocatalytic activity. It falls under the biotechnology section dealing with mutation or genetic engineering of microbes and enzymes, addressing novel methods that improve stability, safety or functionality of biotechnological products in their packaging.
[C12N 15/8676] Special methods for targeting systems -- C12N 15/8676 covers specialized techniques for directing genetic editing or engineering tools to specific cellular targets, including methods that use molecular tags, delivery vectors, or programmable nucleases to achieve precise modification of microorganisms or enzymes. These approaches aim to improve selectivity and efficiency in mutation or genetic engineering processes.
[C12N 15/869] Herpesviral vectors -- C12N 15/869 covers herpesviral vectors, which are genetically modified herpesviruses used to deliver genes into target cells for research, therapeutic or vaccine applications. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and specifically mutation or genetic engineering, reflecting the manipulation of viral genomes to exploit their natural infection mechanisms for controlled gene transfer.
[C12N 15/8695] Herpes simplex virus-based vectors -- The C12N 15/8695 entry covers biotechnological applications that employ herpes simplex virus (HSV) as a vector for delivering genetic material. It includes the design, engineering, and use of HSV-based carriers for gene therapy, vaccine development, and recombinant protein production, focusing on the manipulation of the viral genome to achieve desired functions.
[C12N 15/87] Introduction of foreign genetic material using processes not otherwise provided for, e.g. co-transformation -- C12N 15/87 pertains to methods for inserting foreign genetic material into microorganisms or cells by means that are not otherwise specified in the CPC, such as co-transformation techniques that introduce DNA without a defined, separate protocol. This subclass falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and mutation or genetic engineering.
[C12N 15/873] Techniques for producing new embryos, e.g. nuclear transfer, manipulation of totipotent cells or production of chimeric embryos -- This CPC subclass covers methods to generate novel embryos via nuclear transfer, totipotent cell manipulation, or creation of chimeric embryos, and is used in genetic engineering and biotechnology.
[C12N 15/877] Techniques for producing new mammalian cloned embryos -- C12N 15/877 covers methods used to create mammalian cloned embryos, including nuclear transfer, cell-fusion and related procedures that reconstitute a whole embryo from somatic or germ cells. The code applies to techniques that generate genetically identical embryos for research, breeding or therapeutic purposes, and encompasses the necessary preparatory steps such as cell handling and activation.
[C12N 15/8771] Bovine embryos -- C12N 15/8771 pertains to bovine embryos and includes inventions relating to their preparation, manipulation, culture, preservation, and biotechnological applications such as cloning, genetic modification and embryo transfer.
[C12N 15/8772] Caprine embryos -- C12N 15/8772 concerns goat (caprine) embryos, covering their isolation, culture, preservation, genetic alteration and related biotechnological processes. It falls under the chemistry sub-class for microorganisms or enzymes, specifically mutation or genetic engineering of microorganisms.
[C12N 15/8773] Ovine embryos -- C12N 15/8773 covers biotechnological inventions involving ovine embryos, including methods for their preparation, manipulation, genetic modification, cloning and related uses in research or animal breeding. The code is applied to processes that alter or engineer sheep embryos at the molecular or cellular level, such as introducing mutations, transgenes or other engineered traits.
[C12N 15/8775] Murine embryos -- C12N 15/8775 covers subject matter related to the use, manipulation, or preparation of mouse embryos, including techniques for harvesting, culturing, or genetically modifying murine embryonic cells. It also includes methods for creating transgenic or knockout mice using embryonic manipulation. The classification is part of the broader group dealing with genetic engineering of microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 15/8776] Primate embryos -- C12N 15/8776 covers technologies related to primate embryos, including methods for their isolation, culture, genetic modification, and experimental use in research or therapeutic applications. The class falls under biochemistry topics dealing with microorganisms or enzymes and mutation or genetic engineering.
[C12N 15/8777] Rabbit embryos -- The code C12N 15/8777 covers inventions that involve rabbit embryos, especially their use in genetic manipulation, transgenic production, or embryo-culture techniques. It is relevant for methods that modify the genome of rabbits or employ rabbit embryos as biological tools in biotechnology.
[C12N 15/8778] Swine embryos -- C12N 15/8778 covers biotechnological methods involving swine embryos, including their preparation, manipulation, and use in mutation or genetic engineering processes. It includes techniques for embryo culture, gene transfer, and related applications in animal biotechnology and biomedical research.
[C12N 15/88] using microencapsulation, e.g. using liposome vesicle -- C12N 15/88 covers biotechnological methods that use microencapsulation, such as liposome vesicles, to contain genetically engineered microorganisms, enzymes or their products, enhancing their stability, targeting or controlled release. It includes techniques for forming the encapsulating structures and applying them in processes that involve mutation or genetic engineering.
[C12N 15/89] using microinjection -- The C12N 15/89 classification covers methods that employ microinjection to introduce nucleic acids, proteins or other substances directly into microbial or enzymatic cells for the purpose of inducing mutations, genetic modification or recombinant expression. It encompasses techniques that physically deliver the material through a fine needle or micropipette to achieve targeted genetic engineering.
[C12N 15/895] using biolistic methods -- C12N 15/895 covers the use of biolistic, or particle-bombardment, techniques to introduce nucleic acids or other genetic material into microorganisms, cells or enzymes for the purpose of mutation, genetic modification or expression. The subclass includes methods that employ high-velocity microprojectiles to deliver DNA, RNA or proteins and related apparatuses.
[C12N 15/90] Stable introduction of foreign DNA into chromosome -- C12N 15/90 classifies technologies for permanently incorporating foreign DNA into a host chromosome, enabling stable genetic modification of microorganisms or other cells. The entry covers methods such as homologous recombination, transposon-mediated insertion and site-specific integration used in mutation or genetic engineering.
[C12N 15/902] using homologous recombination -- C12N 15/902 covers methods that use homologous recombination to modify the DNA of microorganisms or enzymes, including gene replacement, insertion or deletion by providing donor DNA sequences that share similarity with the target locus. The entry includes the design of vectors, selection of homologous regions, and related techniques for precise genetic engineering and mutation of microbial or enzymatic systems.
[C12N 15/905] in yeast -- C12N 15/905 covers the use of yeast as a host for mutation or genetic engineering techniques. It includes methods for introducing, expressing, or modifying genes in yeast cells to produce desired proteins, metabolites, or phenotypic changes.
[C12N 15/907] in mammalian cells -- The code C12N 15/907 covers methods and compositions for performing genetic manipulation directly in mammalian cells, such as transformation, transfection, or genome editing, and for producing recombinant proteins or engineered cell lines.
[C12N 2015/8518] expressing industrially exogenous proteins, e.g. for pharmaceutical use, human insulin, blood factors, immunoglobulins, pseudoparticles -- The class covers industrial methods for producing exogenous proteins, particularly recombinant pharmaceuticals such as human insulin, blood-clotting factors, immunoglobulins and pseudoparticles, using host cells and expression vectors. It includes the entire process from gene insertion and host cultivation to protein recovery, purification and scale-up for therapeutic use.
[C12N 2015/8527] for producing animal models, e.g. for tests or diseases -- The code is in CPC class C12N, which deals with microbes, enzymes and related biotechnological processes. Subgroup 2015/8527 concerns methods for producing animal models, such as transgenic, knockout or gene-edited animals, that are intended for disease studies or testing purposes. These techniques create organisms that replicate specific human pathologies for research.
[C12N 2015/8536] Animal models for genetic diseases -- C12N 2015/8536 covers inventions that use or create animal models to study or replicate genetic disorders, including the development of transgenic, knockout, or otherwise genetically modified animals that mimic human disease phenotypes for research, screening, or therapeutic testing.
[C12N 2015/8545] for Alzheimer''s disease -- C12N 2015/8545 covers biotechnological agents such as microorganisms, enzymes, or related compositions that are employed in the prevention, diagnosis or treatment of Alzheimer's disease, including pharmaceutical formulations and therapeutic methods based on these biological materials.
[C12N 2015/8554] Invertebrates models for Alzheimer''s disease -- C12N 2015/8554 is a CPC subgroup covering biotechnological inventions that use invertebrate organisms-such as worms, flies, or mollusks-as experimental models to study or screen for Alzheimer's disease mechanisms, therapeutic compounds, or biomarkers. The classification applies to methods, compositions, and genetic modifications involving these non-mammalian models for neurodegenerative research.
[C12N 2015/8563] for autoimmune diseases, e.g. Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus -- C12N 2015/8563 classifies inventions that employ microorganisms, recombinant DNA, or related biotechnological methods for treating autoimmune disorders, with a particular emphasis on applications such as insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus. The entry covers therapeutic agents, vaccines, or diagnostic tools derived from engineered cells or genetic constructs designed to modulate the immune response in these diseases.
[C12N 2015/8572] Animal models for proliferative diseases, e.g. comprising an oncogene -- C12N 2015/8572 covers inventions that use animal models to study proliferative disorders, particularly those involving the introduction or expression of oncogenes to mimic cancer or other hyper-proliferative conditions. It includes methods for creating, evaluating or employing such models in biomedical research.
[C12N 2015/8581] Animal models for infectious diseases, e.g. AIDS -- C12N 2015/8581 refers to the use of animal models to investigate infectious diseases, including AIDS, and covers methods for creating, inoculating, and evaluating such models to study disease mechanisms, drug efficacy, and vaccine responses. It is applied to biotechnological and medical inventions involving disease-model research.
[C12N 2015/859] Animal models comprising reporter system for screening tests -- The subclass C12N 2015/859 covers genetically modified or transgenic animal models that carry a reporter gene-such as luciferase, GFP, or β-galactosidase-used to monitor biological activity in screening assays. These models enable in-vivo detection of molecular events, facilitating drug discovery, toxicity testing, and other biomedical screening applications.
[C12N 2300/00] Active non-coding nucleic acids -- Indexing codes of group C12N2300/00 are only used in combination with groups C12N15/11 - C12N15/117 . An exception is made for C12N2310/20 , which may be applied to CRISPR with any appropriate C12N main trunk symbol.
[C12N 2310/00] Structure or type of the nucleic acid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All indexing information needed to characterise the non-coding nucleic acids used in groups C12N15/11 - C12N15/117 .
[C12N 2310/10] Type of nucleic acid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Information about which class of non-coding nucleic acid is concerned.
[C12N 2310/11] Antisense -- Antisense refers to nucleic-acid sequences that are complementary to a specific target RNA and are employed to block its translation or stimulate its degradation, thereby modulating gene expression. The class covers the design, synthesis, and application of antisense oligonucleotides, related compounds, and delivery methods.
[C12N 2310/111] spanning the whole gene, or a large part of it -- The CPC subclass C12N 2310/111 refers to nucleic acid structures that encompass an entire gene or a substantial portion of it. It is used to classify inventions involving whole-gene or large-segment sequences, typically for expression, manipulation, or functional studies in microorganisms or enzymes. This falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → structure or type of the nucleic acid.
[C12N 2310/113] targeting other non-coding nucleic acids, e.g. antagomirs -- C12N 2310/113 falls under the CPC hierarchy for chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on the structure or type of nucleic acid. It covers inventions that target non-coding nucleic acids such as antagomirs, synthetic molecules designed to bind and inhibit microRNA function.
[C12N 2310/12] catalytic nucleic acids, e.g. ribozymes -- This subclass concerns nucleic acids that act as catalysts, such as ribozymes, which accelerate chemical reactions without being translated into protein. It covers synthetic and natural catalytic RNA or DNA molecules, their design, selection and applications in biotechnology and medicine.
[C12N 2310/121] Hammerhead -- The CPC subclass C12N 2310/121 designates hammerhead ribozymes, a specific RNA secondary-structure motif that forms a self-cleaving catalytic domain. It covers inventions relating to the structure, synthesis, modification or functional use of hammerhead-type nucleic acids in biochemical or biotechnological applications. This subclass falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and the structure or type of nucleic acid.
[C12N 2310/122] Hairpin -- C12N 2310/122 classifies inventions that involve hairpin structures-single-stranded nucleic acid sequences that fold back on themselves to form a stem-loop configuration. It covers the design, synthesis, and use of such hairpins in biological or biochemical applications, including gene regulation, molecular probes, and nucleic-acid-based diagnostics. This subclass falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes, specifically focusing on the structure or type of nucleic acid.
[C12N 2310/123] Hepatitis delta -- C12N 2310/123 designates hepatitis delta, the defective RNA virus that infects human liver cells and can only propagate in the presence of hepatitis B virus. The class groups inventions relating to its nucleic-acid structure, replication mechanisms and related biochemistry.
[C12N 2310/124] based on group I or II introns -- C12N 2310/124 covers nucleic acid structures or types that are based on group I or II introns, including engineered ribozymes or self-splicing sequences used for gene manipulation, expression control, or molecular cloning. The subclass addresses biotechnological applications exploiting the catalytic properties of these introns in microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2310/1241] Tetrahymena -- C12N 2310/1241 designates patent documents that focus on the structure or type of nucleic acids derived from or associated with the ciliate Tetrahymena, a model microorganism widely used in molecular-biology research. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy for biochemistry, specifically the sub-field covering microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2310/126] involving RNAse P -- The C12N 2310/126 subclass covers inventions concerning the structure, composition or functional variants of RNase P, the ribonucleoprotein enzyme that cleaves precursor tRNA to generate mature tRNA molecules. It includes the catalytic RNA component, associated protein subunits, engineered forms and related nucleic-acid constructs used in biotechnological or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2310/127] DNAzymes -- C12N 2310/127 classifies DNAzymes, which are DNA molecules that exhibit catalytic activity similar to enzymes. The entry includes both naturally occurring and engineered DNA sequences that can cleave, ligate, or modify other nucleic acids, and it covers their structure, function, and applications in biotechnology and molecular diagnostics.
[C12N 2310/128] processing or releasing ribozyme -- C12N 2310/128 pertains to methods for processing or releasing ribozymes, the catalytic RNA molecules that can self-cleave or modify other nucleic acids. It falls under chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes-structure or type of the nucleic acid and covers techniques for activating, extracting or liberating ribozymes from biological or synthetic systems for biotechnological or therapeutic use.
[C12N 2310/13] Decoys -- C12N 2310/13 covers nucleic acid decoys, which are short synthetic DNA or RNA fragments designed to mimic natural target sequences and bind enzymes, transcription factors or other binding proteins, thereby inhibiting their activity. The classification applies to biotechnological methods that employ such decoy molecules for controlling gene expression, enzyme function or pathogen interaction.
[C12N 2310/14] interfering nucleic acids [NA]
[C12N 2310/141] MicroRNAs, miRNAs -- The C12N 2310/141 entry covers microRNAs (miRNAs), short non-coding RNA molecules typically 20-24 nucleotides long that regulate gene expression by base-pairing with target mRNAs. It classifies inventions related to the structure, type, synthesis, modification, or functional use of these small nucleic acids in biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2310/15] Nucleic acids forming more than 2 strands, e.g. TFOs -- This CPC subclass covers nucleic acid structures that consist of more than two strands, notably triplex-forming oligonucleotides and related multi-strand assemblies. It includes methods and applications of such nucleic acids in research, diagnostics and therapeutic approaches.
[C12N 2310/151] more than 3 strands, e.g. tetrads, H-DNA -- The CPC code C12N 2310/151 classifies nucleic acid structures that contain more than three strands, including quadruplexes, tetrads and H-DNA conformations. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → structure or type of the nucleic acid.
[C12N 2310/152] on a single-stranded target, e.g. fold-back TFOs -- This subclass covers nucleic-acid technologies that bind a single-stranded target, such as fold-back triplex-forming oligonucleotides (TFOs) that hybridise to themselves or to a complementary region, forming a triple-helix structure for recognition or manipulation. It includes designs where the target strand is single-stranded rather than double-helical.
[C12N 2310/153] with the aid of a protein, e.g. recombinase -- The CPC subclass C12N 2310/153 covers methods for modifying, assembling or manipulating nucleic acids with the assistance of a protein catalyst, such as a recombinase, that mediates site-specific recombination, integration, excision or exchange of DNA or RNA sequences. It pertains to biotechnological techniques that employ these enzymes to alter nucleic acid structure or type.
[C12N 2310/16] Aptamers -- C12N 2310/16 covers aptamers, short single-stranded DNA or RNA molecules that are selected for high-affinity binding to specific targets such as proteins, small molecules or cells. The classification pertains to the structural type of nucleic acids employed in biotechnology and diagnostic applications.
[C12N 2310/17] Immunomodulatory nucleic acids -- Immunomodulatory nucleic acids are DNA or RNA molecules engineered to alter immune function, either by activating or suppressing specific pathways. They include CpG oligodeoxynucleotides, siRNA, aptamers and related sequences used in vaccines, immunotherapies, and anti-inflammatory treatments.
[C12N 2310/18] acting by a non-sequence specific mechanism -- C12N 2310/18 covers nucleic acid agents that exert their effect without needing a specific base-pairing match to a target sequence, such as charge-based binding, intercalation or steric hindrance mechanisms. It applies to methods and compositions where the nucleic acid acts broadly on microorganisms or enzymes rather than through a defined complementary sequence. This subclass groups inventions that rely on non-sequence-specific interactions for therapeutic, diagnostic or biotechnological purposes.
[C12N 2310/20] involving clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats [CRISPR] -- Group C12N2310/20 is impacted by reclassification into groups C12N9/22 , C12N9/222 , C12N9/224 and C12N9/226 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[C12N 2310/30] Chemical structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Chemical modification of the nucleic acids, i.e. all relevant deviations from the natural DNA or RNA forms.
[C12N 2310/31] of the backbone -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Modifications of the phosphate group(s) forming the backbone of the nucleic acid, including the terminal group(s).  **Glossary:** - Phosphonate: O=P(O)2-R - Phosphorodithioate: S=P(O)2-SR, S=P(SO)-OR - Phosphorothioate: S=P(O)2-OR, O=P(O)2-SR - Phosphoramidate: O=P(O)2-NR2, O=P(NO)-OR - Phosphotriester: O=P(O)2-O-R - Phosphonothioate: S= P(O)2-R
[C12N 2310/311] Phosphotriesters -- The CPC subclass C12N 2310/311 groups inventions concerning phosphotriesters, chemical compounds in which a phosphorus atom is bonded to three ester groups. It covers nucleic-acid analogues and related molecules that incorporate this phosphotriester linkage, often used to modify stability, resistance to nucleases, or delivery properties in biotechnology and therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2310/312] Phosphonates -- C12N 2310/312 designates phosphonate compounds, organic molecules that contain a direct carbon-phosphorus bond and are commonly used as nucleic-acid analogues or inhibitors in biochemical and pharmaceutical applications.
