[C12N 2310/3125] Methylphosphonates -- C12N 2310/3125 covers nucleic acid analogs in which a non-bridging oxygen of the phosphate backbone is replaced by a methyl group, forming methylphosphonate linkages. These modified strands are used to increase stability, resist nuclease degradation, and modulate binding in therapeutic and biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2310/313] Phosphorodithioates -- C12N 2310/313 designates nucleic acid analogues in which a non-bridging oxygen atom of the phosphate backbone is replaced by sulfur, forming phosphorodithioate linkages. These modified oligonucleotides are used to increase resistance to nucleases and to alter binding properties in biochemical and therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2310/314] Phosphoramidates -- The CPC class C12N 2310/314 covers phosphoramidate compounds, where a phosphorus atom is bound to nitrogen within a nucleic acid backbone, forming a P-N linkage instead of a conventional phosphate ester. These analogues are used in molecular biology, therapeutic nucleic acids and enzyme studies for improved stability or altered activity.
[C12N 2310/3145] with the nitrogen in 3' or 5'-position -- This subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification concerns nucleic acids whose backbone includes a nitrogen atom placed at either the 3′ or 5′ position, a feature found in certain modified nucleotides and analogues used in biotech or pharmaceutical applications.
[C12N 2310/315] Phosphorothioates -- C12N 2310/315 covers nucleic acids that contain phosphorothioate linkages, where a non-bridging oxygen atom in the phosphate backbone is replaced by sulfur. This modification confers increased resistance to nucleases and alters binding properties, making it useful in antisense oligonucleotides, siRNA and other therapeutic or diagnostic nucleic-acid applications. The class includes synthesis, characterization and use of such sulfur-substituted nucleic acids.
[C12N 2310/316] Phosphonothioates -- Phosphonothioates are nucleic acid analogues in which a non-bridging oxygen of a phosphate group is replaced by sulfur and the phosphorus is bonded directly to carbon. This modification creates a phosphonothioate linkage that confers increased stability against enzymatic degradation and is used in therapeutic oligonucleotides and molecular probes.
[C12N 2310/317] with an inverted bond, e.g. a cap structure -- C12N 2310/317 covers nucleic acids that possess an inverted bond, such as a cap structure, where the linkage between nucleotides is reversed relative to the normal phosphodiester orientation. This classification applies to engineered or naturally occurring nucleic acid molecules whose terminal or internal bonds are modified to create a cap-like configuration that can affect stability or function.
[C12N 2310/318] where the PO2 is completely replaced, e.g. MMI or formacetal -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Nucleic acids where the phosphate units of the backbone are (at least partially) replaced by a different chemical entity.
[C12N 2310/3181] Peptide nucleic acid, PNA -- Peptide nucleic acids (PNA) are synthetic analogues of DNA in which the sugar-phosphate backbone is replaced by a neutral peptide-like chain, giving them high binding affinity and stability toward complementary nucleic acids. The CPC entry C12N 2310/3181 covers compounds, methods, and applications involving PNA structures.
[C12N 2310/3183] Diol linkers, e.g. glycols or propanediols -- The subclass C12N 2310/3183 covers nucleic acids that incorporate diol linkers such as glycols or propanediols as spacers or bridges in the backbone or side chains. These linkers are used to modify flexibility, solubility or binding properties of the molecule, and are common in synthetic oligonucleotides, aptamers or gene-delivery constructs.
[C12N 2310/319] linked by 2'-5' linkages, i.e. having a free 3'-position -- The code C12N 2310/319 designates nucleic acids whose monomer units are joined by 2'-5' phosphodiester bonds, leaving an unbound 3'-hydroxyl group. It includes RNA fragments, synthetic oligonucleotides and related constructs that use this atypical linkage.
[C12N 2310/32] of the sugar -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All modifications made to, or variations of, the (deoxy)ribose part of the nucleic acid:
[C12N 2310/321] 2'-O-R Modification -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All modifications at the 2'-position of the sugar made via an oxygen-atom.
[C12N 2310/322] 2'-R Modification -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All modifications directly linked to the 2'-carbon of the sugar, without an intermediate oxygen.
[C12N 2310/323] modified ring structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Modifications of the classical (deoxy)ribose ring, including replacement by other sugars.
[C12N 2310/3231] having an additional ring, e.g. LNA, ENA -- This CPC subclass covers nucleic acids that contain an extra cyclic moiety in the backbone, such as locked nucleic acids (LNA) or ethylene-bridged nucleic acids (ENA). These modifications introduce an additional ring to improve stability and binding affinity.
[C12N 2310/3233] Morpholino-type ring -- C12N 2310/3233 covers morpholino-type rings, heterocyclic structures in which the ribose oxygen of a nucleic acid is replaced by a methylene bridge and a nucleobase is attached to a morpholine scaffold, creating stable nucleic-acid analogues used in antisense and gene-silencing applications. This subclass sits within the CPC hierarchy for the structure or type of nucleic acids under biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2310/3235] having the O of the ribose replaced by another atom -- A subclass of nucleic-acid structures in which the oxygen atom of the ribose sugar is replaced by another atom, such as sulfur, selenium or a carbon-based group, creating modified backbones that alter stability, enzymatic resistance and functional properties for research or therapeutic use.
[C12N 2310/33] of the base -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Nucleic acids comprising a base other than adenine (A), uracil (U), thymidine (T), guanine (G) or cytosine (C). This includes modifications of said natural bases.
[C12N 2310/331] Universal or degenerate base -- The C12N 2310/331 subclass covers nucleic acids that contain universal or degenerate bases-synthetic nucleobases capable of pairing with more than one of the standard bases. These modified bases are used to increase flexibility in hybridisation, enable primer design that tolerates sequence variation, and facilitate molecular-biology techniques such as PCR and sequencing.
[C12N 2310/332] Abasic residue
[C12N 2310/333] Modified A -- The C12N 2310/333 subclass includes inventions concerning nucleic acids in which the adenine base has been chemically altered, such as by substitution of functional groups, modification of the sugar moiety, or changes to the backbone, to create a "modified A" nucleotide. These modified adenine-containing sequences are used in biotechnological applications, diagnostics, therapeutics and related molecular-biology techniques.
[C12N 2310/334] Modified C -- C12N 2310/334 concerns nucleic acids in which the cytosine base has been chemically altered, such as by methylation, hydroxymethylation or other substituents. It covers methods, compositions and uses of these modified cytosine residues in DNA or RNA, including applications in epigenetic research, gene regulation studies and biotechnological products.
[C12N 2310/3341] 5-Methylcytosine -- The code C12N 2310/3341 refers to 5-methylcytosine, a modified nucleobase in which a methyl group is attached to the carbon-5 position of cytosine. This epigenetic DNA modification occurs in microorganisms and higher organisms, influencing gene expression and DNA stability. The classification places it under the structure or type of nucleic acid within biochemistry and microbiology.
[C12N 2310/335] Modified T or U -- C12N 2310/335 classifies nucleic acids that contain chemically modified thymine or uracil bases, such as methylated, halogenated or other altered derivatives, used in synthetic biology, gene-editing and therapeutic applications. The code falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → structure or type of the nucleic acid, and captures inventions where the altered T or U residues affect base pairing, stability or biological activity.
[C12N 2310/336] Modified G -- The code C12N 2310/336 covers nucleic acids in which the guanine base has been chemically altered, including methylated, alkylated, fluorescent or other functionalized guanine derivatives. It applies to modified-G residues incorporated into DNA, RNA or synthetic oligonucleotides for purposes such as stability improvement, probing, labeling or therapeutic activity.
[C12N 2310/337] in alpha-anomeric form -- C12N 2310/337 covers nucleic acids or related molecules that are described as being in the alpha-anomeric form, meaning the glycosidic bond of the sugar moiety adopts the α-configuration. The classification is used for patents concerning the synthesis, modification, or application of such α-configured nucleic acids, often in the context of biochemistry or molecular biology.
[C12N 2310/34] Spatial arrangement of the modifications -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Nucleic acids where the specific position of a modification within the nucleic acid is relevant.
[C12N 2310/341] Gapmers, i.e. of the type ===---=== -- C12N 2310/341 covers gapmer antisense oligonucleotides, which are short nucleic-acid molecules that combine a central DNA segment with chemically modified flanking regions to enhance binding and stability. The design enables precise targeting of RNA for gene-silencing or therapeutic purposes while improving resistance to nucleases. This subclass falls under the chemistry hierarchy for structures of nucleic acids in microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2310/342] Hemimers, i.e. of the type ====---- -- C12N 2310/342 covers hemimeric nucleic acids, defined as molecules formed from two complementary half-strands that together create a functional duplex or partial duplex structure. The subclass is used for patents describing the design, synthesis or application of such hemimer constructs in biochemical or biotechnological processes.
[C12N 2310/343] having patterns, e.g. ==--==--==-- -- C12N 2310/343 concerns nucleic acids that are engineered to contain defined repeating patterns, such as alternating double-bond or spacer motifs, exemplified by sequences described as ==--==--==--. The classification is used for inventions involving patterned nucleic acid structures in biotechnology and synthetic biology.
[C12N 2310/344] Position-specific modifications, e.g. on every purine, at the 3'-end
[C12N 2310/345] having at least two different backbone modifications -- C12N 2310/345 classifies nucleic acids whose backbone incorporates at least two different chemical modifications, such as altered sugar moieties, phosphorothioate linkages, or other non-natural linkages. The entry applies to engineered DNA, RNA, or aptamer strands designed for enhanced stability, binding, or functional properties.
[C12N 2310/346] having a combination of backbone and sugar modifications -- The CPC subclass C12N 2310/346 relates to nucleic acids-DNA, RNA or analogues-whose structures incorporate both backbone and sugar modifications. It covers molecules engineered with altered phosphodiester linkages together with modified ribose or deoxyribose units, used to improve stability, binding or biological activity.
[C12N 2310/35] Nature of the modification -- C12N 2310/35 addresses the nature of nucleic-acid modifications, defining whether the alteration is natural, synthetic or engineered and covering changes to the backbone, bases or sugar moieties. It applies to biotechnological inventions involving microorganisms or enzymes where the type of modification affects function or stability.
[C12N 2310/351] Conjugate -- C12N 2310/351 addresses nucleic acid conjugates, where a nucleic acid molecule is covalently linked to another entity such as a polymer, peptide, lipid, or small-molecule drug. The subclass covers engineered hybrids used for therapeutic, diagnostic or delivery applications, including antisense oligonucleotide-drug conjugates, siRNA-lipid complexes and other chemically modified nucleic acids. It focuses on the structure and type of the nucleic acid component within these combined molecules.
[C12N 2310/3511] intercalating or cleaving agent -- The CPC subclass C12N 2310/3511 covers chemical compounds that act as intercalating or cleaving agents for nucleic acids. These agents bind between base pairs or induce strand breakage and are used in molecular biology, diagnostics, or as potential drugs targeting DNA or RNA.
[C12N 2310/3513] Protein -- C12N 2310/3513 relates to proteins, the amino-acid polymers that act as functional macromolecules in living systems. It addresses the structure, composition and classification of protein molecules, including primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary forms as well as recombinant or chemically modified variants.
[C12N 2310/3515] Lipophilic moiety, e.g. cholesterol -- C12N 2310/3515 covers nucleic acid compounds that incorporate a lipophilic group, for example cholesterol, attached to the backbone or a base. The lipophilic moiety improves the molecule's affinity for cell membranes and can aid in drug delivery or stability. This subclass falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and nucleic acid structure.
[C12N 2310/3517] Marker -- The CPC subclass C12N 2310/3517 concerns nucleic-acid markers that are employed to detect, identify or quantify specific microorganisms or enzymes. It includes DNA or RNA sequences used as genetic tags or probes for recognizing particular microbial strains or enzymatic functions.
[C12N 2310/3519] Fusion with another nucleic acid -- The C12N 2310/3519 class concerns nucleic acids that have been fused with a different nucleic acid to form a single continuous strand. It includes recombinant or chimeric DNA/RNA constructs in which sequences from distinct origins are joined, covering methods of creating, using and characterising such fused molecules for biotechnological or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2310/352] linked to the nucleic acid via a carbon atom -- This subclass includes nucleic acid conjugates in which the attached group is bonded to the nucleic acid through a carbon atom, such as carbon-carbon linkages to sugars, lipids, polymers or other organic moieties, used in probes, drugs or delivery systems. It falls under biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on nucleic acid structure.
[C12N 2310/3521] Methyl
[C12N 2310/3523] Allyl
[C12N 2310/3525] MOE, methoxyethoxy -- C12N 2310/3525 classifies nucleic acids that incorporate methoxyethoxy (MOE) groups, typically as 2′-O-methoxyethyl substituents on the sugar backbone. These modifications enhance the stability, binding affinity and pharmacokinetic properties of therapeutic oligonucleotides and related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2310/3527] Other alkyl chain -- C12N 2310/3527 classifies nucleic acids that incorporate alkyl chains other than those specifically listed in other CPC subclasses. It captures structures where the nucleic acid backbone or side groups are modified with miscellaneous alkyl substituents, covering a broad range of synthetic or natural variants not otherwise categorized. This entry falls under Chemistry > Biochemistry > Microorganisms or Enzymes > Structure or type of the nucleic acid.
[C12N 2310/3529] Aromatic substituent -- C12N 2310/3529 classifies nucleic acids that carry an aromatic substituent, such as a phenyl or hetero-aromatic group, attached to the backbone or bases. It applies to nucleic-acid structures used in the biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2310/353] linked to the nucleic acid via an atom other than carbon -- This subclass covers nucleic acid derivatives in which the nucleic acid is linked to another moiety through an atom other than carbon, such as oxygen, nitrogen, or phosphorus. It includes conjugates, probes and chemically modified nucleic acids where the attachment bond uses a heteroatom, relevant to microbial or enzymatic biochemistry.
[C12N 2310/3531] Hydrogen -- C12N 2310/3531 covers inventions that specifically address hydrogen as a structural element of nucleic acids, including its role in hydrogen-bonding, tautomeric forms and related modifications of DNA, RNA or analogues. The subclass is used for claims involving the presence, substitution or manipulation of hydrogen atoms within the nucleic-acid framework.
[C12N 2310/3533] Halogen -- C12N 2310/3533 covers nucleic acids that incorporate halogen atoms-fluorine, chlorine, bromine or iodine-into bases, sugars or the backbone, typically to modify stability, binding or pharmacokinetic properties in therapeutic or diagnostic oligonucleotides.
[C12N 2310/3535] Nitrogen
[C12N 2310/50] Physical structure -- C12N 2310/50 covers inventions that describe or exploit the physical structure of nucleic acids, such as their conformation, secondary and tertiary arrangements, and related spatial features. It is applied to patents dealing with the shape, folding or overall organization of DNA or RNA molecules.
[C12N 2310/51] in polymeric form, e.g. multimers, concatemers -- C12N 2310/51 covers nucleic acids that are presented in polymeric form, such as multimers or concatemers, rather than as single strands. It applies to biochemically engineered DNA or RNA molecules that have been linked end-to-end to create longer, repetitive sequences for use in research, diagnostics, or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2310/52] branched -- The subclass C12N 2310/52 addresses nucleic acids that have a branched architecture, where the polymer chain splits into two or more arms from a common junction. It includes both natural and synthetic branched DNA or RNA used in probes, amplification methods, nanostructures and related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2310/53] partially self-complementary or closed -- The CPC subclass C12N 2310/53 covers nucleic acid structures that are partially self-complementary or form closed configurations, such as hairpin loops, where the strand can base-pair with itself to create a secondary structure. These arrangements are relevant for designing oligonucleotides, aptamers and molecular probes.
[C12N 2310/531] Stem-loop -- The CPC subclass C12N 2310/531 covers stem-loop structures in nucleic acids, which are short double-helical regions formed when a single strand folds back on itself and pairs complementary bases, creating a hairpin-like loop. This subclass is used for inventions involving the design, synthesis, or functional use of such secondary structures in DNA or RNA, especially in microbial or enzymatic contexts.
[C12N 2310/532] Closed or circular -- C12N 2310/532 refers to nucleic acids that are closed or circular in structure, including plasmids, viral genomes and circular RNA molecules. The classification is used for inventions related to the preparation, manipulation or use of such circular nucleic acids in biotechnological or pharmaceutical applications.
[C12N 2310/533] having a mismatch or nick in at least one of the strands -- The CPC subclass C12N 2310/533 pertains to nucleic acids that contain a mismatch or a nick in at least one of the strands, encompassing DNA or RNA molecules with deliberate or incidental mismatches, gaps or single-strand breaks, as relevant to biochemical work on microorganisms and enzymes.
[C12N 2320/00] Applications -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Aspects concerning specific uses of the non-coding nucleic acids.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Structural aspects of the nucleic acids -> C12N2310/00 - Methods for producing or obtaining the nucleic acids -> C12N2330/00
[C12N 2320/10] in screening processes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of the non-coding nucleic acid for screening as well as the detection of the nucleic acid.
[C12N 2320/11] for the determination of target sites, i.e. of active nucleic acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of a non-coding nucleic acid in the identification of an accessible site on the target nucleic acid. Hence, the Indexing Code covers also the screening for active non-coding nucleic acids.
[C12N 2320/12] in functional genomics, i.e. for the determination of gene function -- C12N 2320/12 covers methods and tools used in functional genomics to determine the role of specific genes, such as gene knock-out, over-expression, RNA interference or other techniques that reveal gene function in microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2320/13] in a process of directed evolution, e.g. SELEX, acquiring a new function -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All methods and processes directed to provide the non-coding nucleic acid with a new function.
[C12N 2320/30] Special therapeutic applications -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Subject-matter where the method or therapeutic application is part of the invention.
[C12N 2320/31] Combination therapy -- C12N 2320/31 covers the use of microorganisms or enzymes in combination with other therapeutic agents for treating diseases. It relates to pharmaceutical compositions where the biological agent is administered together with a second active compound to achieve synergistic or additive effects.
[C12N 2320/32] Special delivery means, e.g. tissue-specific -- The code C12N 2320/32 covers technologies for delivering agents-such as drugs, nucleic acids or enzymes-directly to particular tissues, cells or organelles. It encompasses carriers, vectors and targeting strategies that enable tissue-specific or cellular-specific transport, often used in therapeutic or diagnostic applications.
[C12N 2320/33] Alteration of splicing -- C12N 2320/33 covers biotechnological methods that modify the splicing of precursor RNA in microorganisms or enzyme-producing cells to alter gene expression or protein products. It includes techniques, reagents and engineered strains used to achieve desired splice variants for industrial, pharmaceutical or research purposes.
[C12N 2320/34] Allele or polymorphism specific uses -- C12N 2320/34 covers inventions that exploit a particular allele or genetic polymorphism for a defined purpose, such as diagnosing a disease, tailoring a therapy, or engineering a microorganism or enzyme based on that specific variation. The classification is used for applications that rely on the unique properties of the chosen genetic variant rather than on generic molecular methods.
[C12N 2320/35] based on a specific dosage / administration regimen -- C12N 2320/35 covers inventions that apply microorganisms or enzymes in a defined dosage or administration regimen, such as specific amounts, timing, or schedules for therapeutic or industrial use. The classification is used for patents describing how the effectiveness, safety, or performance of the biological agent depends on the precise way it is delivered.
[C12N 2320/50] Methods for regulating/modulating their activity -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and means directed to modify the natural activity of the nucleic acids.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - The directed acquisition of a totally new activity -> C12N2320/13
[C12N 2320/51] modulating the chemical stability, e.g. nuclease-resistance -- C12N 2320/51 covers inventions that increase the chemical stability of nucleic acids, for example by providing resistance to nucleases. It is used for biochemistry applications involving microorganisms or enzymes that protect or enhance nucleic-acid durability.
[C12N 2320/52] modulating the physical stability, e.g. GC-content -- The CPC subgroup C12N 2320/52 covers methods and compositions that adjust the physical stability of nucleic acids, for example by altering GC-content to influence melting temperature, durability or storage behavior. It includes techniques for engineering DNA or RNA sequences with higher or lower GC fractions to modulate stability in biotechnological or pharmaceutical applications.
[C12N 2320/53] reducing unwanted side-effects -- C12N 2320/53 relates to the use of microorganisms or enzymes to produce nucleic-acid-based products where the process is designed to minimise adverse effects, such as toxicity or immune reactions, in the final therapeutic, diagnostic or other application. It covers strategies, modifications or formulations that specifically aim to reduce unwanted side-effects of the biologically active nucleic acids.
[C12N 2330/00] Production -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The means for producing or obtaining the non-coding nucleic acids.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - The structural aspects of the nucleic acids -> C12N2310/00 - Specific uses -> C12N2320/00
[C12N 2330/10] naturally occurring -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-coding nucleic acids naturally present in a cell or organism.
[C12N 2330/30] chemically synthesised -- C12N 2330/30 covers methods for producing microorganisms or enzymes that rely on wholly chemical synthesis rather than biological fermentation or recombinant techniques. It includes processes where the target organism or enzyme is assembled from chemically derived precursors, such as synthetic nucleic acids or peptides, to generate functional biologic products.
[C12N 2330/31] Libraries, arrays -- C12N 2330/31 covers the production and use of combinatorial libraries and ordered arrays of biomolecules such as nucleic acids, peptides or enzymes. It includes methods for constructing, screening and manipulating these collections to identify functional variants, as well as the physical formats that hold them, for example microtiter plates or microarrays.
[C12N 2330/50] Biochemical production, i.e. in a transformed host cell -- C12N 2330/50 covers methods and processes for producing biochemicals using a host cell that has been genetically modified to express desired pathways. It includes the engineering of microorganisms or other cells to synthesize metabolites, proteins, or other bio-derived substances.
[C12N 2330/51] Specially adapted vectors -- C12N 2330/51 covers genetically engineered vectors that have been modified to enhance their suitability for specific production tasks in microorganisms or enzymes, such as improved expression, stability, or host range. The classification applies to plasmids, bacteriophages, viral or synthetic carriers that have been specially adapted for biotechnological manufacturing processes.
[C12N 2500/00] Cell culture -- C12N 2500/00 pertains to cell-culture techniques and related materials used to grow, maintain, or manipulate living cells in vitro, encompassing mammalian, plant, microbial or hybrid cell lines for research, diagnostics, vaccine production, or biotechnological synthesis. The subclass includes culture media, substrates, bioreactors, and methods for cell propagation, differentiation, or preservation.
[C12N 2500/02] Atmosphere, e.g. low oxygen conditions -- C12N 2500/02 covers methods and apparatus for cultivating cells in a controlled atmosphere, particularly low-oxygen or hypoxic environments, aimed at influencing cell growth, metabolism, or product formation. It includes techniques for adjusting gas composition, pressure, or humidity during cell culture processes.
[C12N 2500/05] Inorganic components -- C12N 2500/05 relates to the use of inorganic components in cell-culture processes, covering the addition of minerals, salts, metal ions, or inorganic scaffolds to support the growth or maintenance of microorganisms, enzymes, or cultured cells. It includes formulations of culture media and carriers that contain non-organic substances.
[C12N 2500/10] Metals -- C12N 2500/10 relates to the use of metals in cell culture processes. It covers metal ions, salts, complexes or metal-based media that support the growth, maintenance or manipulation of cells, microorganisms or enzymes. The classification is applied to inventions where metals play a functional role in the cultivation environment.
[C12N 2500/12] Light metals, i.e. alkali, alkaline earth, Be, Al, Mg -- C12N 2500/12 covers the use of light metals such as alkali and alkaline-earth elements, beryllium, aluminium and magnesium in cell-culture processes. It includes methods, media formulations or substrates that employ these metals to support the growth, maintenance or manipulation of microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2500/14] Calcium -- C12N 2500/14 relates to the use of calcium in cell-culture processes. It covers the addition of calcium ions or salts to cultivate microorganisms or enzyme-producing cells, affecting growth, metabolism or product formation. This entry falls under the chemistry hierarchy for biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2500/16] Magnesium -- C12N 2500/16 designates inventions that involve magnesium as a component in cell-culture media or processes. It includes the addition of magnesium salts to support growth of microorganisms or enzymes, and methods for preparing or using such magnesium-supplemented culture systems.
[C12N 2500/20] Transition metals -- C12N 2500/20 pertains to the use of transition metals in cell-culture processes, including their role as essential cofactors, media additives, or catalysts that support the growth, metabolism, or genetic manipulation of microorganisms or enzymes cultivated in vitro.
[C12N 2500/22] Zinc -- The CPC subclass C12N 2500/22 designates inventions that involve zinc in cell-culture applications, such as a nutritional supplement, growth factor or catalytic cofactor for microorganisms or enzymes grown in vitro. It covers methods, media and bioprocesses where zinc is a key component.
[C12N 2500/24] Iron -- This CPC entry designates the use of iron in cell-culture practice, covering its role as a nutrient, co-factor or additive in media for microorganisms or enzyme-producing cells. It includes methods for supplying, controlling or measuring iron to influence growth, metabolism or product formation in cultured biological systems.
[C12N 2500/25] Insulin-transferrin -- The C12N 2500/25 entry covers cell-culture formulations or methods that employ a combination of insulin and transferrin to support the growth, maintenance or production of microorganisms, enzymes or other biological materials. It includes compositions, media additives and related processes that use an insulin-transferrin complex as a nutritional or signaling component in biotechnological or pharmaceutical cell-culture applications.
[C12N 2500/30] Organic components -- C12N 2500/30 covers organic components employed in cell-culture processes, such as nutrients, growth factors, vitamins, hormones, lipids and other biologically active molecules that support the growth, maintenance or differentiation of microorganisms or cells in vitro. It is used to classify inventions that involve formulating or using these organic substances in culture media or related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2500/32] Amino acids -- The CPC subclass C12N 2500/32 covers amino acids employed in cell-culture processes, falling under biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes. It pertains to the use, preparation or modification of individual or mixed amino acids as nutrients, growth factors or media components for cultivating cells. This entry is part of the chemistry hierarchy, specifically within the cell-culture section of biochemical applications.
[C12N 2500/33] other than alpha-amino carboxylic acids, e.g. beta-amino acids, taurine -- C12N 2500/33 is a CPC subclass under chemistry, biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes, and cell culture, covering patent subject matter that involves compounds other than alpha-amino carboxylic acids, such as beta-amino acids and taurine.
[C12N 2500/34] Sugars -- C12N 2500/34 covers the selection and use of sugars as carbon or energy sources in the cultivation of microorganisms or cells. It includes specifying types of monosaccharides, disaccharides, oligosaccharides, or polysaccharides, their concentrations, and methods for adding or delivering them to maintain or enhance growth in a cell-culture environment.
[C12N 2500/35] Polyols, e.g. glycerin, inositol -- The CPC subclass C12N 2500/35 addresses the use of polyols such as glycerin or inositol in cell-culture methods. It is situated under chemistry → biochemistry → micro-organisms or enzymes → cell culture and relates to compounds that support the growth or maintenance of cultured cells.
[C12N 2500/36] Lipids -- This subclass covers the use, synthesis or manipulation of lipids in cell-culture systems, such as preparing lipid-containing media, adding fatty acids, sterols or phospholipids to cultures, and related processes for supporting microbial or enzymatic growth.
[C12N 2500/38] Vitamins -- C12N 2500/38 pertains to the production or use of vitamins through cell-culture methods, including microbial or enzymatic synthesis, and the related processes for isolating or purifying these compounds. It encompasses biotechnological techniques for generating vitamin substances in vitro.
[C12N 2500/40] Nucleotides, nucleosides or bases -- C12N 2500/40 pertains to inventions dealing with nucleotides, nucleosides or bases in the context of cell-culture techniques, including their preparation, modification, and use in biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2500/42] Organic phosphate, e.g. beta glycerophosphate -- The C12N 2500/42 subclass in the CPC covers the use of organic phosphate compounds, such as β-glycerophosphate, in cell-culture applications. These phosphates act as a phosphate source or buffering agent to promote cell growth, differentiation or mineralisation, especially in bone-related or osteogenic cultures.
[C12N 2500/44] Thiols, e.g. mercaptoethanol -- C12N 2500/44 covers thiol compounds such as mercaptoethanol that are used in cell-culture processes. These reducing agents supply sulfhydryl groups to maintain a reduced environment, protect cells from oxidative damage and support the growth of microorganisms or enzymes. The classification groups any method or composition where such thiols are added to culture media.
[C12N 2500/46] Amines, e.g. putrescine -- C12N 2500/46 relates to the use of amines, for example putrescine, in cell-culture processes involving microorganisms or enzymes. It covers methods of adding or controlling these compounds to affect growth, metabolism or product formation in biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2500/50] Soluble polymers, e.g. polyethyleneglycol [PEG] -- The CPC subclass C12N 2500/50 covers soluble polymers such as polyethylene glycol that are employed in cell-culture processes, for example as media additives, cryoprotectants or carriers for biomolecules. It includes polymers that dissolve in aqueous environments and are used to modify cell-growth conditions or protect cells during handling.
[C12N 2500/60] Buffer, e.g. pH regulation, osmotic pressure -- The CPC code C12N 2500/60 covers buffers employed in cell-culture processes, specifically those that regulate pH or control osmotic pressure. It pertains to chemical substances that maintain the physiological environment for microorganisms or enzymes during in-vitro cultivation.
[C12N 2500/62] DMSO -- The entry covers the use of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) in cell-culture processes, primarily as a solvent or cryoprotectant to preserve or improve the viability of microorganisms, enzymes or biological samples.
[C12N 2500/70] Undefined extracts -- The C12N 2500/70 class covers undefined extracts derived from microorganisms or enzymes that are employed in cell-culture processes, where the specific composition, source or degree of purification is not characterised. It includes preparation, use and testing of such extracts in biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2500/72] from bacteria -- C12N 2500/72 concerns biotechnological processes that use bacteria as the source material, covering the cultivation, harvesting and manipulation of bacterial cells for the production of biomolecules, enzymes, or other bioproducts.
[C12N 2500/74] from fungi, e.g. yeasts -- A subcategory for cell-culture techniques that employ fungi, particularly yeasts, covering methods of growing fungal cells, harvesting biomass, and using them as hosts for biotechnological or analytical purposes.
[C12N 2500/76] from plants -- C12N 2500/76 pertains to cell-culture techniques that use plant-derived material. It falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and cell culture, covering processes, media, or products obtained from plants for biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2500/78] from protozoa -- The CPC subclass C12N 2500/78 covers cell-culture techniques that use protozoan organisms as the source material, including methods for propagating, maintaining, or manipulating protozoa in vitro for biochemical or microbiological research. It is part of the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and cell culture.
[C12N 2500/80] from animals -- C12N 2500/80 covers techniques, media and compositions used to culture cells that are derived from animal sources. It includes the preparation, maintenance and manipulation of primary animal cells, established animal cell lines and related bioprocesses.
[C12N 2500/82] from invertebrates -- C12N 2500/82 covers cell-culture methods that employ cells taken from invertebrate organisms as the source material for biotechnological processes, such as the production of enzymes, metabolites, or recombinant proteins. It includes techniques for isolating, maintaining and manipulating invertebrate cell lines for research or industrial use.
[C12N 2500/84] from mammals -- The C12N 2500/84 subclass covers cell-culture techniques that specifically use mammalian cells, including methods for harvesting, subculturing, maintaining, and processing such cells for research, diagnostic, or biotechnological applications. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and cell culture.
[C12N 2500/90] Serum-free medium, which may still contain naturally-sourced components
[C12N 2500/92] Medium free of human- or animal-derived components -- C12N 2500/92 covers cell-culture media that contain no human- or animal-derived components, being wholly chemically defined or synthetic. Such media are used to grow microorganisms or enzymes while avoiding variability, pathogen contamination, and ethical concerns associated with biological extracts, and are important in biotechnology, pharmaceutical and research applications.
[C12N 2500/95] Protein-free medium and culture conditions -- A protein-free medium is a cell-culture formulation that supplies all nutrients, growth factors, and buffering capacity needed for microorganisms or enzymes without using animal-derived proteins, enabling defined, reproducible growth conditions and reducing contamination risk. This classification covers the composition of such media and the associated culture parameters such as pH, temperature, and supplementation.
[C12N 2500/98] Xeno-free medium and culture conditions -- The CPC subclass C12N 2500/98 relates to cell-culture technologies that employ a xeno-free medium, meaning the growth formulation contains no animal-derived components. It covers the composition of such media and the associated culture conditions designed to support the growth, maintenance or differentiation of cells while avoiding xenogenic contaminants.
[C12N 2500/99] Serum-free medium -- The C12N 2500/99 entry covers cell-culture media formulated without animal serum, providing nutrients and growth factors for microorganisms or enzymes in biotechnological processes. Such serum-free formulations are designed to reduce variability, avoid contaminating proteins, and meet regulatory or experimental requirements.
[C12N 2501/00] Active agents used in cell culture processes, e.g. differentation -- This CPC subclass pertains to chemicals, biologics or other substances employed to influence cells in vitro, such as agents that induce differentiation, proliferation or maintenance of microorganisms or enzymes. It includes media additives, growth factors, hormones and differentiation inducers used in cell culture processes.
[C12N 2501/01] Modulators of cAMP or cGMP, e.g. non-hydrolysable analogs, phosphodiesterase inhibitors, cholera toxin -- A C12N 2501/01 entry covers compounds that modulate intracellular cAMP or cGMP levels to influence cell-culture processes, such as differentiation. It includes non-hydrolysable cyclic nucleotide analogues, phosphodiesterase inhibitors and agents like cholera toxin that raise cyclic nucleotide concentrations.
[C12N 2501/02] Compounds of the arachidonic acid pathway, e.g. prostaglandins, leukotrienes -- The CPC subclass C12N 2501/02 covers compounds that belong to the arachidonic-acid metabolic pathway, notably prostaglandins, leukotrienes and related molecules, when employed as active agents in cell-culture processes such as differentiation or growth modulation. It includes any use of these eicosanoids for influencing microbial or enzyme-based culture systems.
[C12N 2501/03] Compounds acting on the NO pathway, e.g. nitrososarginine
[C12N 2501/04] Immunosuppressors, e.g. cyclosporin, tacrolimus -- C12N 2501/04 covers immunosuppressive agents used in cell-culture applications, such as cyclosporin and tacrolimus, that modulate immune activity to prevent rejection or influence cellular differentiation. These compounds fall under the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy for microorganisms or enzymes and active agents in cell culture processes.
[C12N 2501/05] Adjuvants -- C12N 2501/05 covers adjuvants employed as active agents in cell-culture processes, such as substances that promote cell growth, differentiation or maintenance. These compounds are used to enhance the physiological environment of microbial or enzymatic cultures in biotechnology and pharmaceutical applications.
[C12N 2501/051] Lipid A (MPA, MPL) -- The C12N 2501/051 class pertains to lipid A molecules, such as monophosphoryl lipid A (MPA) and monophosphoryl lipid A (MPL), when employed as active agents in cell-culture processes, for example to stimulate differentiation or act as immunological adjuvants.
[C12N 2501/052] Lipopolysaccharides [LPS] -- C12N 2501/052 classifies lipopolysaccharides, the complex polysaccharide-lipid molecules that form the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria and are used as active agents in cell-culture processes such as immune stimulation and differentiation. The code groups LPS as biochemical reagents derived from microorganisms for applications in research and biotechnology.
[C12N 2501/054] Muramyle peptides -- This subclass covers muramyl peptides, which are fragments of bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan containing N-acetylmuramic acid linked to amino acids and used as active agents in cell-culture processes such as differentiation.
[C12N 2501/056] Immunostimulating oligonucleotides, e.g. CpG -- These are short synthetic DNA oligonucleotides that contain unmethylated CpG motifs which activate innate immune receptors, serving as active agents to stimulate or direct cell growth and differentiation in culture. They are employed in research and therapeutic contexts to enhance immune responses.
[C12N 2501/06] Anti-neoplasic drugs, anti-retroviral drugs, e.g. azacytidine, cyclophosphamide -- C12N 2501/06 covers anti-neoplastic and anti-retroviral compounds employed as active agents in cell-culture processes, for example azacytidine and cyclophosphamide. The subclass is part of the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy focusing on microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2501/065] Modulators of histone acetylation -- Compounds that influence the addition or removal of acetyl groups on histone proteins, thereby modifying chromatin structure and regulating gene expression in cultured cells, are covered by this class. They include inhibitors or activators of histone acetyltransferases and deacetylases and are used to control cellular differentiation, growth or response to stimuli.
[C12N 2501/07] Heat shock proteins -- Heat shock proteins are a group of molecular chaperones that help maintain protein folding and protect cells from stress-induced damage. In cell-culture applications they are employed to enhance cell viability, promote proper protein expression, and facilitate differentiation or recovery after thermal or chemical stress.
[C12N 2501/10] Growth factors -- C12N 2501/10 covers growth factors, which are natural or recombinant proteins that stimulate cell proliferation, differentiation, or survival and are used as active agents in cell-culture processes. The class also includes methods for producing, purifying, and applying these biomolecules in biotechnological and pharmaceutical applications.
[C12N 2501/105] Insulin-like growth factors [IGF] -- C12N 2501/105 covers the use of insulin-like growth factors (IGFs) as active agents in processes involving cell culture, including their role in stimulating cell growth, differentiation or metabolism. It pertains to biotechnological applications where IGFs are added to culture media to enhance or direct cellular behavior.
[C12N 2501/11] Epidermal growth factor [EGF] -- C12N 2501/11 pertains to the use of epidermal growth factor (EGF) as an active agent in cell-culture processes, especially for inducing cell differentiation. It is classified under biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and refers to biochemical compounds that stimulate growth and differentiation of cultured cells.
[C12N 2501/113] Acidic fibroblast growth factor (aFGF, FGF-1) -- Acidic fibroblast growth factor (aFGF, also known as FGF-1) is a protein that stimulates proliferation and differentiation of a wide range of cell types and is commonly added to culture media to support growth of fibroblasts, stem cells and endothelial cells. In the CPC it falls under C12N 2501/113, the subclass for active agents used in cell-culture processes targeting microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2501/115] Basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF, FGF-2) -- The subclass C12N 2501/115 covers basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF, also called FGF-2), a protein that stimulates proliferation and differentiation of a wide range of cells in vitro and is used as an active agent in cell-culture processes.
[C12N 2501/117] Keratinocyte growth factors (KGF-1, i.e. FGF-7; KGF-2, i.e. FGF-12) -- C12N 2501/117 covers keratinocyte growth factors, specifically KGF-1 (FGF-7) and KGF-2 (FGF-12), when employed as active agents in cell-culture processes to promote cell growth, differentiation or maintenance, particularly for keratinocyte or epithelial cell lines. This subclass falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2501/119] Other fibroblast growth factors, e.g. FGF-4, FGF-8, FGF-10 -- C12N 2501/119 classifies fibroblast growth factor proteins other than the prototypical FGF-1 and FGF-2, such as FGF-4, FGF-8 and FGF-10, that are employed as biologically active agents in cell-culture processes to stimulate growth, proliferation or differentiation of cells. It is part of the chemistry biochemistry subclass for microorganisms or enzymes used as active agents in cell culture differentiation.
[C12N 2501/12] Hepatocyte growth factor [HGF] -- The CPC subclass C12N 2501/12 covers hepatocyte growth factor (HGF) used as an active agent in cell-culture processes, especially to promote cell proliferation, differentiation or maintenance in vitro. It relates to applications of HGF in biotechnology and biomedical research where cultured cells, such as hepatocytes or other cell types, require growth-factor stimulation.
[C12N 2501/125] Stem cell factor [SCF], c-kit ligand [KL] -- C12N 2501/125 covers the protein stem cell factor, also called c-kit ligand, a cytokine that binds the c-kit receptor to regulate proliferation and differentiation of hematopoietic and other stem cells. The entry is used for patents involving SCF as an active agent in cell-culture processes such as promoting cell growth, survival or directed differentiation.
[C12N 2501/13] Nerve growth factor [NGF] -- Nerve growth factor (NGF) is a protein that stimulates the growth, survival and differentiation of neuronal cells. In the CPC scheme it is classified under C12N 2501/13 as an active agent employed in cell culture processes, particularly to induce neuronal differentiation.
[C12N 2501/135] Platelet-derived growth factor [PDGF] -- Platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF) is a signalling protein that promotes cell proliferation, migration and differentiation, commonly employed as an active agent in cell-culture media to support growth of fibroblasts and other cell types. In the CPC it falls under the subclass for active agents used in cell-culture processes.
[C12N 2501/14] Erythropoietin [EPO] -- The CPC subclass C12N 2501/14 relates to the use of erythropoietin as an active agent in cell-culture processes, especially for promoting differentiation or proliferation of target cells. It is positioned under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on biologically derived substances applied in vitro.
[C12N 2501/145] Thrombopoietin [TPO] -- Thrombopoietin (TPO) is a protein cytokine that regulates platelet production by stimulating the proliferation and differentiation of megakaryocyte precursors. In cell-culture applications it is used as an active agent to promote megakaryocyte development and platelet-like cell formation, supporting studies of hematopoiesis and related therapies.
[C12N 2501/148] Transforming growth factor alpha [TGF-a] -- The CPC entry C12N 2501/148 covers transforming growth factor alpha, a peptide cytokine that regulates cell proliferation and differentiation and is employed as an active agent in cell-culture media to promote growth of various mammalian cells.

[C12N 2501/58] Adhesion molecules, e.g. ICAM, VCAM, CD18 (ligand), CD11 (ligand), CD49 (ligand) -- The CPC subclass C12N 2501/58 concerns adhesion-type biomolecules employed as active agents in cell-culture processes. It covers proteins such as ICAM, VCAM, integrin ligands CD18, CD11 and CD49 that modulate cell attachment, signalling and differentiation in vitro.
[C12N 2501/585] Integrins -- The CPC subclass C12N 2501/585 covers integrins, which are cell-surface receptor proteins that mediate adhesion and signaling and are employed as active agents in cell-culture processes such as differentiation, proliferation, or attachment. Patents classified here relate to integrin molecules, fragments, mimetics or their use in biotechnology and pharmaceutical applications.
[C12N 2501/59] Lectins -- Lectins are carbohydrate-binding proteins used as active agents in cell-culture processes, for example to promote cell adhesion, agglutination or differentiation. They find application in biotechnology and biochemistry to manipulate microbial or eukaryotic cells.
[C12N 2501/599] with CD designations not provided for elsewhere -- C12N 2501/599 classifies active agents employed in cell-culture processes, particularly when the material is identified by a CD (cluster of differentiation) designation that is not indexed elsewhere. It captures compositions or methods for influencing cell growth, differentiation or other culture effects where the CD marker is unique or newly introduced. This subclass falls under chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes-active agents used in cell culture.
[C12N 2501/60] Transcription factors -- Transcription factors are proteins or engineered nucleic-acid molecules that bind specific DNA sequences to regulate gene expression in cultured cells, thereby directing growth, differentiation or metabolic activity. In this CPC group they are classified as active agents used in cell-culture processes, especially for manipulating cellular pathways in biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2501/602] Sox-2 -- C12N 2501/602 covers the use of the transcription factor Sox-2 as an active agent in cell-culture processes, particularly to induce or maintain cell differentiation and pluripotency. Sox-2 is employed to modulate gene expression in microbial or mammalian cells for biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2501/603] Oct-3/4 -- The C12N 2501/603 entry pertains to the Oct-3/4 transcription factor used as an active agent in cell-culture processes, especially for inducing or maintaining pluripotency and directing differentiation of mammalian cells. It is classified under agents that modulate cell growth and development.
[C12N 2501/604] Klf-4 -- C12N 2501/604 covers the transcription factor Klf-4 when employed as an active agent in cell-culture processes, such as inducing cellular differentiation. The code falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes and relates to biotechnology applications that manipulate gene expression to control cell fate.
[C12N 2501/605] Nanog -- Nanog is a transcription factor that regulates pluripotency and self-renewal in embryonic stem cells and is employed as an active agent in cell-culture systems to direct differentiation pathways. The classification covers its use in biotechnological processes for maintaining or inducing specific cell states.
[C12N 2501/606] c-Myc -- C12N 2501/606 covers the use of the transcription factor c-Myc as an active agent in cell-culture applications, particularly for influencing cell differentiation, proliferation or reprogramming. The classification applies to methods, compositions or reagents that employ c-Myc to modify biological activity of cultured microorganisms or eukaryotic cells.
[C12N 2501/608] Lin28 -- C12N 2501/608 covers the Lin28 family of RNA-binding proteins that modulate microRNA processing and maintain pluripotency, and are employed as active agents in cell-culture systems to influence cell growth and differentiation.
[C12N 2501/65] MicroRNA -- C12N 2501/65 covers microRNA applied as active agents in cell-culture processes such as inducing or directing cellular differentiation. These short, non-coding RNA molecules modulate gene expression post-transcriptionally, and are used to control the phenotype of cultured microorganisms or eukaryotic cells.
[C12N 2501/70] Enzymes -- This classification covers enzymes used as active agents in cell culture processes, such as the differentiation of microorganisms or cells. It includes recombinant, purified, or immobilized enzymes that modify growth media, stimulate or inhibit cellular functions, or facilitate bioprocessing steps.
[C12N 2501/71] Oxidoreductases (EC 1.) -- C12N 2501/71 designates oxidoreductase enzymes (EC 1) that serve as active agents in cell-culture processes, such as promoting differentiation. The subclass includes any oxidoreductase used to modify the redox state of substrates within microbial or enzymatic cultures, facilitating biochemical transformations essential for cell growth or metabolic studies.
[C12N 2501/72] Transferases [EC 2.] -- C12N 2501/72 designates transferase enzymes (EC 2) used as active agents in cell-culture processes, such as differentiation, within the CPC hierarchy under chemistry, biochemistry and microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2501/724] Glycosyltransferases (EC 2.4.) -- C12N 2501/724 covers glycosyltransferases (EC 2.4), enzymes that catalyze the transfer of sugar residues to acceptor molecules and are employed as active agents in cell-culture processes such as differentiation and biosynthesis. The subclass groups these biocatalysts under microbiology-related chemistry for biotechnology applications.
[C12N 2501/727] Kinases (EC 2.7.) -- C12N 2501/727 identifies kinases, enzymes classified under EC 2.7 that catalyze the transfer of phosphate groups to substrates. The classification falls under active agents used in cell-culture processes such as differentiation, linking biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes to chemical applications.
[C12N 2501/73] Hydrolases (EC 3.) -- The CPC subclass C12N 2501/73 groups hydrolase enzymes (EC 3) that are used as active agents in cell-culture processes, including those that promote substrate breakdown, modify the extracellular environment, or induce differentiation of cultured microorganisms or mammalian cells.
[C12N 2501/734] Proteases (EC 3.4.) -- The subclass C12N 2501/734 covers proteolytic enzymes (EC 3.4) used as active agents in cell-culture processes, such as promoting cell detachment, remodeling the extracellular matrix, or influencing cellular differentiation. It includes microbial or recombinant proteases applied to modify the in-vitro environment for cultured cells.

[C12N 2502/256] Renal cells -- C12N 2502/256 covers biotechnological methods that co-culture renal cells with microorganisms or enzymes. It pertains to the preparation, manipulation, or analysis of kidney-derived cells in a mixed biological system for research, drug screening, or therapeutic production, falling under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and coculture with.
[C12N 2502/27] Lung cells, respiratory tract cells -- C12N 2502/27 refers to inventions that involve coculturing lung or other respiratory-tract cells with additional cell types. The classification covers methods, compositions or devices that enable such mixed-cell cultures for studying respiratory biology, disease models, drug screening or tissue engineering. It falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Coculture with.
[C12N 2502/28] Vascular endothelial cells -- The CPC subclass C12N 2502/28 pertains to the use of vascular endothelial cells in coculture systems, covering methods, compositions and assays where these cells are grown together with other microorganisms or enzymes to study interactions, facilitate tissue engineering, drug screening or model physiological processes.
[C12N 2502/30] tumour cells -- C12N 2502/30 covers inventions that involve co-culturing tumour cells with microorganisms or enzymes, including methods, systems or compositions for growing tumour cells together with other biological agents for research, diagnostic or therapeutic applications.

[C12N 2502/99] genetically modified cells -- C12N 2502/99 covers cells that have been genetically altered through recombinant DNA, gene-editing or similar techniques and are employed in coculture systems. It includes microorganisms or eukaryotic cells engineered to express foreign genes, produce recombinant proteins, or exhibit modified metabolic pathways for biotechnological or research purposes.
[C12N 2503/00] Use of cells in diagnostics -- C12N 2503/00 designates the use of living or cultured cells as tools in diagnostic methods, including assays that detect, identify or quantify disease markers, pathogens or genetic traits by employing cell-based biosensors, reporter cells or similar techniques. It is situated within the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2503/02] Drug screening -- This subclass covers procedures that employ living cells to evaluate potential pharmaceutical agents, including assays that measure biological activity, toxicity or interaction with cellular targets for the purpose of identifying or characterizing drug candidates. It falls under the broader group concerning the use of cells in diagnostic applications within the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes hierarchy.
[C12N 2503/04] Screening or testing on artificial tissues -- The code covers methods that use artificial tissues, such as cultured cells or engineered tissue models, to perform screening or testing, for example to assess drug activity, toxicity, or diagnostic markers. It relates to biochemical or microbiological techniques that evaluate biological responses in these synthetic tissue systems.
[C12N 2503/06] Screening or testing on artificial skin -- C12N 2503/06 covers methods that employ artificial skin models to screen or test substances such as drugs, chemicals, or biologics for biological activity, toxicity or diagnostic purposes. The classification is used for inventions that use cultured cell layers that mimic human skin in vitro.
[C12N 2506/00] Differentiation of animal cells from one lineage to another -- This CPC subclass covers processes and methods that induce an animal cell to change from its original differentiated state into a different cell lineage, including transdifferentiation and cellular reprogramming, usually performed in vitro with genetic, chemical or environmental cues. It relates to biotechnological applications such as tissue engineering, regenerative medicine, and production of specialized cell types.
[C12N 2506/02] from embryonic cells -- The CPC subclass C12N 2506/02 covers methods, reagents and processes that induce or control the differentiation of animal cells that originate from embryonic cells into a different cellular lineage, encompassing genetic, biochemical and culture-based techniques used to direct cell fate.
[C12N 2506/025] from extra-embryonic cells, e.g. trophoblast, placenta -- C12N 2506/025 covers methods and compositions for inducing the differentiation of animal cells from extra-embryonic sources such as trophoblast or placenta into other lineages. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy of biochemistry and microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on cell-type conversion for research or therapeutic applications.

It falls within the chemistry sub-class for biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes and addresses cell-culture techniques that reprogram or convert cell fate without involving embryonic material.

This entry falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes.
