[C12N 2506/45] from artificially induced pluripotent stem cells -- The code C12N 2506/45 refers to methods that convert artificially induced pluripotent stem cells into animal cells of a different lineage, i.e., directed differentiation of iPSC-derived cells. It is situated in the hierarchy chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes and focuses on cell-type conversion rather than natural development.
[C12N 2509/00] Methods for the dissociation of cells, e.g. specific use of enzymes -- C12N 2509/00 covers methods for dissociating cells, particularly those that employ specific enzymes to break cell-cell or cell-matrix contacts. The classification includes protocols, enzyme selections, and conditions used to obtain single-cell suspensions from tissues for research or therapeutic purposes. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry and microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2509/10] Mechanical dissociation -- C12N 2509/10 concerns methods that separate cells or tissues by applying mechanical forces such as grinding, shearing, filtration, or sonication, without using chemical or enzymatic agents. It relates to the dissociation of microorganisms or enzymes for downstream biochemical or biotechnological processes.
[C12N 2510/00] Genetically modified cells -- It pertains to the creation, manipulation and use of cells whose genetic material has been altered by recombinant DNA, gene editing or other techniques, for purposes such as production of bioproducts, research or therapy. The classification includes host cells, transformed microorganisms, engineered cell lines and their culture processes.
[C12N 2510/02] Cells for production -- The C12N 2510/02 CPC subclass covers genetically engineered cells that are employed as biological factories to produce a desired product. It includes microorganisms, plant or animal cells modified to synthesize proteins, metabolites, vaccines, enzymes or other biotechnological compounds, and specifies the cell line itself as the inventive subject rather than the downstream process. This category is used to classify patents where the cell's genetic alteration is central to its production capability.
[C12N 2510/04] Immortalised cells -- C12N 2510/04 covers immortalised cells, which are genetically modified cells engineered to proliferate indefinitely without senescence. These cells retain the characteristics of the original cell type while providing a stable, long-term source for research, drug screening, and biotechnological production. The classification falls within the chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes hierarchy.
[C12N 2511/00] Cells for large scale production -- C12N 2511/00 covers biotechnological processes that employ living cell cultures specifically engineered or selected for the large-scale manufacturing of desired products such as pharmaceuticals, enzymes, or other bio-molecules. It includes methods for optimizing cell growth, maintenance, and productivity in industrial bioreactors.
[C12N 2513/00] 3D culture -- The C12N 2513/00 entry covers methods, apparatus and compositions for cultivating microorganisms, cells or enzymes in three-dimensional environments such as scaffolds, hydrogels, or other carriers that mimic natural tissue architecture. It includes the design of 3D matrices, culture systems and related processes that enable more realistic physiological behavior than conventional two-dimensional cultures.
[C12N 2517/00] Cells related to new breeds of animals -- C12N 2517/00 covers biological cells that have been derived from or engineered to create new animal breeds, including genetically modified or selectively bred cell lines used for breeding, research, or commercial purposes. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, and the microorganisms or enzymes sub-section of the CPC.
[C12N 2517/02] Cells from transgenic animals -- The code C12N 2517/02 covers cells that have been obtained from animals whose genomes have been altered by genetic engineering. It includes any cell types, primary or cultured, originating from transgenic animals and used for research, production of recombinant proteins, or development of biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2517/04] Cells produced using nuclear transfer -- C12N 2517/04 covers cells that are generated by nuclear transfer, a technique in which the nucleus of a donor cell is introduced into an enucleated recipient cell to create a genetically identical or modified cell line. The classification applies to cloned or otherwise engineered animal cells used for breeding new animal varieties, biomedical research, or biotechnological production, and falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2517/10] Conditioning of cells for -- The C12N 2517/10 subclass covers procedures that condition, treat or prepare cells for use in creating new animal breeds, including pre-culture steps, media modifications, stress-inducing regimes and other techniques that optimise cellular performance before genetic manipulation, cloning or other breeding applications. It lies within the broader domain of biochemical methods applied to microorganisms or enzymes, specifically focusing on cell-based processes in animal biotechnology.
[C12N 2521/00] Culture process characterised by the use of hydrostatic pressure, flow or shear forces -- This CPC subclass covers methods for cultivating microorganisms or enzymes where the growth environment is subjected to hydrostatic pressure, fluid flow or shear stress, using these physical forces to influence cell metabolism or product yield. It relates to culture processes that apply pressure or mechanical forces to improve growth, product formation or stress tolerance.
[C12N 2521/10] Sound, e.g. ultrasounds -- C12N 2521/10 covers biotechnological culture processes that employ sound, including ultrasounds, as a hydrostatic, flow-or shear-type force to affect microorganisms or enzymes. It includes methods where acoustic energy is applied to stimulate growth, modify metabolism or enhance product formation, and is used for patents describing such sound-based culture techniques.
[C12N 2523/00] Culture process characterised by temperature -- C12N 2523/00 relates to culture processes for microorganisms or enzymes in which temperature is the principal defining parameter, covering methods that specify temperature ranges, steps, shifts, gradients, or control regimes during growth, fermentation, or other biotechnological operations, and the associated equipment used to maintain or adjust these temperatures.
[C12N 2525/00] Culture process characterised by gravity, e.g. microgravity -- The code refers to methods for cultivating microorganisms, cells or enzymes where the effect of gravity is a defining factor, including processes performed under altered-gravity conditions such as microgravity. It covers techniques that exploit or control gravitational forces to influence growth, productivity or product characteristics.
[C12N 2527/00] Culture process characterised by the use of mechanical forces, e.g. strain, vibration -- C12N 2527/00 refers to culture methods for microorganisms or enzymes that deliberately apply mechanical forces such as strain, vibration, shear or acoustic energy. These forces are used to improve mixing, stimulate growth, or alter product formation during biotechnological cultivation processes.
[C12N 2529/00] Culture process characterised by the use of electromagnetic stimulation -- C12N 2529/00 covers methods for growing microorganisms or enzymes in which electromagnetic fields, such as radio-frequency, microwave, or static magnetic fields, are applied to influence the culture. The classification includes processes that use these fields to enhance growth rates, product formation, or metabolic activity and may describe specific apparatus or parameters for the stimulation. It is part of the chemistry hierarchy under biochemistry and microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2529/10] Stimulation by light -- C12N 2529/10 refers to culture processes for microorganisms or enzymes that are driven by light stimulation. It includes methods where electromagnetic radiation, typically visible or ultraviolet light, is applied to promote growth, metabolic activity, or product formation in biotechnological fermentations.
[C12N 2531/00] Microcarriers -- C12N 2531/00 covers microcarriers, which are small solid or gel particles that provide a surface for the attachment and growth of cells, microorganisms or enzymes in biotechnological processes. The code falls under Chemistry to Biochemistry to Microorganisms or enzymes and is used for technologies such as cell culture, tissue engineering and scaled-up production of biologics.
[C12N 2533/00] Supports or coatings for cell culture, characterised by material -- C12N 2533/00 covers materials employed as supports or surface coatings for cell-culture applications, where the defining feature is the nature of the material itself. It includes polymers, gels, membranes, fibres and other substrates that provide a defined environment for the attachment, growth or differentiation of microorganisms or cells in vitro.
[C12N 2533/10] Mineral substrates -- C12N 2533/10 covers mineral substrates employed as supports or coating materials for cell-culture applications. The entry includes inorganic materials such as calcium phosphates, hydroxyapatite, silicates and related compounds that provide a solid surface for the growth of microorganisms, tissues or enzymatic systems. It is used to classify inventions that exploit these mineral matrices to improve cell attachment, viability or productivity.
[C12N 2533/12] Glass -- C12N 2533/12 refers to the use of glass as a support or coating material for cell culture, particularly in applications involving microorganisms or enzymes. It falls under the chemistry-biochemistry branch of the CPC, covering materials that provide a substrate for biological growth or enzyme immobilisation.
[C12N 2533/14] Ceramic -- C12N 2533/14 pertains to ceramic materials employed as supports or coatings in cell-culture applications. It classifies biotechnological processes where microorganisms or enzymes are grown on ceramic substrates, which may provide structural stability, biocompatibility or specific surface properties.
[C12N 2533/18] Calcium salts, e.g. apatite, Mineral components from bones, teeth, shells -- This subclass concerns calcium salts such as apatite and other mineral components obtained from bone, teeth or shells, used as supports or coatings in cell-culture applications. It is part of the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms-enzymes hierarchy, focusing on material-based cell-culture substrates.
[C12N 2533/20] Small organic molecules -- C12N 2533/20 covers the use of small organic molecules as materials for supports or coatings applied in cell-culture systems, where the molecules function as substrates, adhesives or functional layers to promote cell growth or maintenance. This subclass falls under the broader category of microorganisms or enzymes and specifically addresses cell-culture supports characterized by their chemical composition.
[C12N 2533/30] Synthetic polymers -- C12N 2533/30 relates to synthetic polymers employed as supports or coatings for cell culture, providing a material substrate for the growth of microorganisms or enzymes. This classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry and the sub-category of supports or coatings characterised by material.
[C12N 2533/32] Polylysine, polyornithine -- C12N 2533/32 covers polymeric amino acids such as polylysine and polyornithine used as material-based supports or coatings for cell culture. These substances provide a positively charged surface that promotes cell attachment and growth, often employed in tissue-engineering and in vitro assays.
[C12N 2533/40] Polyhydroxyacids, e.g. polymers of glycolic or lactic acid (PGA, PLA, PLGA) -- C12N 2533/40 covers polyhydroxyacids such as polyglycolic acid, polylactic acid and their copolymers, used as supportive or coating materials for cell culture. These biodegradable polymers are employed to create scaffolds or surface treatments that enhance cell attachment and growth.
[C12N 2533/50] Proteins -- The CPC subclass C12N 2533/50 covers proteins that are employed as support materials or coatings for cell-culture applications, where the defining feature is the material itself. It falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and specifically relates to cell-culture supports characterized by their protein composition.
[C12N 2533/52] Fibronectin -- C12N 2533/52 designates fibronectin, a high-molecular-weight glycoprotein used as a material for supports or coatings in cell-culture systems. The subclass covers inventions where fibronectin is applied to enhance cell attachment, growth or differentiation on engineered surfaces.
[C12N 2533/54] Collagen -- C12N 2533/54 pertains to collagen used as a material for supports or coatings in cell-culture applications. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on biomaterials that provide a scaffold for cell growth and attachment. This subclass groups collagen-based substrates employed in biomedical and laboratory culture systems.
[C12N 2533/56] Fibrin -- Fibrin is a protein polymer formed from fibrinogen during blood clotting, used as a biologically derived matrix for cell culture. It provides a natural scaffold that supports cell attachment, growth and differentiation, and can be applied as a coating or three-dimensional support.
[C12N 2533/70] Polysaccharides -- C12N 2533/70 relates to the use of polysaccharides as materials for supports or coatings in cell-culture applications. It covers biotechnological methods where carbohydrate-based matrices provide surfaces for the growth, attachment or immobilisation of microorganisms or enzymes, facilitating processes such as tissue engineering, fermentation or biocatalysis. This subclass is part of the broader chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy for microbial and enzymatic systems.
[C12N 2533/72] Chitin, chitosan -- C12N 2533/72 covers the use of chitin and chitosan as material-based supports or coatings for cell-culture applications, providing a biomaterial surface that promotes cell growth. It falls under the chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes hierarchy.
[C12N 2533/74] Alginate -- The CPC subclass C12N 2533/74 covers alginate, a polysaccharide derived from seaweed, when used as a support or coating material for cell-culture applications. It includes formulations where the alginate itself provides the matrix or surface that sustains the growth of microorganisms or mammalian cells.
[C12N 2533/76] Agarose, agar-agar -- C12N 2533/76 covers agarose and agar-agar used as supports or coatings for cell culture, where the material itself provides the substrate.
[C12N 2533/78] Cellulose -- C12N 2533/78 refers to cellulose used as a support or coating material for cell culture applications. The subclass covers cellulose-based matrices that provide a surface for the growth, attachment, or maintenance of microorganisms or enzymes in biotechnological processes.
[C12N 2533/80] Hyaluronan -- C12N 2533/80 covers hyaluronan, a high-molecular-weight glycosaminoglycan employed as a material for supports or coatings in cell-culture applications. It includes compositions where hyaluronan forms the primary matrix or surface layer to enhance cell adhesion, proliferation, or differentiation.
[C12N 2533/90] Substrates of biological origin, e.g. extracellular matrix, decellularised tissue -- This subclass concerns biologically derived support or coating materials for cell culture, such as extracellular-matrix components or decellularised tissues. It includes natural substrates that provide a scaffold to promote cell attachment, growth, or differentiation in biotechnological and medical applications.
[C12N 2533/92] Amnion -- The CPC subclass C12N 2533/92 covers the use of amnion, a natural membrane from the innermost fetal sac, as a support or coating material in cell-culture applications. It includes methods and compositions wherein the amnion provides a biologically compatible substrate for growing cells and tissues.
[C12N 2535/00] Supports or coatings for cell culture characterised by topography -- C12N 2535/00 covers materials used as supports or coatings in cell culture that are defined by their surface topography. It includes substrates engineered with specific micro- or nanostructures to influence cell attachment, growth and differentiation. The classification is applied to patents on patterned scaffolds, textured plates and other topographically defined culture surfaces.
[C12N 2535/10] Patterned coating -- C12N 2535/10 covers patterned coatings used as supports or surface layers for cell culture that are defined by their topographical features. These coatings provide a defined micro- or nanoscale relief to influence cell attachment, growth, or enzyme activity, and are applied in biotechnology, tissue engineering, and related microbiological processes.
[C12N 2537/00] Supports and/or coatings for cell culture characterised by physical or chemical treatment -- C12N 2537/00 covers materials used as supports or surface coatings for culturing cells that have been modified by physical or chemical treatments to influence cell adhesion, growth or differentiation. The classification includes treated polymers, ceramics, metals or composites whose surface properties are altered by methods such as plasma treatment, roughening, grafting or coating with bioactive molecules.
[C12N 2537/10] Cross-linking -- C12N 2537/10 covers methods of cross-linking that create or modify supports and coatings used in cell-culture applications, where the support has been physically or chemically treated to affect microorganisms or enzymes. It is situated under chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes in the CPC hierarchy.
[C12N 2539/00] Supports and/or coatings for cell culture characterised by properties -- C12N 2539/00 covers supports and coatings used in cell-culture systems that are defined by specific physical, chemical or biological properties, such as surface texture, functional groups or biocompatibility, to enhance the growth or activity of microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2539/10] Coating allowing for selective detachment of cells, e.g. thermoreactive coating -- C12N 2539/10 covers supports or coatings used in cell culture that are designed to allow selective detachment of cells, such as thermoreactive surfaces that release cells when temperature changes. The classification emphasizes the functional properties of the coating that enable controlled removal of specific cells without harming the culture substrate.
[C12N 2710/00] Viruses -- C12N 2710/00 is a CPC entry for viruses within the chemistry hierarchy, specifically under biochemistry and microorganisms or enzymes. It covers the definition, isolation, structural and functional characterization, and applications of viruses, including virus-derived vectors and genetic elements used in research, diagnostics, and biotechnology.
[C12N 2710/00011] Details
[C12N 2710/00021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- C12N 2710/00021 pertains to patents directed to viruses themselves, including newly isolated strains, engineered mutants, and the nucleic-acid sequences that define them.
[C12N 2710/00022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- C12N 2710/00022 pertains to inventions dealing with novel viral proteins or individual genes, as well as newly discovered structural or functional characteristics of already known viral proteins or genes. It includes any biotechnological methods, recombinant DNA constructs, or therapeutic applications that exploit these new viral elements.
[C12N 2710/00023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- The C12N 2710/00023 entry covers virus-like particles (VLPs), which are non-infectious structures that mimic the morphology of viruses but lack viral genetic material. It includes methods for producing, purifying, and characterising VLPs, as well as their use as vaccines, diagnostic tools, or delivery vehicles. This subclass falls under the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy for microorganisms or enzymes, specifically viruses.
[C12N 2710/00031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- The subclass C12N 2710/00031 covers applications of viruses that are not intended for therapy or vaccination, such as using viral particles or components as biocidal agents, disinfectants, or in other non-medical industrial processes. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy for microorganisms or enzymes, specifically viruses.
[C12N 2710/00032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The C12N 2710/00032 subclass addresses applications of viruses as therapeutic agents that are not intended as vaccines, including their use as cytolytic or oncolytic agents to destroy target cells such as tumor cells. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and viruses.
[C12N 2710/00033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/00033 covers the use of viral proteins as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, such as agents that induce apoptosis or provide anti-inflammatory effects.
[C12N 2710/00034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- The CPC code C12N 2710/00034 covers biotechnological methods that employ whole viruses or viral parts as vaccines, including live-attenuated or inactivated viruses, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins, aimed at inducing protective immune responses against the targeted pathogen.
[C12N 2710/00041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- The CPC entry C12N 2710/00041 covers the use of whole viruses, viral particles or isolated viral components as vectors for transferring genetic material into target cells. It includes methods for gene therapy, vaccine delivery, and biotechnological applications where the virus serves as a carrier for nucleic acids or functional proteins.
[C12N 2710/00042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- C12N 2710/00042 classifies the use of whole viruses or viral particles as carriers for small organic substances. It includes methods where the virus or its capsid encapsulates pharmaceuticals, diagnostics or other molecules for delivery into cells or tissues.
[C12N 2710/00043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2710/00043 covers the use of entire viral genomes or selected viral genetic elements as vectors for introducing nucleic acids into host cells, encompassing engineered viruses employed in gene therapy, vaccine production, and other biotechnological applications where viral DNA or RNA serves as a delivery vehicle.
[C12N 2710/00044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- A chimeric viral vector that incorporates genetic components from different virus species is used as a production system to generate a second, distinct viral vector. The design combines heterologous viral elements to improve manufacturing efficiency, stability or host range while preserving the functional properties of the target vector. This approach falls under the CPC subclass for engineered viruses employed in biotechnological production processes.
[C12N 2710/00045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- C12N 2710/00045 covers methods and compositions that equip viral vectors with specialized targeting mechanisms to direct them to particular cell types or tissues. The classification includes engineered envelope proteins, receptor ligands, or other molecular tags that improve specificity and reduce off-target effects in gene therapy or vaccine applications.
[C12N 2710/00051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- C12N 2710/00051 covers techniques for producing and purifying viral material, including methods to cultivate viruses in host cells, harvest viral particles, and apply purification steps such as filtration, centrifugation, or chromatographic separation to obtain clean virus preparations for research or commercial use.
[C12N 2710/00052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- C12N 2710/00052 covers methods and compositions that use complementing cell lines together with packaging systems to generate recombinant viruses or virus-like particles. It addresses the provision of missing viral functions by host cells to enable production of infectious or non-infectious viral particles for research, vaccine or gene-therapy applications.
[C12N 2710/00061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- A CPC entry for viruses that covers techniques for rendering a virus non-infectious or less pathogenic, including chemical, physical, enzymatic or genetic methods of inactivation or attenuation. These methods are used to produce safe viral vaccines, to deactivate pathogens in laboratory work, and to create attenuated strains for research or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2710/00062] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2710/00062 covers viruses that are obtained, altered or used through genetic engineering, including recombinant virus vectors and genetically modified viral particles for research, diagnostic or therapeutic purposes. It falls within the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes hierarchy, specifying engineered viral strains rather than naturally occurring ones.
[C12N 2710/00063] by chemical treatment -- The code C12N 2710/00063 covers chemical treatments applied to viruses, including methods for inactivating, modifying, or otherwise manipulating viral particles through the use of chemical agents. It pertains to biotechnological processes that employ chemicals to affect viral structure, replication, or infectivity, facilitating vaccine production, diagnostics, or antiviral research.
[C12N 2710/00064] by serial passage -- This classification covers methods of modifying viruses through repeated culture in a host or cell line, known as serial passage. The technique is used to generate strains with altered properties such as attenuation, increased virulence, or changed host range, often for vaccine research or virological studies.
[C12N 2710/00071] Demonstrated -- C12N 2710/00071 covers biotechnological inventions that demonstrate the presence, identity or activity of a virus, including methods, assays, kits or diagnostic procedures that provide proof of a viral agent. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses and relates to demonstrated viral detection or confirmation.
[C12N 2710/00088] for redistribution -- C12N 2710/00088 pertains to viruses that are supplied for redistribution, including viral stocks, vectors or kits intended to be transferred to other parties for further research, development or production. The classification covers the provision of these viral materials as a commercial or collaborative service rather than their initial creation or specific use.
[C12N 2710/10011] Adenoviridae -- C12N 2710/10011 designates the family Adenoviridae, a group of non-enveloped double-stranded DNA viruses that infect vertebrate hosts and are used in gene-therapy vectors and vaccine development. The class encompasses the structure, replication, and manipulation of adenoviral particles for biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2710/10021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- C12N 2710/10021 covers inventions that concern viruses in their entirety, such as newly isolated strains, genetically altered mutants, or the nucleic-acid sequences that define them. The subclass is used to classify patent claims focusing on the virus itself rather than on applications or treatments.
[C12N 2710/10022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The subclass C12N 2710/10022 covers inventions that disclose previously unknown viral proteins or individual viral genes, as well as newly identified structural or functional features of already known viral proteins or genes. It includes engineered variants, novel functional domains, and detailed characterisation that enable biotechnological, diagnostic or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2710/10023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- C12N 2710/10023 covers virus-like particles (VLPs), which are non-infectious structures that mimic the morphology and antigenic properties of viruses but lack viral genetic material. The code is used for inventions involving the design, production, or application of VLPs in vaccines, diagnostics, or delivery systems, reflecting their role within the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms-enzymes-viruses hierarchy.
[C12N 2710/10031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2710/10031 covers inventions that use viruses for purposes other than therapy or vaccination, such as biocidal or disinfectant applications. The subclass falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Viruses and defines non-medical functions of viral agents.
[C12N 2710/10032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- This subclass covers methods that employ viruses as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, including oncolytic viruses that selectively infect and destroy target cells such as cancer cells. It encompasses the design, modification and application of viral vectors or wild-type viruses for therapeutic purposes beyond immunization.
[C12N 2710/10033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10033 covers inventions that employ viral proteins as therapeutic agents without forming vaccines, such as proteins that trigger apoptosis or exert anti-inflammatory activity. It falls under the hierarchy chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses.
[C12N 2710/10034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein
[C12N 2710/10041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10041 covers methods that use whole viruses, viral particles, or isolated viral components as vectors to deliver genetic material, therapeutic agents or other substances into target cells. It includes applications such as gene therapy, vaccine development and molecular-biology techniques that rely on viral-mediated transport.
[C12N 2710/10042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- This subclass covers the use of whole viruses or virus-derived particles as delivery vehicles, for example to encapsulate and transport small organic molecules such as drugs or imaging agents. The focus is on employing the viral structure as a carrier rather than on the therapeutic activity of the virus itself.
[C12N 2710/10043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2710/10043 pertains to the use of whole viral genomes or fragments thereof as carriers for foreign genetic material. It includes inventions where viruses function as vectors to introduce, replicate or express nucleic acids in host cells, such as in gene-therapy, vaccine development or recombinant expression systems.
[C12N 2710/10044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- The class C12N 2710/10044 covers chimeric viral vectors that incorporate genetic elements from different viruses to serve as carriers for producing another viral vector. It specifically addresses constructs where heterologous viral sequences are combined to improve replication, packaging or targeting in virus-based manufacturing.
[C12N 2710/10045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- A special targeting system for viral vectors comprises engineered components that direct the vectors to particular cell types or tissues, improving delivery precision for gene-therapy or vaccine applications. It may involve modified capsids, ligand attachments, or receptor-binding motifs that recognize specific cellular markers.
[C12N 2710/10051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- C12N 2710/10051 covers techniques for producing or purifying viral material, including processes that isolate, concentrate, or otherwise prepare viruses for research, diagnostics, or therapeutic use. The methods may involve cell culture, fermentation, chromatography, filtration, or other biotechnological steps to obtain virus particles or viral components in a usable form. This subclass falls under the CPC hierarchy of Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Viruses.
[C12N 2710/10052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The C12N 2710/10052 subclass covers technologies that use engineered or complementing host cells together with specialized packaging systems to produce whole viruses or viral particles, often for applications such as gene-therapy vectors, vaccine manufacturing, or research on viral replication. It focuses on the combination of cell-based complementation and particle assembly methods.
[C12N 2710/10061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- C12N 2710/10061 relates to biotechnological procedures that render viruses non-infectious or less virulent, including chemical, physical, or genetic approaches used to inactivate or attenuate viral particles for vaccine production, diagnostic reagents, or laboratory safety. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Viruses.
[C12N 2710/10062] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2710/10062, within the Cooperative Patent Classification for chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and viruses, covers inventions that employ genetic engineering techniques to modify viral genomes. This includes the insertion, deletion, or alteration of nucleic acid sequences to confer new properties or functionalities.
[C12N 2710/10063] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2710/10063 refers to processes for treating viruses with chemical agents to inactivate, remove, or modify them. It covers the use of disinfectants, solvents, surfactants, oxidizing or reducing chemicals, and other reagents applied to viral preparations, surfaces, or products for decontamination or functional alteration.
[C12N 2710/10064] by serial passage -- C12N 2710/10064 covers methods of obtaining or modifying viruses by serial passage, wherein a virus is repeatedly transferred to fresh host cells or organisms to select for desired traits such as increased replication, altered host range, or reduced virulence. This technique is commonly used in virus strain improvement, attenuation for vaccine development, and studies of viral evolution.
[C12N 2710/10071] Demonstrated -- This CPC subclass covers methods for demonstrating viruses, including detection, identification and confirmation of viral presence in a sample. It is situated under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, viruses.
[C12N 2710/10088] for redistribution -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10088 covers methods and compositions for the redistribution of viruses, including techniques for moving, delivering or spreading viral particles or vectors within a host, between hosts, or in the environment. It is nested under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses.
[C12N 2710/10111] Atadenovirus, e.g. ovine adenovirus D -- C12N 2710/10111 covers atadenoviruses, a group of dsDNA viruses that includes ovine adenovirus D. The entry pertains to the biological material, cloning, propagation or use of these viruses in biotechnology, such as vaccine or gene-delivery applications. It falls under the CPC hierarchy chemistry-biology-biochemistry-microorganisms-enzymes-viruses.
[C12N 2710/10121] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- C12N 2710/10121 classifies inventions that deal directly with viruses, including novel isolates, engineered mutants and the nucleic-acid sequences that define them. It covers biotechnological applications such as virus discovery, genetic modification, and the use of viral genomes for research, diagnostics or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2710/10122] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The CPC entry C12N 2710/10122 covers inventions concerning newly identified viral proteins or individual genes, as well as novel structural or functional features of already known viral proteins or genes. It pertains to biotechnological developments in virology within the broader fields of chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2710/10123] Virus like particles [VLP] -- This classification covers virus-like particles, non-infectious structures that mimic the organization and shape of authentic viruses but lack viral genetic material. It is applied to inventions concerning the preparation, composition, and use of VLPs in areas such as vaccines, diagnostics, and nanotechnology.
[C12N 2710/10131] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- This classification covers non-medical applications of viruses, such as using viral particles or components as disinfectants, biocontrol agents, or in other industrial processes, excluding therapeutic or vaccine uses. It falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, viruses.
[C12N 2710/10132] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- C12N 2710/10132 covers inventions that employ viruses as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, such as cytolytic or oncolytic agents used to destroy target cells. It is situated within the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy under microorganisms or enzymes, specifically viruses.
[C12N 2710/10133] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- This CPC subclass covers the therapeutic use of viral proteins that are not employed as vaccines, including applications such as inducing apoptosis or providing anti-inflammatory effects. The entry is identified by code C12N 2710/10133 in the Cooperative Patent Classification used by the EPO and USPTO.
[C12N 2710/10134] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- C12N 2710/10134 covers methods that employ whole viruses or viral parts as vaccines, including live-attenuated strains, inactivated viruses, virus-like particles and purified viral proteins. The classification situates these approaches within the chemistry of biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically viruses.
[C12N 2710/10141] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- The CPC class C12N 2710/10141 covers inventions that employ whole viruses, viral particles, or components derived from viruses as delivery vectors for genetic material, therapeutic agents, or other functional cargo. It relates to biotechnology applications where viral mechanisms are harnessed for targeted transport in medical, agricultural, or industrial settings.
[C12N 2710/10142] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- The subclass C12N 2710/10142 covers the use of whole viruses or virus-derived particles as carriers that encapsulate and transport small organic molecules, such as drugs or imaging agents, to target sites. It includes methods and compositions where the viral vector serves primarily as a delivery vehicle rather than as a therapeutic agent itself.
[C12N 2710/10143] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2710/10143 classifies the use of whole viral genomes or specific viral genetic elements as vectors for delivering genes into target cells. It encompasses technologies that exploit viral structures to introduce, express, or modify genetic material for purposes such as gene therapy, vaccine development, and recombinant protein production. This entry falls within the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and viruses.
[C12N 2710/10144] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10144 covers chimeric viral vectors that incorporate genetic or structural elements from different viruses in order to serve as platforms for the manufacture of a second, distinct viral vector. It addresses designs where heterologous viral components are combined to enhance vector production, stability or targeting, and falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and viruses.
[C12N 2710/10145] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10145 covers technologies that provide specialised targeting systems for viral vectors, enabling selective delivery of genetic material to particular cell types or tissues. It includes engineered modifications of viral surface proteins, attachment of targeting ligands or synthetic adapters, and related methods that improve the specificity and safety of gene-therapy, vaccine or gene-editing applications.
[C12N 2710/10151] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- C12N 2710/10151 refers to techniques for producing or purifying viral particles or related material, encompassing processes such as cultivation, harvesting, concentration, and removal of contaminants. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy for biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes, specifically viruses, and is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify patent documents on viral manufacturing methods.
[C12N 2710/10152] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- A method that uses engineered complementing cell lines together with dedicated packaging systems to assemble and release recombinant viruses or virus-like particles, enabling production of viral vectors, vaccines or research tools without requiring the full viral genome in the host cell.
[C12N 2710/10161] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- A method under the CPC code C12N 2710/10161 concerns techniques for rendering viruses non-infectious or less virulent, either by chemical, physical, or biological means. It covers procedures that inactivate viral particles or attenuate their pathogenicity for use in research, vaccine production, or safety testing.
[C12N 2710/10162] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2710/10162 designates processes that produce or modify viruses through genetic-engineering techniques such as recombinant DNA, genome editing, or synthetic biology. It includes the creation of recombinant viral vectors, engineered virus strains and genetically altered viral particles for research, therapeutic or diagnostic use, and is situated under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Viruses.
[C12N 2710/10163] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2710/10163 covers chemical methods for treating viruses, including the use of disinfectants, inactivating agents, or other chemical processes to alter viral particles for research, vaccine production, or sterilization. The subclass falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes, focusing specifically on chemical treatment of viruses.

[C12N 2710/10171] Demonstrated -- This CPC subclass covers viruses that have been experimentally demonstrated, meaning their presence, isolation, replication or functional activity has been proven. It includes the methods, assays and evidence used to establish the existence of a specific virus.
[C12N 2710/10188] for redistribution -- This CPC subclass covers inventions directed at the redistribution of viruses, i.e., methods, compositions or devices that move viruses from one location or host to another, including formulations for transport, carriers, or delivery systems used in virology and gene therapy. It also encompasses protocols for controlled spread in research or industrial settings.
[C12N 2710/10211] Aviadenovirus, e.g. fowl adenovirus A -- The CPC code C12N 2710/10211 identifies aviadenoviruses, a group of viruses that infect birds such as fowl adenovirus A. It is placed in the hierarchy chemistry - biochemistry - microorganisms or enzymes - viruses, indicating its position among biochemical classifications for viral agents.
[C12N 2710/10221] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- C12N 2710/10221 covers viruses themselves, including newly isolated virus strains, engineered mutants and the nucleotide sequences that define them. The classification is used for patent documents describing the discovery, modification or sequencing of whole viruses rather than specific applications or products derived from them. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy, sub-field of biochemistry, focusing on microorganisms or enzymes, specifically viruses.
[C12N 2710/10222] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- C12N 2710/10222 covers inventions that disclose previously unknown viral proteins or individual genes, as well as novel structural or functional features of already known viral proteins or genes. It includes the characterization, modification and engineering of such viral components for biotechnological or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2710/10223] Virus like particles [VLP] -- C12N 2710/10223 covers virus-like particles (VLPs), which are assembled structures that mimic the morphology of viruses but lack viral genetic material. They are used in vaccine development, diagnostics, and nanotechnology as safe platforms to display antigens or deliver molecules.
[C12N 2710/10231] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10231 covers applications of viruses that are not intended for treatment or vaccination, such as employing viral particles or components as disinfectants, biocides or for environmental decontamination. It includes methods where viruses are used for their inherent antimicrobial properties rather than for therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2710/10232] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- This subclass covers therapeutic uses of viruses that do not involve vaccination, focusing on their ability to lyse target cells such as cancer cells. It includes methods that employ naturally lytic or genetically engineered viruses as cytolytic or oncolytic agents to treat disease by direct cell killing or delivery of therapeutic payloads.
[C12N 2710/10233] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10233 covers applications of isolated viral proteins as therapeutic agents that are not vaccines, such as proteins engineered to trigger apoptosis in target cells or to modulate inflammation. It falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Viruses.
[C12N 2710/10234] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- This CPC subclass concerns the use of whole viruses, viral parts or virus-like particles as immunising agents. It includes live-attenuated, inactivated or recombinant viral vaccines and formulations based on viral proteins for prophylactic or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2710/10241] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- The CPC entry C12N 2710/10241 covers the use of whole viruses, viral particles or viral components as vectors for delivering genetic material, therapeutic agents or vaccines in biotechnological applications. It pertains to methods and compositions that exploit viral mechanisms for gene transfer, drug delivery or immunisation, and falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Viruses.
[C12N 2710/10242] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- C12N 2710/10242 covers the use of whole viruses or virus-derived particles as carriers that encapsulate small organic molecules for delivery. It includes methods where the viral capsid or particle acts as a transport vehicle for drugs, imaging agents, or other low-molecular-weight compounds, exploiting the natural ability of viruses to protect and convey cargo.
[C12N 2710/10243] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- A viral genome or its components used as a genetic vector refers to recombinant virus particles, virus-derived particles or nucleic-acid fragments that carry foreign DNA or RNA into target cells for gene transfer, vaccine development or therapeutic applications. This subclass groups methods, constructs and devices that exploit viral elements to deliver genetic material.
[C12N 2710/10244] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10244 covers chimeric viral vectors that incorporate heterologous viral components to produce a different viral vector. It pertains to biotechnological methods involving engineered viruses for gene-delivery or vaccine production.
[C12N 2710/10245] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- The C12N 2710/10245 class covers technologies that impart selective targeting to viral vectors, enabling them to deliver genetic material or therapeutic agents to particular cells, tissues, or receptors. It includes engineered viral capsids, surface ligands, antibodies, or other molecular modifications that direct vector binding and entry, improving specificity and safety in virotherapy, gene delivery and related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2710/10251] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- The CPC code C12N 2710/10251 covers methods for producing or purifying viral material, including techniques for culturing, harvesting and isolating viruses for research or pharmaceutical use. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy related to biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically viruses.
[C12N 2710/10252] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- It covers methods and biological materials that provide missing functions for viruses in host cells, using complementing cell lines and packaging constructs to enable production of recombinant viruses or virus-like particles, typically for gene-therapy, vaccine or research purposes.
[C12N 2710/10261] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- It covers techniques used to render viruses non-infectious or less virulent for research, vaccine production, or safe handling. Includes chemical, physical, genetic or biological approaches that inactivate or attenuate viral particles while preserving antigenic structures. It is applied in biotechnology and virology to develop attenuated vaccines and study viral functions.
[C12N 2710/10262] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2710/10262 pertains to viruses that are altered or created by genetic engineering. The subclass includes recombinant viruses engineered for research, vaccine development, gene therapy, or industrial uses where nucleic-acid sequences are modified to give the virus new functions or properties.
[C12N 2710/10263] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2710/10263 is a CPC subclass that deals with viruses that are subjected to chemical treatment. It includes methods for inactivating, modifying, or purifying viruses using chemical reagents, typically for research, diagnostic or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2710/10264] by serial passage -- C12N 2710/10264 covers methods of modifying viruses through serial passage, wherein the virus is repeatedly cultured in host cells or animals to induce genetic changes that alter its phenotype such as virulence, host range, or vaccine properties. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and viruses.
[C12N 2710/10271] Demonstrated -- The entry C12N 2710/10271 is a CPC subclass under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Viruses, covering subject matter concerning viruses that have been experimentally demonstrated, i.e., isolated, identified and shown to possess specific properties, typically used in diagnostics, therapeutics or research.
[C12N 2710/10288] for redistribution -- The CPC class C12N 2710/10288 pertains to methods, formulations or carriers that facilitate the redistribution of viruses, encompassing techniques for delivering viral particles to new hosts, tissues or environments and the associated compositions used in such distribution processes.
[C12N 2710/10311] Mastadenovirus, e.g. human or simian adenoviruses -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10311 is assigned to mastadenoviruses, the adenovirus genus that includes human and simian types. It covers the viruses themselves, their genomic material and biological properties, and the use of these viruses as vectors for gene delivery, vaccine development, or other biotechnological applications. This falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses.
[C12N 2710/10321] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10321 concerns inventions directed to viruses themselves, encompassing newly isolated strains, mutant variants and the genomic sequences that define them.
[C12N 2710/10322] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- C12N 2710/10322 covers inventions that disclose novel viral proteins or individual genes, or that reveal new structural or functional features of already known viral proteins or genes, including engineered variants and detailed characterizations. The classification is used for biotechnological applications such as vaccine development, gene therapy, diagnostics, and basic research on virus biology. It falls under the hierarchy Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Viruses.
[C12N 2710/10323] Virus like particles [VLP] -- C12N 2710/10323 covers virus-like particles (VLPs), which are self-assembled macromolecular structures that mimic the morphology of authentic viruses but lack viral genetic material, rendering them non-infectious. The classification is used for inventions related to the production, engineering, purification, and application of VLPs, particularly in vaccine development, diagnostics, and nanocarrier technologies.
[C12N 2710/10331] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- This subclass covers non-therapeutic and non-vaccine applications of viruses, such as employing viral particles or derivatives as disinfectants, biocontrol agents, or in other industrial processes. It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses.
[C12N 2710/10332] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- C12N 2710/10332 covers methods that employ viruses as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, such as oncolytic viruses that selectively kill target cells. It pertains to using viral particles or constructs to treat diseases by cytolysis or related therapeutic mechanisms.
[C12N 2710/10333] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- The C12N 2710/10333 subclass covers inventions that employ viral proteins for therapeutic purposes other than vaccination, such as agents that trigger programmed cell death, modulate inflammatory pathways, or provide other medical benefits by exploiting specific viral functions. It addresses the pharmaceutical use of isolated or engineered viral proteins in treating disease.
[C12N 2710/10334] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10334 relates to inventions that use whole viruses or viral components as vaccines, including live-attenuated or inactivated viruses, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins. It resides in the hierarchy Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Viruses and is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify patent documents concerning vaccine technologies.
[C12N 2710/10341] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- This CPC subclass covers the use of viruses, viral particles, or viral components as vectors for delivering genetic material or other payloads in biotechnological applications. It includes methods for constructing viral vectors, modifying viral genomes, and applying them in gene therapy, vaccine development, or functional genomics.
[C12N 2710/10342] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- C12N 2710/10342 pertains to the use of whole viruses or viral particles as delivery vehicles, for example encapsulating small organic compounds for therapeutic or diagnostic purposes. It covers methods of loading, stabilising and administering such virus-based carriers.
[C12N 2710/10343] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2710/10343 designates the use of whole viral genomes or genome fragments as genetic vectors, covering recombinant viral particles and engineered nucleic-acid constructs employed to deliver genetic material into host cells. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses, and pertains to biotechnological applications such as gene therapy, vaccine development and molecular cloning.
[C12N 2710/10344] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- A chimeric viral vector that incorporates genetic elements from a different virus and is employed as a platform to manufacture another viral vector, enabling the production of the desired vector while retaining functional components from the heterologous source.
[C12N 2710/10345] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- The C12N 2710/10345 subclass covers specially designed targeting systems that direct viral vectors to specific cells or tissues. It includes engineered ligands, receptor-binding modifications, or carrier molecules that enhance the precision and safety of viral delivery in biotechnological and therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2710/10351] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- This subclass covers techniques for culturing, harvesting, and isolating viruses, including steps to concentrate, purify, and prepare viral particles for research, diagnostics, or vaccine production. It includes downstream processing methods such as centrifugation, chromatography, filtration, and precipitation applied to viral stocks.
[C12N 2710/10352] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- C12N 2710/10352 covers biotechnological methods that use complementing cell lines together with packaging systems to produce recombinant viruses or virus-like particles, enabling the generation of viral vectors or vaccine particles for research, therapeutic or diagnostic applications.
[C12N 2710/10361] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- A CPC entry for methods that render viruses non-infectious or less virulent, covering chemical, physical or genetic techniques used to inactivate or attenuate viral particles for vaccine production, diagnostics or research. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses.
[C12N 2710/10362] by genetic engineering -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/10362 covers viruses that are produced or altered through genetic engineering techniques. It includes recombinant viral particles, engineered viral vectors and methods for constructing, modifying or using viruses by inserting, deleting or substituting nucleic-acid sequences.
[C12N 2710/10363] by chemical treatment -- The code C12N 2710/10363 denotes methods that modify or inactivate viruses through the application of chemical agents. It includes the use of antiviral compounds, disinfectants, or other reagents to treat viral particles or infected cells. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and viruses.
[C12N 2710/10364] by serial passage -- The CPC entry C12N 2710/10364 covers methods in which viruses are propagated by serial passage, meaning the virus is repeatedly transferred to fresh host cells or organisms to select for desired traits such as increased virulence, attenuation, or host-range adaptation. This technique is used in virology research, vaccine development, and strain improvement.
[C12N 2710/10371] Demonstrated -- C12N 2710/10371 covers inventions in which a virus has been experimentally demonstrated, including methods that prove the virus's presence, replication, infectivity or biological effect. It applies to patent claims that provide concrete evidence of the virus's activity rather than mere theoretical description.
[C12N 2710/10388] for redistribution -- C12N 2710/10388 is a Cooperative Patent Classification entry under viruses that concerns inventions dealing with the redistribution of viral particles, including methods or compositions for spreading, delivering or circulating viruses in a controlled way, often for uses such as vaccines or gene-therapy vectors.
[C12N 2710/12011] Asfarviridae -- Asfarviridae is a family of large double-stranded DNA viruses that primarily infect swine and other mammals, with African swine fever virus as its most notable member. Members replicate in the cytoplasm and cause severe hemorrhagic disease in domestic pigs, leading to major economic losses in agriculture.
[C12N 2710/12021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- C12N 2710/12021 classifies inventions concerning viruses themselves, such as newly isolated strains, mutants, or their genomic sequences. It is applied when the subject matter relates to the discovery, isolation, characterization or modification of viruses independent of a particular downstream use.
[C12N 2710/12022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- This subclass concerns inventions that disclose new viral proteins or individual genes, or reveal new structural or functional aspects of already known viral proteins or genes, covering biotechnological uses such as diagnostics, therapeutics and research tools.
[C12N 2710/12023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- This CPC subclass covers the design, production and use of virus-like particles, which mimic the structure of viruses but contain no infectious genetic material. It includes methods for assembling VLPs, expression systems and their application in vaccines, diagnostics or gene-delivery technologies.
[C12N 2710/12031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2710/12031 covers uses of viruses that are not related to therapy or vaccination, such as employing viral particles as disinfectants or for other non-medical purposes. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, viruses.
[C12N 2710/12032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The code refers to inventions that employ viruses to treat disease by means other than vaccination, such as using them as cytolytic or oncolytic agents that directly destroy target cells. It also covers methods where viruses are engineered or selected to deliver therapeutic payloads or induce cell-killing effects for medical applications.
[C12N 2710/12033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- C12N 2710/12033 covers the use of isolated viral proteins for therapeutic purposes that are not vaccine related, such as agents that trigger cell death or reduce inflammation. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and specifically viruses.
[C12N 2710/12034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- The subclass C12N 2710/12034 covers inventions that employ a virus or viral component to induce immunity, including the use of live-attenuated or inactivated viruses, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins as vaccines.
[C12N 2710/12041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- The classification covers methods and compositions where viruses or virus-derived particles are employed to deliver genetic material or other payloads into target cells, serving as vectors for gene therapy, vaccine development, or molecular-biology applications. It includes the use of whole viral particles, engineered viruses, virus-like particles, or isolated viral components for transduction or expression of heterologous genes.
[C12N 2710/12042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/12042 covers methods and compositions that employ whole viruses or viral particles as carriers for small organic molecules, such as drugs or imaging agents. It includes the design, preparation, and use of viral vectors that encapsulate these molecules to achieve targeted delivery, protection, or controlled release in biomedical applications.
[C12N 2710/12043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2710/12043 classifies the use of entire viral genomes or selected viral genetic elements as vectors for transporting nucleic acids into host cells, covering engineered or replication-defective viruses employed in gene-therapy, vaccine design and molecular-biology applications. It includes recombinant viral vectors and viral components that function as delivery vehicles for DNA or RNA.
[C12N 2710/12044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- A chimeric viral vector is engineered by combining genetic elements from different viruses, creating a hybrid particle that can be used as a manufacturing platform to produce a separate, defined viral vector. This approach enables the production of gene-therapy or vaccine vectors with improved yield, safety or specificity.
[C12N 2710/12045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- C12N 2710/12045 relates to engineered viral vectors that incorporate specialised targeting mechanisms to direct gene-delivery or therapeutic payloads to particular cell types or tissues. It covers designs such as surface-displayed ligands, receptor-binding peptides or antibody fragments that confer selective entry, as well as the associated methods for producing and using such targeted viral carriers.
[C12N 2710/12051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- C12N 2710/12051 covers methods for producing or purifying viral material, including whole virions, subviral particles, viral nucleic acids and proteins, for use in research, vaccine development, diagnostics or therapy. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and specifically viruses, and is used in EPO and USPTO patent searches.
[C12N 2710/12052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- C12N 2710/12052 covers methods and materials that use complementing cell lines together with packaging systems to generate viruses or viral particles, often for research or vaccine production. The cell lines supply missing viral functions, allowing safe production of replication-defective particles.
[C12N 2710/12061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- This CPC subclass covers techniques for rendering viruses non-infectious or less virulent, including chemical, physical, genetic, or biological procedures used to inactivate or attenuate viral particles for vaccine production or research.
[C12N 2710/12062] by genetic engineering -- The C12N 2710/12062 entry covers viruses that are created, modified, or utilized through genetic engineering techniques, encompassing recombinant virus production, engineered viral vectors, and related biotechnological processes for research, therapy, or industrial applications.
[C12N 2710/12063] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2710/12063 covers chemical treatment methods applied to viruses, including disinfection, inactivation, or modification using chemical agents. It pertains to biotechnological processes that alter viral particles for research, vaccine production, or safety. The code falls under the CPC section for chemical and biochemical methods involving microorganisms or enzymes, specifically viruses.
[C12N 2710/12064] by serial passage -- The code C12N 2710/12064 covers methods for obtaining virus variants by serial passage, meaning the virus is repeatedly cultured in a host or cell line to induce mutations and adapt it to new conditions. It is classified under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, viruses in the CPC.
[C12N 2710/12071] Demonstrated -- The subclass C12N 2710/12071 covers viruses that have been experimentally demonstrated to possess a defined biological activity, such as infectivity, replication ability or expression of a specific gene, and includes methods that provide proof of that functional characteristic.
[C12N 2710/12088] for redistribution -- C12N 2710/12088 covers technologies for the redistribution of viruses, encompassing methods and devices that transfer viral particles from one host or environment to another for purposes such as research, therapeutic applications, or vaccine production. It includes processes that facilitate the controlled dissemination of viral material while maintaining its viability and functionality.
[C12N 2710/14011] Baculoviridae -- C12N 2710/14011 refers to the family Baculoviridae, a group of large double-stranded DNA viruses that primarily infect insects, especially Lepidoptera. These viruses are notable for their use as biological control agents against pest species and as vectors for recombinant protein production in biotechnology.
[C12N 2710/14021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- This classification covers inventions that claim viruses themselves, such as newly isolated strains, genetically altered variants, or the nucleic-acid sequences that define them. It is used for patent documents describing the virus as a composition, not just a method of use.
[C12N 2710/14022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/14022 covers inventions relating to newly identified viral proteins or individual viral genes, as well as newly discovered structural or functional characteristics of already known viral proteins or genes. It is used for patent documents describing novel viral molecular components.
[C12N 2710/14023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- Virus-like particles (VLPs) are self-assembled, non-infectious structures that replicate the external morphology of viruses but lack viral genetic material, making them safe carriers for antigen presentation, vaccine development, and molecular-biology research. This subclass groups methods and compositions that produce, modify, or employ VLPs derived from diverse viral families.
[C12N 2710/14031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2710/14031 covers non-therapeutic uses of viruses, such as employing viral particles as disinfectants, biopesticides or in industrial processes. The code applies to methods where viruses act as agents for decontamination or control, rather than as medicines or vaccines.
[C12N 2710/14032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/14032 covers inventions that employ viruses as therapeutic agents beyond vaccination, including oncolytic or cytolytic viruses that selectively kill diseased cells, viral vectors for delivering therapeutic genes, and other non-vaccine uses of viruses to treat or manage disease. This classification falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses.
[C12N 2710/14033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- This CPC subclass deals with the therapeutic use of viral proteins that are not employed as vaccines, covering applications such as inducing apoptosis in target cells or providing anti-inflammatory effects. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses.
[C12N 2710/14034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- This classification covers methods that use whole viruses, either live-attenuated or inactivated, virus-like particles, or isolated viral proteins as immunogenic agents for vaccination, including their formulation and delivery. It addresses the preparation and application of these viral components to elicit protective immune responses against disease.

The category sits within the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy under microorganisms or enzymes, specifically viruses, and is used in patent documents describing gene-therapy, vaccine, or molecular-biology applications.

It focuses on methods that obtain viral material in a form suitable for further study or application.

This includes any engineered or naturally occurring variants that provide distinct properties or functions compared to previously described viral components.
