[C12N 2710/24122] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- This CPC subclass covers inventions concerning newly discovered viral proteins or individual genes and newly identified structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes, including methods for their isolation, characterization, and use in diagnostics, therapeutics, vaccines, or other biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2710/24123] Virus like particles [VLP] -- Virus-like particles are self-assembled protein structures that mimic the morphology of viruses but lack viral genetic material, and they are employed in vaccine development, diagnostics and nanocarrier applications. They fall under CPC C12N 2710/24123 covering VLPs.
[C12N 2710/24131] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/24131 covers inventions that employ viruses for purposes other than therapy or vaccination, such as using viral particles, fragments or derivatives as disinfectants, biocidal agents or in other non-medical applications. It lies within the chemistry hierarchy under biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and specifically the viruses category.
[C12N 2710/24132] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/24132 covers the therapeutic use of viruses for purposes other than vaccination, notably employing viral agents that selectively destroy target cells such as cancer cells. It includes methods that use native or engineered viruses as cytolytic agents, viral vectors for gene delivery, or other mechanisms that exploit viral replication or lysis to treat disease.
[C12N 2710/24133] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- This CPC subclass covers methods that use a protein derived from a virus as a therapeutic agent for purposes other than vaccination, such as inducing apoptosis or providing anti-inflammatory effects. It focuses on pharmaceutical applications of viral proteins in treatment rather than prophylaxis.
[C12N 2710/24134] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- This subclass pertains to methods that use whole viruses or viral components to confer immunity, including live-attenuated or inactivated viruses, virus-like particles, and isolated viral proteins employed as vaccines.
[C12N 2710/24141] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- This CPC subclass covers methods that employ viruses, viral particles, or components derived from viruses as carriers for delivering genetic material, therapeutic agents, or other payloads into target cells. It includes the design, preparation and application of viral vectors for gene therapy, vaccination and biotechnology.
[C12N 2710/24142] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/24142 relates to the use of whole viruses or viral particles as carriers for small organic molecules such as drugs or diagnostic agents. It includes methods for encapsulating, stabilising and delivering these molecules with viral vectors, covering preparation and functional modification of the particles.
[C12N 2710/24143] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2710/24143 covers the use of whole viral genomes or portions of them as genetic vectors for delivering nucleic acids into host cells. It includes engineered viruses employed in gene therapy, vaccine development, and recombinant protein production, where the viral material serves to transport genetic information without necessarily retaining pathogenic functions.
[C12N 2710/24144] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/24144 covers chimeric viral vectors that incorporate genetic elements from different viruses in order to generate a separate viral vector, typically for gene-therapy or vaccine production. It includes designs where heterologous viral components are combined to improve vector yield, stability or targeting, and is situated under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses.
[C12N 2710/24145] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- The C12N 2710/24145 code covers engineered methods that give viral vectors the ability to home to particular cells, tissues or receptors, such as modified capsid proteins, attached ligands or engineered tropism determinants. It includes designs for retargeting, detargeting or enhancing specificity of viruses used as gene-delivery or therapeutic agents. This classification falls under chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes-viruses.
[C12N 2710/24151] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- Methods for producing or purifying viral material include procedures for culturing host cells, harvesting viruses, and applying purification steps such as centrifugation, filtration, precipitation or chromatography to obtain virus particles or components for research, diagnostics or vaccine production.
[C12N 2710/24152] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- C12N 2710/24152 covers technologies that employ complementing cell lines together with specialized packaging systems to generate full virus particles or viral vectors, enabling efficient production of infectious virions for vaccine, gene-therapy or research purposes. The classification pertains to biochemistry of viruses and the methods for assembling them in vitro.
[C12N 2710/24161] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- C12N 2710/24161 covers techniques for rendering viruses non-infectious or reducing their virulence, including chemical, physical, and genetic approaches such as heat treatment, radiation, solvent exposure, or targeted mutations. These methods are employed in vaccine development, viral-vector safety, and research on virus deactivation.
[C12N 2710/24162] by genetic engineering -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/24162 covers inventions that involve viruses that have been created, modified, or utilized through genetic-engineering techniques, including recombinant viruses, viral vectors and virus-based products obtained by manipulating nucleic acid sequences. It pertains to biotechnological methods applied to viral organisms.
[C12N 2710/24163] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2710/24163 covers methods and compositions for chemically treating viruses, including the use of antiviral agents, disinfectants or other chemical means to inactivate or destroy viral particles. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Viruses and is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify inventions that address virus control through chemical intervention.
[C12N 2710/24164] by serial passage -- C12N 2710/24164 covers methods that alter viruses by repeatedly culturing them in a host or cell line-serial passage-to induce genetic changes, such as attenuation or adaptation, often used in vaccine development or strain selection. It includes procedures for selecting variants with desired properties through successive replication cycles.
[C12N 2710/24171] Demonstrated -- C12N 2710/24171 covers methods and materials that demonstrate the presence, identity or functional activity of viruses, such as assays, bioassays or experimental setups used to confirm viral infection or replication.
[C12N 2710/24188] for redistribution -- C12N 2710/24188 relates to biotechnological methods involving viruses that are intended for redistribution, such as delivering viral vectors to target cells or spreading viral particles for therapeutic or research purposes. It falls under the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes-viruses hierarchy.
[C12N 2710/24211] Parapoxvirus, e.g. Orf virus -- C12N 2710/24211 designates viruses of the genus Parapoxvirus, exemplified by the Orf virus that infects mammals and is studied for veterinary vaccines and biomedical applications. This subclass sits within the CPC hierarchy under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Viruses, grouping genetic, structural and therapeutic aspects of parapoxviral inventions.
[C12N 2710/24221] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- C12N 2710/24221 covers viruses in their entirety, including newly isolated strains, genetically modified mutants and the nucleotide sequences that define them, as distinct from specific applications or compositions containing the viruses. It applies to inventions that identify, characterize or claim the virus itself or its genomic information.
[C12N 2710/24222] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- This subclass covers inventions that disclose new viral proteins or individual viral genes, as well as newly identified structural or functional aspects of already known viral proteins or genes. It includes methods for recombinant expression, analysis and characterisation of these viral components.
[C12N 2710/24223] Virus like particles [VLP] -- This classification covers virus-like particles, which are non-infectious structures that mimic the morphology of viruses but lack viral genetic material and are used in vaccines and nanotechnology. They are produced by expression of viral capsid proteins in microorganisms or cell cultures.
[C12N 2710/24231] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2710/24231 classifies non-therapeutic applications of viruses, including their use as disinfectants, biocontrol agents, or in other industrial processes. It falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Viruses and covers methods that exploit viral properties without aiming at treatment or vaccination.
[C12N 2710/24232] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The subclass C12N 2710/24232 covers the use of viruses as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, such as oncolytic or cytolytic viruses employed to treat diseases. It includes methods for selecting, engineering, formulating, and administering these viruses to achieve a therapeutic effect.
[C12N 2710/24233] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- This CPC subclass covers inventions that use viral proteins as therapeutic agents that are not vaccines, such as proteins that induce apoptosis or have anti-inflammatory effects. It is situated within chemical biotechnology for viruses and defines non-vaccinal medical applications of viral-derived proteins.
[C12N 2710/24234] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- C12N 2710/24234 pertains to the use of whole viruses, viral components or virus-like particles as vaccines, encompassing approaches such as live-attenuated or inactivated viruses, viral protein preparations and recombinant viral elements.
[C12N 2710/24241] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- This subclass covers the use of whole viruses, viral particles or isolated viral components as delivery vectors for genetic material, proteins or other agents in biotechnological and medical applications such as gene therapy, vaccine production and functional studies. It includes recombinant viral vectors, virus-like particles and purified viral elements employed to introduce substances into target cells.
[C12N 2710/24242] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- C12N 2710/24242 covers the use of whole viruses or virus-derived particles as carriers for small organic molecules, such as drugs, imaging agents or other bioactive compounds. The entry addresses methods of encapsulating these molecules within viral capsids or engineered viral vectors to exploit the natural delivery capabilities of viruses for therapeutic or diagnostic purposes.
[C12N 2710/24243] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2710/24243 refers to the use of whole viral genomes or selected viral components as genetic vectors for transferring nucleic acids into host cells. It includes engineered viruses employed in gene-therapy, vaccine development and recombinant DNA techniques to deliver therapeutic or experimental genes, covering a range of viral families and vector designs.
[C12N 2710/24244] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- This class covers viral vectors engineered by fusing genetic components from different viruses to serve as a production platform for a second, distinct viral vector, often used in gene-therapy, vaccine or research applications. It addresses the design, construction and use of such chimeric vectors to package or deliver the genetic material of the target vector.
[C12N 2710/24245] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- This CPC subclass covers engineered targeting systems that guide viral vectors to particular cells or tissues, improving delivery precision for gene therapy, vaccine or other biomedical applications. It includes modifications such as capsid engineering, ligand attachment or molecular tags that confer selective binding and entry into the desired host.
[C12N 2710/24251] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- The CPC subclass C12N 2710/24251 covers methods for producing or purifying viral material, including techniques for culturing viruses, harvesting, and downstream processing to obtain virus particles or nucleic acids for research, vaccine or therapeutic use.
[C12N 2710/24252] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- C12N 2710/24252 covers technologies that provide complementing cell lines or packaging systems supplying missing viral functions to enable the production of viruses or viral particles, often used in vaccine or gene-therapy manufacturing.
[C12N 2710/24261] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The C12N 2710/24261 entry covers methods for inactivating or attenuating viruses, including chemical, physical, enzymatic or genetic approaches that reduce viral infectivity while preserving immunogenic properties for use in vaccines, diagnostics or research. It falls under chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms-viruses in the CPC system.
[C12N 2710/24262] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2710/24262 covers viruses that are obtained, altered, or produced through genetic-engineering methods, including recombinant DNA techniques used to modify viral genomes for applications such as vaccines, gene therapy, or research. This subclass falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses and defines the scope of claims relating to genetically engineered viral particles.
[C12N 2710/24263] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2710/24263 covers methods that treat viruses with chemicals, including inactivation, neutralisation or modification of viral particles for purposes such as vaccine production, disinfection, or analytical preparation. The classification is used for biotechnological processes where chemical agents are applied to manipulate or destroy viruses.
[C12N 2710/24264] by serial passage -- The CPC entry C12N 2710/24264 concerns methods of obtaining or modifying viruses through serial passage, i.e., repeatedly culturing the virus in a host or cell line to select for altered characteristics such as increased virulence, host range, or resistance. It covers the procedural steps, conditions, and biological materials used to achieve these serially-passaged viral strains.
[C12N 2710/24271] Demonstrated -- C12N 2710/24271 is a CPC entry under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Viruses that classifies patents describing methods or compositions in which a virus has been experimentally demonstrated to possess a specific biological activity, such as infectivity, replication, or a therapeutic effect. It is used for disclosures that prove the virus's functional properties.
[C12N 2710/24288] for redistribution -- The CPC entry C12N 2710/24288 concerns viruses that are employed for redistribution purposes, such as the use of viral particles to transfer genetic material, therapeutic agents, or other payloads from one host to another. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → viruses, and covers methods, compositions and applications that facilitate the delivery and spread of such payloads via viral vectors.
[C12N 2720/00] dsRNA viruses -- C12N 2720/00 refers to double-stranded RNA viruses, encompassing any virus whose genome consists of two complementary RNA strands. The subclass includes the characterization, manipulation and production of such viruses for biotechnological or pharmaceutical purposes, and falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes.
[C12N 2720/00011] Details
[C12N 2720/00021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- This CPC subclass C12N 2720/00021 addresses dsRNA viruses themselves, including newly isolated strains, mutant forms and their genomic sequences, without reference to specific applications. It falls under the hierarchy CHEMISTRY → BIOCHEMISTRY → MICROORGANISMS OR ENZYMES → dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/00022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- C12N 2720/00022 covers inventions related to new viral proteins or individual genes and to new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes, specifically for double-stranded RNA viruses. It resides in the Cooperative Patent Classification under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/00023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- Virus-like particles (VLPs) are self-assembling protein complexes that mimic the exterior of dsRNA viruses but lack infectious nucleic acid, making them safe carriers for vaccines, diagnostics and nanotechnologies. They are produced by expressing viral capsid proteins in host cells and retain the antigenic properties of the parent virus while presenting no replication capability.
[C12N 2720/00031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2720/00031 covers non-therapeutic and non-vaccine uses of double-stranded RNA viruses, including applications where the virus functions as a biocidal or disinfectant agent. The entry addresses methods that exploit viral particles or components to inactivate or control unwanted microorganisms rather than for medical treatment or immunisation.
[C12N 2720/00032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- This CPC subclass C12N 2720/00032 falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, dsRNA viruses. It covers the use of dsRNA viruses as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, such as oncolytic or cytolytic applications.
[C12N 2720/00033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- This subgroup covers inventions that use proteins derived from double-stranded RNA viruses as therapeutic agents rather than vaccines, for example molecules that induce apoptosis or have anti-inflammatory activity. It falls under chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes-dsRNA viruses and relates to biomedical applications of viral proteins.
[C12N 2720/00034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- C12N 2720/00034 covers the use of whole viruses or viral parts as vaccines, including live-attenuated, inactivated, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy through biochemistry and microorganisms or enzymes, specifically targeting dsRNA viruses, and defines methods for immunisation that exploit viral structures or genetic material.
[C12N 2720/00041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- C12N 2720/00041 covers the use of dsRNA viruses, viral particles or viral components as vectors for delivering nucleic acids or functional payloads in biotechnology, medicine or research. It includes methods that exploit the virus's natural infection mechanisms to introduce genes, therapeutic agents or markers into target cells.
[C12N 2720/00042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- The CPC entry C12N 2720/00042 pertains to dsRNA viruses used as carriers, specifically the exploitation of whole viruses or viral particles to encapsulate and deliver small organic molecules. It includes methods and compositions where the virus functions as a vehicle for transporting the encapsulated compound to a target site. This subclass falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/00043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- This subclass covers the use of whole viral genomes or viral components as vectors for delivering genetic material into host cells, including engineered or recombinant viruses, virus-derived particles and disassembled genome fragments employed in gene-therapy, vaccine design, and molecular-biology applications to introduce, modify or express target genes.
[C12N 2720/00044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector
[C12N 2720/00045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- The CPC entry C12N 2720/00045 covers engineered targeting systems that direct viral vectors-specifically dsRNA virus-based vectors-to particular cells or tissues. It includes designs such as modified capsid proteins, ligand-conjugated particles or synthetic adapters that improve specificity for gene-delivery or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2720/00051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/00051 covers procedures for producing or purifying viral material, especially double-stranded RNA viruses. It includes methods such as cell-culture infection, fermentation, virus harvesting, clarification, precipitation, chromatography and other techniques used to obtain virus particles or viral nucleic acids for research, diagnostics or vaccine production.
[C12N 2720/00052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The CPC class C12N 2720/00052 covers technologies for complementing cell lines and packaging systems used to produce viruses or viral particles, particularly dsRNA viruses, facilitating efficient assembly and replication of viral genomes in vitro. It relates to biotechnological methods for generating recombinant viral vectors.
[C12N 2720/00061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The CPC entry C12N 2720/00061 covers methods for inactivating or attenuating double-stranded RNA viruses, including chemical, physical, and genetic approaches that reduce or eliminate viral infectivity for use in vaccines, diagnostics, or research. It details procedures such as heat treatment, solvent exposure, UV irradiation, and targeted mutations that impair replication while preserving structural antigens. These techniques are applied to control pathogenicity of dsRNA viruses in biomedical and industrial contexts.
[C12N 2720/00062] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2720/00062 covers the creation or modification of double-stranded RNA viruses through genetic engineering techniques, such as recombinant DNA methods for inserting, deleting or altering viral genes. It is used for producing recombinant viral particles for research, vaccine development or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2720/00063] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2720/00063 covers chemical treatment of double-stranded RNA viruses, describing processes that use chemical agents to inactivate, modify or isolate these viruses. The classification falls under chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes-dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/00064] by serial passage -- The C12N 2720/00064 subclass pertains to biotechnological methods that generate or modify double-stranded RNA viruses by serial passage, i.e., repeated replication cycles in a host or cell culture to select for desired properties. It covers the use of this evolutionary approach for virus strain improvement, vaccine development, or vector engineering within the realm of biochemical processes involving microorganisms or enzymes.

[C12N 2720/10011] Birnaviridae -- C12N2720/10011 designates the family Birnaviridae, a group of double-stranded RNA viruses that infect a range of hosts such as fish, birds and insects. The classification is used for patent documents dealing with the composition, isolation or genetic manipulation of these viruses and their associated proteins.
[C12N 2720/10021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences
[C12N 2720/10022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/10022 covers inventions concerning new viral proteins or individual genes of double-stranded RNA viruses, as well as previously known viral proteins or genes that exhibit newly discovered structural or functional aspects. It is applied to classify biotechnological claims related to these novel viral elements.
[C12N 2720/10023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- Virus-like particles (VLPs) are self-assembled structures composed of viral capsid proteins that mimic the morphology of authentic viruses but lack the viral genome, rendering them non-infectious. They are used in vaccine development, gene delivery and as tools for studying virus assembly, particularly for dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/10031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2720/10031 pertains to the use of double-stranded RNA viruses in ways that are not therapeutic or vaccine-related, such as employing the virus as a disinfectant or for other non-medical purposes. It is nested under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/10032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- This CPC subclass covers the therapeutic use of double-stranded RNA viruses as agents other than vaccines, such as cytolytic or oncolytic viruses that kill target cells.
[C12N 2720/10033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/10033 covers inventions that use proteins derived from double-stranded RNA viruses as therapeutic agents, excluding vaccine applications. It includes uses where the viral protein induces apoptosis, modulates inflammation or provides other direct medical effects, and is classified under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/10034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/10034 covers the use of viruses or viral components as vaccines, including live-attenuated or inactivated viruses, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins, specifically for dsRNA viruses. It falls under the hierarchy Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → dsRNA viruses. This entry defines patentable subject matter related to vaccine approaches that exploit whole viruses or parts thereof.
[C12N 2720/10041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- This subclass covers the use of viruses, viral particles, or components derived from them as vectors for delivering genetic material or other payloads into host cells. It includes applications such as gene therapy, vaccine development, and molecular cloning that exploit dsRNA viruses as delivery vehicles.
[C12N 2720/10042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/10042 covers the use of whole viruses or virus-like particles as carriers for delivering small organic molecules such as drugs, vaccines or diagnostic agents. It includes methods that encapsulate the molecules within the viral capsid or employ the virus as a vector for targeted delivery.
[C12N 2720/10043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N2720/10043 covers the use of the whole genome or fragments of double-stranded RNA viruses as vectors for delivering genetic material into host cells. It includes methods and compositions where viral RNA, capsids or engineered viral particles serve as carriers for genes, regulatory sequences or other nucleic-acid constructs in research, therapy or biotechnology.
[C12N 2720/10044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- A chimeric viral vector that incorporates genetic elements from one or more heterologous-viral sources and is employed as a production system for a second, distinct viral vector, typically to improve yield, safety, or targeting in biotechnology applications such as gene therapy or vaccine manufacturing.
[C12N 2720/10045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- C12N 2720/10045 classifies inventions that provide a special targeting system for viral vectors, especially those based on dsRNA viruses, enabling the vectors to recognize and enter specific cell types or tissues. The entry covers engineered surface proteins, peptides, antibodies or other ligands incorporated into the viral capsid or envelope to improve delivery precision in gene therapy, vaccine or other biotechnology applications.
[C12N 2720/10051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- C12N2720/10051 covers techniques for producing or isolating viral particles, nucleic acids, or other viral components, especially those derived from double-stranded RNA viruses. The classification includes cultivation, replication, extraction and purification processes used in research, vaccine development and biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2720/10052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The C12N 2720/10052 subclass covers technologies that use complementing host cells together with specialized packaging systems to generate recombinant dsRNA viruses or virus-like particles, enabling the production of viral vectors or vaccine candidates. It includes methods for supplying missing viral functions in trans and for assembling complete viral particles in engineered cell lines.
[C12N 2720/10061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- C12N 2720/10061 covers techniques for rendering double-stranded RNA viruses non-infectious or less virulent, such as chemical, physical, or genetic methods that inactivate or attenuate the virus. These methods are used in vaccine production, diagnostic tools, and research on virus biology.
[C12N 2720/10062] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2720/10062 classifies inventions that involve double-stranded RNA viruses that are created, altered or utilized through genetic-engineering techniques, such as recombinant DNA methods, gene synthesis or genome editing, to obtain new viral strains, vectors or virus-based biotechnological tools.
[C12N 2720/10063] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2720/10063 designates chemical treatment methods for dsRNA viruses, covering the use of chemical agents to inactivate, neutralize or otherwise control these viruses in microbial or enzymatic contexts. It falls under the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy for microorganisms or enzymes, specifically targeting dsRNA viral agents.
[C12N 2720/10064] by serial passage -- C12N 2720/10064 covers biotechnological methods that modify double-stranded RNA viruses through serial passage, i.e., repeated cycles of replication in host cells or culture to select for desired traits such as increased yield, altered host range, or attenuation.
[C12N 2720/10071] Demonstrated -- C12N2720/10071 concerns inventions that provide a demonstration of double-stranded RNA viruses, including methods that confirm their presence, activity, or properties. It covers experimental evidence, assays, or diagnostic techniques that establish that a dsRNA virus is present or functional. The subclass is used for biochemistry and microbiology applications related to virus demonstration.
[C12N 2720/10088] for redistribution -- C12N 2720/10088 covers processes and compositions that facilitate the redistribution of double-stranded RNA viruses, including methods for delivering these viruses to new host cells or environments, often for research, vaccine or gene-therapy purposes.
[C12N 2720/12011] Reoviridae -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/12011 covers members of the Reoviridae family, double-stranded RNA viruses that infect a range of hosts and are studied for their replication mechanisms and pathogenic properties. It falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/12021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- C12N 2720/12021 is a Cooperative Patent Classification for inventions relating to viruses themselves, such as newly isolated strains, genetically altered mutants, or their nucleic-acid sequences. It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → dsRNA viruses, and is used for patent documents describing viral material rather than specific applications.
[C12N 2720/12022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- C12N 2720/12022 covers inventions relating to novel viral proteins or individual genes, as well as newly discovered structural or functional features of known viral proteins or genes. It falls under the chemistry branch, specifically biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on dsRNA viruses.

[C12N 2720/12044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- The classification C12N 2720/12044 covers chimeric viral vectors that incorporate genetic elements from different viruses to serve as platforms for producing a second, distinct viral vector. These engineered constructs are used in biotechnology applications such as vaccine development, gene-therapy vector manufacturing, and research tools where the heterologous components enhance packaging, host range or expression of the target vector.
[C12N 2720/12045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/12045 covers inventions that provide specialised targeting mechanisms for viral vectors, enabling them to deliver genetic material to selected cells or tissues. It includes modifications to viral envelope proteins, attachment ligands, antibodies or other molecular cues that direct the vectors toward specific receptors, improving delivery precision and safety, particularly for dsRNA-virus-based vectors.

[C12N 2720/12052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- This CPC subclass (C12N 2720/12052) is nested under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → dsRNA viruses and concerns technologies that employ complementing cell lines together with packaging systems to generate whole viruses or viral particles, often for vaccine production, gene-therapy vectors, or virology research.
[C12N 2720/12061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- C12N 2720/12061 covers techniques for rendering double-stranded RNA viruses inactive or less virulent, including chemical, physical, or genetic approaches that disrupt viral replication or pathogenicity. These methods are used to produce safer viral preparations, such as attenuated vaccines or inactivated viral vectors.
[C12N 2720/12062] by genetic engineering -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/12062 covers the use of genetic-engineering techniques to modify or construct double-stranded RNA viruses. [C12N 2720/12063] by chemical treatment -- This sub-group addresses processes that act on double-stranded RNA viruses using chemical agents, such as disinfectants, antiviral compounds, or reagents that modify viral particles or genomes. It includes methods for inactivating, altering, or preparing dsRNA viruses for further use or analysis through chemical treatment.
[C12N 2720/12064] by serial passage -- C12N 2720/12064 covers techniques for modifying or adapting double-stranded RNA viruses through serial passage, where the virus is repeatedly cultured under defined conditions to select for desired properties such as increased virulence, host range, or resistance. The subclass falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically dsRNA viruses.

[C12N 2720/12132] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The C12N 2720/12132 subclass covers inventions that employ double-stranded-RNA viruses as therapeutic agents for purposes other than vaccination, such as oncolytic virotherapy, targeted cell lysis, or delivery of therapeutic molecules. The focus is on vaccine formulations that exploit the antigenic properties of the virus to induce protective immunity.

It includes methods of encapsulating, attaching or loading the molecules onto the viral capsid or nucleic-acid core.

[C12N 2720/12151] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- This CPC subclass covers techniques for producing or isolating viral material from double-stranded RNA viruses, including methods for culturing host cells, harvesting virus particles, and purifying them by steps such as centrifugation, filtration, chromatography or precipitation. [C12N 2720/12263] by chemical treatment -- The C12N 2720/12263 subclass concerns chemical treatment of double-stranded RNA viruses, including methods that inactivate, disinfect or otherwise modify viral particles using chemical agents. It falls within the biochemistry domain for microorganisms or enzymes, specifically targeting dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/12264] by serial passage -- The code C12N 2720/12264 refers to biotechnological methods that alter double-stranded RNA viruses by serial passage, i.e., repeatedly culturing the virus in host cells or organisms to select for specific traits such as reduced virulence, improved stability or higher productivity. This classification is used for inventions that obtain modified viral strains for applications like vaccine development, gene delivery vectors or production of viral proteins.
[C12N 2720/12271] Demonstrated -- C12N 2720/12271 designates dsRNA viruses for which their existence, identity or biological activity has been experimentally demonstrated, encompassing detection, isolation or functional assays of such viruses. It is used in patent literature to claim inventions involving proven dsRNA viral agents, their vectors or related diagnostic methods.
[C12N 2720/12288] for redistribution -- The subclass C12N 2720/12288 pertains to double-stranded RNA viruses that are employed for redistribution, encompassing the preparation, formulation and use of these viruses as vectors to deliver genetic material, enzymes or other payloads in biotechnological and research applications.
[C12N 2720/12311] Rotavirus, e.g. rotavirus A -- This CPC subclass covers rotavirus, particularly rotavirus A, a double-stranded RNA virus of the Reoviridae family. It includes patent subject matter such as isolated viruses, viral proteins, nucleic acids, detection methods, vaccines, and therapeutic applications. It falls under chemistry - biochemistry - microorganisms or enzymes - dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/12321] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- C12N 2720/12321 covers viruses themselves, including newly isolated strains, engineered mutants and the genomic sequences that define them, and is used for patent applications relating to viral material, its modification or use in biotechnological processes.
[C12N 2720/12322] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- This CPC subclass (C12N 2720/12322) concerns inventions that disclose new proteins or individual genes from double-stranded RNA viruses, as well as novel structural or functional characteristics of previously known viral proteins or genes. It lies within the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/12323] Virus like particles [VLP] -- This subclass covers the design, production and use of virus-like particles (VLPs), non-infectious structures that mimic natural viruses for purposes such as vaccines, drug delivery or diagnostics. It includes methods for expressing VLPs in various host systems, as well as modifications to improve their stability, assembly or immunogenic properties.
[C12N 2720/12331] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2720/12331 concerns non-therapeutic applications of double-stranded RNA viruses, such as using the virus as a disinfectant or biocontrol agent. The entry falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → dsRNA viruses, and it covers methods that exploit viral properties for sanitation or environmental purposes rather than for treating disease or vaccination.
[C12N 2720/12332] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The subclass covers therapeutic uses of double-stranded RNA viruses that are applied to treat disease without functioning as a vaccine, such as oncolytic or cytolytic agents that selectively destroy target cells.
[C12N 2720/12333] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- This CPC subclass (C12N 2720/12333) covers inventions that use proteins from double-stranded RNA viruses as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, for example to induce apoptosis or provide anti-inflammatory effects. It is nested under chemistry - biochemistry - microorganisms or enzymes - dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/12334] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/12334 concerns the use of viruses or viral components as vaccines, encompassing live-attenuated or inactivated viruses, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins for immunisation purposes. It resides within chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/12341] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- C12N 2720/12341 covers methods that employ double-stranded RNA viruses, their particles, or derived components as vectors for delivering genetic material or therapeutic agents into target cells. The classification is used for biotechnological applications such as gene therapy, vaccine development, and recombinant protein expression.
[C12N 2720/12342] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/12342 relates to employing whole viruses or isolated viral particles as carriers for small organic molecules, such as drugs or imaging agents. It covers methods and compositions where the viral vector encapsulates the compound to facilitate targeted delivery or controlled release.
[C12N 2720/12343] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2720/12343 covers the use of whole viral genomes or fragments of viral genetic material as vectors for delivering genes into target cells. It includes methods and compositions that exploit dsRNA viruses to transport nucleic acids for biotechnology, gene therapy, or vaccine development, falling under the chemistry branch of biochemistry and microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2720/12344] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- The code C12N 2720/12344 covers inventions that use engineered chimeric viral vectors containing genetic elements from different viruses to serve as a production system for a second, separate viral vector, often for gene-delivery or vaccine applications, with particular emphasis on double-stranded RNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/12345] Special targeting system for viral vectors
[C12N 2720/12351] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/12351 covers methods for producing or purifying viral material derived from double-stranded RNA viruses, encompassing biotechnological techniques used in virology and related fields. It is positioned within the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and dsRNA viruses.
[C12N 2720/12352] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The classification covers methods and materials that use complementing cell lines or packaging systems to supply missing functions required for the replication or assembly of double-stranded RNA viruses, enabling the production of virus particles or viral-like particles in vitro. It includes engineered host cells, helper plasmids or vectors and related bioprocesses that facilitate viral genome packaging and particle formation.
[C12N 2720/12361] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The CPC subclass C12N 2720/12361 covers biotechnological techniques for rendering double-stranded RNA viruses non-infectious or less virulent, including chemical, physical or genetic approaches that inactivate or attenuate the virus. These methods are used primarily in the preparation of safe viral vaccines and research tools.
[C12N 2720/12362] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2720/12362 designates inventions that employ genetic-engineering techniques to modify double-stranded RNA viruses. It covers recombinant construction, alteration, or expression of viral genomes for uses such as vaccine development, gene-delivery vectors, or studies of viral biology, falling under the biochemistry of microorganisms and enzymes.
[C12N 2720/12363] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2720/12363 covers chemical treatments aimed at double-stranded RNA viruses in the context of microorganisms or enzymes. It includes processes that inactivate, degrade, or modify such viruses by applying chemicals, for purposes such as disinfection, preservation, or therapeutic use.
[C12N 2720/12364] by serial passage -- C12N 2720/12364 relates to methods that modify or analyze dsRNA viruses by serial passage, meaning the virus is repeatedly transferred through host cells or organisms to select for traits such as enhanced virulence, altered host range, or greater stability.
[C12N 2720/12371] Demonstrated -- C12N 2720/12371 covers methods and compositions that demonstrate the presence, activity or characteristics of double-stranded RNA viruses, including detection, identification, quantification and functional assays. The classification is used for biotechnological and diagnostic techniques that prove a virus's existence or biological effect.
[C12N 2720/12388] for redistribution -- This CPC subclass C12N2720/12388 pertains to methods, devices or processes for the redistribution of double-stranded RNA viruses, including the transfer of viral particles or their genetic material in biotechnological or therapeutic applications. It resides under C12N2720 for dsRNA viruses within the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2730/00] Reverse transcribing DNA viruses -- C12N 2730/00 covers reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, a group of viruses whose DNA genomes are replicated through an RNA intermediate using reverse transcriptase, exemplified by the Hepadnaviridae family such as hepatitis B virus. The classification includes inventions related to the viruses themselves, their components, detection methods, therapeutic, prophylactic or diagnostic applications, and virus-derived vectors.
[C12N 2730/00011] Details
[C12N 2730/00021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- The CPC code C12N 2730/00021 covers inventions related to reverse-transcribing DNA viruses themselves, such as newly isolated strains, genetically engineered mutants and the nucleic-acid sequences that define them. It applies to patents describing the viruses as whole entities rather than specific applications or treatment methods.
[C12N 2730/00022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- This CPC subclass C12N 2730/00022 covers inventions relating to novel viral proteins or genes of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, including newly discovered structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes. It is used for patents concerning the identification, isolation, or modification of such proteins or genes for biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2730/00023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- The code C12N2730/00023 designates inventions related to virus-like particles that mimic the structure of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses but lack viral genetic material, and are used for vaccine delivery, diagnostics, or as nanocarriers.
[C12N 2730/00031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2730/00031 covers applications of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses that are not intended for therapy or vaccination, such as using the virus as a disinfectant or for other non-medical purposes. The class groups methods that exploit viral properties for environmental or industrial decontamination rather than clinical treatment.
[C12N 2730/00032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- This CPC subclass covers patents that disclose using reverse-transcribing DNA viruses as therapeutic agents for purposes other than vaccination, such as cytolytic or oncolytic applications. It includes methods where the virus is employed to kill or modify target cells, typically in disease treatment.
[C12N 2730/00033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory
[C12N 2730/00034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- C12N 2730/00034 classifies inventions that employ viruses or viral elements as vaccines, covering approaches such as live-attenuated or inactivated viruses, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins. It falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Reverse transcribing DNA viruses in the CPC system.
[C12N 2730/00041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- This subclass covers methods that employ reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, viral particles or specific viral components as carriers for delivering genetic material or other functional molecules into target cells, typically for gene therapy, vaccine production or molecular biology applications. It is situated within the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → reverse transcribing DNA viruses.
[C12N 2730/00042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- A classification for reverse-transcribing DNA viruses that are employed as carriers, where the virus or viral particle is used as a delivery vehicle to encapsulate and transport small organic molecules, typically in applications such as gene therapy, vaccine development or targeted drug delivery.
[C12N 2730/00043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2730/00043 refers to the use of whole viral genomes or fragments as genetic vectors, encompassing engineered viral particles that carry DNA or RNA into host cells for purposes such as gene therapy, vaccine development, or molecular cloning. It classifies technologies that exploit viral delivery mechanisms to introduce genetic material into target organisms.
[C12N 2730/00044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- This subclass covers chimeric viral vectors that incorporate genetic or structural elements from different viruses to serve as a production platform for a second, distinct viral vector. Such constructs are employed in gene-transfer, vaccine and recombinant-virus manufacturing, enabling efficient replication or packaging of the target vector.
[C12N 2730/00045] Special targeting system for viral vectors
[C12N 2730/00051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- This CPC subclass covers processes for obtaining viral material from reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, including methods that generate virus particles, nucleic acids, proteins or sub-viral components and the subsequent purification steps such as filtration, centrifugation or chromatography. It applies to production techniques for retroviruses, hepadnaviruses and related agents.
[C12N 2730/00052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The C12N 2730/00052 subclass deals with complementing cell lines and packaging systems employed to generate reverse-transcribing DNA viruses or viral particles. It covers methods and components that supply missing functions for virus replication in production hosts. This classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically reverse-transcribing DNA viruses.
[C12N 2730/00061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The C12N 2730/00061 CPC entry covers methods for inactivating or attenuating reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as retroviruses, to diminish their infectivity while preserving useful properties for vaccine development, diagnostics or research. It includes chemical, physical, enzymatic or genetic approaches that render the virus non-pathogenic but still antigenically active.
[C12N 2730/00062] by genetic engineering -- This CPC subclass concerns the creation or alteration of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses by genetic-engineering methods, including the insertion, deletion or modification of viral genetic material and the use of recombinant vectors. It is nested under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2730/00063] by chemical treatment -- The code C12N 2730/00063 covers chemical treatment of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as retroviruses, by using specific chemical agents to inactivate, modify, or inhibit the virus. It applies to antiviral compounds, disinfectants or other reagents that act on the viral genome or proteins, fitting within the biochemistry of microorganisms and enzymes. This classification is used to index patents describing such chemical approaches.
[C12N 2730/00064] by serial passage -- C12N 2730/00064 covers techniques in which reverse-transcribing DNA viruses are subjected to serial passage, i.e., repeated rounds of replication in host cells or culture, to induce genetic changes, adapt the virus to new conditions, or attenuate its pathogenicity. The classification lies within chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on viral manipulation for research, vaccine development, or biotechnology.
[C12N 2730/00071] Demonstrated -- C12N 2730/00071 identifies the demonstration of a reverse-transcribing DNA virus, covering experimental examples that prove the virus's existence, activity or engineered traits. It is applied to disclosures where the virus is produced or its characteristics are shown, rather than to detailed production methods or therapeutic uses.
[C12N 2730/00088] for redistribution -- This CPC subclass covers inventions relating to the redistribution of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as engineered viral vectors used to deliver genetic material. It includes methods for propagating, packaging, or transferring these viruses to host cells or organisms for research, therapeutic or diagnostic purposes. The code C12N2730/00088 designates this subclass.
[C12N 2730/10011] Hepadnaviridae -- The CPC subclass C12N 2730/10011 covers the Hepadnaviridae family, a group of small enveloped viruses with partially double-stranded DNA genomes that replicate via a reverse-transcription step. Members, such as hepatitis B virus, infect liver cells of vertebrates and can cause acute and chronic hepatitis. This subclass groups the structural, genetic and pathogenic features specific to these reverse-transcribing DNA viruses.
[C12N 2730/10021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences
[C12N 2730/10022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The CPC subclass C12N 2730/10022 covers inventions concerning reverse-transcribing DNA viruses that disclose new viral proteins or individual genes. It also includes discoveries of novel structural or functional characteristics of already known viral proteins or genes.
[C12N 2730/10023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- The CPC subclass C12N 2730/10023 covers virus-like particles (VLPs), which are self-assembled structures that resemble authentic viruses but contain no viral genome. It encompasses methods for producing, purifying and using VLPs in vaccine formulation, drug delivery and diagnostic applications, particularly for reverse-transcribing DNA viruses.
[C12N 2730/10031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2730/10031 covers non-therapeutic applications of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as using the virus or its components as disinfectants, biocides, or for surface decontamination, rather than for medical treatment or vaccination. This falls under the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms-enzymes branch of the CPC.
[C12N 2730/10032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- C12N 2730/10032 covers the therapeutic use of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses in ways other than vaccination, such as employing them as cytolytic or oncolytic agents, gene-delivery vectors, or other medical treatments that exploit the virus's ability to infect and destroy target cells. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy for biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically reverse-transcribing DNA viruses.
[C12N 2730/10033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- This CPC subclass covers the use of proteins from reverse-transcribing DNA viruses as therapeutic agents that are not vaccines, such as molecules that induce apoptosis or provide anti-inflammatory effects. It falls under the biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes, specifically targeting applications of viral proteins for medical treatment.
[C12N 2730/10034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein

It covers the design, modification and use of such vectors to introduce DNA, RNA or proteins into target cells for purposes such as gene therapy, vaccine development, or recombinant protein production.
[C12N 2730/10042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- This subclass covers the use of whole viruses or viral particles as carriers for small organic compounds, such as drugs, imaging agents or other active substances. It includes methods where the viral vector encapsulates the molecule for delivery, typically applied in therapeutic or diagnostic contexts.
[C12N 2730/10043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2730/10043 relates to the use of viral genomes or genome fragments from reverse-transcribing DNA viruses as genetic vectors, covering constructs that deliver genetic material into host cells for purposes such as recombinant DNA production, gene therapy, or biotechnology applications. This subclass focuses on the design, preparation, and application of such viral-based delivery systems.
[C12N 2730/10044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- The class covers engineered viral vectors that fuse genetic parts from different viruses to serve as a platform for producing a second, distinct viral vector. It includes chimeric constructs where heterologous viral elements are combined to enable replication, packaging or delivery of another vector for gene therapy, vaccine or research applications.
[C12N 2730/10045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- C12N 2730/10045 covers methods and compositions that direct viral vectors to specific cells or tissues, including engineered envelope proteins, surface ligands or nucleic-acid tags that confer selective entry. The classification is used for inventions that improve the safety and efficacy of gene delivery by controlling vector tropism, especially for reverse-transcribing DNA viruses such as lentiviruses.
[C12N 2730/10051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- The C12N 2730/10051 subclass covers techniques for producing or purifying viral material, specifically targeting reverse-transcribing DNA viruses such as retroviruses. It includes methods that isolate, concentrate, or otherwise prepare viral particles, nucleic acids, or related components for research, diagnostic, or therapeutic use.
[C12N 2730/10052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The C12N 2730/10052 subclass deals with complementing cell lines and packaging systems used to generate virus or viral particles, particularly for reverse-transcribing DNA viruses. It covers methods and compositions in which cells supply missing viral functions, enabling the production of recombinant or helper-dependent viral vectors for research, gene-therapy or vaccine applications.
[C12N 2730/10061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The C12N 2730/10061 subclass covers techniques for reducing the infectivity or pathogenicity of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as lentiviruses and hepadnaviruses, by chemically, physically or genetically inactivating or attenuating them. These methods are employed in vaccine development, viral vector preparation and biosafety procedures.
