[C12N 2730/10063] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2730/10063 classifies processes that act on reverse-transcribing DNA viruses by means of chemical treatment. It covers the use of chemical agents to inactivate, modify, or otherwise treat such viruses, including methods for disinfection, purification, or preparation of viral material. This subclass is nested under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically targeting reverse-transcribing DNA viruses.
[C12N 2730/10064] by serial passage -- A method that entails repeatedly culturing reverse-transcribing DNA viruses in a host system so that each successive passage selects for desired genetic or phenotypic changes. The technique is used to adapt, attenuate or otherwise modify viral strains through the accumulation of mutations acquired during serial replication cycles. It falls under the CPC subclass for microorganisms or enzymes, specifically reverse-transcribing DNA viruses.
[C12N 2730/10071] Demonstrated -- The CPC subclass C12N2730/10071, titled Demonstrated, relates to inventions that provide experimental proof or verification of the presence, activity, or function of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as retroviruses, within a biochemical or microbial context. It includes assays, detection methods and evidence of viral replication or integration.
[C12N 2730/10088] for redistribution -- This subclass covers inventions related to the redistribution of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as retroviral particles used as vectors for delivering genetic material. It includes methods for transferring, spreading or re-introducing these viruses in host cells, as well as formulations that facilitate their distribution. The focus is on the handling and dissemination of the viruses rather than their production or modification.
[C12N 2730/10111] Orthohepadnavirus, e.g. hepatitis B virus -- C12N 2730/10111 classifies reverse-transcribing DNA viruses of the genus orthohepadnavirus, including hepatitis B virus. These are enveloped, partially double-stranded DNA viruses that replicate through an RNA intermediate using reverse transcriptase. The code falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes, indicating relevance to viral genetics and therapeutic research.
[C12N 2730/10121] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- This subclass covers inventions concerning whole reverse-transcribing DNA viruses themselves, including newly isolated strains, genetically altered mutants, and their complete genomic sequences. It relates to the virus particles or isolates as entities rather than to individual genes, proteins or recombinant fragments derived from them.
[C12N 2730/10122] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- C12N 2730/10122 covers inventions concerning newly identified proteins or individual genes of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, as well as novel structural or functional characteristics of previously known viral proteins or genes. It is used to classify biotechnological and pharmaceutical applications involving these viral components.
[C12N 2730/10123] Virus like particles [VLP] -- C12N 2730/10123 covers virus-like particles, non-infectious structures that mimic the morphology of viruses but lack viral genetic material. These particles are engineered from viral capsid proteins and are used as vaccines, delivery vehicles, or research tools to study immune responses without the risk of replication.
[C12N 2730/10131] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- The C12N 2730/10131 class covers non-therapeutic or non-vaccine uses of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as employing the viruses or their components for disinfection, biocontrol, or other industrial and agricultural applications. It pertains to biotechnological exploitation of these viruses beyond medical treatment.
[C12N 2730/10132] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The CPC subclass C12N 2730/10132 relates to using reverse-transcribing DNA viruses as therapeutic agents that are not vaccines, such as cytolytic or oncolytic viruses employed to kill target cells. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes and specifically addresses virus-based treatments for disease control.
[C12N 2730/10133] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- The CPC subclass C12N 2730/10133 covers the use of proteins taken from reverse-transcribing DNA viruses as therapeutic agents that are not vaccines, for example compounds that induce apoptosis or provide anti-inflammatory effects. It resides within the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically reverse-transcribing DNA viruses.
[C12N 2730/10134] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- This CPC subclass covers inventions that employ reverse-transcribing DNA viruses or their parts, such as live-attenuated or inactivated whole viruses, virus-like particles, or purified viral proteins, as immunogenic agents for vaccination. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2730/10141] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- This CPC subclass covers methods where reverse-transcribing DNA viruses or components thereof are employed to deliver genetic material into host cells for research, therapy, or vaccine production. It includes the use of intact virions, viral capsids or engineered genome fragments as vectors to introduce nucleic acids.
[C12N 2730/10142] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- This CPC subclass concerns the use of whole viruses or isolated viral particles as carriers for small organic molecules, such as pharmaceuticals or chemical agents, by encapsulating the compounds within the viral capsid or envelope. It includes methods for preparing, modifying, and employing virus-based delivery systems.
[C12N 2730/10143] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2730/10143 pertains to the use of whole viral genomes or fragments thereof as genetic vectors, especially those derived from reverse-transcribing DNA viruses such as retroviruses. The classification covers methods for constructing, modifying, and employing these viral elements to transfer nucleic acids into host cells for applications in gene therapy, vaccine development, and molecular biology.
[C12N 2730/10144] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- In the CPC, under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → reverse transcribing DNA viruses, code C12N 2730/10144 pertains to chimeric viral vectors that incorporate heterologous viral elements to produce another viral vector, a strategy commonly used in recombinant vector production and gene-therapy applications.
[C12N 2730/10145] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- The subclass covers engineered systems that confer cell-type or tissue specificity to viral vectors derived from reverse-transcribing DNA viruses such as retroviruses and lentiviruses. It includes modified envelope proteins, ligand or aptamer attachments, and other molecular strategies that direct the vector to selected target cells for gene-therapy, vaccine or research applications.
[C12N 2730/10151] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- C12N 2730/10151 covers processes for producing and purifying viral material from reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as methods to grow, isolate, and refine whole virus particles, nucleic acids, or proteins. These techniques enable laboratory research, vaccine manufacturing, and gene-therapy vector preparation.
[C12N 2730/10152] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- A method that uses complementing cell lines together with specialized packaging systems to generate reverse-transcribing DNA viruses or viral particles, enabling production of infectious virions without the need for full viral genomes in the host cells. This approach supports research, vaccine development and gene-therapy vector manufacturing.
[C12N 2730/10161] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- C12N 2730/10161 covers techniques for rendering reverse-transcribing DNA viruses non-infectious or less virulent, including chemical, physical or genetic approaches that inactivate viral particles or attenuate their pathogenicity for research, vaccine development or safety testing. It falls within the microbiology-biochemistry hierarchy of the CPC.
[C12N 2730/10162] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2730/10162 covers the use of genetic-engineering techniques to modify or construct reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as retroviruses, for research, therapeutic or industrial purposes. The classification addresses methods for inserting, deleting, or altering viral genetic material, as well as the production of recombinant viral particles and their application in gene delivery or vaccine development.
[C12N 2730/10163] by chemical treatment -- The CPC subclass C12N 2730/10163 covers methods for treating or inactivating reverse-transcribing DNA viruses-such as hepatitis B virus-through the application of chemical agents. It includes processes that chemically modify, degrade, or otherwise neutralize viral particles or their genetic material, typically for therapeutic, prophylactic, or decontamination purposes. This subclass falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2730/10164] by serial passage -- The CPC subclass C12N 2730/10164 relates to the use of serial passage techniques to alter or adapt reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as retroviruses, for purposes including strain improvement, attenuation, or studying viral evolution. It covers methods where the virus is repeatedly cultured in successive host systems to induce genetic changes.
[C12N 2730/10171] Demonstrated
[C12N 2730/10188] for redistribution -- This subclass concerns inventions directed at the redistribution of reverse-transcribing DNA viruses, such as lentiviruses, including methods, vectors, or formulations for transferring the virus to new host cells or organisms. It covers biotechnological processes that use these viral agents for purposes such as gene delivery, vaccine development, or related therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2740/00] Reverse transcribing RNA viruses -- The CPC subclass C12N 2740/00 covers reverse transcribing RNA viruses, which are RNA viruses that replicate via a DNA intermediate using reverse transcriptase. These include retroviruses and related agents, and the classification is used for patent documents concerning their genetics, replication mechanisms, and related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2740/00011] Details
[C12N 2740/00021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- The CPC entry C12N 2740/00021 covers viruses themselves, including newly isolated strains, mutants and the genomic sequences that define them. It applies to reverse-transcribing RNA viruses and is used to classify patent applications that claim the virus particles, modified versions or their genetic material.
[C12N 2740/00022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The CPC subclass C12N 2740/00022 covers inventions concerning novel proteins or individual genes of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, as well as newly discovered structural or functional features of already known viral proteins or genes. It includes designs for recombinant expression, characterization, and uses of such viral components in diagnostics, therapeutics, or biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2740/00023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- The CPC subclass C12N 2740/00023 covers virus-like particles (VLPs) that mimic the morphology of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses but lack the viral genome, making them non-infectious. VLPs are employed in vaccine formulation, diagnostic assays and as delivery vectors for therapeutic molecules.
[C12N 2740/00031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- The CPC class C12N 2740/00031 covers applications of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses that are not intended for therapy or vaccination, such as using the virus as a disinfectant, biocontrol agent, or for decontamination purposes.
[C12N 2740/00032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- This subclass relates to the therapeutic application of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses that are not employed as vaccines, for example as oncolytic agents that selectively lyse target cells, particularly cancer cells. It covers methods of using such viruses to treat disease through their cytolytic activity.
[C12N 2740/00033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- This CPC subclass covers inventions that employ proteins derived from reverse-transcribing RNA viruses for therapeutic purposes other than vaccination, such as agents that trigger cell death or modulate inflammation. It is nested under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and reverse-transcribing RNA viruses.
[C12N 2740/00034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- This CPC subclass (C12N 2740/00034) relates to the use of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses or their components as vaccines. It includes live-attenuated or inactivated whole viruses, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins employed to elicit an immune response.
[C12N 2740/00041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- This subclass covers the use of whole viruses, viral particles, or components such as capsids, genomes or envelope proteins as delivery vehicles for genetic material, therapeutic agents or vaccines. It includes methods for constructing, modifying and employing viral vectors for gene transfer, expression or immunisation.
[C12N 2740/00042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- C12N 2740/00042 covers the use of viruses or virus-like particles as carriers, especially for encapsulating small organic molecules such as drugs or imaging agents. The class is applied to reverse-transcribing RNA viruses and related biotechnological processes where the viral capsid serves as a delivery vehicle. It includes methods of loading, stabilising, and employing viral particles for targeted transport in medical or industrial applications.
[C12N 2740/00043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- The subclass C12N 2740/00043 relates to inventions that employ a viral genome-or fragments, genes or regulatory elements derived from it-as a vector for transferring genetic material into a host cell, such as in gene-therapy, vaccine or recombinant-DNA applications. It is nested under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → reverse-transcribing RNA viruses.
[C12N 2740/00044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- The class covers engineered chimeric viral vectors that combine genetic elements from different viruses to serve as a production platform for a second viral vector. It applies to reverse-transcribing RNA viruses and includes constructs where heterologous viral parts are fused to enhance vector yield, specificity or safety.
[C12N 2740/00045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- The CPC entry C12N 2740/00045 covers engineered viral vectors that incorporate special targeting systems to direct reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as lentiviruses, to specific cell types or tissues. It includes modifications of envelope proteins, ligand-binding domains or other strategies that enhance selective infection for gene-therapy or research applications.
[C12N 2740/00051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- This subclass covers techniques for producing or isolating viral particles, nucleic acids, or proteins from reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, including cell culture propagation, vector systems, and purification steps such as centrifugation or chromatography. It is used to protect inventions related to manufacturing viral vectors for gene therapy or vaccine production.
[C12N 2740/00052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The entry covers cell lines or other biological systems engineered to supply missing viral functions (complementing cells) together with packaging components that enable the assembly and release of reverse-transcribing RNA virus particles. These tools are used to produce viral vectors or particle stocks for research, gene therapy and vaccine applications.
[C12N 2740/00061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- Methods for reducing the infectivity of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, by chemical, physical or genetic means to produce attenuated strains for vaccines, diagnostic reagents or laboratory study. Techniques include heat or radiation treatment, chemical fixation, enzymatic degradation, and targeted deletions or mutations that impair replication while preserving antigenic properties.
[C12N 2740/00062] by genetic engineering -- The C12N 2740/00062 subclass covers biotechnological methods that employ genetic engineering to create, modify or utilize reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, for research, therapeutic or industrial purposes. It lies within the chemistry hierarchy under biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and specifically focuses on engineered viral vectors and related constructs.
[C12N 2740/00063] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2740/00063 covers chemical treatment of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, within the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes. The sub-class deals with using chemical agents to inactivate, inhibit, or otherwise modify these viruses for therapeutic or research purposes.
[C12N 2740/00064] by serial passage -- A method for modifying reverse-transcribing RNA viruses through repeated cycles of infection or replication in host cells, known as serial passage, to select for desired traits such as altered virulence, host range, or resistance. This technique exploits natural viral evolution under controlled laboratory conditions to generate adapted or attenuated virus strains.
[C12N 2740/00071] Demonstrated -- C12N 2740/00071 covers methods for demonstrating the presence or activity of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, including assays that confirm viral replication, integration or infectivity. The classification applies to diagnostic techniques, cell-based systems and molecular tools used to prove that a virus is functional or present.
[C12N 2740/00088] for redistribution
[C12N 2740/10011] Retroviridae -- C12N 2740/10011 designates the Retroviridae family, a group of enveloped RNA viruses that replicate through a DNA intermediate using reverse transcriptase. Members include HIV, HTLV and related pathogens that integrate into host genomes, causing diseases such as immunodeficiency and leukemia. This subclass covers their structure, replication cycle and related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2740/10021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- C12N 2740/10021 covers biotechnological subject matter concerning viruses themselves, specifically reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, including newly isolated strains, engineered mutants and their genomic sequences. The class is used for inventions that focus on the virus as a biological entity rather than on methods of treatment or diagnostic use.
[C12N 2740/10022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes
[C12N 2740/10023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- The C12N 2740/10023 entry covers virus-like particles, which are non-infectious structures that mimic the morphology of viruses, typically formed from self-assembled capsid proteins of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses. These particles are used as platforms for vaccine delivery, gene therapy vectors, and diagnostic reagents, exploiting their ability to present antigens without containing viral genetic material.
[C12N 2740/10031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- The C12N 2740/10031 classification covers applications of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses that are not intended for therapeutic or vaccine purposes, such as using the viruses as disinfectants, sterilising agents or for other non-medical functions. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → reverse transcribing RNA viruses.
[C12N 2740/10032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- This classification covers methods that employ reverse-transcribing RNA viruses as therapeutic agents, not for vaccination but for direct treatment, such as using them to selectively kill cancer cells. It includes the design, modification, and application of oncolytic retroviral vectors.
[C12N 2740/10033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- The CPC subclass C12N 2740/10033 covers the use of proteins from reverse-transcribing RNA viruses as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, including compounds that induce apoptosis or have anti-inflammatory activity. It lies within the hierarchy chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → reverse transcribing RNA viruses.
[C12N 2740/10034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- This subclass covers inventions wherein a virus or parts of a virus, such as whole attenuated or inactivated particles, virus-like particles, or viral proteins, are employed as vaccines against diseases, specifically relating to reverse-transcribing RNA viruses. It includes methods for preparation, formulation, and use of these biological immunogens.
[C12N 2740/10041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- The code C12N 2740/10041 covers inventions that use a virus, viral particle or viral element as a delivery vector, typically for genetic material, in the field of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy of biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes and addresses the use of viral vectors in biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2740/10042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- C12N 2740/10042 covers the use of a virus or viral particle as a delivery vehicle, for example to encapsulate and transport small organic molecules. It applies to reverse-transcribing RNA viruses and related biotechnological applications such as gene therapy, drug delivery, or vaccine formulation. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2740/10043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- This classification covers viral genomes or genome fragments derived from reverse-transcribing RNA viruses that are used as vectors for delivering genetic material into host cells. It includes engineered retroviral vectors, replicon systems and related constructs employed in gene therapy, vaccine development and molecular biology.
[C12N 2740/10044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- The classification covers chimeric viral vectors that combine genetic components from different viruses to serve as factories for generating a separate viral vector. Such constructs incorporate heterologous viral elements and are employed in the production of reverse-transcribing RNA virus vectors for research or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2740/10045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- C12N 2740/10045 concerns engineered targeting mechanisms that direct viral vectors to specific cells or tissues, especially for reverse-transcribing RNA viruses such as retroviruses. It covers specialized ligands, receptors or molecular tags incorporated into the vector to improve delivery precision and therapeutic efficacy.
[C12N 2740/10051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- The C12N 2740/10051 subclass covers methods for producing, isolating and purifying viral material from reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses. It includes techniques for culturing infected cells, extracting viral particles or nucleic acids, and downstream purification steps like precipitation, chromatography or filtration to obtain usable virus preparations.
[C12N 2740/10052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The C12N 2740/10052 classification covers methods and biological materials that use complementing cell lines together with packaging systems to generate recombinant reverse-transcribing RNA viruses or viral particles. It includes engineered host cells providing missing viral functions and the vector components required for virus assembly, enabling production of replication-deficient or pseudotyped viral vectors for research or therapeutic use.
[C12N 2740/10061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The CPC subclass C12N 2740/10061 covers methods for inactivating or attenuating reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, to reduce their pathogenicity for purposes like vaccine production, diagnostic testing or basic research. It falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Reverse transcribing RNA viruses, and includes chemical, physical or genetic approaches that render the virus non-infectious while preserving antigenic structures.
[C12N 2740/10062] by genetic engineering -- This CPC subclass addresses the use of genetic-engineering techniques to modify reverse-transcribing RNA viruses. It includes recombinant constructs, engineered host cells or vectors designed to alter viral genomes for purposes such as research, vaccine development, or therapy.
[C12N 2740/10063] by chemical treatment -- The subclass C12N 2740/10063 concerns chemical approaches for treating reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, encompassing the use of agents that inactivate, destroy, or otherwise modify such viruses, for example through chemical inhibition of viral replication or disruption of viral particles.
[C12N 2740/10064] by serial passage -- C12N 2740/10064 relates to techniques in which reverse-transcribing RNA viruses are subjected to serial passage, i.e., repeated rounds of infection in host cells or organisms, to select for desired traits such as increased virulence, drug resistance, or altered host range. The classification is used for patents describing the generation or optimisation of retroviral strains through this iterative propagation.
[C12N 2740/10071] Demonstrated -- The CPC subclass C12N 2740/10071 relates to inventions that provide a demonstration of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses. It covers methods, assays or experimental evidence that confirm the presence, replication or functional activity of these viruses, typically for diagnostic or research purposes.
[C12N 2740/10088] for redistribution -- C12N2740/10088 pertains to biotechnological methods and compositions used for the redistribution of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, including techniques for delivering viral particles to target cells or environments. The entry covers processes that facilitate controlled spread or transfer of these viruses for research, therapeutic or diagnostic purposes.
[C12N 2740/11011] Alpharetrovirus, e.g. avian leucosis virus -- The code refers to alpharetroviruses, a subclass of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses that infect birds, exemplified by avian leucosis virus. It encompasses the virus's structure, replication cycle and related biotechnological uses within the broader field of microbial biochemistry.

It is used for patent claims directed at novel viral components that may serve as targets for diagnostics, therapeutics, or vaccine development.

[C12N 2740/13044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- In the CPC class C12N 2740/13044, which falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, the entry covers chimeric viral vectors that incorporate heterologous viral elements and are used to produce a second viral vector for gene delivery or vaccine production.

[C12N 2740/13061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The C12N 2740/13061 classification covers techniques for inactivating or attenuating reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, to reduce their infectivity while preserving structural or antigenic properties for research, vaccine development, or diagnostic use. These methods may involve physical, chemical, or genetic approaches that render the virus non-replicative but still recognizable to the immune system.
[C12N 2740/13062] by genetic engineering -- This CPC subclass covers the genetic engineering of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, where their genomes are modified to produce recombinant vectors, vaccine strains or other biotechnological tools.
[C12N 2740/13063] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2740/13063 classifies chemical approaches for treating infections caused by reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses and hepatitis B virus, encompassing the use of antiviral compounds, inhibitors, or other chemically active agents that interfere with viral replication or assembly. The category falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → reverse transcribing RNA viruses.
[C12N 2740/13064] by serial passage -- The CPC subclass C12N 2740/13064 covers techniques that use repeated, sequential infection cycles-serial passage-to modify reverse-transcribing RNA viruses such as retroviruses or hepatitis B virus. It includes methods for adapting viruses to new host cells, selecting phenotypic traits, or attenuating pathogenicity for research or vaccine development.
[C12N 2740/13071] Demonstrated -- This subclass covers inventions that provide experimental evidence that a specific reverse-transcribing RNA virus is present, functional, or has been isolated, including assays, detection methods, or proof of replication. It is situated in the chemistry hierarchy under biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and deals specifically with reverse-transcribing RNA viruses.
[C12N 2740/13088] for redistribution -- The code C12N 2740/13088 covers inventions dealing with the redistribution of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as methods for transferring, delivering or dispersing these viral particles to new host cells or organisms. It includes compositions, vectors or processes that facilitate the spread or reuse of the viruses for research, therapy or biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2740/14011] Deltaretrovirus, e.g. bovine leukeamia virus -- C12N 2740/14011 designates deltaretroviruses, notably bovine leukemia virus, within the subgroup of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses. The classification is applied to inventions involving the isolation, manipulation, diagnostic detection or therapeutic use of these retroviral particles and their genetic material. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → reverse transcribing RNA viruses.
[C12N 2740/14021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- The class C12N 2740/14021 pertains to reverse-transcribing RNA viruses themselves, covering inventions related to newly isolated virus strains, their mutants and the corresponding genomic sequences. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on the viruses as whole biological entities rather than specific functional components.
[C12N 2740/14022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The CPC subclass C12N 2740/14022 covers inventions related to reverse-transcribing RNA viruses and includes the disclosure of newly identified viral proteins or individual genes, as well as novel structural or functional aspects of already known viral proteins or genes.
[C12N 2740/14023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- C12N 2740/14023 covers virus-like particles, which are self-assembled protein structures that imitate the morphology of viruses but lack viral genetic material, making them non-infectious. They are employed in vaccine development, diagnostics and as carriers for therapeutic molecules. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and reverse transcribing RNA viruses.
[C12N 2740/14031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2740/14031 covers the use of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses for non-therapeutic purposes, such as disinfectants, decontamination agents or other industrial applications, where the virus is employed for its functional properties rather than as a vaccine or drug.
[C12N 2740/14032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The C12N 2740/14032 subclass covers the therapeutic use of viruses that are not intended as vaccines, including their application as cytolytic agents, oncolytic virotherapy, or vectors for delivering therapeutic genes to treat disease. It pertains to reverse-transcribing RNA viruses employed for direct medicinal effects.
[C12N 2740/14033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- C12N 2740/14033 refers to the use of proteins derived from reverse-transcribing RNA viruses as therapeutic agents that are not intended as vaccines, such as compounds that induce apoptosis or provide anti-inflammatory effects. This falls under chemistry and biochemistry, specifically the study of microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2740/14034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- This subclass covers methods that employ reverse-transcribing RNA viruses or their parts as immunogens, including live-attenuated or inactivated whole viruses, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins, to induce protective immunity as vaccine candidates.
[C12N 2740/14041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- The class covers methods that employ reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, viral particles, or derived viral elements as vectors for delivering nucleic acids into target cells, enabling gene therapy, vaccine development, or molecular biology applications.
[C12N 2740/14042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- This subclass concerns the use of viruses or viral particles as delivery vehicles that encapsulate small organic molecules, such as pharmaceuticals or imaging agents, within their capsids. It applies specifically to reverse-transcribing RNA viruses and related biotechnology applications.
[C12N 2740/14043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- This subclass covers the use of whole viral genomes or genome fragments from reverse-transcribing RNA viruses as vectors for delivering genetic material into host cells. It includes designs where the viral nucleic acid is modified to carry foreign genes for gene therapy, vaccine production, or functional genomics. The classification pertains to biochemical applications involving such engineered vectors.
[C12N 2740/14044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- C12N 2740/14044 covers chimeric viral vectors that incorporate genetic or structural components from different viruses to serve as a platform for generating a second, distinct viral vector. The classification is used for inventions involving reverse-transcribing RNA viruses engineered for vector production in biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2740/14045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- C12N 2740/14045 covers methods and compositions that confer specific cellular or tissue targeting to viral vectors, especially those derived from reverse-transcribing RNA viruses such as lentiviruses. The classification includes engineered envelope proteins, ligand-display strategies, or other molecular modifications that direct the vector to particular host cells while preserving infectivity.
[C12N 2740/14051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- C12N 2740/14051 addresses processes for producing or purifying viral particles, nucleic acids, or other components of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses such as retroviruses. The classification applies to biotechnological methods that isolate, concentrate, or prepare viral material for research, diagnostics, or therapeutic use.
[C12N 2740/14052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The class covers genetically engineered complementing cell lines and packaging systems designed to produce reverse-transcribing RNA viruses or viral particles, enabling efficient generation of virus vectors for research, vaccine or gene-therapy applications. It addresses methods for providing missing viral functions in trans and for assembling viral components in safe producer cells.
[C12N 2740/14061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- C12N 2740/14061 covers methods for inactivating or attenuating reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, using physical, chemical or genetic techniques that reduce or eliminate infectivity for vaccine production, diagnostic reagents, or basic research.
[C12N 2740/14062] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2740/14062 covers biotechnological inventions in which reverse-transcribing RNA viruses are created, modified, or used through genetic-engineering techniques. It includes engineered retroviral vectors, recombinant viral particles, and related methods for producing or applying such viruses in research, therapy or diagnostics.
[C12N 2740/14063] by chemical treatment -- C12N2740/14063 classifies inventions that use chemical compounds or processes to treat reverse-transcribing RNA viruses such as retroviruses. It covers antiviral agents, formulations, or chemical modification methods aimed at inhibiting or destroying these viruses. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and specifically the subcategory of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses.
[C12N 2740/14064] by serial passage -- The code C12N 2740/14064 covers methods that use serial passage-repeated rounds of replication in host cells or culture-to adapt, attenuate, or otherwise modify reverse-transcribing RNA viruses. By continuously culturing the virus, researchers can select for desired phenotypic changes such as increased infectivity, resistance to antiviral compounds, or reduced pathogenicity. This technique is commonly employed in virology and vaccine development.

[C12N 2740/14088] for redistribution -- The subclass C12N 2740/14088 covers inventions dealing with the redistribution of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviral or lentiviral particles, including methods for delivering, spreading, or transferring these viruses to target cells, tissues, or organisms for applications like gene therapy, vaccine production, or research. It focuses on the means of moving the viral vectors rather than their creation or modification.

It is part of the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes hierarchy.

These VLPs are employed as vaccine carriers, gene-delivery vectors, or diagnostic reagents, exploiting the capsid architecture of retroviruses while remaining biologically inert.

The class includes methods, compositions, and virus-engineered constructs designed to exploit viral replication for treatment of cancers or other disorders.

It resides in the CPC hierarchy under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically reverse-transcribing RNA viruses.

[C12N 2740/15043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- This subclass covers the use of whole viral genomes or fragments such as capsid, envelope or regulatory sequences as vectors to deliver genetic material into host cells for recombinant DNA, gene therapy, vaccine or other biotechnological applications. It includes methods of constructing, modifying and applying such vectors derived from reverse-transcribing RNA viruses.
[C12N 2740/15044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- This subclass covers engineered, chimeric viral vectors that incorporate genetic elements from different reverse-transcribing RNA viruses in order to serve as a production platform for a second viral vector, a technology commonly applied in gene-therapy and vaccine development.

It encompasses molecular or protein-based systems that confer selective entry or integration of these vectors.

It includes processes for culturing, harvesting, and isolating the virus or its nucleic acids for research or commercial use.
[C12N 2740/15052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The CPC subclass C12N 2740/15052 covers engineered complementing cell lines and associated packaging systems that supply missing viral components needed to assemble functional reverse-transcribing RNA viruses or virus-like particles. It includes methods and vectors for producing recombinant viruses, viral vectors, or virus particles for research, vaccine development, or gene-therapy applications.
[C12N 2740/15061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The CPC entry C12N 2740/15061 covers methods for inactivating or attenuating reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses and hepatitis B virus, to reduce their pathogenicity while preserving immunogenic properties. These techniques are used in vaccine production, viral vector preparation, and basic research on virus biology.

It includes techniques for creating recombinant viral vectors, altering viral properties, and producing modified viruses for research, vaccine or gene-therapy purposes.
[C12N 2740/15063] by chemical treatment -- The subclass C12N 2740/15063 covers chemical treatment of reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, including the use of agents, conditions and processes to inactivate, modify, or otherwise prepare these viruses for further study or application. It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and specifically addresses chemical methods applied to such viral entities.
[C12N 2740/15064] by serial passage -- The C12N 2740/15064 subclass covers methods that employ serial passage to modify or adapt reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as retroviruses, through repeated replication cycles in host cells or cultures. This technique is used to select for traits like increased virulence, host range, or resistance to antiviral agents by allowing natural genetic variation to accumulate over successive passages.
[C12N 2740/15071] Demonstrated -- The C12N 2740/15071 subclass groups patent disclosures that provide experimental proof of concept for reverse-transcribing RNA viruses, such as demonstrated replication, infectivity, or functional activity in a host or test system, confirming the virus's biological properties.
