[C12N 2795/00] Bacteriophages -- C12N 2795/00 relates to bacteriophages, viruses that infect bacteria, and covers their isolation, characterization, genetic modification and use in therapeutic, diagnostic or biocontrol applications. It also includes compositions containing phages and methods for employing them to treat bacterial infections or to detect bacterial strains.
[C12N 2795/00011] Details
[C12N 2795/00021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/00021 pertains to inventions dealing with viruses themselves, such as newly isolated viral strains, genetically altered mutants, or their disclosed genomic sequences, regardless of their use. It is used to classify biotechnological claims that focus on the virus as a material rather than on specific applications or products derived from it.
[C12N 2795/00022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/00022 covers inventions that disclose new viral proteins or individual genes, as well as novel structural or functional characteristics of already known viral proteins or genes, primarily in the context of bacteriophages and related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/00023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- This CPC subclass (C12N 2795/00023) pertains to virus-like particles, which are non-infectious assemblies that imitate the outer structure of viruses-often derived from bacteriophages-without containing functional genetic material, and are employed in vaccines, diagnostics and nanotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/00031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- This subclass covers non-therapeutic and non-vaccine applications of bacteriophages, describing the use of the virus as a disinfectant, biocontrol agent, or for other industrial purposes. It is situated under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Bacteriophages in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[C12N 2795/00032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- This CPC subclass covers the therapeutic use of viruses, especially bacteriophages, for purposes other than vaccination, including employing them as cytolytic agents that directly kill target cells or pathogens. It falls under chemistry - biochemistry - microorganisms or enzymes - bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/00033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/00033 covers therapeutic applications of viral proteins derived from bacteriophages that are not used as vaccines. It includes uses such as inducing apoptosis in target cells or providing anti-inflammatory effects, focusing on the protein's biological activity rather than immunization.
[C12N 2795/00034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/00034 covers inventions that use whole viruses, virus-like particles or viral proteins as vaccines, including live-attenuated, inactivated, virus-like particle and recombinant protein formulations. It falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/00041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- This CPC subclass covers inventions that use viruses, viral particles or viral elements as delivery vectors for genetic material, therapeutic agents, or other functional payloads. It includes methods for employing viral vectors in gene transfer, vaccine production, and related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/00042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- The class deals with the use of whole viruses or virus-like particles as carriers that encapsulate small organic molecules such as drugs, imaging agents or other active agents. It includes methods for loading, protecting, targeting and releasing the encapsulated compounds, as well as the resulting compositions and pharmaceutical formulations.
[C12N 2795/00043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2795/00043 pertains to the use of viral genomes or viral elements as vectors for transferring genetic material into host organisms, including the design, construction and application of such vectors for gene delivery, expression or therapy. It falls under the bacteriophage subcategory of biotechnology.
[C12N 2795/00044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- This CPC subclass defines chimeric viral vectors that incorporate genetic or structural elements from different viruses and are used to produce a second, distinct viral vector, often for gene delivery, vaccine manufacturing or protein expression purposes.
[C12N 2795/00045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- The subclass concerns engineered targeting mechanisms that direct viral vectors, particularly bacteriophage-based carriers, to specific host cells or tissues. It includes modifications of capsid proteins, attachment ligands or receptor-binding domains designed to improve delivery specificity for gene therapy, bacterial eradication or diagnostic applications.
[C12N 2795/00051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- C12N 2795/00051 covers techniques for producing and purifying viral particles, including bacteriophages, used in research, diagnostics, or therapeutic applications. The code includes methods for culturing host microorganisms, harvesting viruses, and applying processes such as precipitation, centrifugation, chromatography, or filtration to obtain purified viral material.
[C12N 2795/00052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/00052 covers technologies for complementing host cells and providing packaging systems used to generate bacteriophages or virus-derived particles. It includes methods that supply missing functions in cells and the accompanying vectors or components that enable efficient assembly and release of viral particles for research or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2795/00061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The C12N 2795/00061 subclass covers techniques for rendering bacteriophages non-infectious or less virulent. It includes chemical, physical, enzymatic or genetic methods that inactivate or attenuate phage particles while preserving their structural integrity for use in research, diagnostics or phage-based therapies.
[C12N 2795/00062] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2795/00062 covers methods that employ genetic engineering to create or modify bacteriophages. This includes inserting, deleting or rearranging nucleic-acid sequences in phage genomes to confer new properties such as altered host specificity, delivery of therapeutic genes, or production of recombinant proteins.
[C12N 2795/00063] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2795/00063 pertains to the use of chemical agents to treat bacteriophages, encompassing methods that inactivate, modify, or otherwise affect viral particles through chemical means. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, and microorganisms or enzymes, specifically addressing chemical treatment processes applied to phages.
[C12N 2795/00064] by serial passage -- A C12N 2795/00064 classification refers to techniques that alter bacteriophages through serial passage, repeatedly culturing the phage on host bacteria to select for desired properties such as increased infectivity, host range or resistance to environmental stresses. This falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/00071] Demonstrated -- C12N 2795/00071 covers bacteriophage inventions where the biological activity or therapeutic effect has been experimentally demonstrated, encompassing methods, compositions, and uses that provide proof of efficacy against target microorganisms.
[C12N 2795/00088] for redistribution -- The subclass C12N 2795/00088 concerns inventions that provide bacteriophages in a form or system suitable for redistribution, such as carriers, formulations, packaging or delivery methods that enable the spread of phage particles for therapeutic, diagnostic or industrial applications. It addresses the means by which phages are prepared, stabilized and released to reach target environments or hosts.
[C12N 2795/10011] dsDNA Bacteriophages -- C12N 2795/10011 covers bacteriophages that contain double-stranded DNA as their genetic material. It is used to classify inventions relating to the composition, production, and use of dsDNA phages that infect bacteria. The code falls under the chemistry subclass for microorganisms or enzymes, specifically the category of bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/10021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/10021 concerns viruses viewed as independent biological entities, encompassing the discovery of new isolates, the creation of mutant strains and the analysis of their genomic sequences. It resides in the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy under microorganisms or enzymes, specifically within the bacteriophage group.
[C12N 2795/10022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- This subclass covers inventions that disclose newly discovered viral proteins or individual viral genes, as well as novel structural or functional characteristics of already known viral proteins or genes. It includes biotechnological methods, compositions, or applications that rely on these newly identified or re-characterised viral elements, typically for research, diagnostics, therapeutics or vaccine development.
[C12N 2795/10023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- Virus-like particles (VLPs) are self-assembling protein shells that resemble the outer structure of viruses yet contain no viral genome, making them non-infectious. They are employed as platforms for vaccines, diagnostic reagents and nanocarriers, exploiting their ability to present antigens in a native-like conformation.
[C12N 2795/10031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2795/10031 pertains to the use of viruses, especially bacteriophages, for purposes other than therapy or vaccination, such as disinfectants, biocontrol agents or decontamination processes. It is classified under chemistry - biochemistry - microorganisms or enzymes - bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/10032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The CPC entry C12N 2795/10032 pertains to the use of viruses, especially bacteriophages, as therapeutic agents that are not vaccines, for example as cytolytic agents that destroy target cells.
[C12N 2795/10033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- C12N 2795/10033 concerns the use of viral proteins originating from bacteriophages as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, including applications that trigger apoptosis or provide anti-inflammatory effects. It lies within the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes-bacteriophages hierarchy.
[C12N 2795/10034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- This CPC subclass covers inventions that employ whole viruses or virus-derived elements as immunizing agents, including live-attenuated, inactivated, virus-like particles, or recombinant viral proteins, for the purpose of vaccination. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and specifically bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/10041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/10041 covers inventions that employ viruses, viral particles, or viral components as vectors for delivering genetic material or therapeutic agents. It includes methods for constructing, modifying and using viral vectors in gene therapy, vaccination, and other biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/10042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- This subclass covers the use of whole viruses or viral particles as carriers for small organic molecules, where the virus functions as a delivery vehicle or encapsulation system, often involving engineered bacteriophages for targeted transport or release of the encapsulated compound.
[C12N 2795/10043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2795/10043 covers the use of viral genomes or parts of them as genetic vectors, focusing on engineered bacteriophages or related viruses that deliver DNA or RNA into host cells for research, therapeutic or biotechnological applications. It falls under the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes hierarchy.
[C12N 2795/10044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- A chimeric viral vector that incorporates genetic elements from different viruses is used as a production platform for a second, distinct viral vector, enabling efficient packaging, replication or delivery of the target vector in applications such as gene therapy or vaccine manufacturing. This approach leverages heterologous viral components to improve yield, stability or specificity of the produced vector.
[C12N 2795/10045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- C12N 2795/10045 covers inventions that provide engineered systems for directing viral vectors, such as bacteriophages, to specific cells, tissues or molecular targets. These special targeting mechanisms use modified capsid proteins, peptide ligands, antibodies or receptor-binding domains to improve delivery efficiency in gene-therapy, vaccine and biotechnology applications.
[C12N 2795/10051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- C12N 2795/10051 covers processes for producing or purifying viral material, specifically bacteriophage particles or their components. It includes methods such as culturing host bacteria, lysing cells, separating viruses by centrifugation, filtration, precipitation, chromatography and other techniques used to isolate and concentrate viral nucleic acids or proteins.
[C12N 2795/10052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/10052, within the bacteriophage group of the microbiology-biochemistry hierarchy, covers technologies that use complementing host cells and specialized packaging systems to produce complete viruses or viral particles, often for research, therapy, or vaccine development.
[C12N 2795/10061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The C12N 2795/10061 entry covers techniques for rendering bacteriophages non-infectious or less virulent, including chemical, physical, or genetic approaches that inactivate or attenuate the viruses. It addresses methods used to control phage activity in research, therapy, and industrial settings.
[C12N 2795/10062] by genetic engineering -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/10062 pertains to bacteriophages that have been modified through genetic-engineering techniques. It includes the creation of recombinant phage particles, insertion of foreign DNA, alteration of host-range specificity and related methods for producing or using engineered viruses in research, diagnostics or therapy.
[C12N 2795/10063] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2795/10063 addresses processes in which bacteriophages are subjected to chemical agents to modify, inactivate, purify, stabilize or otherwise alter their properties, employing acids, bases, solvents, oxidising or reducing reagents, and similar chemicals.
[C12N 2795/10064] by serial passage -- The code C12N 2795/10064 covers methods that improve or alter bacteriophages by serial passage, i.e., repeatedly infecting host cells and re-isolating phage progeny to select variants with desired traits such as expanded host range, enhanced stability or reduced virulence. This approach is used to evolve phages for therapeutic, diagnostic or industrial applications.
[C12N 2795/10071] Demonstrated -- The subclass C12N 2795/10071 covers bacteriophages that have been experimentally demonstrated to exhibit a defined activity, such as confirmed infectivity, therapeutic effect, or a specific genetic function. It includes any phage for which laboratory data or in-use evidence substantiate the claimed property.
[C12N 2795/10088] for redistribution -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/10088 concerns inventions related to the redistribution of bacteriophages, covering methods, formulations, and systems for transferring or supplying phage preparations for therapeutic, diagnostic, or industrial applications, including packaging, logistics, and delivery mechanisms.
[C12N 2795/10111] Myoviridae -- C12N 2795/10111 designates the Myoviridae family, a group of bacteriophages characterized by a contractile tail used to inject DNA into bacterial hosts. The entry covers their structure, genetics, infection mechanisms and applications such as phage therapy, bacterial detection and molecular biology tools.
[C12N 2795/10121] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- The CPC entry C12N 2795/10121 pertains to viruses themselves, encompassing newly isolated virus strains, genetically altered mutants and the nucleic-acid sequences that define them. It is classified under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically bacteriophages, and covers patentable subject matter related to virus identity and composition.
[C12N 2795/10122] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- This CPC subclass covers inventions relating to newly identified viral proteins or individual genes, and to newly discovered structural or functional aspects of previously known viral proteins or genes, specifically in the context of bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/10123] Virus like particles [VLP] -- C12N 2795/10123 covers virus-like particles (VLPs) that mimic the structure of bacteriophages but lack viral genetic material, and are employed in vaccine development, nanocarriers and diagnostic tools. The classification relates to biochemical methods for assembling protein capsids that resemble phage morphology while being non-infectious.
[C12N 2795/10131] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N2795/10131 covers applications of bacteriophages or other viruses where the purpose is not therapeutic or vaccine related, such as using them as disinfectants, biocontrol agents, or in cleaning processes. The subclass falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/10132] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The C12N 2795/10132 classification pertains to the biomedical use of viruses, particularly bacteriophages, as therapeutic agents that are not vaccines. It covers applications where the virus acts as a cytolytic or oncolytic agent to destroy target cells such as cancer cells or pathogenic bacteria.
[C12N 2795/10133] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- C12N 2795/10133 covers the use of viral proteins derived from bacteriophages as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, such as compounds that induce apoptosis or provide anti-inflammatory effects, falling under the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms-or-enzymes-bacteriophages hierarchy.

It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → bacteriophages.

It includes designs where heterologous viral components are combined to facilitate production of gene-therapy or vaccine vectors.
[C12N 2795/10145] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- This subclass covers engineered systems that confer selective targeting of viral vectors to designated host cells or tissues. It includes surface modifications, ligand attachments, receptor-binding domains or genetic payloads designed to direct infection or gene transfer with improved specificity.
[C12N 2795/10151] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- The subclass C12N 2795/10151 covers procedures for producing or purifying viral material, specifically bacteriophages. It includes methods for culturing host microorganisms, inducing infection and lysis, and downstream steps such as precipitation, filtration, chromatography or centrifugation to isolate and concentrate phage particles for research or therapeutic use.
[C12N 2795/10152] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/10152 covers biotechnological techniques that use complementing host cells and specialized packaging systems to produce bacteriophages or viral particles. It includes methods for supplying missing functions in mutant cells and for assembling viral components in vitro or in vivo to generate functional viruses.
[C12N 2795/10161] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- C12N 2795/10161 covers processes for reducing the activity or virulence of bacteriophages, including chemical, physical, or genetic techniques that render the viruses non-infectious or less pathogenic. These methods are used to create safe phage preparations for research, diagnostics, or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2795/10162] by genetic engineering -- The code C12N 2795/10162 covers the use of recombinant-DNA or related genetic-engineering techniques to create, modify or alter bacteriophages, including insertion, deletion or substitution of genetic material to confer new traits such as altered host range, improved stability or novel functions.
[C12N 2795/10163] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2795/10163 covers methods that apply chemical treatment to bacteriophages, including the use of reagents to inactivate, modify, or otherwise manipulate phage particles. [C12N 2795/10164] by serial passage -- C12N 2795/10164 covers methods that employ serial passage to adapt or evolve bacteriophages. It includes techniques where phages are repeatedly propagated through host cells to select for traits such as altered host range, increased virulence, or resistance to environmental factors, facilitating their use in biotechnological and therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2795/10171] Demonstrated -- C12N 2795/10171 covers the demonstration of bacteriophage characteristics, including methods that prove a phage's infectivity, host range, or therapeutic effect. It encompasses experimental procedures that verify the activity or utility of a phage in a biological or industrial context. This subclass falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/10188] for redistribution -- C12N 2795/10188 pertains to inventions that involve the redistribution of bacteriophages, such as formulations, delivery systems or processes that enable the spread or re-application of phage particles for therapeutic, diagnostic or biocontrol purposes.
[C12N 2795/10211] Podoviridae -- Podoviridae is a family of bacteriophages distinguished by short, non-contractile, icosahedral heads and rigid tails that lack a sheath. Members infect a range of bacterial hosts and are employed as tools in genetics and phage therapy research.
[C12N 2795/10221] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- The CPC entry C12N 2795/10221 covers viruses in their own right, encompassing newly isolated virus strains, mutant forms and the corresponding genomic DNA or RNA sequences, specifically within the bacteriophage sub-category of microbiology.

[C12N 2795/12041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- C12N 2795/12041 falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically bacteriophages. It covers inventions that employ whole viruses, viral particles, or viral components as delivery vectors for genetic material, therapeutic agents, or other functional payloads in biotechnology, medicine, or industrial processes.
[C12N 2795/12042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- This subclass pertains to the use of whole viruses or virus-like particles as carriers that encapsulate small organic molecules, such as drugs or diagnostic agents, for delivery to a target. It includes methods wherein the viral capsid protects the encapsulated compound during transport.
[C12N 2795/12043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/12043 covers the use of whole viral genomes or fragments of viruses as vectors for delivering genetic material into host cells, a technique employed in gene-therapy, vaccine production and other genetic-engineering applications. It includes constructs derived from bacteriophages and other viruses that are engineered to transport nucleic acids for functional expression in target organisms.
[C12N 2795/12044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- A chimeric viral vector that incorporates genetic elements from different viruses and is employed to generate a separate viral vector, often for gene-therapy, vaccine or cloning applications. The classification addresses designs where heterologous viral components are combined to improve production, packaging or host-range characteristics of the final vector.
[C12N 2795/12045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- This class covers engineered systems that guide viral vectors to specific cells or tissues, often using bacteriophage-derived targeting moieties or selective ligands, enabling precise gene delivery or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2795/12051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- The C12N 2795/12051 class covers patented techniques for producing or purifying viral material, primarily bacteriophages. It includes processes such as culturing host microorganisms, inducing viral replication, extracting the virus, and downstream steps like filtration, precipitation, chromatography, and formulation to obtain a clean viral preparation.
[C12N 2795/12052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The classification covers methods and biological tools that use genetically engineered host cells together with specialized packaging systems to assemble and release bacteriophage particles or virus-like particles. It includes complementing cell lines that provide missing functions for phage replication and the vector components that package viral genomes for production, enabling the manufacture of functional viruses or virus-derived particles for research or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2795/12061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/12061 covers techniques for rendering bacteriophages inactive or less virulent, including chemical, physical, or genetic approaches that attenuate their ability to infect bacteria. These methods are used in research, safety testing, and the development of phage-based products where controlled activity is required.
[C12N 2795/12062] by genetic engineering -- This CPC subclass covers inventions that involve the use of genetic engineering techniques to modify bacteriophages. It includes methods for altering phage genomes, constructing recombinant phages and related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/12063] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2795/12063 covers methods that use chemical agents to modify, inactivate, or otherwise treat bacteriophages. It includes processes such as chemical disinfection, stabilization, or functional alteration of phage particles, facilitating their use in research, therapy, or industrial applications. This subclass falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/12064] by serial passage -- The CPC entry C12N 2795/12064 covers bacteriophage methods that use serial passage, meaning the phage is repeatedly propagated through successive host cultures to select or enhance specific properties such as host range, infectivity, stability or therapeutic efficacy. It encompasses techniques for adapting phages by consecutive infection cycles, often to improve performance for research or commercial applications.
[C12N 2795/12071] Demonstrated -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/12071 covers bacteriophage-related inventions in which the phage, its particles or derived components have been experimentally demonstrated to achieve a specific functional effect, such as treating bacterial infections, delivering genetic material, or acting as a diagnostic or biocontrol agent. It includes compositions, methods and uses where the efficacy of the phage-based solution has been proven.
[C12N 2795/12088] for redistribution -- C12N 2795/12088 (for redistribution) classifies inventions dealing with the redistribution of bacteriophages, such as formulations, delivery vehicles, or processes that disperse phage particles for therapeutic, diagnostic or industrial use. It falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Bacteriophages in the CPC system.
[C12N 2795/14011] ssDNA Bacteriophages -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/14011 covers single-stranded DNA bacteriophages, viruses that use a linear or circular ssDNA genome to infect bacterial hosts. It includes members of the Inoviridae and related families, their replication mechanisms, and biotechnological uses such as phage display. The class is nested under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/14021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- This subclass addresses inventions concerning bacteriophages as independent entities, such as newly isolated strains, genetically engineered mutants, and their DNA or RNA sequences, which may be used in therapeutic, diagnostic, or biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/14022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/14022 covers inventions that disclose novel viral proteins or individual genes from bacteriophages, as well as new structural or functional characteristics of already known phage proteins or genes. It is used for claims relating to engineered or naturally occurring proteins and genetic elements that confer specific activities such as host specificity or enzymatic function. The class falls under chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes-bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/14023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- C12N 2795/14023 designates virus-like particles (VLPs), non-infectious assemblies that mimic the structure of viral capsids, frequently employed in biotechnology for vaccine development, gene delivery or diagnostic tools. The code falls under the chemistry hierarchy for biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically covering bacteriophage-derived VLPs and related manufacturing or application methods.
[C12N 2795/14031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2795/14031 pertains to the use of bacteriophages or related viruses for purposes other than therapy or vaccination, such as disinfectants, sterilisation agents, or biocontrol applications. It includes methods where the virus acts as a biocidal or decontamination tool in medical, industrial, or environmental settings.
[C12N 2795/14032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- C12N 2795/14032 covers the use of viruses as therapeutic agents when the purpose is not vaccination, for example employing bacteriophages to lyse pathogenic bacteria or using oncolytic viruses to destroy cancer cells. The classification includes methods, preparations and compositions that exploit the natural cytolytic activity of viruses for treatment.
[C12N 2795/14033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- C12N 2795/14033 covers inventions that employ viral proteins as therapeutic agents not intended as vaccines, such as proteins that trigger cell death or reduce inflammation. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/14034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- C12N 2795/14034 pertains to the pharmaceutical application of viruses or viral parts as vaccines, encompassing live-attenuated or inactivated viruses, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy for biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/14041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- C12N 2795/14041 covers inventions that employ whole viruses, viral particles or components derived from viruses as carriers to introduce genetic material, therapeutic agents or other functional payloads into target cells or organisms. The classification is used for methods and compositions where the virus serves as a vector for delivery, including engineered bacteriophages and other viral vectors in biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/14042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- The C12N 2795/14042 entry covers inventions that employ whole viruses or viral particles as carriers, for example by encapsulating small organic molecules within them. It pertains to using such biological vectors to transport active agents, typically in contexts such as drug delivery, imaging or diagnostics.
[C12N 2795/14043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- The code designates technologies that employ whole viral genomes or portions of them as carriers for introducing foreign DNA into target cells. It includes engineered bacteriophages, viral vectors and recombinant viral particles used in gene-therapy, vaccine development and molecular-biology applications.
[C12N 2795/14044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- The code C12N 2795/14044 covers chimeric viral vectors that incorporate genetic or structural components from different viruses to serve as a platform for generating a second, distinct viral vector. Such constructs are engineered to exploit heterologous viral elements for efficient production, packaging, or delivery of the target vector, often used in gene-therapy or vaccine development.
[C12N 2795/14045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- C12N 2795/14045 covers inventions that provide engineered targeting mechanisms for viral vectors, such as bacteriophage-derived capsids or surface proteins, to direct the vectors to specific host cells or tissues. The focus is on molecular modifications that improve selectivity, reduce off-target effects, and enable precise delivery of genetic material for therapy or research.
[C12N 2795/14051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/14051 covers methods for producing and purifying viral material, particularly bacteriophages. It includes techniques for culturing host bacteria, inducing phage replication, harvesting lysates and separating intact virions using processes such as centrifugation, filtration, precipitation, chromatography or affinity capture.
[C12N 2795/14052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- C12N2795/14052 covers methods that employ complementing cell lines together with packaging systems to produce bacteriophages or other viral particles. The classification applies to biotechnological processes that supply missing functions in host cells and assemble virus-like particles for research or therapeutic use.
[C12N 2795/14061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- C12N 2795/14061 covers procedures for reducing the infectivity or virulence of bacteriophages, including chemical, physical, or genetic treatments that inactivate or attenuate the virus for use in research, therapy, or manufacturing.
[C12N 2795/14062] by genetic engineering -- The code C12N 2795/14062 pertains to genetically engineered bacteriophages, covering methods for modifying phage genomes and the resulting recombinant phage particles. It includes techniques for inserting, deleting or altering genetic material to alter host range, increase stability, or confer new functionalities for therapeutic, diagnostic or biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/14063] by chemical treatment -- The code C12N 2795/14063 classifies chemical treatments applied to bacteriophages, including agents that inactivate, modify, or preserve viral particles. It covers processes such as disinfecting, stabilising, or chemically altering phages for use in therapeutic, diagnostic, or biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/14064] by serial passage -- The CPC entry C12N 2795/14064 covers methods that employ serial passage to isolate, adapt or modify bacteriophages, typically by repeatedly infecting host bacteria in order to select for desired traits such as increased infectivity, host range changes, or resistance to environmental stresses. This technique is used in research and development of phage-based therapies and biocontrol agents.
[C12N 2795/14071] Demonstrated -- C12N2795/14071 covers methods for demonstrating bacteriophage activity, including assays and techniques that confirm the presence, infectivity, or biological effect of a phage on its host bacteria. It encompasses procedures for visualising, measuring or otherwise proving that a phage is functional for diagnostic, therapeutic or research applications.
[C12N 2795/14088] for redistribution -- It covers inventions relating to the redistribution of bacteriophages, such as formulations, carriers, or processes that enable the spread or re-application of phages for therapeutic or biocontrol purposes. The entry falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → bacteriophages in the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[C12N 2795/14111] Inoviridae -- This CPC subclass C12N 2795/14111 designates inventions relating to the family Inoviridae, filamentous bacteriophages that infect bacteria and are used as vectors for DNA delivery, genetic engineering, and nanomaterial production.
[C12N 2795/14121] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- The class C12N 2795/14121 pertains to viruses themselves, covering novel isolates, mutant forms and their genomic DNA or RNA sequences. It includes any biotechnological, therapeutic or diagnostic application of whole viruses, including bacteriophages, irrespective of the source or method of isolation.
[C12N 2795/14122] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- C12N 2795/14122 covers inventions relating to newly identified viral proteins or individual genes and to novel structural or functional aspects of already known viral proteins or genes. The class is used for claims concerning engineered or discovered phage-derived biomolecules that have distinct properties or activities. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy, specifically biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and the bacteriophage subgroup.
[C12N 2795/14123] Virus like particles [VLP] -- C12N 2795/14123 covers virus-like particles, which are non-infectious assemblies that mimic the shape and surface of viruses but contain no viral genome. VLPs are exploited in vaccine development, drug delivery and nanomaterial applications, and are classified under microorganisms or enzymes, specifically within the bacteriophage subgroup of the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy.
[C12N 2795/14131] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2795/14131 designates the use of viruses, particularly bacteriophages, for purposes other than therapy or vaccination, such as disinfectants, biocontrol agents, or other industrial applications. It belongs to the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy under microorganisms or enzymes and specifically bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/14132] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- C12N2795/14132 classifies inventions that use viruses, especially bacteriophages, as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, for example as cytolytic agents that kill target cells. It resides under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes and bacteriophages in the CPC hierarchy. Patents in this subclass protect medical or biotechnological applications of viral killing activity.
[C12N 2795/14133] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- C12N 2795/14133 covers the use of viral proteins as therapeutic agents that are not vaccines, including applications that induce apoptosis or provide anti-inflammatory effects. It falls under the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes hierarchy, specifically relating to bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/14134] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- C12N 2795/14134 covers the use of whole viruses or viral elements as vaccines, including live-attenuated or inactivated viruses, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins. It includes methods, compositions and engineered viral vectors used in gene therapy, vaccine production, diagnostics and other biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/14142] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- C12N 2795/14142 refers to inventions that employ viruses or viral particles, including bacteriophages, as carriers for transporting small organic molecules such as drugs, diagnostics or other active agents. The classification covers methods of encapsulating the molecules within the viral structure and using the resulting viral-based delivery system for targeted application.

It resides within the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/14144] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- The CPC code C12N 2795/14144 covers engineered chimeric viral vectors that incorporate genetic elements from different viruses to serve as platforms for producing a second viral vector. It addresses engineered host cells that supply missing viral functions and the associated vector components required for assembling functional viral particles. This enables controlled production of recombinant viruses for research or therapeutic purposes.

These methods are used in research, pharmaceutical production and safety measures for phage-based applications.
[C12N 2795/14162] by genetic engineering -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/14162 covers bacteriophages that have been altered through genetic engineering, including recombinant phage construction, gene insertion, deletion or modification to change host range, lytic activity, or other functional traits. It applies to biotechnological methods that create engineered phage particles for research, therapeutic, or industrial uses.
[C12N 2795/14163] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2795/14163 relates to chemical procedures applied to bacteriophages, including inactivation, modification or purification using reagents, solvents or catalysts. It is used for patents describing how chemical agents alter phage structure, stability or activity for research, diagnostic or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2795/14164] by serial passage -- C12N 2795/14164 belongs to the CPC subclass dealing with bacteriophages and covers methods that obtain or modify phages by serial passage, i.e., repeated infection cycles to select for desired traits. It is used to classify inventions related to phage evolution, host range alteration, or increased efficacy.
[C12N 2795/14171] Demonstrated -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/14171 falls under the chemistry hierarchy for biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes, specifically bacteriophages. It covers inventions where a bacteriophage has been experimentally demonstrated to exhibit a particular activity such as therapeutic, prophylactic or diagnostic effect. The entry is used to classify patents that provide proof of concept or performance data for phage-based applications.
[C12N 2795/14188] for redistribution -- This subclass covers inventions that deal with the redistribution of bacteriophages, including methods and devices for delivering, dispersing, or re-introducing phage particles into a target environment. It encompasses formulations, carriers, and application techniques aimed at spreading therapeutic or biocontrol phages.
[C12N 2795/14211] Microviridae -- Microviridae are a family of small, single-stranded DNA bacteriophages that infect bacterial hosts, primarily Enterobacteria. They possess icosahedral capsids with a circular genome and are studied for their role in phage therapy, microbial ecology, and as tools in molecular genetics.
[C12N 2795/14221] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/14221 covers viruses themselves, including newly isolated strains, genetically altered mutants and their genomic sequences. It is used to classify inventions that involve virus particles rather than virus-derived products, falling under the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → bacteriophages. This entry helps differentiate claims directed at whole viruses from those concerning viral proteins or nucleic-acid fragments.
[C12N 2795/14222] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/14222 covers inventions that disclose novel viral proteins or individual genes, as well as new structural or functional characteristics of already known viral proteins or genes. It is used for patent applications involving engineered or newly discovered components of bacteriophages and related viruses, focusing on their biochemical properties and potential biotechnological uses.

VLPs are engineered for applications such as vaccines, gene-delivery systems, and diagnostic tools, and fall within the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy under microorganisms or enzymes and bacteriophages.

[C12N 2795/14234] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/14234 covers the use of whole viruses or viral components as vaccines, including live-attenuated or inactivated viruses, virus-like particles and isolated viral proteins for immunisation.

It includes formulations that employ bacteriophage-derived particles or other viral vectors to transport drugs, diagnostics or other low-molecular-weight agents to specific sites.

[C12N 2795/14264] by serial passage -- C12N 2795/14264 refers to methods that modify bacteriophages by repeatedly transferring them through successive host infections - a serial passage. The technique selects for phage variants with altered characteristics such as broader host range, increased virulence or resistance to bacterial defenses, and is commonly used in phage-therapy development and strain optimisation.

[C12N 2795/16011] ssRNA Bacteriophages negative-sense -- This CPC subclass covers bacteriophages that possess a single-stranded RNA genome of negative polarity, including their nucleic-acid sequences, vectors and related methods for production, manipulation or use.

The classification is used for inventions that directly involve virus particles or their genomes, rather than merely their application or the methods of producing them.
[C12N 2795/16022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The CPC code C12N 2795/16022, under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Bacteriophages, covers inventions that disclose new viral proteins or individual genes, or reveal novel structural or functional features of already known viral proteins or genes. It is used to classify biotechnological claims focused on previously uncharacterised viral biomolecules.
[C12N 2795/16023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/16023 addresses virus-like particles, which are non-infectious structures that imitate the morphology of viruses but contain no genetic material and are employed in vaccines, diagnostics and nanotechnology. It falls under the bacteriophage category within the biochemistry of microorganisms and enzymes, itself a subfield of chemistry. [C12N 2795/16043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N 2795/16043 pertains to the use of whole viral genomes or fragments as vectors for transferring genetic material into host cells. [C12N 2795/16044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- C12N 2795/16044 covers chimeric viral vectors that contain genetic or structural elements from a different virus and are employed to generate another viral vector, typically for gene-therapy or vaccine applications. The classification includes the design, construction and production methods for such recombinant viral systems.
[C12N 2795/16045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- C12N 2795/16045 covers engineered systems that direct viral vectors to specific cells or tissues, often by attaching targeting ligands, antibodies or receptor-binding domains to the vector's surface. The technology improves the precision of gene-delivery or therapeutic applications, reducing off-target effects and enhancing efficacy in fields such as gene therapy, vaccine development and molecular diagnostics.
[C12N 2795/16051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- This CPC subclass covers techniques for producing or purifying viral material, specifically focusing on bacteriophages. It includes processes such as cultivation, isolation, concentration and downstream purification methods used to obtain phage particles for research or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2795/16052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- These patents cover techniques that use complementing cell lines and specialized packaging systems to generate viruses or virus-like particles, enabling production of recombinant or therapeutic viral vectors. This entry falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/16062] by genetic engineering -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/16062 covers bacteriophages that have been altered by genetic-engineering methods, including recombinant construction, host-range modification, and insertion of functional genes for therapeutic, diagnostic or biotechnological applications. It pertains to engineered phages used as tools or products rather than naturally occurring viruses.
[C12N 2795/16063] by chemical treatment -- C12N 2795/16063 covers methods that treat bacteriophages with chemicals, such as agents for inactivation, stabilization, or modification. The classification is used for inventions that employ chemical means to alter or process phage particles for therapeutic, diagnostic, or biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/16064] by serial passage -- C12N 2795/16064 covers the use of serial passage to obtain or improve bacteriophages. The method involves repeatedly infecting host microorganisms with a phage population, selecting for desired properties such as broader host range, increased stability or altered genetics. It is applied in biotechnology, therapy and research on phage-bacteria interactions.
[C12N 2795/16071] Demonstrated -- C12N 2795/16071 pertains to bacteriophage technologies in which the phage's activity, specificity or therapeutic effect has been experimentally demonstrated. The classification is applied to patents that provide data confirming the phage's ability to infect or lyse a particular bacterial strain, or to achieve a defined biomedical or biotechnological outcome.
[C12N 2795/16088] for redistribution
[C12N 2795/18011] ssRNA Bacteriophages positive-sense -- This subclass covers positive-sense single-stranded RNA bacteriophages, viruses that infect bacteria and carry a genome that can serve directly as mRNA for protein synthesis. It includes classification of their structure, replication mechanisms and related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/18021] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- C12N 2795/18021 covers inventions directed to viruses themselves, including newly isolated strains, genetically altered mutants and their nucleic-acid sequences. It applies to biotechnological claims that define a virus as the subject of protection rather than a composition or method using the virus. This entry falls under the microbiology sub-section of the CPC for chemicals and biochemistry.
[C12N 2795/18022] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- C12N 2795/18022 covers inventions involving novel viral proteins or isolated genes, as well as newly identified structural or functional properties of already known viral proteins or genes, particularly in the context of bacteriophages. This subclass is used for patent claims related to genetic engineering, protein engineering, and functional analysis of viral components.
[C12N 2795/18023] Virus like particles [VLP] -- C12N 2795/18023 classifies virus-like particles, which are non-infectious protein assemblies that mimic the morphology of viruses but lack genetic material. These VLPs are used in vaccine development, diagnostic assays and research on viral assembly.
[C12N 2795/18031] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- The subclass C12N 2795/18031 concerns non-therapeutic and non-vaccine applications of viruses, such as their use as disinfectants, biocides, or in other cleaning and sterilisation processes. It is situated under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → bacteriophages, and covers any inventive use of viral particles for purposes like surface decontamination, water treatment or agricultural biocontrol.
[C12N 2795/18032] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- The subclass C12N 2795/18032 covers applications of bacteriophages as therapeutic agents that are not vaccines, such as using the virus to directly lyse target cells or organisms for treatment purposes, including oncolytic or antibacterial phage therapy. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/18033] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- C12N 2795/18033 covers the therapeutic use of viral proteins derived from bacteriophages or other viruses, excluding vaccine applications. It includes methods where these proteins act as drugs, for example to trigger apoptosis in target cells or to provide anti-inflammatory effects. The subclass sits under the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy for microorganisms or enzymes, specifically bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/18034] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- The subclass C12N 2795/18034 defines inventions that use whole viruses, virus-like particles, or isolated viral proteins as immunising agents, encompassing live-attenuated, inactivated, subunit and VLP vaccines. It falls under the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes-bacteriophages branch of the CPC.
[C12N 2795/18041] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- This CPC subclass falls under chemistry biochemistry microorganisms or enzymes bacteriophages covering inventions that employ viruses viral particles or components thereof as delivery vehicles for genetic material therapeutic agents or other payloads. It includes methods of constructing modifying and using viral vectors for gene therapy vaccination or biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/18042] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- This CPC subclass deals with using whole viruses or virus-derived particles as carriers to encapsulate and transport small organic molecules, such as drugs, diagnostics or chemical reagents. It includes engineered bacteriophages, viral capsids and other viral vectors that protect the payload and deliver it to target sites, covering methods of preparation, loading and functionalisation.
[C12N 2795/18043] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- C12N2795/18043 covers the use of whole viral genomes or fragments as vectors for delivering genetic material into host cells. It includes designs where viral DNA or RNA is engineered to carry therapeutic, industrial or research genes and the methods for constructing and applying such vectors.
[C12N 2795/18044] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- A chimeric viral vector that incorporates genetic elements from different viruses to serve as a production platform for a second, distinct viral vector. The design exploits heterologous viral components to enhance replication or assembly efficiency, enabling manufacturing of therapeutic or research viruses. This technology is classified under C12N 2795/18044 within the bacteriophage subgroup of microbial biochemistry.
[C12N 2795/18045] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- This subclass covers engineered bacteriophage-derived viral vectors that incorporate specific targeting mechanisms-such as receptor-binding ligands, antibody fragments or modified capsid proteins-to direct infection toward particular cells or tissues. It includes methods and compositions that confer selective entry, enhancing the delivery of genetic material for research, diagnostics or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2795/18051] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- C12N 2795/18051 pertains to techniques for producing or purifying viral material, focusing on bacteriophages. The code covers processes such as cultivation, extraction, concentration, and downstream processing used to obtain phage particles in a form suitable for research, therapeutic, or industrial applications. It falls under the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes hierarchy in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[C12N 2795/18052] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- The C12N 2795/18052 subclass covers methods that use complementing host cells together with specialized packaging systems to generate bacteriophages or virus-like particles. It includes techniques for supplying missing functions in the cell, assembling viral genomes, and producing complete virions or viral capsids for research or commercial applications.
[C12N 2795/18061] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- C12N 2795/18061 covers techniques for rendering bacteriophages non-infectious or less virulent, including chemical, physical, enzymatic or genetic approaches that inactivate or attenuate phage particles for therapeutic, industrial or research applications. It falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/18062] by genetic engineering -- C12N 2795/18062 covers bacteriophages that have been modified by genetic engineering techniques, including recombinant phages and those engineered to express foreign genes or altered host range. It pertains to biotechnological methods for altering phage genomes for therapeutic, diagnostic, or industrial applications.
[C12N 2795/18063] by chemical treatment -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/18063 covers methods that chemically treat bacteriophages, including the use of reagents to inactivate, modify, or otherwise manipulate phage particles. It pertains to processes such as enzymatic digestion, oxidation, reduction, or other chemical reactions applied to phages for research, therapeutic, or industrial purposes.
[C12N 2795/18064] by serial passage -- C12N 2795/18064 covers the use of serial passage to evolve bacteriophages, i.e., repeatedly infecting host bacteria with phage progeny to select for altered properties such as expanded host range, increased virulence, or resistance to environmental stresses.
[C12N 2795/18071] Demonstrated -- C12N 2795/18071 covers patents that disclose bacteriophages whose functional properties have been experimentally demonstrated, such as confirmed infectivity, host specificity, or therapeutic efficacy. The subclass groups inventions where the phage's activity is proven rather than merely described.
[C12N 2795/18088] for redistribution -- This CPC subclass covers inventions relating to the redistribution of bacteriophages, such as methods for transferring, spreading, or delivering phage particles after initial production. It includes techniques for formulation, packaging, and release of phage preparations intended for therapeutic, diagnostic, or biocontrol applications.
[C12N 2795/18111] Leviviridae -- C12N 2795/18111 designates the family Leviviridae, a group of small, non-enveloped, single-stranded RNA bacteriophages that infect Gram-negative bacteria such as Escherichia coli. The classification covers their structure, genome organization, replication mechanisms and related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2795/18121] Viruses as such, e.g. new isolates, mutants or their genomic sequences -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/18121 concerns viruses themselves, such as newly isolated strains, mutant forms, and their genomic sequences, when they are the focus of a patent claim. It is situated within chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → bacteriophages, and addresses the protection of biological material and related genetic information.
[C12N 2795/18122] New viral proteins or individual genes, new structural or functional aspects of known viral proteins or genes -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/18122 covers inventions relating to novel viral proteins or individual genes from bacteriophages, as well as newly discovered structural or functional features of previously known phage proteins or genes. It is used to classify biotechnological methods and products that exploit these new viral elements.
[C12N 2795/18123] Virus like particles [VLP] -- Virus-like particles (VLPs) are self-assembled protein structures that imitate the size, shape and surface features of authentic viruses while containing no viral genome, making them non-infectious. They are employed as vaccine antigens, carriers for drug or gene delivery, and tools for studying virus assembly and immune responses.
[C12N 2795/18131] Uses of virus other than therapeutic or vaccine, e.g. disinfectant -- C12N 2795/18131 covers applications of bacteriophages or other viruses that are not intended for treatment or vaccination, such as their use as disinfectants, biocontrol agents, or in industrial processes to reduce microbial contamination. It addresses non-therapeutic, non-prophylactic functions of viral particles.
[C12N 2795/18132] Use of virus as therapeutic agent, other than vaccine, e.g. as cytolytic agent -- This CPC subclass (C12N 2795/18132) concerns the therapeutic use of viruses, excluding vaccine purposes, where the virus acts as a cytolytic or otherwise disease-modifying agent; it includes applications such as oncolytic virotherapy and other treatments that employ bacteriophages or related viruses to directly destroy or alter target cells.
[C12N 2795/18133] Use of viral protein as therapeutic agent other than vaccine, e.g. apoptosis inducing or anti-inflammatory -- The CPC entry C12N 2795/18133 covers the use of viral proteins as therapeutic agents other than vaccines, including applications that trigger apoptosis or provide anti-inflammatory effects. It lies within the hierarchy CHEMISTRY → BIOCHEMISTRY → MICROORGANISMS OR ENZYMES → BACTERIOPHAGES.
[C12N 2795/18134] Use of virus or viral component as vaccine, e.g. live-attenuated or inactivated virus, VLP, viral protein -- The CPC subclass C12N2795/18134 falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, bacteriophages and relates to the use of viruses or viral components as vaccines, including live-attenuated, inactivated, virus-like particles or isolated viral proteins.
[C12N 2795/18141] Use of virus, viral particle or viral elements as a vector -- This CPC subclass (C12N 2795/18141) covers the use of viruses, viral particles or viral elements as vectors for delivering genetic material, therapeutic agents or for biotechnological purposes such as gene therapy, vaccine production and protein expression. It is situated under microbiology and bacteriophages within the broader chemistry and biochemistry categories.
[C12N 2795/18142] virus or viral particle as vehicle, e.g. encapsulating small organic molecule -- C12N 2795/18142 covers the use of a virus or viral particle, such as a bacteriophage, as a delivery vehicle that encapsulates small organic molecules. It lies within the hierarchy chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → bacteriophages.
[C12N 2795/18143] viral genome or elements thereof as genetic vector -- This subclass covers inventions where the nucleic acid of a bacteriophage or derived viral genome is employed as a vector to deliver genetic material into target cells. It includes engineered phage genomes, fragments, or synthetic viral elements used for gene-transfer, cloning, or expression in biotechnology and molecular biology.
[C12N 2795/18144] Chimeric viral vector comprising heterologous viral elements for production of another viral vector -- This CPC subclass concerns chimeric viral vectors that incorporate genetic elements from different viruses to enable the production of a separate viral vector. It covers designs where heterologous viral components are combined to improve replication, packaging, or host range for manufacturing purposes, particularly in the context of bacteriophage-related biotechnology.
[C12N 2795/18145] Special targeting system for viral vectors -- This classification covers technologies that modify viral vectors to deliver genetic material to specific cells, using engineered targeting ligands or receptor-binding domains to achieve selective infection. It includes design of tropism-modified bacteriophage-derived vectors and methods for improving specificity in gene-therapy or molecular-biology applications.
[C12N 2795/18151] Methods of production or purification of viral material -- C12N 2795/18151 relates to processes for producing or purifying viral material, particularly bacteriophages, within the field of biochemical methods for microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2795/18152] relating to complementing cells and packaging systems for producing virus or viral particles -- A subcategory of bacteriophage biotechnology that focuses on the use of complementing host cells and specialized packaging systems to produce whole viruses or virus-like particles. It covers methods and vectors that supply missing functions for assembly, maturation or release of the viral progeny.
[C12N 2795/18161] Methods of inactivation or attenuation -- The CPC subclass C12N 2795/18161 covers processes for rendering bacteriophages non-infectious or less virulent, including chemical, physical, enzymatic or genetic techniques used to inactivate or attenuate phage particles for safety or research purposes. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy for microorganisms or enzymes, specifically focusing on methods that reduce phage activity.
[C12N 2795/18162] by genetic engineering
[C12N 2795/18163] by chemical treatment -- The subclass C12N 2795/18163 covers methods in which bacteriophages are subjected to chemical treatment, including the use of solvents, acids, bases, oxidising or reducing agents, and other reagents to modify, inactivate, purify or otherwise alter phage particles for research or commercial purposes. It addresses processes that rely on chemical reactions rather than physical or biological techniques to affect phage structure or activity.
[C12N 2795/18164] by serial passage -- The code C12N 2795/18164 covers methods for obtaining or improving bacteriophages by serial passage, i.e., repeated cycles of infection, replication and isolation in a host organism to select for desired traits such as altered host range, increased virulence, stability or resistance to environmental factors. It includes the use of successive passages to evolve phage characteristics without direct genetic engineering.
[C12N 2795/18171] Demonstrated -- The code C12N 2795/18171 classifies inventions in which a bacteriophage's presence, activity, or effect has been experimentally demonstrated, such as proven therapeutic, diagnostic, or biocontrol efficacy. It is used for disclosures that provide concrete data confirming the phage's functional properties against target microorganisms.
[C12N 2795/18188] for redistribution -- C12N 2795/18188 is a Cooperative Patent Classification sub-group under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → bacteriophages that covers inventions relating to the redistribution of bacteriophages, such as methods, formulations, or delivery systems used to spread the phages after production or isolation.
[C12N 2796/00] Viruses not covered by groups -- C12N 2796/00 classifies inventions that involve viruses which are not assigned to any of the defined virus groups in the CPC scheme. It covers novel, undefined or otherwise ungrouped viral entities, including their isolation, characterization, genetic manipulation and related biotechnological applications, without reference to a specific taxonomic family.
[C12N 2799/00] Uses of viruses -- C12N 2799/00 covers the use of viruses as tools in biotechnology and medicine, including viral vectors for gene therapy, vaccine production, diagnostic assays, and biocontrol. It encompasses methods where whole viruses, virus-like particles, or viral components are employed to achieve a functional purpose.
[C12N 2799/02] as vector -- The code C12N2799/02 designates inventions that employ viruses as vectors, for example to introduce genetic material into cells, produce recombinant proteins, or develop viral vaccines. It covers methods and compositions where the virus functions primarily as a delivery vehicle rather than as a therapeutic agent itself.
[C12N 2799/021] for the expression of a heterologous nucleic acid
[C12N 2799/022] where the vector is derived from an adenovirus -- C12N 2799/022 covers biotechnological applications in which a virus vector is derived from an adenovirus. It includes methods and uses where the adenovirus-based vector serves as a carrier for genetic material, vaccine development, gene therapy or other molecular biology techniques involving engineered adenoviral particles.
[C12N 2799/023] where the vector is derived from a poxvirus -- The CPC subclass C12N 2799/023 covers applications of viruses in which the vector is derived from a poxvirus, including poxvirus-based delivery systems for gene therapy, vaccine development, recombinant protein production and related biotechnological methods.
[C12N 2799/025] where the vector is derived from a parvovirus -- C12N 2799/025 covers biotechnological applications in which the genetic vector employed is sourced from a parvovirus. It falls under uses of viruses in microbiology and biochemistry, describing methods that exploit parvovirus-derived vectors for gene delivery, vaccine development or related therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2799/026] where the vector is derived from a baculovirus -- The C12N 2799/026 subclass covers biotechnological inventions in which a recombinant vector is based on a baculovirus. It includes methods and compositions that employ baculovirus-derived vectors for delivering genetic material, expressing heterologous proteins, producing vaccines or other therapeutic agents, and related processes.
[C12N 2799/027] where the vector is derived from a retrovirus -- This classification covers biotechnological applications in which a viral vector derived from a retrovirus is used. It includes methods for producing, modifying, or employing retrovirus-derived vectors for gene transfer, therapy, or molecular biology. The code C12N2799/027 falls under uses of viruses in biotechnology.
[C12N 2799/028] where the vector is derived from a herpesvirus -- C12N 2799/028 covers biotechnological applications that employ vectors whose genetic material is derived from a herpesvirus. Such vectors are used to introduce nucleic acids into host cells for purposes like gene therapy, vaccine production, or functional studies. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry and microorganisms or enzymes, specifically uses of viruses.
[C12N 2799/04] in vivo -- C12N 2799/04 categorises inventions that involve the use of viruses within a living organism, such as therapeutic, diagnostic or vaccine applications where the virus functions in vivo. It falls under the chemistry sub-section dealing with biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes, specifically the uses of viruses.
[C12N 2799/06] in vitro -- This subclass deals with in vitro uses of viruses, encompassing laboratory techniques where viruses are employed for diagnostic, screening, or research purposes outside a living organism, including assays, vaccine production, and gene delivery systems.
[C12N 2800/00] Nucleic acids vectors -- C12N 2800/00 covers nucleic-acid vectors, including plasmids, viral particles, cosmids and other recombinant constructs designed to deliver DNA or RNA into host cells for genetic manipulation, expression, or cloning. The category encompasses the structure, preparation, and functional use of such vectors in biotechnology.
[C12N 2800/10] Plasmid DNA -- C12N 2800/10 pertains to plasmid DNA, describing isolated circular nucleic acid molecules that replicate autonomously and serve as vectors in recombinant DNA technology. The entry includes methods for preparing, modifying, and employing plasmid DNA in biotechnology and molecular-biology applications.
[C12N 2800/101] for bacteria -- This CPC subclass pertains to nucleic-acid vectors intended for use with bacteria, covering compositions, methods and genetic constructs that deliver, express or modify nucleic acids in bacterial hosts. It includes engineered plasmids, viral-derived vectors and related delivery systems designed for bacterial transformation or conjugation.
[C12N 2800/102] for yeast -- The C12N 2800/102 subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification addresses nucleic-acid vectors that are designed for use in yeast. It includes DNA constructs, plasmids, viral or non-viral delivery systems employed to introduce, manipulate or express genetic material in yeast cells for biotechnological, research or industrial purposes.
[C12N 2800/103] for invertebrates -- C12N 2800/103 designates nucleic-acid vectors that are specifically engineered for use in invertebrate organisms. It covers DNA or RNA delivery systems such as plasmids, viral or synthetic vectors applied in gene-transfer, functional studies or recombinant expression within insects, crustaceans and other invertebrates.
[C12N 2800/105] for insects -- C12N 2800/105 pertains to nucleic-acid vectors intended for use in insects, encompassing plasmids, viral constructs or other DNA delivery systems employed in genetic manipulation, pest-control strategies or functional studies of insect biology.
[C12N 2800/106] for vertebrates -- C12N 2800/106 covers nucleic acid vectors specifically adapted for use in vertebrate organisms. It includes DNA or RNA constructs, plasmids, viral or non-viral delivery systems, and related methods intended to introduce or express genetic material in fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds or mammals. The class is applied to biotechnological inventions such as gene therapy, transgenic animal production and vertebrate cell engineering.
[C12N 2800/107] for mammalian -- C12N 2800/107 covers nucleic-acid vectors specifically engineered for use in mammalian systems, including plasmids, viral and non-viral delivery constructs designed to introduce, express or modify genes in animal cells. It pertains to biotechnological tools for gene therapy, recombinant protein production and functional genomics in mammals.
[C12N 2800/108] episomal vectors -- Episomal vectors are DNA molecules capable of replicating independently of the host chromosome, used to deliver genetic material into microorganisms without integration. They are employed in molecular cloning, gene therapy, and synthetic biology to maintain transgenes episomally. This class falls under nucleic acid vectors for microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2800/20] Pseudochromosomes, minichrosomosomes -- C12N 2800/20 relates to the design, construction and application of artificial chromosome-like elements, such as pseudo-chromosomes and minichromosomes, that can replicate and carry genetic material within microorganisms for biotechnological and research purposes.
[C12N 2800/202] of bacteriophage origin -- This subclass pertains to nucleic-acid vectors that originate from bacteriophage genetic material, covering phage-derived cloning or expression vectors used in molecular biology and biotechnology. It is applied to patents describing the design, preparation, and use of such bacteriophage-based vectors.
[C12N 2800/204] of bacterial origin, e.g. BAC -- The CPC subclass C12N 2800/204 relates to nucleic-acid vectors derived from bacterial sources, such as bacterial artificial chromosomes (BACs). It covers vectors that incorporate bacterial DNA elements to facilitate cloning, replication, and manipulation of large genomic fragments in bacterial hosts. This subclass is used for inventions involving the construction, use, or modification of such bacterial-origin vectors.
[C12N 2800/206] of yeast origin, e.g. YAC, 2u -- This subclass groups nucleic-acid vectors that are obtained from yeast, including yeast artificial chromosomes (YACs) and related constructs such as the 2u vector, which are employed for cloning, mapping and functional analysis of large DNA fragments. It is used to categorize biotechnological inventions that exploit yeast-derived carrier systems for molecular genetics and recombinant DNA work.
[C12N 2800/208] of mammalian origin, e.g. minichromosome -- This subclass covers nucleic-acid vectors that originate from mammalian cells, such as engineered minichromosomes, and are used to deliver genetic material in research, biotechnology or gene-therapy applications. It includes vectors that retain or mimic mammalian chromatin structure and function.
[C12N 2800/22] Vectors comprising a coding region that has been codon optimised for expression in a respective host -- This subclass covers nucleic-acid vectors whose coding region has been altered to use synonymous codons that match the preferred codon usage of a particular host, thereby enhancing transcription and translation efficiency in that organism. It applies to engineered genetic constructs designed for optimal expression of recombinant proteins in the chosen host cell.
