[C12N 2800/24] Vectors characterised by the absence of particular element, e.g. selectable marker, viral origin of replication -- C12N 2800/24 covers nucleic-acid vectors that are deliberately lacking specific components such as selectable markers or viral origins of replication. These marker-free or origin-free constructs are used to improve safety, avoid undesired cellular effects, and simplify downstream processing in recombinant DNA and genetic-engineering applications.
[C12N 2800/30] Vector systems comprising sequences for excision in presence of a recombinase, e.g. loxP or FRT -- This CPC entry covers vector systems that contain DNA sequences designed to be removed by site-specific recombinases such as Cre/loxP or Flp/FRT, enabling conditional excision of genetic elements in microorganisms or cells. They are used in molecular biology for controlled gene expression, genome editing, and creation of transgenic organisms.
[C12N 2800/40] Systems of functionally co-operating vectors -- Systems of functionally co-operating vectors are combinations of two or more nucleic-acid vectors designed to work together to achieve a biological task, such as coordinated gene expression, sequential enzymatic steps, or shared regulatory control. The arrangement may involve split-function components, complementary replication origins, or cooperative delivery mechanisms that enhance stability, efficiency, or specificity of the overall genetic engineering process. This classification covers the structural and functional aspects of such multi-vector systems.
[C12N 2800/50] Vectors for producing vectors -- The CPC subclass C12N 2800/50 covers nucleic-acid vectors that are specifically engineered to produce other vectors. It includes plasmid, viral or synthetic carrier systems designed to replicate or modify additional vector constructs for use in molecular cloning, gene therapy and other biotechnology applications.
[C12N 2800/60] Vectors containing traps for, e.g. exons, promoters -- C12N 2800/60 covers vectors that incorporate genetic traps intended to capture or disrupt specific DNA elements such as exons or promoters, enabling functional analysis of genes and regulatory sequences.
[C12N 2800/70] Vectors containing special elements for cloning, e.g. topoisomerase, adaptor sites -- This CPC subclass addresses nucleic-acid vectors engineered with special cloning elements such as topoisomerase recognition sites or adaptor sequences. The added features simplify the insertion, ligation or recombination of DNA fragments during molecular cloning procedures.
[C12N 2800/80] Vectors containing sites for inducing double-stranded breaks, e.g. meganuclease restriction sites -- The CPC subclass C12N 2800/80 covers nucleic-acid vectors engineered with specific sequences that create double-stranded DNA breaks, such as recognition sites for meganucleases. These vectors are used to facilitate targeted genome editing or recombination in microorganisms or other biological systems.
[C12N 2800/90] Vectors containing a transposable element -- Vectors that incorporate a transposable element are genetic tools designed to move DNA segments within or between genomes; they may be plasmids, viral particles or other delivery systems that carry transposon sequences enabling stable integration of the carried gene into a host organism's chromosome. This technology is used in molecular biology, functional genomics and biotechnology to achieve targeted gene insertion and mutagenesis.
[C12N 2800/95] Protection of vectors from inactivation by agents such as antibodies or enzymes, e.g. using polymers -- The CPC sub-group C12N 2800/95 covers methods and compositions that protect nucleic-acid vectors from inactivation by biological agents such as antibodies or nucleases, for example by coating the vector with polymers.
[C12N 2810/00] Vectors comprising a targeting moiety -- The CPC subclass C12N 2810/00 covers genetic vectors that incorporate a targeting moiety engineered to direct the vector to particular cells, tissues, or organelles. Such vectors may be plasmids, viruses, or other delivery systems where the targeting element - such as a ligand, antibody fragment, or peptide - confers specificity for delivery of nucleic acids or other cargo.
[C12N 2810/10] Vectors comprising a non-peptidic targeting moiety -- Vectors comprising a non-peptidic targeting moiety are genetic delivery systems, such as plasmids, viruses or other carriers, that contain a chemically defined, non-peptide ligand (for example, carbohydrates, nucleic acids, lipids or synthetic small molecules) designed to direct the vector to a specific microorganism, cell type or enzyme.
[C12N 2810/40] Vectors comprising a peptide as targeting moiety, e.g. a synthetic peptide, from undefined source -- The CPC subclass C12N 2810/40 covers nucleic-acid vectors that incorporate a peptide as the targeting moiety, such as a synthetic peptide or one from an undefined source, to direct the vector to specific cells or tissues. It falls within the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → vectors comprising a targeting moiety. This class is used to classify engineered vectors for gene delivery, vaccine or therapeutic applications that rely on peptide-mediated targeting.
[C12N 2810/405] Vectors comprising RGD peptide -- The CPC subclass C12N 2810/405 covers biotechnological vectors that contain an RGD-peptide as a targeting moiety. The RGD sequence binds integrin receptors, enabling directed delivery of nucleic acids, proteins or drugs to specific cell types. This subclass falls under microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on vector design for targeted therapy.
[C12N 2810/50] Vectors comprising as targeting moiety peptide derived from defined protein -- This subclass covers biological vectors that contain a peptide derived from a defined protein and used as a targeting moiety to direct the vector to specific cells or tissues. The peptide is covalently attached or displayed on the vector surface to provide selective binding. Applications include gene delivery, vaccine vectors, and therapeutic targeting.
[C12N 2810/55] from bacteria -- C12N 2810/55 classifies biotechnological vectors that incorporate a targeting moiety derived from bacteria. It falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically vectors comprising a targeting component. The entry covers bacterial-derived targeting elements used to direct genetic material or therapeutic agents.
[C12N 2810/60] from viruses
[C12N 2810/6009] dsDNA viruses -- The C12N 2810/6009 entry covers engineered vectors based on double-stranded DNA viruses that contain a targeting moiety to direct the virus to specific cells or tissues, typically for gene delivery, therapeutic or research purposes. It pertains to the use of dsDNA viral particles as biotechnological tools for precise molecular or cellular targeting.
[C12N 2810/6018] Adenoviridae -- C12N 2810/6018 covers adenoviridae that are engineered as vectors containing a targeting moiety for delivering therapeutic or diagnostic payloads. It includes modifications to the viral capsid or genome that direct the adenovirus to specific cells or tissues, facilitating gene therapy, vaccine development, or molecular imaging applications.
[C12N 2810/6027] ssDNA viruses -- C12N 2810/6027 classifies single-stranded DNA viruses that are employed as vectors containing a targeting moiety, such as a ligand or peptide, to direct the delivery of genetic material into specific cells. The code falls under the chemistry hierarchy through biochemistry and microorganisms or enzymes, reflecting its use in molecular-biology techniques and therapeutic gene transfer.
[C12N 2810/6036] DNA rev transcr viruses -- This subclass covers viral vectors derived from reverse-transcribing (retro-) viruses that are engineered to include a targeting moiety directing the vector to specific cells or tissues. It includes the composition, modification and use of DNA-based vectors based on these viruses for gene delivery or related biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2810/6045] RNA rev transcr viruses -- The CPC subclass C12N 2810/6045 covers viral vectors whose genetic payload is RNA and that are engineered with a targeting moiety to direct delivery to specific cells. It includes retroviral-based systems used for transferring nucleic acids in biotechnological or therapeutic applications, emphasizing the combination of a reverse-transcribing viral backbone and a targeting ligand.
[C12N 2810/6054] Retroviridae -- C12N 2810/6054 designates vectors that incorporate a targeting moiety derived from members of the Retroviridae family, covering retroviral particles engineered for delivery of genetic material. It lies within the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy under microorganisms or enzymes and specifies retroviral-based gene-transfer vectors.
[C12N 2810/6063] ds RNA viruses -- This subclass covers double-stranded RNA viruses used as vectors that incorporate a targeting moiety to deliver genetic material or therapeutic agents to specific cells or tissues. It includes the design and engineering of viral capsids or genomes with selective binding properties and is relevant to biotechnology, gene therapy, and vaccine development.
[C12N 2810/6072] negative strand RNA viruses -- C12N 2810/6072 covers biotechnological vectors that contain a targeting moiety and are derived from negative-strand RNA viruses. These vectors are used to deliver genetic material into cells for research, vaccine or gene-therapy applications, and are classified under chemistry, biochemistry and microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2810/6081] rhabdoviridae, e.g. VSV -- The CPC subclass C12N 2810/6081 pertains to vectors that incorporate a targeting moiety derived from rhabdoviridae viruses, exemplified by vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV). Such vectors are engineered for delivering genetic material into specific cells for applications in research, gene therapy, or vaccine development.
[C12N 2810/609] positive strand RNA viruses -- C12N 2810/609 covers inventions related to vectors that contain a targeting moiety for delivering genetic material of positive-strand RNA viruses. The classification is used for biotechnological tools and methods that exploit these viruses for gene therapy, vaccine development, or molecular biology applications.
[C12N 2810/65] from plants -- The CPC subclass C12N 2810/65 covers biological vectors that contain a targeting moiety derived from plants, used for delivering genetic material or enzymes into microorganisms or cells. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy of biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on plant-origin targeting components. This subclass is applied in patent filings at both the EPO and USPTO.
[C12N 2810/70] from fungi -- The C12N 2810/70 subclass covers vectors that contain a targeting moiety derived from fungi, such as fungal-origin promoters, signal peptides or surface proteins used to direct the vector to specific cells or tissues. It pertains to biotechnological tools that exploit fungal components for targeted delivery of genetic material, enzymes or other bioactive agents.
[C12N 2810/75] from invertebrates -- C12N 2810/75 classifies vectors that incorporate targeting moieties sourced from invertebrates, such as insects, crustaceans or other non-vertebrate organisms. These vectors are designed to deliver nucleic acids, proteins or enzymes to specific cells or tissues, often in gene-therapy, vaccine or biotechnology applications. The subclass groups inventions that specifically rely on invertebrate-derived elements for selective targeting.
[C12N 2810/80] from vertebrates -- C12N 2810/80 covers genetic vectors that incorporate a targeting moiety sourced from vertebrate organisms, such as proteins or peptides that direct the vector to specific cells or tissues. These vectors are used in biotechnology and therapeutic applications to improve delivery of nucleic acids or other cargo to designated vertebrate targets.
[C12N 2810/85] mammalian -- The subclass C12N 2810/85 covers vectors that incorporate a targeting moiety for use in mammalian systems. It includes recombinant or synthetic delivery vehicles-viral or non-viral-engineered to direct genetic material specifically to mammalian cells or tissues, such as gene-therapy vectors bearing cell-type-specific ligands.
[C12N 2810/851] from growth factors -- C12N 2810/851 covers nucleic-acid or viral vectors that contain a targeting moiety derived from growth-factor proteins. The classification applies to constructs engineered to direct the vector to specific cell types by exploiting growth-factor receptors, thereby enhancing delivery of therapeutic genes or other cargo. It falls under vectors comprising a targeting moiety in the biotechnology hierarchy.
[C12N 2810/852] from cytokines -- C12N 2810/852 pertains to biotechnological vectors that incorporate a targeting moiety derived from cytokines to achieve cell-specific delivery of nucleic acids. The classification covers recombinant viral or non-viral carriers engineered with cytokine fragments or whole cytokine molecules that direct the vector to receptors for those cytokines.
[C12N 2810/853] from tumor necrosis factor, TNF -- C12N 2810/853 covers recombinant vectors that contain a targeting moiety derived from tumor necrosis factor (TNF). The classification is used for biotechnology inventions where the TNF-based ligand directs the vector to specific cells or tissues, often for therapeutic or diagnostic purposes. It falls under chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on engineered vectors with biologically active targeting components.
[C12N 2810/854] from hormones -- C12N 2810/854 covers vectors that incorporate a targeting moiety derived from hormones to direct the vector to specific cells or tissues. The classification applies to biotechnological constructs such as viral or non-viral carriers that use hormonal ligands for selective delivery of nucleic acids or proteins.
[C12N 2810/855] from receptors
[C12N 2810/856] from integrins -- C12N 2810/856 covers vectors that incorporate a targeting moiety derived from integrins to direct delivery of nucleic acids or other payloads to specific cells or tissues. These integrin-based vectors are employed in gene-therapy, vaccine and molecular-biology applications where cell-specific binding enhances efficacy.
[C12N 2810/857] from blood coagulation or fibrinolysis factors -- C12N 2810/857 classifies recombinant vectors that incorporate nucleic acid segments encoding blood-coagulation or fibrinolysis factors. These vectors are engineered for expression of clotting or clot-dissolving proteins, often used in therapeutic or diagnostic applications.
[C12N 2810/858] from apolipopeptides -- The C12N 2810/858 subclass concerns biotechnological vectors that contain a targeting moiety derived from apolipopeptides. It pertains to the design and use of such vectors for precise delivery of genetic material, drugs, or enzymes to specific microorganisms or enzymatic systems.
[C12N 2810/859] from immunoglobulins -- A subclass for vectors that contain a targeting moiety derived from immunoglobulins, used to direct the delivery of genetic material or therapeutic agents to specific microorganisms, cells or enzymes. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes and concerns engineered delivery systems employing antibody-based targeting.
[C12N 2810/90] avian -- This CPC entry concerns vectors that incorporate a targeting moiety derived from or specific to avian organisms, such as viral or non-viral delivery systems engineered to recognize avian cells or tissues. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → vectors comprising a targeting moiety, and is used to classify inventions involving avian-directed genetic or therapeutic vectors.
[C12N 2820/00] Vectors comprising a special origin of replication system -- C12N 2820/00 covers biotechnological vectors-such as plasmids, viral or artificial nucleic-acid carriers-engineered to contain a non-standard or specially designed origin of replication that controls how the vector duplicates within a host cell. The classification includes inventions that modify, substitute, or combine replication origins to achieve particular copy-number, host-range or stability characteristics for use in gene cloning, expression or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2820/002] inducible or controllable -- The C12N 2820/002 subclass covers vectors that contain a specialized origin of replication which can be switched on or off, or otherwise regulated, to control plasmid copy number or gene expression. Such inducible or controllable systems are used to modulate the production of recombinant proteins or metabolic pathways in microbial hosts.
[C12N 2820/005] cell-cycle regulated -- C12N 2820/005 covers vectors that contain a specialized origin of replication whose activity is controlled by the host cell-cycle. Such vectors are designed to replicate only at specific phases, enabling timed gene expression or stability in microbial or eukaryotic hosts. The classification falls under chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on engineered DNA tools.
[C12N 2820/007] tissue or cell-specific -- C12N 2820/007 classifies recombinant vectors that incorporate a special origin of replication system and are engineered to operate preferentially in a particular tissue or cell type. The subclass includes vectors using tissue-specific promoters, enhancers or other regulatory elements to restrict gene expression or replication to the target cells, as employed in gene-therapy, vaccine and functional-genomics applications.
[C12N 2820/10] multiple origins of replication -- C12N 2820/10 covers vectors that contain more than one origin of replication, enabling them to replicate in a host microorganism by using several distinct replication-origin systems. This facilitates stable maintenance of the vector and can improve yield of recombinant products in biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2820/55] from bacteria -- C12N 2820/55 covers bacterial genetic vectors that contain a specially engineered origin of replication, enabling controlled replication of the vector in host cells. These vectors are used in molecular cloning, recombinant protein production and gene-function studies, and fall under the biotechnology sub-field of microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2820/60] from viruses -- C12N 2820/60 covers viral vectors that incorporate a specialized origin of replication system, enabling the vector to replicate within a host cell after delivery. The classification applies to engineered viruses used as carriers for genetic material in research, vaccine development, or gene-therapy applications.
[C12N 2820/65] from plants -- A C12N 2820/65 classification covers recombinant vectors that contain a plant-derived origin of replication, used to replicate genetic material in plant cells or plant-based systems. It applies to engineered DNA constructs designed for plant biotechnology, including transformation, expression, and gene-editing applications.
[C12N 2820/70] from fungi -- C12N 2820/70 covers vectors that contain a specialized origin of replication derived from fungi. These vectors are designed for use in genetic engineering to enable replication of the introduced DNA within fungal or compatible host cells, supporting cloning, gene expression, or other biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2820/702] yeast -- The subclass C12N 2820/702 covers DNA vectors that contain a specialised origin-of-replication system for use in yeast. Such vectors are designed to replicate autonomously in yeast cells, facilitating genetic manipulation, expression of heterologous genes and production of recombinant proteins. It is a subcategory of vectors for microorganisms or enzymes within the broader chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy.
[C12N 2820/704] S. cerevisae -- The classification C12N 2820/704 covers genetic vectors that contain a specialized origin of replication for use in the yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae, enabling stable replication and maintenance of engineered DNA within this microorganism. It pertains to biotechnological tools for yeast molecular biology.
[C12N 2820/706] S. pombe -- C12N 2820/706 refers to genetic vectors that incorporate a replication origin taken from the fission yeast Schizosaccharomyces pombe. Such vectors enable autonomous replication in host cells, facilitating cloning, gene expression or genome editing. The class is used for inventions involving these pombe-derived origin systems.
[C12N 2820/708] C. albicans -- The entry covers vectors that incorporate a specialized origin of replication derived from the fungus Candida albicans. It pertains to biotechnological tools used to maintain and propagate recombinant DNA in C. albicans or related yeast systems, enabling genetic manipulation and protein expression in this microorganism.
[C12N 2820/75] from invertebrates -- The CPC subclass C12N 2820/75 covers vectors that incorporate a special origin of replication system sourced from invertebrate organisms. These vectors are used in genetic engineering and biotechnology to replicate DNA within host cells, and the classification falls under Chemistry › Biochemistry › Microorganisms or Enzymes › Vectors comprising a special origin of replication system.
[C12N 2820/80] from vertebrates -- C12N 2820/80 covers recombinant vectors that contain a special origin of replication derived from vertebrate sources. These vectors are used in genetic engineering to replicate DNA in host cells, enabling cloning, expression or functional studies of vertebrate genes. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on vector design employing vertebrate-origin replication elements.
[C12N 2820/85] mammalian -- C12N 2820/85 refers to vectors that contain a specialized origin of replication enabling them to function in mammalian cells. The subclass covers plasmids, viral or other genetic delivery systems engineered for efficient replication and expression of recombinant DNA in mammalian hosts, often used in gene-therapy, vaccine and protein-production applications.
[C12N 2820/90] avian -- The subclass deals with avian-derived biological vectors that incorporate a specially engineered origin of replication for use in gene delivery or expression applications. It falls under microorganisms or enzymes and addresses engineered replication systems originating from birds.
[C12N 2830/00] Vector systems having a special element relevant for transcription -- C12N 2830/00 covers recombinant DNA vectors that incorporate a distinct transcription-relevant element such as a promoter, enhancer, operator or regulatory RNA sequence. The classification applies to engineered plasmids, viral or bacterial vectors designed to control gene expression in microorganisms, cells or enzymes, and includes constructs where the element is engineered, substituted or fused to affect transcriptional activity. This enables precise regulation of recombinant protein or metabolite production.
[C12N 2830/001] controllable enhancer/promoter combination -- The CPC subclass C12N 2830/001 covers vector systems that incorporate a controllable enhancer-promoter combination designed to regulate transcription of a target gene. It applies to biotechnological constructs used in microorganisms or enzymatic contexts where transcriptional activity can be turned on or off by external signals.
[C12N 2830/002] inducible enhancer/promoter combination, e.g. hypoxia, iron, transcription factor -- C12N 2830/002 covers vector systems that include a transcription-relevant element such as an inducible enhancer or promoter. It pertains to constructs whose expression can be switched on by stimuli like hypoxia, iron availability or binding of a specific transcription factor. This subclass falls under microbiology and enzyme-related biotechnology.
[C12N 2830/003] tet inducible -- C12N 2830/003 covers nucleic-acid vectors that incorporate tetracycline-responsive elements to control transcription. It includes constructs where a tet-repressor or tet-operator sequence enables gene expression to be turned on or off by tetracycline or its analogues, providing inducible regulation in microbial or cellular systems.
[C12N 2830/005] repressible enhancer/promoter combination, e.g. KRAB -- The entry C12N 2830/005 covers vector systems that include a transcription-regulating element whose activity can be repressed, such as an enhancer or promoter combined with a KRAB-type repressor domain. These constructs allow conditional down-regulation of gene expression in microorganisms or engineered cells, facilitating controlled transcriptional studies and biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2830/006] tet repressible -- The CPC entry C12N 2830/006 covers vector systems that contain a tetracycline-repressible transcriptional element, allowing gene expression to be switched off when tetracycline or a similar ligand is present. Such regulatable vectors are used in molecular biology to achieve conditional control of target genes in microbial or cellular hosts.
[C12N 2830/007] cell cycle specific enhancer/promoter combination -- A cell-cycle-specific enhancer/promoter combination is a genetic element engineered into vector systems to drive transcription only during particular phases of the cell division cycle, allowing precise temporal control of gene expression in microorganisms or eukaryotic cells for research and biotech applications.
[C12N 2830/008] cell type or tissue specific enhancer/promoter combination -- C12N 2830/008 classifies vector systems that incorporate a cell-type or tissue-specific enhancer-promoter combination to direct transcription only in designated biological contexts, such as particular tissues or cell lines. This code covers engineered DNA elements designed for targeted gene expression in biotechnological and therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2830/15] chimeric enhancer/promoter combination -- C12N 2830/15 covers DNA constructs in which an enhancer and a promoter from different origins are fused to form a chimeric regulatory element, used in vectors to modulate transcription of target genes in microbial or cellular systems.
[C12N 2830/20] transcription of more than one cistron -- The CPC subclass C12N 2830/20 covers methods and vector systems that enable transcription of two or more cistrons in a single RNA transcript, i.e., polycistronic transcription. It pertains to engineered microorganisms, enzymes or nucleic-acid constructs that contain special transcriptional elements allowing multiple genes to be co-expressed from one promoter.
[C12N 2830/205] bidirectional -- The subclass C12N 2830/205 covers recombinant vectors that incorporate a bidirectional transcription element, allowing two genes to be expressed simultaneously in opposite directions. It pertains to vector systems designed for use in microorganisms or enzymatic contexts where coordinated transcription of multiple genes is required.
[C12N 2830/30] being an enhancer not forming part of the promoter region -- The CPC subclass C12N 2830/30 pertains to vector systems that include a DNA element functioning as an enhancer, which influences transcription but is situated outside the promoter region and therefore is not part of the promoter itself.
[C12N 2830/32] being an silencer not forming part of the promoter region -- It covers recombinant DNA vectors that contain a transcriptional silencer element positioned outside the promoter region, allowing down-regulation of gene expression without being part of the promoter itself. Such vectors are employed in microbial or enzymatic systems to modulate transcriptional activity.
[C12N 2830/34] being a transcription initiation element
[C12N 2830/36] being a transcription termination element -- This subclass concerns vectors that contain a transcription termination element-a DNA sequence that causes RNA polymerase to stop transcription and release the nascent RNA. Such terminators are used to define the end of a transcribed unit in plasmids, viral vectors or other genetic constructs, ensuring proper expression and preventing read-through into downstream sequences.
[C12N 2830/38] being a stuffer -- C12N 2830/38 relates to vector systems where a stuffer DNA segment is inserted into a transcription-relevant element. The stuffer serves as a non-functional placeholder to adjust vector size, facilitate cloning, or modulate expression without altering the functional gene. This classification is used for biotechnological constructs involving microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2830/40] being an insulator -- C12N 2830/40 covers vector systems that incorporate an insulating element to block unwanted transcriptional influence on adjacent genes. It applies to molecular constructs used in microorganisms or enzymatic contexts where a transcriptional insulator improves expression specificity and stability. This subclass falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2830/42] being an intron or intervening sequence for splicing and/or stability of RNA -- This CPC subclass covers vector systems that incorporate introns or other intervening sequences designed to influence RNA splicing and stability, thereby modulating gene expression in biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2830/46] elements influencing chromatin structure, e.g. scaffold/matrix attachment region, methylation free island -- This CPC subclass covers vector constructs that contain DNA elements which modify chromatin organization, such as scaffold/matrix attachment regions or CpG-poor islands, to enhance transcriptional activity. It is used for recombinant DNA vectors where these elements improve gene expression by altering nucleosome positioning or epigenetic state.
[C12N 2830/48] regulating transport or export of RNA, e.g. RRE, PRE, WPRE, CTE -- The CPC subclass C12N 2830/48 covers vector systems that contain elements that control the movement of RNA out of the cell or its export from the nucleus, such as regulatory RNA elements like RRE, PRE, WPRE and CTE, which are used to enhance transcription or translation of recombinant genes.
[C12N 2830/50] regulating RNA stability, not being an intron, e.g. poly A signal -- The CPC subclass C12N 2830/50 covers vector systems that incorporate special elements for controlling RNA stability, such as poly-A signals, rather than intron sequences. It pertains to biochemistry of microorganisms or enzymes and deals with regulatory features that influence transcriptional output by affecting RNA decay or processing. This entry is used to classify inventions related to engineered vectors designed to modulate mRNA half-life.
[C12N 2830/52] encoding ribozyme for self-inactivation
[C12N 2830/55] from bacteria -- This subclass covers bacterial-derived vector systems that contain a transcription-relevant element such as a promoter, operator, enhancer or other regulatory sequence, used to drive expression of cloned genes. It includes plasmids, bacteriophage-based vectors and other carriers engineered with bacterial transcription elements for recombinant DNA applications.
[C12N 2830/60] from viruses -- This subclass covers viral-derived vector systems that contain a specific transcription-related element such as a promoter, enhancer or regulatory sequence used to drive expression of introduced genes. It includes the design, construction and application of these viral vectors for gene expression or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 2830/65] from plants -- C12N 2830/65 covers vector systems that contain a plant-derived element important for transcription, such as promoters, enhancers or regulatory sequences taken from higher plants and used to control gene expression in microbial hosts. The classification is used for biotechnological inventions where plant transcriptional components are incorporated into vectors for recombinant DNA work.
[C12N 2830/70] from fungi -- C12N 2830/70 covers vector systems that are derived from fungi and contain transcription-related elements such as fungal promoters, enhancers or terminators. These vectors are used to introduce, express or regulate genes in host organisms, exploiting fungal regulatory sequences for biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2830/702] yeast -- C12N 2830/702 designates yeast employed as a vector component that provides a transcription-relevant element in biotechnological applications. The entry covers yeast strains, plasmids or other constructs in which yeast serves as a host or delivery system for genes whose expression is controlled at the transcriptional level.
[C12N 2830/704] S. cerevisiae -- The code C12N 2830/704 covers the use of the yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae in vector systems that incorporate a transcription-relevant element such as a promoter, enhancer or regulatory sequence. It pertains to biotechnological applications where the yeast serves as a host for recombinant gene expression driven by these specialized transcriptional components.
[C12N 2830/706] S. pombe -- C12N 2830/706 classifies nucleic-acid vectors that incorporate a transcription-regulatory element derived from the fission yeast Schizosaccharomyces pombe, such as promoters, enhancers or terminators. These vectors are used to control gene expression in recombinant DNA work with eukaryotic or prokaryotic host cells.
[C12N 2830/708] C. albicans -- C12N 2830/708 pertains to genetic vectors that incorporate transcription-related elements derived from the yeast Candida albicans. It covers constructs using C. albicans promoters, enhancers, or other regulatory sequences to drive expression of recombinant genes in vector systems. This subclass is relevant for biotechnological applications involving this organism as a host or source of transcriptional machinery.
[C12N 2830/75] from invertebrates -- The C12N 2830/75 class covers vector systems that incorporate a transcription-relevant element sourced from invertebrate organisms. It includes recombinant DNA constructs where the regulatory sequence originates from insects, mollusks, crustaceans or similar taxa and is used to drive gene expression in host cells.
[C12N 2830/80] from vertebrates -- C12N 2830/80 covers vector systems that incorporate elements derived from vertebrate species and are used to drive transcription. It includes viral, plasmid or other nucleic-acid carriers that contain vertebrate-specific promoters, enhancers or regulatory sequences for gene expression in research or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2830/85] mammalian -- C12N 2830/85 covers mammalian vector systems that contain a transcription-related element such as promoters, enhancers or regulatory sequences used to drive gene expression in mammalian cells. It includes plasmids, viral vectors and other delivery constructs engineered for transcriptional control in mammalian hosts.
[C12N 2830/90] avian -- C12N 2830/90 covers vector systems that contain a genetic element originating from or functional in avian species, used to direct transcription of recombinant nucleic acids.
[C12N 2840/00] Vectors comprising a special translation-regulating system -- C12N 2840/00 covers genetically engineered vectors that contain a specialized translation-regulating system, such as promoters, ribosome-binding sites or regulatory RNA elements, designed to control protein synthesis in microorganisms or enzymes. These vectors enable precise modulation of gene expression for biotechnological, pharmaceutical or industrial applications.
[C12N 2840/002] controllable or inducible -- C12N 2840/002 covers recombinant vectors that incorporate a specialized system for regulating protein translation, allowing the expression of the inserted gene to be switched on, off, or modulated in response to an external signal. These controllable or inducible vectors are used in molecular biology and biotechnology to study gene function, produce therapeutic proteins, or engineer metabolic pathways with precise timing.
[C12N 2840/005] cell cycle specific -- The C12N 2840/005 class covers genetic vectors that contain a translation-regulating system designed to be active only at specific stages of the cell cycle. Such vectors enable precise, phase-dependent expression of inserted genes in microorganisms or engineered cells, facilitating controlled studies of cell-cycle processes and biotechnological applications.
[C12N 2840/007] cell or tissue specific -- C12N 2840/007 covers vectors that contain a specialised translation-regulating system which restricts gene expression to particular cells or tissues. The classification includes constructs employing tissue-specific promoters, enhancers, riboswitches or other regulatory elements that ensure protein production occurs only in the intended cell type. It is used for biotechnological applications where precise spatial control of transgene expression is required.
[C12N 2840/10] regulates levels of translation -- C12N 2840/10 covers genetic vectors that contain a specialized system for controlling translation, such as regulatory RNA elements, riboswitches, upstream open reading frames or other mechanisms that adjust the amount of protein produced from a coding sequence. This sub-class focuses on engineered constructs that modulate translation efficiency to fine-tune gene expression.
[C12N 2840/102] inhibiting translation -- C12N 2840/102 refers to vectors that contain a specialized translation-regulating system designed to suppress or block translation of target genes in microorganisms. These constructs are employed in biotechnology to control gene expression, often for research or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2840/105] enhancing translation -- The subclass C12N 2840/105 covers biotechnological inventions that improve protein synthesis by using vectors that contain a specialized translation-regulating system. It includes methods, nucleic-acid constructs or modified hosts designed to increase the efficiency of translation of a target gene, thereby enhancing overall protein production in microorganisms or other cells.
[C12N 2840/107] inhibiting translational read-through -- C12N 2840/107 includes vectors that carry a specialized system for regulating translation and are employed to suppress or inhibit translational read-through, the phenomenon where ribosomes ignore stop codons and extend protein synthesis. The entry sits within chemistry, biochemistry and microorganisms or enzymes, focusing on engineered genetic tools that control gene expression.
[C12N 2840/20] translation of more than one cistron -- The CPC subclass C12N 2840/20 covers vectors and methods that enable the simultaneous translation of more than one cistron, such as polycistronic mRNA designs or systems that coordinate the expression of several genes within a single construct. It includes specially engineered translation-regulating elements that allow multiple proteins to be produced from one transcript, a technique commonly used in recombinant DNA and synthetic biology applications.
[C12N 2840/203] having an IRES -- The CPC subclass C12N 2840/203 covers vectors that incorporate a special translation-regulating system, specifically an internal ribosome entry site (IRES), enabling cap-independent initiation of protein synthesis in microbial or enzymatic hosts. Such vectors are used to drive expression of downstream genes without reliance on conventional ribosomal scanning.
[C12N 2840/206] having multiple IRES -- This subclass covers vectors that contain two or more internal ribosome entry sites to allow cap-independent translation of several genes from a single mRNA. Such vectors are used to co-express multiple proteins in microbial or eukaryotic cells.
[C12N 2840/44] being a specific part of the splice mechanism, e.g. donor, acceptor
[C12N 2840/445] for trans-splicing, e.g. polypyrimidine tract, branch point splicing -- This CPC subclass covers recombinant vectors that incorporate engineered elements for controlling trans-splicing, such as a polypyrimidine tract or branch-point sequence, thereby providing a special translation-regulating system for the inserted genetic material.
[C12N 2840/50] utilisation of non-ATG initiation codon -- The C12N 2840/50 subclass covers the use of alternative start codons other than the standard ATG to initiate protein synthesis in engineered vectors that contain specialized translation-regulating elements. It addresses methods for designing and employing such non-ATG initiation signals to control expression of genes in microbial or enzymatic systems. This enables fine-tuning of protein production and functional studies of novel proteins.
[C12N 2840/55] from bacteria
[C12N 2840/60] from viruses -- This CPC subclass covers vectors that incorporate a viral-derived system for regulating translation of inserted genes. It includes plasmids, viral particles or other delivery vehicles engineered with promoter, ribosome-binding or regulatory elements originally found in viruses to control protein expression. Used in genetic engineering and biotechnology.
[C12N 2840/65] from plants -- C12N 2840/65 covers plant-derived vectors that incorporate specialized translation-regulating systems, such as promoters, enhancers, or ribosome binding sites, to control protein synthesis in host cells. These vectors are employed in molecular biology and biotechnology for expressing recombinant genes using plant-based regulatory elements.
[C12N 2840/70] from fungi -- The C12N 2840/70 subclass covers vectors that incorporate a specialized translation-regulating system derived from fungi. These vectors are used to control protein synthesis in host cells, employing fungal regulatory elements to modulate translational efficiency. They fall within the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy under microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 2840/702] yeast -- The CPC subclass C12N 2840/702 covers biotechnological vectors designed for yeast that incorporate a specialized translation-regulating system. These vectors contain tailored regulatory sequences or elements that modulate ribosomal activity, allowing precise control of protein synthesis in yeast hosts.
[C12N 2840/704] S. cerevisiae -- The C12N 2840/704 subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers vectors that contain a specialized translation-regulating system for use in the yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae. It includes plasmids, viral or artificial chromosomes engineered to control protein synthesis at the translational level.
[C12N 2840/706] S. pombe -- This CPC subclass (C12N 2840/706) covers vectors that incorporate a special translation-regulating system derived from or employed with the fission yeast Schizosaccharomyces pombe. It includes genetic constructs such as promoters, regulatory elements and related sequences designed for controlled protein expression in S. pombe.
[C12N 2840/708] C. albicans -- C12N 2840/708 covers genetic vectors that incorporate a specialized translation-regulating system for the fungal organism Candida albicans. The classification falls under chemistry to biochemistry to microorganisms or enzymes and is used for patents involving engineered expression constructs for this yeast.
[C12N 2840/75] from invertebrates -- C12N 2840/75 covers genetic vectors that contain a specially designed translation-regulating system and are derived from invertebrate sources. Such vectors are used to control protein synthesis in microbial or enzymatic applications, enabling precise expression of recombinant genes.
[C12N 2840/80] from vertebrates -- C12N 2840/80 covers vectors that contain a specialised translation-regulating system derived from vertebrate sources. These vectors are used in biotechnological and genetic-engineering applications to control protein synthesis in vertebrate cells, often involving engineered promoters, ribosome-binding elements or RNA-based regulators. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically vectors with a special translation-regulating system.
[C12N 2840/85] mammalian -- C12N 2840/85 covers mammalian vectors that incorporate a special translation-regulating system, such as promoters, enhancers or regulatory RNA elements designed to control protein synthesis in host cells. These vectors are employed in biotechnology for gene expression, protein production and therapeutic applications.
[C12N 2840/90] avian -- A vector derived from or intended for use in avian species that incorporates a specialized system to control translation of the introduced genetic material, such as avian-specific promoters, ribosome-binding sites, or regulatory RNA elements. This classification covers engineered carriers for gene transfer in birds, including viral and non-viral vectors designed to exploit avian translation mechanisms.
[C12N 2999/00] Further aspects of viruses or vectors not covered by groups -- C12N 2999/00 relates to additional aspects of viruses or viral vectors that are not assigned to any more specific subclass within the C12N hierarchy, including novel uses, engineering methods, or ancillary processes that fall outside the defined groups.
[C12N 2999/002] Adverse teaching
[C12N 2999/005] Biological teaching, e.g. a link between protein and disease, new virus causing pandemic -- C12N 2999/005 addresses inventions that provide educational or demonstrative material on biological topics such as linking specific proteins to disease processes or describing newly identified viruses that could cause pandemics, covering aspects of microorganisms or enzymes not captured by more specific virus groups.
[C12N 2999/007] Technological advancements, e.g. new system for producing known virus, cre-lox system for production of transgenic animals -- C12N 2999/007 covers technological innovations related to viruses or viral vectors that are not otherwise classified, such as novel production systems for known viruses and the use of Cre-lox recombination for generating transgenic animals. The subclass captures methods and devices that improve manufacturing, manipulation or delivery of viral agents beyond existing groups.
[C12N 3/00] Spore forming or isolating processes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes for spore formation and for isolation of spores.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Spores, spore-containing compositions or processes using spores.Spores, compositions containing spores, processes using spores can be classified according to microorganisms -> C12N1/00 , C12P , C12R  **Glossary:** - Spore: Reproductive structure that is adapted for dispersal and surviving for extended periods of time, e.g. in unfavorable conditions. Spores form part of the life cycles of many microorganisms such as bacteria, microalgae, fungi and some protozoa.
[C12N 5/00] Undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells, e.g. cell lines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Further details of subgroups  The highest rank head group is to be used only in desperate cases, for documents which do not fit elsewhere, e.g. culture processes characterised by temperature  By exception to the dividing line general C12N5/00 to the specific C12N5/06 , documents describing the modification of membranes of a specific cell type are classified both in C12N5/0006 and C12N5/06 and subgroups.  Only generic encapsulation here: Encapsulation of a specific cell type is C12N5/06 (and subgroups), C12N2533/00 Orthogonal Indexing codes may be used for the capsule material. Classifying the document in A61L27/00 and/or A61K47/00 should be considered. Encapsulation in fermenters is C12N13/00 . Note that encapsulated hepatocytes and pancreatic cells have specific subgroups: C12N5/0671 and C12N5/0677 .  Generic media for potentially any type of cells. Culture processes using a particular agent are treated as a culture medium containing this agent (e.g. use of propranolol in cell culture: C12N5/0018 , C12N2501/81 ). All documents should have at least one Orthogonal Indexing code from the range C12N2500/00 - C12N2502/00 , and possibly many of them. Examples using CHO, 3T3, BHK or other common cell types should be regarded as generic disclosure and classified under C12N5/00 rather than under C12N5/06 .  When a new medium is fully disclosed, most if not all C12N2500/00 Orthogonal Indexing codes potentially apply; codes should be given for any useful information, i.e. any specific component with a specific concentration range (e.g. Ala 20 µ, Pro 10 µ … is indexed C12N2500/32 ; reference to a standard composition of essential amino-acids as one component need not be indexed)-which can mean that many codes are actually given, but this appears to be the best way to retrieve reasonably quickly a document with a medium comprising, e.g., 10-100 nM Cu 2+ or 20-75 µ Glu. It may not be necessary to index the most common additives (e.g. glutamine) and no code is foreseen for antibiotics, as these appear to be invariably present.  Note that serum-free media for specific cell types are C12N5/06 (and subgroups), C12N2500/99 .  By "negative selection", it is meant that the document defines what is to be rejected but does not define in any positive manner what is to be retained. The combination of negative and positive markers is regarded as positive selection, and should normally allow to assign a specific type to the cells, and to classify in C12N5/06 (and subgroups). In practice, C12N5/0087 and C12N5/0093 deal with purging blood or bone marrow (but also, rarely, of other tissues) from, respectively, immune cells and tumour cells before transplantation; C12N5/0081 is thus very rarely used.  Documents should have at least one C12N2533/00 Orthogonal Indexing Code. Supports for specific cell types are C12N5/06 (and subgroups), C12N2531/00 - C12N2539/00 .  Classification is made in the appropriate group below C12N5/06 ; group C12N5/06 implies that the teachings apply only to specific cells and it should always be possible to know whether the cells derive from vertebrates or invertebrates.  General Considerations:  The scope of any sub-group comprises the cells of the title, any tissue explant essentially consisting of these cells (e.g. skin = keratinocytes = C12N5/0629 ), processes for isolating these cells (e.g. the definition of a set of markers to isolate a specific cell by FACS, but a document dealing with a specific antibody for one single marker can be classified solely in C07K16/00 ), processes for preparing these cells (culture, differentiation), culture media and/supports specifically adapted for these cells and medical uses of these cells (as far as the nature of the cells is known and is relevant; e.g. medical composition of cultivated pancreatic cells C12N5/0676 , A61K35/12 , "low-tech" composition of animal tissue extracts A61K35/00 and subgroups, photochemically-treated blood A61K41/0057 , A61K35/14 ).  Controlled language: Title note of C12N5/06 : In this group, the following words are used with the meanings indicated:  This has been adopted to clarify classification practice with respect to the use of the words "pluri-" and "multipotent." Under this definition, totipotent cells need to be able to generate placenta and amnion, i.e. only the zygote, blastula and morula cells strictly qualify; applicants tend to use the term more liberally … Conversely, the archetypal example of an adult pluripotent cell in the sense of C12N5/0607 , has been named "Multipotent Adult Stem (Progenitor) Cell" (MASC, MAPC) by its discoverers, which is too restrictive in view of our definitions.  Immediate precursors:  "Four dot" stem/precursor/progenitor groups usually cover (multipotent) stem cells as well as (restricted) precursors and committed progenitors, but there are two important exceptions with respect to the latter: myoblasts go with myotubes in C12N5/0658 (since myotubes do not proliferate, culture can only be directed to their precursors). In the blood/immune hierarchy C12N5/0634 , immediate, committed, precursors are classified with their progeny; C12N5/0647 is reserved for stem cells and multi-lineage progenitors.  Sub-headgroups C12N5/0603 , C12N5/0608 , C12N5/0613 , C12N5/0618 , etc vs. specific subgroups C12N5/0604 , C12N5/0605 , C12N5/0606 , C12N5/0608 :  The "three dot" (sub)headgroups are used for two different purposes:  E.g. a document pertaining to neural differentiation of BM-MSC is C12N5/0618 , C12N2506/1353 while a document pertaining to neuronal differentiation of BM- MSC, with detailed, extensive, evidence as to the neuronal phenotype is C12N5/0619 , C12N2506/1353 . In the intermediate situation where a document claims neuronal differentiation with only limited evidence (e.g. one or two markers), it is preferred to use the headgroup C12N5/0618 , especially if the document additionally provides examples of oligodendrocyte/glial differentiation at the same poor level of evidence.  The head group should be used only for specific vertebrate cells which do not fit elsewhere in the scheme. Generic disclosure for vertebrate cells is C12N5/00 (and subgroups).  This is actually "embryonic or foetal cells and tissues", but interpreted in a very restricted sense to encompass only cells that are solely embryonic and/or foetal; embryonic/foetal cells of recognisable type which are also present in an adult are classified as adult cells. "Rejuvenated" cells claimed to have been brought back to pluripotency or cells described by an applicant as "ES-like" do not qualify for C12N5/0606 : These cells do not actually originate from an embryo or foetus, see C12N5/0607 (A). In practice, C12N5/0603 itself only contains embryoid bodies and cells being in an intermediate stage of differentiation between pluripotent cells (ES) and "adult" (typable) tissue; e.g. "definitive endoderm" cells C12N5/0603 (exists only in embryo), pancreatic cells C12N5/0676 (adult). Embryonic germ (EG) cells, which can be regarded as an equivalent for ES, are C12N5/0611 , but embryonic carcinoma (EC) cells are classified in C12N5/0606 as far as they are used as a model for ES (now that human ES have been isolated, EC technology appears to be obsolete).  Pluripotent adult stem cells are still a controversial and largely speculative topic. Extreme care must be exercised when allocating this class: to qualify, a cell must have demonstrated pluripotency (note that the corresponding examples of differentiation into at least two distinct lineages are not to be indexed) and must not belong to any other subgroup of the scheme.  This covers all kinds of (demonstrated) "rejuvenated" cells or induced pluripotent stem cells (iPS). The rejuvenation method/agents are to classified appropriately ( C12N2510/00 and C12N2501/60 for typical iPS obtained by forced expression of Oct-3/4, Sox-2, Klf4, Nanog, cMyc…; C12N2501/00 , C12N2500/00 , C12N2502/00 as appropriate for chemical agents). Detailed aspects regarding the construction of a suitable expression vector are to be classified in C12N15/00 .  The head group is to remain empty until a third mammalian sex is discovered or engineered. The few documents dealing both with oocytes and spermatozoa are classified in both subgroups C12N5/0609 and C12N5/061 .  For practical reasons, precursors (myoblasts) have been grouped with their progeny (myotubes); as a result, only few documents remain in C12N5/0659 (satellite cells).  Owing to the complexity of the lineage, committed precursors are grouped with their immediate progeny: e.g. pre-T cells go with T cells in C12N5/0636 ; lymphoid stem cells, which can give rise either to B or T, remain in C12N5/0647 .  Vaccines: Immunogenic preparations (e.g. stimulated T cells, antigen-loaded dendritic cells or microphages) are classified in A61K39/00 and subgroups according to the antigen with the appropriate A61K2039/51 - A61K2039/892 Indexing Code(s).  Immunotherapy: Cells classified in C12N5/0634 - C12N5/0639 are additionally classified in A61K40/00 - A61K40/50 with Orthogonal indexing codes A61K2239/00 - A61K2239/59 , when the cells are modified for immunotherapy (e.g. CAR-T cells).  C12N5/0629 contains purified keratinocytes and whole skin biopsies, assigned to what is regarded as the most relevant cell type, but artificially reconstructed skin is C12N5/0698 .  Encapsulation is assimilated to 3D culture. C12N2533/00 Orthogonal indexing codes may apply to the capsule material.  For practical reasons, this group contains isolated vascular smooth muscle cells (not C12N5/0661 ) as well as elaborated three-dimensional constructs comprising multiple types (e.g. vascular endothelium C12N5/069 , fibroblasts C12N5/0656 ); in the latter case, the further cell types may be indexed with C12N2502/00 Orthogonal Indexing codes as in C12N5/0697 .  Haemangioblasts, which are precursors for both the haematopoietic and the vascular endothelial lineages, always get a double class: C12N5/0647 , C12N5/0692 .  Tumour cells considered for themselves (i.e. as tumours) are classified here, but tumour cells used as convenient immortalised equivalents/models of their untransformed counterparts are classified as normal cells: e.g. embryonic carcinoma C12N5/0606 ; medium specially adapted for hepatomas C12N5/067 , C12N2500/00 , C12N2501/00 , C12N2502/00 , C12N2503/00 , C12N2506/00 , C12N2509/00 .  Myeloma cell lines for use in the making of hybridomas are directly classified in C12N5/163 , with their application, rather than as C12N5/0694 .  "Tissue equivalent" is to be construed broadly as any in vitro construct associating different cell types to achieve one function; the precise structure or function of an actual tissue need not be achieved but it must be an artificial construct, not an actual tissue explant ( C12N5/0602 and subgroups, according to the source or dominant cell type), and no single cell type must be responsible for the desired effect, as is the case for a typical co-culture. Examples: model of neuromuscular junction where a neuron commands a muscle cell C12N5/0697 , C12N2502/081 , C12N2502/1335 ; testicular prosthesis consisting of chondrocytes (for shape) and of Sertoli cells (for hormonal function), C12N5/0697 , C12N2502/1317 , C12N2502/24 ; but embryonic stem cells on a feeder layer of embryonic fibroblast C12N5/0606 , C12N2502/13 . C12N2502/00 codes are used to index all cell types, including accessory ones (e.g. vascular cells or tumour cells added in some illustrated embodiments to study vascular or tumour growth inside a tissue).  Not to be used. Genetic engineering itself is classified in C12N15/00 ; cells modified for a particular application (e.g. recombinant expression, promoter-reporter constructs for testing) are classified with the application ( C07K14/00 - C07K14/16 , G01N33/00 , C12Q1/00 ). Classification in C12N5/00 is done only if there is actual interest in the cell itself; e.g. transfecting pdx1 in MSC to yield insulin-secreting cells (assimilated to pancreatic delta cells, according to the sought therapeutic effect, even though full differentiation and full functionality may not be achieved) C12N5/0676 , C12N2506/1353 , C12N2510/00 .  Has only been used for extremely rare cases of generic fused cell technology applicable equally to animal, vegetal, fungal (yeast) or bacterial cells.  Fusion partners are not indexed. C12N5/163 covers also cell lines to be used as fusion partners, but not specific hybridomas producing a specific antibody.  Some "rejuvenated" cells prepared by introducing "young" cytoplasm into "old" cells, or even by transferring "old" nuclei into enucleated "young" cells" have been assimilated to fused cells and classified here. See also Orthogonal Indexing code C12N2506/00 . Note that nuclear transfer in itself is C12N15/873 and circulation is warranted. Proper cloning is not classified here.  Immunotherapy: Cells classified in C12N5/0634 - C12N5/0639 are additionally classified in A61K40/00 - A61K40/50 with indexing codes A61K2239/00 - A61K2239/59 , when the cells are modified for immunotherapy (e.g. CAR-T cells).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Plants reproduction by tissue culture techniques -> A01H4/00
[C12N 5/0006] Modification of the membrane of cells, e.g. cell decoration -- C12N 5/0006 pertains to methods that alter the outer membrane of undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells, including the attachment, insertion or modification of molecules on the cell surface to change its properties, such as labeling, targeting or functionalization.
[C12N 5/0012] Cell encapsulation -- Cell encapsulation refers to the practice of enclosing living cells within a semipermeable polymeric or inorganic matrix to isolate them from the surrounding environment while allowing exchange of nutrients, waste and signaling molecules. This technique is used to protect cells from immune rejection, control their micro-environment, and enable sustained delivery of therapeutic cells or bioactive products.
[C12N 5/0018] Culture media for cell or tissue culture  media for specific animal cell type  C12N5/06 -- C12N 5/0018 designates compositions of culture media that are specifically formulated for the growth of particular animal cell types, including the required nutrients, growth factors, buffering agents and other additives optimised for that cell line. The classification is used for patents describing specialised cell-culture media in the fields of biotechnology, cell biology and tissue engineering.
[C12N 5/0025] Culture media for plant cell or plant tissue culture -- C12N 5/0025 covers media formulations used to grow undifferentiated plant cells or tissues in vitro. The classification falls under chemistry > biochemistry > microorganisms or enzymes > undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells. It includes nutrient compositions, growth regulators and additives that support plant cell proliferation and differentiation.
[C12N 5/0031] Serum-free culture media -- C12N 5/0031 designates serum-free culture media used for growing undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells, including cell lines, without animal-derived serum. Such formulations supply essential nutrients, growth factors and hormones to support cell proliferation while avoiding serum-related variability and contamination.
[C12N 5/0037] Serum-free medium, which may still contain naturally-sourced components -- This subclass relates to culture media formulated without animal serum but still containing naturally derived components such as plant extracts, growth factors, hormones or other biomolecules, designed to support the growth and maintenance of undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells.
[C12N 5/0043] Medium free of human- or animal-derived components -- The CPC subclass C12N 5/0043 covers media formulations for culturing undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells that contain no components derived from human or animal sources. Such serum-free or chemically defined media are used to reduce variability, avoid contamination and meet regulatory requirements in biotechnology and cell-therapy processes.
[C12N 5/005] Protein-free medium -- Protein-free medium refers to a cell-culture formulation that contains no animal-derived proteins, providing a defined, serum-free environment for the growth of undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells, such as cell lines. It is used to reduce variability, avoid contamination with animal components, and support research or biotechnological applications requiring defined nutritional conditions.
[C12N 5/0056] Xeno-free medium -- The CPC subclass C12N 5/0056 covers cell-culture media formulated without any animal-derived (xenogeneic) components, intended for the growth of undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells such as cell lines. It includes defined, serum-free or chemically defined media that provide all necessary nutrients while eliminating the risk of contamination from foreign species.
[C12N 5/0062] General methods for three-dimensional culture -- C12N5/0062 covers general techniques for cultivating undifferentiated cells in three-dimensional formats, including the formation of spheroids, organoids and scaffold-based constructs. The code addresses methods that enable cells to grow, differentiate or maintain function in a 3-D environment, applicable to human, animal or plant cell lines.
[C12N 5/0068] General culture methods using substrates  for specific animal cell type  C12N5/06 -- The CPC subclass C12N5/06 covers general methods for culturing specific animal cell types on selected substrates, such as surface coatings, scaffolds or media additives that promote cell adhesion, growth and differentiation, and is part of the biotechnology group dealing with undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells.
[C12N 5/0075] using microcarriers -- The code C12N 5/0075 covers biotechnological techniques that employ micro-carrier particles to support the growth or cultivation of undifferentiated cells such as animal, human or plant cell lines. It includes processes for attaching, expanding or harvesting these cells on solid or coated micro-carriers in suspension cultures, and related methods for improving cell viability and productivity.
[C12N 5/0081] Purging biological preparations of unwanted cells -- The subclass covers methods for eliminating unwanted or contaminating cells from biological preparations such as cell cultures, tissues or therapeutic products. Techniques include selective killing, sorting or depletion to obtain purified cell populations for research, diagnostics or clinical use.
[C12N 5/0087] Purging against subsets of blood cells, e.g. purging alloreactive T cells -- C12N 5/0087 covers methods for selectively removing particular blood-cell subsets from cell preparations, such as the elimination of alloreactive T cells to reduce immune reactions in transfusion or transplantation. Techniques may include immunomagnetic separation, antibody-mediated depletion, or other purification processes applied to undifferentiated human, animal or plant cell lines.
[C12N 5/0093] Purging against cancer cells -- The subclass C12N 5/0093 covers methods for eliminating cancer cells from populations of undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells, such as cell lines, using selective killing, separation or other purification techniques to purge malignant cells.
[C12N 5/04] Plant cells or tissues -- C12N 5/04 covers biotechnological inventions involving plant cells or tissues, such as the isolation, cultivation, and manipulation of undifferentiated plant cell lines. The code is used for patents concerning plant cell culture, regeneration, transformation, and related processes in the field of biochemistry and microbiology.
[C12N 5/06] Animal cells or tissues; Human cells or tissues  preservation of excised living parts  A01N1/10
[C12N 5/0601] Invertebrate cells or tissues, e.g. insect cells; Culture media therefor -- C12N 5/0601 relates to the use of culture media specifically formulated for invertebrate cells or tissues, such as insect cell lines. It includes the composition, preparation and supplementation of media that support the growth, maintenance or differentiation of these non-vertebrate biological materials.
[C12N 5/0602] Vertebrate cells -- C12N 5/0602 pertains to inventions involving vertebrate cells, such as mammalian, avian or other vertebrate cell lines used in biotechnological, medical or research applications. The classification covers methods of obtaining, culturing, modifying or employing these cells for purposes ranging from protein production to drug screening and therapeutic development.
[C12N 5/0603] Embryonic cells  production of embryos, nuclear transfer  A01K67/027 ; Embryoid bodies -- C12N 5/0603 covers methods for producing embryonic cells, including creating embryos by nuclear transfer and forming embryoid bodies from undifferentiated human, animal or plant cell lines. It addresses biotechnological processes that manipulate early-stage cells for research or therapeutic purposes, falling under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → undifferentiated cells.
[C12N 5/0604] Whole embryos; Culture medium therefor -- The CPC subclass C12N 5/0604 pertains to inventions involving whole embryos, including methods for producing, handling, or utilizing embryos, and the composition of media used to culture them. It covers embryo culture systems, formulations, and related procedures.
[C12N 5/0605] Cells from extra-embryonic tissues, e.g. placenta, amnion, yolk sac, Wharton's jelly -- C12N 5/0605 covers cells obtained from extra-embryonic sources such as placenta, amnion, yolk sac and Wharton's jelly. These are undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells derived from tissues that support embryonic development. The code is used to classify inventions involving isolation, culture or use of such cells.
[C12N 5/0606] Pluripotent embryonic cells, e.g. embryonic stem cells [ES]  embryonic germ cells  C12N5/0611 , induced pluripotent stem cells  C12N5/0696 -- The CPC subclass C12N 5/0606 covers pluripotent embryonic cells, such as embryonic stem cells and embryonic germ cells, together with induced pluripotent stem cells derived from undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells, including cell lines. It pertains to the preparation, manipulation and use of these cells for research, therapeutic and biotechnological applications.
[C12N 5/0607] Non-embryonic pluripotent stem cells, e.g. MASC  induced pluripotent stem cells  C12N5/0696 -- C12N 5/0607 covers non-embryonic pluripotent stem cells, such as multipotent adult stem cells (MASC) and induced pluripotent stem cells, which are derived from differentiated somatic cells and retain the ability to differentiate into multiple cell types. The subclass deals with the preparation, characterization, and use of these cells in biotechnological and therapeutic applications.
[C12N 5/0608] Germ cells  production of embryos, nuclear transfer  A01K67/027
[C12N 5/0609] Oocytes, oogonia  fertilised oocytes  C12N5/0604 -- The CPC subclass C12N 5/0609 covers biotechnological processes and applications involving oocytes, oogonia and fertilised oocytes, including their preparation, manipulation and use in cell-culture or genetic engineering. It falls under chemistry-biochemistry research on undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells such as cell lines. This category is relevant to reproductive biology, assisted-reproduction technologies and related research on early-stage germ cells.
[C12N 5/061] Sperm cells, spermatogonia -- This CPC subclass covers sperm cells and spermatogonia, the male germ cells used in reproductive biology and biotechnology. It includes methods for isolation, culture, genetic modification and applications such as fertilization, breeding and medical research.
[C12N 5/0611] Primordial germ cells, e.g. embryonic germ cells [EG] -- This subclass covers the use or manipulation of primordial germ cells, including embryonic germ cells, which are undifferentiated cells capable of giving rise to gametes. It pertains to biotechnological methods involving these early-stage cells for research, breeding, or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 5/0612] sorting of gametes, e.g. according to sex or motility -- C12N 5/0612 covers biotechnological methods for separating or sorting gametes, such as sperm or eggs, according to characteristics like sex or motility, enabling selective use in breeding, assisted reproduction or research on fertilization processes.
[C12N 5/0613] Cells from endocrine organs  pancreas  C12N5/0676 , gonads  C12N5/0681 -- C12N 5/0613 pertains to the use of undifferentiated cells from humans, animals or plants, including established cell lines. The sub-entries C12N5/0676 and C12N5/0681 focus on cells taken from endocrine organs, specifically pancreatic cells and gonadal cells. These categories are used to classify inventions involving the isolation, culture, genetic manipulation or therapeutic application of such cells.
[C12N 5/0614] Adrenal gland
[C12N 5/0615] Pineal gland -- C12N 5/0615 covers biotechnological inventions involving the pineal gland, including its isolation, cultivation, or use in undifferentiated cell lines of human, animal or plant origin. The classification is applied to processes, compositions or genetic modifications that exploit pineal gland tissues or secretions for therapeutic, diagnostic or research purposes.
[C12N 5/0616] Pituitary gland -- C12N 5/0616 covers biotechnological inventions involving the pituitary gland, such as the isolation, culture, modification or use of pituitary tissue or cells, including undifferentiated human, animal or plant cell lines derived from this gland, for diagnostic, therapeutic or research purposes. It falls under the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy for microorganisms or enzymes and addresses applications where pituitary-derived material is engineered or employed as a biological tool.
[C12N 5/0617] Thyroid and parathyroid glands -- C12N 5/0617 covers biotechnological methods and applications involving undifferentiated cells derived from the thyroid or parathyroid glands, such as their isolation, culture, genetic modification, and use in diagnostic or therapeutic contexts.
[C12N 5/0618] Cells of the nervous system -- C12N 5/0618 designates inventions that involve cells of the nervous system, such as neurons, glial cells or derived cell lines, used in biotechnological or diagnostic applications. The code falls under the CPC hierarchy CHEMISTRY - BIOCHEMISTRY - MICROORGANISMS OR ENZYMES - undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells. It covers methods of cultivation, modification and use of neural cells for therapy, screening or research.
[C12N 5/0619] Neurons -- C12N 5/0619 covers inventions dealing with neurons derived from undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells, including methods for producing, cultivating, genetically modifying, or otherwise handling such neuronal cells, as well as their use in therapeutic, diagnostic or research applications. It falls under the CPC hierarchy CHEMISTRY → BIOCHEMISTRY → MICROORGANISMS OR ENZYMES → Undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells, e.g., cell lines.
[C12N 5/062] Sensory transducers, e.g. photoreceptors; Sensory neurons, e.g. for hearing, taste, smell, pH, touch, temperature, pain -- This subclass covers devices and biological constructs that function as sensory transducers such as photoreceptors and sensory neurons detecting hearing, taste, smell, pH, touch, temperature or pain, especially when engineered in undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells.
[C12N 5/0621] Eye cells, e.g. cornea, iris pigmented cells  photoreceptors  C12N5/062 -- The CPC subclass C12N 5/0621 covers inventions that use undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells specifically derived from eye tissues, such as corneal cells, pigmented iris cells or retinal photoreceptor cells. It lies within the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 5/0622] Glial cells, e.g. astrocytes, oligodendrocytes; Schwann cells -- C12N 5/0622 classifies inventions involving glial cells such as astrocytes, oligodendrocytes and Schwann cells, covering their isolation, culture, genetic modification or use in therapeutic, diagnostic or biotechnological processes.
[C12N 5/0623] Stem cells -- The C12N 5/0623 subclass covers stem cells, which are undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells capable of self-renewal and differentiation into multiple specialized cell types. It includes embryonic, adult and induced pluripotent stem cells and related methods for their isolation, culture, genetic modification and therapeutic or research use.
[C12N 5/0625] Epidermal cells, skin cells; Cells of the oral mucosa -- C12N 5/0625 covers biotechnological inventions involving epidermal and skin cells as well as cells from the oral mucosa. It pertains to the preparation, manipulation or use of these differentiated cell types, including cell lines derived from human, animal or plant sources, for research, therapeutic or diagnostic applications.
[C12N 5/0626] Melanocytes -- C12N 5/0626 covers inventions that involve melanocytes, the pigment-producing cells derived from undifferentiated human, animal or plant cell lines. It includes methods for culturing, genetically modifying, or using these cells in pharmaceutical, cosmetic or diagnostic applications. The code is used for patents relating to research on melanin synthesis, skin pigmentation disorders, and related biotechnological processes.
[C12N 5/0627] Hair cells -- The C12N 5/0627 subclass covers hair cells, the specialised sensory cells of the inner ear, and related biotechnological aspects such as their isolation, culture, genetic modification, or use as cellular models. It applies to inventions involving undifferentiated or derived cell lines that exhibit hair-cell characteristics for research, screening, or therapeutic purposes.
[C12N 5/0628] Hair stem cells; Hair progenitors  mesenchymal stem cells from hair follicles  C12N5/0666 -- C12N 5/0628 classifies inventions relating to hair stem cells and hair-derived progenitor mesenchymal stem cells sourced from hair follicles. The entry covers the isolation, characterization, culture, and use of these undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells for biomedical and cosmetic applications.
[C12N 5/0629] Keratinocytes; Whole skin -- C12N 5/0629 pertains to keratinocytes and whole skin, covering inventions that involve the cultivation, manipulation or use of these cells or intact skin derived from undifferentiated human, animal or plant cell lines for biotechnological, pharmaceutical or cosmetic purposes. This subclass falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells.
[C12N 5/063] Kereatinocyte stem cells; Keratinocyte progenitors -- The code refers to undifferentiated keratinocyte stem cells and their progenitor cells, which are isolated from the epidermis of humans, animals or plants and retain the capacity to self-renew and differentiate into mature skin cells. These cells are central to research on skin regeneration, disease modelling and therapeutic cell-based treatments.
[C12N 5/0631] Mammary cells -- C12N 5/0631 refers to mammary cells, including those derived from human, animal or plant mammary tissue, within the broader class of undifferentiated cells. The code is used to classify inventions involving isolation, culture, manipulation or therapeutic use of mammary epithelial or stem cells in biotechnology and medical applications.
[C12N 5/0632] Cells of the oral mucosa -- This subclass of the CPC covers undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells, specifically cells derived from the oral mucosa, which are employed in biotechnological work such as tissue engineering, drug testing and regenerative medicine. It resides in the hierarchy chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → undifferentiated cells.
[C12N 5/0633] Cells of secretory glands, e.g. parotid gland, salivary glands, sweat glands, lacrymal glands -- C12N 5/0633 designates patents concerning cells derived from secretory glands such as parotid, salivary, sweat, or lacrimal glands. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells, and covers the use, cultivation or manipulation of these glandular cells.
[C12N 5/0634] Cells from the blood or the immune system -- C12N 5/0634 covers cells that originate from blood or the immune system, including peripheral blood mononuclear cells, lymphocytes, macrophages and related cell lines. These cells are used in biotechnological applications such as vaccine production, gene therapy, immunological assays and regenerative medicine. The classification falls under chemistry - biochemistry - microorganisms or enzymes - undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells.
