[C12N 5/565] Pressure processes, e.g. following predefined pressure changes over time -- Group C12N5/565 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group A01N1/165 . Groups A01N1/165 and C12N5/565 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[C12N 5/568] using electromagnetic fields or radiation; using acoustic waves or corpuscular radiation -- Group C12N5/568 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group A01N1/168 . Groups A01N1/168 and C12N5/568 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[C12N 7/00] Viruses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Viruses are ultramicroscopic (20 to 300 nm in diameter), metabolically inert, infectious agents that replicate only within the cells of living hosts, mainly bacteria, plants, and animals: composed of an RNA or DNA core, a protein coat, and, in more complex types, a surrounding envelope.  Note that a virus (or VLP) as such is not considered a nanoparticle even if it falls under the size constraints of nanotechnology (see B82Y5/00 ).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Phage display -> C12N15/1037 - Therapeutic viral non-coding nucleic acid (siRNA) -> C12N15/11 - Antiviral drugs -> A61K31/00 - Antibodies against viral antigens -> C07K16/00 - Diagnostics using viral genes -> C12Q1/70 - Diagnostics using viral protein -> G01N33/569 , G01N33/576
[C12N 7/02] Recovery or purification -- C12N 7/02 pertains to processes for recovering or purifying viruses, including isolation of viral particles, nucleic acids, proteins, or subviral components from biological or culture media. It encompasses techniques such as centrifugation, filtration, precipitation, chromatography, and affinity methods used to obtain pure viral material for research, diagnostics, or pharmaceutical production.
[C12N 7/025] Packaging cell lines, e.g. transcomplementing cell lines, for production of virus -- C12N 7/025 covers the creation and use of packaging cell lines, including transcomplementing cells, that supply missing viral functions to enable the production of recombinant or wild-type viruses. These engineered cells provide viral proteins or genomes in trans, facilitating virus assembly without the need for infectious seed viruses, and are employed in vaccine manufacturing, gene therapy vector production, and virological research.
[C12N 7/04] Inactivation or attenuation -- C12N 7/04 covers techniques for reducing the infectivity of viruses, including chemical, physical, or biological methods that render a virus non-pathogenic while preserving its antigenic properties, often employed in vaccine development or diagnostic preparations.
[C12N 7/045] Pseudoviral particles; Non infectious pseudovirions, e.g. genetically engineered -- C12N 7/045 covers the design, preparation and use of pseudoviral particles that mimic viruses but are non-infectious, typically created through genetic engineering. These non-infectious pseudovirions serve as tools for studying viral entry, delivering genes, or as vaccine and diagnostic platforms.
[C12N 7/06] by chemical treatment -- C12N 7/06 covers biotechnological processes that treat viruses through the application of chemicals, including agents that inactivate, neutralize, or otherwise alter viral particles. It pertains to methods where chemical compounds are used to control or modify viruses, falling under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and viruses.
[C12N 7/08] by serial passage of virus -- C12N 7/08 covers methods in which a virus is adapted or modified through repeated cycles of infection and replication, known as serial passage, to alter its properties such as virulence, host range or stability. These techniques are used in vaccine development, strain improvement and basic virology research.
[C12N 9/00] Enzymes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Catalytic nucleic acids e,g, ribozymes -> C12N15/113 - Antisense nucleotides against enzymes -> C12N15/1137 - Antibodies against enzymes -> C07K16/00 - Measuring or testing processes involving enzymes; Compositions therefore (including kits, test papers etc); Preparing such compositions -> C12Q1/00 - Immunoassays for enzymes -> G01N33/573  **Glossary:** - Antisense: DNA or RNA composed of the complementary sequence to the target DNA/RNA - Enzyme: Proteinaceous materials, which cause a chemical change in a starting material without being consumed in the reaction. - Fusion polypeptide: A polypeptide consisting of (parts of) two or more different proteins covalently linked to each other by a peptide bond - Kit: A collection of individual reagents for use in an assay grouped together but being present as separate entities in separate compartments - Mutation: Any change that alters the sequence of bases along the DNA thereby changing the genetic material of a microorganism. - Non-coding nucleic acid sequence: Nucleic acid sequence which does not contain information for the amino acid sequence of a gene product. - Operon: A DNA construct containing a cluster of genes under the control of a single regulatory signal or promoter - Proenzyme: An enzyme precursor - Ribozyme: RNA molecule capable of catalysing a chemical reaction - Signal sequence: A 3-60 amino acids long peptide that directs the transport of the protein that is attached to it
[C12N 9/0002] Antibodies with enzymatic activity, e.g. abzymes -- The subclass C12N 9/0002 concerns antibodies that possess catalytic activity, often called abzymes. It covers naturally occurring or engineered immunoglobulins that function as enzymes, including their design, characterization and use in biotechnology, diagnostics and therapy.
[C12N 9/0004] Oxidoreductases (1.) -- C12N 9/0004 covers oxidoreductase enzymes, which catalyze oxidation-reduction reactions by transferring electrons between molecules, often involving cofactors such as NAD(P)H. The sub-class applies to biotechnological inventions that exploit these enzymes for synthesis, degradation, or analytical processes.
[C12N 9/0006] acting on CH-OH groups as donors (1.1) -- The subclass C12N 9/0006 groups enzymes that use CH-OH groups as donors, encompassing oxidoreductases such as dehydrogenases that act on primary or secondary alcohols to transfer hydrogen or other groups in biochemical reactions. It falls under the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy for microorganisms or enzymes.
[C12N 9/0008] acting on the aldehyde or oxo group of donors (1.2) -- C12N 9/0008 designates enzymes that catalyze reactions involving the aldehyde or oxo group of a donor substrate. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically enzymes. These biocatalysts modify carbonyl functionalities in metabolic or synthetic processes.
[C12N 9/001] acting on the CH-CH group of donors (1.3) -- C12N 9/001 designates enzymes that catalyse reactions involving the CH-CH group of donor molecules, typically oxidising or reducing carbon-carbon double bonds. This subclass falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Enzymes, covering biocatalytic processes that modify unsaturated carbon frameworks.
[C12N 9/0012] acting on nitrogen containing compounds as donors (1.4, 1.5, 1.6, 1.7) -- C12N 9/0012 covers enzymes that catalyze the transfer of nitrogen-containing groups from donor molecules to acceptors, encompassing reaction types 1.4 to 1.7 such as amidation, amination, nitrile formation and related transformations.
[C12N 9/0014] acting on the CH-NH 2  group of donors (1.4) -- C12N 9/0014 refers to enzymatic reactions that modify substrates bearing a CH-NH2 group, i.e., primary amine donors, by acting on that functional group. The classification includes processes such as oxidation, reduction, transfer or other chemical changes of the CH-NH2 moiety catalyzed by enzymes.
[C12N 9/0016] with NAD or NADP as acceptor (1.4.1) -- C12N 9/0016 classifies enzymes that employ nicotinamide-adenine dinucleotide (NAD) or its phosphate form (NADP) as the electron-acceptor in catalytic reactions, typically encompassing oxidoreductases such as dehydrogenases. The subclass is used to identify biocatalytic processes where these cofactors mediate redox transformations in biochemical pathways or industrial applications.
[C12N 9/0018] Phenylalanine dehydrogenase (1.4.1.20) -- C12N 9/0018 covers phenylalanine dehydrogenase, an enzyme that catalyzes the oxidative deamination of L-phenylalanine to phenylpyruvate with concurrent reduction of NAD(P)+ to NAD(P)H, and is commonly used in biocatalytic processes and amino-acid synthesis.
[C12N 9/002] with a cytochrome as acceptor (1.4.2) -- C12N 9/002 covers enzymatic processes in which a cytochrome molecule functions as the electron-acceptor, corresponding to subclass 1.4.2. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and enzymes, and is used to classify patents describing catalytic reactions that employ cytochromes to receive electrons.
[C12N 9/0022] with oxygen as acceptor (1.4.3) -- C12N 9/0022 designates enzymes that catalyze redox reactions in which molecular oxygen serves as the ultimate electron acceptor, producing water, hydrogen peroxide or other oxygen-derived products. The class includes oxidases, mono-oxygenases and related biocatalysts that incorporate or reduce O₂ during substrate transformation.
[C12N 9/0024] D-Amino acid oxidase (1.4.3.3) -- C12N 9/0024 covers the enzyme D-amino acid oxidase, a flavin-dependent oxidoreductase that catalyzes the oxidative deamination of D-amino acids to their corresponding imino acids, producing hydrogen peroxide and ammonia. The classification is used for patents relating to the production, purification, engineering, and applications of this enzyme in diagnostics, biocatalysis, and therapeutic research.
[C12N 9/0026] acting on CH-NH groups of donors (1.5) -- C12N 9/0026 classifies enzymes that catalyze reactions acting on CH-NH groups of donor molecules, covering amine-forming transformations and related processes. It is a subcategory of the enzyme section within the Cooperative Patent Classification, falling under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and enzymes.
[C12N 9/0028] with NAD or NADP as acceptor (1.5.1) -- C12N 9/0028 is a subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification for biotechnological processes involving enzymes that employ nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD) or its phosphate form (NADP) as the electron-acceptor in redox reactions. It groups inventions where these cofactors serve as the primary oxidizing agent.
[C12N 9/003] Dihydrofolate reductase [DHFR] (1.5.1.3) -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/003 pertains to the enzyme dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), which catalyzes the reduction of dihydrofolic acid to tetrahydrofolic acid in folate metabolism. This classification is used for patents involving DHFR gene sequences, recombinant production, inhibitors, and related diagnostic or therapeutic applications.
[C12N 9/0032] with oxygen as acceptor (1.5.3) -- C12N 9/0032 covers enzymes that catalyze reactions in which molecular oxygen serves as the electron acceptor, encompassing oxidases, monooxygenases and related biocatalytic processes that incorporate or reduce O₂ in biochemical transformations.
[C12N 9/0034] Sarcosine oxidase (1.5.3.1) -- C12N 9/0034 covers sarcosine oxidase, an enzyme that oxidatively deaminates sarcosine to glycine, formaldehyde and hydrogen peroxide. It is used for patents concerning the enzyme's production, purification, immobilisation or application in diagnostics, biocatalysis or environmental remediation.
[C12N 9/0036] acting on NADH or NADPH (1.6) -- C12N 9/0036 designates enzymes that act on the reduced forms of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide, NADH, or its phosphate counterpart, NADPH. It covers biocatalytic processes where these cofactors serve as electron donors or acceptors in oxidation-reduction reactions, including oxidoreductases that specifically use NAD(H) or NADP(H) as substrates.
[C12N 9/0038] with a heme protein as acceptor (1.6.2) -- C12N 9/0038 is a CPC subclass within the chemistry hierarchy for biochemistry, specifically enzymes, covering inventions that employ a heme protein as the electron acceptor in enzymatic reactions. It designates processes or compositions where the heme protein functions as the acceptor in a redox transformation.
[C12N 9/004] Cytochrome-b5 reductase (1.6.2.2) -- C12N 9/004 covers inventions relating to the enzyme cytochrome-b5 reductase, including its isolated gene, polypeptide, recombinant production, purification methods, and uses such as diagnostic assays or biocatalytic processes.
[C12N 9/0042] NADPH-cytochrome P450 reductase (1.6.2.4) -- C12N 9/0042 falls under the CPC hierarchy for enzymes and designates NADPH-cytochrome P450 reductase, a flavoprotein that mediates electron transfer from NADPH to cytochrome P450 enzymes. The enzyme is essential for oxidative metabolism of xenobiotics, steroid biosynthesis and other monooxygenase reactions, and is commonly studied in biotechnology and drug development.
[C12N 9/0044] acting on other nitrogen compounds as donors (1.7) -- C12N 9/0044 designates enzymes that use nitrogen-containing compounds other than the usual amine donors as the source of a nitrogen group in catalytic reactions. The class includes transaminases, aminotransferases and similar nitrogen-transfer enzymes that act on substrates such as nitriles, nitro compounds or heterocyclic nitrogen rings. These enzymes facilitate the transfer of nitrogen atoms to acceptor molecules in biochemical pathways.
[C12N 9/0046] with oxygen as acceptor (1.7.3) -- C12N 9/0046 covers enzymes that catalyze redox reactions in which molecular oxygen serves as the electron-acceptor. These oxidoreductases typically incorporate oxygen into the substrate (as in mono-oxygenases and dioxygenases) or reduce O₂ to water or hydrogen peroxide during the transformation of organic compounds. The subclass is used to classify biocatalysts that employ O₂ as the terminal oxidant in biochemical processes.
[C12N 9/0048] Uricase (1.7.3.3) -- C12N 9/0048 classifies inventions relating to uricase, an enzyme that catalyzes the oxidation of uric acid to allantoin and hydrogen peroxide. The entry covers protein engineering, production, formulation and uses of uricase in medical treatment, diagnostics and industrial processes such as waste remediation. It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → enzymes.
[C12N 9/0051] acting on a sulfur group of donors (1.8) -- The C12N 9/0051 subclass covers enzymatic reactions that act on sulfur-containing donor groups, such as transferring, adding, or removing a sulfhydryl or thio-group from substrates. It includes processes where enzymes modify the sulfur atom of donor molecules in biochemical pathways.
[C12N 9/0053] acting on a heme group of donors (1.9) -- C12N 9/0053 covers chemical or biochemical processes, agents, or modifications that specifically act on the heme group of donor molecules, such as altering its redox state, binding, or structural configuration. It includes enzymes, synthetic compounds, or reaction conditions designed to interact with the iron-porphyrin center of heme-containing substrates.
[C12N 9/0055] acting on diphenols and related substances as donors (1.10) -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/0055 falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Enzymes and designates enzymes that catalyse reactions in which diphenols or structurally related compounds serve as electron donors. These enzymes typically oxidise phenolic substrates, often generating quinones or related oxidised products, and are relevant to biotransformation, biosynthesis and analytical applications.
[C12N 9/0057] with oxygen as acceptor (1.10.3) -- C12N 9/0057 covers enzymes that use molecular oxygen as the electron-acceptor in redox reactions, such as mono-oxygenases and dioxygenases that incorporate one or both atoms of O₂ into substrates. This subclass groups biocatalysts that catalyse oxidation where O₂ serves as the terminal oxidant.
[C12N 9/0059] Catechol oxidase (1.10.3.1), i.e. tyrosinase -- Catechol oxidase, also known as tyrosinase, is an enzyme that catalyzes the oxidation of phenolic compounds such as catechol to o-quinones, a key step in melanin biosynthesis and browning reactions in plants and animals. It belongs to the oxidoreductase family that acts on paired donors with incorporation of molecular oxygen.
[C12N 9/0061] Laccase (1.10.3.2) -- Laccase is a multicopper oxidase enzyme that catalyzes the one-electron oxidation of phenolic and aromatic amine substrates while reducing molecular oxygen to water. It is widely employed in biotechnological processes such as bioremediation, textile bleaching, and biosensor development due to its ability to act on a broad range of aromatic compounds.
[C12N 9/0063] Ascorbate oxidase (1.10.3.3) -- C12N 9/0063 designates the enzyme ascorbate oxidase (EC 1.10.3.3), a copper-containing oxidoreductase that catalyzes the oxidation of ascorbic acid to dehydroascorbic acid, typically produced by microorganisms. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Enzymes.
[C12N 9/0065] acting on hydrogen peroxide as acceptor (1.11) -- C12N 9/0065 designates enzymes that use hydrogen peroxide as the electron acceptor in catalytic reactions, encompassing peroxidases and related biocatalysts that oxidize substrates while reducing H₂O₂ to water.
[C12N 9/0067] acting on hydrogen as donor (1.12) -- The C12N 9/0067 subclass groups enzymes that use hydrogen as an electron donor in catalytic reactions, such as hydrogenases and related biocatalysts that facilitate redox transformations by transferring hydrogen atoms to substrates. It covers biochemical processes where hydrogen serves as the reducing agent.
[C12N 9/0069] acting on single donors with incorporation of molecular oxygen, i.e. oxygenases (1.13) -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/0069 falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → enzymes and covers enzymes that act on a single donor while incorporating molecular oxygen, i.e., oxygenases that catalyse oxidative reactions using O₂ as the oxidant.
[C12N 9/0071] acting on paired donors with incorporation of molecular oxygen (1.14) -- Enzymes that use two donor substrates and molecular oxygen to insert an oxygen atom into one of the donors, the other oxygen atom being reduced to water. This class includes mono-oxygenases, dioxygenases and other oxidative enzymes that catalyse reactions of the type "paired donors + O₂ → oxidized product + H₂O". These biocatalysts typically perform hydroxylations, epoxidations, desaturations or ring-forming oxidations.
[C12N 9/0073] with NADH or NADPH as one donor, and incorporation of one atom of oxygen 1.14.13 -- This CPC subclass covers enzymatic reactions in which NADH or NADPH functions as the electron donor and exactly one atom of oxygen is incorporated into the substrate, corresponding to EC 1.14.13 oxidoreductases. It is situated within the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and enzymes.
[C12N 9/0075] Nitric-oxide synthase (1.14.13.39) -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/0075 covers nitric-oxide synthase enzymes, which catalyse the conversion of L-arginine to nitric oxide using NADPH and molecular oxygen. These enzymes belong to oxidoreductases that incorporate oxygen into two substrates, classified under EC 1.14.13.39. Patents in this group relate to medical, diagnostic or biotechnological applications of nitric-oxide synthase.
[C12N 9/0077] with a reduced iron-sulfur protein as one donor (1.14.15) -- The subclass C12N 9/0077 covers biocatalytic processes in which a reduced iron-sulfur protein acts as the electron-donor substrate, enabling redox transformations such as those found in nitrogen fixation, hydrogenase activity or other enzymatic reactions that require iron-sulfur clusters as cofactors.
[C12N 9/0079] Steroid 11 beta monooxygenase (P-450 protein)(1.14.15.4) -- This CPC subclass covers the cytochrome P450 enzyme that catalyzes the 11-beta-hydroxylation of steroid substrates, classified as EC 1.14.15.4. The enzyme is a monooxygenase used by microorganisms for steroid biotransformation, often exploited in pharmaceutical synthesis and metabolic engineering.
[C12N 9/0081] Cholesterol monooxygenase (cytochrome P 450scc)(1.14.15.6) -- C12N 9/0081 covers cholesterol monooxygenase, the cytochrome P450 scc enzyme that catalyzes the side-chain cleavage of cholesterol to form pregnenolone, corresponding to EC 1.14.15.6. The classification groups this specific monooxygenase within the broader category of enzymes acting on microorganisms or enzymes in biochemistry.
[C12N 9/0083] Miscellaneous (1.14.99) -- C12N 9/0083 refers to enzyme-related subject matter that does not fit into any more specific enzyme subclass, covering miscellaneous inventions such as novel enzyme preparations, unconventional applications, or unique processes involving enzymes that are not otherwise classified.
[C12N 9/0085] Steroid 17 alpha-monooxygenase (1.14.99.9) -- C12N 9/0085 designates enzymes that catalyse the 17-alpha hydroxylation of steroid substrates, specifically the steroid 17-alpha-monooxygenase classified under EC 1.14.99.9. This monooxygenase is a microbial enzyme that introduces an oxygen atom into the 17-alpha position of steroids, typically using reduced cofactors and molecular oxygen.
[C12N 9/0087] Steroid 21-monooxygenase (1.14.99.10) -- Steroid 21-monooxygenase (EC 1.14.99.10) is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the hydroxylation of the C-21 position of steroid substrates, incorporating one atom of molecular oxygen while reducing the other to water. It is typically produced by microorganisms and is employed in biocatalytic processes for the synthesis of hydroxylated steroids used in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals. This enzyme falls under the CPC class C12N 9/0087, covering enzymes acting on steroids.
[C12N 9/0089] acting on superoxide as acceptor (1.15) -- C12N 9/0089 classifies enzymes that employ superoxide as the electron acceptor in catalytic reactions. It includes superoxide-reducing or superoxide-utilizing enzymes such as superoxide reductases and related biocatalysts.
[C12N 9/0091] oxidizing metal ions (1.16) -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/0091 relates to enzymes that catalyze the oxidation of metal ions, describing biocatalytic processes where metals serve as substrates or cofactors. It includes enzymatic conversion such as Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺ and the oxidation-based detoxification of heavy metals.
[C12N 9/0093] acting on CH or CH 2  groups (1.17) -- C12N 9/0093 designates enzymes that catalyze chemical transformations of aliphatic carbon atoms bearing a single hydrogen (CH) or a methylene group (CH2). Typical reactions include oxidation, reduction, or substitution at these positions, often employed in synthesis of fine chemicals, pharmaceuticals, or bioconversions. The subclass falls under microorganisms or enzymes within biotechnology.
[C12N 9/0095] acting on iron-sulfur proteins as donor (1.18) -- The C12N 9/0095 subclass covers enzymes that use iron-sulfur proteins as electron donors in catalytic reactions, such as redox processes involving ferredoxins or related iron-sulfur clusters. It includes methods and compositions where the enzyme transfers electrons from these protein donors to substrates, enabling biochemical transformations. This classification is used for patents on biocatalysis and metabolic pathways that rely on iron-sulfur protein donors.
[C12N 9/0097] acting on reduced flavodoxin as donor (1.19) -- The code C12N 9/0097 covers enzymatic reactions in which reduced flavodoxin serves as the electron donor. It includes processes where enzymes transfer electrons from flavodoxin to substrates for biosynthetic or degradative purposes.
[C12N 9/10] Transferases (2.) -- C12N 9/10 designates enzymes that act as transferases, catalyzing the transfer of a specific functional group from a donor molecule to an acceptor. The subclass includes biocatalytic processes where the transferred group may be a methyl, glycosyl, phosphoryl, or other moiety, and it is applied to microbial or recombinant enzyme systems. This code lies within the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → enzymes.
[C12N 9/1003] transferring one-carbon groups (2.1) -- C12N 9/1003 covers enzymatic processes that transfer one-carbon groups such as methyl, formyl or methylene units between molecules. The classification includes natural and engineered enzymes that catalyze these transfers, commonly used in metabolic pathways, biocatalysis and synthetic biology, and falls within the subgroup of enzymes acting on microorganisms or isolated enzymes.
[C12N 9/1007] Methyltransferases (general) (2.1.1.) -- C12N 9/1007 covers patents concerning methyltransferases, enzymes that catalyse the transfer of a methyl group from a donor to an acceptor molecule. It includes both natural and engineered methyltransferases, their structures, mechanisms, production methods and uses in fields such as drug synthesis, biotech and industrial chemistry.
[C12N 9/1011] Catechol O-methyltransferase (2.1.1.6) -- C12N 9/1011 relates to the enzyme catechol O-methyltransferase, a transferase (EC 2.1.1.6) that catalyzes the methylation of catechol compounds using S-adenosyl-L-methionine as the methyl donor. The classification covers the protein, its variants, production methods, and applications in drug metabolism and biosynthesis.
[C12N 9/1014] Hydroxymethyl-, formyl-transferases (2.1.2) -- C12N 9/1014 covers enzymes that catalyze the transfer of hydroxymethyl or formyl groups between molecules. These transferases belong to subclass 2.1.2 and are used in biosynthetic and degradative pathways involving one-carbon units. Applications include the synthesis of specialized metabolites and biotechnological processes that modify nucleic acids or proteins.
[C12N 9/1018] Carboxy- and carbamoyl transferases (2.1.3) -- C12N 9/1018 covers enzymes that catalyze the transfer of carboxyl or carbamoyl groups between molecules, including carboxy-transferases and carbamoyl-transferases. Typical examples are aspartate transcarbamoylase, carbamoyl-phosphate synthase and other enzymes that move these functional groups in metabolic pathways.
[C12N 9/1022] transferring aldehyde or ketonic groups (2.2) -- C12N 9/1022 covers enzymes that catalyze the transfer of aldehyde or ketonic groups from a donor to an acceptor molecule, encompassing reactions such as aldehyde-transferases, ketone-transferases and related biotransformations used in metabolic pathways and synthetic applications.
[C12N 9/1025] Acyltransferases (2.3) -- C12N 9/1025 designates enzymes that catalyse the transfer of acyl groups from a donor to an acceptor molecule, covering acyltransferases of the 2.3 subclass. The classification is applied to biocatalytic processes involving lipid, peptide or carbohydrate modification by acyl group attachment. It is used to index inventions related to the production, engineering or use of such acyltransferase enzymes.
[C12N 9/1029] transferring groups other than amino-acyl groups (2.3.1) -- C12N 9/1029 pertains to enzymes that catalyse the transfer of non-amino-acyl groups such as sugars, phosphates or other small moieties between molecules. It falls within the broader class of microbial or biochemical enzymes and covers reactions where the transferred group is not an amino-acyl residue.
[C12N 9/1033] Chloramphenicol O-acetyltransferase (2.3.1.28) -- Chloramphenicol O-acetyltransferase (EC 2.3.1.28) is an enzyme that transfers an acetyl group from acetyl-CoA to the antibiotic chloramphenicol, inactivating it and conferring bacterial resistance. It belongs to the group of transferases acting on carboxylic acid derivatives and is classified under C12N 9/1033 for enzymes that modify antimicrobial agents.
[C12N 9/1037] Naringenin-chalcone synthase (2.3.1.74), i.e. chalcone synthase -- C12N 9/1037 covers the enzyme naringenin-chalcone synthase (EC 2.3.1.74), a type-III polyketide synthase that catalyzes the condensation of a coumaroyl-CoA starter with three malonyl-CoA units to produce naringenin chalcone, the first committed step in flavonoid biosynthesis. This reaction is central to the formation of plant pigments, phytoalexins and other secondary metabolites.
[C12N 9/104] Aminoacyltransferases (2.3.2) -- Aminoacyltransferases are enzymes that catalyze the transfer of an aminoacyl group from a donor molecule to an acceptor, typically forming peptide bonds in protein synthesis or modifying other biomolecules. They play a key role in attaching amino acids to tRNAs, creating peptidyl intermediates, and can also participate in biosynthetic pathways that remodel peptides or other substrates. This class falls under the CPC sub-group C12N 9/104.
[C12N 9/1044] Protein-glutamine gamma-glutamyltransferase (2.3.2.13), i.e. transglutaminase or factor XIII -- C12N 9/1044 covers protein-glutamine gamma-glutamyltransferases, commonly known as transglutaminases or factor XIII, enzymes that catalyze the formation of ε-(γ-glutamyl)lysine cross-links between protein substrates by transferring an amide group from glutamine residues. These enzymes are important in blood coagulation, tissue repair and various industrial applications.
[C12N 9/1048] Glycosyltransferases (2.4) -- C12N 9/1048 designates enzymes that catalyze the transfer of glycosyl groups, known as glycosyltransferases, which form glycosidic bonds by moving a sugar unit from an activated donor to an acceptor molecule. This subclass falls under the chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes hierarchy and groups together all such transferases involved in carbohydrate biosynthesis and modification.
[C12N 9/1051] Hexosyltransferases (2.4.1) -- Hexosyltransferases (EC 2.4.1) are enzymes that catalyse the transfer of a hexose moiety from a donor molecule, typically a nucleotide-sugar, to an acceptor such as another carbohydrate, protein or lipid, forming glycosidic bonds. They play key roles in carbohydrate metabolism, cell-wall biosynthesis and glycosylation pathways in microorganisms, enabling the construction and modification of diverse glycoconjugates.
[C12N 9/1055] Levansucrase (2.4.1.10) -- Levansucrase (EC 2.4.1.10) is a glycosyl-transferase that catalyzes the transfer of fructosyl units from sucrose to growing β-(2→6)-linked fructan chains, producing levan and glucose. The enzyme is secreted by various bacteria and fungi and is exploited in food, pharmaceutical and biotechnological applications for synthesizing high-molecular-weight fructans.
[C12N 9/1059] Cellulose synthases (2.4.1.12; 2.4.1.29) -- C12N 9/1059 designates cellulose synthases, glycosyltransferases that catalyze the polymerization of glucose residues to produce cellulose. These enzymes, classified under EC 2.4.1.12 and EC 2.4.1.29, are essential for plant cell wall formation and are also found in certain bacteria and algae.
[C12N 9/1062] Sucrose synthase (2.4.1.13) -- C12N 9/1062 covers the enzyme sucrose synthase (EC 2.4.1.13), which catalyzes the reversible transfer of a glucosyl unit from sucrose to UDP, producing UDP-glucose and fructose. The classification applies to biocatalytic processes, recombinant production, and functional variants of this carbohydrate-metabolising enzyme.
[C12N 9/1066] Sucrose phosphate synthase (2.4.1.14) -- Sucrose-phosphate synthase (EC 2.4.1.14) is an enzyme that catalyzes the transfer of a glucose unit from UDP-glucose to fructose-6-phosphate, forming sucrose-6-phosphate and UDP. It belongs to the glycosyltransferase family involved in carbohydrate biosynthesis in microorganisms and plants, and is classified under CPC code C12N 9/1066 for enzymes acting on sugars.
[C12N 9/107] 1,4-Alpha-glucan branching enzyme (2.4.1.18) -- C12N 9/107 designates the 1,4-alpha-glucan branching enzyme (EC 2.4.1.18), which catalyzes the transfer of a segment of a linear α-1,4-glucan to create an α-1,6 branch, modifying glycogen or amylopectin structure. The enzyme is used in microbial biotechnology and industrial starch processing.
[C12N 9/1074] Cyclomaltodextrin glucanotransferase (2.4.1.19) -- Cyclomaltodextrin glucanotransferase (EC 2.4.1.19) is an enzyme that catalyzes the intramolecular transfer of glucosyl residues in starch, producing cyclomaltodextrins of various ring sizes. It belongs to the carbohydrate-active enzymes group used in food, pharmaceutical and industrial applications for modifying polysaccharides. The CPC entry C12N 9/1074 places it under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically enzymes.
[C12N 9/1077] Pentosyltransferases (2.4.2) -- C12N 9/1077 covers enzymes that catalyze the transfer of pentose residues from donor molecules to acceptors, forming glycosidic bonds. These pentosyltransferases belong to the broader class of transferases (EC 2.4.2) and are relevant in carbohydrate metabolism and nucleic-acid biosynthesis in microorganisms.
[C12N 9/1081] transferring other glycosyl groups (2.4.99) -- C12N 9/1081 covers enzymes that catalyze the transfer of non-standard glycosyl residues from donor molecules to acceptors, encompassing glycosyltransferases classified under EC 2.4.99. These biocatalysts are used in the synthesis of diverse glycoconjugates, often derived from microorganisms, and are relevant for drug development, biotechnology, and carbohydrate chemistry.
[C12N 9/1085] transferring alkyl or aryl groups other than methyl groups (2.5) -- C12N 9/1085 covers enzymatic methods that transfer alkyl or aryl groups larger than a methyl group to a substrate, such as prenyl, butyl or phenyl residues, typically involving transferases that catalyze these specific group-addition reactions. This subclass is used to classify biocatalytic processes and engineered enzymes that effect non-methyl alkyl/aryl group transfers in chemical synthesis or metabolic pathways.
[C12N 9/1088] Glutathione transferase (2.5.1.18) -- C12N 9/1088 denotes enzymatic preparations classified under glutathione transferases, which catalyze the conjugation of glutathione to a variety of electrophilic substrates (EC 2.5.1.18). The code falls within the hierarchy of Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Enzymes, covering biotechnological applications, production methods, and uses of these detoxifying enzymes.
[C12N 9/1092] 3-Phosphoshikimate 1-carboxyvinyltransferase (2.5.1.19), i.e. 5-enolpyruvylshikimate-3-phosphate synthase -- This classification covers enzymes that catalyze the transfer of a vinyl group from phosphoenolpyruvate to shikimate-3-phosphate, forming 5-enolpyruvylshikimate-3-phosphate, a key step in the shikimate pathway for aromatic amino acid biosynthesis. It corresponds to EC 2.5.1.19 and is commonly targeted by glyphosate herbicides.
[C12N 9/1096] transferring nitrogenous groups (2.6) -- C12N 9/1096 pertains to enzymes that catalyze the transfer of nitrogen-containing groups such as amino, amido or nitrile fragments from a donor to an acceptor molecule. The class includes transaminases, amidotransferases and related biocatalysts used in synthesis, degradation or modification of organic compounds.
[C12N 9/12] transferring phosphorus containing groups, e.g. kinases (2.7) -- C12N 9/12 denotes enzymes that catalyse the transfer of phosphorus-containing groups, commonly referred to as kinases. The subclass includes biocatalytic processes in which a phosphate moiety is moved from a donor molecule to an acceptor, encompassing various phosphorylation reactions.
[C12N 9/1205] Phosphotransferases with an alcohol group as acceptor (2.7.1), e.g. protein kinases -- C12N 9/1205 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to enzymes that catalyse the transfer of a phosphate group to an alcohol moiety, corresponding to EC 2.7.1, and includes protein kinases. It falls under the hierarchy CHEMISTRY → BIOCHEMISTRY → MICROORGANISMS OR ENZYMES → Enzymes, and is used to classify inventions involving phosphorylation reactions of alcohol-containing substrates.
[C12N 9/1211] Thymidine kinase (2.7.1.21) -- C12N 9/1211 designates thymidine kinase, an enzyme (EC 2.7.1.21) that catalyzes the transfer of a phosphate group to thymidine to form thymidine-monophosphate, a key step in nucleic-acid metabolism. It falls under the chemistry hierarchy of biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → enzymes, and is used to classify patents concerning the production, purification, variants, or applications of this kinase.
[C12N 9/1217] Phosphotransferases with a carboxyl group as acceptor (2.7.2) -- C12N 9/1217 covers enzymes classified as phosphotransferases that transfer a phosphate group to a carboxyl group on the acceptor molecule, corresponding to the EC subclass 2.7.2. These enzymes catalyze the formation of phospho-esters on carboxyl residues, such as the conversion of acetate to acetyl-phosphate, and are relevant in metabolic pathways involving energy transfer and biosynthesis.
[C12N 9/1223] Phosphotransferases with a nitrogenous group as acceptor (2.7.3) -- C12N 9/1223 is a CPC subclass under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Enzymes that designates phosphotransferases in which the phosphate donor is transferred to a nitrogen-containing acceptor. It includes enzymes such as kinases that phosphorylate amines, ammonium ions or other nitrogen-bearing groups.
[C12N 9/1229] Phosphotransferases with a phosphate group as acceptor (2.7.4) -- C12N 9/1229 designates enzymes that catalyze the transfer of a phosphoryl group to another phosphate group, i.e., phosphotransferases whose acceptor is a phosphate. It includes kinases that phosphorylate nucleotides, diphosphates, polyphosphates and related compounds.
[C12N 9/1235] Diphosphotransferases (2.7.6) -- Diphosphotransferases (EC 2.7.6) are transferase enzymes that catalyse the transfer of a diphosphate (pyrophosphate) group from a donor molecule to an acceptor, often modifying nucleotides, sugars or other metabolites. Classified under CPC code C12N 9/1235, they play key roles in biochemical pathways such as nucleic-acid synthesis and energy metabolism.
[C12N 9/1241] Nucleotidyltransferases (2.7.7) -- C12N 9/1241 covers nucleotidyltransferases, enzymes that catalyze the transfer of nucleotidyl groups between molecules and are classified under the EC number 2.7.7. These enzymes include polymerases, ligases and other proteins that form phosphodiester bonds using nucleoside triphosphates as donors.
[C12N 9/1247] DNA-directed RNA polymerase (2.7.7.6) -- C12N 9/1247 classifies DNA-directed RNA polymerases, enzymes (EC 2.7.7.6) that catalyse the synthesis of RNA strands using a DNA template. The entry covers the structure, function and applications of these polymerases in transcription, recombinant expression and biotechnological processes involving nucleic acid synthesis.
[C12N 9/1252] DNA-directed DNA polymerase (2.7.7.7), i.e. DNA replicase -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/1252 covers DNA-directed DNA polymerases, classified under EC 2.7.7.7, which catalyze the synthesis of DNA strands using a DNA template. It includes enzymes that function as replicases in cellular replication and in biotechnological applications such as PCR and genome editing.
[C12N 9/1258] Polyribonucleotide nucleotidyltransferase (2.7.7.8), i.e. polynucleotide phosphorylase -- C12N 9/1258 designates the enzyme polyribonucleotide nucleotidyltransferase, commonly called polynucleotide phosphorylase, which catalyzes the synthesis and phosphorolytic degradation of RNA chains. The classification covers its structure, catalytic mechanisms and applications in microbial biotechnology and molecular biology.
[C12N 9/1264] DNA nucleotidylexotransferase (2.7.7.31), i.e. terminal nucleotidyl transferase -- C12N 9/1264 covers DNA nucleotidylexotransferase, classified as a terminal nucleotidyl transferase (EC 2.7.7.31), an enzyme that catalyzes the addition of nucleotides to the 3′-end of DNA strands without a template. It is used in molecular biology for labeling, tailing and repairing DNA molecules.
[C12N 9/127] RNA-directed RNA polymerase (2.7.7.48), i.e. RNA replicase -- RNA-directed RNA polymerase (EC 2.7.7.48), commonly called RNA replicase, is an enzyme that synthesises a complementary RNA strand using an RNA template. It catalyses the formation of phosphodiester bonds by transferring phosphate from nucleoside-triphosphates to the growing RNA chain, a key step in the replication of many RNA viruses and in some cellular RNA-dependent processes.
[C12N 9/1276] RNA-directed DNA polymerase (2.7.7.49), i.e. reverse transcriptase or telomerase -- This CPC subclass is in the chemistry branch, specifically biochemistry and enzymes, and pertains to RNA-directed DNA polymerases such as reverse transcriptase and telomerase. It covers inventions on their composition, production, or application.
[C12N 9/1282] RNA uridylyltransferase (2.7.7.52) -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/1282 refers to RNA uridylyltransferases, enzymes (EC 2.7.7.52) that catalyze the transfer of a uridylyl group from nucleoside-triphosphate to RNA, producing uridine-modified or poly-uridylated RNA molecules. These transferases belong to the nucleotidyltransferase family and act on RNA substrates.
[C12N 9/1288] Transferases for other substituted phosphate groups (2.7.8) -- C12N 9/1288 covers enzymes that catalyze the transfer of substituted phosphate groups between molecules, corresponding to EC class 2.7.8 transferases. These biocatalysts are used in metabolic pathways and biotechnological processes to modify nucleotides, sugars and other substrates by attaching diverse phosphate derivatives. The class groups together a variety of phosphotransferases that act on non-standard phosphate groups.
[C12N 9/1294] Phosphotransferases with paired acceptors (2.7.9) -- C12N 9/1294 covers enzymes that act as phosphotransferases with paired acceptors, transferring a phosphate group from a donor to one acceptor while simultaneously moving a phosphate to a second acceptor. These enzymes belong to EC class 2.7.9 and participate in metabolic processes such as nucleotide metabolism and signal transduction.
[C12N 9/13] transferring sulfur containing groups (2.8) -- C12N 9/13 covers enzymes that catalyse the transfer of sulfur-containing groups, such as sulfotransferases and related biocatalysts. The subclass is used for inventions involving the enzymatic formation of sulphur-linked bonds, including the synthesis of sulphated metabolites, conjugates and modified biomolecules.
[C12N 9/14] Hydrolases (3) -- C12N 9/14 designates hydrolase enzymes, a subclass of biocatalysts that accelerate the cleavage of chemical bonds by adding water, covering the third group of hydrolases within the CPC enzyme hierarchy.
[C12N 9/16] acting on ester bonds (3.1) -- C12N 9/16 covers enzymes that act on ester bonds, encompassing hydrolases such as lipases, esterases and related biocatalysts that cleave or modify ester linkages in biological molecules. This subclass falls under enzymes within the broader categories of biochemistry and microbiology, identifying catalytic activities directed at ester functional groups.
[C12N 9/18] Carboxylic ester hydrolases -- C12N 9/18 classifies enzymes that catalyze the hydrolysis of carboxylic ester bonds, commonly known as esterases or lipases, which break down esterified substrates into acids and alcohols. The subclass falls under chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms or enzymes-enzymes in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[C12N 9/20] Triglyceride splitting, e.g. by means of lipase -- C12N 9/20 relates to methods for splitting triglycerides, typically by enzymatic hydrolysis using lipases, to produce fatty acids and glycerol; it includes processes and apparatuses for catalyzing this conversion in industrial or laboratory settings.
[C12N 9/22] Ribonucleases -- Group C12N9/22 is incomplete pending reclassification of documents from group C12N2310/20 . Group C12N9/22 is also impacted by reclassification into groups C12N9/222 - C12N9/226 . Groups C12N9/22 , C12N9/222 - C12N9/226 and C12N2310/20 should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[C12N 9/222] Clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeats [CRISPR]-associated [CAS] enzymes -- Groups C12N9/222 - C12N9/226 are incomplete pending reclassification of documents from groups C12N9/22 and C12N2310/20 . All groups listed in this Warning should be considered in order to perform a complete search.
[C12N 9/224] Class 1 CAS enzyme complex, e.g. multi-enzyme -- The C12N 9/224 subclass covers enzyme preparations that combine two or more distinct enzymes into a single, functional complex, often termed a Class 1 cascade (CAS) enzyme complex or multi-enzyme system, designed to carry out sequential biochemical reactions in a coordinated manner. It includes formulations where the enzymes are physically linked, co-immobilized, or otherwise assembled to operate together on the same substrate.
[C12N 9/226] Class 2 CAS enzyme complex, e.g. single CAS protein -- C12N 9/226 designates enzyme complexes that belong to class 2 of the calcium-binding (CAS) family, typically represented by a single CAS protein that functions independently or as part of a larger assembly. The subclass covers compositions, isolated proteins and methods involving such CAS enzymes used in biochemical applications.
[C12N 9/24] acting on glycosyl compounds (3.2) -- C12N 9/24 covers enzymatic methods that act on glycosyl compounds, including hydrolysis, transfer, synthesis or modification of sugars and related substrates. The subclass is used for patents describing enzyme-catalyzed reactions such as glycosidase, transglycosylase or glycosyltransferase processes.
[C12N 9/2402] hydrolysing O- and S- glycosyl compounds (3.2.1) -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/2402 covers enzymes that catalyze the hydrolysis of O-glycosyl and S-glycosyl compounds. These biocatalysts break the oxygen- or sulfur-linked glycosidic bond between a sugar unit and its attached group.
[C12N 9/2405] Glucanases -- C12N 9/2405 relates to glucanases, enzymes that hydrolyze β-glucan polysaccharides by cleaving β-1,3 or β-1,4 glycosidic bonds. These biocatalysts act on substrates such as cellulose, lichenin or other glucans and are employed in biotechnology, food processing and biofuel production.
[C12N 9/2408] acting on alpha -1,4-glucosidic bonds -- The CPC class C12N 9/2408 falls under chemistry biochemistry microorganisms or enzymes enzymes and covers enzymes that catalyze the cleavage or modification of alpha-1,4-glucosidic linkages in polysaccharides such as starch. These include amylases and related hydrolases used in industrial or research applications.
[C12N 9/2411] Amylases -- C12N 9/2411 covers amylases, enzymes that catalyze the hydrolysis of starch and related polysaccharides into sugars. The subclass includes α-amylase, β-amylase, glucoamylase and similar variants employed in food processing, biotechnology and biofuel production.
[C12N 9/2414] Alpha-amylase (3.2.1.1.) -- C12N 9/2414 designates patents concerning the enzyme alpha-amylase (EC 3.2.1.1), a hydrolase that catalyzes the breakdown of starch into smaller sugars. It covers the production, modification, and application of this enzyme in processes such as food processing, detergent formulation, and bio-fuel production.
[C12N 9/2417] from microbiological source -- C12N 9/2417 designates inventions relating to enzymes that are obtained from microbiological sources, including bacteria, fungi or other microorganisms. The subclass covers methods of isolation, purification and characterization of such microbial enzymes, distinguishing them from plant- or animal-derived enzymes.
[C12N 9/242] Fungal source -- C12N 9/242 covers enzymes that are obtained from fungal sources, including their production, isolation and use in biotechnological applications. It is part of the CPC hierarchy under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes and enzymes.
[C12N 9/2422] from plant source -- C12N 9/2422 covers enzymes obtained from plant sources, including their isolation, purification and characterization, as well as the use of these plant-derived enzymes in biotechnological and industrial processes.
[C12N 9/2425] Beta-amylase (3.2.1.2) -- C12N 9/2425 designates the enzyme beta-amylase, classified under EC 3.2.1.2, which catalyzes the hydrolytic removal of successive maltose units from the non-reducing ends of starch polymers. It belongs to the broader CPC hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and enzymes.
[C12N 9/2428] Glucan 1,4-alpha-glucosidase (3.2.1.3), i.e. glucoamylase -- The CPC code C12N 9/2428 falls under chemistry biochemistry microorganisms or enzymes enzymes and designates the enzyme glucan 1,4-alpha-glucosidase, also known as glucoamylase (EC 3.2.1.3). It covers inventions related to this enzyme, its production, purification and use in processes such as starch hydrolysis.
[C12N 9/2431] Beta-fructofuranosidase (3.2.1.26), i.e. invertase -- C12N 9/2431 designates beta-fructofuranosidase, an invertase (EC 3.2.1.26) that catalyzes the hydrolysis of sucrose into glucose and fructose. The enzyme belongs to the glycosyl-hydrolase family acting on O-glycosyl compounds and is employed in food processing, biotechnology and research on carbohydrate metabolism.
[C12N 9/2434] acting on beta-1,4-glucosidic bonds -- C12N 9/2434 designates enzymes that act on beta-1,4-glucosidic bonds, such as cellulases and β-glucosidases, catalyzing hydrolysis or transglycosylation of these linkages in polysaccharides like cellulose. The subclass encompasses catalytic processes that cleave or rearrange the beta-1,4 glycosidic connections in carbohydrate substrates.
[C12N 9/2437] Cellulases (3.2.1.4; 3.2.1.74; 3.2.1.91; 3.2.1.150) -- This CPC subclass covers enzymes that catalyze hydrolysis of beta-1,4-glycosidic bonds in cellulose, i.e., cellulases including EC 3.2.1.4, 3.2.1.74, 3.2.1.91 and 3.2.1.150. It is used for patents concerning the production, purification, engineering or application of these cellulolytic enzymes.
[C12N 9/244] Endo-1,3(4)-beta-glucanase (3.2.1.6) -- The code C12N 9/244 covers endo-1,3(4)-beta-glucanase, an enzyme classified as EC 3.2.1.6 that hydrolyzes internal β-1,3 and β-1,4 linkages in glucans, facilitating the breakdown of polysaccharides such as fungal cell-wall components. It is used in biotechnological applications involving carbohydrate processing and microbial degradation.
[C12N 9/2442] Chitinase (3.2.1.14) -- C12N 9/2442 designates chitinases, classified as EC 3.2.1.14, which are enzymes that hydrolyze the β-1,4-linkages in chitin and related polysaccharides. The entry covers isolated or recombinant chitinases, their variants, and methods for producing or using them in biotechnological or industrial applications.
[C12N 9/2445] Beta-glucosidase (3.2.1.21) -- C12N 9/2445 designates the enzyme beta-glucosidase (EC 3.2.1.21), a hydrolase that cleaves β-glucosidic linkages in carbohydrates, commonly used in biomass conversion and pharmaceutical synthesis. The code falls within the CPC hierarchy under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and enzymes.
[C12N 9/2448] Licheninase (3.2.1.73) -- C12N 9/2448 designates the enzyme licheninase, classified under EC 3.2.1.73, which hydrolyzes the β-1,3- and β-1,4-glycosidic bonds in lichenin, a mixed-linkage glucan found in lichens and some plants. The entry groups this biocatalyst with other microbial or recombinant enzymes used in carbohydrate modification and analytical processes.
[C12N 9/2451] acting on alpha-1,6-glucosidic bonds -- C12N 9/2451 designates enzymes that specifically act on alpha-1,6-glucosidic bonds in carbohydrates, such as 6-alpha-glucosidases and related transferases involved in starch metabolism and carbohydrate processing. This subclass falls under biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, and more narrowly under enzymes.
[C12N 9/2454] Dextranase (3.2.1.11) -- The CPC entry C12N 9/2454 covers the enzyme dextranase (EC 3.2.1.11), which hydrolyzes the α-1,6-glycosidic bonds in dextran polymers. It is classified under enzymes used in biochemistry and microbiology for processing polysaccharides, particularly in applications such as starch processing, sugar beet extraction and biofilm control.
[C12N 9/2457] Pullulanase (3.2.1.41) -- C12N 9/2457 designates the enzyme pullulanase (EC 3.2.1.41), a debranching hydrolase that specifically cleaves α-1,6-glycosidic linkages in pullulan and branched starch polymers. It is employed in biotechnology and industrial processes for starch conversion, improving saccharification efficiency and producing maltodextrins.
[C12N 9/246] Isoamylase (3.2.1.68) -- Isoamylase (EC 3.2.1.68) is a debranching enzyme that hydrolyzes α-1,6-glucosidic bonds in branched polysaccharides such as glycogen and amylopectin. It is employed in starch processing to generate linear dextrins and to aid the production of high-amylose or resistant starches.
[C12N 9/2462] Lysozyme (3.2.1.17) -- C12N 9/2462 covers lysozyme, the EC 3.2.1.17 enzyme that hydrolyzes the beta-1,4-glycosidic bonds in bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan. The classification includes the production, purification, genetic engineering and use of lysozyme in medical, food and industrial applications. It resides in the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy for enzymes derived from microorganisms.
[C12N 9/2465] acting on alpha-galactose-glycoside bonds, e.g. alpha-galactosidase (3.2.1.22) -- C12N 9/2465 classifies enzymes that cleave alpha-galactose-glycoside bonds, such as alpha-galactosidase (EC 3.2.1.22). These enzymes hydrolyze the α-linked galactosyl residues in oligosaccharides and polysaccharides, facilitating the breakdown or modification of carbohydrate structures in biotechnological and industrial applications.
[C12N 9/2468] acting on beta-galactose-glycoside bonds, e.g. carrageenases (3.2.1.83; 3.2.1.157); beta-agarase (3.2.1.81) -- The subclass C12N 9/2468 includes enzymes that catalyse the hydrolysis of β-galactose-glycosidic bonds, such as carrageenases and β-agarases, which break down polysaccharides like carrageenan and agar. It is used to classify biocatalysts involved in the degradation of these marine-derived carbohydrates.
[C12N 9/2471] Beta-galactosidase (3.2.1.23), i.e. exo-(1-->4)-beta-D-galactanase -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/2471 covers the enzyme beta-galactosidase (EC 3.2.1.23), specifically exo-(1→4)-beta-D-galactanase activity that hydrolyzes terminal beta-D-galactosidic linkages in polysaccharides, and is relevant to processes such as lactose degradation, galactose production and various biotechnological applications.
[C12N 9/2474] Hyaluronoglucosaminidase (3.2.1.35), i.e. hyaluronidase -- C12N 9/2474 designates hyaluronoglucosaminidase, the enzyme classified as EC 3.2.1.35 that catalyzes the hydrolysis of hyaluronic acid into smaller glucosaminoglycan fragments. It covers biotechnological processes and applications involving production, purification and use of this hyaluronidase in medical, cosmetic or research contexts.
[C12N 9/2477] Hemicellulases not provided in a preceding group -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/2477 includes hemicellulases that are not listed in any earlier group. These are enzymes that hydrolyze hemicellulose components of plant cell walls, facilitating the breakdown of lignocellulosic biomass for applications such as biofuel production, food processing, or paper manufacturing.
[C12N 9/248] Xylanases -- C12N 9/248 designates xylanases, enzymes that hydrolyze the β-1,4-xylosidic bonds of xylan, a major hemicellulose component of plant cell walls. These biocatalysts are employed in pulp and paper processing, animal feed improvement, and the production of xylo-oligosaccharides for food and pharmaceutical applications.
[C12N 9/2482] Endo-1,4-beta-xylanase (3.2.1.8) -- Endo-1,4-beta-xylanase (EC 3.2.1.8) is an enzyme that cleaves internal β-1,4-xylosidic bonds in the hemicellulose xylan, yielding xylo-oligosaccharides. It is produced by a range of microorganisms and is applied in pulp-and-paper processing, animal feed improvement and the saccharification step of bio-fuel production. The CPC code C12N 9/2482 places it within the chemistry-biochemistry subgroup for enzymes that act on polysaccharides.
[C12N 9/2485] Xylan endo-1,3-beta-xylosidase (3.2.1.32), i.e. endo-1,3-beta-xylanase -- C12N 9/2485 designates enzymes that hydrolyze internal 1,3-beta-xylosidic bonds of xylan, commonly called endo-1,3-beta-xylanases (EC 3.2.1.32). These biocatalysts are produced by microorganisms and are employed in the breakdown of hemicellulosic polymers in industrial and research applications.
[C12N 9/2488] Mannanases -- Mannanases are enzymes that hydrolyze the β-1,4-mannosidic linkages in mannans and related polysaccharides, commonly used in food processing, pulp and paper, and biofuel production. They belong to the glycoside-hydrolase families and are produced by various microorganisms.
[C12N 9/2491] Beta-mannosidase (3.2.1.25), i.e. mannanase -- C12N 9/2491 covers the enzyme beta-mannosidase (EC 3.2.1.25), commonly called mannanase, which catalyzes the hydrolysis of β-1,4-mannosidic bonds in mannans and related polysaccharides. The classification applies to biotechnological uses such as biomass degradation, food processing, and the production of oligosaccharides.
[C12N 9/2494] Mannan endo-1,4-beta-mannosidase (3.2.1.78), i.e. endo-beta-mannanase -- This classification covers enzymes classified as mannan endo-1,4-beta-mannosidase (EC 3.2.1.78), which catalyze the random hydrolysis of internal β-1,4-mannosidic bonds in mannans and mannooligosaccharides. They are used in the degradation of hemicellulose in plant biomass, feed additives, and various biotechnological processes.
[C12N 9/2497] hydrolysing N- glycosyl compounds (3.2.2) -- C12N 9/2497 designates enzyme-catalyzed hydrolysis of N-glycosyl compounds, which have an N-glycosidic bond such as nucleosides or N-linked sugars. This subclass sits within chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, enzymes and covers processes where the enzyme cleaves the N-glycosidic linkage, releasing a free amine and a carbohydrate.
[C12N 9/48] acting on peptide bonds (3.4) -- C12N 9/48 designates enzymes that cleave peptide bonds, i.e., proteases or peptidases. This subclass includes catalytic proteins that hydrolyze proteins or peptides into smaller fragments and are used in biotechnology, medicine and various industrial processes.
[C12N 9/485] Exopeptidases (3.4.11-3.4.19) -- C12N 9/485 designates exopeptidases, enzymes that cleave terminal peptide bonds at the ends of protein chains. The class includes enzymes such as aminopeptidases and carboxypeptidases falling under EC 3.4.11-3.4.19. It resides within the CPC hierarchy under chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, enzymes.
[C12N 9/50] Proteinases -- C12N 9/50 designates proteinases, enzymes that catalyse the hydrolysis of peptide bonds in proteins. These biocatalysts are employed in research, medical diagnostics, therapeutic agents and industrial processes such as food processing and waste treatment. The code falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Enzymes.
[C12N 9/503] derived from viruses -- C12N 9/503 covers enzymes that are obtained from viral sources, including viral polymerases, proteases and other proteins isolated or expressed from viruses for biotechnological or pharmaceutical use.
[C12N 9/506] derived from RNA viruses -- This subclass concerns biotechnological material that originates from RNA viruses, including nucleic acid sequences, proteins, vectors, and derivatives used in research, diagnostics, or therapeutic applications. It covers isolation, cloning, modification, and use of viral RNA-derived elements such as reverse transcriptase, viral polymerases, and viral genome fragments.
[C12N 9/52] derived from bacteria -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Proteinases (, e.g. Endopeptidases (3.4.21 - 3.4.25)) derived from bacteria or proteinases derived from Archaea, formerly known as Archaebacteria.
[C12N 9/54] bacteria being Bacillus -- C12N 9/54 relates to microorganisms belonging to the genus Bacillus, covering their isolation, cultivation, genetic modification and use in biotechnological processes such as enzyme production, fermentation and biocatalysis.
[C12N 9/58] derived from fungi -- C12N 9/58 includes enzymes, metabolites or other biotechnological products that are obtained from fungal sources, covering processes where fungi are the origin of the material. It is used for patents describing isolation, purification or modification of such fungal-derived compounds within the broader chemistry-biochemistry-microorganisms-enzymes hierarchy.
[C12N 9/60] from yeast -- The entry C12N 9/60 covers biotechnological processes that employ yeast as a source of enzymes, including the production, modification, or utilization of yeast-derived enzymes in chemical or industrial applications.
[C12N 9/62] from Aspergillus -- C12N 9/62 covers enzymes that are obtained from the genus Aspergillus, a filamentous fungus commonly used in industrial biotechnology. The code is applied to processes, products and applications involving Aspergillus-derived proteases, amylases, lipases and other biologically active proteins.
[C12N 9/63] derived from plants -- C12N 9/63 designates enzymes that are obtained from plant sources, covering their isolation, purification and biotechnological use. It is part of the enzymes subgroup in the microorganisms or enzymes class of the CPC system, focusing on plant-derived catalysts for industrial and research applications.

The class includes natural and engineered enzymes, their genetic material, production methods, and uses in biotechnology, such as protein processing, diagnostics, and therapeutic applications. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → microorganisms or enzymes → enzymes in the CPC system.
[C12N 9/641] Cysteine endopeptidases (3.4.22) -- C12N 9/641 covers cysteine endopeptidases, a subclass of proteolytic enzymes that use a cysteine residue in their active site to hydrolyze peptide bonds within proteins. [C12N 9/6413] Aspartic endopeptidases (3.4.23) -- C12N 9/6413 covers aspartic endopeptidases, a class of proteolytic enzymes that use an aspartate residue in the active site to cleave peptide bonds. These enzymes are typically found in microorganisms and are employed in biotechnology, medicine, and protein-processing.
[C12N 9/6416] Metalloendopeptidases (3.4.24) -- C12N 9/6416 covers metalloendopeptidases, enzymes that hydrolyze internal peptide bonds by employing a bound metal ion, corresponding to EC class 3.4.24. These catalysts are commonly used in protein processing, therapeutic development, and analytical applications across biotechnology and pharmaceutical research.
[C12N 9/6418] from snakes -- C12N 9/6418 covers enzymes that are obtained from snakes, including venom-derived proteases, phospholipases and other bioactive proteins used in pharmaceutical, diagnostic or biotechnological applications. The subclass is used to index patents describing isolation, composition or use of snake-derived enzymes.
[C12N 9/6421] from mammals -- C12N 9/6421 covers enzymes that are sourced directly from mammalian tissue, body fluids, or cells, including recombinant proteins expressed in mammalian systems. The classification is used for inventions involving purification, characterization, or application of such mammalian enzymes in diagnostics, therapeutics, or industrial processes. It excludes enzymes from other kingdoms such as plants or microorganisms.
[C12N 9/6424] Serine endopeptidases (3.4.21) -- Code C12N 9/6424 falls under the CPC hierarchy for chemistry, biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically enzymes. It covers serine endopeptidases, the class of proteolytic enzymes that use a serine residue in their active site to cleave peptide bonds (EC 3.4.21).
[C12N 9/6427] Chymotrypsins (3.4.21.1; 3.4.21.2); Trypsin (3.4.21.4) -- C12N 9/6427 is a CPC subclass for serine protease enzymes, specifically chymotrypsins (EC 3.4.21.1 and 3.4.21.2) and trypsin (EC 3.4.21.4). It covers the preparation, use, and applications of these enzymes in biochemical processes.
[C12N 9/6429] Thrombin (3.4.21.5) -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/6429 pertains to the enzyme thrombin, a serine protease (EC 3.4.21.5) involved in blood clotting. [C12N 9/6432] Coagulation factor Xa (3.4.21.6) -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/6432 covers the enzyme coagulation factor Xa, a serine protease that plays a key role in the blood-clotting cascade. It includes patents on the production, purification, modification and pharmaceutical applications of this specific factor.
[C12N 9/6435] Plasmin (3.4.21.7), i.e. fibrinolysin -- C12N 9/6435 covers the enzyme plasmin, known as fibrinolysin and classified as EC 3.4.21.7, which catalyzes the breakdown of fibrin clots. The subclass groups patent documents relating to the preparation, modification, or application of this serine protease in medical, diagnostic, or biotechnological contexts.
[C12N 9/6437] Coagulation factor VIIa (3.4.21.21) -- C12N 9/6437 designates the enzyme coagulation factor VIIa, classified under recombinant or purified protein engineering. The code is applied to patents describing therapeutic or diagnostic applications of factor VIIa.
[C12N 9/644] Coagulation factor IXa (3.4.21.22) -- C12N 9/644 covers the enzyme coagulation factor IXa, classified as EC 3.4.21.22, a serine protease that activates factor X in the blood-clotting cascade. The entry pertains to biochemistry of enzymes derived from microorganisms or other sources and includes methods, variants and applications of factor IXa.
