[C12N 9/6443] Coagulation factor XIa (3.4.21.27) -- C12N 9/6443 designates the enzyme coagulation factor XIa, a serine protease involved in the intrinsic pathway of blood clotting. It falls under the CPC hierarchy for enzymes within biochemistry and microorganisms, identifying the specific biochemical activity (EC 3.4.21.27).
[C12N 9/6445] Kallikreins (3.4.21.34; 3.4.21.35) -- C12N 9/6445 denotes the subclass for kallikrein enzymes, which are serine proteases classified under EC 3.4.21.34 and 3.4.21.35. These enzymes catalyze the cleavage of peptide bonds in physiological processes such as blood pressure regulation and coagulation, and are the subject of biotechnological and pharmaceutical patents.
[C12N 9/6448] Elastases, e.g. pancreatic elastase (3.4.21.36); leukocyte elastase (3.4.31.37) -- C12N 9/6448 classifies elastases, a subgroup of proteolytic enzymes that hydrolyze elastin and related peptide bonds. It includes pancreatic elastase (EC 3.4.21.36) and leukocyte elastase (EC 3.4.31.37), covering their structures, functions, and uses in biotechnology and medical applications.
[C12N 9/6451] Coagulation factor XIIa (3.4.21.38) -- C12N 9/6451 covers inventions concerning the serine protease coagulation factor XIIa, the activated form of factor XII that initiates the intrinsic blood-clotting cascade. The classification includes recombinant or natural forms, variants, pharmaceutical compositions, diagnostic methods and uses of the enzyme.
[C12N 9/6454] Dibasic site splicing serine proteases, e.g. kexin (3.4.21.61); furin (3.4.21.75) and other proprotein convertases -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/6454 covers serine proteases that recognize and cleave dibasic (arginine-lysine) sites in precursor proteins, such as the kexin (EC 3.4.21.61) and furin (EC 3.4.21.75) enzymes and related proprotein convertases. These enzymes perform post-translational processing by removing specific peptide segments to activate or mature proteins in microbial and cellular pathways.
[C12N 9/6456] Plasminogen activators -- C12N 9/6456 covers plasminogen activators, enzymes that catalyze the conversion of plasminogen to plasmin to dissolve fibrin clots. The classification includes natural and recombinant forms used in thrombolytic therapy, diagnostic assays and research on blood-coagulation pathways.
[C12N 9/6459] t-plasminogen activator (3.4.21.68), i.e. tPA -- C12N 9/6459 designates the enzyme tissue-type plasminogen activator, known as tPA, which catalyzes the conversion of plasminogen to plasmin and is used therapeutically to dissolve blood clots. The entry groups both natural and recombinant forms of this serine protease under the enzymes subclass of biochemistry.
[C12N 9/6462] u-Plasminogen activator (3.4.21.73), i.e. urokinase -- The code C12N 9/6462 designates the enzyme urokinase, also known as u-plasminogen activator (EC 3.4.21.73), which catalyzes the conversion of plasminogen to plasmin and is used in clinical applications such as thrombolytic therapy. It falls within the subclass of enzymes that act on proteins, specifically those involved in fibrinolysis.
[C12N 9/6464] Protein C (3.4.21.69) -- Protein C (EC 3.4.21.69) is a vitamin K-dependent serine protease that regulates blood coagulation by inactivating factors Va and VIIIa, thereby exerting an anticoagulant effect. In the CPC system it falls under C12N 9/6464, grouping it with enzymes from microorganisms or other biological sources.
[C12N 9/6467] Granzymes, e.g. granzyme A (3.4.21.78); granzyme B (3.4.21.79) -- The CPC subclass C12N 9/6467 covers granzyme enzymes, specifically granzyme A (EC 3.4.21.78) and granzyme B (EC 3.4.21.79), which are serine proteases secreted by cytotoxic lymphocytes to trigger apoptosis in target cells. It pertains to biotechnological applications involving these immune-related proteins.
[C12N 9/647] Blood coagulation factors not provided for in a preceding group or according to more than one of the proceeding groups -- C12N 9/647 classifies inventions dealing with blood-coagulation factors that are not specifically listed in earlier subclasses or that combine features from several preceding groups, covering novel or hybrid coagulation proteins and related therapeutic or diagnostic applications.
[C12N 9/6472] Cysteine endopeptidases (3.4.22) -- C12N 9/6472 covers cysteine endopeptidases, enzymes that hydrolyze peptide bonds using a catalytic cysteine residue and classified under EC 3.4.22. The entry includes methods for producing, isolating, purifying or modifying these proteases, as well as their use in biotechnological applications such as protein processing, therapeutic agents or diagnostic tools.
[C12N 9/6475] Interleukin 1-beta convertase-like enzymes (3.4.22.10; 3.4.22.36; 3.4.22.63) -- C12N 9/6475 designates enzymes that are functionally similar to interleukin-1-beta convertase, a class of serine proteases that process precursor proteins into active forms. It includes members classified under EC 3.4.22.10, 3.4.22.36, and 3.4.22.63, covering a range of proteolytic activities relevant to immune regulation.
[C12N 9/6478] Aspartic endopeptidases (3.4.23) -- C12N 9/6478 covers aspartic endopeptidases, a subgroup of proteolytic enzymes that employ an aspartic acid residue in their active site to hydrolyse peptide bonds. Classified under EC 3.4.23, examples include pepsin and cathepsins D and E, which play roles in digestion, pathogen virulence and various biotechnological processes. The entry sits in the CPC hierarchy under chemistry - biochemistry - microorganisms or enzymes - enzymes.
[C12N 9/6481] Pepsins (3.4.23.1; 3.4.23.2; 3.4.23.3) -- C12N 9/6481 concerns pepsin enzymes, including pepsin A, pepsin B and related aspartic proteases used in protein hydrolysis. The subclass addresses their production, purification, characterization and applications in food processing, biotechnology and pharmaceutical preparations.
[C12N 9/6483] Chymosin (3.4.23.4), i.e. rennin -- C12N 9/6483 covers the enzyme chymosin, also known as rennin, a protease that clots milk by cleaving casein, widely used in cheese production and derived from calf stomach or recombinant sources. The subclass falls under enzymes in biochemistry and microbiology within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[C12N 9/6486] Renin (3.4.23.15) -- C12N 9/6486 covers the enzyme renin, classified under the EC number 3.4.23.15, which catalyzes the conversion of angiotensinogen to angiotensin I and plays a key role in blood-pressure regulation. The entry pertains to biochemical applications involving this protease, including its production, purification, and use in pharmaceutical or research contexts.
[C12N 9/6489] Metalloendopeptidases (3.4.24) -- C12N 9/6489 designates metalloendopeptidases, enzymes that hydrolyze internal peptide bonds with the aid of a metal ion cofactor, corresponding to EC 3.4.24. These zinc-dependent proteases include matrix metalloproteinases and related microbial enzymes and are important in biocatalysis, drug development and studies of cellular physiology.
[C12N 9/6491] Matrix metalloproteases [MMP's], e.g. interstitial collagenase (3.4.24.7); Stromelysins (3.4.24.17; 3.2.1.22); Matrilysin (3.4.24.23) -- This CPC subclass (C12N 9/6491) covers matrix metalloproteases, zinc-dependent proteolytic enzymes that degrade extracellular-matrix proteins such as collagen. It includes interstitial collagenase, stromelysins and matrilysin, which are important in tissue remodeling, disease progression and drug development.
[C12N 9/6494] Neprilysin (3.4.24.11), i.e. enkephalinase or neutral-endopeptidase 24.11 -- The entry C12N 9/6494 designates the enzyme neprilysin, also called enkephalinase or neutral-endopeptidase 24.11, a metalloprotease that hydrolyzes peptide bonds in neuropeptides such as enkephalins and amyloid-β. It falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Enzymes in the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[C12N 9/6497] Endothelin-converting enzyme (3.4.24.71) -- The CPC entry C12N 9/6497 classifies inventions related to the endothelin-converting enzyme, a metalloprotease (EC 3.4.24.71) that processes precursor peptides into active endothelins involved in vascular tone regulation. It falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or enzymes → Enzymes.
[C12N 9/78] acting on carbon to nitrogen bonds other than peptide bonds (3.5) -- C12N 9/78 relates to enzymes that catalyse reactions on carbon-nitrogen bonds that are not peptide bonds, covering methods and compositions for modifying, cleaving or forming such non-peptidic C-N linkages. This subclass falls under the hierarchy of Chemistry → Biochemistry → Microorganisms or Enzymes → Enzymes.
[C12N 9/80] acting on amide bonds in linear amides -- C12N 9/80 designates enzymes that catalyze reactions involving the cleavage or modification of amide bonds in linear-amide compounds. It includes amidases, peptidases and related biocatalysts that act on simple peptide-like substrates rather than cyclic or complex structures.
[C12N 9/82] Asparaginase -- C12N 9/82 designates asparaginase, an enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of L-asparagine to aspartic acid and ammonia, commonly employed in therapeutic agents for acute lymphoblastic leukemia and in food processing to reduce acrylamide formation. The code falls under the chemistry branch for biochemistry, microorganisms or enzymes, specifically enzymes.
[C12N 9/84] Penicillin amidase -- C12N 9/84 covers penicillin amidase, an enzyme that hydrolyzes the amide bond of penicillin to produce 6-aminopenicillanic acid, a key intermediate in the industrial synthesis of semisynthetic penicillins. The classification falls under chemistry - biochemistry - microorganisms or enzymes - enzymes.
[C12N 9/86] acting on amide bonds in cyclic amides, e.g. penicillinase -- This CPC subclass C12N 9/86 pertains to enzymes that act on amide bonds in cyclic amides, such as penicillinases that hydrolyze β-lactam rings. It is applied to patents describing biocatalysts that modify or degrade cyclic amide compounds.
[C12N 9/88] Lyases (4.) -- C12N 9/88 covers enzymes classified as lyases, which catalyze the cleavage of chemical bonds without hydrolysis or oxidation-reduction, typically forming a new double bond, a ring, or a new functional group. These biocatalysts are important in metabolic pathways and industrial biotransformations.
[C12N 9/90] Isomerases (5.) -- C12N 9/90 covers enzymes that function as isomerases, catalyzing the interconversion of molecular structures without adding or removing atoms. These biocatalysts facilitate rearrangements such as geometric, optical, or positional isomerizations in biochemical pathways and are used in patents involving microbial or recombinant production of such enzymes.
[C12N 9/92] Glucose isomerase -- Glucose isomerase is an enzyme that catalyzes the reversible isomerisation of D-glucose to D-fructose, often employed in the industrial production of high-fructose corn syrup and other sweeteners. The classification covers the protein itself, its variants and recombinant forms, as well as processes using it for carbohydrate modification.
[C12N 9/93] Ligases (6) -- C12N 9/93 designates the class of ligases, enzymes that catalyze the formation of covalent bonds between two substrates, typically coupling the reaction to the hydrolysis of a nucleoside triphosphate such as ATP. The entry covers ligases used in biotechnological applications, including those that join nucleic acids, peptides, or other biomolecules.
[C12N 9/94] Pancreatin -- Pancreatin, covered by CPC class C12N 9/94, refers to a pancreatic enzyme preparation, typically a mixture of amylase, protease and lipase derived from animal pancreas, used in digestive aid formulations. The class includes the composition, its preparation, and pharmaceutical applications for treating digestive disorders.
[C12N 9/96] Stabilising an enzyme by forming an adduct or a composition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Enzymes stabilized by mutation -> C12N9/00 - C12N9/94  **Glossary:** - Stabilisation: preserving activity in time and/or amount under certain conditions of pH, temperature etc. - Adduct: a product of a direct addition of two or more distinct molecules, resulting in a single reaction product containing all atoms of all components - Enzyme conjugate: enzyme covalently bound to one or more disctinct molecules
[C12N 9/98] Preparation of granular or free-flowing enzyme compositions -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparation of granular or free-flowing enzyme compositions if it is done to stabilise the enzyme -> C12N9/96
[C12N 9/99] Enzyme inactivation by chemical treatment -- C12N 9/99 concerns chemical methods for disabling enzymes, encompassing reagents that denature, oxidise, or otherwise modify active sites to stop catalytic activity. It includes treatments with acids, bases, solvents, metal ions or specific inhibitors that render the enzyme inactive.
[C12P] FERMENTATION OR ENZYME-USING PROCESSES TO SYNTHESISE A DESIRED CHEMICAL COMPOUND OR COMPOSITION OR TO SEPARATE OPTICAL ISOMERS FROM A RACEMIC MIXTURE -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Explanatory Note:  This subclass covers both major and minor chemical modifications.  **Glossary:** - Microorganism Microorganisms: Comprises single-celled organisms such as bacteria, actinomycetales or single-celled fungi, e.g. yeasts; for the purposes of classification, this term also includes viruses, undifferentiated human, animal or plant cells, protozoa, tissues and unicellular algae. - Saccharide: polyhydroxy-aldehyde or -ketone, or derivative thereof; the exact definition is given under C12P19/00
[C12P 1/00] Preparation of compounds or compositions, not provided for in groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compounds "not sufficiently identified" are compounds not identified with a chemical structure, or complex mixtures of compounds with no principal chemical compound identified. Examples are compounds identified only by their empirical formulae, their activity, their method of preparation, and/or their physical or chemical properties. Examples of "complex mixtures" are compositions obtained by fermentation without further specification of the (principal) chemical product(s) contained therein. In all other cases, a composition is classified with the relevant compound groups (one or more of C12P5/00 - C12P37/00 ).  Notable exceptions, which should NOT get a C12P1/00 classification, are (see also below table):  An positive example of a document to be classified in C12P1/00 :  E.g., a document relating to an antibiotic produced by a certain bacterium, where the antibiotic is only defined by its activity and/or its preparation method. Such a document would have to be classified in C12P1/04 . "not sufficiently characterized" may e.g. mean that, although it is specified in the description that the antibiotic compound bears an alcohol group, it is not clear if it also bears other functional groups (e.g. amine) or a heterocycle, i.e. it could fall under C12P7/02 , C12P13/00 or C12P17/00 . Note that in this case the document should also be classified in C07G11/00 .  Generally, C12P1/00 should not simply be used if the product or composition obtained by fermentation or enzymatic reaction is not found in C12P3/00 - C12P37/00 . Reference is made to the list of fields not covered in C12P1/00 (see below table).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Baking; treatment of flour or dough -> A21D - Foods or foodstuffs; their treatment -> A23 - Cosmetic preparations containing MOs -> A61K8/99 - Medicinal preparations containing undetermined materials from MOs -> A61K35/66 - Elimination of harmful chemicals by enzymes or MOs -> A62D3/02 - Biological purification of waste gases -> B01D53/84 - Bioremediation of contaminated soil -> B09C1/10 - Biological treatment of water, waste water or sewage -> C02F3/00 - Biological treatment of sludge -> C02F11/02 - Preparation of fertilisers characterized by a composting step -> C05F17/00 - Antibiotics of unknown structure -> C07G11/00 - Recovery of CO2 as by-product -> C12F3/02 - MOs (compositions containing them, processes of propagating, maintaining, preserving them, culture media) -> C12N1/00 - Microorganism recombinantly expressing an enzyme -> C12N9/00 - Microorganism recombinantly expressing an enzyme cluster/ an enzymatic pathway -> C12N15/52 - Biochemical fuel cell (product= electricity) -> H01M8/16
[C12P 1/02] by using fungi -- C12P 1/02 covers the preparation of chemicals or compositions by employing fungi as the biocatalyst or production organism in fermentation or enzyme-based processes, including the synthesis of target compounds and the resolution of racemic mixtures, when such methods are not specified in other groups.
[C12P 1/04] by using bacteria -- The CPC subclass C12P 1/04 covers biotechnological processes that employ bacteria to carry out fermentation or enzyme-driven reactions for preparing chemical compounds or compositions that are not specified in other groups, including the synthesis of desired products or the separation of optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 1/06] by using actinomycetales -- C12P 1/06 designates processes that prepare chemical compounds or compositions, not otherwise classified, by employing actinomycetales in fermentation or enzyme-mediated reactions. It falls under the hierarchy chemistry - biochemistry - fermentation or enzyme-using processes to synthesize a desired chemical or separate optical isomers. The subclass addresses biotechnological methods that exploit actinomycete microorganisms for novel product synthesis or chiral resolution.
[C12P 11/00] Preparation of sulfur-containing organic compounds -- C12P 11/00 covers biochemical and enzymatic methods for producing sulfur-containing organic molecules, including their synthesis, transformation, and purification, as well as the enzymatic separation of optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 13/00] Preparation of nitrogen-containing organic compounds -- The CPC subclass C12P 13/00 covers processes that use microorganisms or enzymes to prepare nitrogen-containing organic compounds, including the synthesis of desired chemicals and the resolution of racemic mixtures into optical isomers. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, and fermentation or enzyme-using synthesis methods.
[C12P 13/001] Amines; Imines -- C12P 13/001 covers biotechnological methods for preparing nitrogen-containing organic compounds, specifically amines and imines, using fermentation or enzyme-catalyzed processes. The class includes steps for synthesising these compounds or for separating optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 13/002] Nitriles (-CN) -- The CPC subclass C12P 13/002 covers biocatalytic or enzymatic methods for preparing or transforming nitriles (compounds containing a -CN group) as part of nitrogen-containing organic synthesis or optical isomer separation. It includes fermentation-based routes and enzyme-mediated steps that produce nitrile intermediates or convert nitriles into desired products.
[C12P 13/004] Cyanohydrins -- C12P 13/004 covers processes for preparing cyanohydrins, organic compounds in which a cyano group is bonded to a carbon bearing a hydroxyl group, typically produced by reacting hydrogen cyanide with aldehydes or ketones. These methods are used in the synthesis of nitrogen-containing chemicals and for separating optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 13/005] Amino acids other than alpha- or beta amino acids, e.g. gamma amino acids -- C12P 13/005 covers the preparation of amino acids whose amino group is attached beyond the α- or β-positions, such as γ-amino acids and other non-proteinogenic varieties. The class includes methods for synthesising these nitrogen-containing compounds, which are used as building blocks in pharmaceuticals, polymers, and specialty chemicals.
[C12P 13/007] Carnitine; Butyrobetaine; Crotonobetaine -- C12P 13/007 covers biotechnological routes for making or isolating nitrogen-containing compounds such as carnitine, butyrobetaine and crotonobetaine, typically using enzymes or fermentation. The subclass is part of the broader group on preparing nitrogen-containing organic substances by enzyme-mediated synthesis or by separating optical isomers from racemic mixtures. It includes microbial or enzymatic steps that yield these specific betaine derivatives.
[C12P 13/008] containing a N-O bond, e.g. nitro (-NO 2 ), nitroso (-NO) -- This CPC subclass deals with methods of preparing nitrogen-containing organic compounds that bear an N-O functional group such as nitro (-NO2) or nitroso (-NO) moieties, typically employed in fermentation or enzyme-mediated syntheses and in the resolution of optical isomers.
[C12P 13/02] Amides, e.g. chloramphenicol -- C12P 13/02 classifies biochemical or enzyme-using methods for preparing amide compounds, such as the antibiotic chloramphenicol, within the broader group of nitrogen-containing organic synthesis via fermentation or enzyme-using processes.
[C12P 13/04] Alpha- or beta- amino acids -- C12P 13/04 covers processes for preparing α- or β-amino acids, a subclass of nitrogen-containing organic compounds produced by fermentation or enzyme-using methods. It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, and fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesising desired chemicals or separating optical isomers.
[C12P 13/06] Alanine -- C12P 13/06 relates to methods for preparing the α-amino acid alanine, specifically using fermentation or enzyme-mediated synthesis, and includes techniques for isolating its optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 13/08] Lysine -- C12P 13/08 covers processes that use fermentation or enzymes to produce lysine, an essential nitrogen-containing amino acid, or to separate its optical isomers from racemic mixtures. The entry falls within the CPC hierarchy for biochemical methods of synthesising desired compounds and is part of the broader class of preparation of nitrogen-containing organic compounds.
[C12P 13/10] Citrulline -- C12P 13/10 relates to methods of preparing the nitrogen-containing amino acid citrulline, especially using fermentation or enzyme-based steps and techniques for isolating it from racemic mixtures. It falls under the broader category of biochemical processes for synthesising or separating desired chemical compounds.
[C12P 13/12] Methionine -- C12P 13/12 relates to processes for preparing the sulfur-containing amino acid methionine, a nitrogen-rich organic compound, by chemical synthesis, fermentation, enzymatic routes or the resolution of racemic mixtures.
[C12P 13/14] Glutamic acid -- This subclass covers processes for preparing glutamic acid, a nitrogen-containing organic compound, using fermentation or enzyme-using methods, and includes synthesis as well as separation of its optical isomers.
[C12P 13/16] using surfactants, fatty acids or fatty acid esters, i.e. having at least seven carbon atoms in an unbroken chain bound to a carboxyl group or a carboxyl ester group -- C12P 13/16 covers biocatalytic or fermentation routes for making nitrogen-containing organic compounds that employ long-chain surfactants, fatty acids or their esters (with at least seven continuous carbon atoms) as media, co-solvents or phase-transfer agents to improve reaction efficiency or product recovery. This includes processes where such amphiphiles aid synthesis or isolation of amines, heterocycles or related nitrogenous products.
[C12P 13/18] using biotin or its derivatives -- C12P 13/18 covers fermentative or enzyme-based processes that employ biotin or its derivatives as cofactors, growth factors or carrier molecules in the preparation of nitrogen-containing organic compounds. The subclass includes the use of biotin-linked enzymes or biotin-mediated steps for synthesis or for separating optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 13/20] Aspartic acid -- The CPC code C12P 13/20 covers biotechnological routes for producing aspartic acid, including microbial fermentation, enzymatic synthesis and related bioconversion steps, as well as downstream methods for isolating the amino acid from complex mixtures. It applies to processes that generate this nitrogen-containing organic compound or separate its optical forms within the broader field of fermentation-based preparation of nitrogen-containing compounds.
[C12P 13/22] Tryptophan -- This subclass covers biotechnological processes for producing the amino acid tryptophan, an indole-containing nitrogen heterocycle used in nutrition, pharmaceuticals and research, via microbial fermentation or enzymatic synthesis. It is situated in the CPC group for preparation of nitrogen-containing organic compounds.
[C12P 13/222] Phenylalanine -- C12P 13/222 covers processes for preparing the amino acid phenylalanine, typically by microbial fermentation or enzymatic conversion of precursor substrates, falling under the CPC class for preparation of nitrogen-containing organic compounds using biotechnological methods.
[C12P 13/225] Tyrosine; 3,4-Dihydroxyphenylalanine -- C12P 13/225 covers enzymatic or fermentative methods for preparing nitrogen-containing organic compounds, specifically the amino acids tyrosine and 3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (L-DOPA). The class includes processes that synthesize these substances or separate their optical isomers from racemic mixtures, falling under biochemical fermentation techniques for desired chemical production.
[C12P 13/227] Tryptophan -- C12P 13/227 refers to methods for preparing the amino acid tryptophan, a nitrogen-containing organic compound, by fermentation or enzyme-catalysed processes, including routes that separate its optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 13/24] Proline -- C12P 13/24 covers processes for producing the amino acid proline, a nitrogen-containing organic compound, by chemical synthesis, biotechnological fermentation, or related methods, including the separation of its optical isomers from racemic mixtures. It falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, and fermentation or enzyme-using processes for preparing desired compounds.
[C12P 15/00] Preparation of compounds containing at least three condensed carbocyclic rings -- C12P 15/00 concerns the preparation of chemical compounds that contain three or more fused carbocyclic rings, typically through biotechnological or enzymatic routes. It falls within the broader class of fermentation or enzyme-using processes used to synthesize target molecules or to separate optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 17/00] Preparation of heterocyclic carbon compounds with only O, N, S, Se or Te as ring hetero atoms -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Peptides, proteins and antibodies -> C07K
[C12P 17/02] Oxygen as only ring hetero atoms -- C12P 17/02 covers methods for preparing heterocyclic carbon compounds whose rings contain only oxygen as the hetero atom, excluding nitrogen, sulfur, selenium or tellurium. It applies to processes, including fermentation or enzyme-mediated routes, that produce the target compound or separate its optical isomers from a racemic mixture. This subclass falls under the broader chemistry hierarchy for biochemical synthesis of desired chemicals.
[C12P 17/04] containing a five-membered hetero ring, e.g. griseofulvin -- C12P 17/04 covers methods for preparing five-membered hetero-rings that contain only O, N, S, Se or Te as hetero atoms, especially when the target is a biologically active compound such as griseofulvin. The processes may employ fermentation or enzyme-based steps to synthesize the compound or to resolve optical isomers from a racemic mixture.
[C12P 17/06] containing a six-membered hetero ring, e.g. fluorescein -- The CPC subclass C12P 17/06 covers processes that prepare heterocyclic carbon compounds whose six-membered rings contain only oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, selenium or tellurium atoms, exemplified by substances such as fluorescein, and is situated under enzyme-using or fermentation methods for synthesising desired chemicals.
[C12P 17/08] containing a hetero ring of at least seven ring members, e.g. zearalenone, macrolide aglycons -- This subgroup covers the preparation of heterocyclic carbon compounds where the hetero-ring contains seven or more atoms and includes only O, N, S, Se or Te as heteroatoms, exemplified by zearalenone and macrolide aglycons.
[C12P 17/10] Nitrogen as only ring hetero atom -- C12P 17/10, titled Nitrogen as only ring hetero atom, refers to methods for preparing heterocyclic carbon compounds whose ring contains only nitrogen as the hetero element, excluding oxygen, sulfur, selenium or tellurium. It falls under the CPC subclass covering fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesis or optical isomer separation.
[C12P 17/12] containing a six-membered hetero ring -- C12P 17/12 designates processes for preparing heterocyclic carbon compounds that contain a six-membered ring in which the only heteroatoms are oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, selenium or tellurium. The class falls within the fermentation or enzyme-using methods for synthesising target chemicals or separating optical isomers. It is part of the CPC hierarchy under chemistry → biochemistry → fermentation/enzyme-using processes.
[C12P 17/14] Nitrogen or oxygen as hetero atom and at least one other diverse hetero ring atom in the same ring
[C12P 17/16] containing two or more hetero rings -- C12P 17/16 covers processes for preparing heterocyclic carbon compounds that contain two or more hetero rings where the hetero atoms are limited to oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, selenium or tellurium. It falls under fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesising chemicals, specifically within biochemistry. The classification applies to methods that assemble such multi-heterocycle structures.
[C12P 17/162] Heterorings having oxygen atoms as the only ring heteroatoms, e.g. Lasalocid -- C12P 17/162 classifies processes for preparing heterocyclic carbon compounds whose rings contain only oxygen atoms as hetero-atoms, such as the ionophore lasalocid. The subclass falls under fermentation or enzyme-using processes used to synthesize desired chemicals or separate optical isomers.
[C12P 17/165] Heterorings having nitrogen atoms as the only ring heteroatoms -- C12P 17/165 covers the preparation of heterocyclic carbon compounds in which nitrogen is the sole hetero-atom in the ring. It includes methods, reagents and processes for synthesising such nitrogen-only heterorings, whether by cyclisation, substitution or other chemical transformations, and addresses related purification steps.
[C12P 17/167] Heterorings having sulfur atoms as ring heteroatoms, e.g. vitamin B1, thiamine nucleus and open chain analogs -- C12P 17/167 covers the preparation of heterocyclic carbon compounds that contain only sulfur as the ring heteroatom, exemplified by the thiamine (vitamin B1) nucleus and related open-chain analogues. These processes fall within the broader class of fermentation or enzyme-using methods for synthesising desired chemicals or separating optical isomers.
[C12P 17/18] containing at least two hetero rings condensed among themselves or condensed with a common carbocyclic ring system, e.g. rifamycin -- The C12P 17/18 subclass covers processes for preparing heterocyclic carbon compounds that contain only O, N, S, Se or Te and feature at least two hetero-rings fused together or condensed with a common carbocyclic ring system, such as the rifamycin family. It is used for describing biocatalytic or chemical syntheses that create these multi-ring heterocyclic structures or separate their optical isomers.
[C12P 17/181] Heterocyclic compounds containing oxygen atoms as the only ring heteroatoms in the condensed system, e.g. Salinomycin, Septamycin -- C12P 17/181 covers the preparation of heterocyclic carbon compounds whose condensed ring system contains only oxygen as the heteroatom, such as salinomycin and septamycin. This subclass falls under fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesising desired chemicals and is part of the broader chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy.
[C12P 17/182] Heterocyclic compounds containing nitrogen atoms as the only ring heteroatoms in the condensed system  alloxazine or isoalloxazine, e.g. riboflavine  C12P25/00
[C12P 17/183] containing an indolo[4,3-F,G]quinoleine nucleus, e.g. compound containing the lysergic acid nucleus as well as the dimeric ergot nucleus -- The CPC subclass C12P 17/183 covers heterocyclic carbon compounds whose ring system includes an indolo[4,3-F,G]quinoline nucleus, such as lysergic-acid derivatives and dimeric ergot structures. It is assigned to fermentation or enzyme-using processes that synthesise these compounds or separate their optical isomers from racemic mixtures, falling under the broader chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy.
[C12P 17/184] containing a beta-lactam ring, e.g. thienamycin -- The CPC subclass C12P 17/184 covers processes for preparing heterocyclic carbon compounds in which the only heteroatoms in the ring are oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, selenium or tellurium, specifically those that contain a beta-lactam ring such as thienamycin. These methods include fermentation or enzyme-based synthesis and can be used to separate optical isomers from racemic mixtures.

It falls under fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesising or separating chemicals, a subset of biochemical methods for preparing heterocyclic carbon compounds.
[C12P 17/186] containing a 2-oxo-thieno[3,4-d]imidazol nucleus, e.g. Biotin -- The C12P 17/186 subclass covers heterocyclic carbon compounds whose rings contain only O, N, S, Se or Te and specifically includes molecules that incorporate a 2-oxo-thieno[3,4-d]imidazol nucleus, such as biotin, prepared by fermentation or enzyme-mediated processes and useful for separating optical isomers.

It is used to synthesize target chemicals or to separate optical isomers from racemic mixtures. This subclass falls under chemistry → biochemistry → fermentation or enzyme-using processes for desired compounds.
[C12P 19/00] Preparation of compounds containing saccharide radicals -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sugars (saccharides) prepared by enzymatic and/or fermentative methods.  For the definition of the term "saccharide radical" the same criteria as in C07H apply, i.e.:  A "saccharide radical" is derived from acyclic polyhydroxy-aldehydes or acyclic polyhydroxy-ketones, or from their cyclic tautomers,  in accordance with either of the following definitions it:  a) consists of an uninterrupted carbon skeleton and oxygen atoms directly attached thereto, and;  b) is considered to be terminated by every bond to a carbon atom of a cyclic structure and by every bond to a carbon atom having three bonds to hetero atoms, e.g. ester or nitrile radicals, and;  c) contains within the carbon skeleton an unbranched sequence of at the most six carbon atoms in which at least three carbon atoms have one single bond to an oxygen atom as the only hetero bond (at least two in the case of a skeleton having only four carbon atoms, but at least three for compounds in which one or more carbon to oxygen bonds involved in a) or b) has been replaced by a carbon bond to a hetero atom other than oxygen), and  i) in a cyclic or acyclic sequence, at least one other carbon atom (that is not doubly bound to a carbon atom, e.g. glycals) has two single bonds to oxygen atoms as the only hetero bonds, or  ii) in an acyclic sequence, at least one other carbon atom (that is not doubly bound to a carbon atom) has one double bond to an oxygen atom as the only hetero bond;  d) has in the gamma or delta position in respect to the carbon atom bearing those two single bonds or this double bond to oxygen a carbon atom bearing one single bond to oxygen  e) is also a radical derived from a radical as defined in a) above by replacing at the most four of the specified hetero bonds to oxygen by the same number of hetero bonds to halogen, nitrogen, sulfur, selenium, or tellurium.  The terms "heterocyclic radical" or "hetero ring" are considered to exclude saccharide radicals as defined above.  Note: sorbitol and xylitol are NOT saccharide radicals, but they are polyhydric alcohols (classified in C12P7/18 )  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Keto-aldonic acids -> C12P7/58 - Measuring or testing processes involving enzymes or microorganisms: nucleic acid amplification reactions -> C12Q1/6844 - Purification of sugar juices using MOs or enzymes -> C13B20/002 - Biochemical treatment of fibres, threads, yarns, fabrics or fibrous goods made from such materials, e.g. enzymatic -> D06M16/00 - Process for obtaining cellulose by treatment of cellulose-containing material with MOs or enzymes -> D21C5/005
[C12P 19/02] Monosaccharides -- C12P 19/02 covers methods for preparing compounds that contain monosaccharide radicals, i.e., single sugar units attached to larger molecules, typically via chemical or enzymatic processes. The class includes synthesis of glycosyl derivatives used as intermediates, protecting groups, or building blocks in carbohydrate chemistry.
[C12P 19/04] Polysaccharides, i.e. compounds containing more than five saccharide radicals attached to each other by glycosidic bonds -- C12P 19/04 relates to polysaccharides, defined as compounds composed of more than five saccharide units linked by glycosidic bonds. The class includes natural and engineered polymers such as starches, cellulose derivatives, and other high-molecular-weight carbohydrates used in fermentation, enzymatic synthesis, or for separating optical isomers. It falls under the broader category of fermentation or enzyme-using processes for chemical synthesis.
[C12P 19/06] Xanthan, i.e. Xanthomonas-type heteropolysaccharides -- The C12P 19/06 classification covers xanthan, a heteropolysaccharide produced by Xanthomonas bacteria. It includes processes for fermentative synthesis, isolation and purification of this high-molecular-weight polysaccharide, which is widely used as a thickening, stabilising and emulsifying agent in food, cosmetics and industrial applications.
[C12P 19/08] Dextran -- Dextran is a high-molecular-weight polysaccharide composed of α-1,6-linked glucose units produced by fermentation with certain bacteria. The CPC subclass C12P 19/08 covers processes for preparing saccharide-radical compounds where dextran is the target product, including its synthesis, modification and recovery from fermentation broths.
[C12P 19/10] Pullulan -- C12P 19/10 covers processes for preparing pullulan, a water-soluble extracellular polysaccharide composed of repeated maltotriose units linked α-1,6, typically produced by the fungus Aureobasidium pullulans. The entry includes microbial fermentation, purification and chemical modification methods to obtain pullulan or its derivatives for uses such as thickeners, film formers or drug delivery matrices.
[C12P 19/12] Disaccharides -- C12P 19/12 covers the preparation of disaccharides, compounds made of two saccharide units, using biochemical or enzymatic methods such as fermentation. It includes processes that synthesize, modify or isolate disaccharides from mixtures, especially when targeting specific optical isomers.
[C12P 19/14] produced by the action of a carbohydrase , e.g. by alpha-amylase -- C12P 19/14 covers processes in which a carbohydrase such as alpha-amylase is used to produce compounds containing saccharide radicals, typically in fermentation or enzyme-using synthesis of desired chemicals or separation of optical isomers.
[C12P 19/16] produced by the action of an alpha-1, 6-glucosidase, e.g. amylose, debranched amylopectin -- This subclass covers processes in which alpha-1,6-glucosidase enzymes break down polysaccharides such as amylose or debranched amylopectin to produce saccharide radicals. It falls under fermentation or enzyme-using methods for synthesising desired chemicals or separating optical isomers.
[C12P 19/18] produced by the action of a glycosyl transferase, e.g. alpha-, beta- or gamma-cyclodextrins -- The subclass covers enzymatic routes that use glycosyl-transferases to build cyclodextrins, namely alpha-, beta- or gamma-cyclodextrin, from appropriate carbohydrate precursors. These processes involve the transfer of glucose units to form cyclic oligosaccharides and fall under the preparation of compounds containing saccharide radicals.
[C12P 19/20] produced by the action of an exo-1,4 alpha-glucosidase, e.g. dextrose -- C12P 19/20 covers enzyme-catalyzed processes that use exo-1,4-alpha-glucosidase to generate saccharide radicals such as dextrose, typically in the preparation of carbohydrate-based compounds. The class falls under fermentation or enzyme-using methods for synthesising desired chemicals or separating optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 19/22] produced by the action of a beta-amylase, e.g. maltose -- C12P 19/22 covers methods that use beta-amylase to convert starch or related polysaccharides into saccharide products such as maltose, focusing on enzymatic generation of these sugars in fermentation or synthesis processes. It includes the preparation of compounds containing saccharide radicals where the beta-amylase-catalyzed cleavage yields the desired oligosaccharide.
[C12P 19/24] produced by the action of an isomerase, e.g. fructose -- This subclass covers enzymatic methods that produce saccharide-containing compounds by the action of isomerases, such as the conversion of glucose to fructose, and includes processes for preparing saccharide-radical derivatives or separating optical isomers in fermentation or other enzyme-using syntheses.
[C12P 19/26] Preparation of nitrogen-containing carbohydrates -- The CPC subclass C12P 19/26 covers processes for preparing nitrogen-containing carbohydrates, i.e., saccharide derivatives in which one or more nitrogen atoms are incorporated such as amino sugars or glycosylamines. It includes enzymatic or fermentation-based methods that attach nitrogen-bearing groups to saccharide radicals, facilitating the synthesis of functionalized sugars or the resolution of racemic mixtures.
[C12P 19/28] N-glycosides -- C12P 19/28 covers the preparation of N-glycosides, where a saccharide unit is linked to a nitrogen-containing aglycone through a glycosidic bond. The subclass deals with methods for synthesising such compounds, often via enzymatic or fermentation routes, and includes processes for separating optical isomers of the products.
[C12P 19/30] Nucleotides -- C12P 19/30 relates to the enzymatic or fermentative preparation of nucleotides, covering processes that construct or modify saccharide-radical compounds containing a nucleobase, sugar and phosphate, as well as methods for isolating or separating such nucleotides from mixtures.
[C12P 19/305] Pyrimidine nucleotides -- This subclass covers methods for preparing pyrimidine nucleotides, which are nucleotides that contain a pyrimidine base such as cytosine, uracil or thymine, often linked to a saccharide radical. It includes chemical, biochemical or enzymatic routes for synthesizing, modifying or isolating these compounds, especially when derived from fermentation or enzyme-catalyzed processes.
[C12P 19/32] having a condensed ring system containing a six-membered ring having two N-atoms in the same ring, e.g. purine nucleotides, nicotineamide-adenine dinucleotide -- C12P 19/32 covers processes for preparing compounds that contain a condensed ring system with a six-membered ring bearing two nitrogen atoms, such as purine nucleotides and nicotinamide-adenine dinucleotide. It falls under fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesizing desired chemicals and separating optical isomers.
[C12P 19/34] Polynucleotides, e.g. nucleic acids, oligoribonucleotides -- C12P 19/34 is a CPC subclass within chemistry → biochemistry → fermentation or enzyme-using processes to synthesize a desired chemical compound or to separate optical isomers, focusing on the preparation of compounds that contain saccharide radicals. It specifically covers the production of polynucleotides, including nucleic acids and oligoribonucleotides, using enzymatic or biotechnological methods.
[C12P 19/36] Dinucleotides, e.g. nicotineamide-adenine dinucleotide phosphate -- The subclass C12P 19/36 covers processes for preparing dinucleotides, such as nicotinamide-adenine dinucleotide phosphate, where a saccharide radical is linked to two nucleobases. It includes enzymatic or fermentative methods for synthesising, modifying or isolating these nucleotide compounds.
[C12P 19/38] Nucleosides -- The CPC subclass C12P 19/38 covers processes for preparing nucleosides, compounds that include a saccharide radical linked to a nucleobase, using fermentation or enzyme-based methods. It falls under the broader category of biochemistry and the specific area of synthesising desired chemical compounds or separating optical isomers.
[C12P 19/385] Pyrimidine nucleosides -- C12P 19/385 covers pyrimidine nucleosides, which are nucleoside compounds containing a pyrimidine base attached to a sugar moiety. These substances are used in pharmaceutical, biotechnological and research applications, and the classification includes their preparation, derivatization and use in therapeutic or diagnostic contexts.

[C12P 19/42] Cobalamins, i.e. vitamin B -- The CPC subclass C12P 19/42 covers processes for preparing vitamin B12 (cobalamins) and related compounds that contain saccharide radicals, including methods that attach carbohydrate groups to the cobalamin core to modify solubility or biological activity.
[C12P 19/44] Preparation of O-glycosides, e.g. glucosides -- C12P 19/44 covers methods for preparing O-glycosides such as glucosides, where a saccharide unit is linked to an aglycone through an oxygen atom. It includes chemical routes to form the glycosidic bond and related steps for activating, protecting or deprotecting the saccharide radicals.
[C12P 19/445] The saccharide radical is condensed with a heterocyclic radical, e.g. everninomycin, papulacandin -- C12P 19/445 covers the preparation of compounds in which a saccharide radical is joined to a heterocyclic radical, for example to produce conjugates such as everninomycin or papulacandin derivatives. The class includes methods for condensing the sugar moiety with heterocyclic groups to obtain novel bioactive compounds.
[C12P 19/46] having an oxygen atom of the saccharide radical bound to a cyclohexyl radical, e.g. kasugamycin -- C12P 19/46 covers the preparation of saccharide-derived compounds in which the oxygen atom of the sugar radical is covalently linked to a cyclohexyl group, a structural motif found in antibiotics such as kasugamycin. It includes enzymatic or fermentation processes used to synthesize these glycosyl-cyclohexyl derivatives or to separate their optical isomers.
[C12P 19/48] the cyclohexyl radical being substituted by two or more nitrogen atoms, e.g. destomycin, neamin -- C12P 19/48 classifies the preparation of saccharide-derived compounds in which a cyclohexyl radical is substituted by two or more nitrogen atoms, such as the antibiotics destomycin and neamin. It falls under the broader category of fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesising desired chemicals or separating optical isomers. This subclass is part of the CPC hierarchy for chemistry, biochemistry and fer­mentation-based synthesis.

It lies within the broader category of fermentation or enzyme-using processes for preparing saccharide-radical compounds.
[C12P 19/50] having two saccharide radicals bound through only oxygen to adjacent ring carbon atoms of the cyclohexyl radical, e.g. ambutyrosin, ribostamycin -- C12P 19/50 covers compounds in which two saccharide radicals are linked solely by oxygen atoms to adjacent carbon atoms of a cyclohexyl radical, forming structures such as ambutyrosin and ribostamycin. [C12P 21/00] Preparation of peptides or proteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  General enzymatic and/or fermentative methods to produce peptides or proteins. This comprises methods of general applicability not restricted to a specific protein (except expression vectors), such as in vitro translation systems, or methods indicating precise fermentation conditions (e.g. specific growth conditions) for the production of a peptide or protein ( C12P21/00 and C12P21/02 ), for glycosylation methods ( C12P21/005 ), or methods wherein a protein is obtained by hydrolysis ( C12P21/06 ).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Macromolecular products derived from proteins -> C08H1/00 - Microorganisms (compositions containing them, processes of propagating, maintaining, preserving them, culture media), and especially single cell protein prepared thereof -> C12N1/00
[C12P 21/005] Glycopeptides, glycoproteins -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods to produce glycosylated peptides/proteins. "Glycosylated" means covalent attachment of one or more sugar molecules to the protein or peptide, wherein a "sugar" is defined as above ( C12P19/00 ). This includes methods to specifically glycosylate proteins, to modify the glycosylation pattern, and/or to deglycosylate glycoproteins (wherein the produced protein still contains at least one glycosyl group).
[C12P 21/02] having a known sequence of two or more amino acids, e.g. glutathione -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods which produce peptides or proteins having a known sequence, wherein details of the fermentation conditions are indicated. This comprises methods indicating specific growth conditions such as medium ingredients, pH, temperature, aeration, time intervals etc.) for the production of a peptide or protein of known sequence (this may be a native peptide or protein or a recombinantly expressed peptide or protein).  Also comprised are methods of general applicability not restricted to a specific peptide or protein, such as in vitro translation methods which methods intrinsically produce proteins having a known sequence. Excepted from this are methods defined only by the use of expression vectors (these are classified in C12N15/63 ).  Recombinant gene expression methods without the indication of further details about the fermentation conditions are not classified in C12P , but instead in C07K and/or C12N (see above under C12P21/00 ).
[C12P 21/06] produced by the hydrolysis of a peptide bond, e.g. hydrolysate products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods to produce hydrolysed proteins, i.e. wherein a peptide bond of a peptide or protein has been cleaved. Examples are methods to produce a protein hydrolysate using a peptidase or a protease, or methods to produce a specific protein involving the hydrolysis of a precursor protein.
[C12P 2201/00] Pretreatment of cellulosic or lignocellulosic material for subsequent enzymatic treatment or hydrolysis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for the pretreatment of the cellulosic or lignocellulosic material to facilitate enzymatic decomposition or to enhance digestibility e.g. by cellulases, wherein the pretreatment method is an essential part of the method and constitutes the technical contribution to the field.
[C12P 2203/00] Fermentation products obtained from optionally pretreated or hydrolyzed cellulosic or lignocellulosic material as the carbon source -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for producing fermentation products other than ethanol from (ligno)cellulosic material, wherein the carbon source for growth of the microorganism or the substrate to be converted by the microorganism or by the enzyme comprises (ligno)cellulosic material or material immediately derived therefrom.  Examples are methods wherein in a first step, sugars are obtained by hydrolysis of lignocellulose, which are then fermented to produce a product of interest.  Methods that only pertain to the production of sugars from (ligno)cellulose using hydrolases, with no follow-up fermentation product specified, are sufficiently described by the symbol C12P19/14 and should therefore normally not be additionally classified in C12P2203/00 . Exceptions may be methods wherein a specific sugar or sugar derivative is produced.

[C12P 3/00] Preparation of elements or inorganic compounds except carbon dioxide -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of inorganic compounds, i.e. all chemical compounds except organic compounds. Comprised within this group are inorganic gases (except carbon dioxide), fluids or solids including inorganic salts or inorganic polymers, minerals and metals.  Salts of organic compounds are not classified in C12P3/00 , but are instead classified as that organic compound ( C12P5/00 - C12P37/00 ).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Extraction of metal compounds from ores or concentrates using MOs or enzymes -> C22B3/18

[C12P 31/005] by fermentation or enzyme-using processes from marine organisms, e.g. Plexaura Homomalla -- The CPC subgroup C12P 31/005 covers fermentation or enzyme-driven methods that employ marine organisms, such as Plexaura homomalla, to synthesize five-membered heterocyclic compounds bearing two ortho-adjacent side-chains, at least one oxygen bound to the ring, and the described functional group pattern, exemplified by prostaglandins. This includes processes that generate such structures and separate optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 33/00] Preparation of steroids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of compounds having the steroid skeleton, e.g. cholesterol:
[C12P 33/005] Degradation of the lateral chains at position 17 -- The C12P 33/005 entry covers methods for removing or shortening the side-chain attached to carbon-17 of a steroid nucleus. It includes chemical or enzymatic steps that cleave, oxidise or otherwise degrade the lateral group at position 17 to produce the desired steroid core or intermediate.
[C12P 33/02] Dehydrogenating -- C12P 33/02 covers the use of dehydrogenase enzymes to convert steroid intermediates during biotechnological synthesis, including oxidation steps that introduce double bonds or alter functional groups in steroid molecules.
[C12P 33/04] Forming an aryl ring from A ring -- C12P 33/04 covers biocatalytic or enzymatic methods that create an aryl (aromatic) ring on the A-ring of a steroid framework, a key step in the microbial or enzymatic preparation of steroid compounds. The techniques include ring-forming reactions that convert the non-aromatic A-ring into a fused aryl system, facilitating the synthesis of complex steroid derivatives.
[C12P 33/06] Hydroxylating -- C12P 33/06 covers enzymatic hydroxylation steps employed in the biotechnological preparation of steroids, where microorganisms or isolated enzymes introduce hydroxyl groups into the steroid skeleton to modify its properties or enable further conversion. The subclass is part of the broader category of fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesising desired compounds or separating optical isomers. It specifically addresses the use of hydroxylating reactions in steroid production.
[C12P 33/08] at 11 position -- C12P 33/08 defines biotechnological methods for preparing steroids in which a functional group is introduced, altered or removed at the 11-position of the steroid skeleton, often by microbial or enzymatic oxidation, reduction or substitution to obtain specific pharmaceutical intermediates.
[C12P 33/10] at 11 alpha-position -- C12P 33/10 covers methods for preparing steroids that involve chemical or biocatalytic transformations at the 11-alpha position of the steroid nucleus. It includes steps to introduce, modify, or protect functional groups at that site, often to influence biological activity or enable further synthesis.
[C12P 33/12] Acting on D ring -- C12P 33/12 covers biotechnological methods that modify the D ring of steroid structures, such as enzymatic oxidation, reduction, or functional group introduction, employed in the preparation or derivatization of steroids. It belongs to the CPC group for fermentation or enzyme-using processes for steroid preparation.
[C12P 33/14] Hydroxylating at 16 position -- The CPC subclass C12P 33/14 covers methods for introducing a hydroxyl group at the 16-position of a steroid nucleus, encompassing both chemical and enzymatic steps used in the preparation of steroid compounds. It is a specific process within the broader field of fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesising desired chemicals and separating optical isomers.
[C12P 33/16] Acting at 17 position -- C12P 33/16 covers processes for preparing steroid compounds where a chemical modification or functional group is introduced at the carbon-17 position of the steroid nucleus. This includes selective reactions, protecting-group strategies, and conversion steps that target the 17-position to produce pharmaceutical or research steroids.
[C12P 33/18] Hydroxylating at 17 position -- C12P 33/18 covers enzymatic or biocatalytic methods that introduce a hydroxyl group at the 17-position of steroid molecules during their synthesis. It is used in the preparation of pharmaceuticals and other bioactive steroids where precise modification of the 17-carbon is required.
[C12P 33/20] containing heterocyclic rings -- The code C12P 33/20 refers to biotechnological methods for preparing steroids that contain heterocyclic ring structures. It includes enzyme- or fermentation-based syntheses and separations where the target steroid incorporates a heterocycle, such as a pyridine or thiazole moiety, within its molecular framework.
[C12P 35/00] Preparation of compounds having a 5-thia-1-azabicyclo [4.2.0] octane ring system, e.g. cephalosporin -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of compounds containing the 5-thia-1-azabicyclo [4.2.0] octane ring system, e.g. cephalosporin:
[C12P 35/02] by desacylation of the substituent in the 7 position -- This subclass covers processes for preparing 5-thia-1-azabicyclo[4.2.0]octane-containing compounds, such as cephalosporins, by removing the acyl group attached to the 7-position of the nucleus. The method involves a desacylation step that converts a 7-acylated intermediate into the desired bicyclic product.
[C12P 35/04] by acylation of the substituent in the 7 position -- C12P 35/04 covers the preparation of 5-thia-1-azabicyclo[4.2.0]octane-based compounds, for example cephalosporin derivatives, by acylating the substituent at the 7-position. The subclass falls under fermentation or enzyme-using processes used to synthesize desired chemicals or to separate optical isomers of racemic mixtures.
[C12P 35/06] Cephalosporin C -- C12P 35/06 covers methods for preparing cephalosporin C and related molecules that contain a 5-thia-1-azabicyclo[4.2.0]octane ring system. It is situated in the chemistry hierarchy for biochemical synthesis, specifically fermentation or enzyme-using processes that generate target compounds or separate optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 35/08] disubstituted in the 7 position -- This CPC subclass covers enzymatic or fermentation processes for preparing cephalosporin-type compounds that contain a 5-thia-1-azabicyclo[4.2.0]octane ring system with two substituents attached at the 7-position, as well as related methods for separating the resulting optical isomers.
[C12P 37/00] Preparation of compounds having a 4-thia-1-azabicyclo [3.2.0] heptane ring system, e.g. penicillin -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparation of compounds having a 4-thia-1-azabicyclo [3.2.0] heptane ring system, e.g. penicillin:
[C12P 37/02] in presence of phenylacetic acid or phenylacetamide or their derivatives -- C12P 37/02 covers fermentative or enzyme-mediated methods for making 4-thia-1-azabicyclo[3.2.0]heptane-containing compounds, such as penicillins, where phenylacetic acid, phenylacetamide or their derivatives are employed as substrates or precursors in the preparation process.
[C12P 37/04] by acylation of the substituent in the 6 position -- C12P 37/04 covers the acylation of the substituent at the 6-position of compounds containing a 4-thia-1-azabicyclo[3.2.0]heptane ring system, such as penicillins, in fermentation or enzyme-using processes aimed at preparing the desired chemical or separating optical isomers.
[C12P 37/06] by desacylation of the substituent in the 6 position -- C12P 37/06 pertains to enzymatic or fermentative processes that produce 4-thia-1-azabicyclo[3.2.0]heptane ring systems, including penicillin, by removing the acyl group attached to the substituent at the sixth position of the molecule. This sub-class falls under the broader category of fermentation or enzyme-using methods for synthesising target chemicals or separating optical isomers.
[C12P 39/00] Processes involving microorganisms of different genera in the same process, simultaneously -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes wherein two or more species of microorganisms of different genera are used together to produce a product.  The microorganisms may be in direct contact with each other like in a consortium. Alternatively, the microorganisms may also be separated by a physical barrier (e.g. a permeable membrane), but still are indirectly in contact via a medium (e.g. aqueous buffer).  Such processes may involve the co-transformation of a substrate, or a stepwise transformation wherein a first microorganism produces an intermediate that is transformed by a second microorganism. Alternatively, the microorganisms are not each involved in product formation but act together as a consortium, e.g. as symbionts, wherein one type of microorganism supports growth of another.
[C12P 41/00] Processes using enzymes or microorganisms to separate optical isomers from a racemic mixture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods to obtain an optical isomer (=enantiomer) of a compound from the racemic mixture of that compound or of a precursor substrate that is not optically active, using an enzyme or a microorganism.  Such methods may comprise:
[C12P 41/001] by metabolizing one of the enantiomers -- A method in which a microorganism or enzyme selectively metabolizes one enantiomer of a racemic mixture, thereby removing that enantiomer and allowing the remaining counterpart to be isolated, is covered by CPC code C12P 41/001. This approach uses biological or enzymatic activity to achieve chiral separation without chemical modification of the desired enantiomer.
[C12P 41/002] by oxidation/reduction reactions -- C12P 41/002 relates to biocatalytic processes that resolve racemic mixtures by selective oxidation-reduction, using enzymes or microorganisms to separate optical isomers. The class addresses methods that exploit redox reactions to achieve chiral purification of desired compounds.
[C12P 41/003] by ester formation, lactone formation or the inverse reactions -- C12P 41/003 relates to enzymatic or microbial methods for resolving racemic mixtures by forming or breaking esters or lactones, i.e., using esterification, lactonization or the reverse reactions to separate optical isomers. The process is classified under fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesizing desired compounds within the chemistry and biochemistry sections of the CPC.
[C12P 41/004] by esterification of alcohol- or thiol groups in the enantiomers or the inverse reaction -- C12P 41/004 covers methods that separate optical isomers from a racemic mixture by esterifying the alcohol or thiol groups of the enantiomers, or by reversing that esterification. The approach employs enzymes or microorganisms to selectively modify one enantiomer, enabling its isolation from the mixture.
[C12P 41/005] by esterification of carboxylic acid groups in the enantiomers or the inverse reaction -- C12P 41/005 covers processes that separate optical isomers from a racemic mixture by esterifying the carboxyl groups of the enantiomers or by the reverse reaction. This method uses enzymatic or microbial catalysis to form or break ester bonds, facilitating resolution of the racemate.
[C12P 41/006] by reactions involving C-N bonds, e.g. nitriles, amides, hydantoins, carbamates, lactames, transamination reactions, or keto group formation from racemic mixtures -- C12P 41/006 covers biocatalytic methods that resolve racemic mixtures by forming or modifying C-N bonds. It includes enzymatic or microbial steps such as nitrile hydrolysis, amide formation, transamination, hydantoin or carbamate synthesis, and conversion of keto groups to achieve optical isomer separation.
[C12P 41/007] by reactions involving acyl derivatives of racemic amines -- C12P 41/007 concerns the resolution of racemic amines by first converting them into acyl derivatives and then applying enzymatic or microbial reactions that selectively convert one enantiomer, allowing the optical isomers to be separated.
[C12P 41/008] by reactions involving carbamates -- C12P 41/008 covers enzyme- or microorganism-mediated processes that resolve racemic mixtures through reactions involving carbamates. The methods typically form or hydrolyze carbamate derivatives to preferentially convert one enantiomer, enabling separation of optical isomers. It falls under the broader category of biochemical fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesizing desired chemicals.
[C12P 41/009] by reactions involving hydantoins or carbamoylamino compounds -- C12P 41/009 covers enzyme- or microorganism-based methods for resolving racemic mixtures by employing hydantoins or carbamoylamino compounds, enabling the selective separation of optical isomers through specific chemical reactions involving these substrates.
[C12P 5/00] Preparation of hydrocarbons -- **Definition:** This place covers: The preparation of hydrocarbons or halogenated hydrocarbons  **Glossary:** In this place, the following terms or expressions are used with the meaning indicated: Attention is drawn to the fact that, depending on the definition given in an application, a "diesel", "biodiesel" (or a "fuel" or "biofuel" in general) may be meant to designate a hydrocarbon ( C12P5/00 ), in contrast to a fatty acid ester ( C12P7/649 ) or other "biofuels" such as bioethanol ( C12P7/06 ). Similarly, the term "biogas" may designate a methane containing gas ( C12P5/023 ) or a hydrogen-containing gas ( C12P3/00 ) or mixtures thereof (in this case, both classes would be given).
[C12P 5/002] cyclic  compounds containing at least three condensed carbocyclic rings  C12P15/00
[C12P 5/005] aromatic  naphthacene  C12P29/00
[C12P 5/007] containing one or more isoprene units, i.e. terpenes  carotenes  C12P23/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The preparation of terpenes, i.e. compounds containing and/or built from isoprene units
[C12P 5/02] acyclic -- C12P 5/02 covers biotechnological methods for producing acyclic hydrocarbons, such as linear alkanes and alkenes, using fermentation or enzyme-catalyzed pathways. It includes processes that synthesize these molecules from renewable feedstocks or separate them from mixtures, focusing on non-cyclic structures.
[C12P 5/023] Methane -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bioreactors for gas production, e.g. biogas -> C12M21/04  **Glossary:** - Biogas: methane containing gas produced by fermentation; in some instances, the term is also used to designate other gases produced by fermentation, such as hydrogen
[C12P 5/026] Unsaturated compounds, i.e. alkenes, alkynes or allenes
[C12P 7/00] Preparation of oxygen-containing organic compounds -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Animal feeding stuffs from distillers' or brewers' waste -> A23K10/38 - Distillation or rectification of fermented solutions -> B01D3/001 - Refining of hydrocarbon oils by MOs -> C10G32/00
[C12P 7/02] containing a hydroxy group -- The subclass C12P 7/02 covers processes for preparing organic compounds that contain a hydroxy group, such as alcohols, using chemical or biotechnological methods. It includes synthesis steps and related techniques for producing these oxygen-containing molecules.
[C12P 7/04] acyclic -- The C12P 7/04 subclass covers enzymatic or fermentation processes used to prepare acyclic oxygen-containing organic compounds, such as alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, acids and esters, from simple precursors or to separate their optical isomers. It applies to biocatalytic routes that do not involve cyclic intermediates and includes methods for producing or isolating these linear molecules in pure form.
[C12P 7/06] Ethanol, i.e. non-beverage -- C12P 7/06 covers industrial processes that prepare ethanol by non-beverage fermentation or enzyme-using methods, focusing on the synthesis of this oxygen-containing organic compound and related separation techniques.
[C12P 7/065] with microorganisms other than yeasts -- C12P 7/065 includes methods that employ microorganisms other than yeasts, such as bacteria or filamentous fungi, for the biotechnological synthesis or optical-isomer separation of oxygen-containing organic compounds.
[C12P 7/08] produced as by-product or from waste or cellulosic material substrate -- C12P 7/08 covers fermentation or enzyme-mediated processes that produce oxygen-containing organic compounds as by-products, or that use waste streams or cellulosic feedstocks as the substrate for synthesis. It falls under the broader category of biochemical methods for preparing such compounds, emphasizing the valorisation of renewable or discarded material.
[C12P 7/10] substrate containing cellulosic material -- The C12P 7/10 subclass covers fermentative or enzymatic procedures that use a substrate containing cellulosic material to prepare oxygen-containing organic compounds, including the conversion of cellulose into target chemicals or the separation of optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 7/12] substrate containing sulfite waste liquor or citrus waste -- C12P 7/12 pertains to biotechnological methods that employ waste streams such as sulfite liquor or citrus residues as substrates for microbial or enzymatic fermentation to produce oxygen-containing organic compounds. The subclass covers the preparation of acids, alcohols, esters and similar products using these waste-derived feedstocks.
[C12P 7/14] Multiple stages of fermentation -- C12P 7/14 covers processes in which a target compound is produced through two or more sequential fermentation steps, often using different microorganisms or conditions to convert intermediates into the final oxygen-containing product, and may include separation of optical isomers.
[C12P 7/16] Butanols -- C12P 7/16 covers the biotechnological preparation of oxygen-containing butanol compounds, focusing on fermentation or enzyme-mediated routes that synthesize butanols and related derivatives, as well as methods for separating optical isomers of these products from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 7/18] polyhydric -- The CPC subclass C12P 7/18 covers biotechnological methods that use fermentation or enzyme-catalyzed steps to prepare polyhydric oxygen-containing organic compounds, such as sugars, polyols and related molecules, including their synthesis or separation from racemic mixtures. It falls under the broader category of preparing oxygen-containing compounds by biochemical processes.
[C12P 7/20] Glycerol -- C12P 7/20 relates to processes for preparing glycerol, a three-carbon polyol employed as a solvent, humectant and chemical feedstock, often obtained by fermenting sugars or chemically converting fats.
[C12P 7/22] aromatic -- **Glossary:** - Aromatic: refers to compounds containing an aromatic group, wherein the hydroxy group need not be directly attached to the aromatic group, i.e. compounds such as phenylethanol are also comprised in this group.
[C12P 7/24] containing a carbonyl group -- C12P 7/24 covers biochemical methods for preparing oxygen-containing organic compounds that include a carbonyl group, using fermentation or enzyme-based processes. It lies within the chemistry hierarchy under biochemistry and focuses on synthesis or optically active separation of such carbonyl-bearing molecules.
[C12P 7/26] Ketones -- This subclass covers fermentation- or enzyme-catalysed processes for making or isolating ketones, a type of oxygen-containing organic compound, including synthesis from precursors and separation of optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 7/28] Acetone-containing products -- C12P 7/28 covers the preparation of organic compounds that contain acetone, including solvents, intermediates and formulated products, using chemical or biotechnological methods. It falls under fermentation or enzyme-using processes for synthesising desired chemicals or separating optical isomers.
[C12P 7/30] produced from substrate containing inorganic compounds other than water -- This subclass deals with the preparation of oxygen-containing organic compounds by fermentation or enzyme-catalyzed processes that use a substrate containing inorganic compounds other than water. It falls under C12P 7/30 in the CPC, a narrow segment of biotechnology that focuses on biocatalytic synthesis from inorganic-rich feedstocks.
[C12P 7/32] produced from substrate containing inorganic nitrogen source -- C12P 7/32 covers fermentation or enzyme-using processes that prepare oxygen-containing organic compounds when the microbial or enzymatic substrate includes an inorganic nitrogen source, such as ammonium salts or nitrates, to produce the desired chemical or to separate optical isomers from a racemic mixture.
[C12P 7/34] produced from substrate containing protein as nitrogen source -- C12P 7/34 designates biotechnological processes in which a microorganism or enzyme uses a substrate that supplies protein as the nitrogen source to produce oxygen-containing organic compounds. The classification lies under fermentation or enzyme-using methods for synthesising target chemicals or separating optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 7/36] produced from substrate containing grain or cereal material -- This subclass covers biotechnological routes that convert grain- or cereal-based substrates into oxygen-containing organic compounds, using fermentation or enzyme-mediated processes. It includes procedures that employ wheat, corn, rice or similar materials as feedstock to produce acids, alcohols, esters or other oxygenated chemicals through microbial or enzymatic transformation.
[C12P 7/38] Cyclopentanone- or cyclopentadione-containing products -- C12P 7/38 covers the preparation and use of compounds that contain a cyclopentanone or cyclopentadione moiety, especially when they are obtained through biotechnological or enzymatic routes. The class includes methods for producing these oxygen-containing molecules and their application as intermediates or end-products in chemical synthesis.
[C12P 7/40] containing a carboxyl group -- C12P 7/40 covers methods for preparing oxygen-containing organic compounds in which the product includes a carboxyl group, such as carboxylic acids, esters or derivatives. The subclass addresses biochemical or enzymatic routes that introduce or retain the -COOH functionality during synthesis or separation, typically involving fermentation or enzyme-mediated transformations. It is part of the broader CPC group for preparing oxygen-containing organics.
[C12P 7/42] Hydroxy-carboxylic acids -- The C12P 7/42 subclass pertains to biotechnological or enzymatic routes for making hydroxy-carboxylic acids, a group of oxygen-bearing organic compounds. It includes fermentation or enzyme-driven processes that synthesize these acids or use them to separate optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 7/44] Polycarboxylic acids -- C12P 7/44 concerns the enzymatic or fermentative preparation of oxygen-containing organic compounds that are polycarboxylic acids, i.e. compounds bearing two or more carboxyl groups. The class includes methods for synthesising such acids and for separating optical isomers from racemic mixtures using biocatalytic techniques.
[C12P 7/46] Dicarboxylic acids having four or less carbon atoms, e.g. fumaric acid, maleic acid -- The CPC subclass C12P 7/46 covers dicarboxylic acids with four or fewer carbon atoms, such as fumaric acid and maleic acid, and includes processes for preparing oxygen-containing organic compounds using fermentation or enzyme-based methods.
[C12P 7/48] Tricarboxylic acids, e.g. citric acid -- The code C12P 7/48 covers processes for preparing tricarboxylic acids such as citric acid, falling under the preparation of oxygen-containing organic compounds. It pertains to fermentation or enzyme-using methods that synthesize or isolate these acids, often from carbohydrate feedstocks. This classification is part of the CPC subgroup for biochemical synthesis within chemistry.
[C12P 7/50] having keto groups, e.g. 2-ketoglutaric acid -- C12P 7/50 covers fermentation- or enzyme-based processes for preparing oxygen-containing organic compounds that possess keto groups, exemplified by substances such as 2-ketoglutaric acid. It falls under the broader class of biochemical methods for synthesising desired chemicals or separating optical isomers.
[C12P 7/52] Propionic acid -- C12P 7/52 covers biochemical or enzymatic processes for preparing propionic acid, an oxygen-containing organic acid, including its synthesis by fermentation and methods for separating optical isomers from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 7/54] Acetic acid -- C12P 7/54 covers methods for producing acetic acid, an oxygen-containing organic compound, using fermentation or enzyme-catalysed techniques, including its isolation from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 7/56] Lactic acid -- C12P 7/56 relates to processes for producing lactic acid, a hydroxy-carboxylic organic compound commonly obtained by microbial fermentation of sugars or by chemical synthesis, and includes methods for its isolation, purification and handling.
[C12P 7/58] Aldonic, ketoaldonic or saccharic acids -- This subgroup covers methods for preparing, using, isolating or separating aldonic, keto-aldonic and saccharic acids-oxidation products of sugars-within fermentative or enzyme-using processes that produce oxygen-containing organic compounds, including synthesis routes and purification techniques for these polyhydroxy acids and their derivatives.
[C12P 7/60] 2-Ketogulonic acid -- The CPC subclass C12P 7/60 covers methods for preparing 2-ketogulonic acid, an oxygen-containing organic compound, typically using fermentation or enzyme-catalysed processes to synthesize or isolate the molecule, often for subsequent conversion to vitamin C. It falls under the broader category of biochemical fermentation routes for desired chemicals and optical isomer separation.
[C12P 7/62] Carboxylic acid esters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This place covers the production of carboxylic acid esters, except fatty acid esters.
[C12P 7/625] Polyesters of hydroxy carboxylic acids -- C12P 7/625 covers the preparation of polyesters formed from hydroxy-carboxylic acids, typically produced by enzymatic or microbial polymerisation of monomers such as lactic or glycolic acid. The class includes processes for obtaining linear or branched polyesters used as biodegradable polymers, films or fibers.
[C12P 7/64] Fats -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of:  Nitrogen- or saccharide-containing lipids and any lipids containing a residue falling under product classes C12P11/00 - C12P33/00 should be classified both in C12P7/64 and in the appropriate place in C12P11/00 - C12P33/00 .
