Examples are (the appropriate classification is indicated in parentheses):  Figure: (left) a ceramide, (right) phosphatidylcholine (R= fatty acid acyl)  **Glossary:** - Esterification: Formation of an ester by reaction of an acid with an alcohol - Transesterification: Formation of an ester by the reaction of an ester with an alcohol (=alcoholysis), by reaction of an ester with an acid (=acidolysis) or by reaction of an ester with another ester (=interesterification, ester exchange) - Biodiesel or (bio)fuel: Fatty acid alkyl esters used as biodiesel, generally C 1 -C 3 alkyl (methyl, ethyl or propyl) esters or C 4 and C 5 alkyl esters of fatty acids - Fatty acid: Carboxylic acid having at least seven carbon atoms in an unbroken linear or branched chain bound to a carboxyl group, i.e. n-heptanoic acid and higher carboxylic acids, as well as unsaturated, or oxidized derivatives thereof, wherein oxidized derivative means that the carboxylic acid may contain hydroxy-, keto-, epoxy-, carboxy, peroxy, groups attached to the carbon chain.
[C12P 7/6409] Fatty acids -- C12P 7/6409 designates processes that use fermentation or enzyme-based methods to prepare oxygen-containing organic compounds that are fatty acids, or to separate their optical isomers from racemic mixtures. It covers synthesis, modification and purification steps involving fatty acids within biochemical production.
[C12P 7/6418] by hydrolysis of fatty acid esters -- C12P 7/6418 refers to biotechnological methods that employ hydrolysis of fatty-acid esters, typically using enzymes or fermentative microorganisms, to produce oxygen-containing organic compounds such as free fatty acids or related acids. The process may be used in the synthesis of target chemicals or in the resolution of racemic mixtures by separating optical isomers.
[C12P 7/6427] Polyunsaturated fatty acids [PUFA], i.e. having two or more double bonds in their backbone -- C12P 7/6427 relates to biotechnological methods for preparing polyunsaturated fatty acids, organic compounds that contain two or more carbon-carbon double bonds in their carbon chain. The subclass covers fermentation or enzyme-based synthesis and the separation of optical isomers from racemic mixtures of such fatty acids.
[C12P 7/6431] Linoleic acids [18:2[n-6]] -- The C12P 7/6431 subclass covers biocatalytic or enzymatic methods for producing or isolating linoleic acid (18:2 n-6), a polyunsaturated fatty acid containing oxygen. It includes fermentation-based synthesis, enzymatic alteration of the double-bond configuration, and resolution of optical isomers from racemic mixtures, all within the broader field of preparing oxygen-containing organic compounds.
[C12P 7/6432] Eicosapentaenoic acids [EPA] -- The CPC subclass C12P 7/6432 deals with the production or isolation of eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA) by fermentation or enzyme-catalyzed processes, fitting within the preparation of oxygen-containing organic compounds. It covers methods for synthesising EPA or separating it from racemic mixtures in biochemical contexts.
[C12P 7/6434] Docosahexenoic acids [DHA] -- C12P 7/6434 covers methods for producing docosahexenoic acid, a long-chain polyunsaturated omega-3 fatty acid, using fermentation or enzyme-based processes that introduce oxygen into the molecule. It includes microbial or enzymatic synthesis routes and steps for isolating the pure acid or its optical isomers.
[C12P 7/6436] Fatty acid esters -- This CPC subclass covers methods that use microbial or enzymatic fermentation to produce fatty acid esters, which are oxygen-containing organic compounds often used as intermediates or final products in chemical synthesis. It also includes processes for separating optical isomers of these esters from racemic mixtures.
[C12P 7/6445] Glycerides -- C12P 7/6445 covers processes for preparing glycerides, which are oxygen-containing organic compounds comprising glycerol esterified with fatty acids. The classification includes enzymatic or fermentative routes to synthesize or isolate glycerides and to resolve optical isomers from racemic mixtures. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under chemistry, biochemistry, and fermentation or enzyme-using processes for desired chemicals.
[C12P 7/6454] by esterification -- This CPC subgroup covers fermentation- or enzyme-based processes that prepare oxygen-containing organic compounds by forming esters, i.e., joining an acid (or its derivative) with an alcohol to produce an ester product. It is used for biocatalytic synthesis of esters as intermediates or final compounds in chemical manufacturing.
[C12P 7/6458] by transesterification, e.g. interesterification, ester interchange, alcoholysis or acidolysis -- The entry C12P 7/6458 concerns enzymatic or fermentative methods that prepare oxygen-containing organic molecules by transesterification reactions, including interesterification, ester interchange, alcoholysis and acidolysis. It falls under processes for synthesising desired chemicals or separating optical isomers.
[C12P 7/6463] obtained from glyceride producing microorganisms, e.g. single cell oil -- The code C12P 7/6463 refers to processes that employ microorganisms capable of producing glycerides, such as single-cell oil producers, to synthesize oxygen-containing organic compounds. It covers the fermentation-based preparation of these glyceride-rich oils and related downstream steps for obtaining desired chemicals.
[C12P 7/6472] containing polyunsaturated fatty acid [PUFA] residues, i.e. having two or more double bonds in their backbone -- The CPC entry C12P 7/6472 falls under chemistry, biochemistry and fermentation or enzyme-using processes for preparing oxygen-containing organic compounds, and it specifically covers compounds that contain polyunsaturated fatty-acid (PUFA) residues, meaning the molecules have two or more double bonds in their carbon backbone.
[C12P 7/6481] Phosphoglycerides -- This CPC subclass concerns phosphoglycerides, glycerophospholipids composed of a glycerol backbone esterified with fatty acids and a phosphate-linked head group. It covers the preparation, modification and use of these compounds in processes that produce oxygen-containing organic molecules or separate optical isomers, fitting within fermentation or enzyme-using methods for chemical synthesis.
[C12P 7/649] Biodiesel, i.e. fatty acid alkyl esters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of biodiesel, wherein "biodiesel" has the following definition:
[C12P 7/66] containing the quinoid structure -- The C12P 7/66 subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification concerns methods for preparing oxygen-containing organic compounds in which a quinoid structure is present, specifically when the synthesis or optical isomer separation is carried out by fermentation or enzyme-using processes. It falls under the broader category of biochemical routes for producing desired chemicals.
[C12P 9/00] Preparation of organic compounds containing a metal or atom other than H, N, C, O, S or halogen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of:  Frequent examples are organic compounds (except lipids) containing a phosphate group (-PO4), organometallic compounds, or compounds containing a hetero atom like Se, Si, As.  Not included are salts of organic compounds, which are classified with the organic compound.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Phosphoglycerides having less than 7 carbon atoms -> C12P7/62 - Phosphoglycerides having fatty acids with at least 7 carbon atoms -> C12P7/6481
[C12Q] MEASURING OR TESTING PROCESSES INVOLVING ENZYMES, NUCLEIC ACIDS OR MICROORGANISMS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes in which there is a direct or indirect qualitative or quantitative measurement or test of a material which contains enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.  Processes in which a material containing enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms is used to perform a qualitative or quantitative measurement or test, e.g. testing for antimicrobial activity or cholesterol, geomicrobiological testing.  In vivo or in vitro or in silico measuring or testing processes involving nucleic acid e.g. nucleic acid hybridisation including PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction).  Compositions or test papers containing enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms which can be used to detect or identify a chemical compound or composition, e.g. paper strips for the testing of blood sugar.  Compositions or test papers distinguished by the use of indicators which can be used to detect or identify the presence of enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.  Processes of making such test compositions.  Processes involving enzymes or microorganisms in which a process parameter is measured and that or another process parameter is varied in response to such measurement, i.e. condition responsive control.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Immunoassay -> G01N33/53 - Immunoassay with enzyme label -> G01N33/535 - Immunoassay with the carrier being a biological cell or cell fragment -> G01N33/554 - Immunoassay for microorganisms -> G01N33/569 - Immunoassay for venereal diseases -> G01N33/571 - Immunoassay for enzymes and isoenzymes -> G01N33/573 - Immunoassay for cancer -> G01N33/575 - Immunoassay for hepatitis -> G01N33/576  **Glossary:** - Enzyme: Proteinaceous material which causes a chemical change in a starting material without being consumed in the reaction. - Involving: When used in relation to a substance, includes the testing for the substance as well as employing the substance as a determinant or reactant in a test for a different substance. - Microorganism: For the purposes of classification, this term includes bacteria, fungi, viruses, protozoa and algae. - Nucleic acid: Comprises nucleic acids as in vitro compounds as well as sub-cellular parts in vivo like chromosome territories within the nucleus, plasmids, gene sequences, genetic information, mutations, polymorphisms such as SNPs, in silico base sequences, aptamers (ligand binding nucleic acids) and ribozymes (catalytic active RNA molecules).
[C12Q 1/00] Measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes in which there is a direct or indirect qualitative or quantitative measurement or test of a material which contains enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.  Processes in which a material containing enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms is used to perform a qualitative or quantitative measurement or test, e.g. testing for antimicrobial activity or cholesterol, geomicrobiological testing.  In vivo or in vitro or in silico measuring or testing processes involving nucleic acid e.g. nucleic acid hybridisation including Polymerase Chain Reaction [PCR]. See section range C12Q1/68 - C12Q1/708 .  Compositions or test papers containing enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms which can be used to detect or identify a chemical compound or composition, e.g. paper strips for the testing of blood sugar.  Compositions or test papers distinguished by the use of indicators which can be used to detect or identify the presence of enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.  Processes of making such test compositions.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Measuring or testing apparatus with condition measuring or sensing means, e.g. colony counters -> C12M1/34
[C12Q 1/001] Enzyme electrodes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Enzyme-based Electrochemical sensors where inventive concept lies in the enzyme aspect e.g. enzyme used, how attached to electrode, enzyme mediator involvement, enzyme sensing mechanism/system.
[C12Q 1/002] Electrode membranes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Enzyme electrodes where inventive concept lies in the use of or construction of a membrane on or in which an enzyme or multi-enzyme sensing system is attached or entrapped  **Glossary:** - Membrane: Any non-conductive porous structure
[C12Q 1/003] Functionalisation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inventive concept lies in chemical e.g. silylation or physical e.g. plasma treatment of the electrode membrane to alter/create functional groups for attachment of enzyme. May also include crosslinking or other treatments of membrane polymers. Overlap with G01N33/54353 , G01N33/5436 , G01N33/54393 .
[C12Q 1/004] mediator-assisted -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Enzyme electrodes where the enzyme or multi-enzyme sensing system requires a mediator e.g. co-factors (NAD/FAD), ferrodoxins.
[C12Q 1/005] involving specific analytes or enzymes  including groups of enzymes, e.g. oxydases;  C12Q1/004  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Enzyme electrodes directed to analysis of specific molecules or use of specific enzymes. Use of multi-enzyme systems such as oxido-reductase systems may also be classified in C12Q1/004 if the mediator is of importance.
[C12Q 1/006] for glucose -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Enzyme electrodes specifically designed for the analysis of glucose.
[C12Q 1/007] involving isoenzyme profiles  for detection of an individual isoenzyme  C12Q1/25  -  C12Q1/66 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for determining isoenzyme profiles.Overlap with G01N33/573 , G01N33/5735 .
[C12Q 1/008] for determining co-enzymes or co-factors, e.g. NAD, ATP -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for detecting, measuring or identifying co-enzymes or co-factors e.g. NAD, ATP involved in enzyme reactions.
[C12Q 1/02] involving viable microorganisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or processes for living microorganisms which cannot be classified elsewhere in C12Q1/00 - C12Q1/66 . Includes Total Viable Organism (TVO) testing and electrophysical measurements such as ion channel current.  C12Q1/02 and subgroups includes testing for microorganisms where the desired result indicates non-viability.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Specific binding assays/Immunoassays for microorganisms are classified in -> G01N33/569 - G01N33/571 - For hepatitis -> G01N33/576
[C12Q 1/025] for testing or evaluating the effect of chemical or biological compounds, e.g. drugs, cosmetics  antimicrobial activity  C12Q1/18 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or processes for testing or evaluating non antimicrobial chemical or biological compounds such as drugs, cosmetics.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Antimicrobial activity -> C12Q1/18
[C12Q 1/04] Determining presence or kind of microorganism -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or processes (qualitative testing) designed to determine the presence or identity (variety, species, genus or Gram +/-) of a microorganism, including compositions containing an indicator for presence or identity of a microorganism.
[C12Q 1/045] Culture media therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions wherein the inventive concept lies in the composition or content of the culture media e.g. percentage ratio of components, compounds present in medium itself (carbon source, nitrogen source, vitamins etc.)
[C12Q 1/06] Quantitative determination -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and processes (quantitative testing) for numerical counting the number of viable microorganisms or viable/non-viable ratio in a sample.
[C12Q 1/08] using multifield media -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving use of a multifield media (single media permitting identification of multiple results or single item e.g. petri dish comprising more than one medium to allow multiple results) in methods and processes (quantitative testing) for numerical counting the number of viable microorganisms or viable/non-viable ratio in a sample.
[C12Q 1/10] Enterobacteria -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving quantitative determination of Enterobacteria e.g. Citrobacter, Serratia, Proteus, Providencia, Morganella, Yesinia, Escherichia, Shigella, Salmonella, Klebsiella, Enterobacter, Erwinia, Hafnia.
[C12Q 1/12] Nitrate to nitrite reducing bacteria -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving quantitative determination of bacteria under nitrate to nitrite reducing conditions. Some bacteria e.g. E.Coli use nitrate under anaerobic growth conditions.
[C12Q 1/14] Streptococcus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving quantitative determination of Streptococcus or Staphylococcus bacteria.
[C12Q 1/16] using radioactive material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions for detecting presence or kind of microorganism (qualitative testing) designed to determine the presence or identity (variety, species, genus or Gram +/-) of a microorganism. wherein the inventive concept lies in the use of radioisotopes (e.g. 11C, 13 C, 14C, 2H, 3H, 15N, 35S, 35P).
[C12Q 1/18] Testing for antimicrobial activity of a material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or processes for testing of antimicrobial activity of a compound on living microorganisms.
[C12Q 1/20] using multifield media -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving the use of a multifield media (single media permitting identification of multiple results or single item e.g. petri dish comprising more than one medium to allow multiple results) in methods or processes for testing of antimicrobial activity of a compound on living microorganisms.
[C12Q 1/22] Testing for sterility conditions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or processes for testing if sterility conditions have been achieved or are being maintained. Examples are labels for food packaging, testing of medical instrument sterilization methods, air or water quality.
[C12Q 1/24] Methods of sampling, or inoculating or spreading a sample -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for sampling/physically isolating intact microorganisms (including non-viable microorganisms) are classified in C12Q1/24 irrespective of what becomes of them afterwards. If the isolated microorganisms are further subject to immunoassay/biospecific binding assay a further symbol from G01N33/569 or subgroups would be added.
[C12Q 1/25] involving enzymes not classifiable in groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes having unidentifiable EC number and enzymes which cannot be classified elsewhere in C12Q1/26 - C12Q1/66 . Classified under this symbol are methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified EC 6.X.X.X. These enzymes are characterised by bond formation C-O (6.1), C-S (6.2), C-N (6.3), C-C (6.4), P-O (.5), N-Met (6.6) and may commonly be known as ligase, synthase, carboxylase, cyclase, chelatase.
[C12Q 1/26] involving oxidoreductase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified as EC 1.X.X.X oxidoreductases, not comprising as part of the IUB name 'dehydrogenase' (see C12Q1/32 ) and which cannot be classified elsewhere in C12Q1/26 - C12Q1/32 . Enzymes are characterised by the catalysis of oxidation/reduction reactions and may comprise as part of their IUB name reductase, oxidase, synthase, dismutase, hydrogenase, oxygenase
[C12Q 1/28] involving peroxidase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving peroxidase enzymes classified EC 1.11.1.X including peroxidase enzyme itself (EC 1.11.1.7).
[C12Q 1/30] involving catalase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving catalase enzyme, EC 1.11.1.6.
[C12Q 1/32] involving dehydrogenase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes having an EC number 1.X.X.X and which contain 'dehydrogenase' in the IUB standard enzyme name.
[C12Q 1/34] involving hydrolase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified as EC 3.X.X.X hydrolases and which cannot be classified elsewhere in C12Q1/37 - C12Q1/46 . Enzymes are characterised by the catalysis of the addition or removal of a water molecule and may comprise as part of their IUB name hydrolase, lipase, lactonase, nuclease, nucleotidase, NTPase, helicase, amidase, sulfatase, depolymerase, glycosylase and variants e.g. ribonuclease. Methods, processes or compositions involving urease (EC 3.5.1.5) - C12Q1/58 . Methods, processes or compositions involving (phospho)lipase - C12Q1/61 .
[C12Q 1/37] involving peptidase or proteinase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified as EC 3.4.X.X. The enzymes are classified as acting on peptide bonds and may comprise as part of the IUB name peptidase and variants e.g. dipeptidase, aminopeptidase. Methods, processes or compositions involving clotting factors - C12Q1/56 .  There are many enzymes classified in the area EC 3.4.21.X - 3.4.23.X which retain the 'original' names e.g. trypsin, complement factors, kallikrein, subtilisin, papain, Meprin A, renin.
[C12Q 1/40] involving amylase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified as EC 3.2.1.X. Enzymes are characterised by hydrolysis of O - and S -glycosyl compounds and may comprise as part of the IUB name (sugar residue)sidase e.g. galactosidase, mannosidase.  There are many enzymes classified in the area EC 3.2.1.X which retain the 'original' names e.g. amylase, lysozyme, lactase.
[C12Q 1/42] involving phosphatase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified as EC 3.1.3.X. Enzymes are characterised by hydrolysis of phosphoric monoesters and usually comprise as part of the IUB name phosphatase.
[C12Q 1/44] involving esterase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified as EC 3.1.X.X having as part of the IUB name 'esterase' or variant e.g. diesterase, thioesterase. Enzymes are characterised by acting on ester bonds.
[C12Q 1/46] involving cholinesterase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving acetylcholinesterase, EC 3.1.1.7 or cholinesterase EC 3.1.1.8.
[C12Q 1/48] involving transferase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified as EC 2.X.X.X transferases and which cannot be classified elsewhere in C12Q1/485 - C12Q1/52 . Enzymes are characterised by the transfer of a functional group and may comprise as part of their IUB name kinase, transferase, synthase, phosphorylase and variants e.g. aminotransferase.
[C12Q 1/485] involving kinase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified as EC 2.7.X.X having as part of the IUB name 'kinase' or variant. Enzymes are characterised by the transfer of phosphorus-containing groups.
[C12Q 1/50] involving creatine phosphokinase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzyme creatine (phospho)kinase, EC 2.7.3.2.
[C12Q 1/52] involving transaminase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified as EC 2.6.1.X and may comprise as part of the IUB name 'transaminase'. Enzymes are characterised by the transfer of nitrogenous groups.
[C12Q 1/527] involving lyase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified as EC 4.X.X.X lyases and may comprise as part of the IUB name lyase, carboxylase, aldolase, hydratase and variants e.g. decarboxylase, dehydratase. Enzymes are characterised by the catalysis of reactions involving the formation of or addition to a double bond.
[C12Q 1/533] involving isomerase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving enzymes classified as EC 5.X.X.X isomerases and may comprise as part of the IUB name racemase, mutase, epimerase, isomerase, tautomerase, synthase and variants e.g. aminomutase. Enzymes are characterised by the catalysis of isomerisation reactions.
[C12Q 1/54] involving glucose or galactose -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving glucose or galactose where glucose or galactose are the final analyte or subject of the test e.g. diabetes testing, glucose demand for testing presence of microorganisms, Glucose Tolerance Test, use of glucose or galactose in the production of enzymes. Electrochemical glucose sensors where the inventive concept is in an electrode or other sensor structure to specifically enhance glucose determinations are classified in C12Q1/006 .
[C12Q 1/56] involving blood clotting factors, e.g. involving thrombin, thromboplastin, fibrinogen -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving blood clotting factors e.g. thrombin, fibrinogen, thromboplastin. Includes investigation and/or identification of compounds which are present in or modulate the clotting pathway.
[C12Q 1/58] involving urea or urease -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving detection of urea or urease (EC 3.5.1.5). Includes measurement of Biological Nitrogen Demand. Urea electrodes where the inventive concept is in the electrode are classified in C12Q1/001 - C12Q1/005 . Includes detection of ammonia.
[C12Q 1/60] involving cholesterol -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving detection of cholesterol or LDL-cholesterol. Cholesterol electrodes where the inventive concept is in the electrode are classified in C12Q1/005 .
[C12Q 1/61] involving triglycerides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving detection of triglycerides, e.g. as biomarkers for disease, HDL, LDL, CM values or acting as substrate for determination of (phospho)lipase enzymes.
[C12Q 1/62] involving uric acid -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving detection of uric acid, often using the enzyme uricase (EC 1.7.3.3). Includes detection of uric acid as breakdown product indicative of other analytes e.g. purine bases, nucleotides.
[C12Q 1/64] Geomicrobiological testing, e.g. for petroleum -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving detection of microbiological degradation or contamination of in-situ hydrocarbon reserves, hydrocarbon reserve prospecting using microorganisms, monitoring of microorganism contamination of liquid hydrocarbon fuels, carbon dioxide sequestering by subterranean microorganism methane production.
[C12Q 1/66] involving luciferase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, processes or compositions involving luciferase (EC 1.13.12.X or EC 1.14.14.3).
[C12Q 1/68] involving nucleic acids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All documents which cannot be classified in any of the other groups but relate to the enzymatic manipulation of nucleic acids.
[C12Q 1/6804] Nucleic acid analysis using immunogens -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Applications characterised by immunological compounds which are used in the analysis of nucleic acids. This group also includes applications characterised by nucleic acids which are used for analysing or detecting proteins and immunogens, e.g. immuno PCR).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Immunoassay -> G01N33/53 - Immunoassay for nucleic acids -> G01N33/5308  **Glossary:** - Immunogens: means immunological compounds such as antibodies and antigens
[C12Q 1/6806] Preparing nucleic acids for analysis, e.g. for polymerase chain reaction [PCR] assay -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications which deal with the preparation/modification of nucleic acids in order to use them or prepare them for subsequent analysis (e.g. amplification techniques (PCR), hybridisation techniques, sequencing of nucleic acids). This group also contains applications dealing with the preservation of DNA or RNA samples.
[C12Q 1/6809] Methods for determination or identification of nucleic acids involving differential detection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All documents where the invention concerns a method for determining differential expression (RNA level) and comparative genomics (genomic DNA level) and improvements to such methods. However, if the methods disclosed by an application are known, these applications are classified as products based on the use of the products identified.
[C12Q 1/6811] Selection methods for production or design of target specific oligonucleotides or binding molecules -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The design of primers and probes using enzymatic techniques for obtaining them.
[C12Q 1/6813] Hybridisation assays -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications dealing with hybridisation assays which can not be classified in any of the hybridisation subgroups.
[C12Q 1/6816] characterised by the detection means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Applications dealing with the detection of hybridisation assays characterised by the detection means.  **Glossary:** - Means of detection: the mechanism used to detect the hybridisation of a nucleic acid probe to its nucleic acid target (e.g. labels,...)
[C12Q 1/6818] involving interaction of two or more labels, e.g. resonant energy transfer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications dealing with the detection of hybridisation events using the interaction between the labels as principle.
[C12Q 1/682] Signal amplification -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications where the detection signal generated in a hybridisation reaction is amplified (for instance the use of branched probes or rolling circle amplification to amplify the hybridisation signal).
[C12Q 1/6823] Release of bound markers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications wherein the hybridisation detection depends on the physical separation and subsequent detection of a signalling moiety.
[C12Q 1/6825] Nucleic acid detection involving sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications wherein the detection of the hybridisation reaction depends on the electrical or physical properties of the label or of the nucleic acid molecules themselves.
[C12Q 1/6827] for detection of mutation or polymorphism -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All methods dealing with the detection of polymorphisms using an hybridisation assay and which cannot be classified in group C12Q1/683 . The detection of methylation and splice variants is seen as polymorphism detection and therefore classified in this group if the detection principle is based on an hybridisation assay.
[C12Q 1/683] involving restriction enzymes, e.g. restriction fragment length polymorphism [RFLP] -- C12Q 1/683 relates to methods that employ restriction enzymes to cleave nucleic acids for analytical purposes, including restriction fragment length polymorphism techniques used to detect genetic variations. It falls under measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 1/6832] Enhancement of hybridisation reaction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications dealing with the enhancement of the binding between a target and its probe, e.g. use of special buffer components, temperatures, probe modifications.
[C12Q 1/6834] Enzymatic or biochemical coupling of nucleic acids to a solid phase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications dealing with the enzymatic and biochemical coupling of nucleic acids to solid surfaces for the use in low throughput assays and the application of those solid surfaces in the subsequent analysis of a nucleic acid.
[C12Q 1/6837] using probe arrays or probe chips -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All nucleic acid analysis methods which depend on the use of probe arrays (biochips, microarray). If the use of the array is in the context of a method which can be classified in another group of the hybridisation based assays, e.g. C12Q1/6813 , the classifier has to decide based on the relevance of the method to classify the application in either one of these groups or even to classify the application in both groups if necessary. However, if the use is for sequencing then the application is only classified in group C12Q1/6874 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Involving nucleic acid arrays, e.g. sequencing by hybridisation [SBH] -> C12Q1/6874
[C12Q 1/6839] Triple helix formation or other higher order conformations in hybridisation assays -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All methods dealing with the formation of a triple helix DNA conformation. This group also covers other higher order conformations of nucleic acids (quadruplex).
[C12Q 1/6841] In situ  hybridisation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications dealing with methods for the analysis of a nucleic acid in a cell or positionally in a chromosome like Fluorescent In Situ Hybridisation [FISH].
[C12Q 1/6844] Nucleic acid amplification reactions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All amplification methods which do not belong in any of the amplification groups. Generally, amplification techniques which use a mechanism for amplifying nucleic acids and for which no group exists are classified in group C12Q1/6844 . An example of such an amplification technique is strand displacement amplification [SDA].
[C12Q 1/6846] Common amplification features -- The code C12Q 1/6846 covers general aspects common to nucleic-acid amplification techniques such as PCR, LAMP or isothermal methods, including shared steps like template preparation, primer or probe design, cycle conditions, enzyme use and detection of amplified products. It pertains to the fundamental features that apply across different amplification protocols rather than specific applications or unique variations.
[C12Q 1/6848] characterised by the means for preventing contamination or increasing the specificity or sensitivity of an amplification reaction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preventing contamination in an amplification reaction such as the use of wax barriers, containers, uracil glycosylase, hot start and nested PCR. In addition, all methods relating to increasing the specificity or sensitivity of an amplification reaction are classified in this group.  This group also covers means for reducing false positive or false negative signals in an amplification reaction.  These include the use of modified nucleotides, e.g. in amplification reactions designed for amplifying GC-rich templates, special buffer components, pH, reaction conditions, etc.  If the method is designed for a specific amplification technique like PCR in group C12Q1/686 , then it is both classified in the specific amplification group, i.e. C12Q1/686 , and in C12Q1/6848 .
[C12Q 1/6851] Quantitative amplification -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for the quantitative amplification of nucleic acids including the use of standards or mathematical models. This group also covers methods (both again enzymatic and mathematical) for determining the amplification efficiency.
[C12Q 1/6853] using modified primers or templates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods using modified primers or templates.
[C12Q 1/6855] Ligating adaptors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods where the primer or the template is modified by the ligation to an adaptor.
[C12Q 1/6858] Allele-specific amplification -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All methods dealing with the detection of polymorphisms using an amplification assay. The detection of methylation and splice variants is seen as polymorphism detection and therefore classified in this group if the detection principle is based on an amplification assay. This includes allele specific primer extension (also when only one dNTP or ddNTP is incorporated using a polymerase).
[C12Q 1/686] Polymerase chain reaction [PCR] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications dealing with PCR and modifications/improvements thereof (e.g. Taqman, multiplex-PCR, and etc.).
[C12Q 1/6862] Ligase chain reaction [LCR] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications dealing with LCR and modifications/improvements thereof.
[C12Q 1/6865] Promoter-based amplification, e.g. nucleic acid sequence amplification [NASBA], self-sustained sequence replication [3SR] or transcription-based amplification system [TAS] -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications dealing with promoter based amplification and modifications/improvements thereof.  **Glossary:** - NASBA: Nucleic acid sequence based amplification - 3SR: selfsustained sequence replication - TAS: transcription-based amplification system
[C12Q 1/6867] Replicase-based amplification, e.g. using Q-beta replicase -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications dealing with replicase based amplifications and modifications/improvements thereof.
[C12Q 1/6869] Methods for sequencing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All nucleic acid sequencing methods which cannot be classified in the subgroups for sequencing using mass spectrometry, i.e. in group C12Q1/6872 and sequencing using solid surfaces, i.e. in group C12Q1/6874 . This group also covers methods for sequencing using nanopores and other sequencing systems based on physical properties of nucleic acids, e.g. Atomic Force Microscopy [AFM].
[C12Q 1/6872] involving mass spectrometry -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications dealing with mass spectrometry based sequencing and modifications/improvements thereof.
[C12Q 1/6874] involving nucleic acid arrays, e.g. sequencing by hybridisation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All applications dealing with nucleic acid array based sequencing and modifications/improvements thereof.
[C12Q 1/6876] Nucleic acid products used in the analysis of nucleic acids, e.g. primers or probes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All nucleic acid products used in the analysis of nucleic acids (e.g. primers, probes, controls) which cannot be classified in any of the subgroups C12Q1/6879 - C12Q1/6895 . If an application relates both to methods and nucleic acid products, than these applications are classified in both the appropriate method and product subgroups.
[C12Q 1/6879] for sex determination -- C12Q 1/6879 pertains to biochemical or molecular methods used to determine the sex of a biological sample, typically by detecting sex-specific nucleic acid sequences, proteins or other biomarkers. These assays may employ enzymes, PCR, hybridisation or similar techniques to identify male or female characteristics. It falls within measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 1/6881] for tissue or cell typing, e.g. human leukocyte antigen [HLA] probes -- C12Q 1/6881 covers analytical methods that determine the type of a tissue or cell by using specific probes, such as human leukocyte antigen (HLA) probes, to detect characteristic nucleic-acid or protein markers. These techniques belong to the broader class of measuring or testing processes that involve enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, and are used for diagnostic or research typing of biological samples.
[C12Q 1/6883] for diseases caused by alterations of genetic material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All nucleic acid based diagnostic products. Those include both products for detecting the alterations (polymorphisms including methylation and splice variants) of genetic material and for detecting differential expression of a disease gene. If an application also discloses methods for detecting such polymorphisms or differential expression, the classifier should decide based on the relevance of this method to classify the application also in the appropriate method groups, e.g. C12Q1/6827 , C12Q1/683 , C12Q1/6858 , or C12Q1/6809 .
[C12Q 1/6886] for cancer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All nucleic acid based cancer diagnostic products.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cancer diagnostic immunoassays -> G01N33/575
[C12Q 1/6888] for detection or identification of organisms -- C12Q 1/6888 covers methods and apparatus for detecting or identifying organisms, such as bacteria, viruses, or cells, using biochemical or molecular assays that involve enzymes, nucleic acids, or whole microorganisms. The classification includes diagnostic kits, biosensors and protocols that reveal the presence or identity of the target organism.
[C12Q 1/689] for bacteria -- C12Q 1/689 covers methods, devices and kits used to detect, quantify or characterize bacteria in a sample. It includes biochemical, molecular or immunological techniques that test bacterial presence, activity or composition, and may involve reagents, instruments or assay formats designed specifically for bacterial analysis.
[C12Q 1/6893] for protozoa -- The subclass C12Q 1/6893 defines measuring or testing processes specifically directed at protozoa, including methods for detecting, quantifying, or assessing their viability, activity, or genetic material using enzymatic, nucleic-acid-based, or other microbiological assays.
[C12Q 1/6895] for plants, fungi or algae -- C12Q 1/6895 pertains to measuring or testing processes that employ enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms specifically applied to plants, fungi or algae, such as assays for growth, metabolic activity, genetic traits or contamination.
[C12Q 1/6897] involving reporter genes operably linked to promoters -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All methods which use the detection of reporter genes operably linked to promoters for screening and nucleic acid analysis.
[C12Q 1/70] involving virus or bacteriophage -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All methods which are specifically designed for the analysis of viral nucleic acids or for the analysis of nucleic acids of bacteriophages. (NOTE: According to the hierarchy this subgroup should not be limited to analysis involving nucleic acids. In practice, however, as Immunoassays/protein based Biospecific binding assays for viruses are classified in G01N , this subgroup is effectively limited to analysis of viral/bacteriophagal nucleic acids). Methods which are generally applicable to nucleic acid analysis should also be classified in the relevant C12Q1/68 subgroup.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Immunoassay/protein based Biospecific binding assay for viruses -> G01N33/56983
[C12Q 1/701] Specific hybridization probes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  All probes and primers for the detection and analysis of viruses and bacteriophages not covered by any of the subgroups C12Q1/703 - C12Q1/708 .
[C12Q 1/702] for retroviruses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Probes and primers for the detection and analysis of retroviruses. Methods specifically designed for retroviruses are covered in C12Q1/70 and C12Q1/68 if necessary.
[C12Q 1/703] Viruses associated with AIDS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Probes and primers for the detection and analysis of AIDS associated viruses. Methods specifically designed for AIDS associated viruses are covered in C12Q1/70 and C12Q1/68 if necessary.
[C12Q 1/705] for herpetoviridae, e.g. herpes simplex, varicella zoster -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Probes and primers for the detection and analysis of herpetoviridae. Methods specifically designed for herpetoviridae are covered in C12Q1/70 and C12Q1/68 if necessary.
[C12Q 1/706] for hepatitis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Probes and primers for the detection and analysis of hepatitis. Methods specifically designed for hepatitis are covered in C12Q1/70 and C12Q1/68 if necessary.
[C12Q 1/707] non-A, non-B Hepatitis, excluding hepatitis D -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Probes and primers for the detection and analysis of non-A, non-B, and non-D hepatitis. Methods specifically designed for non-A, non-B, and non-D hepatitis are covered in C12Q1/70 and C12Q1/68 if necessary.
[C12Q 1/708] for papilloma -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Probes and primers for the detection and analysis of papilloma. Methods specifically designed for papilloma are covered in C12Q1/70 and C12Q1/68 if necessary.
[C12Q 2304/00] Chemical means of detecting microorganisms -- C12Q 2304/00 covers chemical methods for detecting microorganisms, such as reagents, reagents-based assays, kits and probes that identify bacteria, fungi or viruses by reacting with specific biological markers, metabolic products or nucleic acids, enabling qualitative or quantitative microbial analysis.
[C12Q 2304/10] DNA staining -- C12Q 2304/10 covers methods and chemical agents used to stain DNA for visualizing genetic material, typically in microscopy or flow cytometry. It includes dyes and protocols that bind to nucleic acids to enable detection of microorganisms or cells.
[C12Q 2304/12] Ethidium -- Ethidium is a fluorescent dye that intercalates into nucleic acids and is employed as a chemical reagent for detecting microorganisms. In the Cooperative Patent Classification it falls under C12Q 2304/12, which covers chemical means of identifying microbes through nucleic-acid staining. The compound enables rapid visualization of bacterial or viral DNA in diagnostic and research assays.
[C12Q 2304/13] Propidium -- Propidium is a fluorescent nucleic-acid binding dye employed to detect microbial cells, especially to differentiate dead from live bacteria because it penetrates compromised cell membranes. It is used in fluorescence microscopy, flow cytometry and viability assays as a chemical means of detecting microorganisms.
[C12Q 2304/16] Acridine orange -- Acridine orange is a fluorescent dye employed as a chemical means of detecting microorganisms and nucleic acids; it intercalates into DNA and, when excited by ultraviolet light, emits orange fluorescence that enables rapid visualization of cells in microbiological assays. The entry covers the use of this compound in measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2304/18] Thionin-type dyes, e.g. Azure, Toluidine Blue -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2304/18 covers thionin-type dyes such as Azure and Toluidine Blue that are used as chemical reagents for detecting microorganisms in biochemical assays. These compounds facilitate measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microbial samples.
[C12Q 2304/20] Redox indicators -- C12Q 2304/20 relates to redox indicators employed as chemical agents for detecting microorganisms. These compounds undergo a change in oxidation state that produces a visible signal when microbial metabolic activity alters the redox environment, and they are used in assays that monitor enzyme, nucleic-acid or microbial processes.
[C12Q 2304/22] Resazurin -- Resazurin is a redox-active dye used as a chemical means of detecting microorganisms, especially in viability and metabolic assays. It is reduced by living cells from a blue-non-fluorescent form to a pink-fluorescent compound, allowing rapid measurement of microbial growth or enzymatic activity. This property makes it useful in testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2304/24] Tetrazolium -- Tetrazolium compounds are redox indicators that become colored formazans when reduced by cellular enzymes, allowing detection and quantification of viable microorganisms in a sample. They are used in assays to measure metabolic activity of bacteria, fungi and other microbes.
[C12Q 2304/26] Quinone -- Quinones are aromatic redox-active compounds employed as chemical indicators in assays that detect microorganisms, where their reduction or oxidation produces a visible color change signalling microbial metabolic activity. They are commonly used in enzyme-based tests to monitor the presence or growth of bacteria, fungi or viruses.
[C12Q 2304/40] Detection of gases -- C12Q 2304/40 refers to chemical methods for detecting gases, typically employing enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms as sensing elements. The classification covers assays, reagents and sensor designs that generate a measurable signal when a target gas interacts with a biologically active component, enabling rapid identification of volatile compounds in environmental, industrial or medical applications.
[C12Q 2304/44] Oxygen -- This CPC subclass covers methods and compositions that use oxygen as a chemical indicator for detecting microorganisms. It includes techniques where measurement of oxygen consumption or production signals the presence of microbial activity, often in assays involving enzymes or metabolic processes.
[C12Q 2304/46] Carbon dioxide -- C12Q2304/46 pertains to the use of carbon dioxide in chemical assays for detecting microorganisms, including methods that monitor CO2 production or consumption as an indicator of microbial activity. The subclass falls within biochemical testing processes employing chemical means to identify or quantify microorganisms.
[C12Q 2304/48] Ammonia or volatile amines -- C12Q 2304/48 relates to the detection of microorganisms by measuring ammonia or other volatile amines released during their metabolic activity. It encompasses chemical test methods that use these gases as indicators for rapid screening in food, water, clinical or environmental samples.
[C12Q 2304/60] Chemiluminescent detection using ATP-luciferin-luciferase system -- This subclass covers methods that use the ATP-luciferin-luciferase enzymatic reaction to generate light for detecting microorganisms. The technique measures chemiluminescence produced when cellular ATP fuels luciferase, providing rapid, sensitive identification in clinical, food-safety or environmental samples.
[C12Q 2304/80] Electrochemical detection via electrodes in contact with culture medium -- A technique that detects microorganisms by placing an electrode directly in the culture medium and measuring an electrochemical signal such as current, voltage, impedance or redox change caused by microbial activity. The method relies on the interaction between the living cells and the electrode surface to generate a measurable electrical response, providing a rapid and quantitative means of microbial detection.
[C12Q 2326/00] Chromogens for determinations of oxidoreductase enzymes -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2326/00 covers chromogenic compounds used to detect or quantify oxidoreductase enzymes in analytical assays. These reagents produce a measurable colour change when the target enzyme catalyzes a redox reaction, facilitating enzyme activity measurement in biochemical testing.
[C12Q 2326/10] Benzidines
[C12Q 2326/12] 3,3',5,5'-Tetramethylbenzidine, i.e. TMB -- The CPC entry C12Q 2326/12 covers 3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), a chromogenic substrate used mainly in peroxidase-based assays such as ELISA. Upon oxidation by oxidoreductase enzymes it produces a blue-colored product that is measured spectrophotometrically to quantify enzyme activity. This falls under chemistry-biochemistry methods for measuring enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2326/14] Ortho-Tolidine, i.e. 3,3'-dimethyl-(1,1'-biphenyl-4,4'-diamine) -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2326/14 covers chromogenic compounds used to detect oxidoreductase activity, specifically ortho-tolidine (3,3′-dimethyl-1,1′-biphenyl-4,4′-diamine). This chemical serves as a color-forming reagent in enzyme, nucleic-acid or microorganism assays, allowing visual or spectrophotometric measurement of catalytic reactions.
[C12Q 2326/20] Ortho-Phenylenediamine -- Ortho-phenylenediamine is a chromogenic reagent employed in enzymatic assays to detect oxidoreductase activity, producing a colored product upon oxidation. It is commonly used in diagnostic tests for enzymes such as peroxidases and dehydrogenases, allowing quantitative measurement of enzyme levels. This compound falls under the CPC class C12Q 2326/20 for chromogens used in enzyme-based determinations.
[C12Q 2326/30] 2,2'-Azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid), i.e. ABTS -- ABTS (2,2-Azinobis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)) is a synthetic chromogen employed in biochemical assays to detect and quantify oxidoreductase enzymes such as peroxidases and laccases. Upon oxidation it forms a colored radical cation, allowing spectrophotometric measurement of enzyme activity in solution.
[C12Q 2326/32] 3-Methyl-2-benzothiazolinone hydrazone hydrochloride hydrate, i.e. MBTH -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2326/32 covers chromogenic reagents used to assay oxidoreductase enzymes, the example being 3-methyl-2-benzothiazolinone hydrazone hydrochloride hydrate (MBTH). MBTH reacts with hydrogen peroxide in the presence of peroxidase to form a colored quinoneimine product, enabling spectrophotometric measurement of enzyme activity. It is employed in biochemical assays for detecting peroxidase, oxidase and related enzyme functions.
[C12Q 2326/40] Triphenylmethane dye chromogens, e.g. fluorescein derivatives -- C12Q 2326/40 designates triphenylmethane dye chromogens, including fluorescein derivatives, employed as chromogenic substrates in assays that detect oxidoreductase enzyme activity. These compounds generate a measurable color change when reduced or oxidized, enabling quantitative determination of enzymatic reactions in biochemical and diagnostic applications.
[C12Q 2326/50] Phenols -- Phenols are employed as chromogenic substrates in assays that detect oxidoreductase enzymes, where enzymatic oxidation converts the phenolic compound into a colored product, allowing visual or spectrophotometric measurement of enzyme activity. This use falls under biochemistry methods for measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, specifically chromogens for oxidoreductase determinations, and is classified under CPC code C12Q 2326/50.
[C12Q 2326/90] Developer -- The class C12Q 2326/90 covers chemical developers used in oxidoreductase enzyme assays, i.e., substances that react with the enzyme-generated product to produce a measurable color or fluorescence signal. These developers facilitate the quantification of enzyme activity in biochemical testing kits.
[C12Q 2326/92] Nitro blue tetrazolium chloride, i.e. NBT -- Nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT) is a water-soluble tetrazolium salt that forms a blue formazan precipitate when reduced by oxidoreductase enzymes such as NADH-dependent reductases, allowing visual or spectrophotometric detection of enzyme activity. It is used in microbiology, immunology and biochemistry to measure superoxide production and other redox reactions.
[C12Q 2326/96] 4-Amino-antipyrine -- The CPC class C12Q 2326/96 covers chromogenic compounds for detecting oxidoreductase enzyme activity, with 4-Amino-antipyrine as an example that yields a colored product upon reaction. It falls under chemistry, biochemistry, measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2334/00] O-linked chromogens for determinations of hydrolase enzymes, e.g. glycosidases, phosphatases, esterases -- O-linked chromogenic substrates are compounds in which a color-producing group is attached through an oxygen bond to a molecule that is cleaved by hydrolase enzymes such as glycosidases, phosphatases or esterases; enzyme action releases the chromogen, producing a measurable colour change that enables rapid quantitative or qualitative determination of enzyme activity.
[C12Q 2334/10] p-Nitrophenol derivatives -- C12Q 2334/10 covers p-nitrophenol derivatives that act as O-linked chromogenic substrates for measuring hydrolase enzymes such as glycosidases, phosphatases and esterases. When the enzyme cleaves the substrate, p-nitrophenol is released, producing a yellow color that can be quantified spectrophotometrically.
[C12Q 2334/20] Coumarin derivatives -- The CPC class C12Q 2334/20 covers coumarin-based compounds that serve as O-linked chromogenic substrates for hydrolase enzymes such as glycosidases, phosphatases and esterases, enabling the measurement of enzyme activity in biochemical assays. It pertains to the design, synthesis and use of these fluorescent or color-producing derivatives in diagnostic and research testing.
[C12Q 2334/22] 4-Methylumbelliferyl, i.e. beta-methylumbelliferone, 4MU -- 4-Methylumbelliferyl, also called beta-methylumbelliferone, is a fluorescent O-linked chromogenic substrate that releases a brightly fluorescing product when cleaved by hydrolase enzymes such as glycosidases, phosphatases or esterases, making it useful for quantitative enzyme assays.
[C12Q 2334/30] Naphthol derivatives, e.g. alpha-naphthyl-esters, i.e. alpha-NE, beta-naphthyl-esters, i.e. beta-NE -- C12Q 2334/30 relates to naphthol-based compounds, such as alpha-naphthyl-esters and beta-naphthyl-esters, used as O-linked chromogenic substrates in assays that detect hydrolase enzymes like glycosidases, phosphatases and esterases. These derivatives generate coloured products when cleaved, enabling measurement of enzymatic activity.
[C12Q 2334/40] Triphenylmethane dye chromogens, e.g. fluorescein derivatives -- Triphenylmethane dye chromogens such as fluorescein derivatives are O-linked substrates that release a coloured or fluorescent signal when cleaved by hydrolase enzymes like glycosidases, phosphatases or esterases. They enable sensitive measurement of enzyme activity in biochemical assays by producing a detectable colour change or fluorescence proportional to the extent of hydrolysis.
[C12Q 2334/50] Indoles -- C12Q 2334/50 covers indole compounds used as O-linked chromogenic substrates in assays that detect hydrolase activity such as glycosidases, phosphatases or esterases. These indole derivatives produce a colored or fluorescent product when cleaved by the target enzyme, enabling measurement of enzyme activity in biochemical testing.
[C12Q 2334/52] 5-Bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl, i.e. BCI -- 5-Bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl (BCI) is an O-linked chromogenic substrate that, when hydrolyzed by enzymes such as β-glucosidases, phosphatases or esterases, releases a colored indigo-blue product. It is used in diagnostic and analytical assays to visualize enzyme activity on gels, membranes or microtiter plates. The bromine and chlorine substituents increase its stability and intensity of the colour formed.
[C12Q 2334/70] the product, e.g. phenol, naphthol being diazotised -- C12Q 2334/70 covers O-linked chromogenic substrates used to detect hydrolase enzymes such as glycosidases, phosphatases or esterases, wherein the enzymatic reaction releases a phenol- or naphthol-based product that is subsequently diazotised for colour development. The classification falls under chemistry → biochemistry → measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, microorganisms or nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2337/00] N-linked chromogens for determinations of peptidases and proteinases -- These are N-linked chromogenic compounds designed for detecting peptidase or proteinase activity. The nitrogen atom connects a chromophore to a peptide or amino-acid substrate, releasing a colored product when the enzyme cleaves the bond, enabling quantitative or qualitative enzyme assays.
[C12Q 2337/10] Anilides -- Anilides are N-acyl derivatives of aniline that function as chromogenic substrates in assays for peptidases and proteinases. When they are cleaved by the target enzyme, they release a colored product that enables measurement of enzymatic activity. This class falls under C12Q 2337/10, covering N-linked chromogens used in biochemical testing.
[C12Q 2337/12] Para-Nitroanilides p-NA -- Para-nitroanilides (p-NA) are chromogenic substrates employed to assay peptidases and proteinases. When the enzyme cleaves the N-linked bond, p-nitroaniline is released, producing a yellow color that can be quantified spectrophotometrically. This class is used in biochemical measurement of enzyme activity.
[C12Q 2337/20] Coumarin derivatives -- Coumarin derivatives are chemical compounds employed as N-linked chromogenic substrates in assays that measure the activity of peptidases and proteinases, providing a fluorescent signal when the enzyme cleaves the linked group. This enables quantitative testing of enzyme function in biochemical and microbiological analyses.
[C12Q 2337/22] 7-Amino-4-methylcoumarin, i.e. AMC, MCA -- The entry C12Q 2337/22 concerns 7-amino-4-methylcoumarin (AMC or MCA), a fluorescent coumarin derivative used as a chromogenic or fluorogenic tag in assays that monitor peptidase and protease activity. When the linked substrate is cleaved by an enzyme, AMC is released and emits fluorescence, allowing quantitative measurement of enzymatic reactions.
[C12Q 2337/24] 7-Amino-4-trifluoromethylcoumarin, i.e. AFC -- C12Q 2337/24 covers 7-amino-4-trifluoromethylcoumarin (AFC), a fluorescent N-linked chromogen used as a substrate in enzymatic assays to detect peptidase and proteinase activity. The compound releases a fluorescent coumarin moiety upon cleavage, enabling sensitive measurement of enzyme kinetics in biochemical and diagnostic testing. It falls under the chemistry subclass for enzyme-based testing reagents.
[C12Q 2337/30] Naphthyl amides, e.g. beta-NA, 2-NA, 4-methoxy-beta-naphthylamine, i.e. 4MNA -- Naphthyl amides such as β-napthyl-N-acetyl-alanine (β-NA), 2-NA, and 4-methoxy-β-napthylamine are N-linked chromogenic substrates used to detect peptidase or proteinase activity. When the target enzyme cleaves the amide bond, a colored naphthol is released, enabling spectrophotometric or fluorometric measurement of proteolysis in biochemical assays. This subclass groups compounds that function as enzyme-linked indicators for quantitative testing.
[C12Q 2337/40] Rhodamine derivatives -- C12Q 2337/40 covers rhodamine derivatives that serve as N-linked chromogenic labels in assays measuring peptidase or proteinase activity. These compounds are employed to produce a visible color change when the target enzyme cleaves a linked substrate, facilitating quantitative or qualitative detection in biochemical testing.
[C12Q 2337/50] Indoles -- Indoles are heterocyclic aromatic compounds used as N-linked chromogenic substrates in assays that measure the activity of peptidases and proteinases. The classification covers their preparation, functionalization and application in enzymatic testing methods that generate a detectable color change when the indole moiety is cleaved by the target enzyme.
[C12Q 2337/52] 5-Bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl, i.e. BCI -- 5-Bromo-4-chloro-3-indolyl (BCI) is a chromogenic substrate employed in assays that detect peptidase or proteinase activity. When cleaved by the target enzyme it releases a colored indole derivative, enabling visual or spectrophotometric measurement of enzymatic reactions. It is classified under N-linked chromogens for enzyme-based determinations.
[C12Q 2500/00] Analytical methods involving nucleic acids -- Indexing codes C12Q2500/00 - C12Q2565/634 are only used as subsequent symbols in C-Sets and are not allocated as single symbols. The detailed information about the C-Sets construction and the associated syntax rules is present in the Definitions of C12Q .
[C12Q 2520/00] Reactions involving nucleic acids -- This subclass covers chemical reactions where nucleic acids serve as reactants, products or catalysts, including synthesis, cleavage, modification and labeling of DNA, RNA or analogues, often employed in analytical or biotechnological assays.
[C12Q 2521/00] Reaction characterised by the enzymatic activity -- This subclass covers enzyme-catalyzed processes in which the defining feature of the reaction is the activity of an enzyme, rather than the chemical transformation itself. It includes methods where the presence, amount or activity of the enzyme is measured or used to control the process, typical in biotech and diagnostic applications.
[C12Q 2521/10] Nucleotidyl transfering -- The C12Q 2521/10 class covers assays, methods and processes that exploit enzymes transferring nucleotidyl groups, such as polymerases, kinases or ligases, to generate, modify or detect nucleic acids. It includes any reaction where a nucleotidyl moiety is moved from a donor to an acceptor, forming the basis for diagnostic, analytical or synthetic biochemistry applications.
[C12Q 2521/101] DNA polymerase -- C12Q 2521/101 covers DNA polymerases, enzymes that catalyse the synthesis of DNA strands from nucleoside-triphosphate substrates. The classification includes isolated polymerase proteins, recombinant forms, and preparations used in techniques such as PCR, DNA sequencing, and other nucleic-acid amplification or manipulation processes.
[C12Q 2521/107] RNA dependent DNA polymerase,(i.e. reverse transcriptase) -- C12Q 2521/107 designates RNA-dependent DNA polymerases, commonly called reverse transcriptases, which catalyze synthesis of DNA using an RNA template. The class covers methods, compositions and uses of these enzymes in virology, gene therapy, diagnostics and biotechnology.
[C12Q 2521/113] Telomerase -- Telomerase is an enzyme that adds repeated DNA sequences to the ends of linear chromosomes, using an intrinsic RNA template to extend telomeres and thus maintain chromosome stability during cell division. It is central to methods that measure or manipulate nucleic-acid reactions and is targeted in biotechnological applications for telomere length control.
[C12Q 2521/119] RNA polymerase -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2521/119 covers inventions that involve RNA polymerase, the enzyme that synthesises RNA from a DNA template. It includes methods for producing, purifying, engineering, or using the polymerase in transcription-based assays, synthesis of RNA molecules, and related biotechnological applications. This class falls under chemistry, biochemistry and the measurement or testing of enzymatic reactions involving nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2521/125] Methyl transferase, i.e. methylase -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2521/125 covers methyl-transferase enzymes, also called methylases, which catalyse the transfer of a methyl group from a donor to an acceptor molecule such as DNA, RNA, protein or other substrates. These enzymes are used in biotechnological processes for site-specific modification of biomolecules and are classified under measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2521/131] Terminal transferase -- Terminal transferase is a DNA-polymerizing enzyme that adds nucleotides to the 3′-hydroxyl end of a nucleic-acid strand without requiring a template, enabling tailing, labeling and cloning of DNA fragments. The classification covers methods and devices that measure or test this enzymatic activity, such as assays for polymerase activity, product analysis and related analytical techniques.
[C12Q 2521/30] Phosphoric diester hydrolysing, i.e. nuclease -- The subclass C12Q 2521/30 covers inventions involving enzymes that hydrolyse phosphodiester bonds in nucleic acids, i.e., nucleases. It encompasses methods, compositions and applications that use this enzymatic activity to cleave DNA or RNA, supporting biotechnological, diagnostic and molecular-biology processes.
[C12Q 2521/301] Endonuclease -- C12Q 2521/301 covers endonucleases, enzymes that cleave phosphodiester bonds within nucleic acid strands by cutting at specific internal sites rather than removing nucleotides from the ends. The classification applies to processes and compositions where the reaction is defined by this enzymatic activity, including recombinant and natural variants used in molecular biology, diagnostics, and therapeutic applications.
[C12Q 2521/307] Single strand endonuclease -- C12Q 2521/307 covers single-strand endonucleases, enzymes that cleave phosphodiester bonds in a single strand of nucleic acid. The classification is used for inventions relating to the preparation, use or detection of such enzymes in biochemical assays or industrial processes.
[C12Q 2521/313] Type II endonucleases, i.e. cutting outside recognition site -- A subclass for type II endonucleases that cleave DNA at positions outside their specific recognition sequence rather than within it. These enzymes are employed in molecular biology to generate defined fragment ends for cloning, mapping and genome-editing applications, distinguishing them from conventional restriction enzymes that cut within the site.
[C12Q 2521/319] Exonuclease -- Exonuclease denotes enzymes that catalyse the stepwise removal of nucleotides from the termini of DNA or RNA molecules, a reaction used in nucleic-acid manipulation, sequencing, and diagnostic assays. The CPC subclass covers compositions, methods and apparatuses that exploit this activity for measuring, testing or modifying genetic material.
[C12Q 2521/325] Single stranded exonuclease -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2521/325 covers enzymatic reactions in which a single-stranded exonuclease removes nucleotides from the end of single-stranded DNA or RNA, a key step in nucleic-acid processing, analysis and purification. It includes methods, kits and reagents that employ such enzymes for measuring or testing biochemical samples.
[C12Q 2521/327] RNAse, e.g. RNAseH -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2521/327 covers ribonucleases, particularly RNase H enzymes that cleave the RNA strand of RNA-DNA hybrids. It relates to biotechnological applications where such enzymes are used for nucleic-acid manipulation, diagnostics, or recombinant DNA techniques. This subclass falls under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry and enzymatic measuring or testing processes.
[C12Q 2521/331] Methylation site specific nuclease -- A methylation-site-specific nuclease is an enzyme that recognizes and cleaves nucleic acid at positions bearing methyl groups, enabling selective modification or analysis of methylated DNA or RNA. Such enzymes are used in molecular biology for targeted cleavage, mapping methylation patterns, and epigenetic studies.
[C12Q 2521/337] Ribozyme -- The entry C12Q 2521/337 defines ribozymes, RNA molecules that possess catalytic activity and can accelerate specific chemical reactions such as cleavage, ligation or polymerisation of nucleic acids. It covers the use of these self-acting RNA enzymes in biotechnological processes where the reaction is characterized by the enzymatic function of the ribozyme.
[C12Q 2521/343] Abzyme -- Abzyme denotes a catalytic antibody that mimics enzyme activity, enabling specific biochemical transformations. In the CPC system it is classified under C12Q 2521/343, which covers reactions characterised by enzymatic activity involving such artificial enzymes.
[C12Q 2521/345] DNAzyme -- A DNAzyme is a DNA molecule that functions as a catalyst, accelerating a specific chemical reaction through its intrinsic enzymatic activity. This entry covers such nucleic-acid based catalysts and their use in biochemical assays, molecular diagnostics, and synthetic biology applications.
[C12Q 2521/50] Other enzymatic activities -- C12Q 2521/50 designates patent documents that disclose reactions defined principally by an enzymatic activity not covered by more specific subclasses. It includes inventions where a catalyst's biological function - such as hydrolysis, oxidation or polymerisation - is the defining feature of the process, regardless of the substrate or application. The subclass falls under measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms in the CPC hierarchy.
[C12Q 2521/501] Ligase -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2521/501 for Ligase covers enzymes that catalyze the formation of phosphodiester bonds between nucleic-acid strands, enabling the joining of DNA or RNA fragments. It includes methods, assays and apparatus that employ ligase activity for measuring, testing or modifying nucleic acids in biotechnological and analytical processes.
[C12Q 2521/507] Recombinase -- The C12Q 2521/507 subclass covers recombinant recombinases-engineered enzymes that catalyse site-specific DNA strand exchange. It includes recombinant forms and variants of these proteins used in genetic manipulation, molecular cloning and other nucleic-acid-based testing or measurement processes.
[C12Q 2521/513] Winding/unwinding enzyme, e.g. helicase -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2521/513 pertains to enzymes that modify the winding of nucleic-acid strands, such as helicases that unwind or rewind DNA or RNA. It covers both the enzymes themselves and related methods or compositions that employ these winding-unwinding activities in biochemical processes like replication, transcription or nucleic-acid manipulation.
[C12Q 2521/514] Mismatch repair protein -- The entry C12Q 2521/514 covers mismatch-repair proteins, which recognize and correct base-pairing errors that arise during DNA replication. It includes isolated or recombinant proteins, their variants and fragments, as well as methods for using these proteins to improve the fidelity of nucleic-acid synthesis or to detect mismatches.
[C12Q 2521/519] Topoisomerase -- Topoisomerases are enzymes that modify the topological state of DNA by transiently cutting, rotating and re-joining the strands, allowing relaxation, overwinding or decatenation during replication, transcription and other cellular processes. The class includes methods for measuring or testing the enzymatic activity of these proteins.
[C12Q 2521/525] Phosphatase -- C12Q 2521/525 - Phosphatase refers to inventions relating to enzymes that catalyze the removal of phosphate groups from substrates, i.e., phosphatase activity. The class includes methods, assays, compositions and biotechnological applications involving such enzymes for analysis or modification of biomolecules.
[C12Q 2521/531] Glycosylase -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2521/531 covers glycosylases, enzymes that catalyze the cleavage or modification of glycosidic bonds in nucleic acids. It is used to classify methods and assays that measure the activity of these enzymes in biochemical testing.
[C12Q 2521/537] Protease -- The CPC class C12Q 2521/537 pertains to proteases, enzymes that catalyze the hydrolysis of peptide bonds in proteins. It includes methods and applications that rely on the specific enzymatic activity of these proteins, such as protein digestion, analytical assays, and biotechnological processes for modifying or degrading polypeptides.
[C12Q 2521/539] Deaminase -- Deaminase denotes enzymes that catalyze removal of an amino group from nucleic-acid bases, converting them to deaminated products. These reactions are employed in biochemical assays and nucleic-acid manipulation techniques.
[C12Q 2521/543] Immobilised enzyme(s) -- C12Q 2521/543 refers to the use of enzymes that have been immobilised on a solid support, carrier or matrix to facilitate their recovery, reuse, or stability in biochemical reactions. This sub-class covers methods and devices for fixing enzymes in place for processes such as synthesis, analysis, or biocatalysis.
[C12Q 2522/00] Reaction characterised by the use of non-enzymatic proteins -- C12Q 2522/00 covers chemical or biochemical reactions in which a protein that does not act as an enzyme is employed as a reagent or catalyst. The subclass includes methods for preparing, using, or exploiting non-enzymatic proteins to effect transformations, often for analytical or preparative purposes within the broader field of enzyme- and nucleic-acid-based testing processes.
[C12Q 2522/10] Nucleic acid binding proteins -- This CPC subclass covers non-enzymatic proteins that selectively bind to DNA or RNA and are employed in assays or analytical methods for detecting, quantifying or manipulating nucleic acids. Such nucleic-acid-binding proteins include transcription factors, histones, polymerase-auxiliary proteins and engineered affinity reagents used in biochemical testing.
[C12Q 2522/101] Single or double stranded nucleic acid binding proteins -- C12Q 2522/101 covers proteins that bind single- or double-stranded nucleic acids but lack enzymatic activity, such as DNA-binding or RNA-binding proteins used in analytical or diagnostic methods. The subclass groups non-catalytic protein reagents employed in measuring or testing processes involving nucleic acids or microorganisms. It falls under the broader chemistry hierarchy of biochemistry and enzyme-free nucleic-acid assays.
[C12Q 2523/00] Reactions characterised by treatment of reaction samples -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2523/00 covers procedures that involve treating samples taken from chemical or biochemical reactions-such as filtering, extracting, separating or otherwise processing the reaction mixture-before analysis or further use. It is situated under chemistry > biochemistry > measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2523/10] Characterised by chemical treatment -- C12Q 2523/10 covers biotechnological assays in which the reaction mixture is chemically treated to modify or detect enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms. It applies to methods that alter the sample composition by adding reagents, solvents or catalysts before measurement. The subclass falls under chemistry, biochemistry, measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, and reactions characterised by treatment of reaction samples.
[C12Q 2523/101] Crosslinking agents, e.g. psoralen -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2523/101 covers crosslinking agents, such as psoralen, that chemically link nucleic acid strands or other biomolecules. These agents are employed in assays that measure or test enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms by stabilising reaction intermediates or creating covalent bonds between target molecules. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry and measuring/testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2523/107] Chemical cleaving agents -- C12Q 2523/107 covers chemical cleaving agents employed in measuring or testing processes that involve enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, where the reaction is defined by treatment of the sample. It includes reagents that break specific chemical bonds-such as nucleases, proteases, or other cleavage chemicals-used to prepare or analyse reaction mixtures in biochemical assays.
[C12Q 2523/109] chemical ligation between nucleic acids -- C12Q 2523/109 covers chemical ligation techniques that join nucleic acid fragments through non-enzymatic covalent bonding, enabling the synthesis of DNA or RNA constructs without the use of ligases. The classification includes methods that treat reaction samples to form phosphodiester or other linkages, applications in gene assembly, probe preparation and the development of synthetic genetic materials.
[C12Q 2523/113] Denaturating agents -- Denaturing agents are chemicals that disrupt the structure of enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, thereby inactivating them for analytical or testing purposes. This subclass covers the use of such agents in the treatment of reaction samples during measurement or testing processes.
[C12Q 2523/115] oxidising agents -- C12Q 2523/115 pertains to the use of oxidising agents in reaction-sample treatment steps that are part of biochemical measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms. It includes methods where an oxidising compound is added to a sample to modify or detect analytes during assay procedures.
[C12Q 2523/119] Renaturing agents -- Renaturing agents are chemicals added to reaction samples to restore the functional conformation of enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms that have been denatured, enabling them to regain activity for accurate measurement or testing. They facilitate refolding or reactivation of biomolecules after exposure to destabilising conditions.
