[C12Q 2523/125] Bisulfite(s) -- The C12Q 2523/125 entry covers the use of bisulfite reagents in biochemical testing, including methods that treat reaction samples with bisulfite to modify, detect, or quantify nucleic acids, proteins or other biomolecules in enzymatic or microbial assays. It encompasses analytical procedures where bisulfite serves as a reagent for sample preparation, derivatization or detection in enzyme-based, nucleic-acid-based, or microorganism-based measurement processes.
[C12Q 2523/30] Characterised by physical treatment -- This subclass covers biochemical assay methods in which reaction samples are characterized after being subjected to a physical treatment such as heating, cooling, stirring, centrifugation, filtration, irradiation or other mechanical or thermal actions, and the effect of that treatment on enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms is measured.
[C12Q 2523/301] Sonication -- C12Q 2523/301 Sonication covers the use of ultrasonic energy to treat reaction samples in biochemical assays, including those involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms. It includes methods where sonication is applied to improve mixing, disrupt cells, or accelerate reactions for analytical or preparative purposes.
[C12Q 2523/303] Applying a physical force on a nucleic acid -- The CPC entry C12Q 2523/303 covers methods that apply a physical force-such as shear, acoustic, electric, magnetic or mechanical stress-to nucleic acids in order to modify their conformation, facilitate separation, or enable downstream analysis. These techniques are used in biotechnological and analytical processes that treat reaction samples containing DNA or RNA, falling under the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy for measuring or testing enzymatic or nucleic-acid processes.
[C12Q 2523/305] Denaturation or renaturation by physical action -- The CPC entry C12Q 2523/305 covers methods that denature or renature biological reaction samples by applying physical actions such as heat, pressure, agitation, radiation or mechanical forces. These techniques are used in enzyme, nucleic-acid or microorganism testing to alter or restore molecular structure without chemical reagents.
[C12Q 2523/307] Denaturation or renaturation by electric current/voltage -- This CPC subclass covers methods that apply an electrical current or voltage to biological samples to induce denaturation or to promote renaturation of enzymes, nucleic acids, or microorganisms. The techniques are used for sample preparation, analysis, or recovery of function after electrophoretic or electrothermal treatment.
[C12Q 2523/308] Adsorption or desorption -- Code C12Q 2523/308 pertains to methods that involve adsorption or desorption of substances from reaction samples in biochemical or molecular testing. It covers techniques for binding, releasing, or separating analytes such as enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms to facilitate measurement, purification or analysis. The classification is used for processes that treat reaction mixtures to modify the distribution of target components.
[C12Q 2523/31] Electrostatic interactions, e.g. use of cationic polymers in hybridisation reactions -- The subclass details methods and reagents that exploit electrostatic forces, such as cationic polymers, to promote or regulate nucleic-acid hybridisation reactions. It includes the use of positively charged polymers to enhance binding of complementary strands in assays and other testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2523/313] Irradiation, e.g. UV irradiation -- This CPC subclass deals with irradiating reaction samples-such as exposure to ultraviolet light-to modify or analyse them in biochemical or microbiological testing. It is situated under measuring or testing processes that involve enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms within the broader chemistry and biochemistry fields.
[C12Q 2523/319] Photocleavage, photolysis, photoactivation -- C12Q 2523/319 designates methods that use light to break chemical bonds, induce molecular fragmentation, or trigger biochemical activity in reaction samples. It covers photocleavage, photolysis and photoactivation steps applied during enzymatic, nucleic-acid or microbial assays.
[C12Q 2523/32] Centrifugation -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2523/32 relates to the use of centrifugation for separating or concentrating components of reaction mixtures in biochemical testing, such as enzyme or nucleic-acid assays. It includes methods that apply centrifugal forces to reaction samples to isolate cells, proteins, nucleic acids or other analytes for further measurement.
[C12Q 2525/00] Reactions involving modified oligonucleotides, nucleic acids, or nucleotides -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2525/00 covers chemical processes that transform or manipulate nucleic acids, nucleotides or oligonucleotides in which the molecules have been chemically altered, such as incorporation of modified bases, backbone changes, or other synthetic modifications, and includes the synthesis, derivatization and functionalization steps required for these altered biomolecules.
[C12Q 2525/10] Modifications characterised by -- C12Q 2525/10 addresses chemical modifications of oligonucleotides, nucleic acids or nucleotides that are defined by a particular characteristic such as a functional group, label, or structural alteration. It is used to classify inventions where the modification itself is the subject of the claim and is identified by its distinctive properties.
[C12Q 2525/101] incorporating non-naturally occurring nucleotides, e.g. inosine -- C12Q 2525/101 relates to the incorporation of non-naturally occurring nucleotides, such as inosine, into oligonucleotides, nucleic acids or nucleotides. It covers processes for modifying the chemical structure of these biomolecules, enabling altered base pairing or enhanced stability for use in assays, diagnostics or therapeutic applications.
[C12Q 2525/107] incorporating a peptide nucleic acid -- This CPC subclass C12Q 2525/107 covers methods and compositions for incorporating a peptide nucleic acid into a nucleic acid strand or related structure, typically as part of modified oligonucleotide synthesis or biochemical testing. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms → reactions involving modified oligonucleotides, nucleic acids or nucleotides, and relates to the use of PNA as a synthetic analogue for hybridization, labeling or therapeutic applications.
[C12Q 2525/113] incorporating modified backbone -- C12Q 2525/113 covers methods and compositions for incorporating a modified backbone into oligonucleotides, nucleic acids or nucleotides, emphasizing chemical synthesis or enzymatic steps that introduce non-natural linkages, sugar or phosphate alterations, and is applied in the production of nucleic-acid based therapeutics, diagnostics or research reagents.
[C12Q 2525/117] incorporating modified base -- C12Q 2525/117 covers methods for incorporating a chemically modified nucleobase into an oligonucleotide, nucleic acid or nucleotide chain. The claim includes synthesis steps where the altered base is attached to a growing polymer or introduced by enzymatic or chemical means, enabling functionalized DNA or RNA molecules.
[C12Q 2525/119] incorporating abasic sites -- The code C12Q 2525/119 covers methods for introducing abasic sites-positions in a DNA or RNA strand that lack a nucleobase-into oligonucleotides, nucleic acids or nucleotides, a technique used in studies of DNA repair, enzymatic activity and synthetic biology. It falls under chemistry, biochemistry and measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2525/121] incorporating both deoxyribonucleotides and ribonucleotides -- C12Q 2525/121 covers processes for synthesizing nucleic acid polymers that contain both deoxyribonucleotide and ribonucleotide units, often using modified oligonucleotides or nucleic acids. It relates to biochemistry methods that incorporate mixed DNA-RNA strands in enzymatic or chemical reactions.
[C12Q 2525/125] incorporating agents resulting in resistance to degradation -- Subgroup C12Q 2525/125 includes methods and compositions in which nucleic acids or oligonucleotides are chemically modified or combined with agents that confer resistance to enzymatic or chemical degradation. The focus is on protective groups, backbone alterations, or conjugates that prolong stability for diagnostic, therapeutic, or research applications.
[C12Q 2525/131] incorporating a restriction site -- The classification covers methods for inserting a specific restriction-enzyme recognition sequence into a nucleic-acid molecule, typically by ligation, polymerase-chain reaction, or site-directed mutagenesis. It includes the design and execution of reactions that create or embed restriction sites within modified oligonucleotides, DNA, or RNA for downstream cloning or analysis.
[C12Q 2525/137] incorporating/modifying moieties to eliminate restriction sites -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2525/137 covers techniques for chemically or enzymatically adding or altering groups on oligonucleotides, DNA or RNA so that existing restriction-enzyme recognition sequences are destroyed or prevented from forming. Such modifications are employed in gene-editing, cloning and synthetic biology to avoid unwanted cleavage during manipulation of nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2525/143] incorporating a promoter sequence -- C12Q 2525/143 covers methods for inserting a promoter sequence into a nucleic acid strand, thereby creating a construct capable of driving transcription of downstream genes. The classification is used for biotechnological processes that modify oligonucleotides or nucleic acids to control gene-expression.
[C12Q 2525/149] incorporating a coding sequence -- C12Q 2525/149 covers processes for inserting a coding sequence into a nucleic acid or nucleic-acid-derived molecule. It includes recombinant methods that integrate genes, exons or other functional DNA segments into vectors, chromosomes or synthetic oligomers, enabling the expression of desired proteins or RNAs in host cells.
[C12Q 2525/15] incorporating a consensus or conserved sequence -- C12Q 2525/15 covers methods for inserting a consensus or conserved nucleic-acid sequence into a target molecule, often using synthetic oligonucleotides or recombinant techniques to embed functional or regulatory motifs. It is applied in the engineering of genes, probes, or vectors for research, diagnostic or therapeutic purposes.
[C12Q 2525/151] repeat or repeated sequences, e.g. VNTR, microsatellite, concatemer -- C12Q 2525/151 covers technologies that exploit repeat or repeated nucleic-acid sequences such as variable-number tandem repeats, microsatellites and concatemers. It is positioned under chemistry, biochemistry and measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, specifically those dealing with reactions involving modified oligonucleotides.

[C12Q 2531/101] Linear amplification, i.e. non exponential -- The C12Q 2531/101 subclass relates to linear, non-exponential amplification of nucleic acids, where the amount of product increases proportionally with each cycle rather than exponentially. It covers methods and reagents that generate single-strand or double-strand DNA or RNA without the cascade of traditional PCR, often used for precise quantitation or limited template replication.
[C12Q 2531/107] Probe or oligonucleotide ligation -- C12Q 2531/107 covers methods and reagents for ligating nucleic-acid probes or oligonucleotides, typically using ligases to join two complementary strands in a defined sequence. [C12Q 2531/113] PCR -- PCR, or polymerase chain reaction, is a technique for amplifying specific DNA sequences in vitro by repeated cycles of denaturation, primer annealing, and extension using a thermostable DNA polymerase. It enables rapid multiplication of nucleic acids for analysis, detection, or cloning, and is classified under CPC C12Q 2531/113 for reactions of nucleic acids characterized by enzymatic replication.
[C12Q 2531/119] Strand displacement amplification [SDA] -- Strand displacement amplification is an isothermal nucleic-acid amplification technique in which a DNA polymerase with strand-displacement activity synthesizes new strands while displacing downstream DNA, allowing exponential amplification without thermal cycling. It is employed for rapid detection of specific genetic sequences in diagnostics and research.
[C12Q 2531/125] Rolling circle -- The C12Q 2531/125 entry covers rolling-circle reactions of nucleic acids, a process in which a circular DNA template is continuously replicated by polymerase to produce long single-stranded DNA concatamers. This method is employed for nucleic-acid amplification, synthetic biology, and biosensor development, and it falls under the broader class of measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2531/131] Inverse PCR
[C12Q 2531/137] Ligase Chain Reaction [LCR] -- Ligase Chain Reaction (LCR) is an isothermal nucleic-acid amplification technique that employs a thermostable DNA ligase to join adjacent, sequence-specific oligonucleotides on a target strand, generating multiple copies of a defined DNA segment. It is used primarily for highly specific detection of mutations or pathogens and falls under the subclass of reactions of nucleic acids involving measuring or testing processes with enzymes.
[C12Q 2531/143] Promoter based amplification, e.g. NASBA, 3SR, TAS -- The code C12Q 2531/143 pertains to nucleic-acid amplification techniques that employ promoter sequences to drive synthesis, such as nucleic-acid-sequence-based amplification (NASBA), three-step-reaction (3SR) and transcription-mediated (TAS) methods. These processes generate multiple copies of RNA or DNA without thermal cycling, using enzymes like reverse transcriptase and RNA polymerase to produce amplified genetic material for detection or analysis.
[C12Q 2531/149] Replicase based amplification, e.g. Q beta replicase -- The class covers nucleic-acid amplification methods that employ replicase enzymes, such as Q-beta replicase, to generate multiple RNA copies from an RNA template without thermal cycling. These processes are used for measuring or testing nucleic acids and fall under reactions of nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2533/00] Reactions characterised by the enzymatic reaction principle used -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2533/00 concerns methods that use the principle of an enzymatic reaction as the basis for measuring or testing a substance, such as assay formats where the formation or consumption of a product of an enzyme-catalysed reaction indicates the presence, concentration or activity of a target analyte. It applies to biochemical diagnostics, monitoring, and screening procedures that rely on enzyme-mediated signal generation.

[C12Q 2533/101] Primer extension -- C12Q 2533/101 covers primer-extension methods in which a nucleic-acid primer is lengthened by an enzyme, typically a polymerase, using a template strand to generate a complementary extension. The process is employed in DNA sequencing, PCR amplification, labeling and detection of nucleic acids, and other analytical or synthetic applications that rely on enzyme-driven primer elongation.
[C12Q 2533/107] Probe or oligonucleotide ligation -- This CPC subgroup covers methods that join two nucleic-acid fragments using a probe or oligonucleotide ligase, allowing detection or analysis of specific DNA or RNA sequences. [C12Q 2535/101] Sanger sequencing method, i.e. oligonucleotide sequencing using primer elongation and dideoxynucleotides as chain terminators -- The C12Q 2535/101 class covers the Sanger sequencing method, a biochemical assay that determines nucleotide identities by extending a primer with DNA polymerase in the presence of dideoxynucleotides that act as chain terminators. This approach enables the determination of the sequence of an oligonucleotide by analyzing the lengths of terminated fragments.
[C12Q 2535/107] Maxam and Gilbert method, i.e. sequential release and detection of nucleotides -- The CPC class C12Q 2535/107 covers the Maxam-Gilbert method, a chemical sequencing procedure that determines nucleotide identity by sequentially cleaving DNA at specific bases and detecting the released fragments. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2535/113] Cycle sequencing -- C12Q 2535/113 covers cycle sequencing, a biochemical assay that determines the identity of individual nucleotide bases or the order of bases in an oligonucleotide by repeatedly extending a primer with labeled nucleotides and detecting incorporation. It falls within the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy for measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2535/119] Double strand sequencing -- C12Q 2535/119 pertains to analytical methods that determine the sequence of nucleotides in double-stranded nucleic acids. It includes assays that identify each base or the overall order of bases in a DNA or RNA duplex by measuring enzymatic or hybridisation reactions. The classification is used for technologies that generate sequence data from intact double-strand templates.
[C12Q 2535/122] Massive parallel sequencing -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2535/122 covers massive parallel sequencing, a set of high-throughput biochemical methods that use many simultaneous enzymatic reactions to determine the identity of nucleotide bases or whole oligonucleotide sequences. These techniques are used for rapid, large-scale DNA analysis in genomics, diagnostics and research.
[C12Q 2535/125] Allele specific primer extension -- Allele specific primer extension is a biochemical assay in which a DNA primer is extended only when it matches a particular allele, enabling the identification of specific nucleotide bases or sequence variants. The method is used for genotyping, mutation detection and other tests that determine the identity of nucleic-acid sequences by measuring primer-dependent polymerase activity.
[C12Q 2535/131] Allele specific probes -- The entry C12Q 2535/131 covers allele-specific probes employed in assays that determine the identity of a nucleotide base or an oligonucleotide sequence, typically by hybridising to a target DNA strand with a mismatch-sensitive detection method. It falls under measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms within the broader chemistry and biochemistry fields.

It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2535/138] Amplified fragment length polymorphism [AFLP] -- The code C12Q 2535/138 covers the amplified fragment length polymorphism (AFLP) technique, which uses restriction digestion followed by selective PCR amplification to produce DNA fingerprints for identifying nucleotide bases or oligonucleotide sequences. It is classified under chemistry, biochemistry and methods for measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2535/139] Random amplification polymorphism detection [RAPD] -- C12Q 2535/139 covers random amplification polymorphism detection (RAPD), a biochemical assay that uses arbitrary primers to amplify DNA fragments and reveals genetic variation. The method characterises nucleic-acid samples by the pattern of amplified products, enabling identification of specific nucleotide bases or oligonucleotide sequences.
[C12Q 2537/00] Reactions characterised by the reaction format or use of a specific feature -- C12Q 2537/00 covers reactions that are defined by their reaction format or by the use of a particular feature, such as a specific enzyme configuration, substrate arrangement, or assay condition, within the scope of biochemistry and molecular testing. [C12Q 2537/107] Homoduplex formation -- C12Q 2537/107 covers processes that generate homoduplex nucleic acid structures, where two identical strands anneal to form a duplex without mismatches. [C12Q 2537/113] Heteroduplex formation -- Heteroduplex formation refers to the annealing of complementary nucleic acid strands that contain sequence mismatches, resulting in double-stranded duplexes with mismatched base pairs. This reaction is employed in biochemical methods for detecting mutations, measuring hybridization specificity, or analyzing nucleic acid interactions, and is classified under C12Q 2537/113 for reaction formats involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2537/119] Triple helix formation -- C12Q 2537/119 covers methods for inducing or studying the formation of triple-helix nucleic-acid structures, where a third strand binds to the major groove of a double helix via Hoogsteen or reverse-Hoogsteen pairing. The class includes chemical or enzymatic procedures that promote, detect, or exploit these three-strand complexes in biotechnological or diagnostic applications.

[C12Q 2537/137] a displacement step -- C12Q 2537/137 covers chemical processes that include a displacement step, such as the removal or replacement of a bound component, within measuring or testing methods that involve enzymes, nucleic acids, or microorganisms. [C12Q 2537/149] Sequential reactions -- C12Q 2537/149 covers sequential reactions in which two or more biochemical steps are carried out one after another in a single operation, typically involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms. [C12Q 2537/155] Cyclic reactions -- C12Q 2537/155 classifies cyclic reactions in biochemical testing, where a substrate or intermediate is repeatedly transformed and regenerated within a single reaction pathway. [C12Q 2537/157] A reaction step characterised by the number of molecules incorporated or released -- The subclass covers reaction steps that are defined by the exact number of molecules that are incorporated into or released from the system. [C12Q 2537/159] Reduction of complexity, e.g. amplification of subsets, removing duplicated genomic regions -- C12Q 2537/159 covers techniques for simplifying genomic samples by selectively amplifying target regions or eliminating redundant DNA sequences. [C12Q 2537/161] A competitive reaction step -- A competitive reaction step refers to an assay format where a target molecule competes with a labeled or otherwise detectable analogue for binding to an enzyme, nucleic acid or microorganism, allowing quantification of the target based on the extent of competition. This is used in biochemical measurements and testing processes that rely on competitive binding dynamics.
[C12Q 2537/162] Helper probe -- Helper probes are molecular agents used in biochemical assays that bind specific enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms to enhance detection, often by carrying a label such as a fluorophore. They enable indirect measurement of the primary reaction by generating a measurable signal when the target is present.
[C12Q 2537/163] blocking probe -- C12Q 2537/163 covers the use of blocking probes in biochemical assays, where a nucleic-acid probe binds to a specific sequence or site to prevent its interaction and thus enable measurement of enzyme or nucleic-acid activity. [C12Q 2537/164] Methylation detection other then bisulfite or methylation sensitive restriction endonucleases -- C12Q 2537/164 covers methods for detecting DNA methylation that avoid bisulfite conversion or methylation-sensitive restriction enzymes, employing alternative chemical or enzymatic strategies. [C12Q 2537/165] Mathematical modelling, e.g. logarithm, ratio -- This subgroup covers the use of mathematical techniques, such as logarithmic or ratio calculations, to model and analyse biochemical reactions involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms. It includes applying these models to predict reaction behaviour, quantify activity or optimise experimental conditions.
[C12Q 2539/00] Reactions characterised by analysis of gene expression or genome comparison -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2539/00 covers biotechnological methods in which reactions are monitored by analysing gene-expression patterns or by comparing entire genomes. It refers to processes that use molecular data to characterize or control chemical or biological transformations, such as assessing pathway activity, detecting mutations, or optimizing production based on transcriptomic or genomic readouts. This subclass lies under measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2539/10] The purpose being sequence identification by analysis of gene expression or genome comparison characterised by -- C12Q 2539/10 covers methods that identify nucleic-acid sequences by analyzing gene-expression patterns or by comparing whole-genome data. These techniques are used to pinpoint specific DNA or RNA sequences for diagnostic, therapeutic or research purposes, employing enzymatic or molecular assays to detect expression signatures or genomic similarity. The classification lies within chemistry, biochemistry and measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2539/101] Subtraction analysis -- Subtraction analysis comprises techniques that compare nucleic-acid samples by removing sequences common to both, thereby highlighting genes or genomic regions that are present or expressed uniquely in one sample. This approach is used to identify differential gene expression or genomic differences in biotechnology, molecular biology and related fields.
[C12Q 2539/103] Serial analysis of gene expression [SAGE] -- The C12Q 2539/103 entry covers the Serial Analysis of Gene Expression (SAGE) technique, a high-throughput method that quantifies gene expression by extracting short tags from mRNA, concatenating them, and sequencing to create a comprehensive expression profile, enabling analysis of transcriptional activity and comparative genomics.
[C12Q 2539/105] Involving introns, exons, or splice junctions -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2539/105 covers reactions in which gene-expression or genome-comparison analysis specifically targets introns, exons or splice-junction regions. It includes methods for detecting, quantifying or characterising splicing variants, exon-intron structures and related nucleic-acid fragments during enzymatic or biochemical testing. This subclass lies under chemistry → biochemistry → measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2539/107] Representational Difference Analysis [RDA] -- Representational Difference Analysis (RDA) is a molecular technique that compares two DNA samples to uncover sequence differences, allowing detection of rare or novel genomic fragments and assessment of gene expression changes. It involves creating reduced representations of the genomes, hybridizing them, and selectively amplifying unique fragments, facilitating genome subtraction, mutation screening, and comparative genomics.
[C12Q 2539/113] Differential Display Analysis [DDA] -- Differential Display Analysis (DDA) is a molecular technique that compares mRNA expression patterns between biological samples by amplifying and separating cDNA fragments, enabling detection of genes with altered transcription levels. It falls under measuring or testing processes involving nucleic acids, specifically reactions characterised by analysis of gene expression or genome comparison.
[C12Q 2539/115] Comparative genomic hybridisation [CGH] -- Comparative genomic hybridisation (CGH) is a molecular-cytogenetic technique that simultaneously hybridises test and reference DNA to a common array or metaphase spread, allowing detection and quantification of copy-number variations across the genome. It is used to compare gene expression or genome composition between samples, supporting diagnosis of chromosomal abnormalities, cancer profiling and genetic research.
[C12Q 2541/00] Reactions characterised by directed evolution -- This group covers biocatalytic reactions in which an enzyme, microorganism or other biological catalyst has been obtained or improved by directed-evolution techniques such as iterative mutagenesis and selection. It includes processes that use the evolved catalyst to achieve enhanced activity, selectivity, stability or altered substrate range in chemical or biochemical transformations.
[C12Q 2541/10] the purpose being the selection or design of target specific nucleic acid binding sequences -- C12Q 2541/10 covers techniques that employ directed evolution to select or engineer nucleic-acid sequences with specific binding affinity for a target molecule. It includes methods such as SELEX, phage display of nucleic acids, and iterative mutagenesis to generate aptamers, ribozymes or guide RNAs tailored for particular proteins, cells or small compounds.
[C12Q 2541/101] Selex -- The entry C12Q 2541/101 "Selex" covers the systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment, a directed-evolution technique for isolating nucleic-acid or peptide aptamers that bind specific targets. It includes the iterative cycles of binding, partitioning, amplification and conditioning of libraries to obtain high-affinity molecules, and related analytical or testing steps.
[C12Q 2543/00] Reactions characterised by the reaction site, e.g. cell or chromosome -- The subclass covers processes in which the reaction is defined by its specific location within a biological entity, such as a particular cell, organelle, chromosome, or other subcellular region. It includes methods that exploit the spatial context of enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms to control or monitor the reaction. This focus on the reaction site enables tailored biotechnological and diagnostic applications.
[C12Q 2543/10] the purpose being "
[C12Q 2543/101] in situ  amplification -- C12Q 2543/101 covers methods that amplify nucleic-acid sequences directly within biological samples such as cells, tissues or chromosomes, allowing detection or analysis without extracting the DNA or RNA. These in situ amplification techniques are used in diagnostics, research and forensic applications to visualize or quantify target sequences in their native location.
[C12Q 2545/00] Reactions characterised by their quantitative nature -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2545/00 groups chemical or biochemical reactions that are defined primarily by quantitative characteristics such as concentration, amount, rate, yield or other measurable parameters, typically involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms and used for precise analytical or process control.
[C12Q 2545/10] the purpose being quantitative analysis -- The C12Q 2545/10 entry covers measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms when the primary aim is quantitative analysis. It includes methods that determine the amount, concentration, activity or kinetic parameters of the biological material, emphasizing numerical results rather than merely qualitative detection.
[C12Q 2545/101] with an internal standard/control -- C12Q 2545/101 covers quantitative assays in biochemistry where an internal standard or control is added to the reaction mixture. The internal reference enables accurate measurement of enzyme activity, nucleic-acid reactions, or microbial processes by compensating for variations in sample handling or instrument response.
[C12Q 2545/107] with a competitive internal standard/control -- This CPC subclass (C12Q 2545/107) covers quantitative biochemical assays that employ a competitive internal standard or control to improve accuracy when measuring enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms. The method relies on the analyte competing with a known reference compound, enabling precise determination of concentration or activity in the tested sample.
[C12Q 2545/113] with an external standard/control, i.e. control reaction is separated from the test/target reaction -- This CPC subclass C12Q 2545/113, under chemistry-biochemistry-measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, addresses quantitative reaction assays that employ an external standard or control, with the control reaction physically separated from the test or target reaction.
[C12Q 2545/114] involving a quantitation step -- The subclass C12Q 2545/114 refers to biochemistry procedures that incorporate a quantitation step, i.e., any reaction where the amount of product, substrate, nucleic acid or microorganism is measured to provide numerical data. It covers assays such as enzyme-linked assays, PCR quantification, and other analytical methods that determine concentration or activity as part of the process.
[C12Q 2547/00] Reactions characterised by the features used to prevent contamination -- C12Q 2547/00 covers chemical or biochemical reactions in which specific design features or procedural steps are incorporated to avoid contamination of the reaction medium, reagents, or biological components. It applies to processes that involve enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms and includes measures such as sterile containment, contaminant-blocking reagents, or physical barriers to maintain reaction integrity.
[C12Q 2547/10] the purpose being preventing contamination -- C12Q 2547/10 covers biochemical measuring or testing processes such as enzyme, nucleic-acid or microbial assays where the primary aim is to prevent contamination of the sample, reagents or environment. It includes features or steps designed to exclude external microorganisms, nucleic acids or interfering substances during the reaction.
[C12Q 2547/101] by confinement to a single tube/container -- C12Q 2547/101 covers techniques that avoid cross-contamination by performing the entire biochemical or microbiological assay inside a single tube or container. It is used for measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms where the reaction is confined to one closed vessel to maintain sterility and integrity.
[C12Q 2547/107] Use of permeable barriers, e.g. waxes -- This subgroup covers methods that employ permeable barriers such as waxes to isolate reaction zones while allowing diffusion of reagents, thereby reducing contamination during enzymatic, nucleic-acid or microbial assays. The barriers permit selective transport of substrates or products while maintaining sterility of the test environment.
[C12Q 2549/00] Reactions characterised by the features used to influence the efficiency or specificity -- This subclass groups chemical or biochemical reactions whose efficiency or specificity is deliberately altered by using particular features such as reaction conditions, specialised catalysts, enzyme or nucleic-acid modifications, or microorganism engineering. It covers methods that adjust these features to enhance yield, selectivity, or rate of the reaction.
[C12Q 2549/10] the purpose being that of reducing false positive or false negative signals -- C12Q 2549/10 covers biochemical assay methods or reagents designed to lower the incidence of false-positive or false-negative results, thereby improving the reliability of tests that rely on enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms. It pertains to strategies that modify reaction conditions or detection mechanisms to enhance signal specificity and overall test accuracy.
[C12Q 2549/101] Hot start -- Hot start is a PCR technique in which the DNA polymerase is kept inactive at lower temperatures and only becomes active after an initial heating step, thereby reducing non-specific amplification and primer-dimer formation. This approach enhances the efficiency and specificity of enzymatic nucleic-acid amplification processes.
[C12Q 2549/107] Cold start -- This subclass covers methods that begin enzyme, nucleic-acid or microbial reactions by first keeping the system at a low temperature and then initiating the process-a "cold start" used to improve control, reduce non-specific activity and enhance efficiency or specificity in measuring or testing biochemical reactions.
[C12Q 2549/113] using nested probes -- Techniques employing nested probes, i.e., sets of oligonucleotide primers where one primer anneals within the amplicon generated by another, to enhance detection specificity and sensitivity in nucleic acid assays. The approach is commonly applied in PCR, qPCR, or isothermal amplification to discriminate target sequences from closely related variants.
[C12Q 2549/119] using nested primers -- C12Q 2549/119 covers the use of nested primers, a PCR strategy in which a second pair of primers binds within the first amplicon to boost specificity and yield, especially for low-copy targets or complex samples. By performing two successive amplification rounds, the method reduces nonspecific products and enhances detection of nucleic acids in biochemical assays.
[C12Q 2549/125] using sterilising/blocking agents, e.g. albumin -- C12Q 2549/125 concerns methods that use sterilising or blocking agents such as albumin to modify the efficiency or specificity of reactions employed in measuring or testing enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, typically to prevent contamination or non-specific binding during assays.
[C12Q 2549/126] using oligonucleotides as clamps -- The entry covers methods that employ short oligonucleotides designed to bind to a target nucleic-acid strand and act as a clamp, stabilising the hybrid and preventing strand displacement during enzymatic reactions. By fixing the template, the clamp increases the efficiency or specificity of processes such as PCR, sequencing, or other enzyme-based assays that involve nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2560/00] Nucleic acid detection -- C12Q 2560/00 relates to methods and apparatus for detecting nucleic acids, such as DNA or RNA, in a sample. It includes assays, probes, amplification techniques and analytical devices used in molecular diagnostics, environmental monitoring, food safety and forensic analysis.
[C12Q 2561/00] Nucleic acid detection characterised by assay method -- C12Q 2561/00 relates to methods for detecting nucleic acids where the detection principle is defined by the specific assay protocol used. It includes assays that employ enzymes, probes, or other reagents to identify DNA or RNA sequences, typically in diagnostic or research settings. The classification covers techniques such as PCR-based detection, hybridization assays, and is intended for inventions where the assay design itself is the distinguishing feature.
[C12Q 2561/101] Taqman -- C12Q 2561/101 covers nucleic acid detection methods that employ the TaqMan assay, a real-time PCR technique using a fluorogenic probe cleaved by DNA polymerase during amplification. The method enables quantitative monitoring of specific DNA or RNA sequences as the reaction proceeds.
[C12Q 2561/107] Enzyme complementation -- C12Q 2561/107 defines assay methods that employ enzyme complementation to detect nucleic acids, whereby the presence of a specific nucleic-acid target restores the activity of a split or otherwise inactive enzyme, producing a measurable signal. The class covers techniques in which enzyme fragments combine only when the target is present, enabling quantitative or qualitative nucleic-acid testing.
[C12Q 2561/108] Hybridisation protection assay [HPA] -- The hybridisation protection assay (HPA) is a homogeneous nucleic-acid detection method in which a labeled probe remains stable only when it hybridises to its target sequence, allowing enzymatic degradation of unbound probe and generating a measurable signal without washing steps. It is used to quantify specific DNA or RNA in samples and is categorized under CPC code C12Q 2561/108 within measuring or testing processes involving nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2561/109] Invader technology -- Invader technology is a nucleic-acid detection method that employs structure-specific cleavage enzymes to generate a fluorescent signal only when a target DNA or RNA sequence is present, allowing highly specific and quantitative identification of genetic variants or pathogens. It falls under measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids, or microorganisms, and is classified in the Cooperative Patent Classification under C12Q 2561/109.
[C12Q 2561/113] Real time assay -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2561/113 covers real-time nucleic-acid detection assays, where the assay method itself characterises the detection. It falls under chemistry - biochemistry - measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, specifically for nucleic-acid detection by assay method.
[C12Q 2561/119] Fluorescence polarisation -- Fluorescence polarisation refers to a nucleic-acid detection technique that measures changes in the polarization of fluorescent light emitted by a labelled probe when it binds to a target sequence. The assay provides a rapid, homogeneous method for quantifying DNA or RNA without the need for separation steps.
[C12Q 2561/12] Fluorescence lifetime measurement -- This CPC subclass covers techniques for detecting nucleic acids using fluorescence lifetime measurements, where the assay method relies on measuring the decay time of fluorescence emitted after excitation. It falls under chemistry, biochemistry, measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, specifically nucleic acid detection by assay method.
[C12Q 2561/125] Ligase Detection Reaction [LDR] -- The Ligase Detection Reaction (LDR) is a nucleic-acid-based assay that uses a DNA ligase to join two adjacent oligonucleotide probes only when they are perfectly complementary to a target sequence, producing a ligated product that can be amplified or directly detected. This method enables highly specific identification of single-nucleotide variations, gene mutations, or pathogen DNA and is frequently combined with PCR or fluorescence readouts for rapid molecular diagnostics.
[C12Q 2561/127] Protein truncation assay -- The C12Q 2561/127 protein truncation assay covers methods that detect nucleic acid sequences by monitoring whether a synthesized protein is prematurely terminated, indicating the presence of mutations such as nonsense or frameshift changes. The assay typically involves in-vitro transcription-translation of a target gene and analysis of the resulting protein length, providing a rapid tool for genetic screening and disease diagnosis.
[C12Q 2563/00] Nucleic acid detection characterized by the use of physical, structural and functional properties -- C12Q 2563/00 covers methods and devices for detecting nucleic acids that rely on the inherent physical, structural or functional attributes of the target molecules, such as hybridisation-induced conformational changes, melting behaviour, or specific binding-induced signals, rather than on external labels or amplification steps. It includes assay formats that translate these intrinsic properties into measurable outputs for diagnostic or analytical purposes.
[C12Q 2563/101] radioactivity, e.g. radioactive labels -- A subcategory of nucleic-acid detection methods that employ radioactive materials, such as radioactive isotopes or labels, to identify or quantify nucleic acid sequences. It falls under testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms and focuses on exploiting the physical and functional properties of radioactivity for analytical purposes.
[C12Q 2563/103] luminescence -- C12Q 2563/103 concerns luminescent techniques for detecting nucleic acids, where the signal is generated by the emission of light from a probe or reaction product. It includes methods that exploit fluorescence, bioluminescence, chemiluminescence or other light-emitting processes to indicate the presence or quantity of target DNA or RNA.
[C12Q 2563/107] fluorescence -- C12Q 2563/107 covers nucleic acid detection methods that employ fluorescence, where the target molecule is identified or quantified by measuring light emitted after excitation of a fluorescent label or inherent fluorophore. Techniques include fluorophore-tagged probes, intercalating dyes and fluorescence resonance energy transfer applied to DNA, RNA or related biopolymers.
[C12Q 2563/113] the label being electroactive, e.g. redox labels -- This subclass covers nucleic-acid detection methods that employ an electroactive (redox) label as the reporter, generating an electrical signal when the label interacts with the target. It includes assays using redox-active molecules to transduce hybridisation events into measurable electrochemical responses.
[C12Q 2563/116] electrical properties of nucleic acids, e.g. impedance, conductivity or resistance -- C12Q 2563/116 relates to the measurement of electrical characteristics of nucleic acids, including impedance, conductivity and resistance, using their physical, structural or functional properties. It is classified under chemistry, biochemistry and methods for detecting nucleic acids based on such properties.
[C12Q 2563/119] the label being proteinic -- C12Q 2563/119 pertains to nucleic-acid detection methods that use protein-based labels as reporters. The subclass covers assays in which a protein tag such as an enzyme, antibody or fluorescent protein is attached to a nucleic-acid probe or target to generate a measurable signal based on the protein's physical or functional properties.
[C12Q 2563/125] the label being enzymatic, i.e. proteins, and non proteins, such as nucleic acid with enzymatic activity -- C12Q 2563/125 covers nucleic-acid detection methods that employ a label with intrinsic enzymatic activity, such as protein enzymes or catalytic nucleic acids (ribozymes, DNAzymes). The label itself catalyzes a reaction that produces a measurable signal, enabling detection of the target nucleic acid. This sub-group falls under detection techniques based on physical, structural and functional properties.
[C12Q 2563/131] the label being a member of a cognate binding pair, i.e. extends to antibodies, haptens, avidin -- C12Q 2563/131 covers nucleic acid detection methods that employ a label which is part of a cognate binding pair, for example an antibody-antigen, hapten-antibody, or avidin-biotin system, to generate a signal indicating the presence of the target nucleic acid.
[C12Q 2563/137] Metal/ion, e.g. metal label -- C12Q 2563/137 pertains to nucleic-acid detection techniques that employ metal or ion labels as reporters. The metal-based tags-such as nanoparticles, quantum dots, or ionic complexes-are attached to probes or target strands and generate a measurable signal (optical, electrochemical, magnetic) reflecting the nucleic acid's presence or quantity. This subclass falls under biochemical testing methods that exploit physical, structural and functional properties for assay read-out.
[C12Q 2563/143] Magnetism, e.g. magnetic label -- C12Q 2563/143 falls under nucleic acid detection methods that exploit physical, structural and functional characteristics, focusing on magnetic techniques such as magnetic labeling. It covers the use of magnetic particles or fields to bind, isolate, or signal nucleic acids in assays, enabling detection or quantification.
[C12Q 2563/149] Particles, e.g. beads -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2563/149 relates to the use of particles such as beads in nucleic-acid detection methods that exploit physical, structural or functional properties of the target molecules. It covers bead-based formats employed in assays measuring or testing enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2563/155] Particles of a defined size, e.g. nanoparticles -- C12Q 2563/155 covers particles of a defined size, such as nanoparticles, employed in nucleic-acid detection methods that rely on physical, structural and functional properties. It falls within chemistry > biochemistry > measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, specifically the subclass for nucleic-acid detection. The entry focuses on size-controlled carriers or probes used for diagnostic or analytical applications.
[C12Q 2563/157] Nanotubes or nanorods -- The subclass covers the use of nanotubes or nanorods as assay components for detecting nucleic acids, relying on the physical, structural or functional properties of the nanomaterials such as conductivity, fluorescence or surface-binding characteristics.
[C12Q 2563/159] Microreactors, e.g. emulsion PCR or sequencing, droplet PCR, microcapsules, i.e. non-liquid containers with a range of different permeability's for different reaction components -- A microreactor system that encloses enzymatic or nucleic-acid reactions in isolated compartments such as emulsified droplets, microcapsules or other non-liquid containers, each engineered with specific permeability to allow selective passage of reagents, gases or products. These devices support PCR, sequencing or similar assays by controlling the physical, structural and functional environment of the reaction.
[C12Q 2563/161] Vesicles, e.g. liposome -- This subclass concerns the use of vesicles such as liposomes as carriers or platforms in nucleic-acid detection methods that rely on the physical, structural or functional properties of the target molecules. It falls under chemistry → biochemistry → measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, specifically the category for nucleic-acid detection by such properties.
[C12Q 2563/167] Mass label -- Mass label refers to the use of isotopic or other mass-adding tags attached to nucleic acid targets to enable detection and quantification, typically by mass-spectrometric or similar techniques. The approach exploits the distinct physical, structural or functional properties introduced by the label to differentiate the target from background material. It is employed in assays that identify nucleic acids through the added mass signature of the label.
[C12Q 2563/173] staining/intercalating agent, e.g. ethidium bromide -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2563/173 covers reagents that bind to nucleic acids and allow their detection by exploiting physical, structural or functional properties, such as intercalating or staining agents. Typical examples include ethidium bromide, which fluoresces when intercalated into DNA or RNA for visualization in gels and other assays.
[C12Q 2563/179] the label being a nucleic acid -- This subclass covers methods for detecting nucleic acids where the detection label itself is a nucleic acid, such as a complementary probe, aptamer or nucleic-acid-based reporter. The approach exploits the physical, structural or functional properties of the label to generate a measurable signal, enabling identification or quantification of target nucleic acid sequences.
[C12Q 2563/185] Nucleic acid dedicated to use as a hidden marker/bar code, e.g. inclusion of nucleic acids to mark art objects or animals -- A C12Q 2563/185 entry covers nucleic acids designed to serve as concealed identifiers or bar-codes, such as DNA sequences embedded in artworks, objects, livestock or wildlife to enable later detection and authentication. It concerns the creation of synthetic or natural nucleic acid markers that remain hidden under normal conditions but can be revealed by specific analytical or molecular-biological techniques. This falls within the chemistry subfield of nucleic-acid-based testing and measurement.
[C12Q 2565/00] Nucleic acid analysis characterised by mode or means of detection -- C12Q 2565/00 covers methods and devices for analysing nucleic acids that are distinguished by the specific mode or means used to detect the target, such as optical, electrochemical, or mass-spectrometric techniques. It falls within the broader category of biochemical measuring or testing processes that involve enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2565/10] Detection mode being characterised by the assay principle -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2565/10 covers nucleic-acid analysis where the detection mode is defined by the underlying assay principle, such as hybridisation, enzymatic conversion, amplification, optical or electrochemical read-out, rather than by the target or sample type. It groups methods that rely on a specific mechanistic approach to generate a measurable signal for identifying or quantifying nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2565/101] Interaction between at least two labels -- This CPC subclass C12Q 2565/101 concerns nucleic-acid analysis methods that employ at least two interacting labels, such as dual-probe or reporter-pair systems, to generate a detectable signal. These techniques are used for measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, often relying on fluorescence resonance energy transfer or similar mechanisms to indicate the presence or quantity of a target sequence.
[C12Q 2565/1015] labels being on the same oligonucleotide -- The code C12Q 2565/1015 covers nucleic-acid analysis techniques where two or more detectable labels-such as fluorescent, radioactive, or enzymatic tags-are attached to a single oligonucleotide probe, enabling simultaneous or multiplexed signal generation during hybridisation or amplification assays. This arrangement is used to improve assay sensitivity, allow internal referencing, or provide multiple detection modalities on one target-binding strand.
[C12Q 2565/102] Multiple non-interacting labels -- The class covers nucleic-acid analysis methods that employ several distinct, non-interacting labels to detect multiple targets in the same assay. These labels function independently, allowing simultaneous measurement without cross-reactivity, and are used in enzyme-linked or other biochemical detection formats.
[C12Q 2565/1025] labels being on the same oligonucleotide -- C12Q 2565/1025 covers nucleic-acid detection methods in which two or more labels are attached to the same oligonucleotide probe. The arrangement is used to generate combined or distinct signals for improved sensitivity, multiplexing or internal referencing in assays such as PCR, hybridisation or sequencing. This subclass falls under measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms within biochemistry and chemistry.
[C12Q 2565/107] Alteration in the property of hybridised versus free label oligonucleotides -- A method for analysing nucleic acids that monitors how the physical or chemical properties of a labelled oligonucleotide change when it forms a hybrid with a target strand compared with its unbound state. This detection approach is used to identify or quantify specific DNA or RNA sequences in biochemical assays.
[C12Q 2565/113] based on agglutination/precipitation -- This CPC subclass (C12Q 2565/113) covers nucleic-acid analysis methods whose detection relies on agglutination or precipitation reactions. It is used for assays that identify DNA or RNA by the visual formation of clumps or insoluble complexes rather than fluorescence or enzymatic signals.
[C12Q 2565/119] based on extraction of label to an organic phase, i.e. partitioning of label between different organic phases -- This subclass concerns nucleic-acid analytical methods where the detection signal is generated by moving a labelled component into an organic solvent, allowing the label to partition between two organic phases. It includes procedures that extract the label from the aqueous sample into an organic phase for measurement.
[C12Q 2565/125] Electrophoretic separation -- C12Q 2565/125 covers electrophoretic separation, a technique for analyzing nucleic acids in which molecules are driven through a gel or other medium by an electric field to resolve them by size or charge. It falls under the CPC group for nucleic acid analysis by detection mode, within the broader chemistry and biochemistry categories.
[C12Q 2565/131] Single/double strand conformational analysis, i.e. SSCP/DSCP -- This subclass covers methods that examine the three-dimensional shape of single- or double-stranded nucleic acids, such as single-strand conformation polymorphism (SSCP) and double-strand conformation polymorphism (DSCP), to detect sequence variations by observing mobility changes during electrophoretic separation. It is used for rapid genetic screening and mutation analysis without direct sequencing.
[C12Q 2565/133] conformational analysis -- C12Q 2565/133 covers techniques for examining the three-dimensional structure and conformational changes of nucleic acids. It includes methods that detect or measure folding, bending, or secondary-structure alterations using spectroscopic, electrophoretic, or computational approaches.
[C12Q 2565/137] Chromatographic separation -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2565/137 pertains to chromatographic separation methods applied in nucleic-acid analysis. It covers techniques such as liquid, gas, or affinity chromatography used to separate DNA, RNA, or related nucleic-acid fragments before detection or further testing. This entry is nested under biochemical measuring processes involving nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2565/20] Detection means characterised by being a gene reporter based analysis -- This CPC subclass covers methods that use gene reporters - DNA or RNA sequences that produce a detectable signal - to identify nucleic acid targets. It includes assays where the reporter gene is expressed or its product is measured, enabling indirect detection of specific DNA or RNA sequences.
[C12Q 2565/201] Two hybrid system -- This CPC subclass covers nucleic-acid analysis methods that employ two distinct hybrid-system components to detect target sequences, combining separate hybridisation mechanisms-such as a probe-target pair and a reporter-probe complex-to improve specificity or signal generation. It includes arrangements where the dual system works jointly to produce a measurable output.
[C12Q 2565/207] Three hybrid system -- This subclass covers nucleic acid detection methods that employ a three-component hybrid system, where a target strand is simultaneously bound by two or more complementary probes together with a third element such as a label, enzyme or nanoparticle to generate a measurable signal. It is used in assays that combine hybridization with signal amplification or transduction.
[C12Q 2565/30] Detection characterised by liberation or release of label -- C12Q 2565/30 covers nucleic acid analysis methods where detection is based on the liberation or release of a label, such as fluorescent dyes, enzymes, or chemiluminescent tags, from a probe or carrier upon binding to the target. The technique signals the presence of specific DNA or RNA sequences in assays that involve enzymatic or chemical reactions.
[C12Q 2565/301] Pyrophosphate (PPi) -- The C12Q 2565/301 subclass covers analytical methods that detect or quantify pyrophosphate (PPi), a common by-product of nucleic-acid polymerization and other enzymatic reactions. It includes assays, sensors and instrumental techniques that use PPi as an indirect indicator of nucleic-acid synthesis, cleavage or amplification, often to monitor enzyme activity or confirm the presence of target nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2565/40] Detection characterised by signal amplification of label -- The subclass covers nucleic-acid detection methods that rely on amplifying the signal from a label rather than amplifying the target itself, including enzymatic, chemical or physical means to boost the reporter's output. It applies to assays using labeled probes where signal amplification improves sensitivity and enables quantitative or qualitative analysis.
[C12Q 2565/401] Signal amplification by chemical polymerisation -- Signal amplification by chemical polymerisation refers to techniques that increase the detectable signal of nucleic acids or related biomolecules through catalytic polymer growth, such as the formation of a polymer matrix or precipitate triggered by a target-specific reaction. The resulting amplified signal is measured in diagnostic or analytical assays to improve sensitivity.
[C12Q 2565/50] Detection characterised by immobilisation to a surface -- This CPC subclass covers nucleic-acid analysis methods where detection relies on immobilising the target or a probe onto a solid surface such as a chip, membrane or bead, and then measuring a resulting signal. It includes micro-array, biosensor and other surface-bound hybridisation techniques used for DNA or RNA detection.
[C12Q 2565/501] being an array of oligonucleotides -- This CPC subclass covers nucleic acid analysis methods that employ arrays of oligonucleotides as detection elements. It includes techniques where many distinct short DNA or RNA sequences are immobilized on a solid support to simultaneously probe for complementary targets, enabling multiplexed identification or quantification of nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2565/507] characterised by the density of the capture oligonucleotide -- C12Q 2565/507 covers nucleic-acid analysis methods that detect target sequences by exploiting the density of capture oligonucleotides on a solid support. The approach uses variations in probe spacing or surface loading to influence hybridisation efficiency and signal generation, enabling quantitative or qualitative detection of nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2565/513] characterised by the pattern of the arrayed oligonucleotides -- This subclass pertains to nucleic acid analysis methods where detection relies on the specific spatial arrangement or pattern of oligonucleotides immobilized on a solid support. The pattern of the arrayed probes is used to identify target sequences, often in microarray or chip formats. It falls under the CPC group C12Q 2565/513.
[C12Q 2565/514] characterised by the use of the arrayed oligonucleotides as identifier tags, e.g. universal addressable array, anti-tag or tag complement array -- C12Q 2565/514 relates to nucleic acid analysis methods that use arrayed oligonucleotide identifier tags, such as universal addressable arrays, anti-tag or tag-complement arrays, to detect target sequences. It is a sub-class within the chemistry hierarchy for measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2565/515] characterised by the interaction between or sequential use of two or more arrays -- C12Q 2565/515 covers nucleic-acid analysis methods that detect targets by using two or more arrays in combination, either interacting directly or applied sequentially. It pertains to techniques where multiple micro- or bio-arrays are employed together to improve specificity, multiplexing, or signal amplification in enzyme-, nucleic-acid- or microorganism-based assays.
[C12Q 2565/518] characterised by the immobilisation of the nucleic acid sample or target -- C12Q 2565/518 covers nucleic-acid analysis methods that involve immobilising the sample or target on a solid support, such as microarray spots, bead-based assays or sensor surfaces, to facilitate detection and measurement.
[C12Q 2565/519] characterised by the capture moiety being a single stranded oligonucleotide -- This subclass covers nucleic-acid detection methods in which the capture element that binds the target is a single-stranded oligonucleotide. It includes assays, devices and platforms that rely on hybridisation of the probe to DNA or RNA for analysis, such as microarrays, biosensors and other hybridisation-based test formats.
[C12Q 2565/525] characterised by the capture oligonucleotide being double stranded -- This subclass covers nucleic-acid analysis methods where the capture oligonucleotide is provided as a double-stranded probe. It includes techniques that use a pre-hybridized pair of strands to bind a target nucleic acid, such as hybridization arrays, biosensor assays, or other detection formats that rely on the double-stranded capture element.
[C12Q 2565/531] characterised by the capture moiety being a protein for target oligonucleotides -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2565/531 covers nucleic-acid analysis methods in which the target oligonucleotide is captured by a protein moiety that serves as the detection element. It belongs to the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy under measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms. This entry specifically relates to protein-based capture agents used to bind and identify selected oligonucleotide sequences.
[C12Q 2565/537] characterised by the capture oligonucleotide acting as a primer -- C12Q 2565/537 covers nucleic-acid analysis methods in which the capture oligonucleotide that binds the target sequence also functions as a primer to initiate polymerase-mediated amplification. The subclass is used for techniques that combine target capture and primer extension in a single oligonucleotide probe for detection.
[C12Q 2565/543] characterised by the use of two or more capture oligonucleotide primers in concert, e.g. bridge amplification -- This CPC subclass covers nucleic-acid analysis methods that use two or more capture oligonucleotide primers together, for example bridge-amplification techniques that enrich target sequences. It falls under measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms within the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy.
[C12Q 2565/549] characterised by the capture oligonucleotide being a reporter labelled capture oligonucleotide -- This CPC entry covers nucleic-acid analysis methods in which the capture oligonucleotide is itself a reporter-labelled probe, allowing direct detection of target sequences during hybridisation. The label, such as a fluorophore or enzyme, generates a measurable signal when the capture probe binds the nucleic acid of interest.
[C12Q 2565/60] Detection means characterised by use of a special device -- C12Q 2565/60 covers nucleic-acid analysis methods in which the detection step relies on a dedicated device, such as an optical, electro-chemical or micro-fluidic sensor, rather than solely on chemical reagents. It includes specialized hardware that generates, amplifies or reads the analytical signal for DNA or RNA detection.
[C12Q 2565/601] being a microscope, e.g. atomic force microscopy [AFM] -- C12Q 2565/601 covers nucleic-acid analysis methods that employ a microscope, such as atomic-force microscopy, to detect or image the target. It is a sub-category of biochemical measuring processes that use optical or scanning probe techniques to characterize nucleic acids.
[C12Q 2565/607] being a sensor, e.g. electrode -- C12Q 2565/607 covers nucleic-acid analysis methods that use a sensor, such as an electrode, to detect the target. These devices typically transduce a biochemical interaction-binding or enzymatic reaction-into an electrical signal that can be measured. The classification applies to electrochemical or similar sensor-based detection schemes.
