[C12Q 2565/625] being a nucleic acid test strip device, e.g. dipsticks, strips, tapes, CD plates -- The code C12Q 2565/625 covers nucleic-acid test-strip devices such as dipsticks, strips, tapes and CD plates that perform detection of DNA or RNA without the need for complex instrumentation. These devices belong to the category of biochemical measuring and testing processes using enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2565/626] being a flow cytometer -- This subclass covers flow cytometers, instruments that analyze nucleic acids by detecting fluorescent signals from labeled particles or cells as they pass through a fluid stream. It pertains to methods of nucleic-acid analysis that rely on optical detection, sorting, or counting of microscopic samples, and is situated under measuring and testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2565/627] being a mass spectrometer -- The subclass C12Q 2565/627 covers the use of mass spectrometry as a detection method in nucleic-acid analysis. It includes instruments, setups and procedures where ionised nucleic-acid fragments are measured to identify sequence, composition or modifications.
[C12Q 2565/628] being a surface plasmon resonance spectrometer -- C12Q 2565/628 designates a surface plasmon resonance spectrometer, an optical instrument that monitors real-time binding events of nucleic acids by measuring changes in refractive index at a metal surface, enabling label-free detection and kinetic analysis of molecular interactions.
[C12Q 2565/629] being a microfluidic device -- This CPC subclass falls under chemistry-biochemistry and includes inventions that employ a microfluidic device to carry out nucleic-acid analysis, where detection is performed on-chip. It covers lab-on-a-chip platforms that manipulate tiny fluid volumes for enzyme-based or nucleic-acid-based assays, enabling rapid, integrated measurement or testing of biological samples.
[C12Q 2565/631] being a biochannel or pore -- C12Q 2565/631 covers nucleic-acid analysis that employs a biological channel or pore as the detection element. It includes methods and devices where a bio-channel or pore functions as a sensor to identify or quantify nucleic acids, often by measuring ionic or molecular transport through the pore.
[C12Q 2565/632] being a surface enhanced, e.g. resonance, Raman spectrometer -- C12Q 2565/632 classifies surface-enhanced Raman spectrometers, including variants such as surface-enhanced resonance Raman devices, that are employed to detect nucleic acids. The category falls under measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, specifically covering nucleic-acid analysis defined by the mode or means of detection.
[C12Q 2565/633] NMR -- C12Q 2565/633 covers nucleic-acid analysis methods that employ nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) as the detection technique. It falls under chemistry and biochemistry categories for measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, specifically addressing analytical procedures where the mode of detection is based on NMR spectroscopy.
[C12Q 2565/634] being an acoustic wave sensor -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2565/634 covers acoustic-wave sensors employed for nucleic-acid analysis, where detection is achieved by measuring changes in acoustic wave properties caused by DNA or RNA binding. It includes devices such as quartz-crystal microbalances, surface-acoustic-wave, and bulk-acoustic-wave biosensors used to monitor nucleic-acid interactions.
[C12Q 2600/00] Oligonucleotides characterized by their use -- C12Q 2600/00 covers oligonucleotides that are defined primarily by the purpose they serve, such as primers for amplification, probes for detection, antisense or RNA-i agents, or other therapeutic or diagnostic uses, rather than by their specific sequence or chemical modifications.
[C12Q 2600/106] Pharmacogenomics, i.e. genetic variability in individual responses to drugs and drug metabolism -- C12Q 2600/106 relates to oligonucleotides employed in pharmacogenomics, focusing on the influence of genetic variations on how individuals respond to drugs and process them. The classification falls under chemistry, biochemistry, and measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2600/112] Disease subtyping, staging or classification -- This CPC subclass groups inventions that use oligonucleotides to distinguish, stage or otherwise classify diseases, enabling molecular subtyping based on genetic or RNA markers. It covers diagnostic or prognostic methods that assign patients to disease categories or stages through nucleic-acid-based analysis.
[C12Q 2600/118] Prognosis of disease development -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2600/118 covers the use of oligonucleotides to predict how a disease will progress in a patient. It includes methods that assess biomarkers, gene expression patterns or nucleic-acid targets to provide a prognosis of disease development, severity or outcome, often for personalized medicine or therapeutic decision-making.
[C12Q 2600/124] Animal traits, i.e. production traits, including athletic performance or the like -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2600/124 relates to the identification, selection or modification of animal traits, especially production characteristics such as growth, milk yield or athletic performance, using nucleic-acid-based methods. It falls under the broader group of measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms, specifically oligonucleotides characterized by their use.
[C12Q 2600/13] Plant traits -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2600/13 relates to oligonucleotides that are defined by the plant traits they confer, such as disease resistance, improved yield, stress tolerance or quality attributes, and to their use in plant breeding, genetic modification or testing of those traits. It falls within the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry and measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2600/136] Screening for pharmacological compounds -- C12Q 2600/136 relates to the use of oligonucleotides as tools in assays that screen substances for pharmacological activity. The classification covers methods that employ nucleic-acid probes or primers to detect, quantify or evaluate the effect of candidate compounds on biological targets, often by measuring binding, inhibition or modulation of enzymes or receptors.
[C12Q 2600/142] Toxicological screening, e.g. expression profiles which identify toxicity -- C12Q 2600/142 covers methods that use oligonucleotides to perform toxicological screening, such as gene-expression profiling, to identify compounds or conditions that cause toxicity. The classification includes assays that monitor changes in nucleic-acid or protein expression patterns as indicators of toxic effects, enabling early detection of harmful substances in biological systems.
[C12Q 2600/148] Screening for cosmetic compounds -- C12Q 2600/148 covers methods that employ oligonucleotides to screen or evaluate candidate compounds for cosmetic purposes, such as agents affecting skin, hair or nails. It includes assay formats that detect biological activity, safety or efficacy of these compounds using nucleic-acid-based probes or primers.
[C12Q 2600/154] Methylation markers -- C12Q2600/154 covers oligonucleotide probes specifically designed as methylation markers for detecting DNA methylation patterns. These nucleic-acid sequences are used in biochemical assays that measure epigenetic modifications, enabling diagnostics, research, or monitoring of gene regulation. The class falls under chemistry, biochemistry and testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2600/156] Polymorphic or mutational markers -- C12Q 2600/156 defines oligonucleotides employed as genetic markers that detect natural polymorphisms or induced mutations, such as SNPs, microsatellites or other sequence variants. The subclass covers reagents and methods for genotyping, disease-related mutation screening, and related analytical applications using enzyme-based or nucleic-acid testing processes.
[C12Q 2600/158] Expression markers -- This subclass covers oligonucleotides employed as expression markers to detect or quantify the activity of specific genes, proteins or cellular processes. The molecules may be used as probes, primers, reporters or other detection elements that indicate transcriptional or translational levels in a sample. It is part of the CPC group for measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2600/16] Primer sets for multiplex assays -- The classification covers primer sets designed for simultaneous amplification of multiple target sequences in a single reaction, enabling multiplex PCR or other multiplex assay formats. It includes compositions of primers that are tailored to work together without cross-interference, facilitating detection or quantification of several nucleic acid targets in one assay.
[C12Q 2600/166] Oligonucleotides used as internal standards, controls or normalisation probes -- The CPC subclass C12Q 2600/166 covers oligonucleotides that are deliberately added to a test as internal standards, controls or normalisation probes. These nucleic-acid sequences serve to calibrate assay performance, verify accuracy and compensate for variability in measurements that involve enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2600/172] Haplotypes -- C12Q 2600/172 covers inventions that define or exploit specific haplotypes-sets of linked genetic variants-within oligonucleotide-based technologies. It applies to methods, compositions, or diagnostic tools that use haplotype information for detecting, treating, or studying biological samples, situated in the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy that addresses measuring or testing processes involving enzymes, nucleic acids or microorganisms.
[C12Q 2600/178] miRNA, siRNA or ncRNA -- This CPC subgroup includes oligonucleotide agents such as micro-RNA, small interfering RNA and other non-coding RNAs that are claimed for their specific functional use, for example in diagnostics, therapeutics, or as molecular probes. It covers the composition, preparation, and applications of these RNA molecules for regulating gene expression or detecting nucleic-acid targets.
[C12Q 3/00] Condition responsive control processes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes involving enzymes or microorganisms in which a process parameter is measured and that or another process parameter is varied in response to such measurement.
[C12R] INDEXING SCHEME ASSOCIATED WITH SUBCLASSES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This subclass is intended to enable a comprehensive search of subject matter related to microorganisms by combination of classification symbols of this subclass with classification symbols from other subgroups. Therefore, this subclass covers aspects of microorganisms that might also be entirely or partially covered elsewhere. This subclass is for secondary classification, i.e. supplementary classification of subject matter already classified as such in other classification places( C12C - C12Q ).  The classification symbols of this subclass are not listed first when assigned to patent documents.  This group is obligatory in combination with C12N1/145 , C12N1/165 , C12N1/185 or C12N1/205 , and optional in combination with C12N1/105 or C12N1/125 .  **Glossary:** - Microorganism: includes bacteria and other generally unicellular organisms with dimensions beneath the limits of vision which can be propagated and manipulated in a laboratory, including fungi (including yeasts), microalgae and protozoa
[C12R 2001/00] Microorganisms -- C12R 2001/00 designates patents concerning microorganisms in general, encompassing bacteria, fungi, viruses and other microscopic life forms. It includes inventions about discovering, isolating, characterizing, cultivating or applying these organisms in biotechnological, medical, agricultural or industrial processes.
[C12R 2001/01] Bacteria or Actinomycetales -- C12R 2001/01 designates inventions that involve bacteria or Actinomycetales, covering their isolation, cultivation, genetic modification, and use in biotechnological processes such as production of enzymes, antibiotics, or other metabolites. The subclass groups patents dealing with microbial strains, methods for their manipulation, and applications in health, agriculture, or industrial biotechnology.
[C12R 2001/02] Acetobacter -- C12R 2001/02 designates the genus Acetobacter within the microorganism subclass of the CPC, covering acetic-acid-producing bacteria used in industrial fermentation, vinegar production, and bioconversion processes, and encompassing methods of their cultivation, genetic manipulation, and applications.
[C12R 2001/025] Achromobacter -- C12R 2001/025 designates the genus Achromobacter within the CPC section on microorganisms. It covers inventions involving these Gram-negative bacteria, such as methods of isolation, genetic manipulation, or biotechnological applications. The classification is used for patents relating to bioprocesses, enzymes, or bioremediation using Achromobacter species.
[C12R 2001/03] Actinomadura -- The CPC code C12R 2001/03 designates the genus Actinomadura, a filamentous actinobacterium known for producing antibiotics and other bioactive metabolites. It falls within section C (Chemistry), subclass 12 (Biochemistry), group R (Microorganisms) of the EPO/USPTO cooperative patent classification.
[C12R 2001/04] Actinomyces -- C12R 2001/04 classifies inventions dealing with the genus Actinomyces, a group of filamentous bacteria used in biotechnological and medical applications. The code covers methods of cultivation, genetic manipulation, preparation of biomass or extracts, and the use of Actinomyces-derived compounds in pharmaceuticals, diagnostics or industrial processes.

The entry groups this microorganism within the biotechnology and microbiology subclass of the CPC system.

[C12Y] ENZYMES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Enzymes of which the EC number is known.  In this subclass, each enzyme is classified according to their EC number of the "Enzyme Nomenclature" (as valid on 1 January 2013) recommended by the Nomenclature Committee of the International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology.The EC number appears in the subgroups within parentheses and is reflected in the classification symbol: the EC number with the notation a.bb.cc.ddd is rendered into a CPC symbol C12Y ABB/CCDDD (with no trailing zeroes at the end) where:  (*) not all numbers are used  Examples: Alcohol dehydrogenase: EC 1.1.1.1.-------- C12Y101/01001  Togavirin: EC 3.4.21.90------ C12Y304/2109  Hepsin: EC 3.4.21.106----- C12Y304/21106  Cobaltochelatase EC 6.6.1.2---------- C12Y606/01002  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Compositions containing enzymes and use of the compositions and compounds for preservation of bodies of humans or animals or parts thereof -> A01N1/00 - Compositions containing enzymes and use of the compositions and compounds for preservation of plants or parts thereof -> A01N3/00 - Biocides, pest repellents or attractants or plant growth regulators containing enzymes -> A01N63/00 - Bakery products which contain enzymes -> A21D - Foods or foodstuffs containing or treated with enzymes -> A23 - Preparations for medical dental or toilet purposes containing enzymes -> A61K - Medical preparations containing nucleic acids encoding enzymes -> A61K31/7088 - Medical preparations containing genetic material encoding enzymes which is inserted into cells of the living body to treat genetic diseases; Gene therapy -> A61K48/00 - Methods or apparatus for disinfecting or sterilising contact lenses in combination with enzymes -> A61L12/082 - Bandages, dressings or absorbent pads for physiological fluids containing enzymes -> A61L15/38 - Material for protheses subjected to enzyme treatment prior to implantation -> A61L27/3683 - Protease inhibitors -> C07K14/81 - Antibodies against enzymes -> C07K16/40 - Detergent compositions containing enzymes -> C11D3/386 - Preparation of malt -> C12C1/00 - Apparatus for enzymology -> C12M - Enzymes -> C12N9/00 - Carrier-bound or immobilised enzymes -> C12N11/00 - Treatment of enzymes with electrical or wave energy -> C12N13/00 - Nucleic acids encoding (part of) enzymes -> C12N15/00 - Antisense nucleotides against enzymes -> C12N15/113 - Preparation of compounds using enzymes -> C12P - Measuring or testing processes involving enzymes; Compositions therefore (including complete kits, test papers etc); Preparing such compositions -> C12Q1/00 - Immunoassays for enzymes -> G01N33/573
[C12Y 101/00] Oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors (1.1) -- The CPC subclass C12Y 101/00 falls under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Enzymes and designates oxidoreductases that act on a CH-OH group of the donor molecule, corresponding to subclass 1.1. It covers patents involving enzymes catalyzing oxidation-reduction of alcohol-derived substrates.
[C12Y 101/01] with NAD+ or NADP+ as acceptor (1.1.1) -- C12Y 101/01 designates oxidoreductase enzymes that catalyze the oxidation of a CH-OH group with NAD⁺ or NADP⁺ serving as the electron-acceptor. The subclass is used for patent documents covering biocatalytic processes, synthesis methods or compositions that employ these NAD-dependent oxidoreductases.
[C12Y 101/01001] Alcohol dehydrogenase (1.1.1.1) -- The CPC entry C12Y 101/01001 designates alcohol dehydrogenase, an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the oxidation of alcohols by acting on CH-OH groups of donors. It falls within the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → enzymes → oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors (1.1). This code is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify patents involving this specific enzyme.
[C12Y 101/01002] Alcohol dehydrogenase (NADP+) (1.1.1.2), i.e. aldehyde reductase
[C12Y 101/01003] Homoserine dehydrogenase (1.1.1.3) -- The entry C12Y 101/01003 designates the enzyme homoserine dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.3), an oxidoreductase that acts on the CH-OH group of donors. It catalyzes the NAD(P)-dependent reduction of L-aspartate-β-semialdehyde to homoserine, a key step in the aspartate-derived amino-acid biosynthetic pathway.
[C12Y 101/01004] R,R-butanediol dehydrogenase (1.1.1.4) -- C12Y 101/01004 covers the enzyme R,R-butanediol dehydrogenase, an oxidoreductase (EC 1.1.1.4) that catalyzes the NAD-dependent oxidation of the R,R-enantiomer of butanediol to the corresponding ketone. It belongs to the subclass of enzymes acting on the CH-OH group of donors within the chemistry-biochemistry-enzymes hierarchy.
[C12Y 101/01005] Acetoin dehydrogenase (1.1.1.5) -- Acetoin dehydrogenase is an oxidoreductase enzyme that catalyzes the oxidation of acetoin, converting it into diacetyl while reducing an electron acceptor. It belongs to the subclass acting on CH-OH groups of donors, specifically the 1.1.1.5 subgroup, and is used in biochemical pathways for flavor compound production and microbial energy metabolism.
[C12Y 101/01006] Glycerol dehydrogenase (1.1.1.6) -- Glycerol dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.6) is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the NAD⁺-dependent oxidation of glycerol to dihydroxyacetone, converting the CH-OH group of glycerol into a carbonyl. It is classified in the CPC hierarchy under Chemistry → Biochemistry → Enzymes → Oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors.
[C12Y 101/01007] Propanediol-phosphate dehydrogenase (1.1.1.7) -- Propanediol-phosphate dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.7) is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the NAD-dependent oxidation of 1,2-propanediol-phosphate to hydroxy-acetone-phosphate, playing a role in carbohydrate metabolism. It is classified in the CPC under chemistry → biochemistry → enzymes → oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors, code C12Y 101/01007.
[C12Y 101/01008] Glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (NAD+) (1.1.1.8) -- The CPC subclass C12Y 101/01008 covers enzymes that catalyze the NAD⁺-dependent oxidation of glycerol-3-phosphate to dihydroxyacetone phosphate. It belongs to the oxidoreductases acting on a CH-OH group of donors, specifically glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (NAD+).
[C12Y 101/01009] D-Xylulose reductase (1.1.1.9), i.e. xylitol dehydrogenase -- The CPC entry C12Y 101/01009 covers the enzyme D-xylulose reductase (EC 1.1.1.9), also known as xylitol dehydrogenase, which catalyzes the NAD(P)H-dependent reduction of D-xylulose to xylitol in pentose-phosphate metabolism and is used in biotechnological production of the sweetener xylitol. It falls under the oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors within the broader chemistry, biochemistry, and enzymes hierarchy.
[C12Y 101/0101] L-Xylulose reductase (1.1.1.10) -- The CPC entry C12Y 101/0101 covers the enzyme L-xylulose reductase, classified under oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors. This enzyme catalyzes the NAD(P)H-dependent reduction of L-xylulose to xylitol and participates in uronic acid metabolism. It is relevant for biocatalysis, diagnostic assays, and therapeutic protein engineering.
[C12Y 101/01011] D-Arabinitol 4-dehydrogenase (1.1.1.11) -- The enzyme D-arabinitol 4-dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.11) catalyzes the NAD(P)+-dependent oxidation of the CH-OH group of D-arabinitol to produce D-xylulose and NAD(P)H. It is indexed in the Cooperative Patent Classification under chemistry → biochemistry → enzymes → oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors, with entry code C12Y 101/01011.
[C12Y 101/01012] L-Arabinitol 4-dehydrogenase (1.1.1.12) -- L-Arabinitol 4-dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.12) is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the oxidation of L-arabinitol to L-xylulose using NAD⁺ as a cofactor. In the Cooperative Patent Classification it appears under chemistry → biochemistry → enzymes → oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors, with code C12Y 101/01012.
[C12Y 101/01013] L-Arabinitol 2-dehydrogenase (1.1.1.13) -- C12Y 101/01013 designates patents concerning the enzyme L-arabinitol 2-dehydrogenase, classified under oxidoreductases that act on a CH-OH group of donors (EC 1.1.1.13). The code covers methods, compositions and applications involving this NAD(P)+-dependent catalyst that converts L-arabinitol to its corresponding keto-sugar. It falls within the chemistry-biochemistry-enzymes hierarchy of the CPC system.
[C12Y 101/01014] L-Iditol 2-dehydrogenase (1.1.1.14), i.e. sorbitol-dehydrogenase -- The CPC entry C12Y 101/01014 covers the enzyme L-Iditol 2-dehydrogenase, commonly called sorbitol dehydrogenase, which catalyzes the oxidation of sorbitol to fructose and belongs to the EC 1.1.1.14 family of oxidoreductases acting on CH-OH donors. It is situated in the hierarchy under Chemistry, Biochemistry, Enzymes and specifically oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors.
[C12Y 101/01015] D-iditol 2-dehydrogenase (1.1.1.15) -- The CPC entry C12Y 101/01015 covers inventions concerning the enzyme D-iditol 2-dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.15), an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the NAD(P)+-dependent oxidation of D-iditol to D-sorbose. It includes methods for producing, purifying, immobilizing or applying the enzyme and compositions that contain it.
[C12Y 101/01016] Galactitol 2-dehydrogenase (1.1.1.16) -- The CPC subclass C12Y 101/01016 covers the enzyme galactitol-2-dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.16), which catalyzes the NAD⁺-dependent oxidation of galactitol to galactonate. It falls under oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors within the chemistry-biochemistry-enzymes hierarchy.
[C12Y 101/01017] Mannitol-1-phosphate 5-dehydrogenase (1.1.1.17) -- Mannitol-1-phosphate 5-dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.17) is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the NAD⁺-dependent oxidation of D-mannitol-1-phosphate to D-fructose-6-phosphate, generating NADH and a proton. It falls under the subclass of enzymes acting on the CH-OH group of donors.
[C12Y 101/01018] Inositol 2-dehydrogenase (1.1.1.18) -- The CPC subclass C12Y 101/01018 groups inventions concerning the enzyme inositol 2-dehydrogenase (EC 1.1.1.18), an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the oxidation of the CH-OH group of myo-inositol to produce scyllo-inosose using NAD⁺ or NADP⁺ as the electron acceptor. The entry covers methods for producing, purifying, engineering, or applying the enzyme, as well as compositions that contain it for biotechnological or pharmaceutical purposes.
[C12Y 101/01019] Glucuronate reductase (1.1.1.19) -- The CPC entry C12Y 101/01019 classifies glucuronate reductase, an enzyme designated EC 1.1.1.19 that catalyzes the reduction of D-glucuronic acid to L-iditol using NAD(P)H as a cofactor. It falls under oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors within the broader chemistry-biochemistry-enzymes hierarchy.
[C12Y 101/0102] Glucuronolactone reductase (1.1.1.20) -- Glucuronolactone reductase (EC 1.1.1.20) is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the reduction of glucuronolactone, acting on the CH-OH group of the donor. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under Chemistry, Biochemistry, Enzymes and specifically the subclass of oxidoreductases on CH-OH groups, coded C12Y 101/0102.
[C12Y 101/01021] Aldehyde reductase (1.1.1.21), i.e. aldose-reductase -- C12Y 101/01021 designates aldehyde reductases, enzymes classified as oxidoreductases that act on the CH-OH group of donors, specifically the EC 1.1.1.21 aldose-reductase. These biocatalysts catalyze the NAD(P)H-dependent reduction of aldehydes to their corresponding alcohols, playing key roles in carbohydrate metabolism and industrial biotransformations.
[C12Y 101/01022] UDP-glucose 6-dehydrogenase (1.1.1.22) -- Code C12Y 101/01022 designates UDP-glucose 6-dehydrogenase, an oxidoreductase (EC 1.1.1.22) that oxidizes the CH-OH group of UDP-glucose, converting it to UDP-glucuronic acid in carbohydrate biosynthesis. It falls under enzymes, specifically oxidoreductases acting on CH-OH donors, within the chemistry-biochemistry hierarchy.
[C12Y 101/01023] Histidinol dehydrogenase (1.1.1.23) -- The CPC entry C12Y 101/01023 designates the enzyme histidinol dehydrogenase, which catalyzes the NAD⁺-dependent oxidation of L-histidinol to L-histidine, completing the final step of histidine biosynthesis. It falls under oxidoreductases acting on the CH-OH group of donors (class 1.1).

It falls under the Chemistry → Biochemistry → Enzymes hierarchy, specifically the oxidoreductases acting on CH-CH groups. The code is used in patent documents to tag technologies related to the production, use, or modification of this enzyme.
[C12Y 103/03009] Secologanin synthase (1.3.3.9) -- Secologanin synthase (EC 1.3.3.9) is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the oxidative cleavage of the CH-CH bond in loganin to produce secologanin, a key intermediate in monoterpene indole alkaloid biosynthesis. The CPC entry C12Y 103/03009 classifies inventions involving this enzyme, its variants, production methods and applications in pharmaceutical or agricultural biotechnology.

Situated under enzymes acting on CH-CH groups of donors (EC 1.3), this classification is used for patents describing the enzyme's composition, production, or applications in biochemical processes.

It falls under oxidoreductases acting on CH-CH groups of donors (EC 1.3) within the broader categories of enzymes, biochemistry and chemistry.

[C12Y 103/05004] Fumarate reductase (menaquinone) (1.3.5.4) -- Fumarate reductase (menaquinone) is an oxidoreductase enzyme that reduces fumarate to succinate using menaquinone as the electron acceptor, belonging to EC 1.3.5.4. It transfers electrons from the CH-CH group of fumarate to the quinone pool in anaerobic respiration, facilitating energy generation in many bacteria. [C12Y 104/00] Oxidoreductases acting on the CH-NH2 group of donors (1.4) -- The CPC subclass C12Y 104/00 covers enzymes that catalyze oxidation-reduction reactions in which the donor possesses a carbon-nitrogen single bond bearing an amine group. It includes oxidoreductases that act specifically on the CH-NH₂ functional group, encompassing deamination, oxidative deamination and related biotransformations.

It resides in the oxidoreductases acting on CH-NH2 donors branch of the chemistry-biochemistry-enzymes hierarchy.

[C12Y 106/01] with NAD+ or NADP+ as acceptor (1.6.1) -- The CPC subclass C12Y 106/01 covers oxidoreductase enzymes that use NAD⁺ or NADP⁺ as the electron-acceptor in reactions involving NADH or NADPH. [C12Y 106/01001] NAD(P)+ transhydrogenase (B-specific) (1.6.1.1) -- NAD(P)+ transhydrogenase (B-specific) is an oxidoreductase that catalyses the reversible transfer of a hydride ion between NADH and NADP+, converting NADH to NAD+ while reducing NADP+ to NADPH. It belongs to the subclass of enzymes acting on NADH or NADPH as donors, facilitating cellular redox balance.
[C12Y 106/01002] NAD(P)+ Transhydrogenase (AB-specific) (1.6.1.2) -- This CPC entry C12Y 106/01002 classifies NAD(P)+ transhydrogenases that are AB-specific, enzymes that catalyze the reversible transfer of a hydride ion between NADH and NADP+. They belong to oxidoreductases acting on NADH or NADPH, facilitating redox balance in cellular metabolism.
[C12Y 106/02] with a heme protein as acceptor (1.6.2) -- C12Y 106/02 covers oxidoreductase reactions where NADH or NADPH transfer electrons to a heme protein that serves as the acceptor. It is applied to patents describing enzymatic oxidation or reduction processes involving heme-containing proteins such as cytochromes.

It falls under the hierarchy Chemistry → Biochemistry → Enzymes → Oxidoreductases acting on NADH or NADPH (1.6).

It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, enzymes, specifically oxidoreductases acting on NADH or NADPH. [C12Y 106/02006] Leghemoglobin reductase (1.6.2.6) -- Leghemoglobin reductase is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the NADH- or NADPH-dependent reduction of leghemoglobin, a plant hemoprotein involved in oxygen transport within nitrogen-fixing root nodules. It belongs to the subclass of enzymes acting on NADH or NADPH (C12Y 106/02006) and plays a key role in maintaining leghemoglobin in its functional, reduced state.
[C12Y 106/03] with oxygen as acceptor (1.6.3) -- This CPC subclass groups enzymes that catalyse redox reactions in which NADH or NADPH donate electrons and molecular oxygen acts as the electron acceptor, producing oxidised substrates and water or hydroxylated products. It includes oxidases, monooxygenases and related biocatalysts that use O₂ as the final acceptor.

It falls under the hierarchy Chemistry → Biochemistry → Enzymes → Oxidoreductases acting on NADH or NADPH.

It covers biocatalytic processes where NADH/NADPH-dependent oxidoreductases transfer electrons to quinone-like acceptors, including assays and production methods.

The enzyme belongs to the biochemical subclass of enzymes that act on sulfur groups, supporting redox balance and parasite survival.

[C12Y 110/99003] Violaxanthin de-epoxidase (1.10.99.3) -- C12Y 110/99003 designates the enzyme violaxanthin de-epoxidase, an oxidoreductase that uses diphenols or related compounds as donors. It falls under the CPC hierarchy Chemistry - Biochemistry - Enzymes - Oxidoreductases acting on diphenols and related substances as donors (1.10). The code is used by the EPO and USPTO to classify patents involving this enzyme.
[C12Y 111/00] Oxidoreductases acting on a peroxide as acceptor (1.11) -- This CPC subclass groups enzymes that catalyze oxidation-reduction reactions where a peroxide serves as the electron acceptor, i.e., oxidoreductases acting on a peroxide as acceptor. It lies under the hierarchy chemistry-biochemistry-enzymes and is used to classify patents describing such catalytic processes.
[C12Y 111/01] Peroxidases (1.11.1) -- C12Y 111/01 covers peroxidase enzymes that catalyze oxidation-reduction reactions using peroxides as electron acceptors. These enzymes break down hydrogen peroxide or organic peroxides, serving in cellular defense, biotechnology and industrial processes.
[C12Y 111/01001] NADH peroxidase (1.11.1.1) -- NADH peroxidase is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the reduction of hydrogen-peroxide to water using NADH as the electron donor, placing it in the class of enzymes acting on a peroxide as acceptor (1.11). It is indexed under CPC code C12Y 111/01001 within the chemistry-biochemistry-enzymes hierarchy.
[C12Y 111/01002] NADPH peroxidase (1.11.1.2) -- The CPC subclass C12Y 111/01002 covers NADPH peroxidase enzymes, which are oxidoreductases that use NADPH to reduce hydrogen peroxide, falling under the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry, enzymes, and oxidoreductases acting on a peroxide as acceptor. These enzymes catalyze the conversion of NADPH and peroxide to NADP+ and water, playing a role in cellular oxidative stress defense.
[C12Y 111/01003] Fatty-acid peroxidase (1.11.1.3) -- C12Y 111/01003 classifies fatty-acid peroxidases, enzymes that belong to the oxidoreductase family acting on peroxides as acceptors (CPC 1.11). These biocatalysts catalyze the reduction of peroxide substrates using fatty-acid donors, and are indexed under chemistry → biochemistry → enzymes in the CPC system.
[C12Y 111/01005] Cytochrome-c peroxidase (1.11.1.5) -- Cytochrome-c peroxidase is an oxidoreductase enzyme that catalyzes the reduction of hydrogen peroxide using cytochrome-c as an electron donor, fitting within CPC subgroup 1.11.1.5 for enzymes acting on peroxides as acceptors. It belongs to the broader categories of chemistry, biochemistry and enzymes, and is indexed under code C12Y 111/01005 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[C12Y 111/01006] Catalase (1.11.1.6) -- The CPC entry C12Y 111/01006 classifies patents concerning the enzyme catalase, a biologically active protein that accelerates the breakdown of hydrogen peroxide into water and oxygen. It falls under oxidoreductases acting on a peroxide as acceptor, a subgroup of enzymes in biochemistry.
[C12Y 111/01007] Peroxidase (1.11.1.7), i.e. horseradish-peroxidase -- The CPC subclass C12Y 111/01007 groups patents concerning peroxidase enzymes, specifically those classified under oxidoreductases that use peroxide as an electron acceptor, such as horseradish peroxidase. It falls under chemistry - biochemistry - enzymes - oxidoreductases acting on a peroxide as acceptor.
[C12Y 111/01008] Iodide peroxidase (1.11.1.8) -- Iodide peroxidase is an enzyme that catalyzes the oxidation of iodide using peroxide as the electron acceptor, fitting within the oxidoreductases acting on a peroxide as acceptor subclass (1.11). It is indexed under the Cooperative Patent Classification code C12Y 111/01008, linking the chemistry hierarchy of biochemistry, enzymes, and specifically oxidoreductases.
[C12Y 111/01009] Glutathione peroxidase (1.11.1.9) -- Glutathione peroxidase is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the reduction of hydrogen peroxide or organic peroxides by using reduced glutathione as electron donor, protecting cells from oxidative damage. It belongs to the subclass of enzymes that act on a peroxide as acceptor (CPC 1.11) and is classified under code C12Y 111/01009.
[C12Y 111/0101] Chloride peroxidase (1.11.1.10) -- Chloride peroxidase is an oxidoreductase enzyme that uses a peroxide as the electron acceptor to catalyze the oxidation of chloride, forming hypochlorous acid or related compounds. It falls under the CPC hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → enzymes → oxidoreductases acting on a peroxide as acceptor (1.11), specifically entry C12Y 111/0101 for chloride peroxidase (1.11.1.10).

It is classified under oxidoreductases acting on peroxides as acceptors (CPC 1.11) within the chemistry-biochemistry-enzymes hierarchy.
[C12Y 111/01013] Manganese peroxidase (1.11.1.13) -- Manganese peroxidase is an oxidoreductase enzyme (C12Y 111/01013) that uses hydrogen peroxide to oxidize manganese ions, facilitating lignin breakdown and other oxidative processes. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under chemistry, biochemistry, enzymes, specifically oxidoreductases acting on a peroxide as acceptor (1.11).
[C12Y 111/01014] Lignin peroxidase (1.11.1.14) -- Lignin peroxidase is an oxidoreductase enzyme that uses hydrogen peroxide to oxidize lignin polymers, breaking down complex plant cell-wall components into smaller phenolic compounds. Classified under CPC code C12Y 111/01014, it falls within the hierarchy of Chemistry → Biochemistry → Enzymes → Oxidoreductases acting on a peroxide as acceptor (1.11). This enzyme is important in biotechnological processes for lignocellulosic waste treatment and biofuel production.
[C12Y 111/01015] Peroxiredoxin (1.11.1.15) -- Peroxiredoxins are a family of antioxidant enzymes that reduce peroxides, protecting cells from oxidative damage. They belong to oxidoreductases acting on peroxides as acceptors, classified under CPC C12Y111/01015, and participate in cellular redox regulation and signaling.
[C12Y 111/01016] Versatile peroxidase (1.11.1.16) -- The C12Y 111/01016 subclass defines versatile peroxidase enzymes that catalyze the oxidation of a range of organic substrates by using hydrogen peroxide as the electron acceptor. These oxidoreductases are notable for their broad substrate specificity and are applied in biotechnological processes such as lignin breakdown, wastewater treatment and industrial bleaching.

[C12Y 111/01018] Bromide peroxidase (1.11.1.18) -- The CPC code C12Y 111/01018 designates bromide peroxidase, an oxidoreductase that uses a peroxide as the electron acceptor to catalyse the oxidation of bromide ions, typically producing hypobromous acid. [C12Y 111/01019] Dye decolorizing peroxidase (1.11.1.19) -- The CPC entry C12Y 111/01019 designates dye-decolorizing peroxidase, an oxidoreductase enzyme that uses peroxide as the electron acceptor to break down colored compounds. [C12Y 111/0102] Prostamide/prostaglandin F2-alpha synthase (1.11.1.20) -- C12Y 111/0102 identifies enzymes that synthesize prostamide or prostaglandin F2-alpha, belonging to oxidoreductases that act on a peroxide as acceptor (class 1.11). These enzymes catalyze the reduction of hydroperoxy intermediates to form the respective prostanoid, a specific activity within the broader group of oxidoreductases.
[C12Y 111/01021] Catalase-peroxidase (1.11.1.21) -- C12Y 111/01021 designates the enzyme catalase-peroxidase, a type of oxidoreductase that uses peroxide as the electron acceptor to both decompose hydrogen peroxide and oxidize organic substrates. These bifunctional enzymes are found in many bacteria and are exploited for bioremediation, biosensor development, and oxidative stress studies.

[C12Y 112/00] Oxidoreductases acting on hydrogen as donor (1.12) -- C12Y 112/00 designates oxidoreductase enzymes that employ hydrogen as the electron donor. These biocatalysts catalyze redox reactions in which hydrogen is oxidized, and are relevant to metabolic pathways, industrial biotransformations, and bioenergy processes.
[C12Y 112/01] with NAD+ or NADP+ as acceptor (1.12.1) -- C12Y 112/01 classifies inventions involving oxidoreductase enzymes that use hydrogen donors and specifically employ NAD⁺ or NADP⁺ as the electron-acceptor co-factor. The subclass falls under chemistry, biochemistry and enzymes, focusing on oxidoreductases acting on hydrogen (1.12) with the NAD/NADP acceptor variant (1.12.1).

It addresses biocatalysts that reversibly convert hydrogen gas to protons and electrons and is applied to patents on biohydrogen production, fuel-cell enzymes and related biotechnological processes.
[C12Y 112/01003] Hydrogen dehydrogenase (NADP+) (1.12.1.3) -- The CPC entry C12Y 112/01003 covers hydrogen dehydrogenases that use NADP⁺ as the electron-acceptor, catalyzing the oxidation of molecular hydrogen. It falls under oxidoreductases acting on hydrogen as donor (class 1.12) within the biochemistry branch of chemistry. This classification identifies enzymes that facilitate the reversible conversion of H₂ to protons while reducing NADP⁺ to NADPH.
[C12Y 112/01004] Hydrogenase (NAD+, ferredoxin)(1.12.1.4) -- C12Y112/01004 classifies hydrogenases that transfer electrons from molecular hydrogen to NAD+ or ferredoxin, enzymes that catalyze the reversible oxidation of H₂. These oxidoreductases belong to the subgroup of hydrogen-activating enzymes under oxidoreductases acting on hydrogen as donor (1.12). The entry is used to index patents concerning biocatalysts, biofuel cells and related biotechnological applications.

[C12Y 112/99006] Hydrogenase (acceptor) (1.12.99.6) -- C12Y 112/99006 classifies hydrogenases that use hydrogen as the electron donor and transfer electrons to a non-standard acceptor, covering enzymes that catalyse the oxidation of hydrogen with various acceptor molecules. The entry falls within the oxidoreductases acting on hydrogen as donor subgroup of enzyme classifications.
[C12Y 113/00] Oxidoreductases acting on single donors with incorporation of molecular oxygen (oxygenases) (1.13) -- This CPC subclass C12Y 113/00 covers enzymes that oxidize a single donor while incorporating molecular oxygen, i.e., oxygenases such as monooxygenases and dioxygenases. It falls under the hierarchy Chemistry - Biochemistry - Enzymes and includes oxidoreductases that catalyze oxygen-addition reactions in biological and industrial processes.
[C12Y 113/11] with incorporation of two atoms of oxygen (1.13.11) -- C12Y 113/11 identifies enzymes that act as oxidoreductases on a single donor and incorporate both atoms of molecular oxygen into the substrate, classifying them as dioxygenases. This subcategory falls under the broader group of oxygenases that incorporate oxygen in biochemical reactions.
[C12Y 113/11001] Catechol 1,2-dioxygenase (1.13.11.1) -- C12Y 113/11001 classifies catechol 1,2-dioxygenase, an enzyme that uses molecular oxygen to cleave catechol at the ortho position, yielding cis,cis-muconic acid. It falls under oxidoreductases acting on single donors with incorporation of oxygen (EC 1.13.11.1) within the biochemistry of enzymes.
[C12Y 113/11002] Catechol 2,3-dioxygenase (1.13.11.2) -- The CPC entry C12Y 113/11002 designates catechol 2,3-dioxygenase, an oxidoreductase that incorporates molecular oxygen into catechol, cleaving the aromatic ring to form cis,cis-muconic semialdehyde. It falls under the subclass of enzymes acting on single donors with incorporation of molecular oxygen (oxygenases).
[C12Y 113/11003] Protocatechuate 3,4-dioxygenase (1.13.11.3) -- The entry C12Y 113/11003 designates protocatechuate 3,4-dioxygenase, an oxidoreductase that incorporates molecular oxygen into a single donor (subclass 1.13.11.3). It falls under CPC group 1.13.11.4 for oxygenases acting on single donors with incorporation of O₂.
[C12Y 113/11005] Homogentisate 1,2-dioxygenase (1.13.11.5) -- The entry C12Y 113/11005 designates the enzyme homogentisate 1,2-dioxygenase, classified under oxidoreductases that incorporate molecular oxygen (CPC 1.13). This oxygenase catalyzes the oxidative ring-opening of homogentisate in the catabolic pathway of aromatic amino acids, converting it to maleylacetoacetate while consuming one molecule of O₂. It falls within the chemistry-biochemistry-enzymes hierarchy of the Cooperative Patent Classification.

It catalyzes the oxidative cleavage of 2,5-dihydroxypyridine, converting it into open-chain products as part of pyridine-derived catabolism.

It belongs to the subclass of oxygenases acting on single donors with incorporation of molecular oxygen (C12Y 113/11026) and is used in biochemical pathways of tryptophan catabolism.
[C12Y 113/11027] 4-Hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase (1.13.11.27) -- 4-Hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase is an oxidoreductase (oxygenase) that incorporates molecular oxygen into 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate, converting it to homogentisate in the tyrosine catabolic pathway. The enzyme requires Fe²⁺ as a cofactor and is a target for certain herbicides that inhibit plant growth. It is classified under CPC code C12Y 113/11027.

[C12Y 113/12] with incorporation of one atom of oxygen (internal monooxygenases or internal mixed function oxidases)(1.13.12) -- C12Y 113/12 classifies enzymes that incorporate a single atom of molecular oxygen into a substrate, known as internal monooxygenases or internal mixed-function oxidases. These oxidoreductases act on a single donor and use oxygen, inserting one oxygen atom while the second atom is reduced to water, and are relevant in many biosynthetic and degradative pathways.

[C12Y 113/99001] Inositol oxygenase (1.13.99.1), i.e. myo-inositol oxygenase -- C12Y 113/99001 classifies enzymes that are oxidoreductases incorporating molecular oxygen into a single donor, specifically the oxygenase that acts on myo-inositol. [C12Y 114/00] Oxidoreductases acting on paired donors, with incorporation or reduction of molecular oxygen (1.14) -- C12Y 114/00 classifies enzymes that catalyse oxidation-reduction reactions where two substrates (paired donors) are involved and molecular oxygen is either incorporated into one of the products or reduced, commonly known as oxygenases. This subclass groups biocatalysts that facilitate the addition of oxygen atoms to organic compounds or the reduction of O₂ in coupled reactions. It falls under the hierarchy of chemistry → biochemistry → enzymes.
[C12Y 114/11] with 2-oxoglutarate as one donor, and incorporation of one atom each of oxygen into both donors (1.14.11) -- This CPC subclass covers oxidoreductase enzymes that use paired donors and molecular oxygen, incorporating one oxygen atom into each donor, where one of the donors is 2-oxoglutarate. Such enzymes catalyse reactions that split O₂ and insert an oxygen atom into both the 2-oxoglutarate and the second substrate, fitting within the broader category of oxidoreductases acting on paired donors.
[C12Y 114/11001] Gamma-butyrobetaine dioxygenase (1.14.11.1) -- C12Y 114/11001 classifies inventions dealing with the enzyme gamma-butyrobetaine dioxygenase, a member of the oxidoreductases that act on paired donors with incorporation or reduction of molecular oxygen (CPC subgroup 1.14.11.1). It covers methods, compositions, and applications involving the catalytic hydroxylation of gamma-butyrobetaine in biochemical processes.
[C12Y 114/11002] Procollagen-proline dioxygenase (1.14.11.2), i.e. proline-hydroxylase -- C12Y 114/11002 classifies the enzyme prolyl-hydroxylase, also known as procollagen-proline dioxygenase, which catalyzes the hydroxylation of proline residues in procollagen using molecular oxygen and 2-oxoglutarate. It falls under the CPC subgroup for oxidoreductases acting on paired donors with incorporation or reduction of molecular oxygen, specifically the 1.14.11.2 subclass of enzymes.

This enzyme falls under the hierarchy of chemistry→biochemistry→enzymes→oxidoreductases acting on paired donors with incorporation or reduction of molecular oxygen.

It falls under oxidoreductases acting on paired donors with incorporation or reduction of molecular oxygen, subclass 1.14.11.4.

This enzyme catalyses the hydroxylation of thymine, a key step in DNA repair and pyrimidine metabolism, and falls under the enzymes hierarchy of oxidoreductases acting on paired donors.

This enzyme is essential for collagen maturation by catalyzing the 3-hydroxylation of specific proline residues.

[C12Y 114/11009] Flavanone 3-dioxygenase (1.14.11.9), i.e. naringenin-3-dioxygenase -- Flavanone 3-dioxygenase, classified under the chemistry-biochemistry-enzymes hierarchy, is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the oxygen-dependent hydroxylation of naringenin to dihydrokaempferol, a key step in flavonoid biosynthesis. The enzyme utilizes molecular oxygen, Fe²⁺, and 2-oxoglutarate as co-substrates, incorporating one atom of oxygen into the flavanone while reducing the other to water. [C12Y 114/13011] Trans-cinnamate 4-monooxygenase (1.14.13.11) -- Trans-cinnamate 4-monooxygenase (EC 1.14.13.11) is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the hydroxylation of trans-cinnamate to 4-hydroxycinnamate, using molecular oxygen and NADPH as electron donors. It belongs to the subgroup of enzymes acting on paired donors with incorporation of one atom of oxygen, classified under CPC code C12Y 114/13011.
[C12Y 114/13012] Benzoate 4-monooxygenase (1.14.13.12) -- Benzoate 4-monooxygenase is an oxidoreductase that uses NAD(P)H and molecular oxygen to hydroxylate benzoate at the 4-position, forming 4-hydroxybenzoate while reducing O₂ to water. It belongs to the enzyme subclass 1.14.13, acting on paired donors with incorporation of one oxygen atom from O₂.
[C12Y 114/13013] Calcidiol 1-monooxygenase (1.14.13.13), i.e. 25-hydroxyvitamin D-1-alpha-hydroxylase -- This CPC entry C12Y 114/13013 covers the enzyme calcidiol 1-monooxygenase, also called 25-hydroxyvitamin D-1-alpha-hydroxylase, an oxidoreductase that incorporates one atom of molecular oxygen to hydroxylate 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 into the active hormone 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3. It is relevant to vitamin D metabolism and pharmaceutical applications.
[C12Y 114/13014] Trans-cinnamate 2-monooxygenase (1.14.13.14) -- The CPC entry C12Y 114/13014 designates trans-cinnamate 2-monooxygenase, an oxidoreductase (EC 1.14.13.14) that catalyzes the incorporation of one atom of molecular oxygen into trans-cinnamate, producing 2-hydroxycinnamate while reducing NAD(P)H. The enzyme belongs to the subclass of oxidoreductases acting on paired donors with incorporation or reduction of molecular oxygen.
[C12Y 114/13015] Cholestanetriol 26-monooxygenase (1.14.13.15) -- The entry C12Y 114/13015 refers to cholestanetriol 26-monooxygenase, an oxidoreductase enzyme that uses paired donors and incorporates one atom of molecular oxygen to hydroxylate cholestanetriol at the 26 position. It falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification hierarchy of chemistry, biochemistry, enzymes, specifically oxidoreductases acting on paired donors with incorporation or reduction of molecular oxygen (1.14).
[C12Y 114/13016] Cyclopentanone monooxygenase (1.14.13.16) -- C12Y 114/13016 classifies cyclopentanone monooxygenase, an oxidoreductase enzyme that incorporates molecular oxygen into paired donors to transform cyclopentanone, falling under the 1.14.13.16 subgroup of enzymes acting on paired donors with oxygen reduction. This entry is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification hierarchy for chemistry, biochemistry and enzymes, specifically oxidoreductases that incorporate or reduce oxygen.
[C12Y 114/13017] Cholesterol 7-alpha-monooxygenase (1.14.13.17) -- This entry classifies cholesterol 7-alpha-monooxygenase, an oxidoreductase that hydroxylates cholesterol at the 7-alpha position using molecular oxygen and NADPH as the electron donor (EC 1.14.13.17). It is placed in the CPC subgroup for oxidoreductases acting on paired donors with incorporation or reduction of oxygen.
[C12Y 114/13018] 4-Hydroxyphenylacetate 1-monooxygenase (1.14.13.18) -- The entry refers to 4-hydroxyphenylacetate 1-monooxygenase, EC 1.14.13.18, a flavin-dependent oxidoreductase that incorporates one atom of oxygen into 4-hydroxyphenylacetate while reducing the other to water, and is classified under CPC code C12Y 114/13018 within the oxidoreductases acting on paired donors with molecular oxygen.
[C12Y 114/13019] Taxifolin 8-monooxygenase (1.14.13.19) -- The CPC entry C12Y 114/13019 classifies the enzyme taxifolin 8-monooxygenase, which catalyzes the oxy-functionalisation of taxifolin by incorporating one atom of molecular oxygen into the substrate while reducing the other atom to water. It falls under oxidoreductases acting on paired donors with incorporation or reduction of molecular oxygen (EC 1.14.13.19).

It pertains to methods, compositions or biocatalysts that catalyze the hydroxylation of 2,4-dichlorophenol, often for environmental detoxification or synthesis applications.

It falls within the hierarchy chemistry → biochemistry → enzymes, specifically oxidoreductases acting on paired donors. This enzyme catalyses the hydroxylation of flavonoids at the 3′ position using oxygen and a reduced partner.
[C12Y 114/13022] Cyclohexanone monooxygenase (1.14.13.22) -- Cyclohexanone monooxygenase is an oxidoreductase that catalyzes the oxygen-dependent conversion of cyclohexanone to ε-caprolactone, incorporating one atom of molecular oxygen into the substrate while reducing the other to water. It falls under the CPC subclass 1.14.13.22 for enzymes acting on paired donors with incorporation or reduction of oxygen.

It is classified as an oxidoreductase (EC 1.14.13.33) that incorporates one atom of molecular oxygen into the substrate. [C13] SUGAR INDUSTRY
[C13B] PRODUCTION OF SUCROSE -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Reducing the size of material from which sugars are to be extracted; Presses and knives therefor,  Production and purification of sugar juices, e.g. juices derived from beet, cane or maple,  Expressing water from material from which sugars have been extracted,  Evaporators or boiling pans specially adapted for sugar juices; Evaporating, or boiling sugar juices,  Crystallisation; Crystallising apparatus; Separating crystals from mother liquors,  Extraction of sucrose from molasses; Extraction apparatus,  Drying sugar,  Cutting machines, combined cutting, sorting, and packing machines specially adapted for sugar, or  Sugar products, i.e. physical forms of sugar, such as powdered, lump or liquid sugar; Working-up of sugar  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Harvesting of sugar beets -> A01D11/02 , A01D13/00 , A01D25/00 - Harvesting of sugar-cane -> A01D45/10 - Tapping of tree-juices -> A01G23/10 - Tapping-spouts, Receptacles for juices -> A01G23/14 - Sweetmeats, Confectionery -> A23G3/00 - Foods containing carbohydrate syrups, sugars, sugar alcohols or starch hydrolysates -> A23L29/30 - Extraction of starch -> C08B30/02 , C08B30/04 - Pressing water from starch-extracted material -> C08B30/10 - Fermentation or enzyme-using processes for preparing compounds containing saccharide radicals -> C12P19/00  **Glossary:** - Aldose: An aldose is a monosaccharide (a simple sugar) containing one aldehyde group per molecule and having a chemical formula of the form C n (H2O) n (n>= 3). With only 3 carbon atoms, glyceraldehyde is the simplest of all aldoses.Aldoses isomerise to ketoses in the Lobry-de Bruyn-van Ekenstein transformation. Aldose differs from ketose in that it has a carbonyl group at the end of the carbon chain whereas the carbonyl group of a ketose is in the middle; this fact allows them to be chemically differentiated through Seliwanoff's test. - Disaccharide: A disaccharide is a sugar (a carbohydrate) composed of two monosaccharides.‘Disaccharide’ is one of the four chemical groupings of carbohydrates (monosaccharide, disaccharide, oligosaccharide and polysaccharide). - Hexose: A hexose is a monosaccharide with six carbon atoms having the chemical formula C 6 H 12 O 6 . - Ketose: A ketose is a sugar containing one ketone group per molecule.With 3 carbon atoms, dihydroxyacetone is the simplest of all ketoses and is the only one having no optical activity. Ketoses can isomerise into an aldose when the carbonyl group is located at the end of the molecule. Such ketoses are reducing sugars. - Monosaccharide: Monosaccharides (from Greek monos: single, sacchar: sugar) are the most basic unit of carbohydrates. They consist of one sugar and are usually colorless, water-soluble, crystalline solids. Some monosaccharides have a sweet taste. Examples of monosaccharides include glucose (dextrose), fructose, galactose, xylose and ribose. Monosaccharides are the building blocks of disaccharides such as sucrose (common sugar) and polysaccharides (such as cellulose and starch). - Oligosaccharide: An oligosaccharide is a saccharide containing less than six monosaccharides. The name derived from the Greek oligos, meaning "a few". - Pentose: A pentose is a monosaccharide with five carbon atoms. - Polysaccharide: Polysaccharides are polymers containing more than five monosaccharides joined together by glycosidic bonds. They are therefore very large, often branched, macromolecules. They tend to be amorphous, insoluble in water and have no sweet taste. When all the monosaccharides in a polysaccharide are the same type, the polysaccharide is called a homopolysaccharide, but when more than one type of monosaccharide is present, they are called heteropolysaccharides.Examples include storage polysaccharides such as starch and glycogen and structural polysaccharides such as cellulose and chitin. - Saccharose: Synonym for sucrose - Sucrose: Sucrose (common name: table sugar, also called saccharose) is a disaccharide of glucose and fructose, with the molecular formula C 12 H 22 O 11 . Its systematic name is α-D-glucopyranosyl- (1↔2)-β-D-fructofuranoside (ending in "oside", because it's not a reducing sugar). - Sugar: Sugar is a class of edible crystalline water-soluble carbohydrates that vary widely in sweetness and typically are optically active, including the mono-, di- and oligosaccharides (e. g. sucrose, lactose, and fructose). Sugar as a basic food carbohydrate primarily comes from sugar cane and from sugar beet, but also appears in fruit, honey, sorghum, sugar maple (in maple syrup), and in many other sources. It forms the main ingredient in much candy.In non-scientific use, the term sugar refers to sucrose (also called "table sugar" or "saccharose") - a white crystalline solid disaccharide. In this informal sense, the word "sugar" principally refers to crystalline sugars.
[C13B 10/00] Production of sugar juices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of sugar juices, sugar juices being solutions of sugar, comprising mainly sucrose, glucose, fructose which are derived from different plants, e.g. beet, cane
[C13B 10/003] using chemicals other than extracting agents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Chemical agents can be for example preservative or antioxidants to avoid oxidation of some components in the sugar juices  Extracting agents being for example water

[C13K] SACCHARIDES OBTAINED FROM NATURAL SOURCES OR BY HYDROLYSIS OF NATURALLY OCCURRING DISACCHARIDES, OLIGOSACCHARIDES OR POLYSACCHARIDES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Saccharides obtained by hydrolysis of naturally-occurring di-, oligo- or polysaccarides,  Glucose; Glucose-containing syrups,  Invert sugar; Separation of glucose or fructose from invert sugar,  Lactose,  Maltose,  Fructose,  Other naturally-occurring mono-, di- and oligosaccharides, or  Production and crystallisation methods.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preserving or chemical ripening of fruits or vegetables with sugar -> A23B7/08 - Sweetmeats, confectionery -> A23G3/00 - Animal feeding stuff from vegetable matter -> A23K10/30 - Manufacture of fodder -> A23K10/32 - Artificial sweetening agents -> A23L27/00 , A23L27/30 - Modifying nutritive qualities of food, dietetic products -> A23L33/00 - Cosmetics or similar toilet preparations containing sugars -> A61K8/60 - Medical preparations containing sugars -> A61K31/70  **Glossary:** - Aldose: An aldose is a monosaccharide (a simple sugar) containing one aldehyde group per molecule and having a chemical formula of the form C n (H 2 O) n (n≥3). With only 3 carbon atoms, glyceraldehyde is the simplest of all aldoses.Aldoses isomerise to ketoses in the Lobry-de Bruyn-van Ekenstein transformation. Aldose differs from ketose in that it has a carbonyl group at the end of the carbon chain whereas the carbonyl group of a ketose is in the middle; this fact allows them to be chemically differentiated through Seliwanoff's test. - Disaccharide: A disaccharide is a sugar (a carbohydrate) composed of two monosaccharides.'Disaccharide' is one of the four chemical groupings of carbohydrates (monosaccharide, disaccharide, oligosaccharide and polysaccharide). - Hexose: A hexose is a monosaccharide with six carbon atoms having the chemical formula C 6 H 12 O 6 . - Invert sugar: mixture of glucose and fructose, obtaining by splliting sucrose (hydrolysis) into these two components - Ketose: A ketose is a sugar containing one ketone group per molecule.With 3 carbon atoms, dihydroxyacetone is the simplest of all ketoses and is the only one having no optical activity. Ketoses can isomerise into an aldose when the carbonyl group is located at the end of the molecule. Such ketoses are reducing sugars. - Monosaccharide: Monosaccharides (from Greek monos: single, sacchar: sugar) are the most basic unit of carbohydrates. They consist of one sugar and are usually colorless, water-soluble, crystalline solids. Some monosaccharides have a sweet taste. Examples of monosaccharides include glucose (dextrose), fructose, galactose, xylose and ribose. Monosaccharides are the building blocks of disaccharides such as sucrose (common sugar) and polysaccharides (such as cellulose and starch). - Oligosaccharide: An oligosaccharide is a saccharide containing less than six monosaccharides. The name derived from the Greek oligos, meaning "a few". - Pentose: A pentose is a monosaccharide with five carbon atoms. - Polysaccharide: Polysaccharides are polymers containing more than five monosaccharides joined together by glycosidic bonds. They are therefore very large, often branched, macromolecules. They tend to be amorphous, insoluble in water and have no sweet taste. When all the monosaccharides in a polysaccharide are the same type, the polysaccharide is called a homopolysaccharide, but when more than one type of monosaccharide is present, they are called heteropolysaccharides.Examples include storage polysaccharides such as starch and glycogen and structural polysaccharides such as cellulose and chitin. - Saccharose: Synonym for sucrose - Sucrose: Sucrose (common name: table sugar, also called saccharose) is a disaccharide of glucose and fructose, with the molecular formula C 12 H 22 O 11 . Its systematic name is α-D-glucopyranosyl- (1↔2)-β-D-fructofuranoside (ending in "oside", because it's not a reducing sugar). - Sugar: Sugar is a class of edible crystalline water-soluble carbohydrates that vary widely in sweetness and typically are optically active, including the mono-, di- and oligosaccharides (e. g. sucrose, lactose, and fructose). Sugar as a basic food carbohydrate primarily comes from sugar cane and from sugar beet, but also appears in fruit, honey, sorghum, sugar maple (in maple syrup), and in many other sources. It forms the main ingredient in much candy.In non-scientific use, the term sugar refers to sucrose (also called "table sugar" or "saccharose") - a white crystalline solid disaccharide. In this informal sense, the word "sugar" principally refers to crystalline sugars.

[C14B 19/00] Hand tools specially adapted for the treatment of hides, skins, or leather in the manufacture of leather and furs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  hand tools specially adapted for the treatment of hides, skins, or leather in the manufacture of leather and furs.
[C14B 21/00] Splitting intestines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  methods and apparatuses for splitting intestines or cutting intestines longitudinally.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cleaning or cutting intestines during processing meat -> A22C17/14 or A22C17/16

[C14B 3/00] Milling leather -- **Definition:** This place covers:  methods and apparatuses for milling leather.  **Glossary:** - Milling: is a process wherein the leather is rotated in a machine, comparable to a large clothes dryer just with the absence of air and heat. Through the soft falling action the natural fine lines in the leather are intensified.
[C14B 5/00] Clicking, perforating, or cutting leather -- **Definition:** This place covers:  methods and apparatuses for clicking, perforating or cutting leather.  **Glossary:** - Clicking: die cutting of leather.
[C14B 5/02] Stamps or dies for leather articles
[C14B 5/04] for making leather belts or strips
[C14B 5/06] Machines for cutting strips spirally from discs of leather
[C14B 7/00] Special leathers and their manufacture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  special leathers, methods and apparatuses for manufacturing special leathers, e.g. leather composites, fish skin leather, snake leather, powdered leather.
[C14B 7/02] Composite leathers
[C14B 7/04] by cementing or pressing together leather pieces, strips or layers, Reinforcing or stiffening leather by means of reinforcing layers
[C14B 7/06] Leather webs built up of interengaged strips or pieces, e.g. by braiding
[C14B 9/00] Making driving belts or other leather belts or strips -- **Definition:** This place covers:  methods and apparatuses for making driving belts or other leather belts or strips.
[C14B 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Definition:** This place covers:  subject matter not provided for in the preceding groups of this subclass.
[C14C] CHEMICAL TREATMENT OF HIDES, SKINS OR LEATHER, e.g. TANNING, IMPREGNATING, FINISHING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Preparatory steps prior to tanning.  Chemical aspects of tanning (chemical compounds).  Impregnation of leather.  Coating of leather with polymers.  Devices such as drums for the chemical treatment of hides, skins or leather.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Gelatine for foodstuff -> A23J1/10 - Layered products next to a fibrous layer -> B32B - Peptides or collagen -> C07K14/78 - Macromolecular products derived from leather -> C08H1/06 - Polishing compositions -> C09G1/00 - Preparation of glue or gelatine from leather scrap -> C09H1/04 - Enzymes and Compositions thereof -> C12N9/00 - Mechanical treatment or processing of skins, hides or leather -> C14B - Artificial leather -> D06N3/00 - Dyeing of leather -> D06P3/00  **Glossary:** - Liming: Treatment of hides or skins with lime solution or other alkalis e.g. caustic soda to destroy the hair or wool, remove proteins and fat - Pelt: Hide or skin in the dehaired or dewooled, limed, fleshed, perhaps bated (delimed) and scudded condition; Ready for tanning - Raw hide: Product made from bovine hides, freed from the hair and the flesh layer and dried out in the limed state - Suede: Leather with a velvet-like surface - Wet-blue: Hide or skin, subjected to the usual beamhouse processes, chrome-tanned and left wet
